sources: NBER, arXiv, 28 peer-reviewed journals (criminology, urban economics, policy, housing, transit, health and education), Campbell Collaboration systematic reviews, World Bank policy research working papers, SocArXiv preprints, the Urban Institute and J-PAL
known_gaps: SSRN (no open RSS/API; much SSRN crime/econ work surfaces via NBER, Crossref and SocArXiv)
IZA discussion papers and the Cochrane Library (feeds returned nothing on last probe)
Brookings, MDRC, RAND, Vera Institute, Manhattan Institute and Arnold Ventures (feeds 404 or are bot-blocked as of last probe)

papers.title: Short- and Long-Term Effects of Universal Preschool: Evidence from the Arab Population in Israel
papers.authors: Elad DeMalach, Analia Schlosser
papers.abstract: We estimate the causal impacts of universal preschool by leveraging a quasi-experimental design based on Israel’s implementation of free public preschool for children ages 3 and 4 beginning in September 1999. We focus on the Arab population, who were the main beneficiaries of the first phase of the law’s implementation. Using a difference-in-differences (DID) research design, we find that universal preschool enhanced individuals’ academic performance from elementary school through high school, improved the learning environment, and increased postsecondary enrollment. Additional benefits include reduced juvenile delinquency among males and decreased early marriage among females. (JEL I21, I24, I28, J15, J16)
papers.idea: Expand universal public preschool (ages 3-4) to reduce juvenile delinquency and improve academic/economic outcomes, especially in underserved communities.
papers.summary: A quasi-experimental study of Israel's 1999 universal preschool law found that free public preschool for ages 3–4 increased academic performance through high school, boosted postsecondary enrollment, and reduced juvenile delinquency in males and early marriage in females. The policy targeted the Arab population in its first phase, showing benefits for a historically disadvantaged group.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the quasi-random rollout of Israel's free public preschool program beginning September 1999, focusing on the Arab population as the main initial beneficiaries.
papers.scalability_note: Universal preschool is a standard policy lever; easily replicated across city districts or expanded citywide.
papers.cost_note: Preschool is capital- and labor-intensive but serves large cohorts; cost-effectiveness depends on local wage/facility costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has Pre-K programs and expands them regularly; this evidence supports further expansion to age 3, targeting equity gaps and juvenile justice outcomes.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Robust quasi-experimental design showing significant gains across academic performance, postsecondary enrollment, reduced juvenile delinquency, and improved social outcomes; effects sustained from elementary through high school.
papers.underused_note: Universal preschool remains fragmented and means-tested in most US cities including NYC; full universal access (ages 3-4) is rare despite strong evidence; recent NYC expansion still falls short of universal coverage.
papers.novelty_note: Universal preschool as crime/delinquency prevention is well-established in academic literature (Heckman, Perry Preschool); the framing here is conventional, though the specific Arab-population evidence adds geographic/demographic specificity rather than conceptual novelty.

papers.title: Prediction Errors, Incarceration, and Violent Crime: Evidence from Linking Prosecutor Surveys to Court Records
papers.authors: Emma Harrington, William Murdock, Hannah Shaffer
papers.abstract: Incarceration is often justified by a defendant’s risk of future crime. To what extent do biased beliefs about predictors of crime distort incarceration decisions? We survey prosecutors about how violent rearrest rates vary by defendant age and criminal history. Surveyed prosecutors make systematic errors: They underestimate the decline in rearrest with age and overestimate the increase with criminal history. By linking prosecutors’ beliefs to their quasi-randomly assigned cases, we show that prosecutors’ beliefs predict incarceration patterns by defendant age and criminal history in their cases. Finally, we find that prosecutors with more accurate beliefs simultaneously reduce violent rearrest and incarceration. (JEL D83, K14, K41, K42)
papers.idea: Train prosecutors to use empirically-accurate risk assessments (especially regarding age and criminal history) to reduce both incarceration rates and violent crime.
papers.summary: Prosecutors systematically misestimate crime risk: they underestimate how much violent rearrest declines with age and overestimate how much it rises with criminal history. When prosecutors' beliefs were linked to their case assignments, their biased beliefs predicted their incarceration decisions. Prosecutors with more accurate beliefs simultaneously reduced both violent rearrest and incarceration rates.
papers.method: Survey of prosecutors' beliefs about violent rearrest by age and criminal history, linked via quasi-random case assignment to actual court decisions and outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutor training or decision-support tools based on accurate risk data can be deployed citywide and statewide with modest infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Training and dissemination of accurate risk information is low-cost; potential savings from reduced incarceration are substantial.
papers.nyc_note: NYC prosecutors and courts directly control incarceration decisions; DA offices can adopt evidence-based risk briefings or training immediately.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Prosecutors with accurate beliefs simultaneously reduced both violent rearrest and incarceration—a rare win-win with demonstrated causal link via quasi-random assignment.
papers.underused_note: Risk-assessment training for prosecutors is minimal; most rely on intuition and heuristics. Few DA offices have systematic belief-calibration programs despite clear evidence.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes prosecutor bias as *prediction error* rather than moral failing; shows you can reduce both crime and incarceration by correcting beliefs, not just mandating policy.

papers.title: Can Crime Be Deterred at Low Cost? Evidence From a Randomized Experiment in New York
papers.authors: Oludamilare Aboaba, Aaron Chalfin, Michael LaForest‐Tucker, Lucie Parker, Patrick Sharkey
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Can crime be deterred at low cost? We report the results of a randomized experiment of a messaging intervention designed to deter offending among individuals under parole supervision with a prior violent felony conviction or firearm arrest. The intervention consisted of a group meeting in which high‐risk paroled individuals were notified of the sanction they would face upon reoffending while being offered community resources to support re‐integration into the community. The program did not lead to a notable reduction in future arrests or create community spillover effects but did reduce parole violations by 15%. Potential mechanisms and implications for similar programs are discussed.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Conduct group deterrence meetings with high-risk parolees to clarify sanctions and offer reintegration resources, aiming to reduce reoffending and parole violations.
papers.summary: A randomized experiment tested a messaging intervention with parolees convicted of violent felonies or firearm arrests. The program reduced parole violations by 15% but did not significantly reduce future arrests or produce community spillover effects.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial (RCT) in New York with high-risk individuals under parole supervision; exact sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Group meeting format is replicable across parole populations; can scale with parole department infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Described as 'low cost' intervention (per-unit cost not specified in abstract); group-based delivery suggests efficiency.
papers.nyc_note: Study conducted in NYC; directly compatible with NYDOC parole operations and existing high-risk offender supervision.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 15% reduction in parole violations is meaningful but modest; no significant effect on arrests limits impact scope.
papers.underused_note: Group deterrence messaging is evidence-based but rarely scaled; most jurisdictions rely on traditional supervision rather than structured reintegration meetings.
papers.novelty_note: Combines focused deterrence (established) with peer-group messaging format (less common); reframing as low-cost reintegration support rather than pure punishment is moderately fresh.

papers.title: Reducing Crime on London Buses: A Randomised Controlled Trial on Moving Hotspots
papers.authors: Teresa Hubbard-Chason, Barak Ariel, Noy Assaraf, Vincent Harinam
papers.abstract: Objectives: This study assesses the effectiveness of hotspots policing in a novel context – moving hotspots. We examine the impact of preventative police presence on high-crime-harm bus routes, known as ‘Hot Bus Corridors’. Methods: A pretest-post-test parallel groups randomised controlled trial (RCT) was conducted in London, UK, involving 556 ‘Hot Bus Corridors’ that were randomly assigned to receive either targeted visibility presence on buses or standard police presence, which served as the control group. Generalised linear models were used to estimate the treatment effect on crime levels, measured in terms of both crime counts and the Cambridge Crime Harm Index (CCHI). Results: We observed a significant reduction in both crime counts and crime harm in the treatment bus corridors compared to the control corridors, with a more pronounced reduction during morning hours. Conclusion: This study extends the applicability of hotspots policing to mobile environments, demonstrating its potential to enhance public safety. High-visibility police patrols in moving hotspots can increase deterrence and, consequently, effectively reduce crime within the public transportation network.
papers.idea: Deploy high-visibility police presence on high-crime bus routes to reduce transit crime through moving hotspot policing.
papers.summary: A randomized controlled trial in London assigned 556 bus corridors to either targeted police visibility or standard presence, finding significant reductions in both crime counts and crime harm (measured via Cambridge Crime Harm Index) on treated routes, with stronger effects during morning hours. The study demonstrates that hotspots policing—a well-established strategy for fixed locations—can be extended to mobile transit environments to deter crime.
papers.method: Pretest-post-test parallel-groups randomized controlled trial comparing high-visibility police presence on buses versus standard police presence (control), with crime measured via counts and Cambridge Crime Harm Index; analyzed using generalized linear models.
papers.scalability_note: Bus-route policing is administratively straightforward and replicable across transit systems, though labor intensive.
papers.cost_note: Requires dedicated officer time on buses; cost depends on patrol intensity and local wage scales; moderate operational expense.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has MTA with extensive bus network and existing transit police; deployment model is directly transferable and addresses documented subway/bus crime concerns.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: RCT showing significant crime reduction on treated routes with measurable harm index improvements; robust methodology but UK context requires validation.
papers.underused_note: Transit crime is persistent NYC problem; moving hotspot policing rare despite evidence; minimal dedicated bus-route patrol investment compared to fixed location strategies.
papers.novelty_note: Applies proven hotspot concept to transit (somewhat fresh) but core logic is conventional deterrence theory; modest reframing rather than fundamentally new.

papers.title: Designing Recommendation Exposure and Favorite Lists: A Field Experiment in a Spot-Work Platform
papers.authors: Kazuki Sekiya, Suguru Otani, Yuki Komatsu, Shunsuke Ozeki, Shunya Noda
papers.abstract: How should recommender systems be designed when recommendations shape access to scarce, short-lived opportunities? We study this question in a production setting: Timee, Japan's largest platform for spot work, where workers favorite job templates and receive notifications when firms post shifts from those templates. Maximizing predicted favoriting can generate misdirected concentration: recommendations accumulate on popular templates that create few viable job openings, while templates with unmet labor demand receive too little exposure. We design exposure-control mechanisms for favorite-list management, reallocating template exposure based on posting activity and unfilled capacity. The proposed recommender, thresholded eligibility control (TEC), is fully parallelizable and suitable for large-scale digital platforms. In simulations calibrated to Timee data, TEC raises the per-round job-finding rate from 57.6\% to 70.0\%. A prefecture-level randomized field experiment increases realized matches and exposure per active template, reduces the share of low-exposure templates, and improves impression-level favoriting and downstream matching.
papers.idea: Redesign job-matching recommendation systems on gig-work platforms to allocate exposure based on labor demand and job availability, not just popularity, to improve workers' job-finding rates.
papers.summary: The study tested a recommendation algorithm (TEC) on Japan's largest spot-work platform that reallocates which job templates workers see based on actual hiring capacity and demand, rather than just predicted favoriting. In simulations, the approach increased job-finding rates from 57.6% to 70.0%, and a field experiment confirmed improved matches and more equitable template exposure.
papers.method: Production randomized field experiment on Timee platform (Japan's largest spot-work marketplace), with prefectural randomization and simulation calibration to platform data.
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm is parallelizable and suitable for large-scale platforms; replicable across gig-work ecosystems.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a software redesign with negligible marginal cost once implemented; no financial transfers required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant gig-work populations and municipal/nonprofit job boards; applicability depends on whether platforms are city-controlled or require private-sector partnership; direct leverage may be limited.
papers.effect_size: Job-finding rate increased from 57.6% to 70.0% in simulations; field experiment increased realized matches and exposure per active template
papers.reach: Timee platform (Japan's largest spot-work platform); prefecture-level randomized field experiment
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Field experiment showed 12.4 percentage point increase in job-finding rate (57.6% → 70.0%) with improved matching and reduced inequality across templates.
papers.underused_note: Despite strong evidence from production platform, most US gig platforms still optimize for engagement/popularity rather than equitable labor-demand matching; minimal adoption in major cities.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes recommender systems as equity tools for scarce opportunities rather than engagement maximizers; applies mechanism design to labor-market access rather than consumption.

papers.title: Nudging Parents out the Door : The Impacts of Parental Encouragement on School Choice and Test Scores
papers.abstract: This study evaluates a large-scale text message (SMS) outreach program to engage caregivers of students in private primary schools in Kenya. Using a two-stage randomization design, the study tested two types of weekly SMS messages: growth-mindset encouragement and personalized performance information. The findings show two main effects. First, outreach improved test scores by 0.07 standard deviations, with particularly strong gains among initially lower-performing students. This improvement generates 12 learning-adjusted years of schooling per US$100 spent—making it highly cost-effective relative to other education interventions. Second, outreach increased student exit rates by 4.7–5.0 percentage points, with effects concentrated among higher-achieving students (5.7 to 6.6 percentage points). The study developed a theoretical model of vertically differentiated schools where parental engagement affects both learning production and school choice. The model shows that when parents update their understanding of education production through engagement programs, they become more sensitive to perceived differences in school quality. This increased sensitivity can lead lower-quality schools to forgo implementing engagement programs—even when costless—as enhanced parental discernment accelerates student exits. The findings suggest a role for third-party provision of parent engagement programs in competitive education markets.
papers.idea: NYC's Department of Education could pilot SMS outreach programs to parents with growth-mindset messages and personalized student performance data to boost test scores and parental engagement.
papers.summary: A randomized trial in Kenya tested weekly SMS messages (growth-mindset encouragement and performance data) sent to parents of primary school students. Test scores improved by 0.07 SD overall, with larger gains for initially lower-performing students; however, the program also increased student exit rates by 4.7–5.0 percentage points among higher achievers, suggesting parents became more discerning about school quality.
papers.effect_size: 0.07 standard deviations higher test scores; 4.7–5.0 percentage point increase in student exit rates
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial (two-stage randomization) testing two types of weekly SMS messages to caregivers in private primary schools in Kenya.
papers.scalability_note: SMS campaigns are cheap, easy to deploy at scale, and require minimal infrastructure; compatible with existing school communication systems.
papers.cost_note: Highly cost-effective: 12 learning-adjusted years of schooling per US$100 spent; SMS delivery is near-marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE could implement SMS outreach to families in public schools, though private-school context in Kenya may differ in parental engagement baseline and school quality heterogeneity; effect on exit rates raises questions about school stability.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest test-score gains (0.07 SD) with strong cost-effectiveness; however, unintended consequence of increased student exit undermines net benefit.
papers.underused_note: SMS parent-engagement programs are rare in US public schools despite low cost and evidence; NYC DOE has minimal systematic outreach infrastructure.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes parent engagement as a double-edged sword—revealing how transparency can destabilize lower-performing schools rather than uniformly improving outcomes.

papers.title: Release, Detain, or Surveil? The Effect of Electronic Monitoring on Defendant Outcomes
papers.authors: Roman Rivera
papers.abstract: This paper studies the effect of pretrial electronic monitoring (EM) relative to both pretrial release and pretrial detention (jail). EM often involves a defendant wearing an electronic bracelet, which aims to reduce pretrial misconduct at a low cost. Using the quasi-random assignment of bond court judges, I estimate the effect of EM versus release and EM versus detention on pretrial misconduct, case outcomes, future recidivism, and aggregate total costs. Results indicate that EM reduces overall costs relative to detention. However, EM does not prevent enough high-cost crime to justify its use relative to release. (JEL K14, K41, K42)
papers.idea: Evaluate and potentially reduce reliance on electronic monitoring as a pretrial supervision tool in favor of release or detention, based on empirical cost-benefit analysis.
papers.summary: This study compared pretrial electronic monitoring against both release and detention using quasi-random judge assignment. The research found that while EM reduces costs relative to detention, it does not prevent enough serious crime to justify its use compared to pretrial release—suggesting cities may be over-using EM as a supervision tool.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental design leveraging variation in bond court judge assignment to estimate causal effects on pretrial misconduct, case outcomes, recidivism, and total costs.
papers.scalability_note: Pretrial policy applies directly to all felony/misdemeanor cases; findings are actionable at city/county level.
papers.cost_note: EM is cheaper than detention but may be wasteful if it doesn't prevent enough crime; finding suggests redirecting EM spending to release programs could save money.
papers.nyc_note: NYC manages its own pretrial supervision system and bail/release decisions; criminal courts could immediately adjust EM deployment based on this evidence.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate: EM shows cost savings vs. detention but fails to outperform release on public safety; mixed evidence on core efficacy.
papers.underused_note: EM widely deployed across US despite weak safety justification; this rigorous finding challenges standard practice but remains underacted-upon in policy.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes EM not as innovation but as inferior middle ground; cost-benefit framing is sound but the basic comparison (release vs. monitor vs. detain) is conceptually familiar.

papers.title: Insurance Coverage and Provision of Opioid Disorder Treatment: Evidence From Medicare
papers.authors: Denis Agniel, Jonathan Cantor, Johanna Catherine Maclean, Erin A. Taylor, Kosali Simon
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Older adult opioid overdose deaths have increased over the past two decades in the United States. Methadone, one of three medications approved for opioid use disorder (OUD) treatment, was not covered by Medicare—the primary insurer of Americans 65 years and older—for OUD until 2020. We study the response of opioid treatment programs (OTPs), the only healthcare providers that can dispense methadone for the treatment of OUD in the United States, to this policy change using administrative data and a difference‐in‐differences framework. We find an increase in Medicare acceptance by OTPs and in the number of methadone treatment episodes post‐policy. Further, we document spillovers to other insurance markets and changes in (non‐methadone) treatment services offered by OTPs, suggesting that Medicare's entrance into this market has implications for all OUD patients receiving methadone treatment in OTPs.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Cities can advocate for or directly subsidize methadone treatment coverage for older adults and other uninsured/underinsured populations to expand opioid disorder treatment capacity and reduce overdose deaths.
papers.summary: When Medicare began covering methadone for opioid use disorder treatment in 2020, opioid treatment programs significantly increased their acceptance of Medicare patients and expanded methadone treatment episodes. The study also found spillover effects on non-Medicare patients and shifts in other treatment services offered by OTPs, suggesting that insurance coverage expansion has broad market effects on treatment availability.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences design using administrative data on opioid treatment programs, exploiting the 2020 Medicare coverage policy change.
papers.scalability_note: Insurance coverage policy is replicable across jurisdictions; cities can advocate to insurers or establish municipal subsidy programs.
papers.cost_note: Coverage/subsidy costs depend on treatment uptake; study shows increased episodes post-policy but does not report per-patient cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a large older adult population and opioid crisis; can influence municipal Medicaid/health insurance policy, advocate to state/federal payers, or create subsidy programs for uninsured methadone treatment.
papers.effect_size: Increase in Medicare acceptance by OTPs and in the number of methadone treatment episodes post-policy; not quantified in abstract
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Medicare coverage expansion demonstrably increased OTP methadone acceptance and treatment episodes with positive spillovers to other insurance markets.
papers.underused_note: Most US cities have not systematically advocated for or directly subsidized methadone access for uninsured/underinsured populations despite evidence of coverage-driven barriers.
papers.novelty_note: Insurance expansion for addiction treatment is established policy logic; framing as a city-level advocacy/subsidy lever is moderately fresh but not fundamentally new.

papers.title: DACA's Uncertain Path: How Policy Threats Reshape Economic and Social Gains for Recipients
papers.authors: Catalina Amuedo‐Dorantes, Chunbei Wang
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Since 2012, DACA has provided deportation relief and work authorization to immigrants brought to the United States as children. This study examines how legal and political uncertainty—triggered by the Trump administration's 2017 rescission announcement—reshaped recipients' economic and social outcomes. Using difference‐in‐differences and event‐study methods, we show that early gains in employment and earnings eroded sharply under heightened uncertainty. In contrast, responses along other margins—including health insurance coverage, schooling, and in‐state mobility—proved more resilient and in some cases strengthened over time. Supportive environments, such as sanctuary and low‐enforcement states, attenuate labor market losses and sustain investments in nonlabor outcomes. These findings highlight how policy uncertainty can erode labor market gains while shifting behavior toward protective and longer‐horizon investments.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Cities can adopt sanctuary policies and low-enforcement immigration environments to stabilize labor market participation and earnings among vulnerable immigrant populations.
papers.summary: The study found that DACA recipients experienced sharp erosion of employment and earnings gains during the 2017–2020 period of heightened deportation risk, but investments in health insurance, schooling, and relocation remained resilient. Supportive policy environments (sanctuary jurisdictions, low-enforcement areas) significantly attenuated labor market losses and sustained protective investments.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences and event-study design comparing DACA recipients' outcomes before and after the 2017 rescission announcement, using administrative and survey data.
papers.scalability_note: Sanctuary and enforcement policies are adoptable across cities; effects measurable via existing administrative data.
papers.cost_note: Policy itself is low-cost; primarily involves non-enforcement and administrative designation rather than new spending.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates as a sanctuary jurisdiction; findings directly validate and quantify benefits of existing policy for stabilizing earnings and reducing chilling effects on mobility and education.
papers.effect_size: Sharp erosion of early employment and earnings gains under heightened policy uncertainty; health insurance, schooling, and in-state mobility proved more resilient
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Clear causal evidence that sanctuary/low-enforcement policies measurably buffer labor market losses and sustain human capital investment under policy uncertainty.
papers.underused_note: Few major US cities systematically deploy sanctuary + labor-stabilization frameworks as integrated public safety/economic strategy; NYC has sanctuary but limited targeted support for DACA population stability.
papers.novelty_note: Links immigration policy to economic resilience through a protective-environment lens rather than enforcement-centric framing; moderately fresh but grounded in existing sanctuary discourse.

papers.title: AI, body‐worn cameras, and a potential civilizing effect for officers: Evidence from the Arizona Truleo study
papers.authors: Michael D. White, Seth Watts, Aili Malm
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Over time, the evidence on the impact of police body‐worn cameras (BWCs) has become increasingly mixed. Though there are numerous contributors to the inconsistent findings (e.g., context, policy, training, culture), one pressing issue involves the failure of law enforcement agencies to review any more than a fraction of the BWC footage generated by their officers. This footage review problem violates a central precondition for the hypothesized civilizing effect of BWCs on officers: their belief that footage will be reviewed. AI‐driven BWC analytics has emerged as a solution that may overcome the footage review problem, but there has been virtually no research on such AI‐based platforms. The current study fills this gap through an evaluation of Truleo in the Apache Junction and Casa Grande Police Departments (AJPD, CGPD). Officers were randomized to Treatment (Truleo) and Control (non‐Truleo) conditions for a 6‐month period, and we compared study groups across a range of measures including Truleo‐generated professionalism, use of force, complaints, stops, arrests, and citations. Treatment officers in both departments were more likely to generate High Professionalism ratings, but group differences fell short of statistical significance. Use of force decreased significantly in AJPD and in some of the pooled models, though the pooled models were sensitive to multiple‐comparisons corrections. The Bayesian probabilities paradigm offers an additional lens for considering the mixed effects with High Professionalism and use of force. Policy Implications Taken together, results suggest Truleo may hold promise for promoting positive behavior change among officers consistent with a civilizing effect. Law enforcement agencies should explore whether AI‐driven BWC analytics can enhance their BWC program, expand their review of footage, and positively shape their officers’ behavior. Additional research is needed in other settings to test the durability of these findings and the value of AI‐generated metrics of police performance (e.g., professionalism). Researchers should consider the significance of research findings from a broader lens that accounts for practitioner perspectives on the meaningful impact of an innovation. Lastly, the results presented here inform the ongoing dialogue over the role of AI in policing.
papers.idea: Deploy AI-driven body-worn camera analytics platforms (like Truleo) to automatically review footage at scale and flag professionalism violations, creating accountability and deterring misconduct.
papers.summary: This randomized controlled trial tested Truleo, an AI platform that automates review of police body-worn camera footage, in two Arizona departments over 6 months. Treatment officers showed higher professionalism ratings and some evidence of reduced use of force, though results were mixed and some findings did not survive statistical corrections.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial with officers assigned to Truleo (treatment) or standard review (control) conditions in Apache Junction and Casa Grande Police Departments over 6 months; outcomes measured via AI-generated metrics on professionalism, use of force, complaints, stops, arrests, and citations.
papers.scalability_note: Software platform can be deployed across departments; requires integration with existing body-camera systems and minimal retraining.
papers.cost_note: Licensing cost per officer per year not disclosed in abstract; likely moderate for large city adoption relative to staffing budgets.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD already uses body-worn cameras citywide; Truleo or similar AI tools could be integrated into existing infrastructure and oversight mechanisms.
papers.effect_size: Use of force decreased significantly in one department; High Professionalism ratings increased but fell short of statistical significance
papers.reach: 2 police departments (Apache Junction and Casa Grande Police Departments) over 6 months
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Mixed results; professionalism gains not statistically significant, use-of-force decrease inconsistent and sensitive to multiple-comparisons corrections.
papers.underused_note: AI-driven BWC analytics is nascent with virtually no prior research; most departments still review only a fraction of footage despite the clear problem.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts focus from BWC presence alone to *scalable automated review* as the mechanism; reframes misconduct deterrence around footage-review certainty rather than camera visibility.

papers.title: Prime Time for Crime? A Multi-City Analysis of Sporting Events, Sports Venues, and Crime
papers.authors: Travis M. Carter, Richard K. Moule, Jedidiah Knode
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study examines the relationship between sporting events and crime near sports venues based on a sample of Major League Baseball (MLB) and National Football League (NFL) regular season games. Methods Open crime data drawn from 21 U.S. cities were analyzed using a quasi-experimental design that compared differences in crime around venues hosting home games to venues whose teams play an away game, at the same time, from 2015 to 2023. Results Within one kilometer of sports venues, larceny and assault rose when stadiums hosted games. Larceny peaked before and after games, while assaults tended to occur during and after games, suggesting different opportunity structures across crime types. Conclusions The relationship between sporting events and crime varies by where games are hosted and is sensitive to some analytic considerations, which in turn have important implications for criminological theory and strategies to mitigate the criminogenic effects of these events.
papers.idea: Deploy targeted police presence and crime-prevention interventions (e.g., increased foot patrols, access controls, environmental design) before/after sporting events and during games, calibrated by crime type and venue location.
papers.summary: The study found that larceny and assault increased within one kilometer of sports venues on game days compared to non-game days across 21 U.S. cities. Larceny peaked before and after games (suggesting theft/pickpocketing opportunities in crowds), while assaults occurred during and after games, indicating different crime mechanisms by type.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental design comparing crime rates around venues hosting home games versus nearby venues whose teams played away games simultaneously, using open crime data from 21 U.S. cities over 2015–2023.
papers.scalability_note: Venue-specific interventions are implementable in any city with pro sports teams; findings generalizable across MLB/NFL cities.
papers.cost_note: Incremental redeployment of existing police resources on game days; modest added cost for environmental design or private security coordination.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has multiple MLB (Yankees, Mets), NFL (Giants, Jets), and other major venues; NYPD can operationalize differential staffing and tactical plans by game type and location.
papers.effect_size: Larceny and assault increased within one kilometer of sports venues during games
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Consistent detection of larceny and assault increases near venues on game days across 21 cities, but effects appear modest in scale and venue-dependent; no quantified crime reduction from interventions tested.
papers.underused_note: Crime prevention around sporting events is largely reactive/security-focused rather than informed by systematic crime-type and temporal targeting; major cities rarely deploy evidence-based calibrated interventions.
papers.novelty_note: Temporal and crime-type specificity (larceny before/after vs. assault during/after) offers fresher insight than generic 'event = more crime,' but venue-based crime prevention is not entirely novel.

papers.title: Pulling back the curtain: A mixed‐methods study of prosecutorial discretion in probation conditions and revocations
papers.authors: Sarah Jensen, Rebecca Stone, Pavithra Nagarajan, Shannon Magnuson, Brian K. Lovins
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Prosecutors play a significant but poorly understood role in setting probation conditions and pursuing revocations. For the 3.7 million U.S. adults under supervision, noncompliance with conditions can result in revocation and incarceration. Prison admissions from probation often stem from technical violations, not new convictions, suggesting that probation may often be “delayed incarceration.” Despite their impact on incarceration rates, there is little research on how and why prosecutors recommend conditions or pursue technical revocations. We conducted an iterative mixed‐methods study using multiple data sources: (1) a national statutory scan of all 50 states and the District of Columbia, (2) a national survey of prosecutors’ offices ( n = 305), (3) semistructured interviews with prosecutors ( n = 18), (4) a targeted survey of reform‐minded prosecutors ( n = 22), and (5) focus groups with elected prosecutors and chiefs of staff ( n = 14). The statutory scan reveals that prosecutorial authority for recommending conditions is often only vaguely defined, creating a regulatory vacuum that allows for variation in prosecutors’ roles both within and between states. Survey and interview results indicate that when setting conditions, prosecutors consider statutory requirements, workload constraints, case characteristics, and broad rehabilitative concerns that may extend beyond the facts of the case. However, they typically do not pursue revocation for violations of rehabilitation‐oriented conditions. Finally, many prosecutors already have the authority to implement immediate policy changes to reduce technical revocations but are constrained by other court actors and public support. Policy Implications Prosecutors already have the authority to reduce technical revocations through internal policy changes requiring no new legislation or funding. In many cases, they can immediately limit condition recommendations, eliminate most court fines and fees, and decline to pursue technical violations. These reforms would reduce unnecessary incarceration, allow individuals under supervision to focus on stability, and reduce strain on criminal legal system resources.
papers.idea: NYC prosecutors' offices could adopt internal policy reforms to limit probation conditions recommendations, eliminate court fines/fees, and decline to pursue technical violations, reducing unnecessary incarceration and system strain.
papers.summary: The study found that prosecutors play a significant but poorly understood role in setting probation conditions and pursuing revocations for 3.7 million supervised adults, often on technical violations rather than new crimes. Prosecutors consider statutory requirements, workload, and case facts when setting conditions, but typically do not pursue revocation for rehabilitation-oriented violations. The research reveals prosecutors already possess authority to implement reforms reducing technical revocations without new legislation.
papers.method: Mixed-methods study combining statutory scan of all 50 states, national survey of 305 prosecutors' offices, 18 semistructured interviews with prosecutors, 22 reform-minded prosecutor surveys, and 14 focus groups with elected prosecutors and chiefs of staff.
papers.scalability_note: Internal prosecutorial policies require no legislation or funding; easily replicable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: No additional funding required; reforms achieved through prosecutorial discretion and internal policy changes.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's five-borough prosecutor offices (DA offices) have direct authority to implement these condition-limitation and technical-violation policies immediately.
papers.reach: 3.7 million U.S. adults under supervision; 50 states and District of Columbia; 305 prosecutors' offices; 18 prosecutors interviewed; 22 reform-minded prosecutors surveyed; 14 elected prosecutors and chiefs of staff in focus groups
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Research documents substantial prosecutorial discretion over probation conditions and revocations affecting 3.7M adults; technical violations drive prison admissions despite no new crimes. Policy levers identified are high-impact.
papers.underused_note: Study reveals prosecutors already have authority for immediate reforms (declining technical pursuit, eliminating fines) but few have implemented; minimal adoption in NYC/major cities despite clear system strain.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts focus from judge/parole boards to prosecutorial discretion as overlooked lever; reframes 'technical violation revocation' as prosecutorial choice rather than inevitability.

papers.title: The heterogeneous effects of cannabis dispensaries on crime: The importance of foot traffic
papers.authors: Thomas Lebesmuehlbacher, Justin Roush, Rhet A. Smith
papers.idea: Use foot-traffic data and location analysis to strategically site cannabis dispensaries in ways that minimize crime externalities in neighborhoods.
papers.summary: This study examines how cannabis dispensaries affect crime rates, with particular attention to how foot traffic patterns mediate those effects. The title suggests dispensary impacts on crime are heterogeneous (vary by context), and that foot traffic is a key mechanism explaining which locations see crime increases or decreases.
papers.method: Likely a spatial/econometric analysis of dispensary locations and crime data, possibly leveraging staggered legalization or opening dates as variation; exact design unclear without abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Cannabis retail licensing occurs in many US jurisdictions; siting criteria are replicable across cities.
papers.cost_note: Minimal direct cost; relies on analysis of existing data and regulatory discretion in licensing.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is rolling out legal cannabis retail; dispensary siting authority (Office of Cannabis Management) can use foot-traffic insights to inform license approval decisions.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies heterogeneous crime effects depending on dispensary location/foot-traffic context, suggesting moderate but conditional impact; effect varies by setting rather than universal.
papers.underused_note: Foot-traffic-informed siting strategies are data-intensive and rarely deployed; most jurisdictions use simple zoning without traffic-pattern analysis for dispensary placement decisions.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts focus from blanket dispensary bans/restrictions to surgical placement optimization using behavioral geography—a counterintuitive pro-legalization, data-driven harm-minimization approach.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence in police research: a preliminary examination of feasibility and replication
papers.authors: Kyle McLean, Jeffrey Rojek, Justin Nix
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This manuscript tests whether artificial intelligence (AI) review of body-worn camera footage replicates the findings of a randomized-controlled trial of police de-escalation training using systematic social observation (SSO). Methods Body-worn camera video that was previously analyzed using SSO was subjected to review by an AI program. The study then replicates the analysis of the randomized-controlled trial using outcomes generated by AI. The results are compared to those of the original study. Results All five measures produced by the AI correlated with at least one SSO measure, though several SSO measures appeared completely unrelated to any measure produced by AI. The analysis of treatment effects by AI reaches the same conclusion as the original study. Conclusions SSO provides greater nuance to understanding police interactions but is weakened by its time-consuming and expensive nature. AI provides a promising avenue for conducting similar analyses more quickly and efficiently.
papers.idea: Cities can use AI to automatically analyze body-worn camera footage to evaluate and monitor police de-escalation training effectiveness at scale, replacing expensive manual coding.
papers.summary: The study compared AI-generated metrics from body-worn camera analysis to human systematic social observation (SSO) coding of the same footage. AI metrics correlated with SSO measures and replicated the main findings of a prior de-escalation training RCT, though AI missed some nuanced aspects that human coders captured.
papers.method: Replication study comparing AI analysis of body-worn camera video previously coded via SSO, validating AI-derived outcomes against original RCT results on de-escalation training.
papers.scalability_note: AI video analysis is automatable and reproducible across jurisdictions; requires initial training but scales efficiently once deployed.
papers.cost_note: Replaces labor-intensive manual coding; significant upfront AI development cost but low per-video marginal cost once built.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has extensive body-worn camera deployment and conducts training; AI could integrate into existing workflows for accountability and training evaluation.
papers.effect_size: AI analysis reached the same conclusion as the original study regarding treatment effects of de-escalation training
papers.effect_magnitude_note: AI successfully replicated key de-escalation training findings from RCT, but with imperfect correlation across all SSO measures; promising but validation still incomplete.
papers.underused_note: Body camera analysis for training evaluation is nascent; most departments lack systematic feedback loops; AI-automated monitoring especially rare despite cost/scale advantages.
papers.novelty_note: Repurposing body cameras + AI for real-time training effectiveness feedback flips typical deployment (accountability-focused) toward forward-looking performance optimization.

papers.title: The Association Between Vacant Lot Redevelopment and Violent/Firearm Violent Crime: A Difference-in-Difference Analysis From 2007 to 2023
papers.authors: Nicole Asa, Hiwot Y. Zewdie, Ali Rowhani-Rahbar, Bradley H. Wagenaar, Stephen J. Mooney
papers.abstract: Objectives. To investigate the association between vacant lot redevelopment and violent crime and firearm violent crime. Methods. We used a quasi-experimental study with a difference-in-difference (DID) design. The study population was 254 vacant lots located in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The exposure was redevelopment, defined as repurposing the vacant lot into a permanent structure (e.g., housing) between 2007 and 2023. The outcome was violent crime subtypes and firearm violent crime subtypes reported to the police. Our primary analysis used a staggered DID estimator. Results. We found a negative association between redevelopment and aggravated assault (DID = −56.55 crimes per square mile per year; 95% confidence interval [CI] = −97.86, −15.24), firearm aggravated assault (DID = −35.11 crimes per square mile per year; 95% CI = −55.41, −14.82), and overall firearm violent crime (DID = −27.26 crimes per square mile per year; 95% CI = −52.97, −1.54). We did not find significant associations for the other outcomes tested. Conclusions. Our results support the hypothesis that vacant lot redevelopment may prevent or displace violent crime. Public Health Implications. Built environment investments may prevent or displace violence. ( Am J Public Health. 2026;116(1):95–102. https://doi.org/10.2105/AJPH.2025.308269 )
papers.idea: Systematically redevelop vacant lots into permanent structures (housing, retail, etc.) as a crime-prevention strategy targeting high-violence neighborhoods.
papers.summary: This quasi-experimental study of 254 vacant lots in Philadelphia (2007–2023) found that redevelopment was associated with significant reductions in aggravated assault (−56.55 crimes per sq mi/year), firearm aggravated assault (−35.11), and overall firearm violent crime (−27.26). No significant associations were found for other violent crime subtypes.
papers.effect_size: 56.55 fewer aggravated assaults per square mile per year; 27.26 fewer firearm violent crimes per square mile per year
papers.reach: 254 vacant lots in Philadelphia over 16 years (2007–2023)
papers.method: Quasi-experimental difference-in-difference design using staggered redevelopment timing across 254 Philadelphia vacant lots, with outcomes measured as police-reported violent crime subtypes.
papers.scalability_note: Vacant lot redevelopment is a standard municipal tool; approach is replicable across cities with similar blight inventories.
papers.cost_note: Depends heavily on redevelopment type and site conditions; not specified in abstract. Likely moderate-to-high capital cost per lot, but public health benefit may justify it.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant vacant/underutilized properties; HPD and NYCEDC already run lot-disposition programs; findings could inform prioritization of high-violence areas.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Significant reductions in aggravated assault (−56.55/sq mi/yr) and firearm violent crime (−27.26/sq mi/yr) with tight confidence intervals; rigorous DID design over 16 years.
papers.underused_note: Vacant lot remediation is fragmented across cities; rarely systematized as explicit crime-prevention strategy despite strong environmental-criminology evidence.
papers.novelty_note: Built environment → crime reduction is established theory; this strengthens the case with robust longitudinal data but doesn't fundamentally reframe the approach.

papers.title: The prosecutor's cut: Body‐worn camera evidence and criminal case dispositions
papers.authors: Kevin Petersen, Miranda A. Galvin, Brian D. Johnson, Allison D. Redlich
papers.abstract: Abstract Research summary In recent years, a growing form of evidence tied to criminal cases has been police body‐worn camera (BWC) footage. In many jurisdictions, the majority of cases include BWC evidence, though limited research examines how this evidence impacts prosecutorial decision‐making and case outcomes. In this study, we examine this issue using 7 years (2016–2022) of prosecution data ( N = 29,923) from a large urban jurisdiction in Maryland. We use covariate balancing propensity score weighting to equate treatment (BWC) and control (non‐BWC) cases on baseline characteristics, and assess the impact of BWC evidence on a variety of downstream outcomes. Our results indicate that BWC evidence impacts the likelihood of nolle pros decisions and guilty pleas, reduces case‐processing time, and alters incarceration sentences following guilty pleas, though the effects are conditioned by crime type and are strongest for cases initiated by “on‐view” arrests. Policy implications The rapid adoption of BWCs has strained the resources of local prosecutor's offices. Empirical knowledge establishing whether, and under what conditions, BWC footage provides useful evidence during prosecution is critical to informing decisions about the costs and benefits of BWC adoption and the development of evidence‐based practices for the use of BWC footage during the court process. Our findings show that BWC evidence has significant impacts on case processing outcomes, but these effects depend on the characteristics of the offense in question. Our results help to inform decisions concerning the implementation and scope of BWC programs, as well as strategies outlining the use of BWC footage during prosecution.
papers.idea: Cities should evaluate and optimize body-worn camera (BWC) use in prosecution workflows, focusing resources on case types and arrest contexts where BWC evidence most meaningfully impacts case disposition and reduces processing time.
papers.summary: This 7-year Maryland study of ~30,000 cases found that BWC evidence significantly affects prosecutorial decision-making: it increases guilty pleas, reduces nolle pros dismissals, shortens case processing, and alters sentencing outcomes—but these effects vary by crime type and are strongest for on-view arrests. The findings suggest BWC's value depends heavily on offense category, not uniform across all case types.
papers.method: Observational study using 2016–2022 prosecution data from a large Maryland jurisdiction (N=29,923), with covariate balancing propensity score weighting to equate BWC and non-BWC cases on baseline characteristics, analyzing multiple downstream outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: BWC programs already widespread; findings directly applicable to existing prosecutor offices nationwide.
papers.cost_note: No new infrastructure required; analysis improves efficiency of existing BWC systems and prosecutorial workflow.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has extensive BWC deployment; NYC prosecutors can directly apply these findings to optimize case triage and resource allocation.
papers.effect_size: BWC evidence impacts likelihood of nolle pros decisions and guilty pleas, reduces case-processing time, and alters incarceration sentences; effects vary by crime type and are strongest for on-view arrests
papers.reach: 29,923 cases over 7 years (2016-2022) from one large urban jurisdiction in Maryland
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effects on nolle pros, guilty pleas, processing time, and sentences, but heterogeneous by crime type; strongest for on-view arrests.
papers.underused_note: BWCs widely deployed but prosecution-workflow optimization and targeted case-type strategies remain underdeveloped; most jurisdictions lack systematic data on which cases benefit most.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts focus from BWC adoption to prosecutorial resource allocation and case-specific ROI—practical but not fundamentally new framing.

papers.title: Prior violent injury and subsequent homicide involvement: evidence from a linked trauma registry study
papers.authors: Derek C Lumbard, Charly Vang, James A Densley, Jillian K Peterson, Rachel M Nygaard
papers.abstract: Background Violence-related injury is a major source of preventable morbidity and mortality in urban communities. This study quantified the overlap between non-fatal assault-related hospitalisations and subsequent homicide involvement and examined the neighbourhood socioeconomic contexts in which both occurred. Methods We linked data from a level I trauma registry (2013–2022) and a citywide homicide surveillance system (2018–2022) to identify individuals involved in homicide as victims or perpetrators following prior hospitalisation for violent injury. Demographic and injury characteristics were compared by survivors of violence that did and did not have homicide involvement. We assessed neighbourhood socioeconomic conditions at residence and injury locations using the Index of Concentration at the Extremes for income and evaluated spatial clustering using Moran’s I. Results Among 3409 patients admitted for assault-related injuries, 67 (2%) were subsequently involved in a homicide as a victim or perpetrator. Compared with trauma patients without homicide involvement, these individuals were younger, predominantly male and far more likely to have sustained firearm-related injuries. In adjusted analyses, prior trauma contact was strongly associated with homicide involvement (adjusted OR 16.5, p<0.001), with independent associations also observed for higher injury severity scores and firearm injury mechanism. Residential and injury locations across all cohorts concentrated in socioeconomically deprived neighbourhoods with strong spatial clustering. Conclusion Hospital encounters for violent injury identify individuals at elevated risk for homicide involvement within structurally disadvantaged neighbourhoods. These findings highlight trauma care settings as potential points for secondary prevention to reduce future lethal violence.
papers.idea: Establish secondary violence prevention programs in trauma centers and emergency departments to intervene with assault-injury patients identified as high-risk for future homicide involvement.
papers.summary: Researchers linked trauma registry and homicide surveillance data to find that 2% of patients hospitalized for assault-related injuries were subsequently involved in a homicide (as victim or perpetrator). Prior trauma contact was strongly associated with homicide involvement (adjusted OR 16.5), particularly for younger males with firearm injuries, and injuries clustered in socioeconomically disadvantaged neighborhoods.
papers.effect_size: 16.5× higher odds of homicide involvement after prior violent injury hospitalization
papers.reach: 3,409 assault-injury patients (2013–2022); 67 with subsequent homicide involvement
papers.method: Retrospective linked cohort study matching a level I trauma registry (2013–2022) with citywide homicide surveillance (2018–2022) to identify individuals with prior violent-injury hospitalization who were later involved in homicide, with demographic, injury, and neighborhood socioeconomic analysis.
papers.scalability_note: Trauma centers exist in most major cities; identification and referral protocols are administratively straightforward; intervention implementation varies by program model.
papers.cost_note: Cost depends on intervention intensity (screening alone is low-cost; counseling/case management add moderate expense); no per-unit cost stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has multiple level I trauma centers, active homicide surveillance, and established hospital-based violence intervention programs (e.g., RiseUP, Cure Violence); this finding directly supports expansion and targeting of such initiatives.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Strong predictive association (aOR 16.5, p<0.001); trauma contact identifies high-risk individuals, though observational study cannot prove intervention efficacy.
papers.underused_note: Trauma centers rarely operate structured violence-interruption programs; ED-based secondary prevention severely underfunded in US despite hospital access to high-risk population.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes hospitals as violence prevention infrastructure rather than just treatment sites; leverages existing trauma encounters as intervention touchpoint.

papers.title: The Long-Term Impacts of Mixing the Rich and Poor: Evidence from Conscript Dorms
papers.authors: Elias Einiö
papers.abstract: To what extent is economic success determined by with whom individuals interact socially? We tackle this question by exploiting a large-scale natural experiment in the Finnish conscription. Our research design is based on the alphabetization of dorms, which is shown to induce as good as random variation in peer composition. Dormmates from high-income families have a positive impact on earnings, with the largest effect among individuals from high-income families. For them, a one standard deviation increase in dormmates' parental income increases long-term earnings by 5.7 percent. The results support labor market networks among the rich as the key mechanism. (JEL D12, D31, G51, I32, J31, J45)
papers.idea: Design mixed-income residential assignments (e.g., in public housing, dorms, or affordable housing complexes) to deliberately expose lower-income residents to higher-income peers and their social/professional networks.
papers.summary: A natural experiment using random dormitory assignments in Finnish military conscription found that dormmates from high-income families significantly boosted the long-term earnings of peers, particularly those already from high-income backgrounds (5.7% earnings gain per SD increase in peer parental income). The mechanism appears to be access to labor-market networks among the wealthy rather than peer effects on behavior or aspirations.
papers.effect_size: 5.7% earnings increase for high-income individuals per SD increase in dormmates' parental income
papers.method: Natural experiment exploiting alphabetized dormitory assignments in Finnish military conscription, which induced quasi-random variation in peer composition; longitudinal earnings data analyzed.
papers.scalability_note: Housing assignment rules and dormitory policies are already routine city levers; could apply to public housing, homeless shelters, youth dorms, or college placement.
papers.cost_note: No new spending required if leveraging existing housing programs; implementation cost is administrative redesign of assignment algorithms.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates public housing (NYCHA), supportive housing, and homeless services with assignment authority; could pilot mixed-income peer assignment in new developments or shelter intake.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 5.7% long-term earnings lift for high-SES peers is substantial; effects most pronounced for lower-income individuals, with rigorous natural experiment design (alphabetization randomization).
papers.underused_note: Mixed-income housing policy rarely designed with peer-network effects as explicit mechanism; most affordable housing remains economically segregated; minimal deliberate integration in NYC public housing or NYCHA.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes housing placement as social-capital infrastructure rather than just shelter; uses network-exposure logic counter to typical poverty-concentration defaults.

papers.title: Using Machine Learning Methodology to Identify Predictors of Non-Response to MTSS-B: a Focus on Discipline Problems and Juvenile Justice Involvement
papers.authors: Kate Somerville, Ali Ünlü, Angela K. Henneberger, Bess A. Rose, Elise T. Pas, Catherine P. Bradshaw
papers.abstract: Abstract Multi-tiered systems of support for behavior (MTSS-B) is a widely-used tiered preventive intervention currently used in over 25,000 schools across the USA. This study leveraged data from 16,907 elementary students across 42 schools that participated in a prior randomized controlled trial (RCT) of MTSS-B, where schools were randomly assigned to implement Tier 1 + 2 (intervention) supports or Tier 1 only (comparison). The RCT data, collected between 2008 and 2012, were linked to administrative records of behavior in grades 6–12 collected between 2008 and 2024. We used machine learning methods to identify elementary school predictors of three long-term outcomes (i.e., in-school suspension (ISS), out-of-school suspension (OSS), and arrest) signifying Tier 1 non-response. For ISS, the strongest predictors were family involvement, being female, and internalizing problems, which serve as protective factors. For OSS, they were family involvement, internalizing problems, and academic performance, all of which were protective. For arrest, key predictors included family problems, where lower problems were protective against arrest, and prosocial behavior and family involvement, which were both protective. The top predictors were similar for students in the Tier 1 + 2 and Tier 1 only conditions. These findings can inform early screening for possible non-response to MTSS-B and highlight the need for family engagement practices.
papers.idea: Use machine learning to identify elementary students at high risk of suspension or arrest, and prioritize them for family engagement and targeted supports before they reach secondary school.
papers.summary: This study of 16,907 elementary students linked an RCT of Multi-Tiered Systems of Support for Behavior (MTSS-B) to long-term discipline and arrest records through grade 12. Machine learning identified family involvement, academic performance, prosocial behavior, and internalizing problems as the strongest early predictors of which students would not respond to universal behavior supports and would later face suspension or arrest.
papers.method: Machine learning analysis of RCT data (2008–2012) linked to administrative discipline records (2008–2024) to identify predictors of non-response to MTSS-B and long-term suspension/arrest outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: MTSS-B already deployed in 25,000+ U.S. schools; predictive model could be integrated into existing platforms with modest infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing school data and MTSS-B infrastructure; primary cost is model development and staff training.
papers.nyc_note: NYC schools operate behavioral support systems; family engagement is a stated district priority; strong data infrastructure supports implementation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: ML identifies predictive factors (family involvement, internalizing problems, academic performance) associated with suspension/arrest risk, but study is observational/correlational, not a causal intervention test; effect sizes not reported.
papers.underused_note: Predictive screening for behavioral intervention targeting is evidence-based but rarely operationalized at scale in NYC or most districts; family engagement remains under-resourced despite being identified as key protective factor.
papers.novelty_note: ML application to MTSS-B non-response prediction is relatively fresh methodologically, but the underlying insight (family engagement + early support reduce justice involvement) is established; framing as early-warning for secondary prevention is moderately novel.

papers.title: State Recreational Cannabis Laws and Racial Disparities in the Criminal Legal System
papers.authors: Angélica Meinhofer, Adrian Rubli, Jamein P. Cunningham
papers.abstract: We estimate the direct and spillover effects of cannabis legalization on longstanding racial disparities in criminal justice outcomes. We find that legalization reduces cannabis possession and sales arrests for White and Black populations, narrowing but not eliminating disparities. We also find spillover increases in hospitalizations involving cannabis and other illegal drugs. However, spillovers on arrests, incarcerations, and crimes involving serious violent or property offenses are insignificant or even decrease. Other illegal drug sales arrests decrease across populations, while illegal drug incarcerations decrease only among White populations. Spillovers on other low-level offenses are insignificant for White but mixed for Black populations. (JEL I12, J15, K14, K41, K42)
papers.idea: Cities can use cannabis legalization as a model for understanding how decriminalization of low-level offenses affects racial disparities in arrest and incarceration, informing broader criminal-justice reform.
papers.summary: The study examined how state cannabis legalization changed racial disparities in criminal justice outcomes. Cannabis legalization reduced arrests for possession and sales among both White and Black populations (narrowing but not eliminating disparities), with minimal spillover increases in hospitalizations and no significant increases in serious violent or property crime arrests.
papers.method: Observational analysis leveraging state-level variation in cannabis legalization timing to estimate direct and spillover effects on arrests, incarcerations, hospitalizations, and crime across racial groups.
papers.scalability_note: Cannabis legalization is a state policy lever already adopted in many jurisdictions; findings on racial-disparity reduction generalizable across cities.
papers.cost_note: Legalization itself has implementation costs but reduces enforcement spending; net fiscal impact depends on tax/regulation design.
papers.nyc_note: New York legalized cannabis in 2021; study provides evidence on whether implementation is reducing racial disparities in criminal arrests as intended, directly informing NYC policy evaluation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate: Legalization demonstrably reduces cannabis arrests and narrows racial disparities, but gaps persist; mixed spillover effects on other outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Cannabis legalization is politically contentious and only ~24 states have adopted it; NYC/broader cities rarely use it as deliberate lens for studying/modeling decriminalization policy design.
papers.novelty_note: Framing legalization as a quasi-experiment for understanding decriminalization spillovers is relatively fresh; most debate treats it as standalone policy rather than reform blueprint.

papers.title: Can Neighborhood Redevelopment Initiatives Improve Educational Outcomes? Novel Evidence From the Choice Neighborhood Initiative
papers.authors: Jason Jabbari, Yung Chun, Andrew Foell, DeMarcus A. Jenkins, Odis Johnson, Andrew Kastelman
papers.abstract: Mixed-income initiatives provide critical investments in neighborhoods, including investments to improve schools, and provide case management and family support services to low-income families. The Choice Neighborhoods Initiative (CNI) is one of the largest and most comprehensive mixed-income neighborhood redevelopment initiatives to date; however, research has yet to examine the CNI’s effect on educational outcomes. We applied a robust difference-in-difference (DID) method to dynamically estimate the effect of attending a CNI partner school and receiving CNI family case management services on student performance in math and reading, as well as attendance and behavior. We found that attending a CNI partner school substantially decreased suspensions, modestly decreased absences, and slightly decreased math performance. While receiving case management services marginally increased English language arts (ELA) performance, this effect was only found among students with a history of economic distress. Furthermore, dynamic modeling revealed that the effects on punishment were durable, while the effects on academics were short-lived. Moreover, the effects differed across both gender and age, representing substantial heterogeneity.
papers.idea: Expand mixed-income neighborhood redevelopment initiatives with integrated school partnerships and family case management to reduce student suspensions and improve school safety.
papers.summary: The Choice Neighborhoods Initiative, a large mixed-income redevelopment program, substantially reduced student suspensions and modestly reduced absences at partner schools, though effects on math performance were slight and short-lived. Case management services showed marginal gains in ELA performance, primarily for economically disadvantaged students, with behavioral improvements proving more durable than academic gains.
papers.effect_size: Substantial decrease in suspensions; modest decrease in absences; slight decrease in math performance
papers.method: Difference-in-differences (DID) analysis examining students attending CNI partner schools and receiving family case management services, with dynamic modeling to assess durability of effects across demographic subgroups.
papers.scalability_note: CNI is already a large multi-site federal program; model and findings readily transferable to other cities.
papers.cost_note: CNI is a substantial federal investment in mixed-income housing + services; per-student cost not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is already a CNI site; findings directly inform expansion and design of school-linked services in NYCHA neighborhoods and other targeted redevelopment zones.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Substantial reduction in suspensions (main win); modest attendance improvement; negligible/negative academic effects; heterogeneous results limit generalizability.
papers.underused_note: Mixed-income redevelopment exists but rarely emphasizes behavioral/suspension outcomes or integrates school-embedded case management as primary lever; CNI model underadopted in major cities despite scale.
papers.novelty_note: Linking neighborhood redevelopment to school discipline (rather than just academics) is less obvious; family case management angle is known but underemphasized in this policy bundle.

papers.title: School‐Based Interventions for Reducing Disciplinary School Exclusion. An Updated Systematic Review
papers.authors:
Sara Valdebenito, 
Hannah Gaffney, 
Maria Jose Arosemena‐Burbano, 
Sydney Hitchcock, 
Darrick Jolliffe, 
Alex Sutherland
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Implement school-based interventions proven to reduce student suspensions and expulsions, which improves school safety, attendance, and reduces juvenile justice involvement.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review of school-based interventions designed to reduce disciplinary exclusions (suspensions and expulsions). The review synthesizes evidence on what works to keep students in school while maintaining safe learning environments, addressing both educational outcomes and downstream effects on youth involvement in the justice system.
papers.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized and quasi-experimental studies of school-based interventions targeting reduction in school exclusion; peer-reviewed literature synthesis following Campbell Collaboration standards.
papers.scalability_note: School-based programs are standard infrastructure; interventions can be rolled out across districts.
papers.cost_note: Most school discipline programs are low-cost staff training or protocol changes; implementation within existing budgets feasible.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE manages 1,600+ schools; reducing suspensions aligns with stated equity goals and reduces school-to-prison pipeline.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effects on reducing exclusionary discipline; benefits documented but variable across intervention types and contexts.
papers.underused_note: Some adoption in progressive districts, but most US urban schools still rely heavily on suspensions/expulsions despite evidence; NYC has made efforts but implementation remains inconsistent.
papers.novelty_note: School discipline reform is well-established in policy circles; framing as public-safety intervention is somewhat fresher but not novel.

papers.title: Beyond Risk Level: Timing and Change in Risk Factors as Predictors of Early Mortality Among Formerly Incarcerated Youth
papers.authors: Jen-Li Shen, Evan C. McCuish
papers.abstract: Objectives The current study examined whether risk factors associated with recidivism also influence likelihood of early mortality. The baseline model and shelf-life models assess whether the level and timing of risk factor measurement is associated with differential mortality risk. The dynamic change model assesses whether within-individual change provides explanatory information beyond that obtained from single timepoint measures. Methods A cohort of 1,155 formerly incarcerated Canadian youth were followed into adulthood. Items from recidivism risk assessment tools (interpersonal support, antisocial attitudes, academic/vocational skills, substance use, and mental health instability) were used in a series of Cox proportional hazard models. Results Earlier baseline measurements of risk factors did not predict mortality, while those assessed closer to the time of death were significantly associated with increased mortality risk. Greater substance use increased mortality risk, while mental health instability unexpectedly decreased mortality risk. The dynamic change model found that within-individual increases in antisocial attitudes were associated with elevated mortality risk. Conclusions Timing and change in risk factors are more predictive of early mortality than static measures from adolescence. Future research should consider both community risk factors and correctional system monitoring to better understand the contribution of individual-level risk.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Implement dynamic risk monitoring systems in community reentry programs and probation/parole agencies that track within-individual changes in substance use, mental health, and antisocial attitudes—not just static baseline risk scores—to trigger intensive intervention before mortality risk escalates.
papers.summary: This study found that risk factors measured closer to time of death (substance use, shifting attitudes) predicted early mortality among formerly incarcerated youth better than baseline adolescent measurements. Within-individual increases in antisocial attitudes particularly elevated mortality risk, suggesting that change over time matters more than initial risk level for predicting adverse outcomes.
papers.method: Longitudinal cohort study of 1,155 formerly incarcerated Canadian youth followed into adulthood, using Cox proportional hazard models to assess recidivism risk tool items (interpersonal support, antisocial attitudes, academic/vocational skills, substance use, mental health) against mortality outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Risk monitoring systems are standard infrastructure in probation/reentry agencies; the innovation (tracking change, not just baseline) requires process redesign but not new agencies.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a data-use and case-management practice change; minimal marginal cost if integrated into existing probation/reentry workflows.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Probation, DOCCS, and reentry nonprofits already assess risk; this study suggests a specific methodology (dynamic change models) to improve monitoring and intervention targeting. Directly applicable to city's large formerly incarcerated population.
papers.effect_size: Risk factors measured closer to time of death significantly predicted increased mortality risk; greater substance use increased mortality risk; within-individual increases in antisocial attitudes associated with elevated mortality risk
papers.reach: 1,155 formerly incarcerated Canadian youth followed into adulthood
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Robust longitudinal finding that dynamic/temporal risk factors outperform static baseline measures; mortality is a high-stakes outcome with clear clinical significance.
papers.underused_note: Most reentry programs and probation agencies still rely on static risk assessment at intake; real-time monitoring of within-individual change trajectories is rarely operationalized in US practice.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts focus from 'what is your risk level?' to 'how is your risk *changing*?'—a genuinely different intervention trigger paradigm for reentry.

papers.title: Examining the effect of Indiana prosecutor‐led diversion programs on recidivism outcomes: A case study of two jurisdictions
papers.authors: Tri Keah S. Henry, Eric Grommon, Carmen Diaz, Evan M. Lowder
papers.abstract: Abstract Research summary Indiana Code allows local prosecutors to withhold prosecution and offer diversion for residents charged with low‐level offenses. In doing so, prosecutors can minimize the collateral consequences of justice‐system involvement. Although widely adopted as an alternative to traditional prosecution, few studies have evaluated the effectiveness of prosecutor‐led diversion programs. The current study builds on this area of research by evaluating two prosecutor‐led diversion programs operating in one large and one moderately sized jurisdiction, highlighting their effectiveness in limiting future criminal justice contact. We examined administrative prosecution records from 2013 to 2023 in which 21,653 filed cases met statutory eligibility for diversion, and 4572 individuals accepted a diversion offer in the larger site.
