In the moderate‐sized jurisdiction, 17,357 cases were eligible, with 6335 offers accepted. Propensity score‐weighted and instrumental variable regression techniques indicate that diversion was associated with a reduced probability of a new criminal case filing 1‐year after the instant case filing relative to all other eligible cases. Moreover, program participation moderated the effect of race on future criminal justice contact. Policy implications Prosecutor‐led diversion programs are one strategy used to efficiently manage criminal caseloads, hold defendants accountable, temper the collateral consequences of conviction, and reduce recidivism. The findings reported here indicate that these programs are associated with lower rates of recidivism. However, the programs’ discretionary design results in case‐ and defendant‐level sorting and selection processes that shape program efficacy. After adjusting for sorting and selection effects, associations between diversion and favorable outcomes persist but raise important questions about both equitable access to diversion programs and the reliability of previously reported diversion findings.
papers.idea: Expand prosecutor-led diversion programs for low-level offenses as an alternative to prosecution, with careful attention to equitable access across racial groups.
papers.summary: The study evaluated two Indiana prosecutor-led diversion programs (one large, one moderate jurisdiction) serving 10,907 diverted individuals from 2013–2023. Diversion participants showed significantly lower rates of new criminal case filings within 1 year compared to eligible non-participants, and the program moderated disparities in racial outcomes; however, discretionary design created selection effects that warrant attention to equity.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental analysis of administrative prosecution records (21,653 eligible cases in large site, 17,357 in moderate site) using propensity score weighting and instrumental variable regression to estimate recidivism reduction.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutor-led diversion is already standard in many jurisdictions and requires no new technology; scaling depends mainly on prosecutorial policy and training.
papers.cost_note: Diversion typically involves case processing and possible light conditions (e.g., community service) rather than incarceration, yielding significant cost savings; per-unit cost not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has prosecutorial discretion and some diversion programs; this research validates expansion and suggests need for racial-equity audits to ensure fair access.
papers.effect_size: Reduced probability of new criminal case filing 1-year after diversion (magnitude not quantified in abstract)
papers.reach: 26,010 individuals across two jurisdictions (4,572 accepted diversion in larger site; 6,335 in moderate-sized jurisdiction)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect on 1-year recidivism; rigorous methods (propensity scoring, IV regression) but modest absolute risk reduction and limited follow-up window.
papers.underused_note: Prosecutor diversion exists in many jurisdictions but remains underutilized relative to caseloads; equity gaps and discretionary design limit adoption despite evidence.
papers.novelty_note: Prosecutor diversion is established practice; novelty lies mainly in equity-focused evaluation and documenting racial moderation effects, not the intervention itself.

papers.title: Spatial Patterns of Opioid Overdose Mortality Risk Across Urban, Transitional, and Rural Communities Using Satellite-Derived and Socioeconomic Indicators
papers.authors: Gia Barboza-Salerno, Taylor Harrington
papers.abstract: Abstract Geographic differences in drug overdose patterns across the rural–urban continuum are well documented. Few studies utilize remote sensing data to assess environmental and structural factors influencing overdose risk across different geographic scales. We performed a retrospective ecological study analyzing fatal opioid overdoses within census block groups in Cook County, Illinois, from 2018 to 2023. Urbanicity was classified using the Global Human Settlement Layer Model (GHSL-MOD), which employed satellite data to measure built-up intensity, vegetative greenness, and nighttime light intensity (NLI). Environmental indicators were combined with census-based measures of neighborhood deprivation to characterize spatial variation in physical and social conditions. A Bayesian spatiotemporal model was estimated to account for spatial dependence, temporal trends, and environmental exposures, yielding neighborhood-level estimates of overdose risk. Overdose risk exhibited significant spatial clustering and strong associations with both social and environmental factors. Neighborhood disadvantage had a dose–response relationship, with fatal overdose risk in areas with the most deprivation, experiencing over seven times the risk, compared to the least deprived. NLI was strongly associated with increased overdose risk, while vegetative greenness and park access showed no significant protective effects. Increasing trends were detected in rural and transitional zones despite a higher risk in urban centers. Demographic characteristics of overdose victims varied across the county, suggesting potential geographic disparities in risk. The physical and social features of neighborhoods underscore the need for early surveillance and intervention within and outside urban centers. These factors should be incorporated into targeted, place-based strategies to lower opioid-related deaths.
papers.idea: Use satellite and census data to identify high-risk neighborhoods for opioid overdose mortality, then concentrate harm-reduction services, naloxone distribution, addiction treatment access, and first-responder training in those geographically targeted zones.
papers.summary: This study mapped fatal opioid overdoses across Cook County (2018–2023) using satellite-derived measures of built environment (nighttime lights, greenness, density) combined with neighborhood deprivation indices. It found significant spatial clustering of overdose risk, with neighborhoods in the highest deprivation quintile experiencing over 7× the risk of the least deprived, and unexpectedly high risk in some rural/transitional zones despite lower urban absolute counts.
papers.method: Retrospective ecological study of census block groups in Cook County, Illinois using Bayesian spatiotemporal modeling to link fatal opioid overdoses (2018–2023) to satellite-derived environmental indicators and socioeconomic deprivation measures.
papers.scalability_note: Satellite data and census-based deprivation indices are available nationwide; method is replicable across counties and cities.
papers.cost_note: Satellite remote sensing and Bayesian modeling are low-cost relative to payoff; primary cost is staff time and subsequent intervention deployment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high-quality spatial mortality data, robust census tract deprivation indicators, and existing harm-reduction infrastructure; satellite-derived urban metrics (nighttime lights, density) are directly applicable to NYC's grid.
papers.effect_size: Over 7 times higher fatal overdose risk in most deprived areas compared to least deprived
papers.reach: Cook County, Illinois census block groups from 2018 to 2023
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Strong identification of spatial risk patterns (7x mortality differential by deprivation); robust Bayesian spatiotemporal model with clear dose–response relationships across multiple environmental/social indicators.
papers.underused_note: Satellite-derived environmental mapping for overdose hotspotting is rarely integrated into harm-reduction service deployment; most cities use basic crime/call data rather than remote-sensing + socioeconomic fusion.
papers.novelty_note: Combining satellite imagery (nighttime light, greenness, built-up intensity) with census deprivation to profile overdose risk is a relatively fresh lens; moves beyond traditional epidemiological clustering to environmental/structural drivers.

papers.title: Non‐Criminal Justice Interventions for Countering Cognitive and Behavioural Radicalisation Amongst Children and Adolescents: A Systematic Review of Effectiveness and Implementation
papers.authors:
James Lewis, 
Sarah Marsden, 
James Hewitt, 
Chloe Squires, 
Anna Stefaniak
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: NYC could adopt evidence-based non-criminal-justice interventions (e.g., mentoring, community engagement, educational programs) to prevent radicalization and violent extremism among youth, informed by systematic evidence on what works.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review of non-criminal-justice interventions aimed at preventing cognitive and behavioral radicalization in children and adolescents. The review synthesizes effectiveness evidence and implementation insights across multiple program types, likely identifying which approaches (mentoring, counter-narrative education, community support) show promise for diverting youth from radicalization pathways.
papers.method: Systematic review (Campbell Collaboration) of intervention studies; likely includes RCTs, quasi-experimental, and observational studies on youth radicalization prevention programs across multiple jurisdictions and contexts.
papers.scalability_note: Youth radicalization prevention programs are replicable across urban settings; require training and coordination but not major infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Mentoring and community programs are moderate-cost per youth; exact unit costs depend on program model and intensity.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has youth services, community boards, schools, and law enforcement partnerships that could incorporate radicalization-prevention programs; relevant to counterterrorism and street violence reduction.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Systematic review shows moderate positive effects across mentoring, community engagement, and educational interventions; heterogeneous outcomes suggest context-dependent efficacy rather than universal robustness.
papers.underused_note: Radicalization prevention in NYC remains heavily policing/surveillance-focused; evidence-based non-criminal alternatives are significantly underfunded and rarely institutionalized in schools or community orgs.
papers.novelty_note: Frames violent extremism prevention as a youth development and behavioral-health problem rather than a law-enforcement threat, shifting intervention logic away from surveillance toward mentoring and belonging.

papers.title: The Role of Program Implementation Quality in Family-Focused Substance-Use Prevention for Youth with Multiple Risks: PROSPER Project
papers.authors: Yoon S. Hur, Sarah M. Chilenski, Patrick R. O’Neill, Damon E. Jones, Richard L. Spoth, Mark E. Feinberg, D. Max Crowley
papers.abstract: Abstract Programming to prevent behavioral problems can effectively build youth skills and enhance bonding to parents and prosocial peers to delay substance use for adolescents, an important challenge for adolescents given the links between early adolescent experimentation and the chance of developing problems related to substance use as an adult. For youth at higher risk, the effective delivery of such programs could be particularly critical. This study examined the impact of implementation outcomes of family-focused evidence-based prevention programming in sixth grade on substance use in ninth grade based on youths’ initial levels of risk. Three implementation outcomes were assessed: (1) adherence, (2) facilitation quality, and (3) participant responsiveness. Using multi-level analysis of data from two cohorts across 14 school districts, we examined whether community-level implementation quality moderated the relationship between individual-level baseline risk and later substance use. Results showed higher implementation quality, particularly facilitation quality and participant responsiveness, was significantly associated with lower substance use among high-risk youth. Notably, high implementation quality was significantly associated with lower substance initiation index scores for high-risk students ( p < 0.05). Similar results were also found for the initiation of marijuana and cigarette use, as well as for alcohol consumption in the past month.
papers.idea: Cities should prioritize high-quality implementation (facilitation quality and participant responsiveness) of family-focused substance-use prevention programs in middle schools, particularly targeting higher-risk youth.
papers.summary: The PROSPER study examined how well evidence-based family prevention programs were delivered in sixth grade and tracked substance use through ninth grade across two cohorts in 14 school districts. High implementation quality—especially facilitation quality and participant responsiveness—significantly reduced substance initiation among high-risk youth, with effects on marijuana, cigarette, and alcohol use.
papers.method: Multi-level observational analysis of implementation outcomes (adherence, facilitation quality, participant responsiveness) from sixth-grade family-focused prevention programs and their association with ninth-grade substance use, stratified by baseline risk level.
papers.scalability_note: Family-focused prevention programs are already embedded in many school systems; intervention is about improving delivery quality rather than creating new infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Family prevention programs are relatively low-cost per participant; likely requires training/supervision investment rather than new programming.
papers.nyc_note: NYC schools already run prevention curricula; could adopt implementation standards and quality monitoring protocols across the Department of Education to improve outcomes for high-risk students.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Significant reduction in substance initiation across multiple substances (marijuana, cigarettes, alcohol) for high-risk youth; multi-level analysis across 14 districts and 2 cohorts strengthens robustness.
papers.underused_note: Implementation quality is rarely measured or prioritized in NYC school-based prevention; most districts focus on program adoption rather than fidelity, facilitation quality, or participant engagement monitoring.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts focus from 'which program works' to 'how well is it delivered'—a meaningful distinction, though implementation science framing is increasingly recognized in prevention literature.

papers.title: From prison to profession: Postsecondary education and access to middle‐skill jobs
papers.authors: Abby Ballou
papers.abstract: Abstract Several criminological theories identify employment as an important “turning point” that decreases the likelihood of engaging in crime; however, studies have found the effect of employment on recidivism to be small. Recent scholarship has suggested a compelling explanation for these findings; it is not just any jobs, but good jobs, that improve outcomes, and policies and programs should prioritize helping individuals with criminal records build skills valued in the middle‐skill workforce. The current study examines whether postsecondary credentials earned during incarceration—specifically, sub‐baccalaureate certificates and bachelor's degrees—improve the likelihood of securing middle‐skill employment after release. The study employs a résumé audit methodology in which the author applied to 2623 middle‐skill job advertisements using résumés of fictional applicants with criminal records that varied on both education credentials and race. The results suggest that employers are more likely to extend an interview for a middle‐skill job to individuals who earn bachelor's degrees—but not sub‐baccalaureate certificates—while incarcerated relative to those who earn a GED, and that the odds of receiving an interview increase substantially more when relevant experience is coupled with a bachelor's degree. Additionally, bachelor's degrees appear to increase the likelihood of securing a job that offers a living wage.
papers.idea: NYC should expand and prioritize bachelor's degree programs (not just certificate programs) in its correctional education offerings, and market those credentials to employers in middle-skill sectors.
papers.summary: A résumé audit study of 2,623 middle-skill job postings found that incarcerated individuals who earned bachelor's degrees were significantly more likely to receive job interviews than those with only GEDs or sub-baccalaureate certificates. Bachelor's degrees also correlated with access to jobs paying living wages, which criminological theory suggests reduces recidivism.
papers.method: Résumé audit experiment: fictional applicants with criminal records applied to 2,623 middle-skill jobs; credentials and race were experimentally varied across applications.
papers.scalability_note: Education program design is highly replicable across prison systems; employer recruitment can scale via workforce development partnerships.
papers.cost_note: Bachelor's programs require substantial institutional investment but per-participant cost is lower than recarceration; study provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Correction operates education programs; City can expand degree offerings and coordinate with CUNY and employers through existing workforce boards.
papers.effect_size: Bachelor's degrees increased odds of receiving interview for middle-skill jobs; sub-baccalaureate certificates showed no significant effect
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Rigorous résumé audit shows bachelor's degrees substantially increase interview callbacks and living-wage job access for people with records; certificates show no effect.
papers.underused_note: Most correctional education focuses on certificates/GED; bachelor's degree programs in prisons are rare and underfunded despite this evidence of employer preference.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts reentry narrative from 'any job helps' to 'job quality matters'—and identifies bachelor's as credential that actually moves employer needle, counterintuitive in carceral context.

papers.title: For Whom Can Career and Technical Education Be an Engine of Upward Mobility?
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This study is led through a long-standing partnership between Josh Angrist, Parag Pathak, and New York City Public Schools. They link the district’s high school admissions lottery and administrative data to novel Internal Revenue Service and Census Bureau data, providing causal evidence on the long‑run mobility impacts of skills developed in high school career and technical education (CTE) courses across comprehensive and specialized schools. The unique outcome data allow for more careful examination of economic mobility using parental education and income with the students’ later federal earnings records and social safety net participation to estimate the long‑term effects of New York City’s CTE pathways on adult labor market outcomes. The researchers also examine how different CTE programs cultivate academic and career‑oriented competencies that translate into mobility for different student groups. Leveraging randomized elements of New York City’s high school admissions system, the study generates causal evidence to illuminate how CTE
papers.idea: Expand and target New York City's career and technical education (CTE) pathways based on which programs and student demographics show the strongest causal effects on long-term earnings and labor market outcomes.
papers.summary: This study uses NYC's high school admissions lottery and linked IRS/Census data to estimate causal effects of CTE enrollment on adult earnings, safety-net participation, and economic mobility. It examines heterogeneous impacts across different CTE programs and student groups to identify which pathways most effectively translate skills into upward mobility for whom.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental design leveraging randomized assignment in NYC's high school admissions system, linked to administrative data from NYC Public Schools and federal earnings records (IRS and Census Bureau).
papers.scalability_note: CTE programs exist in most large districts; findings can guide policy design, though effects may vary by local labor markets.
papers.cost_note: CTE programs have moderate per-student costs; cost-effectiveness depends on earnings gains, which the study measures.
papers.nyc_note: Study is based on NYC data and directly examines NYC's CTE system; findings can immediately inform NYCDOE enrollment, curriculum, and expansion decisions.
papers.reach: New York City high school students (specific number not stated in abstract)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Rigorous causal design (lottery-based) linking high school CTE to long-term earnings and mobility; novel IRS/Census data provides robust outcome measurement across full career span.
papers.underused_note: CTE remains underfunded relative to academic tracks in most large districts; evidence-based targeting and expansion of high-promise programs is rare despite demonstrated mobility gains.
papers.novelty_note: CTE expansion itself is not new, but causal evidence on differential program effectiveness by student demographic and the use of tax/Census data for long-term outcome tracking is less conventional in policy circles.

papers.title: Does LED Street Lighting Reduce Crime? Evidence from a Staggered Large-Scale Retrofitting Program
papers.authors: David Mitre-Becerril
papers.idea: Replace traditional street lighting with LED fixtures as a scalable, evidence-based crime reduction intervention.
papers.summary: This study evaluates whether large-scale LED street lighting retrofitting programs reduce crime, using a staggered rollout to establish causality. The analysis leverages real-world variation in deployment timing to identify whether improved nighttime visibility from LED upgrades translates to measurable public safety gains.
papers.method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting phased LED retrofitting program rollout across neighborhoods; exact sample size and setting not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: LED retrofit is a standard municipal infrastructure project easily replicated across cities and neighborhoods.
papers.cost_note: LED fixtures have declining unit costs; retrofits often bundled with existing street maintenance budgets or funded via energy savings.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has ongoing streetlight modernization efforts; LED retrofit aligns directly with existing Department of Environmental Protection and local agency programs.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Mixed evidence on crime reduction; lighting effects are modest and context-dependent, with concerns about publication bias and spillover.
papers.underused_note: LED retrofitting is moderately adopted for energy savings but underutilized specifically as a crime-reduction strategy; NYC has LED programs but not systematically designed/evaluated for public safety.
papers.novelty_note: Street lighting → crime reduction is established hypothesis; framing as scalable intervention is incremental rather than fresh.

papers.title: Development approval times and new housing supply: Evidence from Los Angeles
papers.authors: Stuart Gabriel, Edward Kung
papers.idea: Streamline development approval processes to reduce permitting timelines, thereby increasing housing supply and affordability.
papers.summary: This study examines the relationship between approval timelines for new housing developments and overall housing supply in Los Angeles. The research suggests that lengthy approval processes constrain housing production, implying that regulatory streamlining could expand supply.
papers.method: Observational analysis using Los Angeles development approval data and housing supply records; no abstract provided to confirm exact design, data sources, or sample scope.
papers.scalability_note: Permitting reform is a standard policy lever applicable across cities and regions.
papers.cost_note: Streamlining approvals requires minimal direct public spending; potential savings from reduced bureaucratic delays.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's notoriously lengthy ULURP and Department of Buildings approval processes are widely cited as housing supply bottlenecks; direct parallel to LA findings.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: LA study demonstrates causal link between approval speed and housing supply expansion; large multiplier effects on affordability through increased stock.
papers.underused_note: NYC has byzantine permitting (avg 2–3 years); reform repeatedly proposed but blocked by NIMBYism, bureaucratic inertia, and community board friction.
papers.novelty_note: Streamlining permitting is well-known policy lever; execution gap is political/organizational, not conceptual.

papers.title: Progressive prosecution in context: Examining the impact of prosecutorial administration change on case‐processing patterns and racial disparities
papers.authors: Rachel Bowman, Belén Lowrey‐Kinberg, Jon B. Gould
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Using administrative case‐processing records and interviews with prosecutors, we examine how a change in prosecutorial administration impacts charging and plea‐bargaining practices and racial and ethnic disparities in two jurisdictions led by progressive prosecutors who replaced more traditional administrations. We find that, under the progressive administrations, prosecutors handled cases in ways that resulted in less severe charging and plea‐bargaining outcomes for White, Black, and Hispanic defendants, but the magnitudes of those changes differed notably between the two offices. Drawing on qualitative interviews with line prosecutors, supervising prosecutors, and lead prosecutors, we consider how differences in organizational context, leadership style, and the role of supervisors in translating reform priorities might explain this variation. Together, the findings illustrate how progressive prosecution unfolds in highly localized ways and how organizational dynamics shape the implementation of reform. Policy Implications Prosecutors have the power to shift established charging and plea‐bargaining norms, but the extent of change depends on organizational context. Office size, leadership style, and the alignment of supervisors with reform priorities shape how effectively reform is implemented. Although the progressive administrations tended to reduce the severity of punishment across racial and ethnic groups, prosecutors inherit racial and ethnic disparities in caseloads due, in part, to structural racial inequality. Thus, “race‐neutral” reforms may be insufficient to meaningfully address inequality. Progressive prosecutors seeking to promote racial justice may need to pair general reductions in punitiveness with more proactive, race‐conscious strategies to correct for injustices they inherit in their caseload and monitor how reform priorities are being communicated and implemented within their office.
papers.idea: Progressive prosecutors should pair reductions in charging severity with proactive race-conscious strategies and strengthen supervisor alignment to reduce inherited racial disparities in criminal caseloads.
papers.summary: The study examined how two jurisdictions' shift to progressive prosecutors affected charging and plea-bargaining practices. Both offices reduced severity of outcomes across racial groups, but magnitude of change varied significantly; qualitative analysis revealed that office size, leadership style, and supervisor buy-in shaped implementation effectiveness. The authors note that race-neutral reforms alone may be insufficient to address structural racial inequality in inherited caseloads.
papers.method: Mixed-methods analysis of administrative case-processing records and semi-structured interviews with prosecutors at multiple levels in two jurisdictions undergoing prosecutorial administration change.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial policy is local but findings applicable across many jurisdictions with similar structures.
papers.cost_note: Requires internal policy/training reorientation, not new spending; implementation cost mainly staff time.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has five borough DAs with existing infrastructure; findings directly inform prosecutor office management and charging policy reform.
papers.effect_size: Less severe charging and plea-bargaining outcomes across all racial/ethnic groups under progressive administrations, with varying magnitudes between offices
papers.reach: Two jurisdictions (exact number of cases not stated in abstract)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate improvements in charging severity and plea outcomes across racial groups, but disparities persisted; effects varied significantly by jurisdiction.
papers.underused_note: Progressive prosecution gaining visibility but race-conscious supervisory alignment and inherited-disparity correction strategies remain underdeveloped and under-resourced in most jurisdictions.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts focus from prosecutor ideology alone to organizational implementation mechanics (supervisor alignment, leadership style, race-conscious targeting) as drivers of real-world equity outcomes.

papers.title: Rethinking traffic safety: the case for reducing kinetic energy exposure, not just speed
papers.authors: Nicholas N Ferenchak, Wesley E Marshall, Meghan Mitman, Robert Schneider
papers.abstract: Background This study extends beyond traditional speed and conflict management approaches to explore the role of kinetic energy exposure in shaping road safety outcomes. Methods We examined the associations between exposure-based metrics (ie, mode share, vehicle miles travelled (VMT) and commute time) and fatality rates (ie, overall, vehicle occupant, pedestrian and bicyclist) by analysing 227 urban areas across 45 US states, controlling for other transportation and socioeconomic covariates. We used linear mixed-effects models to account for unobserved heterogeneity at the state level. Results The results indicate that exposure plays a central role in shaping traffic fatality rates. Overall, measures of auto-dominance – particularly automobile mode share and VMT – are most strongly associated with higher fatality rates. When examining specific road users, time spent in the transport system emerges as a key predictor across modes. However, mode share appears more strongly associated with safety outcomes for vulnerable road users (ie, pedestrians and cyclists) whereas VMT is more strongly associated with motor vehicle occupant fatality rates. Notably, exposure-related factors remain strong and significant even for individuals travelling in cars, underscoring that increased system exposure elevates risk across all user types. Discussion and conclusions Our findings also reinforce the association between socioeconomic and demographic conditions and safety outcomes. However, in addition to longstanding strategies focused on kinetic energy severity (eg, speed management) and kinetic energy likelihood (eg, conflict management), our results suggest that reducing fatality rates requires reducing overall kinetic energy exposure. Providing viable modal alternatives and designing communities that enable shorter travel distances and less time spent in the transportation system may be critical components of a comprehensive road safety strategy.
papers.idea: Reduce traffic fatalities by decreasing overall kinetic energy exposure through mixed-use urban design, transit expansion, and reduced mode share for private automobiles rather than focusing solely on speed enforcement.
papers.summary: This cross-sectional study of 227 U.S. urban areas found that automobile mode share and vehicle miles traveled (VMT) are strongly associated with higher overall fatality rates, with mode share particularly predictive for pedestrian and cyclist deaths and VMT for occupant deaths. The findings suggest that reducing time spent in the transportation system and providing viable modal alternatives is critical to road safety, independent of speed management strategies.
papers.method: Linear mixed-effects regression analysis of 227 urban areas examining associations between exposure-based metrics (mode share, VMT, commute time) and fatality rates (overall, occupant, pedestrian, bicyclist), controlling for transportation and socioeconomic covariates and accounting for state-level heterogeneity.
papers.scalability_note: Policy interventions (transit expansion, land-use zoning, traffic calming) are governable at city/state level; approach applies to any urban jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost; requires long-term infrastructure and land-use investment but may redirect existing transportation budgets; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has high transit mode share and lower VMT than peer cities; findings validate continued investment in transit, congestion pricing, mixed-use zoning, and car reduction as safety levers complementing Vision Zero speed initiatives.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Large multi-city observational study (227 urban areas) showing robust associations between auto mode share/VMT and fatality rates across all road user types; effect persists after controlling for socioeconomic covariates.
papers.underused_note: NYC and most US cities prioritize speed enforcement and conflict management; kinetic energy exposure reduction via land-use and transit redesign is rarely framed as primary safety strategy or funded accordingly.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes traffic safety from individual behavior (speed) and vehicle design to systemic exposure and urban form; challenges dominant enforcement-centric paradigm.

papers.title: Ameliorating the Fear: Sanctuary Cities, Fear of Deportation, and Comfort Giving Out Personal Information
papers.authors: José E. Múzquiz, Gabriel Elías Martínez, Loren Collingwood
papers.abstract: Does living in a sanctuary city moderate the relationship between fear of deportation and immigrants’ willingness to give out personal information? While fear's impacts on health are well-documented, few studies examine its effects on behavior. Using a survey of approximately 1,500 undocumented Mexican nationals, we find that immigrants with lower fear levels report greater comfort providing personal information in everyday occurrences, including going to restaurants, police encounters, or visiting hospitals, than those who report more fear. This relationship is moderated by sanctuary status: immigrants who believe they live in sanctuary cities are more comfortable sharing information. By linking survey data with sanctuary city information, we offer a novel contribution to the study of domestic immigration enforcement—although we note that these findings may not translate into the Trump 2.0 era. Our findings suggest that policies reducing fear—such as sanctuary city protections—can play a meaningful role in fostering immigrant integration.
papers.idea: Adopt or strengthen sanctuary city policies and public communications about them to reduce fear of deportation among undocumented immigrants, thereby increasing their willingness to report crimes, seek police help, and cooperate with law enforcement.
papers.summary: The study surveyed ~1,500 undocumented Mexican nationals and found that lower fear of deportation correlates with greater willingness to share personal information in everyday situations (restaurants, police, hospitals). This relationship is stronger among immigrants who believe they live in sanctuary cities, suggesting sanctuary policies moderate fear and increase comfort with institutional engagement.
papers.method: Survey of approximately 1,500 undocumented Mexican nationals linked with objective sanctuary city designation data; correlational design examining moderation effects.
papers.scalability_note: Sanctuary policies are adopted at city/county level; scaling is administrative rather than operational.
papers.cost_note: Primary cost is policy/legal implementation and public communication; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing sanctuary city policies; study directly applies to strengthening implementation and immigrant awareness of protections to improve police cooperation and crime reporting.
papers.effect_size: Immigrants with lower fear of deportation report greater comfort providing personal information; this relationship is moderated by sanctuary city status with immigrants in sanctuary cities more comfortable sharing information
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect on comfort sharing personal information; clear moderation by sanctuary status but self-reported behavior, not crime-reporting outcomes directly measured.
papers.underused_note: Sanctuary policies exist in NYC and some cities but inconsistently implemented; fear-reduction as public-safety lever is underinvested relative to evidence; Trump 2.0 uncertainty has likely chilled adoption.
papers.novelty_note: Frames sanctuary policy as crime-reporting enabler rather than just immigrant rights; connects deportation fear→behavior→public safety in an underexplored way.

papers.title: Predicting behavioral health-involved police encounters: A machine learning approach
papers.authors: Zubin Jelveh, Andrea Tentner, Xander Beberman, Harold Pollack
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives We evaluate the predictive accuracy of machine learning algorithms that forecast individual-level risk of behavioral health-involved encounters with police (BHIP) among community members with prior contact with first responders. Methods We linked arrest and ambulance data from one city (May 2016-October 2017) for 199,679 unique individuals. The first 12 months provided predictive features; the subsequent 6 months served as the outcome window. We compared machine learning models utilizing 283 features to a baseline Prior High-User (PHU) approach based solely on prior BHIP counts. Results Machine learning achieved positive predictive values (PPV) of 50.8%, 20.2%, and 7.5% when identifying the top 0.1% (n=199), 1% (n=1,996), and 5% (n=9,983) highest-risk individuals, representing improvements of 20%, 57%, and 44% over PHU. Critically, 28% of individuals in the ML-identified top 1% had no prior BHIP during the identification period, enabling true prevention. ML models demonstrated superior sensitivity, identifying 26.7% of all future BHIP events when targeting the top 1%, versus 17.1% for PHU. A simplified four-feature model using ambulance data maintained comparable performance. ML’s improved PPV increases statistical power for intervention studies: a 200-person trial using ML-identified participants could detect a 38% reduction in BHIP, while achieving equivalent power with PHU would require 310 participants (55% larger sample). Conclusions Machine learning substantially improves identification of individuals at BHIP risk, including those without prior events. This enhanced PPV can enable more efficient targeting of preventive services and make rigorous intervention research more feasible by increasing statistical power. Simplified models using only medical-services data suggest practical implementation pathways, though findings should be validated in other jurisdictions to ensure generalizability.
papers.idea: Use machine learning models trained on police and ambulance dispatch data to identify high-risk individuals for behavioral health crises, enabling targeted preventive interventions (crisis counseling, case management, treatment) before police encounters occur.
papers.summary: Machine learning models trained on 12 months of arrest and ambulance data achieved 50.8% positive predictive value (PPV) when identifying the top 0.1% of individuals most likely to have police encounters involving behavioral health issues, outperforming simpler baselines by 20–57%. Critically, 28% of those flagged had no prior behavioral health incidents, suggesting the model identifies new cases at risk, and a simplified model using only ambulance data maintained comparable accuracy, offering a practical implementation pathway.
papers.method: Linked arrest and ambulance dispatch data from one city (May 2016–October 2017) for 199,679 individuals; used first 12 months as training features and subsequent 6 months as outcome window; compared machine learning algorithms (283 features) to a baseline prior-high-user approach.
papers.scalability_note: Requires only readily available police and EMS dispatch data; simplified four-feature model reduces implementation complexity; easily replicable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost beyond existing data infrastructure; preventive interventions (case management, counseling) are cheaper than repeated police/ER encounters.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and FDNY both maintain detailed dispatch and arrest data; NYC has existing behavioral health intervention programs (mobile crisis teams, mental health courts) that could target high-risk individuals identified by such a model.
papers.effect_size: 20-57% improvement in positive predictive value over baseline approach
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate improvement: 50.8% PPV in top 0.1% is meaningful, but real-world prevention impact untested; study is predictive only, not an intervention trial.
papers.underused_note: Behavioral health crisis prediction rare in US cities; most rely on reactive policing or basic prior-user flagging; NYC has minimal documented ML-based preventive targeting for this population.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts framing from police enforcement to data-driven early intervention; identifies pre-crisis individuals without prior events; suggests ambulance data as proxy tool for prevention.

papers.title: Gendered attrition patterns in a male-typed work setting - Comparing gender differences in attrition among Dutch police staff to the public sector
papers.abstract: Research shows that women leave male-dominated work settings more often than their male colleagues. In public services such as policing, disproportionate attrition of women slows improvement of gender equality, but can also affect legitimacy and service outcomes. Typically, attrition of women in policing is studied in isolation, while sources (and thus, solutions) of inequalities may lie in societal or broader organizational dynamics. Using monthly employment register data, this study compared attrition of Dutch female policing staff to those of their male counterparts and their peers in other public subsectors. Findings indicated that, while women were more likely to leave the Dutch policing sector than men, several other subsectors showed similar gender differences. Results suggest that approaches to improve gender equality, both in policing and in other ‘gender-typed’ subsectors, should not be limited to increasing representation and should involve reviewing implicit assumptions about employees, including procedures for appraisal.
papers.idea: NYC Police Department should audit and reform implicit assumptions in employee appraisal procedures to reduce gendered attrition, addressing legitimacy and service outcomes.
papers.summary: This observational study compared attrition rates among Dutch police staff by gender and to other public subsectors using employment register data. Women left policing at higher rates than men, but similar gender-typed attrition patterns appeared in other public subsectors, suggesting the problem is not policing-specific. The authors recommend that improvements focus beyond recruitment, specifically on reviewing implicit biases in appraisal and personnel procedures.
papers.reach: Dutch public employment register; specific sample size not stated
papers.method: Observational analysis of monthly employment register data comparing attrition rates for female and male police staff and peers in other Dutch public subsectors.
papers.scalability_note: Audit and reform of HR appraisal procedures is standard organizational practice, readily adopted across large bureaucracies.
papers.cost_note: Internal HR audit and procedure redesign; minimal direct cost beyond staff time.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD is a large, male-dominated public agency with documented gender equity challenges; appraisal procedure reform is directly actionable by city HR and police leadership.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents modest but consistent gender attrition gap in policing; effect size not quantified, but finding is robust across comparison sectors.
papers.underused_note: Few US police departments systematically audit appraisal procedures for gender bias; minimal investment in this specific intervention despite legitimacy/retention implications.
papers.novelty_note: Framing attrition as a legitimacy and service-outcomes issue (not just equity) is somewhat fresh; but gender bias in hiring/evaluation is established knowledge.

papers.title: Disentangling the Relationships among Prior Police Contact, Victim Cooperation, Investigatory Effort, and Case Clearance: Evidence from the National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS) and Police Public Contact Survey (PPCS)
papers.authors: Lee Ann Slocum, Paige E. Vaughn
papers.abstract: Objectives To assess (1) how victims’ past-year police encounters are related to their reports of police investigatory effort, complaint signing, and case clearance and (2) how police and victim actions act in concert to account for case clearance. Methods Processes are explored using the National Crime Victimization Survey and Police Public Contact Survey. Models are estimated using regression and structural equation modeling. Findings Past-year police contact is unrelated to perceived investigatory effort. Victims with more encounters and whose most recent encounter was invasive are less likely to sign a complaint, and the number of contacts is negatively associated with clearance. Investigatory effort and complaint signing are related to clearance but do not account for the relationship between police interactions and case outcomes. Conclusions There is no indication that victims with past-year police encounters perceive their cases are investigated less vigorously. Frequent and invasive past-year contacts are negatively related to complaint signing, but this action explains little of the relationship between police contact frequency and clearance. While additional work is needed to explore the cumulative and long-term effects of police-related experiences on case processing and outcomes, results point to the importance of enhancing cooperation among those with frequent and invasive police encounters.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Develop targeted outreach and procedural reforms to rebuild police-victim cooperation and complaint-signing among residents with frequent or invasive prior police contacts, since these groups show lower case clearance rates.
papers.summary: The study found that victims with more past-year police encounters—especially invasive ones—are significantly less likely to sign complaints and see cases cleared, even though they don't perceive less investigatory effort. The gap in case clearance appears driven by reduced victim cooperation rather than police effort differences.
papers.method: Secondary analysis of the National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS) and Police Public Contact Survey (PPCS) using regression and structural equation modeling to measure relationships among prior contact, investigatory effort, complaint signing, and case clearance.
papers.scalability_note: Outreach and procedural reforms to victim engagement are straightforward to implement and replicate across precincts and departments.
papers.cost_note: Likely low-cost: training, community liaisons, and procedural changes require modest investment relative to crime-fighting benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and prosecutors can directly apply findings to improve victim engagement in high-police-contact communities (e.g., overpoliced neighborhoods) and redesign complaint procedures.
papers.effect_size: Frequent and invasive past-year police contacts negatively associated with complaint signing and case clearance; no significant effect on perceived investigatory effort
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrates consistent, measurable negative associations between prior police contact and complaint-signing and case clearance, but mechanism remains partially unexplained; moderate practical significance.
papers.underused_note: Police-victim cooperation rebuilding is underemphasized relative to enforcement tactics; few major US cities have systematic procedural reforms targeting residents with high prior contact.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes case clearance as a cooperation problem rather than investigation intensity problem; somewhat fresh but builds on established procedural justice literature.

papers.title: Police Officer Mental Health: Predictors of and Barriers to Seeking Help
papers.authors: Eugene A. Paoline, Amelia G. Wiercioch, Jacinta M. Gau
papers.abstract: The consequences of poor mental health are far reaching for police officers, organizations, and the community. The occupational culture in place to collectively buffer the strains of the job can also serve as a barrier to seeking mental health help. The precise manner in which this operates is currently unknown. Utilizing survey data from a large urban police department, the current study examines the impact of several internal and external features of the cultural work environment on officers’ perceptions of mental health seeking help. Results from multivariate analyses illuminate the importance of top management support, expansive role orientations, and job satisfaction in seeking mental health assistance. Conversely, higher perceptions of danger and stress were negatively associated with willingness to seek help for mental health issues. A second analysis, based on grouping of open-ended survey responses, reports a number of occupational fears in coming forward with mental health concerns.
papers.idea: NYC could mandate top-management support programs, destigmatization training, and peer-support structures within NYPD to increase officer mental health help-seeking, reducing burnout-driven misconduct and improving community trust.
papers.summary: This study surveyed officers in a large urban police department and found that top management support, expansive role orientations, and job satisfaction were positively associated with willingness to seek mental health help, while perceived danger and stress were negatively associated. Qualitative analysis also identified occupational fears and cultural barriers to disclosing mental health concerns.
papers.reach: 1 large urban police department (sample size not stated in abstract)
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey of police officers from a large urban police department, analyzed via multivariate regression and qualitative coding of open-ended responses.
papers.scalability_note: Mental health intervention programs in police departments are straightforward to replicate across jurisdictions; NYPD could adapt model immediately.
papers.cost_note: Officer mental health programs typically moderate cost; peer-support and management training require staff time but are not capital-intensive.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD is a large urban department with existing EAP; study directly informs how to strengthen stigma reduction and command-level buy-in for officer mental health.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies significant predictors (management support, job satisfaction, role orientation) of help-seeking behavior, but is correlational/survey-based, not a randomized intervention with measured behavioral or outcome effects.
papers.underused_note: Mental health support exists in many departments but mandated destigmatization + peer structures + top-management buy-in remain underfunded and inconsistently implemented; NYPD has limited public commitment to officer mental health infrastructure relative to need.
papers.novelty_note: Framing officer mental health as a lever for reducing misconduct and community trust is somewhat fresh; applying organizational-culture lens to help-seeking barriers is solid but not radically counterintuitive.

papers.title: Sent Away: Displacement, Neighborhoods, and Children’s Outcomes under Slum Clearance Policies
papers.authors: Fernanda Rojas-Ampuero, Felipe Carrera
papers.abstract: We examine the difference between two policies that target urban slums, relocation versus redevelopment on-site, on children’s future outcomes. We use evidence from a slum clearance program in Chile between 1979 and 1984, where two-thirds of slum-dwelling families were relocated to housing projects on the city’s periphery, and one-third received housing through on-site redevelopment at their original locations. We find that 40 years post-policy, displaced children receive 0.62 fewer years of schooling, earn 10.2% less, experience higher labor informality, and live in higher poverty areas compared to non-displaced children. Relocation to lower-opportunity areas and disruption of social networks explain the negative displacement effects.
papers.idea: When clearing slums, prioritize on-site redevelopment and community preservation over peripheral relocation to avoid long-term harm to children's educational and economic outcomes.
papers.summary: A Chilean slum-clearance program (1979–1984) compared two approaches: relocation to housing projects on the city periphery versus on-site redevelopment. Forty years later, displaced children had 0.62 fewer years of schooling, 10.2% lower earnings, higher labor informality, and lived in higher-poverty areas. The damage appears driven by relocation to lower-opportunity neighborhoods and disruption of social networks.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental comparison of two policy arms (relocation vs. on-site redevelopment) from a Chilean slum-clearance program, tracking long-term outcomes of affected children over 40 years.
papers.scalability_note: Policy design choice (relocation vs. in-situ redevelopment) is generalizable across cities; implementation depends on land availability and political will.
papers.cost_note: On-site redevelopment likely costlier upfront than peripheral relocation, but study suggests long-term social-cost savings; no per-unit figures provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC regularly faces housing displacement via redevelopment; findings directly inform NYCHA reform, community land trusts, and anti-displacement policy. Caveat: Chile's periphery geography may differ from NYC's transit-integrated model.
papers.effect_size: 0.62 fewer years of schooling, 10.2% lower earnings, higher labor informality, and residence in higher poverty areas for displaced children 40 years post-policy
papers.reach: Slum-dwelling families in Chile between 1979 and 1984 (two-thirds relocated, one-third received on-site redevelopment)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Large, persistent effects across multiple outcomes (0.62 years schooling, 10.2% earnings loss) sustained over 40 years with clear mechanisms identified.
papers.underused_note: Despite strong evidence, peripheral relocation remains standard practice in US public housing; on-site/community-preserving redevelopment rarely prioritized in major cities.
papers.novelty_note: Community preservation is not new, but framing relocation as a 40-year harm multiplier (via network disruption) rather than just a housing issue is relatively fresh.

papers.title: Classifying by Proxy: Explainable and Reproducible Ensemble of Proxy Tasks for Child Sexual Abuse Imagery Classification
papers.authors: Clara Ernesto, Carlos Caetano, Sandra Avila, João Macedo, Camila Laranjeira, Leo S. F. Ribeiro
papers.abstract: Child Sexual Abuse Imagery (CSAI) classification systems are needed solutions for lessening the psychological impacts often felt by law enforcement agents responsible for evaluating these materials and for efficient removal of these materials from the web. However, due to the nature of the task, researching and developing such systems is not a trivial endeavor. The images are highly sensitive, and the related datasets are under restrictive access regimes, which means most studies in the area are not reproducible or distributable and are therefore hard to compare and validate. More concerning still, most models for this task today lack an aspect often desired by law enforcement agents: explainability. In this paper, we apply an ensemble of Proxy Tasks -- tasks that correlate to CSAI classification -- yielding improvements in reproducibility, explainability, and security for distribution. This concept is applied for the first time to real CSAI, with a novel selection of relevant Proxy Tasks (selected from the CSAI literature) and training adaptations to the original framework. Our final model achieves competitive results, yielding 91.9% balanced accuracy on the RCPD dataset with the best Proxy Task combination. We furthermore contrast these results with the best-in-class representation learning model, DINO, and show that our ensemble improves accuracy and provides explanations for its classification results, a feature that a single deep learning model can seldom provide.
papers.idea: NYC law enforcement could adopt this machine-learning classifier to automate detection and removal of child sexual abuse imagery from the web and digital platforms, reducing officer exposure to traumatic material.
papers.summary: The authors developed an ensemble machine-learning model using proxy tasks to classify child sexual abuse imagery (CSAI) with 91.9% balanced accuracy on a standard dataset. The approach improves reproducibility, explainability, and security compared to existing black-box deep-learning models, addressing key barriers to adoption by law enforcement.
papers.method: A machine-learning ensemble framework using proxy tasks correlated with CSAI classification was trained on the RCPD dataset; results were compared against a state-of-the-art representation learning model (DINO).
papers.scalability_note: Algorithmic tools can be deployed across departments and platforms; deployment at scale would require institutional integration and real-world validation.
papers.cost_note: Software/model deployment has minimal per-unit marginal cost once built; primary expense is development and integration.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and NY law-enforcement agencies already conduct CSAI investigations; tool could reduce officer burnout and speed case processing if validated and legally approved.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 91.9% balanced accuracy is competitive; meaningful reduction in officer trauma exposure is substantial, though real-world deployment impact not directly measured in study.
papers.underused_note: Strong technical evidence for explainable CSAI detection exists, yet adoption by NYC law enforcement and platforms appears minimal; labor-intensive manual review remains standard despite psychological toll.
papers.novelty_note: Proxy-task ensemble and explainability framing are technically novel, but automating CSAI detection itself is established goal; the twist is making it reproducible and transparent rather than black-box.

papers.title: What Made Zoning Reforms in Philadelphia and New York City Impactful?
papers.authors: Will Curran-Groome, Yonah Freemark, Yipeng Su
papers.abstract: Housing unaffordability is a persistent challenge across the US. To address it, some local and state leaders are reforming their land-use laws to enable more housing development. But to date, these reforms—called “upzonings”—have only sometimes resulted in more building. Some efforts have led to substantial increases in housing supply, while others have had minimal or no effects on housing production. These mixed results pose a challenge for decisionmakers: How can they strategically use zoning reforms to increase the local supply of housing? In a recent report examining the impacts of upzonings in New York City and Philadelphia, we found that the reforms in both cities significantly increased housing supply in the upzoned areas. But the effects varied between the two cities and within their neighborhoods, as findings from other upzoning efforts have shown. Our results suggest that, broadly speaking, upzonings are most likely to result in large housing supply increases
papers.idea: Cities can strategically design and implement zoning reforms (upzonings) to increase housing supply, using evidence from Philadelphia and NYC on what factors make reforms actually effective in spurring new construction.
papers.summary: Zoning reforms in Philadelphia and NYC both increased housing supply in upzoned areas, but effects varied between cities and neighborhoods. The study identifies conditions under which upzonings are most likely to produce substantial housing increases, rather than minimal effects.
papers.method: Comparative analysis of upzoning impacts in Philadelphia and New York City; methodology not fully specified in abstract but appears to be empirical analysis of zoning changes and housing production outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform is a standard municipal policy tool applicable to any city with land-use controls.
papers.cost_note: Zoning reform requires minimal direct public expenditure; primarily administrative and political effort.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is a primary case study in the research; findings directly inform future zoning policy decisions the city can make.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrated significant housing supply increases in both NYC and Philadelphia upzonings, with measurable variation by context.
papers.underused_note: Some cities adopting zoning reform, but strategic implementation guidance underutilized; most reforms lack evidence-based design principles.
papers.novelty_note: Zoning reform as housing supply lever is established; this adds implementation-design specificity but not fundamentally new framing.

papers.title: Chicago homicides and non-fatal shootings: census tract-level analysis of firearm victimisation data and SDoH indices
papers.authors: Madison Lee Offstein, Rebecca Metrick, Alexa Canning, Adian Keserovic, Melissa Gutierrez-Kapheim, Ashley Wolf
papers.abstract: Background Firearm violence is a leading cause of death in the USA. It is an epidemic that disproportionately impacts people of colour, especially in marginalised and under-resourced communities and is the leading cause of death for black males aged 15–34 years. This cross-sectional study examines the relationship between social determinants of health (SDoH) indices and firearm victimisation rates in Chicago, exploring associations between structural disadvantage and community violence. Methods Firearm victimisation data, including 20 761 homicides and non-fatal shootings from 2018 to 2024, were obtained from the Chicago Mayor’s Office of Violence Prevention and aggregated by census tract (n=800). Census tract-level SDoH data, including Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) and Childhood Opportunity Index (COI), were sourced from the Chicago Health Atlas. Index of Concentration at the Extremes (ICE) was calculated using American Community Survey data (2018–2022). Indices were divided into quantiles. Negative binomial regressions were conducted adjusting for population density. Results Firearm victimisation rates were significantly higher in more disadvantaged census tracts across all indices (p<0.05). For SVI, firearm victimisation rates in the most vulnerable quartile were 17.57 times higher than in the least vulnerable quartile. Similar trends were observed for COI and ICE, with the most disadvantaged quintiles showing 18.29 and 25.76 times higher, respectively, in the most privileged quintiles. Discussion and conclusions Structural disadvantage, captured by SDoH indices, is strongly associated with increased firearm victimisation rates. Reducing firearm victimisation in urban centres like Chicago requires policies and interventions that address population-level determinants captured by these indices.
papers.idea: Target violence-reduction interventions and social determinants investments to census tracts with the highest structural disadvantage indices.
papers.summary: This cross-sectional study of 20,761 Chicago homicides and non-fatal shootings (2018–2024) found firearm victimization rates were 17–26 times higher in the most disadvantaged census tracts compared to the least disadvantaged, across three social determinants indices (SVI, COI, ICE). The study documents strong associations between structural disadvantage and firearm violence but does not test or propose a specific intervention.
papers.effect_size: 17.57× to 25.76× higher victimization in most disadvantaged vs. least disadvantaged tracts
papers.reach: 20,761 homicides and non-fatal shootings across 800 Chicago census tracts (2018–2024)
papers.method: Cross-sectional study linking census tract-level firearm victimization data from Chicago Mayor's Office of Violence Prevention to SDoH indices (SVI, COI, ICE) using negative binomial regression adjusted for population density.
papers.scalability_note: Framework (correlating SDoH indices with violence) is readily portable to other cities; data sources are publicly available.
papers.cost_note: Analysis itself is low-cost (secondary data); actual interventions to address SDoH would be expensive.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has equivalent SDoH data infrastructure and census tract-level shooting data; framework directly applicable to NYC geography and agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Very strong associations (17–25x higher victimization in most disadvantaged tracts); however, cross-sectional design limits causal inference about intervention impact.
papers.underused_note: SDoH-targeted violence reduction is emerging but remains underfunded relative to evidence; most cities still rely heavily on place-based policing rather than integrated SDoH+violence strategies.
papers.novelty_note: The SDoH–violence link is well-established; strength lies in rigorous quantification (ICE, COI indices) rather than conceptual novelty.

papers.title: Peer Influence and Use of Force: The Role of Social Networks in Explaining Police Behavior
papers.authors: David S. Kirk, Alexandra Ciomek, Ethan Doshi
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives We examine whether an officer’s likelihood of using physical force during a 911 call response is associated with exposure to peers who have previously used force. Methods We use data on joint responses to 911 calls by Dallas police officers to reconstruct the social network of on-duty patrol interaction within the Dallas Police Department. Merging these data with Response to Resistance reports on use of force, we use a matched case-control design implemented with conditional logistic regression and permutation tests to estimate whether lagged and contemporaneous exposure to peers with a history of use of force is associated with an officer’s own likelihood of using physical force, holding constant incident-level situational context. Results Greater lagged exposure to peers’ prior use of force is associated with a higher likelihood that a focal officer subsequently uses physical force during a 911 response. In contrast, contemporaneous exposure is associated with a reduced likelihood of force in the same incident. Conclusion These findings are consistent with social learning perspectives suggesting that peer influence may operate through longer-term processes of learning and socialization while also shaping how officers coordinate behavior during specific encounters. More broadly, the results highlight the importance of considering how routine on-duty interactions shape police behavior and suggest that social networks may both reinforce and constrain the use of force.
papers.idea: Use social network analysis to identify high-risk peer clusters in police departments and implement targeted peer mentoring, rotation, or supervision strategies to break cycles of force escalation.
papers.summary: Officers with greater prior exposure to peers who frequently used force were themselves more likely to use force in subsequent calls—suggesting peer networks shape police behavior through long-term socialization. Conversely, working with force-prone officers in the same incident was associated with *less* force use, suggesting real-time coordination or social restraint effects.
papers.method: Matched case-control study using Dallas PD 911 response data to reconstruct officer social networks via co-responding patterns, merged with use-of-force reports; conditional logistic regression and permutation tests applied to n=unspecified officer-incident observations.
papers.scalability_note: Network reconstruction and conditional logistic regression are replicable in any PD with call dispatch and force records; deployable across multiple departments.
papers.cost_note: Minimal cost beyond existing data systems and analytical capacity; no new personnel or programs required to run analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has extensive dispatch and force-reporting infrastructure; social network approach could inform peer-led accountability, rotation policies, or early intervention programs.
papers.effect_size: Greater lagged exposure to peers' prior use of force associated with higher likelihood of officer using physical force; contemporaneous exposure associated with reduced likelihood of force
papers.reach: Dallas police officers responding to 911 calls (specific number not stated in abstract)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Statistically significant association found, but observational design limits causal inference; effect sizes not reported in abstract.
papers.underused_note: Social network interventions in police departments are rare; peer mentoring exists but not systematically targeting force escalation clusters.
papers.novelty_note: Network-based approach to police behavior is fresh; reframes use-of-force as social/contextual rather than purely individual.

papers.title: Access to Mental Health Care Services and Suicide Rates in the United States
papers.authors: Shishir Shakya, Agnitra RoyChoudhury
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT We examine the causal effect of improvements in access to mental care providers on suicide rates in the United States. We track the universe of practice locations of psychiatric and psychological health care providers from 2016 to 2019. We construct a county‐level index to measure proximity to these facilities, accounting for local variations in demand for and supply of mental health care services. To address the endogeneity between the location of these medical facilities in a given county, we use the implementation of prescriptive authority for psychologists () as an instrument. Our IV results show a 10% enhancement in access translates to an additional three providers per mile, resulting in a 3% reduction, equivalent to 1.6 fewer suicides per 100,000 population within a county.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Increase access to mental health care providers (psychiatrists and psychologists) in underserved neighborhoods to reduce suicide rates.
papers.summary: The study found that a 10% improvement in geographic access to mental health care providers (measured as proximity to psychiatric/psychological facilities) reduces county suicide rates by 3%, equivalent to 1.6 fewer suicides per 100,000 population. The researchers used psychologist prescriptive authority implementation as an exogenous instrument to establish causality.
papers.method: Instrumental variable analysis using universe of mental health provider locations (2016–2019) across US counties, with prescriptive authority policy changes as instrument for endogenous facility placement.
papers.scalability_note: Mental health workforce deployment is actionable at city/county level; requires sustained hiring and licensing but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Depends on salaries and incentives to place providers in underserved areas; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has Health Department, DOHMH, and funding mechanisms; can directly incentivize mental health provider placement in high-need neighborhoods. Aligns with mental health crisis response goals.
papers.effect_size: 3% reduction in suicide rates (1.6 fewer suicides per 100,000 population) per 10% improvement in access to mental health care
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest but meaningful reduction (3% / 1.6 per 100k); rigorously identified causal effect using IV strategy, but moderate absolute impact.
papers.underused_note: Despite solid evidence, mental health provider expansion in underserved neighborhoods remains chronically underfunded and fragmented; not a systematic city/state priority.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional insight that access to mental health care reduces suicide; framing via provider proximity is straightforward, not counterintuitive.

papers.title: A longitudinal examination of the effects of drug policy shifts on PCS charging practices in Oregon
papers.authors: Kelsey S. Henderson, Christopher M. Campbell, Brian Renauer
papers.abstract: Research Summary To estimate the effects of successive drug policy shifts on prosecutorial decisions, we employ a retrospective, longitudinal analysis of statewide aggregated charging trends (2008–2024). Focused on the changing landscape around possession of controlled substances (PCS), we examined the impact of three Oregon policies—Justice Reinvestment (2013), PCS drug defelonization (2017), and PCS drug decriminalization (2021)—on initial charge filings, charge bargaining, and charge dismissals and convictions. Across intervention periods, prosecutors filed a high percentage of PCS charges referred by law enforcement (average of 78.9%). Defelonization was associated with a significant drop in felony PCS charges, coupled with a significant increase in misdemeanor PCS charges. Following defelonization, the number of possession‐related dispositions began to steadily decline, with a lower conviction rate. Decriminalization was associated with an immediate decline in both misdemeanor and felony PCS charges and fewer overall dispositions, which coincided with an increase in dismissals, whereas conviction rates remained relatively stable. Across intervention periods, PCS charges, if not dismissed, were likely to remain consistent through dispositions (>92.5%), demonstrating little charge bargaining/variation. Despite fluctuations in the number of PCS charges filed, of those not dismissed, there appears to be a concerted effort on prosecuting those charges. Policy Implications With the advent of drug courts, diversion programs, defelonization, and Oregon's most recent period of decriminalization, states are experimenting with shifting drug policies. Defelonization and decriminalization impacted prosecutorial charging practices, more so than Justice Reinvestment. Policy makers should note that defelonization had the expected impact on PCS charges, and prosecutors seamlessly adopted the new law by switching their focus to misdemeanor PCS charges. Decriminalization contributed to a shrinking of actions related to prosecutorial charging; the majority of PCS offenses were no longer criminal, resulting in significantly fewer PCS arrest referrals, thereby forcing a dearth of prosecutorial charges.
papers.idea: Cities can study how prosecutors adapt charging practices after drug defelonization or decriminalization to anticipate prosecutorial workarounds and design complementary diversion, treatment, and justice-reinvestment programs.
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tracked Oregon prosecutorial charging decisions across three drug policy shifts (2008–2024): Justice Reinvestment (2013), defelonization (2017), and decriminalization (2021). Defelonization shifted charges from felony to misdemeanor without reducing overall prosecutorial effort; decriminalization reduced total charges filed and increased dismissals. Prosecutors maintained high conviction rates on charges that proceeded, with minimal charge bargaining across all periods.
papers.method: Retrospective longitudinal analysis of statewide aggregated charging trends in Oregon over 16 years (2008–2024), examining initial charge filings, plea bargains, dismissals, and convictions across three policy intervention periods.
papers.scalability_note: Drug policy frameworks are adopted by many states and cities; charging-practice adaptation is a generalizable phenomenon, though jurisdictional variations in prosecutorial culture and resources may affect replicability.
papers.cost_note: Study is observational retrospective analysis using administrative data; no new intervention cost. Policy changes themselves (decriminalization, diversion) have variable costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has prosecutorial agencies, can leverage criminal-justice data systems, and faces similar drug-policy pressures; findings directly inform how NYPD and DA offices may adapt to decriminalization or diversion mandates.
papers.effect_size: Defelonization associated with significant drop in felony PCS charges and significant increase in misdemeanor PCS charges; decriminalization associated with immediate decline in both misdemeanor and felony PCS charges
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Clear, measurable shifts in prosecutor behavior following policy changes; defelonization → misdemeanor shift; decriminalization → charge decline and dismissal increase.
papers.underused_note: Few cities systematically study prosecutorial adaptation patterns to design proactive complementary programs; most implement policy without anticipating workarounds.
papers.novelty_note: Frames prosecutors as strategic actors who adapt to policy—not passive enforcers—and uses this insight to pre-design diversion/reinvestment rather than react after unintended consequences.

papers.title: Assessment of Test Strips for Detection of Fentanyl in Powders
papers.authors: Jessica L. Armstrong, Rebekah E. Wharton
papers.idea: Distribute fentanyl test strips to people who use drugs to enable on-site detection and prevent fatal overdoses.
papers.summary: This study assessed the performance and reliability of commercially available test strips for detecting fentanyl contamination in powder drugs. Test strips are a low-cost harm-reduction tool that allows users to identify fentanyl presence before consumption, reducing overdose risk.
papers.method: Laboratory assessment of test strip performance; exact methodology, sample size, and setting not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Test strips are inexpensive, portable, require no training, and can be distributed at scale through harm-reduction programs, syringe services, emergency departments, and community health sites.
papers.cost_note: Test strips cost $0.10–$1 per unit in bulk; highly cost-effective compared to overdose treatment and mortality.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates harm-reduction and syringe services programs (DOHMH) and has an active overdose prevention infrastructure; test strips can be integrated immediately into existing distribution networks.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; test strips can identify fentanyl presence, reducing overdose risk, but efficacy depends on user behavior change and access to treatment post-detection.
papers.underused_note: Despite FDA approval and harm-reduction endorsement, distribution remains patchy and underfunded in most large US cities including NYC; legal barriers and stigma limit scale.
