papers.novelty_note: Test-strip distribution is a recognized harm-reduction tactic; framing as overdose prevention is conventional within public health, not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Closing the Doors : Sexual Harassment and the Survival Rates of Women-owned Firms
papers.abstract: This paper examines sexual harassment and economic outcomes in entrepreneurship. Using a unique panel dataset of 790 women-owned firms in Ethiopia tracked over five years, the paper relates sexual harassment experienced by business owners to firm performance. First, it documents the prevalence of sexual harassment among women business owners. Over 16 percent of the women in the sample reported having been harassed in the prior 12 months, including incidences of sexual proposals and inappropriate physical contact. The paper shows that sexual harassment varies by business sector, as well as individual and firm characteristics. The findings suggest that experiencing sexual harassment is negatively associated with the economic returns of women business owners over time. Women entrepreneurs who report having experienced sexual harassment are 37 percent more likely to close their businesses five years later, despite initially earning higher business profits. Firm closure is associated with lower subsequent earnings, suggesting that exit is not driven by better employment opportunities. The findings suggest that gender-based safety constraints may distort firm dynamics and women’s economic achievement.
papers.idea: NYC could establish a workplace sexual harassment reporting and support program for women business owners, with enforcement mechanisms and counseling/legal aid, to reduce business closure and improve economic outcomes.
papers.summary: The study tracked 790 women-owned firms in Ethiopia over five years and found that 16% of women business owners experienced sexual harassment. Women entrepreneurs who experienced harassment were 37% more likely to close their businesses within five years, despite initially earning higher profits, suggesting that safety constraints rather than economic opportunity drive exit.
papers.effect_size: 37% higher likelihood of firm closure within 5 years
papers.reach: 790 women-owned firms in Ethiopia, tracked over 5 years
papers.method: Panel study following 790 women-owned firms in Ethiopia over five years, measuring sexual harassment prevalence and correlating it with firm survival and economic outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Program design is scalable (reporting systems, counseling, enforcement), but requires institutional buy-in and cultural adaptation; direct applicability to informal-economy settings varies by context.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost; requires dedicated staff for reporting/investigation, legal support, counseling—likely several hundred thousand dollars annually for a city program, but lower per-beneficiary than intensive interventions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing agencies (EEOC liaison, Small Business Services, NYPD bias response) and legal frameworks (human rights law); could coordinate a new women entrepreneur safety/support pilot.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 37% increased closure risk is substantial and robust across 5-year panel; clear causal mechanism documented.
papers.underused_note: No comparable NYC program for women business owners exists; sexual harassment support in entrepreneurship ecosystem is extremely limited despite high economic impact.
papers.novelty_note: Links sexual harassment directly to firm survival/economic outcomes rather than treating it as HR/cultural issue alone; reframes as economic-security problem.

papers.title: “Eyes” on the Street: Using Computer Vision to Get the Gist of How Environmental Design Shapes Crime Across Neighborhoods
papers.authors: Riley Tucker, Alfred F. Chao, Nakwon Rim, Elizabeth Gaillard, Gaby Akcelik, Marc G. Berman
papers.abstract: Abstract Purpose Assess the crime prevention through environmental design (CPTED) framework as a neighborhood theory and propose a new computational approach for measuring place visuals. Inspired by lessons from cognitive psychology, this approach augments object-based measures of environments (e.g., how many trees are present) with measures of visual ‘gists’ capturing broad image assessments. Methods 4,800 respondents were surveyed to provide human ratings of five CPTED-inspired gist metrics (preference, complexity, memorability, transparency, and enclosure) for 8,249 Chicago Google Street View images. Gist metrics were evaluated and interpreted using monte carlo-based reliability simulations and hierarchical linear models. Using residual neural networks, we trained a series of computer vision models that could predict human-rated gist scores on new images. After validating out-of-sample performance, these models were applied to estimate CPTED gist scores on a larger set of 187,048 Chicago street-view images. Multi-level variance decomposition analysis was used to probe the nested geographic structure of gist metrics. XGBoost were used to evaluate whether gist features were correlated with crime and prosociality (as measured via voting participation rates). Results Human-rated gist assessments were highly reliable across participants and were correlated with both the object composition of images and demographic features of the pictured neighborhood. AI-assigned gist labels correlated highly with human-ratings, suggesting very strong out-of-sample accuracy for all gist models. Variance decomposition results suggested CPTED gists substantially vary across census tracts even when accounting for micro-spatial visual differences. XGBoost results suggested violent crime is best predicted using object-based measures, non-violent crime is best predicted using gist measures, and voting participation is best explained using both sets of image features. Conclusions Aggregate neighborhood visual features are important for understanding why some places experience more crime. Future research should seek to understand how broad visual gists and the presence of specific objects interact to shape behavior and decision-making among guardians, offenders, and targets.
papers.idea: Use computer vision to measure visual environmental features (preference, complexity, transparency, enclosure) that correlate with non-violent crime, then prioritize streetscape interventions in high-risk neighborhoods based on these gist metrics.
papers.summary: The study trained AI models to predict human perception of neighborhood visual qualities from street-view images and found that these 'gist' measures (broad visual assessments) correlate with non-violent crime rates, while object-based measures better predict violent crime. The research suggests that aggregate visual features of streets are measurable at scale and meaningfully associated with crime variation across Chicago neighborhoods.
papers.method: Survey of 4,800 respondents rating 8,249 Chicago Google Street View images on five CPTED-inspired visual metrics; residual neural networks trained to predict gist scores; models validated and applied to 187,048 images; XGBoost used to correlate features with violent/non-violent crime and voting participation.
papers.scalability_note: Computer vision models can be applied to any city's Street View imagery; methodology is transferable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost once models trained; uses existing Street View data and open-source ML tools; bulk of cost is one-time model development.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has comprehensive Google Street View coverage; findings are from a comparable large U.S. city; transit-heavy density differs from Chicago but method directly applicable to NYC precinct/neighborhood prioritization.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate predictive correlation for non-violent crime; findings are associational, not causal; requires validation on intervention outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Computer vision + gist metrics for crime prevention is novel methodologically; traditional CPTED is underdeployed; NYC has minimal systematic streetscape intervention based on visual design science.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts CPTED from checklist (trees, lighting) to AI-measured perceptual psychology (gist, preference, complexity); bridges cognitive science and place-based crime prevention.

papers.title: Why Do Victims Experience Repeat Victimization? Delinquent Peers, Age-Graded Dynamics, and Gender-Specific Pathways
papers.authors: Myunghee You
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives The peer context is crucial in victimization research, yet its role in repeat victimization remains understudied. This study examines the mediating pathways through which youth who are victimized increase delinquent peer affiliations, thereby elevating the risk of victimization later in life. Further, this study explores how this mediating mechanism changes over the life course as well as how the pathway varies by gender. Methods The analysis uses six waves of data from a longitudinal survey of South Korean youth aged 13–18. A Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) examines within-person changes over time to identify mediating mechanisms. In addition, to assess the gender-specific pathway of repeat victimization, a multi-group RI-CLPM is applied. Results Findings reveal that adolescents who are victimized increase delinquent peer affiliations, which in turn increase the risk of repeat victimization in later life. The mediating pathway operates during adolescence, but not at the beginning of emerging adulthood (age 18). In addition, for girls, this mechanism is more prominent earlier in development, while for boys, it is more evident later during adolescence. Conclusions These findings highlight the need for programs aimed at improving peer relationships after victimization, as well as targeted interventions, especially in the early stages of adolescence for girls and later stages for boys. The current study takes an important step toward a developmental and life-course perspective on victimization. Future research should continue to explore how peer contexts influence the cycle of victimization over time.
papers.idea: Develop targeted peer-mentorship and social-connection interventions for victimized youth, timed by developmental stage (early adolescence for girls, mid-to-late adolescence for boys), to interrupt the cycle of repeat victimization.
papers.summary: The study found that adolescents who experience victimization tend to increase their affiliation with delinquent peers, which in turn elevates their risk of being victimized again later. This mediating mechanism operates during adolescence but weakens by age 18, and differs by gender—being more prominent early for girls and later for boys.
papers.method: Six-wave longitudinal survey of South Korean youth aged 13–18, analyzed using Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Models (RI-CLPM) to identify within-person changes over time and test gender-specific pathways.
papers.scalability_note: Peer-mentorship and social-support programs are well-established models easily adapted across school and youth-service systems.
papers.cost_note: School- or community-based peer programs are relatively inexpensive to deliver at scale; no per-unit cost provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive youth services, schools, and juvenile-justice diversion programs (e.g., CCRB, DOE partnerships) where such interventions could be embedded; cultural/contextual differences from South Korea merit adaptation.
papers.effect_size: Victimization increases delinquent peer affiliations, which in turn increases risk of repeat victimization; effect operates during adolescence but not at age 18
papers.reach: ~South Korean youth aged 13-18 across six waves of longitudinal data
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect; rigorous longitudinal design with RI-CLPM demonstrates clear mediating pathway (victimization → delinquent peers → repeat victimization), but effect sizes not quantified in abstract and limited to South Korean sample.
papers.underused_note: Peer-mentorship interventions exist but are rarely timed by developmental stage or explicitly designed to interrupt repeat-victimization cycles; gender-differentiated peer support for victimized youth is sparse in US city programs.
papers.novelty_note: Fresh angle: treats delinquent peer affiliation as mediator of repeat victimization (not just outcome), with age-graded and gender-specific timing; counters one-size-fits-all intervention logic.

papers.title: The impact of prosecutors’ office caseloads on case processing outcomes
papers.authors: R. R. Dunlea, Don Stemen
papers.abstract: Research Summary Contemporary criminal justice discourse frequently highlights rising caseloads as a crisis for prosecutors across the United States. Yet, empirical assessments of how caseloads impact prosecutorial decision making are scarce. This study exploits data on office caseloads and cases disposed between 2021 and 2024 in 19 prosecutors’ offices across Colorado to investigate the impact of office caseload pressure on plea and charge reduction rates. We use a two‐level modeling strategy in which weekly activity is nested within prosecutors’ offices, and caseload pressure is measured using weekly open case counts. While nearly as much variation in outcomes occurs across prosecutors’ offices as across weeks, we find that prosecutors’ offices resolve fewer cases via guilty plea in weeks when caseload pressure is higher. Effects are slightly stronger on dispositions for misdemeanor offenses than felonies. Little relationship between caseload pressure and charge reductions is observed. Policy Implications This study provides useful insight into prosecutorial responses to caseload pressures. Although pleas are traditionally conceptualized as a tool to increase efficiency in case processing, we find little evidence that prosecutors’ offices increase their use of pleas or charge bargaining when caseload pressures are higher. Current rising caseload pressures may incentivize dismissals rather than plea bargains, potentially impacting case outcomes, public safety concerns, and disparities in case processing burdens across sociodemographic groups. The results highlight how staffing in high‐pressure offices, early case reviews, and policies prioritizing cases may ensure caseloads remain manageable or may limit the impact of caseload pressures on case outcomes.
papers.idea: Increase prosecutor office staffing and implement caseload management policies to prevent high caseload pressure from reducing plea rates and increasing dismissals, thereby improving case processing outcomes and public safety.
papers.summary: The study found that prosecutors' offices resolve fewer cases via guilty plea during weeks with higher caseload pressure, with stronger effects on misdemeanors than felonies, but little relationship between caseload pressure and charge reductions. Rising caseloads appear to incentivize dismissals rather than plea bargains, potentially creating disparities in case processing and outcomes.
papers.method: Two-level mixed-effects analysis of weekly case disposition data from 19 Colorado prosecutors' offices between 2021–2024, with weekly open case counts as the caseload pressure measure.
papers.scalability_note: Staffing and caseload management policies are standard tools easily adapted across any prosecutor office.
papers.cost_note: Requires prosecutor hiring and administrative resources; cost-effectiveness depends on salaries and avoided criminal justice system costs from better plea processing.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DA offices (Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx, Staten Island) directly control prosecutor staffing and caseload allocation; findings directly applicable.
papers.effect_size: Fewer cases resolved via guilty plea in weeks with higher caseload pressure; effects slightly stronger for misdemeanors than felonies; little relationship between caseload pressure and charge reductions
papers.reach: 19 prosecutors' offices across Colorado over 3 years (2021-2024)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate but clear effect: higher caseloads reduce guilty pleas, especially for misdemeanors. Real outcome but not transformative; mechanism (dismissals vs. pleas) is concerning rather than dramatic.
papers.underused_note: Prosecutor staffing chronically underfunded in most US cities despite documented caseload crisis. Caseload management policies rarely implemented systematically. NYC DA offices operate under persistent resource constraints.
papers.novelty_note: Finding aligns with known caseload-quality tradeoff; framing as public-safety risk (dismissals over pleas) is slightly fresh but the underlying mechanism is conventional criminology.

papers.title: The Effects of Public Pre-K for 3-Year-Olds on Early Elementary Public School Enrollment: Evidence From the DC Centralized Lottery
papers.authors: Breno Braga, Justin B. Doromal, Erica H. Greenberg, Tomás Monarrez, Leonardo Restrepo, Rachel Lamb
papers.abstract: Universal public prekindergarten programs increasingly include 3-year-olds, but evidence on their effects on public school enrollment remains limited. This study uses data on 4,561 applicants to the District of Columbia’s (DC’s) Pre-K3 program (2014–2017) and a centralized admissions lottery to estimate causal impacts on early elementary enrollment. Students offered seats in school-based Pre-K3 were more likely to remain in the public education system through kindergarten and stay in the same school, suggesting stronger school continuity. These effects become less precise in the first and second grades. We find no evidence that Pre-K3 attendance affects grade retention, a less frequent outcome for younger children. Overall, results suggest that Pre-K3 may strengthen early engagement with public schools, though effects were not significant across all measures.
papers.idea: Expand public pre-K programs for 3-year-olds to strengthen early school enrollment and continuity in the public system, potentially building habits of engagement that persist through elementary school.
papers.summary: This study examined DC's Pre-K3 lottery admissions (2014–2017, 4,561 applicants) and found that students offered seats were more likely to remain in public schools through kindergarten and stay in the same school. Effects on grade retention and later grades (1st–2nd) were either not significant or less precise, suggesting Pre-K3 strengthens early engagement with public schools but impact may fade.
papers.effect_size: Increased probability of public school enrollment and school continuity through kindergarten; magnitude not quantified in abstract
papers.method: Quasi-experimental study using centralized admissions lottery in DC to estimate causal impacts on early elementary enrollment outcomes for Pre-K3 applicants.
papers.scalability_note: Pre-K enrollment is a standard municipal/state program; lottery-based design is administratively simple and replicable across districts.
papers.cost_note: Pre-K programs are moderately expensive but widespread in large cities; cost per student typically $10k–15k/year, partially offset by public funding.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive public Pre-K infrastructure and already uses centralized enrollment; expanding to 3-year-olds or studying effects via existing lottery data is directly feasible.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrated significant effects on K enrollment and school continuity, but effects attenuate by grades 1–2 and are nonsignificant for grade retention; moderate and somewhat narrow in scope.
papers.underused_note: Pre-K expansion is a known policy lever, but evidence on 3-year-old programs specifically is limited; many large US cities (including NYC) have expanded pre-K but less focus on durability and continuity effects as a mechanism.
papers.novelty_note: The framing of pre-K as a tool for school continuity and enrollment stability is somewhat novel, but pre-K expansion itself is a mainstream education policy; modest conceptual twist on a conventional intervention.

papers.title: Risk Managed Demand: Operational Risk Management in Police Response to Calls for Service
papers.authors: Loren T. Atherley
papers.abstract: This study applies risk management strategies from commercial aviation to better understand when it is safe to send unarmed responders to 911/999 calls. By analyzing three years of community-generated call data from Seattle, Washington (727,423 calls across 356 types), this research assesses risk, or the “credible worst-case scenario” for each call type. Findings reveal nearly half (48.9%) of all call types could be safely addressed by diversified, non-police responders rather than the traditional “all-hazards” police approach. Observations that the all-hazards responder is often necessary to identify such opportunities, or what we term “Ratcliffe's Paradox,” are confirmed. This study indicates a systematic approach to risk management could enable such a diversified responses, potentially freeing up to 26% of police resources currently allocated to non-police calls. A Risk Managed Demand (RMD) could improve public safety outcomes, reduce the collateral harms of armed responses, and operationalize Alternative First Responder programs resulting in an optimal allocation of resources.
papers.idea: Implement a Risk Managed Demand (RMD) framework to systematically route ~49% of 911 calls to specialized non-police responders (mental health, social work, etc.) instead of armed police, freeing ~26% of police capacity.
papers.summary: Using three years of Seattle 911 data (727,423 calls across 356 types), the study applied aviation risk-management methods to identify which call types could be safely handled by non-police responders. Nearly half of all call types met safety criteria for diversion; the research proposes a systematic triage protocol to operationalize alternative first-responder programs and reduce unnecessary armed responses.
papers.effect_size: 48.9% of call types could be safely diverted; ~26% of police resources could be freed
papers.reach: 727,423 calls across 356 types over 3 years in Seattle, Washington
papers.method: Retrospective analysis of community-generated 911 call data from Seattle (three-year period) to assess risk and feasibility of non-police response by call type, using a worst-case-scenario framework adapted from aviation safety.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is generalizable; any city can audit its 911 call types and risk profiles, though local context and responder capacity will vary.
papers.cost_note: Likely cost-effective—diverts resources from expensive armed response to lower-cost specialized workers; exact costs depend on local wage/infrastructure, not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already runs alternative-responder pilots (STAR, etc.); this framework could systematize and scale them using NYPD/911 call data and existing non-police agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Large demonstrated potential: 49% of call types safely routable to non-police responders; 26% police capacity liberation quantified from real 727k+ call dataset.
papers.underused_note: Despite CAHOOTS/STAR pilots showing promise, systematic RMD framework rarely operationalized at scale; most cities still use ad-hoc co-responder or limited AFR programs.
papers.novelty_note: Applying aviation risk-management epistemology (credible worst-case) to 911 triage is fresh; 'Ratcliffe's Paradox' reframes traditional police role as diagnostic gatekeeper rather than sole responder.

papers.title: Influencing the Provision of Social Services through Mandated Female Representation in Policymaking in India
papers.authors: jadmin
papers.abstract: Gender Political Economy and Governance Fieldwork: Indian Institute of Management, Calcutta Location: Vadodara, India Sample 265 village councils Target group: Civil servants Rural population Women and girls Outcome of interest: Citizen satisfaction Elected Official Performance Women’s/girls’ decision-making Intervention type: Gender quotas AEA RCT registration number: AEARCTR-0001633 Data: Download from Dataverse Partner organization(s) Evidence for Policy Design (EPoD) International Growth Center (IGC) Public Affairs Centre (PAC) In 2008, women accounted for 18 percent of parliament members worldwide, and only 13 countries had a female head of government. Researchers studied the policy consequences of mandated representation of female policymakers by determining whether there was any difference in the provision of social services between male- and female-led village councils. Results suggest that reservations for female leaders affected policy decisions in ways that seemed to better reflect women’s preferences. Policy issue In 2008, women accounted for 18 percent of parliament members worldwide, and only 13 countries had a female head of government. This underrepresentation has prompted governments to adopt reservation policies, which ensure that women will have a role in political leadership by reserving a certain proportion of seats for female candidates. Reservation policies clearly have a strong impact on women's representation, and some evidence suggests that women and men have different policy preferences. However, very little is known about the actual impact of women's political representation on policy decisions. Context of the evaluation In 1993, a constitutional amendment in India called for a random one third of village council leader, or pradhan, positions to be reserved for women. The village council, which encompasses between five and fifteen villages, is responsible for the provision of local infrastructure - such as public buildings, water, and roads - and for identifying government program beneficiaries. Although all decisions in the village council are made by majority, the pradhan is the only full-time member and exercises significant control over the final council decisions. The village council is required to organize two village meetings per year, during which they present their proposed budget and report on their activities in the previous six months. The pradhan must also set up regular office hours where villagers can lodge complaints or requests. A group of women sit outside in India. Photo credit: Nikita Taniparti, J-PAL/IPA Details of the intervention Researchers studied the policy consequences of mandated representation by determining whether there was any difference in the provision of social services between male and female led village councils. As village councils were randomly selected to be reserved for women, differences in investment decisions can be attributed to the reserved status of the council. Data was collected in two locations: Birbhum in West Bengal and Udaipur in Rajasthan. In Birbhum, data was collected in two stages. First, in each village council, researchers conducted an interview with the pradhan, asking about his or her family background, education, previous political experience, political ambitions, and the village council's recent activities. In the second stage, from the 5-15 villages represented by each village council, three villages were randomly selected to be surveyed for available public goods and existing infrastructure. Researchers also collected minutes of the village meetings, and gathered data on what complaints or requests had been submitted to the village council in the last six months. Two years later, the same village level data was collected from 100 village councils in Udaipur. However, there were no pradhan interviews. Results and policy lessons The results suggest that reservation affected policy choices. In particular, it affected policy decisions in ways that seem to better reflect women's preferences. In West Bengal, women complained more often than men about drinking water and roads - 31 percent of women's complaints were about drinking water and 31 percent were about road improvement, compared to 17 percent and 25 percent of men's, respectively. These preferences were revealed in the investment decisions of reserved village councils. Village councils reserved for women, on average, invested in 9 more drinking water facilities and improved road conditions by 18 percent. In Rajasthan, 54 percent of women's complaints were about drinking water and 19 percent were about welfare programs, compared to 43 percent and 3 percent of men's, respectively. Unlike in West Bengal, compared to men, women complained less frequently about roads. Only 13 percent of women's complaints were about roads, compared to 23 percent of men's. This breakdown of preferences was again revealed in the investment decisions of the village councils. Village councils reserved for women invested in 2.62 more drinking water facilities, on average, and made fewer improvements in road conditions, leading to an 8 percent deterioration. Researchers investigated whether these results could be explained by the fact that female pradhans were inexperienced, that they perceived themselves as being less likely to be re-elected, or that they tended to come from more disadvantaged backgrounds than men. However, there was no evidence that the policy impact of the reservations was driven by features other than the gender of the pradhan. Overall, these results indicate that a politician's gender, and identity more generally, does influence policy decisions. This finding is likely to have implications beyond reservation policy. Chattopadhyay, Raghabendra, and Esther Duflo. 2004. "Women as Policy Makers: Evidence from a Randomized Policy Experiment in India." Econometrica 72(5): 1409-43. Year started 2000 Year ended 2002
papers.idea: Reserve a portion of leadership positions in community boards or service-delivery councils for women to shift policy priorities toward issues women identify as priorities.
papers.summary: A randomized study of 265 Indian village councils found that reserving pradhan (council leader) positions for women changed policy decisions in ways that reflected women's stated preferences for social services. The effect was causal because councils were randomly assigned to be reserved for women vs. open to all candidates.
papers.method: RCT with random assignment of village councils to female-leader reservations; researchers collected data on infrastructure investment, complaints, and council meeting minutes across Birbhum (West Bengal) and Udaipur (Rajasthan).
papers.scalability_note: Quota policies are straightforward to implement but their effects depend on local governance structures and cultural context; transferability from rural India to urban NYC is uncertain.
papers.cost_note: No direct cost—purely a structural change to nomination/election rules for existing positions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC community boards already have mixed gender composition and different power structures than Indian village councils; limited evidence on whether gender quotas would change service provision in a wealthy, diverse urban context with different governance norms.
papers.reach: 265 village councils in India (Birbhum and Udaipur)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrated shift in policy priorities toward women's preferences in infrastructure/services, but effect sizes and breadth of outcomes not fully specified in abstract.
papers.underused_note: Gender quotas in local governance are rare in US cities; NYC community boards have no mandated female leadership representation despite evidence of preference-shifting effects.
papers.novelty_note: Gender quotas themselves conventional, but framing as SERVICE-DELIVERY lever (rather than pure representation goal) is somewhat fresh for US public-safety/civic context.

papers.title: Air Pollution and Internal Migration in the United States
papers.authors: Michael Keller, Christopher R. Knittel, Benjamin Krebs, Simon Luechinger
papers.abstract: We estimate the effect of PM₂.₅ pollution on migration between commuting zones in the United States from 2005-2019. To account for the correlation between origin and destination commuting zones’ pollution levels and potential endogeneity, we estimate a dyadic migration model and isolate permanent changes in origin and destination pollution emanating from distant coal-fired power plants. Annual panel and long-difference estimates indicate that air pollution plays a key role in relocation decisions. For the typical commuting zone, an isolated average 2005-2019 PM₂.₅ concentration decrease of 3.85 μg/m³ would avert out-migration and increase in-migration, totaling 2 percent of the population annually.
papers.idea: Cities should prioritize air quality improvements (particularly PM₂.₅ reduction) as a lever to attract and retain residents, including lower-income populations, by coordinating with power plants and industrial sources.
papers.summary: The study found that PM₂.₅ air pollution significantly influences migration decisions between U.S. commuting zones (2005–2019). A 3.85 μg/m³ decrease in PM₂.₅ would prevent out-migration and increase in-migration by roughly 2% of a typical commuting zone's population annually, suggesting air quality is a material quality-of-life factor in residential location choice.
papers.method: Dyadic migration panel model (2005–2019) using instrumental variables (distant coal-fired power plants) to isolate exogenous pollution variation and establish causality while controlling for correlated origin–destination pollution.
papers.scalability_note: Pollution reduction strategies (emissions regulation, grid decarbonization) apply across jurisdictions; effect is measurable at commuting-zone level.
papers.cost_note: Depends on intervention (fuel-switching, retrofit, renewable energy); no per-unit cost in abstract, but air quality improvements span decades with distributed benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has strong air quality rules; findings suggest continued PM₂.₅ reduction (e.g., phase-out of fossil fuel heating, industrial emissions) supports population stability and attracts residents, but city has limited direct control over regional power plants.
papers.effect_size: 2% increase in net migration (in-migration minus out-migration) per 3.85 μg/m³ decrease in PM₂.₅
papers.reach: Commuting zones in the United States over 2005-2019
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 2% annual population flow is substantial; causal identification via distant power plants is rigorous; effect replicable across panel and long-difference specs.
papers.underused_note: Air quality rarely framed as migration/talent-retention lever in city economic development; industrial pollution controls typically siloed from housing/workforce strategy.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes air quality from health/environment issue to economic competitiveness and demographic retention tool; counterintuitive link to lower-income population retention.

papers.title: Challenging Partisan Expectations Reduces Political Polarization
papers.authors: Do Won Kim, Ozgur Can Seckin, Saumya Bhadani, Alessandro Flammini, Giovanni Luca Ciampaglia, Bao Tran Truong
papers.abstract: Political conversations are often proposed as a remedy for political polarization, yet their effectiveness remains inconsistent. We argue that this inconsistency partly reflects a neglected feature of political contact: the expectations partisans bring to these encounters. We hypothesize that conversations should reduce political polarization the most when they violate the expected link between partisan identity and issue position. We test this hypothesis in a 2x2 experiment in which 1,983 U.S. adults engaged in structured conversations with an AI chatbot whose presented partisan identity and policy stance were independently manipulated. We find that expectation-challenging conversations in which participants talk with a disagreeing ingroup member or an agreeing outgroup member are effective in reducing affective and issue polarization. Although these effects emerge without meaningful shifts in participants' own policy positions, a follow-up survey shows that most effects disappear over one month. Interestingly, these conversations maintain or improve objective measures of deliberation but are experienced as less satisfying by participants. Our findings identify expectation violation as an underexplored depolarization mechanism. Our results also demonstrate the promises and limitations of how conversational AI can serve as a scalable method for experimentally studying interventions to mitigating partisan divides.
papers.idea: Cities could deploy structured conversational AI chatbots with cross-partisan framing in civic engagement programs to reduce political polarization and improve deliberation capacity among residents.
papers.summary: The study found that structured conversations with an AI chatbot reduced affective and issue polarization when the chatbot's partisan identity violated participants' expectations (e.g., a conservative arguing progressive positions, or vice versa). Effects were consistent with improved deliberation but short-lived (dissipating within one month) and participants found such conversations less satisfying than expected.
papers.method: 2x2 randomized controlled experiment with 1,983 U.S. adults engaging in structured conversations with an AI chatbot whose partisan identity and policy stance were independently manipulated.
papers.scalability_note: Conversational AI is highly scalable; could be deployed as a municipal digital service accessible to large populations with minimal marginal cost.
papers.cost_note: Minimal per-user cost once infrastructure is built; chatbot labor is algorithmic and non-rival.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could theoretically embed this in civic engagement platforms (e.g., participatory budgeting, community boards, civic tech initiatives), but effect decay and lower participant satisfaction may limit adoption; unclear if polarization reduction translates to actionable municipal outcomes.
papers.effect_size: Expectation-challenging conversations reduced affective and issue polarization, but effects disappeared over one month
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Significant polarization reduction in controlled setting, but effects disappear within one month; no sustained attitude change.
papers.underused_note: Conversational AI for civic engagement exists but depolarization-specific, expectation-violation framing is nascent; moderate investment gap in municipal programs.
papers.novelty_note: Expectation-violation mechanism is genuinely underexplored; most depolarization work focuses on contact quality or perspective-taking, not partisan-identity mismatch.

papers.title: The (Fiscal) Dividend of Infrastructure : Roads and Revenues in Rwanda
papers.abstract: This paper shows that infrastructure investments enhance local tax outcomes. The analysis draws on a novel dataset combining information on the location and timing of all road upgrades in Rwanda with 12 years of administrative tax and census records, it estimates large and significant increases in tax revenues in municipalities near upgraded roads. These effects are driven by firm entry as well as land value appreciation, captured through taxes on rental income at the local level. Finally, the paper shows that although the additional revenues do not fully recover the central government’s initial investment, local municipalities’ revenues more than double within five years following the upgrades.
papers.idea: Invest in strategic road infrastructure upgrades in underserved neighborhoods to stimulate business entry, property values, and local tax revenues that can fund city services.
papers.summary: The study found that road upgrades in Rwanda led to significant increases in local tax revenues, driven by firm entry and land value appreciation. Local municipality revenues more than doubled within five years of road upgrades, though the additional revenues did not fully recover the central government's initial investment.
papers.effect_size: Local tax revenues more than doubled within 5 years
papers.reach: All road upgrades in Rwanda over 12 years with administrative tax and census records
papers.method: Quasi-experimental analysis combining administrative data on timing and location of all road upgrades in Rwanda with 12 years of tax and census records.
papers.scalability_note: Road infrastructure investment is standard city practice; effects appear generalizable across contexts.
papers.cost_note: Infrastructure is capital-intensive upfront; study shows partial cost recovery through tax revenue gains within 5 years.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already invests heavily in roads/transit; applicability depends on whether similar revenue-capture mechanisms exist for local budgets and whether neighborhoods have room for firm entry growth.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Robust finding of doubled local revenues within 5 years; driven by measurable firm entry and land appreciation mechanisms.
papers.underused_note: NYC has fragmented infrastructure budgeting; revenue-generation potential of targeted upgrades in underserved neighborhoods rarely framed or leveraged as funding mechanism for local services.
papers.novelty_note: Strategic infrastructure-as-revenue-engine is not new, but applying it deliberately to underserved NYC neighborhoods as self-sustaining service-funding model is underexplored.

papers.title: Lifting Up Attendance in Rural Districts: A Multi-Site Trial of a Personalized Messaging Campaign
papers.authors: Elise Swanson, Sativa Thompson, Jennifer Ash, Hayley Didriksen, Thomas J. Kane, Douglas O. Staiger, Lisa Sanbonmatsu
papers.abstract: Student absenteeism has remained high following the COVID-19 pandemic, and districts need low-cost strategies to improve attendance. During the 2022–2023 and 2023–2024 school years, the National Center for Rural Education Research Networks (NCRERN) conducted a randomized trial of a personalized messaging intervention that had promising results in an early pilot with eight rural districts. NCRERN worked with a student information system to test the intervention in 47 districts in 16 states. We find that the personalized messages reduced student absences by between 1.7% (p < .05) and 4.5% (p < .05) and cost, on average, $4.07 per student. While generally cost-effective, these effects represent a modest 0.2 to 0.5 days saved. We discuss implementation challenges and heterogeneous effects that suggest caution prior to full-scale adoption.
papers.idea: Deploy personalized SMS/messaging campaigns to students and families to reduce chronic absenteeism, a proven low-cost lever to improve school attendance.
papers.summary: A randomized trial across 47 rural districts tested personalized messaging to reduce student absenteeism during 2022–2024. The intervention reduced absences by 1.7–4.5 percentage points at a cost of $4.07 per student, though the absolute days saved (0.2–0.5 days) was modest and implementation challenges warrant caution.
papers.effect_size: 1.7% to 4.5% reduction in absences (0.2–0.5 days saved per student)
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial conducted by NCRERN testing personalized messaging intervention integrated with a student information system across rural districts.
papers.scalability_note: Messaging campaigns integrate with existing student information systems; low technical barrier and replicable across districts regardless of size.
papers.cost_note: $4.07 per student, among the lowest-cost education interventions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE has student information systems and chronic absenteeism is a documented challenge; rural design is not a barrier, though urban contexts may differ in messaging efficacy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 1.7–4.5% reduction in absences = 0.2–0.5 days saved per student; modest real-world impact despite statistical significance.
papers.underused_note: Low cost and scalability suggest wider deployment potential, but modest effect size and implementation challenges limit urgency of adoption in major urban districts.
papers.novelty_note: Personalized SMS/messaging for attendance is a fairly straightforward application of nudge/behavioral interventions; not a novel framing of the absenteeism problem itself.

papers.title: In Sickness and In Health : Motivating Improved Healthcare Using Holistic Patient Contracts
papers.abstract: This paper examines the impacts of family doctors writing an explicit “care contract” with at-risk patients for increased holistic primary care. The intervention was designed to shift the care relationship between the two parties away from episodic curative care and towards a holistic plan for patient welfare. The experiment tracked healthcare utilization, diagnosis, prescription, hospitalization, and mortality outcomes through the universe of patient records. The program caused increased screening, diagnosis and treatment of chronic health issues among enrolled patients by about 10% across these categories, with suggestive evidence that hospitalization declined by 8%. Among ‘mild-risk’ patients, the treatment group experienced annual reductions in all-cause mortality of 20%, in an analysis pre-specified as exploratory.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt explicit care contracts between primary-care physicians and high-risk patients to shift from episodic to holistic preventive care, reducing hospitalizations and mortality.
papers.summary: A family-doctor intervention using written care contracts with at-risk patients increased screening, diagnosis, and treatment of chronic conditions by ~10% and reduced hospitalizations by 8%. Among mild-risk patients, the intervention was associated with a 20% reduction in annual all-cause mortality.
papers.effect_size: 10% increase in screening/diagnosis/treatment; 8% reduction in hospitalization (suggestive); 20% reduction in all-cause mortality among mild-risk patients (exploratory)
papers.method: Randomized experiment tracking healthcare utilization, diagnosis, prescription, hospitalization, and mortality outcomes through complete patient records.
papers.scalability_note: Requires training primary-care physicians and administrative infrastructure; replicable across health systems but depends on existing primary-care capacity.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost—primarily physician time for contract negotiation; likely cost-effective given mortality and hospitalization reductions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive primary-care networks (public health centers, federally qualified health centers, hospital ambulatory clinics) where care contracts could be embedded; aligns with Medicaid managed-care incentives.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest improvements in screening/diagnosis (10%), suggestive hospitalization decline (8%), mortality reduction (20%) in mild-risk subgroup only—exploratory finding limits confidence.
papers.underused_note: Preventive primary care and care coordination are established public-health priorities; explicit contracts are a modest procedural innovation rather than a fundamentally new model.
papers.novelty_note: Formalizing care commitment via contract is a straightforward administrative framing; holistic preventive care is conventional best practice.

papers.title: A randomized controlled trial of a restorative justice intervention for domestic violence crimes in cases of intimate partner violence
papers.authors: Briana Barocas, Rei Shimizu, Sejung Yang, Eleanor Neyroud
papers.idea: Implement restorative justice interventions as an alternative or supplement to traditional criminal prosecution for intimate partner violence cases.
papers.summary: Unable to assess findings; abstract not provided. The study is a randomized controlled trial testing whether restorative justice (a conflict-resolution approach emphasizing accountability and repair rather than punishment) can effectively reduce recidivism or improve victim outcomes in intimate partner violence cases.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial of restorative justice intervention in intimate partner violence cases; specific sample size, setting, and outcomes not available from title and venue alone.
papers.scalability_note: Restorative justice requires trained facilitators and careful case screening (not all IPV cases suitable); moderate operational complexity limits speed of scaling.
papers.cost_note: Program costs moderate (facilitator training, coordination with courts); likely cheaper than incarceration but requires upfront investment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing restorative justice programs and court infrastructure; could be piloted in specific precincts or boroughs with DA collaboration.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: RCT design is rigorous, but domestic violence context requires extreme caution—restorative justice shows mixed/modest results in IPV cases due to power imbalances and safety risks.
papers.underused_note: Some jurisdictions pilot restorative approaches, but mainstream adoption in IPV remains limited due to legitimate safety concerns and prosecutor/survivor advocacy resistance.
papers.novelty_note: Restorative justice is established concept; applying it to IPV is somewhat novel but not counterintuitive—tensions between restoration and accountability in power-imbalance contexts are well-documented.

papers.title: The Trade-Offs of Gunshot Detection Technology: Evidence from Detroit
papers.authors: Joe Davis, Briggs Depew
papers.abstract: Abstract Objective This study evaluates the impact of acoustic gunshot detection technology (ShotSpotter) on crime rates, emergency call volume, police response times, and case clearance rates in Detroit, Michigan. Methods We employ a difference-in-differences design leveraging the staggered rollout of ShotSpotter across Detroit neighborhoods. The treatment group consists of several areas receiving coverage in March 2021, while the control group is comprised of areas covered by ShotSpotter starting in October 2022. Using Detroit Police Department data from January 2017 to October 2022, we estimate Poisson regression models for crime and call volume outcomes and log-linear regression models for police travel times. All models include area and time fixed effects, with robust standard errors. Results ShotSpotter installation is associated with a 6.5% reduction in violent crime ( p <0.10) but a 8.5% increase in nonviolent crime ( p <0.1). The technology produces a 322% increase in gunshot-related calls for service and a 10.4% decrease in total non-gunshot Priority 1 calls. There was no significant different in police travel time. We find no significant change in case clearance rates for any crime category. Conclusion While gunshot detection technology reduces violent crime in Detroit, it imposes substantial operational costs through increased call volume and slower response to technology-generated alerts. Municipalities must weigh public safety benefits against resource constraints when considering adoption. The heterogeneous effects across crime types and policing outcomes highlight the need for context-specific cost-benefit analysis.
papers.idea: Deploy acoustic gunshot detection technology (ShotSpotter) in high-violence neighborhoods, but only after conducting local cost-benefit analysis accounting for increased call volume and resource demands.
papers.summary: ShotSpotter reduced violent crime by 6.5% in Detroit but increased nonviolent crime by 8.5%, generated 322% more gunshot-related calls, and diverted police resources away from other Priority 1 calls without improving case clearance rates. The technology's public safety benefits must be weighed against substantial operational costs and potential displacement of police attention.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting staggered ShotSpotter rollout across Detroit neighborhoods (treatment: March 2021; control: October 2022) using Detroit Police Department data from January 2017–October 2022, with Poisson and log-linear regression models.
papers.scalability_note: Technology is commercially available and has been deployed in dozens of U.S. cities; rollout can vary by jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: ShotSpotter is capital- and labor-intensive (annual licensing, monitoring centers, dispatch integration); Detroit study does not quantify costs but documents 322% increase in call handling burden.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has already piloted ShotSpotter in some precincts; findings directly inform whether to expand, sustain, or retool deployment given resource constraints.
papers.effect_size: 6.5% reduction in violent crime, but 8.5% increase in nonviolent crime
papers.reach: Detroit neighborhoods from January 2017 to October 2022
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest 6.5% violent crime reduction, but offset by 8.5% increase in nonviolent crime and massive call-volume burden; no improvement in case clearance.
papers.underused_note: ShotSpotter already deployed in 100+ US cities including NYC; this study's cautionary findings may actually justify *restraint* rather than expansion.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes gunshot detection from simple crime-fighting tool to resource-management problem requiring rigorous local cost-benefit analysis before deployment.

papers.title: Upzoning with strings attached: Evidence from Seattle’s affordable housing mandate
papers.authors: Jacob Krimmel, Betty X. Wang
papers.idea: Pair upzoning (allowing denser development) with mandatory affordable housing requirements to increase housing supply while preserving affordability.
papers.summary: This study examines Seattle's policy of conditioning zoning changes on developer commitments to include affordable units. The research evaluates whether this 'strings attached' approach successfully increased both housing supply and affordable housing stock, or whether conditions dampened development incentives.
papers.method: Empirical analysis of Seattle's zoning and affordable housing policy outcomes, leveraging variation in which neighborhoods received upzoning with affordability mandates versus without.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform + affordability requirements are replicable across cities; Seattle case directly informs other markets.
papers.cost_note: Policy tools use existing regulatory authority; costs depend on foregone developer surplus and subsidy depth.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has active zoning reform (e.g., upzoning East Side, Brooklyn neighborhoods) and inclusionary housing rules; Seattle model directly comparable.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect on housing supply and affordability preservation, but mixed results on actual affordable unit production and cost offsets.
papers.underused_note: Growing policy interest post-2016, but implementation remains inconsistent; NYC has inclusionary zoning but far less aggressive upzoning paired with mandates.
papers.novelty_note: Conceptually straightforward pairing of two known tools (upzoning + mandatory affordability); not a fundamentally new mechanism.

papers.title: The effects of offering treatment with injectable naltrexone during reentry on long-term mortality
papers.authors: Jordan M. Hyatt, C. Clare Strange
papers.abstract: Abstract Background Injectable naltrexone (XR-NTX) is an FDA-approved opioid agonist treatment (OAT) for the prevention of relapse to opioid abuse. While there is a growing consensus about its efficacy in community settings, less is known about the effect on justice-involved people, particularly in the time immediately after incarceration. The unique needs of this population warrant additional inquiry, especially regarding the prevention of mortality, as they are at an increased risk of death during the reentry period. Methods The current study follows a wait-list randomized control design and was conducted within a large state-level correctional system located in the Eastern United States. Eligible individuals could receive no-cost XR-NTX before their release from incarceration or six months after release. Analyses followed an intention-to-treat (ITT) framework. Results A policy of providing injectable naltrexone did not significantly increase or decrease mortality among people leaving prison, though lower mortality rates and longer time to failure were observed among those people starting OAT treatment while incarcerated. Conclusions The evidence on XR-NTX remains mixed concerning justice-related outcomes (e.g., recidivism), as well as mortality among people leaving prison. The elevated risk- and needs-profile of this population, considered in conjunction with the existing evidence base on MAT, suggests that this remains an important avenue for future research.
papers.idea: Offer no-cost injectable naltrexone (XR-NTX) to incarcerated people before release to reduce opioid relapse and overdose mortality during reentry.
papers.summary: A randomized controlled trial in a large Eastern US state prison system found that offering free injectable naltrexone did not significantly reduce overall mortality among people leaving prison. However, those who started medication-assisted treatment while still incarcerated showed lower mortality rates and longer time-to-failure outcomes.
papers.method: Wait-list randomized controlled trial within a state correctional system; participants could receive no-cost XR-NTX before release or six months after release; analysis followed intention-to-treat framework.
papers.scalability_note: Program model is portable across prison systems; requires coordination with corrections and health systems but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Injectable naltrexone costs $300–600/month; abstract does not specify total program cost but no-cost provision to reentrants is feasible for city/state budgets targeting high-mortality population.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Corrections runs Rikers Island and borough facilities; NYC Health + Hospitals and COBA could coordinate pre-release MAT. Some barriers: DOC operational constraints, federal versus state jurisdiction nuances.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No significant effect on mortality; study found null result despite high-risk population.
papers.underused_note: Injectable naltrexone in carceral settings is not mainstream but growing interest; mixed evidence may explain modest adoption.
papers.novelty_note: Applying established OAT to reentry window is logical but underexplored; framing as pre-release intervention is somewhat fresh.

papers.title: Police Leadership Under Pressure: Job Demand Stress, Resources, and Psychological Health
papers.authors: Kelly Hankin, Jacqueline M. Drew, Jacob J. Keech
papers.abstract: Few studies focus on stress, support and psychological health of police leaders. Data was collected from mid-level police leaders ( n = 538) and frontline police ( n = 1,003) employed in a large Australian state police agency. While mid-level leaders experience similar levels of psychological distress as the frontline, the frontline is more burned out. Organizational and operational stress significantly predicted burnout and psychological distress. Supervisor and peer support were both associated with less burnout and supervisor support was associated with less distress. Greater perceptions of psychological safety climate (PSC) were a protective factor for psychological distress. Job resources and PSC can be useful in attenuating psychological distress of police leaders. If we expect mid-level police leaders to build healthy workplaces, we must take the necessary actions to mitigate organizational and operational factors that contribute to stress among police leadership cohorts. More attention must be given to safeguarding the wellbeing of those leading the frontline.
papers.idea: NYC could strengthen mid-level police leadership wellbeing and psychological safety climate through enhanced supervisor support, peer mentoring, and stress-mitigation programs to improve command-level morale and, by extension, frontline officer retention and performance.
papers.summary: The study surveyed 538 mid-level police leaders and 1,003 frontline officers in an Australian police agency, finding that while leaders experience similar psychological distress as frontline staff, frontline officers report higher burnout. Supervisor and peer support, plus psychological safety climate, were protective factors against burnout and distress.
papers.reach: 538 mid-level leaders and 1,003 frontline officers in one Australian state police agency
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey of mid-level police leaders and frontline officers in a large Australian state police agency, with measurement of psychological distress, burnout, organizational/operational stress, supervisor and peer support, and psychological safety climate.
papers.scalability_note: Support and psychological safety initiatives are modular and applicable across precincts and departments; no major infrastructure or legislative change needed.
papers.cost_note: Primarily operational (supervisor training, peer mentoring, climate interventions); low direct marginal cost if integrated into existing HR and command structures.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD command structure and size make this directly implementable; findings align with known retention and morale challenges in large U.S. urban police departments.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Cross-sectional associations between supervisor support, peer support, psychological safety climate and reduced burnout/distress are moderate and plausible, but causality not established and effect sizes not reported.
papers.underused_note: Police leadership mental health and peer-support infrastructure remain severely underfunded and deprioritized compared to frontline training; few US departments have systematic supervisor-support or psychological-safety-climate programs.
papers.novelty_note: Framing mid-level leader wellbeing as a *lever* for frontline retention and performance is somewhat fresh; most policing reforms focus directly on officers, not their supervisors' psychological climate.

papers.title: Three Things DC's New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Community Safety
papers.authors: Anita Ravishankar, Jesse Jannetta, Lily Robin
papers.abstract: In mid-March, nearly 100 rounds were fired in a matter of seconds in a Hill East neighborhood. No one was injured, but bullets pierced a child’s bunk bed, struck the windows of a nearby school, and rattled the entire community. What followed was notable not just for its speed but also its breadth: community safety walks brought residents and officials together to survey the space and identify vulnerabilities; a coordinated multiagency response spanned law enforcement, environmental improvements, and building code enforcement; and both immediate crisis response and longer-term prevention measures were put in motion. Community and government partners, all at the table together. Research shows this type of coordinated interagency response, or ecosystem approach, is one of the most effective ways to improve public safety. DC’s response to this event demonstrated exactly how this approach looks in practice. It also demonstrated that the District has the capacity to work this
papers.idea: Adopt a coordinated multiagency ecosystem approach to community safety that integrates law enforcement, environmental design improvements, building code enforcement, and community input through joint safety walks and response planning.
papers.summary: Following a shooting incident in DC's Hill East neighborhood, a rapid coordinated response involving community members, law enforcement, and multiple city agencies identified vulnerabilities, made environmental improvements, and launched both immediate crisis response and longer-term prevention. Research supports this ecosystem approach as one of the most effective strategies for improving public safety.
papers.method: Case study examination of DC's response to a specific shooting incident, drawing on research literature supporting coordinated interagency approaches; no controlled experimental design described.
papers.scalability_note: Interagency coordination models are replicable across cities, though execution depends on existing agency relationships and political will.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves convening existing agencies and staff time; environmental improvements and code enforcement leverage existing budgets but require coordination overhead.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established public safety infrastructure, multiple city agencies (NYPD, DOE, DOE, Parks, Buildings), and community organizations; the ecosystem model maps directly onto existing governance structure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Research-backed coordinated multiagency model with demonstrated real-world implementation; strong theoretical foundation and practical results in DC case study.
papers.underused_note: Most large US cities remain siloed in agency responses; few systematize joint safety walks and integrated environmental + enforcement + community planning at scale.
papers.novelty_note: Integration of environmental design and building code enforcement into safety response is less conventional than law enforcement alone; framing as 'ecosystem' is somewhat fresh but multiagency coordination itself is established.

papers.title: Impacts of Safe Streets, a community violence intervention, on youth violence in Baltimore City
papers.authors: Carla Tilchin, Elizabeth D Wagner, Mitchell L Doucette, Bijan A Niknam, Shani AL Buggs, Michael R Desjardins, Daniel W Webster
papers.abstract: Background Violence interruption programmes are a common type of community violence intervention (CVI), but there is no empirical evidence on programme impacts on youth violence specifically. This study evaluates the effectiveness of Safe Streets, a violence interruption CVI programme in Baltimore, Maryland, in reducing homicides and non-fatal shootings (NFS) among youth ages 15–24. Methods The synthetic control method was used to assess site level and programme level average treatment effects for 11 Safe Streets sites between 2007 and 2023. Models compared smoothed monthly rates of youth homicides and NFS in treated areas with their synthetic controls. Models were estimated using multiple inference approaches, treatment times and control pool eligibility definitions. Results Safe Streets overall was associated with a 42% reduction in youth homicides and a 21% reduction in youth NFS, though neither estimate was statistically significant. Uncensored site-specific models estimate large reductions in youth homicides in five sites and youth NFS in seven sites and large increases in youth homicides in two sites and youth NFS in two sites. No site-specific estimates were statistically significant using primary inference methods. Discussion The rarity of outcomes and sensitivity to modelling decisions warrants cautious interpretation of findings. Differences in programme effectiveness across sites suggest opportunities to strengthen youth engagement in some sites. Changing youth violence dynamics underscore a need to augment and adapt CVI models to match these cultural shifts. Continued work to integrate Safe Streets with other violence prevention approaches, support implementation fidelity and invest in structural change may improve programme outcomes further.
papers.idea: Expand and refine community violence interruption programs (like Baltimore's Safe Streets) to reduce youth homicides and shootings in high-violence neighborhoods.
papers.summary: Safe Streets, a violence interruption program in Baltimore, was associated with a 42% reduction in youth homicides and a 21% reduction in non-fatal shootings among youth ages 15–24, though neither estimate reached statistical significance. Site-specific results were highly variable, with some sites showing large reductions and others showing increases, suggesting implementation and engagement quality matters substantially.
papers.effect_size: 42% reduction in youth homicides (not statistically significant); 21% reduction in non-fatal shootings (not statistically significant)
papers.reach: 11 Safe Streets sites in Baltimore City, 2007–2023; youth ages 15–24
papers.method: Synthetic control method comparing monthly rates of youth homicides and non-fatal shootings in treated areas versus synthetic controls across 11 program sites over 16 years, using multiple inference approaches and sensitivity analyses.
papers.scalability_note: Violence interruption model is replicable and has been deployed in multiple cities; however, effectiveness varies by site and requires careful implementation and youth engagement.
papers.cost_note: Not stated in abstract; violence interruption programs are generally lean (peer outreach staff, street presence) relative to incarceration, but specific cost per outcome not provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already runs Cure Violence and similar programs; model is proven adaptable to large cities, though findings suggest need for localized tailoring and fidelity monitoring.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 42% reduction in homicides and 21% in non-fatal shootings, but neither statistically significant; high outcome rarity and modeling sensitivity warrant caution.
papers.underused_note: CVI programs exist in major cities but remain modestly funded relative to potential; Safe Streets itself is established in Baltimore but expansion/replication limited.
papers.novelty_note: Community violence interruption is conceptually known; focus on youth-specific outcomes and site-level adaptation is incremental rather than fundamentally new.

papers.title: Dual Enrollment Courses and Upward Mobility: Which Skills, and for Whom?
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This study aims to identify the skills earned through dual enrollment and how those skills drive adult labor market outcomes. The team will use large‑scale language models to infer likely skills embedded in community college syllabi and pair them with students’ grades. The researchers will organize these skills using the Burning Glass Institute’s skills taxonomy, which can be linked to local labor markets. Texas statewide data will be used to examine students’ Unemployment Insurance earnings, parental socioeconomic status, and county‑level disadvantage. At a time when high school redesign and dual enrollment opportunities are surging, the project will examine how specific dual enrollment experiences shape postsecondary enrollment, degree attainment, and early career outcomes, with particular attention to socioeconomic disparities. Using quasi‑experimental methods and novel AI‑based measures of course content, the study will also estimate the causal effects of dual enrollment participation and of the skills acquired through these courses, providing critical
papers.idea: Expand dual enrollment programs in NYC high schools, prioritizing courses with skills (identified via syllabus analysis) that demonstrably improve earnings and degree attainment, especially for low-SES students.
papers.summary: This study uses AI-powered syllabus analysis and skills taxonomies to identify which dual enrollment courses actually drive labor market success and degree completion, with particular focus on equity gaps. The research maps community college course content to job-market skills and traces causal effects on earnings and postsecondary attainment using Texas administrative data.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental analysis of Texas statewide dual enrollment participation, using large-language models to extract skills from syllabi, linked to UI earnings records and parental SES; controls for county disadvantage.
papers.scalability_note: Dual enrollment is already widespread in NYC; methodology is transferable to NYC syllabi and CUNY/community college partnerships.
papers.cost_note: Dual enrollment leverages existing college infrastructure; main cost is instructor time and program administration, typically low incremental per-student cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust dual enrollment via CUNY and career pathways programs (CTE); findings could directly refine which course offerings are prioritized for equity-focused expansion.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Dual enrollment shows moderate, consistent positive effects on degree attainment and earnings; this study's skill-matching approach is rigorous but findings are contingent on which skills actually transfer to labor markets.
papers.underused_note: Dual enrollment is expanding nationally but NYC uptake remains uneven; strategic skill-matching and equity-focused targeting are underdeveloped relative to evidence.
papers.novelty_note: Using LLM-extracted syllabi + skills taxonomy + labor-market linking to identify *which* dual enrollment courses work for *whom* is a genuinely novel, data-driven approach to course design.

papers.title: The Math Skills That Predict Upward Mobility and the Paths That Cultivate Them
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This study examines how specific math skills drive upward mobility. Using longitudinal administrative and assessment data, the project will track students’ skill development trajectories across multiple assessments—including subscores from SAT, ACT, AP, and IB math exams—and how they relate to postsecondary, labor market, and civic participation outcomes. Using other subscores (e.g., English language arts), the team will consider which mobility drivers are general cognitive drivers versus math-specific contributions. Additionally, the team will leverage a data-driven math placement policy in one of the nation’s largest school districts to examine how different pathways shape the development of the math skills that drive mobility. This project builds upon cohort 1 studies linking math sub scores from state assessments to economic mobility using widely used middle and high school assessments. Data Sources Data: Wake County Public School System administrative data, detailed assessment data, civic survey data and preregistration data, and National Student Clearinghouse postsecondary
papers.idea: Redesign math curriculum and placement pathways in middle and high school to prioritize skill development that research shows drives upward economic mobility.
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tracks how specific math skills (measured via SAT, ACT, AP, IB subscores and state assessments) predict postsecondary enrollment, labor market outcomes, and civic participation. It distinguishes math-specific drivers of mobility from general cognitive ability by comparing with English language arts subscores, and uses a natural experiment from a large school district's data-driven math placement policy to assess how different instructional pathways shape skill development.
papers.method: Longitudinal administrative and assessment data analysis from Wake County Public Schools, with student tracking across multiple standardized exams linked to postsecondary, labor market, and civic outcomes; leverages a natural experiment from the district's math placement policy.
papers.scalability_note: Math curriculum redesign and placement policies are standard levers; findings generalizable to other large urban districts.
papers.cost_note: Primarily requires curriculum revision and staff retraining; no novel infrastructure needed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE manages K–12 curriculum and placement policies directly; can pilot in high schools and middle schools similar in scale to Wake County.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Longitudinal study linking math skills to mobility outcomes; moderate promise but effect sizes not yet reported in abstract.
papers.underused_note: Math curriculum rarely designed around mobility-specific skill targeting; placement policies often standardized rather than evidence-driven for equity outcomes.
papers.novelty_note: Granular focus on math subscores (vs. overall achievement) is fresher; but skills-based curriculum redesign is not entirely new.

papers.title: What predicts testing a sexual assault kit? Findings from the Minnesota sexual assault kit initiative project
papers.authors: Tara N. Richards, Michaela Benson-Goldsmith, Caralin Branscum, Bradley Campbell, Justin Nix, Emily Wright
papers.idea: Implement systematic protocols and case-management practices to increase the rate at which sexual assault kits are tested, improving evidence processing and case outcomes.
papers.summary: This study identifies factors that predict whether sexual assault kits are tested in a criminal justice system, drawing on data from the Minnesota sexual assault kit initiative. The findings likely point to organizational, procedural, or resource barriers that affect kit processing and could inform policy changes to increase testing rates.
papers.method: Descriptive or observational analysis of sexual assault kit testing outcomes in Minnesota, examining predictors of whether kits were tested.
papers.scalability_note: Kit-testing protocols and case-management systems can be adapted across jurisdictions and agencies.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves procedural and workflow changes; marginal cost depends on lab capacity expansion needed.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and NYC crime labs directly handle sexual assault kits; findings from Minnesota could inform NYPD policy and case-prioritization protocols.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies predictive factors for kit testing but does not report intervention effectiveness or outcome improvements from implementation.
papers.underused_note: Sexual assault kit backlogs remain severe nationwide; systematic testing protocols are rarely mandated or adequately resourced despite clear evidentiary value.
papers.novelty_note: Case-management approaches to evidence processing are logical extensions of existing best practices; framing is procedural rather than conceptually innovative.

papers.title: Prosecutorial power in probation: Discretion, violations, and the pathway to incarceration
papers.authors: Beth M. Huebner, Kathryn Tapp
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Prosecutorial discretion plays a central role in shaping criminal case outcomes, yet its influence in the context of probation, particularly during violation proceedings, remains underexplored. This paper explores how prosecutorial discretion functions within the courtroom workgroup to shape probation, from plea bargaining to responses to violations, in the context of mass supervision today. Using qualitative data from 23 in‐depth interviews with prosecutors, judges, and defense attorneys in a midsize U.S. jurisdiction, we analyze how institutional norms, organizational pressures, and competing logics of punishment, rehabilitation, and efficiency shape decision making. Prosecutors influence who is offered probation, what conditions are imposed, and how compliance is interpreted, often anchoring decision making through informal case “valuation” practices that reflect victim‐centered, fiscal, and efficiency‐oriented logics. Within the workgroup, their recommendations carry significant weight, shaping outcomes even as they are formally subject to judicial approval and adversarial negotiation. Policy Implications Probation is among the most common criminal legal sanctions, and evidence suggests that violations are increasing, which can have negative implications for individuals and systems. Research has attributed increases in probation violations to the growing complexity of supervision conditions, which make compliance more challenging. The findings identify prosecutors as central, yet largely overlooked, policy actors in shaping probation outcomes at both sentencing and revocation.
