As such, the research underscores the need for policy changes that ‘right size’ the conditions of probation and address the organizational norms and decision‐making practices of the courtroom workgroup.
papers.idea: NYC could audit and reform prosecutorial decision-making practices in probation violation proceedings to reduce unnecessary revocations and establish clearer, evidence-based standards for condition-setting and violation responses.
papers.summary: The study found that prosecutors exercise outsized discretion in probation cases—influencing who gets probation, what conditions are imposed, and how violations are handled—often using informal "case valuation" heuristics driven by victim-centered, fiscal, and efficiency logics rather than rehabilitation or risk-proportionality. These prosecutorial recommendations carry significant weight within the courtroom workgroup despite formal judicial oversight. Rising probation violations are partly attributable to overly complex supervision conditions that make compliance harder.
papers.method: Qualitative study using 23 in-depth interviews with prosecutors, judges, and defense attorneys in one midsize U.S. jurisdiction, analyzing institutional norms and decision-making practices.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial guidelines and probation condition standards are city/state-level policy tools; can be adapted across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Implementing clearer guidelines and auditing practices requires staff time but minimal direct cost; likely saves money if it reduces unnecessary incarceration.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial probation population, DA offices, and sentencing policy apparatus; prosecutors and Unified Court System directly control these levers.
papers.reach: 23 in-depth interviews in one midsize U.S. jurisdiction
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study identifies prosecutorial influence on probation outcomes; documents mechanisms but doesn't quantify impact of reform interventions.
papers.underused_note: Prosecutorial discretion in probation violations is rarely subject to systematic audit or reform; most oversight focuses on sentencing, not revocation decisions.
papers.novelty_note: Frames prosecutors—not judges or probation officers—as the primary lever for reducing unnecessary revocations; shifts accountability focus upstream in courtroom workgroup.

papers.title: The imminence divide: public judgments of deadly force by police and civilians
papers.authors: Nicholas Goldrosen, Ben Jones
papers.abstract: Abstract Objective To assess how threat imminence shapes public judgments of deadly force used by police and by civilians, and whether the public believes the imminence requirement currently applies to police. Methods We fielded a nationally representative US survey experiment ( N = 1,183) that manipulated whether a suspect posed an imminent or non-imminent threat and whether a civilian or police officer faced the decision to use deadly force. Respondents evaluated support for shooting and reported beliefs about relevant legal rules. Results Imminence significantly affected support for deadly force. Although respondents were more supportive of police than civilians using deadly force across conditions, they generally disapproved of shooting a non-imminent threat. Nearly 95% of respondents incorrectly believed that Supreme Court precedent restricts police deadly force to imminent threats. Conclusions The public supports the imminence requirement for police and largely believes it already exists in law. This “imminence divide” between public expectations and legal standards may undermine police legitimacy when officers act lawfully but contrary to public judgment.
papers.idea: Close the gap between public expectations and legal standards on police use of deadly force through revised training, policy, or public education.
papers.summary: A nationally representative survey experiment (N=1,183) found that 95% of the public incorrectly believes Supreme Court precedent restricts police deadly force to imminent threats, when current law permits broader use. Respondents strongly disapproved of shooting non-imminent threats, but the study did not test or measure the effect of any intervention to close this expectation–law gap.
papers.effect_size: 95% public misperception; imminence significantly shaped support but no quantified difference in outcome magnitude given
papers.method: Online survey experiment manipulating threat imminence (imminent vs. non-imminent) and actor type (police vs. civilian); nationally representative sample; respondents evaluated support for deadly force and reported beliefs about legal rules.
papers.scalability_note: Public education or training interventions to address the gap are conceptually scalable but the study identifies the problem, not a tested solution.
papers.cost_note: Cost depends on intervention type; public education campaigns are moderately cost-effective but not quantified here.
papers.nyc_note: Study is national; NYC could adapt findings to local training or communication, but requires further design work. No NYC-specific data or intervention model provided.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Survey evidence shows strong public preference for imminence standard, but no causal evidence that closing the gap reduces actual harms or improves outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Legitimacy-focused training on use-of-force standards is underfunded relative to the disconnect; most departments lack systematic public education on legal standards.
papers.novelty_note: Framing legitimacy as a communications/expectations problem is somewhat fresh; most reform focuses on doctrine or accountability rather than closing the belief gap.

papers.title: The predictive validity of the Stalking Risk Profile in everyday practice
papers.authors: Troy E. McEwan, Reneta Slikboer, Benjamin Spivak, Rajan Darjee, Nina Papalia, Ashley Dunne, Melanie Simmons, James R. P. Ogloff
papers.abstract: The Stalking Risk Profile (SRP) is a structured professional judgment guideline to help practitioners evaluate and respond to risks associated with stalking. Using a sample of 217 Australian adults referred to a community forensic mental health service, this study examined the predictive validity of SRP risk judgments made by assessing clinicians. Discrimination, classification, and prediction statistics were examined at 12-month intervals over a 4-year follow-up using police charges for stalking-related offending. Total scores were positively related to risk judgments. Moderate/high risk judgments generally showed good sensitivity, though weaker specificity. Risk judgments about stalking violence and recurrent stalking of the same victim showed moderate to strong discrimination and predictive benefits over the base rate of reoffending, with minor improvements over time. Judgments about stalking different victims or persistent stalking of the same victim were less effective, possibly due to methodological limitations of the prospective study design.
papers.idea: Adopt the Stalking Risk Profile (SRP) as a structured clinical tool in NYC's mental health and criminal justice systems to improve risk assessment and early intervention in stalking cases.
papers.summary: This study validated the Stalking Risk Profile on 217 Australian adults referred to community forensic mental health services over 4 years, finding that moderate/high risk judgments showed good sensitivity for predicting stalking-related reoffending (especially stalking violence and recurrent same-victim stalking), though specificity was weaker. The SRP demonstrated moderate-to-strong discrimination and predictive benefits over base-rate reoffending predictions.
papers.method: Prospective observational study of 217 adults referred to a community forensic mental health service in Australia, with police charge data collected at 12-month intervals over 4 years to assess predictive validity of clinician SRP risk judgments.
papers.scalability_note: SRP is a structured judgment tool readily deployable across multiple mental health and law enforcement agencies; does not require new technology or extensive infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Training clinicians on a structured guideline is relatively inexpensive; tool itself is non-proprietary clinical protocol.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive community forensic mental health services, NYPD, and criminal courts; SRP could be integrated into threat assessment units, domestic violence bureaus, and mental health crisis intervention teams.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate predictive validity for some stalking subtypes (violence, recurrence of same victim) but weaker for others; good sensitivity but notably lower specificity limits clinical utility.
papers.underused_note: Structured risk assessment tools remain underdeployed in US criminal justice and mental health systems despite evidence; stalking cases typically lack standardized assessment protocols in NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Structured clinical judgment for risk assessment is established practice; applying an existing Australian tool to US context is incremental rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: The Effectiveness of the Parent Support Program: a Randomized Controlled Trial of an Online Intervention to Increase Parental Support Among Parents and Caregivers of Trans and Nonbinary Youth
papers.authors: Em Matsuno, Matt L. Miller, Ga Tin Finneas Wong, Danny Shultz, Jenna Rudo-Stern, Alex E. Colson, Cady Berkel
papers.idea: Implement an online parental support program to increase family acceptance and reduce mental health risks among trans and nonbinary youth.
papers.summary: This RCT tested an online intervention designed to increase parental support for trans and nonbinary youth. The study was registered as a randomized controlled trial but no specific findings are reported in the abstract provided.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial of an online intervention targeting parents and caregivers of trans and nonbinary youth.
papers.scalability_note: Online interventions are inherently scalable; can reach many families simultaneously with minimal marginal cost per participant.
papers.cost_note: Online programs typically have low per-participant delivery costs once developed, though initial platform and content creation required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large LGBTQ+ youth population and existing LGBTQ+ service networks (DOHMH, ACS); program could integrate with school health or community health initiatives, but requires partnership with mental health/family service agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: RCT design suggests rigorous evaluation, but no abstract provided to assess actual effect sizes on outcomes like mental health or safety.
papers.underused_note: Family acceptance interventions for LGBTQ+ youth are underfunded relative to need; online scalable formats rarely deployed by NYC schools or community orgs.
papers.novelty_note: Parent support for trans youth is not new, but digital asynchronous delivery to reach dispersed families with stigma concerns is relatively fresh.

papers.title: Examining the “On the Books” Versus “In the Field” Impacts of Probation Reform
papers.authors: Pranav Athimuthu, Christopher S. Inkpen, Caroline Kery, Stephen J. Tueller, Kristin Bechtel, Emily Burtch, Pamela K. Lattimore
papers.idea: Cities should audit and align their probation supervision practices to match stated policy goals, closing gaps between reform intent and street-level implementation.
papers.summary: This study examines the gap between officially adopted probation reforms and how they are actually implemented by field officers. The research identifies discrepancies between policy-on-paper and practice-in-the-field that may undermine reform effectiveness and reduce benefits to probationers and public safety.
papers.method: Comparative qualitative and/or quantitative study of probation reform implementation across multiple sites, examining policy documents and field practices (exact design, sample size, and settings not provided in abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Probation departments exist in every major city; audit-and-align methodology is generalizable.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves internal audit and retraining; low marginal cost once identified.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Probation is a major agency; this directly applies to implementation of its stated reforms.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Implementation audits can reveal inefficiencies and misalignment, but the study doesn't quantify crime/reoffending impacts of closing gaps.
papers.underused_note: Most cities adopt probation reforms on paper but rarely conduct systematic audits of actual field practice; implementation science undervalued in criminal justice.
papers.novelty_note: Focuses on implementation fidelity and street-level drift rather than the reforms themselves—a meta-level intervention rarely foregrounded in policy.

papers.title: Public transit gains and spatially uneven travel demand changes after NYC congestion pricing
papers.authors: Donghang Li, Dingyi Zhuang, Yunlin Li, Chenan Shen, Nina Cao, Yunhan Zheng, Shenhao Wang, Jinhua Zhao
papers.abstract: New York City implemented the nation's first cordon-based congestion pricing program in January 2025, providing an opportunity to evaluate how system-wide urban mobility responds to large-scale pricing interventions. Because such policies generate spillovers across modes and locations, credible control groups are difficult to construct. We address this challenge using time series foundation models to generate probabilistic counterfactual demand forecasts with calibrated uncertainty. Applying this framework to bus, subway, and aggregate trip volume data, we find that post-policy bus and subway ridership increased significantly relative to expected no-policy demand, while overall travel demand decreased modestly. The effects are spatially heterogeneous: while reductions in overall travel demand are concentrated within the Congestion Relief Zone, transit gains extend beyond Manhattan's core. Socio-demographic analyses further reveal uneven adaptation across neighborhoods, highlighting spatial equity implications. Our framework provides a scalable approach for the uncertainty-aware evaluation of system-wide urban interventions when clean control groups are unavailable.
papers.idea: Use congestion pricing to shift travel demand toward public transit and away from private vehicles, increasing ridership and reducing overall trips in congested zones.
papers.summary: NYC's January 2025 congestion pricing program increased bus and subway ridership above forecasted no-policy demand while modestly reducing overall travel demand. Effects were spatially heterogeneous: transit gains extended beyond Manhattan's core, but overall trip reductions concentrated within the Congestion Relief Zone. Neighborhood-level analysis revealed uneven adaptation across socio-demographic groups, raising equity questions.
papers.method: Time series foundation models generated probabilistic counterfactual demand forecasts with calibrated uncertainty, applied to bus, subway, and aggregate trip volume data post-January 2025 policy implementation.
papers.scalability_note: Congestion pricing is replicable in other major cities (London, Singapore, Stockholm); framework and data sources exist.
papers.cost_note: Congestion pricing is revenue-generating; implementation costs paid by tolls collected. Study uses existing transit and traffic data.
papers.nyc_note: Directly evaluates NYC's live policy (January 2025); findings support continued operation and refinement of the program already in place.
papers.effect_size: Bus and subway ridership increased significantly; overall travel demand decreased modestly
papers.reach: New York City (bus, subway, and aggregate trip volume data)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Significant transit ridership gains and modest overall demand reduction demonstrated, but effects are geographically concentrated; meaningful but not transformative.
papers.underused_note: Congestion pricing itself is rare in US cities (only NYC implemented it), though the transit-shift mechanism is theoretically well-established; limited real-world deployment data available.
papers.novelty_note: Congestion pricing and mode-shift are established policy concepts; the main novelty is using time-series foundation models for counterfactual forecasting rather than the intervention itself.

papers.title: Prison as a Turning Point? The Conditional Nature of Incarceration in Women's Offending Trajectories
papers.authors: Pilar Larroulet, Josefina Rivas, Andrea Canales
papers.abstract: Objectives This study examines incarceration as a turning point in criminal trajectories. It explores how offending behavior changes following incarceration and whether these changes vary based on prior offending patterns and specific prison experiences. Methods Using longitudinal data from 205 incarcerated women, we analyze offending trajectories over a ten-year period—five years before and five years after incarceration. We apply dual group-based trajectory modeling to identify patterns of continuity and change and assess whether transitions differ based on differential prison experiences, including victimization and access to employment. We complement the analysis with C-tests and growth-curve models. Results Continuity in offending is the most common pattern, particularly among individuals with low prior involvement. However, transitions—especially toward lower offending—are observed among those with higher preincarceration offending. Access to employment or training and the lack of visits correspond to transitions to less frequent offending. Conclusions Results are consistent with incarceration as a contingent turning point, with changes in pre- to postincarceration offending varying by prior trajectories and tending to be gradual. Patterns related to prison conditions appear sensitive to selection processes and sample size, warranting replication.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Expand in-prison employment and training programs and facilitate family visitation, as evidence suggests these conditions correlate with reduced post-release offending among women with prior criminal involvement.
papers.summary: The study tracked 205 incarcerated women over ten years (five before, five after incarceration) and found that while most continued offending patterns, those with higher prior involvement showed transitions toward lower offending when they had access to employment/training or lacked regular visits. Prison conditions appear to conditionally moderate recidivism trajectories, though effects are gradual and selection bias is acknowledged.
papers.method: Longitudinal cohort study of 205 incarcerated women analyzed via dual group-based trajectory modeling and growth-curve analysis over a ten-year window.
papers.scalability_note: In-prison employment and visitation policies are implementable across multiple facilities and jurisdictions; logistics are routine.
papers.cost_note: Prison employment programs have modest marginal costs; visitation facilitation is largely administrative; cost-effectiveness unclear from abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates Rikers Island and borough jails; DOC could pilot expanded job training and visitor accommodation. Women's facilities are smaller cohort, making targeted intervention feasible.
papers.effect_size: Transitions toward lower offending observed among those with higher preincarceration offending; access to employment/training and lack of visits correspond to reduced offending frequency
papers.reach: 205 incarcerated women over a ten-year period
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect: employment/training and visitation correlate with reduced post-release offending, but causality unclear and authors acknowledge selection bias; longitudinal but observational design limits confidence.
papers.underused_note: In-prison employment and family visitation programs remain underfunded and inconsistently available in US prisons, especially for women; limited policy uptake despite suggestive evidence.
papers.novelty_note: Framing incarceration as a 'contingent turning point' conditional on prison experiences is moderately fresh; shifts focus from incarceration alone to quality of carceral conditions as levers for change.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Eviction
papers.authors: Leah Hendey
papers.abstract: Preventing evictions benefits both landlords and tenants. For landlords, evictions can cost thousands of dollars. For tenants, an eviction can have consequences that reach far beyond losing their home. Without a stable place to live, families could lose their possessions, be forced to move to a less safe neighborhood, and have a hard time maintaining or finding a job, and children may have to switch schools, compounding families’ stress and financial strain. An eviction can follow the tenant for years, appearing on tenant screening checks, preventing them from finding new housing, and affecting their credit. During the pandemic, a nationwide eviction moratorium was in place, which kept people safely housed and out of congregate settings. Since the federal emergency rental assistance program funds were depleted, DC housing providers have struggled to balance increasing costs and higher rates of unpaid rent. In response, housing providers advocated for speeding up evictions, and
papers.idea: Cities should expand emergency rental assistance and slow eviction timelines to prevent housing instability, which reduces neighborhood displacement, job loss, and associated public safety risks.
papers.summary: The abstract argues that evictions impose substantial costs on both landlords and tenants, with downstream harms to tenant safety, employment, schooling, and credit. During the pandemic, a federal eviction moratorium and rental assistance kept people stably housed; since those funds depleted, DC landlords have sought faster evictions. The piece appears to recommend policy levers to prevent evictions.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief synthesizing evidence on eviction harms and pandemic-era interventions; no original data collection described.
papers.scalability_note: Rental assistance programs and eviction moratoriums are standard municipal/state tools easily replicated across cities.
papers.cost_note: Rental assistance is cost-effective relative to downstream homelessness, incarceration, and health costs, though upfront outlays are significant.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has emergency rental assistance and Housing Court; abstract's lessons directly applicable to NYC's eviction prevention and rental subsidy policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence that eviction prevention reduces housing instability and associated harms; causality somewhat established but effect sizes vary by context.
papers.underused_note: Emergency rental assistance severely underfunded post-pandemic; most cities lack sustained, adequate programs despite demonstrated ROI.
papers.novelty_note: Framing eviction prevention as public-safety intervention is increasingly recognized but remains secondary to housing advocates' primary framing.

papers.title: Latent Classes of Baseline Mental Health and Substance Use Symptoms Predict 3-Month Health and Well-Being Outcomes Among Drug Court Participants
papers.authors: Malaiikha N. Mccormick-Cisse, D. Lynn Homish, Alison M. Haney, Kyler S. Knapp, Jessica A. Kulak, Linda S. Kahn, Gregory G. Homish
papers.abstract: Drug treatment courts (DTCs) serve diverse populations with substance use and co-occurring mental health conditions. These conditions may impact recovery capital (RC) and quality of life (QoL); however, limited research has examined how patterns of substance use and mental health symptoms influence these outcomes in DTC settings. Two waves of data were used from DTC participants ( N = 165). Latent class analysis (LCA) classified participants based on substance use and mental health symptoms. Four unique classes of DTC participants emerged. These classes were then used to predict RC and QoL. Class membership was able to significantly predict QoL but not RC over time. This study is among the first to use LCA to determine DTC subgroups and predict QoL outcomes. Results further establish the variability of DTC participants’ profiles and emphasize the need for tailored treatment approaches.
papers.idea: Drug treatment courts should adopt risk-stratified, tailored treatment protocols based on baseline mental health and substance use symptom profiles to improve participant quality of life and outcomes.
papers.summary: The study used latent class analysis on 165 drug court participants to identify four distinct subgroups based on substance use and mental health symptom patterns. Class membership significantly predicted quality-of-life outcomes at 3 months, though not recovery capital, suggesting that matching treatment intensity and approach to symptom profiles could improve results.
papers.method: Observational longitudinal study using latent class analysis on two waves of data from drug court participants to classify subgroups and predict outcomes over 3 months.
papers.scalability_note: Drug courts exist in all 50 states and NYC operates multiple DTCs; LCA-based risk stratification is administratively feasible to implement city- or statewide.
papers.cost_note: Requires modest clinical assessment infrastructure and staff training; no per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC criminal justice system includes operational drug courts (e.g., Brooklyn DTC, Manhattan DTC) that could adopt tailored protocols; fits existing diversion infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Significant prediction of quality-of-life outcomes at 3 months; failed to predict recovery capital, suggesting mixed but meaningful effects.
papers.underused_note: Risk stratification and tailored protocols are endorsed but rarely implemented systematically in most U.S. drug courts, which tend toward one-size-fits-all models.
papers.novelty_note: Latent class segmentation is methodologically novel for drug courts, but the core concept of matching treatment intensity to risk/need is established; framing via mental health + substance profiles is somewhat fresh.

papers.title: Public opinion and prosecutor-initiated resentencing in California: Can a racial equity norms message increase support?
papers.authors: Jessie Harney, Lauren Woods
papers.abstract: Prosecutors, as elected officials, are theoretically responsive to public opinion. Evidence suggests that messaging interventions may impact support for justice system reform. However, despite lengthy and racially disparate criminal sentences in the United States and empirical evidence demonstrating the ability of social norms messaging to shift public opinion, the impact of these interventions on attitudes toward sentencing reform has not been tested. In this paper, we test the impact of a social norms message—namely that most Californians believe the justice system to be biased against Black Americans—via a survey experiment of 2,517 Californians on their attitudes toward Second Look (SL) sentencing and Prosecutor-Initiated Resentencing (PIR). Though directionally, we see causal increases in support for SL & PIR, this effect is not statistically significant overall. However, supplementary analyses suggest that exposure to this social norm significantly increased support for SL & PIR among Californian Republicans.
papers.idea: Use social-norms messaging about racial bias in the justice system to increase prosecutor and public support for resentencing reform.
papers.summary: The study tested whether messaging that 'most Californians believe the justice system is biased against Black Americans' would shift public support for Second Look sentencing and Prosecutor-Initiated Resentencing via a survey experiment of 2,517 Californians. Results showed directionally positive but not statistically significant effects overall, though the message did significantly increase support among California Republicans.
papers.effect_size: No statistically significant effect overall; significant effect among Republicans (magnitude not quantified in abstract)
papers.method: Survey experiment of 2,517 Californians testing exposure to a social-norms message about public perception of racial bias in the justice system.
papers.scalability_note: Messaging interventions are cheap to scale once designed, but this one showed weak overall effects, limiting confidence in roll-out.
papers.cost_note: Survey-based messaging interventions are low-cost to administer once developed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has different political composition and prosecutorial landscape than California; weak effects in the study limit practical interest for local DA or city agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Directional increase but not statistically significant overall; meaningful only for Republican subgroup.
papers.underused_note: Social-norms messaging on criminal justice is emerging but underdeployed at scale; prosecutor engagement strategies remain limited despite their gatekeeping power.
papers.novelty_note: Applies established social-norms intervention technique to a less-tested domain (sentencing reform messaging); targets prosecutors as opinion-responsive actors rather than just public persuasion.

papers.title: Do Socio-Emotional Skills Pay Off and Can They Be Developed Through Training ? A Meta-Analysis
papers.abstract: This paper synthesizes the growing evidence on the labor market returns to socio-emotional skills and assesses the effectiveness of training programs designed to develop these skills. While existing studies document elevated re-turns to socio-emotional skills in the labor market, there is little consensus on which specific aspects of the skills drive these gains. Using one of the established psychological measures, the Big Five personality traits (the Big Five), the paper defines relevant keywords for socio-emotional skills, conducts web scraping of the literature, and constructs a database of coefficients on returns and training impacts. DerSimonian and Laird random-effects estimates suggest that a one standard deviation increase in each of the Big Five traits is associated with a 1.4 to 3.1 percent increase in real wages, with substantial heterogeneity across individual traits, demographic groups, and country income levels. Among the Big Five, lower agreeableness, often associated with assertiveness and being able to challenge consensus, exhibits the highest wage returns (3.1 percent). Training programs improve the Big Five traits by an average of 0.46 standard deviation, with larger effects for attitude-related traits such as openness (0.67 standard deviation) and conscientiousness (0.40 standard deviation). Although early childhood constitutes a critical period for cognitive skill formation, the evidence presented in this paper indicates that socio-emotional competencies remain markedly malleable in later developmental stages. Well-designed post-secondary interventions can generate substantial gains.
papers.idea: Cities could integrate evidence-based socio-emotional skills training (targeting openness, conscientiousness, and assertiveness) into post-secondary workforce development and adult education programs to improve employment outcomes and earnings.
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of labor market returns to Big Five personality traits found that a one standard deviation increase in socio-emotional skills is associated with 1.4–3.1% higher real wages, with lower agreeableness (assertiveness) showing the highest returns. Training programs improved Big Five traits by an average of 0.46 standard deviations, with larger gains in openness (0.67 SD) and conscientiousness (0.40 SD), suggesting these competencies remain malleable beyond early childhood.
papers.effect_size: 1.4–3.1% wage increase per 1 SD gain in Big Five traits; training programs improve traits by 0.46 SD on average
papers.method: Meta-analysis using systematic web scraping of published literature and DerSimonian-Laird random-effects estimation of coefficients on wage returns and training impacts across studies.
papers.scalability_note: Skills training is readily deployable across multiple adult education and workforce programs; model is replicable but requires institutional design and sustained delivery.
papers.cost_note: Training program costs not specified in abstract; socio-emotional skills interventions are typically moderate-cost relative to other education programs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive adult education (CUNY, workforce boards) and workforce programs through agencies like DOE and HRA that could integrate these trainings; aligned with economic mobility goals.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate but meaningful: 1.4–3.1% wage returns per SD increase; training achieves ~0.46 SD gains. Real but modest in absolute terms; heterogeneity across groups limits generalizability.
papers.underused_note: Strong meta-analytic evidence for malleable skills and measurable ROI, yet socio-emotional training remains peripheral in most US adult workforce programs compared to credential/technical focus.
papers.novelty_note: Not entirely new (soft skills long discussed), but rigorous Big Five framing and quantified wage returns offer fresher evidence base than typical program marketing; assertiveness-as-returns is slightly counterintuitive.

papers.title: Translating Science into Practice Using University-State Government Partnerships: The EPIS Model
papers.authors: Janet A. Welsh, Jochebed G. Gayles, Sarah M. Chilenski, Roger Spaw, Michael Pennington, Mark T. Greenberg
papers.abstract: Abstract For more than two decades, the Edna Bennett Pierce Prevention Research Center (PRC) at Penn State University has partnered with state governmental agencies in Pennsylvania to promote the adoption and successful implementation of evidence based prevention programs by schools and community organizations. This translational arm of the PRC, known as EPIS (Evidence based Prevention and Intervention Support) provides implementation and evaluation support to prevention stakeholders throughout Pennsylvania. The EPIS collaboration involves prevention scientists and implementation specialists from the PRC, as well as agency personnel from multiple Pennsylvania state agencies. This partnership began by supporting the implementation of 10 evidence based interventions (EBIs) from the Blueprints for Violence Prevention program registry and has evolved in response to state partners’ requests that EPIS provide support broadly and equitably across Pennsylvania. Here we present the current EPIS tiered model of implementation support. This approach begins with light touch support designed for a broad reach to the prevention and intervention workforce and becomes increasingly intensive at higher tiers. Data show widespread reach of TA activities across Pennsylvania’s prevention workforce, including over 1,200 online learners from diverse sectors, thousands of group training attendees, and tens of thousands of youths served through EBI delivery. Metrics indicate sustained growth in engagement, high participant satisfaction, improvements in program fidelity and participant program completion rates, and improvements in youth behavioral outcomes over more than a decade of partnership activity. Challenges identified and lessons learned are discussed, leading to five best practice recommendations to continue building prevention infrastructure.
papers.idea: Establish a university–city government partnership modeled on EPIS to provide tiered implementation support for evidence-based violence prevention programs across NYC schools and community organizations.
papers.summary: The EPIS model, a 20+ year university–state partnership at Penn State, provides implementation and evaluation support for evidence-based prevention programs (including violence prevention) to schools and community organizations across Pennsylvania. The tiered approach ranges from light-touch online training to intensive on-site support, reaching over 1,200 online learners, thousands in group trainings, and tens of thousands of youth, with reported improvements in program fidelity, completion rates, and youth behavioral outcomes.
papers.reach: Over 1,200 online learners, thousands of group training attendees, tens of thousands of youths served across Pennsylvania
papers.method: Descriptive case study of a partnership model operated by Penn State's Edna Bennett Pierce Prevention Research Center and Pennsylvania state agencies; metrics tracked over more than a decade on reach, satisfaction, fidelity, completion rates, and youth outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Model is designed for statewide reach and has proven replicable; NYC could adapt it with local universities and agencies.
papers.cost_note: Abstract does not quantify costs; university–government partnerships typically require modest ongoing investment but leverage existing institutional capacity.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has strong university research centers (Columbia, NYU, CUNY) and active Department of Education and community-based organization networks; the model aligns with existing DOE and CUNY initiatives.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrated sustained improvements in fidelity, completion rates, and youth behavioral outcomes across 1,200+ learners and tens of thousands of youth over 10+ years; robust longitudinal data from real-world implementation.
papers.underused_note: NYC has fragmented prevention infrastructure; no equivalent university-city partnership model for systematic EBI scale-up across schools and community orgs despite demonstrated success in PA.
papers.novelty_note: Tiered implementation-support model is sound but not entirely new; value lies in translation infrastructure and sustained partnership structure, which is underdeployed in NYC context.

papers.title: Oklahoma Shows How Affordable Housing Can Also Be an Economic Driver
papers.authors: Jorge González-Hermoso
papers.abstract: Solving the current housing crisis requires that all governments work together to produce and preserve affordable housing. But public agencies are often balancing how to allocate their scarce dollars in ways that address housing shortages, especially those of affordable units, with other policy goals, like strengthening local economies. Our new report shows the decision doesn’t have to be a trade-off. Analyzing 45 recent multifamily housing projects in Oklahoma that received low-income housing tax credit (LIHTC) equity, we found that financing the production and rehabilitation of affordable housing also delivers economic benefits alongside its core social mission. These projects are associated with the creation of more than 4,000 jobs and over $800 million in economic output, and for every $1 of public investment, 43 cents flow back to public coffers through tax revenues. Oklahoma’s experience makes a compelling case for other localities: Investing in affordable housing doesn’t just provide the shelter
papers.idea: Use low-income housing tax credit (LIHTC) financing to develop and rehabilitate affordable multifamily housing as a dual strategy that simultaneously addresses housing shortage and generates local economic activity and tax revenue.
papers.summary: Analysis of 45 LIHTC-financed multifamily projects in Oklahoma found they generated over 4,000 jobs and $800 million in economic output, while returning 43 cents per public dollar invested through tax revenues. The study demonstrates that affordable housing production need not trade off against economic development goals.
papers.method: Retrospective analysis of 45 completed multifamily housing projects in Oklahoma that received LIHTC equity, measuring employment creation, economic output, and tax revenue generation.
papers.scalability_note: LIHTC is a federal program already deployed nationwide; replication requires no new legislative infrastructure, only local strategy and coordination.
papers.cost_note: Strong cost-effectiveness implied by 43-cent return per public dollar; leverages tax credits rather than pure subsidy.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already uses LIHTC extensively; study provides evidence to justify expanded LIHTC deployment and multi-agency coordination around housing-as-economic-driver strategy.
papers.effect_size: 43 cents of tax revenue returned to public coffers for every $1 of public investment
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Substantial demonstrated economic multiplier (43¢ per $1 invested returns to tax coffers); 4,000+ jobs and $800M output across 45 projects is meaningful at scale.
papers.underused_note: LIHTC exists nationwide but rarely leveraged as explicit economic-development tool; most cities treat housing and economic development as separate silos; limited replication of Oklahoma's integrated approach.
papers.novelty_note: Framing affordable housing as economic stimulus is not new, but systematic proof that tax-revenue ROI can offset public cost is a relatively underemphasized angle in policy discourse.

papers.title: Evaluating policy reforms over time: Multiple event time series regression analysis of justice reinvestment in Indiana and Oregon
papers.authors: Christopher W. Dollar
papers.idea: Evaluate whether justice reinvestment policies—redirecting criminal justice spending toward crime prevention and reentry programs—reduce incarceration and improve public safety outcomes.
papers.summary: This study uses multiple event time series regression to assess the effects of justice reinvestment policy reforms implemented in Indiana and Oregon over time. The analysis examines whether these reforms, which reallocate funds from prisons to community-based programs, produce measurable changes in incarceration rates, crime, or reentry outcomes.
papers.method: Multiple event time series regression analysis of justice reinvestment policy reforms in Indiana and Oregon, comparing outcomes over the period of implementation.
papers.scalability_note: Justice reinvestment is a replicable policy framework already adopted by multiple states; NYC could adapt it to its own criminal justice and budget structure.
papers.cost_note: Cost depends on program mix; reinvestment by definition reallocates rather than adds spending, but implementation costs vary.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant control over criminal justice spending (NYPD, DOC, courts, diversion programs); reinvestment models are adaptable to city policy levers.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Justice reinvestment shows moderate promise in reducing incarceration rates, but public safety outcome evidence is mixed and context-dependent across jurisdictions.
papers.underused_note: Several states adopted justice reinvestment frameworks post-2007, but implementation remains incomplete; NYC has not pursued comprehensive reinvestment despite fiscal capacity.
papers.novelty_note: Justice reinvestment is an established policy framework (originated mid-2000s); the evaluation approach is methodologically sound but conceptually conventional.

papers.title: Age at School Entry and Human Capital Development: Evidence from Lesotho
papers.authors: Jan-Walter De Neve, Ramaele Moshoeshoe, Jacob Bor
papers.abstract: Evidence on school-entry age impacts in lower-income countries is limited. We assess how school starting age affects human capital development in Lesotho, exploiting an enrollment age threshold. Children who start primary school at older ages overcome initial skill deficits as they progress. They are more likely to remain in school, spend less time on economic and household activities, and obtain substantially higher total years of schooling. In adulthood they are more likely to have professional occupations and less likely to be married or have children as teenagers, become HIV infected (men), and experience the death of a child (women). (JEL I12, I21, I25, I26, J13, J24, O15)
papers.idea: Implement or adjust school-entry age policies to delay kindergarten enrollment for younger-age cohorts, allowing older entry age to improve educational attainment and reduce adverse life outcomes.
papers.summary: The study exploited Lesotho's enrollment age threshold to show that children entering primary school at older ages experience better long-term outcomes: they catch up academically, stay in school longer, achieve higher total years of schooling, and as adults have better employment and health outcomes (lower teen pregnancy/marriage, lower HIV, lower child mortality). The effect persists and compounds over time.
papers.method: Quasi-experimental design exploiting a school-entry age enrollment threshold in Lesotho to compare cohorts just above and below the cutoff, tracking outcomes through adulthood.
papers.scalability_note: School-entry policy is set at district/system level and applies uniformly; easy to adjust across a school system.
papers.cost_note: No direct cost; policy adjustment to existing school operations; savings may accrue from reduced dropout and better employment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC school-entry age is already regulated by state law (typically age 5 by Sept 1). Study is from Lesotho (very different context, income, educational infrastructure); limited direct policy lever unless state law were reformed.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Substantial impacts on educational attainment, professional employment, and health outcomes (HIV infection, teenage parenthood, child mortality) with consistent effects across multiple life domains.
papers.underused_note: School-entry age policies exist but are inconsistently enforced in US cities; evidence from lower-income contexts is underutilized in NYC policy debates despite relevance to at-risk populations.
papers.novelty_note: School-readiness and entry-age debates are established in education policy; framing as public-health/life-outcome lever is moderately fresh but not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Law enforcement policies for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities: A mixed‐methods analysis
papers.authors: Cooper A. Maher, Brittany E. Hayes, Martha McCullough, Jacquelyn Burckley, Ráchael A. Powers, Amanda K. Simmons, Leigh Anne McKingsley
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDDs) face an elevated risk of victimization relative to their peers. Victims with IDD may view police departmental websites or policies to identify available accommodations in deciding to report their experience. The current study employs a convergent mixed‐methods approach to examine the websites of policing organizations across four states to determine the prevalence and nature of disability‐related policies ( n = 1678), as well as insights from interviews and focus groups with law enforcement professionals ( n = 27). Findings suggested that policies were rare and often did not directly apply to victims with IDD. Coercive processes underlie the formulation of many disability‐related policies. Moreover, professionals often reported agencies not having policies, despite normative processes supporting the need for policies. Although these findings are important, it remains less clear whether agencies’ websites provide accessible information for crime victims with IDD where language access involves more than just translation. Website content, such as clear descriptions of accommodations, may influence victims with IDD decisions to report. However, gaps in implementation and law enforcement's awareness of these policies can significantly hinder the reporting process for victims with disabilities. Policy Implications Findings highlighted the need for greater transparency with respect to police policies. Agencies need to reconsider the utility of existing policies for victims with IDD, if they exist, in terms of accessing support and overcoming communication and access barriers to seeking help. Policies should also be written in plain language and accessible to all victims regardless of disability status.
papers.idea: Mandate that police departments develop and publish plain-language, accessible policies on accommodations for crime victims with intellectual and developmental disabilities, and train officers on their implementation.
papers.summary: The study examined 1,678 police department websites across four states and interviewed 27 law enforcement professionals to assess disability-related policies for victims with IDD. Findings showed that relevant policies were rare, often inaccessible or poorly implemented, and many agencies lacked them entirely despite normative support for such policies.
papers.method: Convergent mixed-methods study combining website content analysis (n=1,678 departments across four states) with interviews and focus groups with law enforcement professionals (n=27).
papers.scalability_note: Policy development and web transparency are standardized municipal functions easily replicated across departments and jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Primarily requires policy drafting, staff training, and website updates—low-cost relative to other public safety interventions.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and other NYC agencies can directly implement plain-language accessible policies on victim accommodations; NYC has strong disability advocacy infrastructure to support compliance.
papers.effect_size: Policies were rare and often did not directly apply to victims with IDD
papers.reach: 1,678 policing organization websites across four states and 27 law enforcement professionals
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents a real gap (rare, inaccessible policies) but measures prevalence/barriers rather than testing an intervention's actual impact on reporting or victim outcomes.
papers.underused_note: IDD victim accommodation in policing is severely underfunded and largely absent from NYC/major-city police reform agendas; minimal competitive pressure to adopt.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes police-disability interaction through accessibility and plain language rather than enforcement compliance; somewhat fresh but grounded in established disability-rights principles.

papers.title: Bounded Engagement and Institutional Interdependency: Exploring Formerly Incarcerated Adults’ (Non)Engagement Experiences and Perceptions
papers.authors: Megan Denver, Oscar Navarro, Rod K. Brunson
papers.abstract: Objectives Quantitative studies of institutional (non)engagement are limited by available measures and temporal ordering concerns. Using unique and detailed qualitative data, we examine (non)engagement behaviors and perceptions among formerly incarcerated adults. Methods We conducted around 100 face-to-face interviews to elicit rich information about an array of behaviors and perceptions for three key formal institutions: financial, medical, and employment. Results Almost everyone in our sample was at least partially engaged post-release and experienced bounded engagement as they navigated the option set available to them. When people were not engaged, common reasons included insufficient funds to meet bank requirements or institutional or logistical barriers to access, which align with an administrative burden perspective. Concerns about wage garnishment, a system avoidance explanation, were also raised; however, avoidance was rare and contained to the relevant institution(s). We also observed institutional interdependency, where barriers encountered with one institution could prevent engagement in another. Conclusions Reducing or removing barriers to healthcare insurance and access, minimum balance requirements, and burdens placed on job applicants with criminal records can improve institutional engagement. Alleviating burdens in one domain (e.g., formal identification or schedule restrictions) may also improve access in other institutions (such as formal employment or bank accounts).
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Remove administrative barriers to financial services, healthcare access, and employment for people with criminal records—such as minimum balance requirements, wage-garnishment fears, and criminal-record hiring restrictions—to improve institutional engagement and reduce recidivism risk factors.
papers.summary: A qualitative study of ~100 formerly incarcerated adults found most were partially engaged with financial, medical, and employment institutions post-release, but encountered bounded engagement due to insufficient funds, administrative barriers, and fear of wage garnishment. Barriers in one domain (e.g., lack of ID or schedule constraints) often prevented engagement in others, suggesting institutional interdependency.
papers.method: Approximately 100 face-to-face interviews with formerly incarcerated adults exploring their engagement behaviors and perceptions across three formal institutions (financial, medical, employment).
papers.scalability_note: Policy changes to bank requirements, healthcare enrollment, and hiring practices are city/state-level levers with wide applicability.
papers.cost_note: Most interventions (removing minimum balance requirements, streamlining ID processes, banning-the-box hiring reforms) impose minimal or negative cost; healthcare access improvements require budget.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large reentry population, municipal control over hiring, and partnerships with banks/health systems; directly actionable via DOC, CCRB, HRA, and workforce programs.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study identifies plausible barriers and mechanisms; no experimental effect size demonstrated, but clear causal pathway documented through participant accounts.
papers.underused_note: Criminal-record hiring restrictions persist widely; banking minimum-balance requirements unchanged; wage-garnishment reform limited. Few jurisdictions systematically remove these barriers despite clear reentry policy focus.
papers.novelty_note: Administrative-burden lens is somewhat fresh; institutional interdependency framing is less common in reentry literature, though barrier removal itself is not new.

papers.title: Changing time of death after major trauma in Scotland
papers.authors: Declan Robert Bradley, Dean Kerslake, Ralph BouHaidar
papers.abstract: Background Classic trauma mortality models, including Trunkey’s trimodal distribution and Wyatt’s 1995 Lothian study, reported most major trauma deaths occurring within the first hour after injury. Recent UK data suggest a shift in trauma demographics towards older patients and low-energy mechanisms, which may alter established mortality timelines. This study aimed to provide the first update of trauma fatality time frames in Scotland. Methods We conducted a retrospective observational study of all patients aged ≥12 years who died from major trauma (Injury Severity Score ≥16) in the Lothian region of Scotland between 1 February 2022 and 31 January 2024. Data were obtained from the Edinburgh University Forensic Pathology database and the Scottish Trauma Audit Group registry. Time from injury to death was categorised as: within 1 hour, 1–4 hours or >4 hours. Mechanism, intent, age and sex were also analysed. Results A total of 208 major trauma deaths were identified. Overall, 67 (32.2%) occurred within 1 hour, 15 (7.2%) between 1 and 4 hours and 126 (60.6%) after 4 hours. Same-level falls accounted for 68 deaths (33%). Patients aged >65 years comprised 54% of the cohort; in this subgroup, late deaths were most common. Conclusion The distribution of trauma deaths in Lothian has shifted markedly since 1995, with fewer immediate deaths and more late in-hospital fatalities, driven largely by same-level falls in older adults. These findings underline the need for trauma systems to focus on targeted prevention and management strategies for elderly patients at risk of late complications.
papers.idea: Develop and implement targeted fall-prevention programs and in-hospital care protocols for older adults, informed by epidemiological data showing that 54% of trauma deaths now occur >4 hours post-injury, mainly from same-level falls in patients >65 years.
papers.summary: This Scottish study found that trauma mortality patterns have shifted since 1995: only 32% of major trauma deaths now occur within 1 hour, while 61% occur >4 hours post-injury. Same-level falls account for one-third of deaths, disproportionately affecting adults over 65, suggesting that late in-hospital complications (not immediate blood loss) are now the dominant cause of preventable deaths.
papers.effect_size: 61% of deaths now occur >4 hours post-injury (vs. historically concentrated in first hour); 54% of decedents were age >65
papers.reach: 208 major trauma deaths in Lothian region, Scotland, 2022–2024
papers.method: Retrospective observational study of all patients aged ≥12 years who died from major trauma (ISS ≥16) in Lothian between February 2022 and January 2024, using forensic pathology and trauma audit registry data.
papers.scalability_note: Fall-prevention and geriatric trauma protocols are evidence-based, low-tech, and readily adapted across health systems; data collection methods are standard.
papers.cost_note: Fall prevention (home modifications, PT, balance training) and optimized in-hospital protocols are relatively low-cost per person and often reduce costly emergency/ICU stays.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has aging population, high falls burden, and mature trauma centers; findings directly applicable to FDNY/EMS protocols and hospital quality initiatives; requires coordination with NYC Health + Hospitals and senior service agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Epidemiological shift is well-documented but study is descriptive; no intervention tested. Implies moderate actionable opportunity if prevention/protocols are developed.
papers.underused_note: Fall prevention and geriatric trauma protocols exist but are fragmented; this specific epidemiological insight (54% late deaths, same-level falls in elderly) is not yet driving coordinated city-wide or health-system interventions in NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes trauma mortality away from 30-year-old trimodal model; highlights preventable late in-hospital deaths rather than emergency-room heroics—shifts focus to chronic/system failures.

papers.title: The Effects of RAD Conversion on Eviction Patterns
papers.authors: Elizabeth Lochhead, Peter Hepburn, Ingrid Gould Ellen
papers.idea: Evaluate Rental Assistance Demonstration (RAD) conversion programs as a policy tool to reduce evictions by converting public housing to project-based vouchers.
papers.summary: This study examines how RAD conversion—a HUD program that converts public housing to private management with project-based rental assistance—affects eviction rates. The research measures whether this policy intervention changes tenant displacement patterns.
papers.method: Comparative analysis of eviction patterns before and after RAD conversion; likely uses administrative eviction records and housing data (specific sample size and precise methodology unknown without abstract).
papers.scalability_note: RAD is a federally administered program; findings could inform HUD policy and replication across cities.
papers.cost_note: RAD involves federal capital investment and restructuring of subsidy mechanisms; cost-effectiveness depends on eviction reduction magnitude.
papers.nyc_note: NYC NYCHA is the largest public housing authority in the US and actively uses RAD conversion; findings directly inform local policy decisions.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: RAD conversions show moderate promise for housing stability, but eviction-specific outcome data is limited; effects depend heavily on implementation quality and local market conditions.
papers.underused_note: RAD is a federal HUD program with growing uptake, but eviction-reduction framing and rigorous local evaluation remain scarce; NYC has converted some stock but lacks targeted eviction-outcome studies.
papers.novelty_note: RAD itself is established policy (since 2012); framing it as an eviction-reduction lever is sensible but not particularly counterintuitive.

papers.title: The wider network of social relationships and desistance from crime
papers.authors: Peggy C. Giordano, Jennifer E. Copp, Megan Swinehart, Wendy D. Manning, Monica A. Longmore
papers.abstract: Abstract Prior research has focused on marriage as a key relationship associated with crime cessation. Yet particularly within the contemporary context, relationships with parents, peers, and other family members may also foster or inhibit progress toward desistance. We rely on a symbolic interactionist perspective on social learning that not only centers on the behaviors of these significant others but foregrounds the role of communication processes within the context of these close ties. Drawing on data from a 22‐year longitudinal study, results of mixed effects models indicate that partner and peer criminal behavior are linked to within‐individual variations in crime, net of traditional marriage indicators. Additionally, parents’ early antisocial behavior was a modest between‐subjects predictor, and a composite measure capturing behaviors across the full network suggests a negative effect of “network encapsulation.” In‐depth interviews ( n = 99) with a subset of respondents, parents, and partners identify four specific dynamics related to communication across these relationships that may influence perspectives and behavioral outcomes: (1) co‐constructing meanings, (2) buttressing specific hooks for change, (3) presenting possibilities for a united front, and (4) advancing cognitive contrasts. Results highlight the importance of attending to relationship assets and challenges as individuals attempt to accomplish a consistent pattern of desistance.
papers.idea: Cities could design desistance-support programs that strengthen communication and coordination across an individual's social network (parents, peers, partners) rather than focusing narrowly on marriage, to reinforce behavioral change and reduce reoffending.
papers.summary: A 22-year longitudinal study found that peer and partner criminal behavior strongly predict within-individual crime variations, and that family communication dynamics—particularly co-constructing meaning, buttressing change hooks, presenting unified fronts, and cognitive contrasts—are associated with crime desistance. Network-wide 'encapsulation' (isolation) had negative effects, suggesting that coordinated social support across multiple relationships facilitates sustained crime cessation.
papers.method: Mixed-effects regression on 22-year longitudinal panel data plus in-depth qualitative interviews (n=99) with respondents, parents, and partners to identify communication mechanisms.
papers.scalability_note: Requires training case managers and coordination across social networks; not as scalable as single-point interventions but feasible for reentry/probation caseloads.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost; would require dedicated staff for coordination and communication facilitation; cheaper than incarceration but more costly than surveillance-only approaches.
papers.nyc_note: NYC probation, parole, and reentry agencies (DOCCS, DOP) already work with clients and families; could integrate structured network-communication protocols into existing programs.
papers.effect_size: Partner and peer criminal behavior linked to within-individual variations in crime; network encapsulation shows negative effect on desistance
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect; longitudinal study shows peer/partner criminal behavior linked to within-individual crime variation, but effect sizes not dramatic. Qualitative findings suggestive but not quantified.
papers.underused_note: Most reentry/desistance programs focus on individual counseling or marriage alone; multi-actor network coordination approach is rare in US city practice despite evidence base.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes desistance support from marriage-centric to whole-network communication model; emphasizes co-construction and symbolic interaction rather than just relationship status.

papers.title: Specialized Units in the Successful Prosecution of Domestic Violence Charges
papers.authors: Sophia Shaiman, Christi Metcalfe, Barbara Koons-Witt
papers.idea: Establish specialized domestic violence prosecution units within district attorney offices to improve case outcomes and victim safety.
papers.summary: The study examines whether dedicated domestic violence prosecution units improve charging and conviction rates in DV cases compared to generalist prosecutors. Specialized units represent a structured prosecutorial intervention aimed at better handling of intimate partner violence cases.
papers.method: Unable to determine from title and missing abstract; likely empirical comparison of jurisdictions with vs. without specialized DV units, but design specifics unknown.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial structure easily replicated across DA offices in other cities and states.
papers.cost_note: Requires staff reallocation/hiring within existing DA budgets; moderate operational cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD and Manhattan/Brooklyn/Queens/Bronx/Staten Island DAs could directly adopt specialized DV prosecution units.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence; specialized prosecution units show improvements in case outcomes and conviction rates, but effects vary by jurisdiction and implementation quality.
papers.underused_note: Some large cities have DV units, but inconsistently resourced and not universally adopted; most jurisdictions lack dedicated specialized prosecution capacity.
papers.novelty_note: Established best practice in criminal justice reform circles; specialized prosecution is conventional thinking, though underdeployed relative to its evidence base.

papers.title: Does Long‐Term Care Subsidy Decrease Healthcare Utilization and Expenditures? Evidence From the Long‐Term Care Insurance in Korea
papers.authors: GaYoung Park, Youjin Hahn
papers.abstract: ABSTRACT We examine the effect of government subsidies for long‐term care (LTC) on healthcare utilization and health outcomes in Korea, which exhibits one of the fastest rates of population aging. Using a regression discontinuity design, our intent‐to‐treat estimates indicate that eligibility for subsidized LTC services reduces out‐of‐pocket medical and prescription drug spending by about 20% and 16% of the baseline mean, respectively, and lowers the likelihood of inpatient hospital admission by about 12%. These reductions in healthcare utilization and expenditures are not associated with adverse health outcomes. In particular, eligibility reduces the likelihood of hospital admission due to fractures, which could be a possible channel for these results. Overall, our findings suggest that LTC subsidies can partially offset medical expenditures without negatively affecting the health outcomes of older adults.
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Expand government-subsidized long-term care services to reduce preventable hospitalizations and out-of-pocket medical costs for seniors, with potential spillover benefits for emergency department utilization and fracture-related injuries.
papers.summary: The study found that eligibility for subsidized long-term care in Korea reduced out-of-pocket medical spending by ~20%, prescription drug spending by ~16%, and hospital admissions by ~12%, without worsening health outcomes. The effect may operate partly through prevention of fall-related fractures in elderly adults.
papers.method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting eligibility cutoff rules in Korea's long-term care insurance program, intent-to-treat analysis of healthcare utilization and spending.
papers.scalability_note: LTC subsidy programs are replicable across jurisdictions with aging populations; program structure portable.
papers.cost_note: Likely cost-effective—subsidies reduce downstream medical spending and hospitalizations, though upfront LTC costs would require budget analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has aging population and existing home care programs, but the Korean program model would require integration with Medicaid/Medicare structures and political will for subsidy expansion.
papers.effect_size: 20% reduction in out-of-pocket medical spending, 16% reduction in prescription drug spending, 12% lower likelihood of inpatient hospital admission
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate, consistent reductions (12-20%) in hospitalizations and spending without adverse health outcomes, but Korea's healthcare context may not fully transfer to US.
papers.underused_note: Long-term care is severely underfunded in US; most seniors lack affordable in-home or facility care. NYC has minimal subsidized LTC relative to need.
papers.novelty_note: Preventive care via LTC support is well-established theory; the Korea-specific RDD evidence is rigorous but the concept itself is conventional.

papers.title: Evaluating the impact of New York’s bail reform on recidivism: a difference-in-differences study
papers.authors: Stephen Koppel, René Ropac
papers.idea: Evaluate whether New York's bail reform policy (eliminating cash bail for most misdemeanors and non-violent felonies) reduces recidivism and public safety risk, informing whether to expand, modify, or maintain the policy.
papers.summary: Unable to determine findings—no abstract provided. This study uses difference-in-differences methodology to measure recidivism outcomes following New York State's 2019 bail reform, which eliminated pretrial detention for most charges.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences quasi-experimental design comparing recidivism trends in New York before and after bail reform, likely using administrative criminal justice data.
papers.scalability_note: Bail reform is state-level policy; findings are NYC/NY-specific but other states/cities have adopted similar reforms.
papers.cost_note: Eliminates cost of pretrial detention; study itself is low-cost secondary analysis of existing data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is the primary implementation site of NY bail reform; directly informs whether current policy is meeting public safety and criminal justice goals.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Bail reform's effect on recidivism is contested; studies show modest or mixed impacts on reoffending, with tradeoffs between pretrial release rates and public safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: This evaluation idea is already well-resourced and actively studied by policy institutes, courts, and legislatures; multiple rigorous evaluations of NY bail reform already exist or are underway.
papers.novelty_note: Evaluating bail reform impact via difference-in-differences is conventional empirical policy analysis; the framing is standard public-safety risk assessment, not a fresh perspective.

papers.title: Decarceration and…? Local Policy Responses to State-Level Criminal Justice Reform
papers.authors: Michaela I. Cushing-Daniels
papers.abstract: In response to the socioeconomic harms caused by hyperincarceration and public demands for reforms, efforts have emerged to reduce the number of people who are incarcerated in the United States. Criminal justice reforms are complex, requiring coordination across multiple levels of government and thousands of public and private actors. This paper investigates whether and how the design of such reforms generates feedback effects across governments by exploring how local policymakers in California responded to Public Safety Realignment. The study documents that cities with higher shares of arrestees increased their social policy expenditures post-Realignment, suggesting the policy's design may have contributed to substantive feedback effects between the state and local governments. The findings contribute to the broader discourse on the political status of justice-involved populations and the diffusion of policy priorities, indicating a potential shift toward a more holistic approach that considers the root causes of criminality.
papers.idea: When state criminal justice reforms shift responsibility to local governments, use the resulting fiscal pressure as a trigger to systematically expand social services (mental health, substance abuse, job training, housing support) as an alternative to arrest and incarceration.
papers.summary: This study examined how California cities responded to Public Safety Realignment, a state reform that shifted some criminal justice costs to counties and cities. Cities with higher arrest rates increased their social policy expenditures post-Realignment, suggesting that the fiscal incentive created by reform design prompted a shift toward addressing root causes of crime rather than purely enforcement-focused responses.
papers.method: Observational analysis of local policy responses in California cities following Public Safety Realignment, comparing social expenditure changes in cities stratified by arrest rates.
papers.scalability_note: Model is portable to any state/city system; requires state-level reform trigger but logic applies across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Requires upfront social service expansion; cost depends on scope and baseline funding but may be offset by reduced incarceration/enforcement costs long-term.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates many social services; applicability depends on whether state would shift criminal justice costs to city, creating fiscal incentive; less clear absent such realignment.
papers.effect_size: Cities with higher shares of arrestees increased their social policy expenditures post-Realignment
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect: demonstrates correlation between fiscal pressure and increased social spending post-Realignment, but causal mechanisms unclear and effects not uniformly distributed.
papers.underused_note: Strategic use of state reform-induced fiscal pressure to trigger systematic social service expansion is rarely intentional policy design; most cities react ad-hoc rather than systematically.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes decarceration not as police/prison constraint but as fiscal/administrative opportunity to build social service infrastructure—shifts lens from what to reduce to what to build.

papers.title: Why Police Pull Back? Organizational Injustice and Depolicing
papers.authors: Gabriel Béliveau, Massimiliano Mulone, Maude Pérusse-Roy
papers.abstract: Depolicing refers to a perceived decline in police engagement, particularly in proactive enforcement. Although widely invoked in political and media debates, the concept remains theoretically and empirically contested. Existing research suggests that overall police activity remains relatively stable and that only a minority of officers report reducing their own interventions, raising questions about the origins of disengagement narratives. Drawing on an organizational justice framework, this study analyzes 59 in-depth interviews with Quebec police officers to examine how officers construct accounts of disengagement through lived experiences of oversight. We identify three interrelated dynamics associated with radical disengagement: employer-initiated internal investigations that constrain blame externalization and disrupt occupational solidarity; precautionary administrative measures that produce stigmatization and isolation; and disciplinary responses to morally framed proactive practices, often interpreted as noble cause corruption. We argue that radical depolicing is rooted less in external scrutiny than in perceived organizational injustice.
papers.idea: Reform internal investigation and disciplinary processes in police departments to reduce perceived organizational injustice, thereby mitigating depolicing and maintaining proactive engagement.
papers.summary: This qualitative study of 59 Quebec police officers found that perceived depolicing is driven primarily by organizational injustice—specifically internal investigations, precautionary administrative measures, and discipline for discretionary enforcement—rather than external scrutiny. Officers' disengagement narratives stem from experiences of blame-shifting, stigmatization, and isolation within their departments, and from disciplinary action against practices they view as morally justified proactive work.
papers.method: Qualitative study of 59 in-depth interviews with Quebec police officers analyzing how officers construct accounts of disengagement through lived experiences of internal oversight.
papers.scalability_note: Organizational reforms to investigation, discipline, and administrative procedures are replicable across police departments; however, culture change requires sustained leadership commitment.
papers.cost_note: Implementation requires redesign of HR/disciplinary processes and training, but no new staffing or technology necessarily required; costs moderate relative to potential public safety gains.
papers.nyc_note: NYC NYPD faces documented depolicing and has active internal investigation/discipline mechanisms (CCRB, IAB); research directly relevant to internal reform levers the city controls.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study identifying mechanisms; demonstrates plausible causal pathway (organizational injustice → depolicing) but lacks experimental validation or quantified outcome measurement.
papers.underused_note: Internal investigation reform is rarely prioritized strategically; most police reform focuses on external oversight, accountability, or training rather than organizational justice and officer retention of proactive work.
papers.novelty_note: Frames depolicing as driven by *internal* organizational fairness rather than external scrutiny or fear of lawsuits—counterintuitive and underexplored in policy discourse.

papers.title: Evaluating the Impact of Rhode Island's Self-Sustaining Reemployment Services and Eligibility Assessment (RESEA) Program on Employment Outcomes
papers.authors: Harrison H Li, Shanna Pearson-Merkowitz, David Yokum
papers.abstract: Prolonged unemployment carries serious economic, health, and wellbeing costs. With federal support, most U.S. states now operate a Reemployment Services and Eligibility Assessment (RESEA) program to help Unemployment Insurance (UI) claimants return to work faster. We report results from a large (N = 23,549) preregistered randomized controlled trial (RCT) evaluating Rhode Island's RESEA program from February 2022 to September 2023. We estimate that selection into the program increased annualized wages by \$1,153, increased reemployment by 1.5 percentage points, and reduced UI duration by nearly two weeks. The vast majority of these wage and reemployment effects appeared within two quarters of claimants' first pay dates and persisted through at least the following year, and we estimate that each dollar spent on the program saved the state \$2.64. Using causal forests, a machine learning technique for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE), we also conduct an exploratory analysis to investigate if there are differential effects of selection into the RESEA program. We find that all participants experienced positive wage benefits from RESEA selection, with particularly large effects for older and lower-income workers.
