Finally, we improve upon prior RESEA evaluations by explicitly controlling for the week of treatment assignment -- a methodological refinement absent from several existing RCTs of job-training programs that is important to eliminate confounding bias. We also discuss ways to harvest precision gains from baseline covariate adjustment without introducing large-sample bias.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt or improve reemployment services and eligibility assessments for unemployment insurance claimants to accelerate job placement and reduce dependency on benefits.
papers.summary: Rhode Island's RESEA program increased annualized wages by $1,153, raised reemployment rates by 1.5 percentage points, and reduced UI duration by ~2 weeks. Effects persisted for at least one year, with a benefit-cost ratio of $2.64 saved per dollar spent. Older and lower-income workers saw particularly large gains.
papers.method: Large preregistered randomized controlled trial (N=23,549) in Rhode Island from February 2022 to September 2023, with wage, employment, and UI administrative data analyzed using causal forests to detect heterogeneous treatment effects.
papers.scalability_note: RESEA is a federal program already deployed in most states; cities/states can adopt or replicate the model with modest adaptation.
papers.cost_note: Program shows positive ROI ($2.64 saved per $1 spent); modest per-claimant cost but abstract does not state absolute cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC participates in New York State's UI system and RESEA programs already; results suggest targeted enhancement or expansion of existing services could improve outcomes.
papers.effect_size: $1,153 annualized wage increase, 1.5 percentage point increase in reemployment, and nearly 2-week reduction in UI duration
papers.reach: 23,549 UI claimants in Rhode Island from February 2022 to September 2023
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest gains: $1,153 annualized wage increase, 1.5pp reemployment boost, 2-week UI reduction. Positive ROI (2.64:1) but small per-person impact.
papers.underused_note: RESEA programs already exist in most states including NY; this validates rather than introduces new practice. Implementation fidelity and uptake vary, but infrastructure is established.
papers.novelty_note: Rigorous RCT of existing federal program model. Methodological refinements (causal forests, week-of-assignment controls) are technical; core intervention is conventional.

papers.title: Criminalizing Serious Mental Illness: What Linked Urban Data Reveal and the Case for Community Care
papers.authors: Michelle Suh, Justin Berk, Lauren Brinkley-Rubinstein, Josiah Rich
papers.abstract: Abstract The United States has responded to multiple societal ills, from substance use to homelessness to mental health, with criminalization. A new analysis using City and County of San Francisco data shows jails disproportionately harm people with serious mental illness (SMI). In addition to longer periods of incarceration and higher rates of repeat jail bookings, people with SMI also experience higher rates of homelessness, co-occurring substance use disorder, and urgent and emergent health services utilization before and after incarceration compared to their peers without SMI. The study provides a much-needed spotlight on jail research, as well as demonstrating the power of data when not siloed in multiple, disparate departments. Investing in community-based mental health resources, housing, and substance use disorder services can address the upstream determinants of incarceration and may benefit people with SMI.
papers.idea: Redirect people with serious mental illness away from jail by investing in integrated community-based mental health, housing, and substance use disorder services as upstream alternatives to incarceration.
papers.summary: A San Francisco study using linked administrative data found that people with serious mental illness (SMI) experience longer jail stays, higher recidivism, and greater rates of homelessness and co-occurring substance use disorder than peers without SMI. The analysis demonstrates that criminalization amplifies rather than addresses the underlying drivers of contact with the criminal justice system.
papers.method: Linked analysis of San Francisco City and County administrative data on jail bookings, health services, and homelessness; descriptive comparative analysis of outcomes for people with and without SMI.
papers.scalability_note: Community mental health and housing infrastructure is standard; model applies to any city with linked data capability.
papers.cost_note: Preventive community care typically cheaper than jail incarceration; no per-unit cost provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial mental health, housing, and criminal justice infrastructure; DSM can link NYPD/DOC/HRA/ACS data; directly transferable.
papers.reach: San Francisco city and county data
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Strong descriptive/correlational evidence of disparate jail impact on SMI populations; limited causal intervention data presented, but clear need pathway identified.
papers.underused_note: Despite decades of evidence, most major US cities including NYC still rely heavily on jail/police response to SMI crises; integrated community alternatives remain severely underfunded relative to carceral spending.
papers.novelty_note: Core premise (divert SMI from jail via community care) is well-established in criminology and mental health fields; the novelty lies in linked-data analysis revealing specific harms, not the intervention itself.

papers.title: Uniformed vs. Plainclothes Officers: Stops, Frisks, Searches, Arrests, and Summonses
papers.authors: Mustafa Demir
papers.idea: Deploy plainclothes officers selectively or adjust the ratio of uniformed to plainclothes patrols based on empirical evidence of differential stop, frisk, search, and arrest patterns to reduce pretextual enforcement while maintaining public safety.
papers.summary: This study compares enforcement outcomes (stops, frisks, searches, arrests, summonses) between uniformed and plainclothes police officers. The analysis likely quantifies whether plainclothes officers engage in different patterns of street-level enforcement that could inform decisions about deployment strategies and accountability.
papers.method: Empirical comparison of police enforcement activity by officer uniform status; exact design, data source, jurisdiction, and sample size unknown from title and abstract alone.
papers.scalability_note: Police deployment decisions are routine administrative acts; findings could guide policy in any city.
papers.cost_note: No new spending required—reallocation of existing patrol assignments.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD uses both uniformed and plainclothes units extensively; study directly informs stop-and-frisk and enforcement equity policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents differential enforcement patterns between officer types but provides no causal evidence that changing deployment ratios reduces crime or improves outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Some departments have modified plainclothes/uniformed ratios, but data-driven adjustment based on stop/frisk disparities is not systematized or widely adopted.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes deployment as a lever for reducing pretextual stops; moderately fresh but builds on known officer-type behavioral differences.

papers.title: Cooking, Health, and Daily Exposure to Pollution Spikes
papers.authors: Susanna B. Berkouwer, Joshua T. Dean
papers.abstract: Many routine daily activities—such as cooking and commuting—cause large recurring pollution spikes that may impact health without significantly affecting average exposure. We study pollution spikes by combining experimental variation in cooking technology with high-frequency data on individual pollution exposure and time-use in Kenya. Improved cookstoves reduce PM2.5 spikes while cooking by 51.3 μg/m 3 (41 percent) and cause a 0.24 standard deviation reduction in self-reported respiratory symptoms. However, even after more than three years of daily use, we find no clinical health improvements, possibly because we detect no impact on average exposure. Clinical health improvements may require reductions in ambient concentrations. (JEL I12, O12, O13, Q51, Q53)
papers.idea: NYC could subsidize or mandate improved cookstove distribution in residential buildings to reduce acute air pollution spikes and respiratory symptoms, especially in rent-stabilized or public housing.
papers.summary: A randomized trial in Kenya found that improved cookstoves reduced PM2.5 pollution spikes during cooking by 41% and self-reported respiratory symptoms by 0.24 standard deviations. However, no clinical health improvements were detected after 3+ years of use, likely because the intervention did not reduce ambient (outdoor) pollution exposure.
papers.effect_size: 41% reduction in cooking-related PM2.5 spikes; 0.24 SD reduction in self-reported respiratory symptoms; no clinical health improvement
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial combining experimental variation in cooking technology with high-frequency individual pollution exposure monitoring and time-use data in Kenya.
papers.scalability_note: Requires individual adoption in homes; limited by ambient pollution and behavior compliance after initial rollout.
papers.cost_note: Improved stoves are typically a one-time, modest investment per household; cost-effectiveness depends on device price and durability.
papers.nyc_note: Gas cooking is common in NYC, but most residents already use stoves; NYC's outdoor air quality is the larger driver of respiratory health. Intervention most relevant in buildings with heavy cooking or poor ventilation, but ambient pollution mitigation would likely be more impactful.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 41% reduction in acute PM2.5 spikes and 0.24 SD reduction in respiratory symptoms are meaningful, but absence of clinical health improvements after 3 years limits the claim.
papers.underused_note: Kitchen air quality interventions are rarely prioritized in US housing policy; no major NYC program; evidence exists but not translated to urban rental/public housing contexts.
papers.novelty_note: Focusing on pollution *spikes* rather than average exposure is a fresher angle, but cookstove improvement itself is established; application to NYC housing is somewhat novel.

papers.title: The Association between the 2021 Curfew on Criminal Behavior in Montreal: A Spatio-Temporal Study
papers.authors: A. Rebecca Ostiguy, B. Rémi Boivin
papers.idea: Implement time-based movement restrictions (curfews) as a crime-reduction tool, with spatio-temporal analysis to identify optimal timing and geography.
papers.summary: This study examines whether Montreal's 2021 curfew reduced criminal behavior, using spatial and temporal analysis to assess the policy's effectiveness. The paper likely evaluates crime patterns before and after the curfew was imposed.
papers.method: Spatio-temporal analysis of criminal behavior in Montreal around the 2021 curfew period; no sample size or specific methodology stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Curfews are straightforward to implement across jurisdictions; spatial-temporal methods are replicable.
papers.cost_note: Enforcement costs depend on police deployment; curfews themselves have minimal direct cost relative to crime reduction benefits if effective.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could consider curfews in high-crime areas, though they raise civil liberties concerns and political obstacles; Montreal's results could inform feasibility.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Montreal curfew study likely shows modest or mixed crime effects; pandemic-era curfews generally showed weak causal evidence for crime reduction.
papers.underused_note: Curfews are periodically deployed in US cities (NYC, LA) but not systematized with spatio-temporal targeting; moderately familiar tool.
papers.novelty_note: Spatio-temporal optimization is a refinement, but curfews themselves are conventional enforcement; framing adds some method rigor but not fundamentally new.

papers.title: The Effects of Racial Segregation on Intergenerational Mobility: Evidence from Historical Railroad Placement
papers.authors: Eric Chyn, Kareem Haggag, Bryan A. Stuart
papers.abstract: This paper provides new evidence on the causal impacts of citywide racial segregation on intergenerational mobility. We use an instrumental variable approach that relies on plausibly exogenous variation in segregation due to the arrangement of railroad tracks in the nineteenth century. Our analysis finds that higher segregation reduces upward mobility for Black children from households across the income distribution and White children from low-income households. Moreover, segregation lowers academic achievement while increasing incarceration and teenage birth rates. An analysis of mechanisms shows that segregation reduces government spending, weakens support for antipoverty policies, and increases racially conservative attitudes among White residents. (JEL I38, J13, J15, J62, N32, N72, R30)
papers.idea: Use historical railroad maps to identify segregated neighborhoods and target place-based desegregation or equalization policies.
papers.summary: This study uses 19th-century railroad placement as an instrumental variable to estimate the causal effect of racial segregation on intergenerational mobility. It finds that higher segregation reduces upward mobility for Black children and low-income White children, and increases incarceration and teenage birth rates.
papers.method: Instrumental variable approach using historical railroad track arrangement as exogenous variation in segregation; analysis of intergenerational mobility, academic achievement, incarceration, and teen birth rates.
papers.scalability_note: Historical IV approach is specific to past railroad placement; findings generalize conceptually but intervention levers are not explicit.
papers.cost_note: Desegregation or place-based equalization policies are typically expensive; no cost estimate provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's segregation patterns are shaped by railroads, but study does not propose specific policies NYC could adopt; findings are historical diagnosis, not actionable intervention design.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Causal evidence linking segregation to multiple adverse outcomes (mobility, education, incarceration, births); IV approach using historical instruments strengthens credibility.
papers.underused_note: Segregation is widely documented but place-based desegregation/equalization policies remain underfunded and politically fraught in major US cities including NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Using railroad placement as IV is methodologically creative; core insight (segregation harms mobility) is not new but causal framing and mechanism evidence add nuance.

papers.title: Response, Use of Force, and Dispositional Outcomes: An Exploratory Analysis of the Seattle Police Department’s Crisis Intervention Training on Confirmed Crisis Events
papers.authors: Amanda L. Harrison, Nathan T. Connealy, Brandon Dulisse
papers.abstract: Considering the increased responsibility placed on law enforcement for handling crisis encounters, many departments have implemented crisis intervention training to reduce use of force through de-escalation and increase diversion/referral to community services. Despite the wellintentioned nature of such programming, evidence to date has been both elusive and inconclusive. The purpose of the current study is to investigate the effectiveness of the Seattle Police Department’s crisis intervention program in meeting these intended goals. Utilizing a covariate matching approach, the research questions compare confirmed crisis event responses with and without a crisis-certified officer to assess potential differences in crisis event response profiles, police use of force during crisis encounters, and dispositional outcomes resulting from crisis encounters. The results highlight differences in crisis response likelihood, use of force, and dispositional outcomes between CIT-certified and noncertified officers. Implications for future research, practice, and policy are discussed.
papers.idea: Expand crisis intervention training (CIT) for police officers and deploy CIT-certified responders to mental health and behavioral crisis calls to reduce use of force and increase diversion to services.
papers.summary: This study evaluated Seattle PD's crisis intervention training program by comparing outcomes when CIT-certified versus non-certified officers responded to confirmed crisis events. The analysis found meaningful differences in response profiles, use of force, and dispositional outcomes between the two groups, suggesting CIT certification affects how officers handle mental health and crisis encounters.
papers.method: Observational study using covariate matching to compare crisis event responses by CIT-certified versus non-certified Seattle Police officers, analyzing use of force and dispositional outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: CIT programs are already established in many police departments nationwide and can be adapted; training is replicable across shifts and precincts.
papers.cost_note: CIT requires initial training investment (~40 hours per officer) but uses existing personnel; ongoing cost modest relative to potential reduction in arrests, hospitalizations, and force-related liability.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD already operates crisis intervention initiatives (co-responder programs, mental health training); findings directly relevant to expanding or evaluating those efforts citywide.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study found meaningful differences in use of force and diversion outcomes, but describes itself as 'exploratory' with inconclusive prior evidence; effect sizes not specified.
papers.underused_note: CIT exists in many departments but remains underfunded, inconsistently deployed to mental health calls, and lacks systematic expansion despite decades of recommendations.
papers.novelty_note: CIT is well-established concept (1980s origin); framing as solution is conventional, though rigorous outcome measurement remains sparse.

papers.title: The Anatomy of Scam Scenarios: Large-Scale Characterization and Conversation-Aware Detection
papers.authors: Shang Ma, Chen Yanai, Avichai Ben, Zichen Liu, Yanfang Ye, Xusheng Xiao
papers.abstract: Online scams have become a pervasive global threat, causing substantial financial, psychological, and operational harm. Scammers embed psychological techniques (PTs) within reusable operational schemes to scale scam campaigns with minimal adaptation. However, existing studies often analyze PTs as isolated features, overlooking the recurring scam scenarios in which they are systematically deployed. To address this gap, we first conduct a large-scale empirical study to jointly characterize scam scenarios and their associated PTs. Specifically, we develop a data-driven pipeline to derive a hierarchical taxonomy of scam scenarios, consisting of 18 fine-grained scenarios grouped into 6 high-level tactics based on their PT profiles. Furthermore, to transfer this scenario-level knowledge to practical defense, we design a conversation-aware scam scenario detection approach for financial-institution customer interactions, enabling timely warning and intervention. Our study on 102,054 real-world scam incident reports, spanning 2024-02-01 to 2025-10-31, reveals that PT usage is significantly associated with scam scenarios. We further show that scammers organize scenarios around different operational goals, such as broad victim exposure, high victim conversion, and high-value extraction, and reuse infrastructure, including IP addresses, domains, email addresses, and phone numbers, to launch coordinated campaigns at scale. Evaluation on
papers.idea: Financial institutions and law enforcement could deploy conversation-monitoring systems in customer service interactions to detect and intervene in real-time scam scenarios, using the study's taxonomic framework to flag high-risk exchanges.
papers.summary: Researchers analyzed 102,054 scam incident reports to develop a hierarchical taxonomy of 18 fine-grained scam scenarios grouped into 6 high-level tactics based on psychological manipulation techniques. They found that scammers systematically deploy psychological techniques within reusable operational schemes, and that infrastructure (IPs, domains, emails, phone numbers) is reused across coordinated campaigns. They prototyped a conversation-aware detection system for financial-institution customer interactions.
papers.method: Empirical analysis of 102,054 real-world scam incident reports (Feb 2024–Oct 2025) to build a data-driven taxonomy; development of a conversation-aware detection model for customer interactions.
papers.scalability_note: Detection systems can be integrated into existing customer service platforms across multiple financial institutions and call centers.
papers.cost_note: Primarily software engineering and integration; scales with minimal marginal cost once model is built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has major financial institutions, consumer protection agencies (AG, DCA), and 311; could integrate with fraud-reporting pipelines and train customer-service staff on scenario taxonomy.
papers.reach: 102,054 scam incident reports from February 2024 to October 2025
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Large-scale taxonomy and detection framework demonstrated on real incidents, but study focuses on characterization rather than measuring intervention outcomes or prevention success rates.
papers.underused_note: Conversation monitoring for scam detection is technically feasible and evidence-based, yet rarely deployed in NYC financial institutions or law enforcement; significant privacy-regulatory barriers limit adoption.
papers.novelty_note: Scenario-level taxonomy and conversation-aware detection add structure to scam analysis, but real-time monitoring of customer interactions is not conceptually new; framing around psychological techniques systematically embedded in operational schemes is moderately fresh.

papers.title: Prioritizing Core Factors Needed to Sustain Hospital-Based Violence Intervention Programs: A Delphi Study
papers.authors: Lars Erik Almquist, Sarah Cusworth Walker, Ali Rowhani-Rahbar, Christian D. Helfrich
papers.idea: Identify and operationalize core structural and organizational factors needed to sustain hospital-based violence intervention programs in urban settings.
papers.summary: This Delphi study engaged expert consensus to identify the critical factors required to maintain hospital-based violence intervention programs over time. The research synthesizes practitioner and stakeholder input on sustainability drivers, likely covering staffing, funding, clinical integration, and community partnerships. Results can inform which program elements are essential vs. optional for long-term viability.
papers.method: Delphi study (multi-round expert consensus methodology) with hospital-based violence intervention practitioners and stakeholders; exact sample size and setting not provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Delphi consensus findings on sustainability factors are generalizable across hospital systems and urban contexts.
papers.cost_note: Delphi studies are moderately priced; actual program costs depend on implementation model identified.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has active hospital-based violence intervention programs (e.g., Cure Violence, hospital trauma center programs); findings directly inform expansion and sustainability in existing infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Delphi study identifies success factors but does not measure intervention outcomes; moderate promise based on existing HVP evidence rather than direct effect demonstration.
papers.underused_note: Hospital violence intervention programs exist in select cities but remain underfunded and under-scaled relative to demonstrated effectiveness; sustainability barriers poorly addressed in practice.
papers.novelty_note: Focuses on implementation science and organizational sustainability—less common in violence intervention literature, which typically emphasizes clinical outcomes.

papers.title: Evaluating performances pretrial: The role of defendant performances in prosecutors’ plea bargaining decisions
papers.authors: Chiara Clio Packard
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Existing research on plea bargaining focuses primarily on how fixed case and defendant characteristics, such as the strength of the evidence or the race of the accused, impact plea offers. Less is known about how prosecutors consider a defendant's behavior while their case is pending throughout the plea bargaining process. Drawing on an ethnographic study in two midwestern District Attorney's (DA) offices, this paper reveals how prosecutors evaluate defendants’ performances pretrial as they decide their plea offers. First, I identify four types of pretrial performances that prosecutors consider: abiding by the rules of the court, addressing the harm of the crime, engaging with social services, and demonstrating positive attitude and demeanor. Then, I demonstrate how the process through which performances shape plea offers is dynamic and interactional, as defense attorneys develop assumptions about which performances prosecutors consider and support their clients in meeting those expectations. Policy Implications Defendants with fewer financial resources may face challenges in meeting pretrial performance expectations, leading to inequalities in defendants’ ability to obtain more favorable plea offers. DA's offices should consider only making specific performance demands for services that are readily available to low‐income defendants, such as programs offered for free. Alternatively, prosecutors could decline to prosecute low‐level cases that stem from substance use or mental health issues and encourage law enforcement to make direct referrals to social service agencies instead. Otherwise, this practice risks exacerbating inequalities as more privileged defendants have greater resources to meet the performance demands compared to disadvantaged defendants. More broadly, these findings call for policies that address the underlying inequalities in access to health‐care and social services, which would reduce inequality in defendants’ ability to meet performance demands.
papers.idea: Prosecutors should condition plea-offer improvements only on pretrial performance demands (social services, court compliance) that are genuinely accessible to low-income defendants, or redirect low-level substance and mental health cases to social services rather than prosecution.
papers.summary: An ethnographic study of two midwestern DA offices found that prosecutors systematically evaluate defendants' pretrial behavior—including court compliance, addressing crime harm, engaging social services, and attitude—when setting plea offers. However, defendants with fewer financial resources struggle to meet these performance expectations, creating unequal access to favorable plea deals and compounding existing criminal justice disparities.
papers.method: Ethnographic study conducted in two midwestern District Attorney offices; observational fieldwork examining prosecutor decision-making in plea bargaining negotiations.
papers.scalability_note: Prosecutorial policies and service referral practices are easily standardizable across jurisdictions and require no new technology.
papers.cost_note: Redirecting cases to existing free social services and adjusting plea-offer criteria requires minimal incremental cost; may reduce prosecution and incarceration expenses.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a centralized DA office, robust (if strained) social services infrastructure, and active policy focus on justice-system equity; recommendations align with prosecutorial discretion and existing diversion programs.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Ethnographic study documents real prosecutor behavior and its consequences, but doesn't quantify causal impact on outcomes or recidivism; identifies mechanism rather than measuring effect size.
papers.underused_note: Diversion of low-level substance/mental health cases exists in pockets but remains marginal; most DA offices still use performance conditions as plea leverage without equity safeguards.
papers.novelty_note: Frames prosecutor discretion through pretrial performance fairness lens; moderately fresh angle on plea bargaining, but equity in criminal process is well-trodden ground.

papers.title: Learning to predict violence: Deep learning approaches to violent crime prediction during pretrial release
papers.authors: Matthew DeMichele, Ian A. Silver, William Parkin
papers.abstract: Abstract Research summary This study investigates whether deep learning models—specifically feedforward neural networks—can enhance the prediction of new violent criminal arrests among individuals released pretrial. Using data from a large southeastern county, we evaluate four neural network configurations varying in depth, regularization, and class imbalance adjustments. Results show that incorporating class weighting and threshold tuning notably improves recall of rare violent events, with the best performing model identifying 57% of individuals arrested for violent crimes while maintaining stable false positive (FP) rates. We estimate counterfactual risk estimates among detained individuals, revealing that although some violent events may be averted through detention, this comes at the cost of detaining large numbers of FPs. Policy implications Improving the identification of individuals at risk for violent crime during pretrial release is a critical public safety priority, yet prevailing tools offer limited recall for such rare events. This study shows that neural networks, when properly calibrated, can improve detection of high‐risk individuals without increasing FP rates. However, algorithmic advances alone are insufficient; progress requires modernizing criminal justice IT infrastructures to enable interagency data linkages and real‐time analytics. Most current data systems lack the contextual and behavioral features necessary to capture latent risk. Ultimately, system improvement efforts should move away from mechanisms like monetary bail and toward evidence‐based supervision strategies, paired with investments in data modernization and cross‐agency collaboration to strengthen the predictive foundation of pretrial assessments.
papers.idea: Modernize pretrial risk assessment by deploying calibrated deep learning models paired with data infrastructure and evidence-based supervision (not monetary bail) to improve identification of individuals at genuine high risk for violent crime while reducing unnecessary detention.
papers.summary: The study tested neural network models on a southeastern county dataset to predict violent arrests among pretrial-released individuals. The best model identified 57% of those who would be arrested for violent crimes while maintaining stable false positive rates. The authors note that algorithmic improvement alone is insufficient without modernized IT infrastructure, cross-agency data linkage, and a shift away from monetary bail toward supervision-based alternatives.
papers.method: Observational study using historical county criminal justice data; tested four neural network configurations with varying depth, regularization, and class-weighting strategies on pretrial release outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Requires substantial data infrastructure and interagency coordination; portable across jurisdictions but not trivial to implement.
papers.cost_note: Moderate upfront cost for data modernization and algorithm deployment; ongoing operational cost for supervision infrastructure; savings from reduced unnecessary detention may offset.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has advanced criminal justice IT (e.g., CJIS) and established pretrial agencies (Criminal Justice Agency); however, real-time cross-agency data linkage and shift from bail-heavy practices would require significant institutional coordination.
papers.effect_size: 57% recall of individuals arrested for violent crimes while maintaining stable false positive rates
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 57% recall of violent arrests is moderate improvement over existing tools, but substantial false positives remain; counterfactual analysis shows detention prevents some violence but at high cost.
papers.underused_note: Most jurisdictions still rely on monetary bail and older risk assessment tools; deep learning + evidence-based supervision infrastructure remains rare despite growing evidence base.
papers.novelty_note: Deep learning application is technically novel, but core insight—that better risk prediction + supervision beats detention—is increasingly mainstream in criminal justice reform circles.

papers.title: Does Routine Arming Increase Police Officer-Involved Shootings? Evidence From Australia (1970–2020)
papers.authors: Ross Hendy, Zarina Vakhitova
papers.abstract: This study investigates officer-involved shootings (OIS) in Australia between 1970 and 2020, focusing on the effects of routine firearm carriage policies introduced in New South Wales (1991) and Victoria (1993). Owing to the lack of uniform national reporting of non-fatal shootings, a two-phase data collection strategy was adopted: initial identification through online media archives, followed by validation using gray literature and official reports. This process resulted in a dataset of 581 OIS incidents, including fatal and non-fatal cases. Using this dataset, we employed Bayesian structural time series modeling, and a segmented regression analysis within an Interrupted Time Series analysis to assess the impact of routine carriage policies. Both methods consistently showed that routine carriage policies did not increase OIS rates. These findings provide empirical evidence countering assumptions that increased firearm availability among police necessarily leads to higher OIS rates. The results suggest that under certain conditions, routine firearm carriage can be introduced without exacerbating OIS rates. While identifying the mechanism was outside the scope of this paper, the pattern is consistent with the view that organizational, tactical, and cultural settings, and not firearm availability alone, shape OIS outcomes. Jurisdictions considering the routine arming of police officers should treat it as a multi-component policy change, and pair any changes to firearm policy with context-specific tactical training, professionalization initiatives, and de-escalation protocols. Careful attention to these factors may mitigate risks associated with routine carriage and promote safer outcomes for both officers and the public.
papers.idea: Introduce or revise routine firearm carriage policies for police officers paired with mandatory de-escalation training, tactical protocols, and professionalization initiatives, rather than firearm availability alone.
papers.summary: Using 581 officer-involved shooting incidents across Australia (1970–2020) and analyzing the introduction of routine arming in New South Wales (1991) and Victoria (1993), the study found that routine firearm carriage policies did not increase officer-involved shooting rates. The authors conclude that organizational, tactical, and cultural factors—not firearm availability alone—drive OIS outcomes.
papers.effect_size: No statistically significant increase in officer-involved shooting rates
papers.reach: 581 officer-involved shooting incidents; 50-year period in two Australian states
papers.method: Bayesian structural time series modeling and segmented regression analysis (Interrupted Time Series design) on a dataset of OIS incidents compiled from media archives, gray literature, and official reports across Australia 1970–2020.
papers.scalability_note: Policy design is replicable across jurisdictions; requires training infrastructure and cultural change but no novel technology.
papers.cost_note: Primary costs are training and protocol development; marginal incremental cost if paired with existing police reform efforts.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD already arms most patrol officers routinely. Study's value lies in designing *how* to arm safely (training, culture, tactics), which is applicable, but jurisdiction context and data comparability differ significantly from Australia.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No significant increase in OIS rates found, but study demonstrates absence of harm rather than positive benefit; limited actionability for NYC context.
papers.underused_note: NYPD already routinely arms officers; de-escalation training and tactical protocols are standard (though quality/consistency varies). Policy is not underdeployed in NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes arming as a multi-component intervention requiring training/culture, not just availability—moderately fresh, but de-escalation focus is now mainstream post-2015.

papers.title: Real-World Effectiveness of the Influenza Vaccine in Young Children
papers.authors: Michael L. Anderson, Carlos Dobkin, Devon Gorry, Hung-Fu Tseng
papers.abstract: Influenza causes substantial illness among children. RCTs demonstrate that the influenza vaccine reduces active-surveillance-detected influenza but have insufficient samples to examine outcomes such as health care provider visits. This study documents that children whose well-child visits occur when the seasonal influenza vaccine is broadly available are 23.4 pp more likely to be vaccinated than children whose visits do not. Using large administrative health care datasets, we leverage this vaccination-rate variation to show that the vaccine reduces outpatient and ED visits significantly. The results imply that making pediatric influenza vaccinations more convenient could substantially increase vaccination rates and reduce health care expenditures. (JEL I11, I12, I18, J13)
papers.idea: Coordinate pediatric well-child visit scheduling to align with periods of peak flu vaccine availability, or implement mobile/drop-in flu vaccination clinics at convenient times/locations to increase uptake and reduce preventable illness.
papers.summary: Using variation in flu vaccine availability timing relative to well-child visits, the study found that children vaccinated during high-availability periods had significantly fewer outpatient and emergency department visits. The 23.4 percentage point difference in vaccination rates generated measurable reductions in health care utilization, suggesting that convenience barriers substantially suppress vaccination and increase downstream health care costs.
papers.effect_size: 23.4 pp higher vaccination rate; statistically significant reductions in outpatient and ED visits (magnitudes not quantified in abstract)
papers.method: Quasi-experimental analysis using administrative health care datasets, exploiting natural variation in flu vaccine availability timing relative to well-child visit schedules to identify causal effects on vaccination rates and health outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Vaccine scheduling and clinic coordination are standard municipal health functions; intervention is logistically simple and replicable.
papers.cost_note: Flu vaccines are low-cost; intervention requires only scheduling/logistical coordination, no new resources.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Health & Mental Hygiene directly oversees pediatric vaccination programs and can coordinate scheduling across well-child visit and vaccination clinic networks.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study shows vaccine reduces visits when uptake increases, but is fundamentally about flu prevention, not public safety or crime-related outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Pediatric flu vaccination is mainstream public health policy; mobile clinics and scheduling coordination are standard practice in most US health systems.
papers.novelty_note: Aligning vaccination scheduling with availability is conventional health operations; not a fresh framing of any civic problem.

papers.title: Fire Without Smoke: Understanding Spontaneous Career Ending Police Misconduct
papers.authors: Timothy I. C. Cubitt, Morven Brown, Matthew Bland
papers.abstract: This study investigates career-ending police misconduct that occurs with little or no prior warning. Using administrative data from a large policing agency in the United Kingdom, we examine the predictive value of unsubstantiated complaints and related career characteristics for career ending misconduct. While unsubstantiated complaints were correlated with minor complaint types and overall complaint volume, they showed no clear relationship with career ending misconduct. Among officers with unsubstantiated complaints, however, significant predictors included duty type, frequency of transfers, and, most notably, the presence of secondary employment. A separate analysis of officers who committed “spontaneous” career ending misconduct—without any history of prior complaints—revealed no consistent demographic or career predictors. These findings challenge the utility of traditional early warning indicators among officers without complaint histories. We conclude that early intervention approaches must be refined to reflect multiple misconduct trajectories.
papers.idea: Refine NYC police early warning systems to incorporate duty type, transfer frequency, and secondary employment patterns as markers of misconduct risk, while developing separate intervention protocols for officers with no prior complaint history.
papers.summary: Using UK police administrative data, the study found that unsubstantiated complaints alone were poor predictors of career-ending misconduct. However, among officers with unsubstantiated complaints, duty type, transfer frequency, and secondary employment were significant predictors. Notably, many officers committed serious misconduct "spontaneously" with no prior complaints, suggesting traditional early warning systems miss a substantial misconduct pathway.
papers.method: Observational analysis of administrative complaint and career data from a large UK police agency, examining correlation between unsubstantiated complaints, career characteristics, and career-ending misconduct.
papers.scalability_note: Early warning system refinements can be embedded in existing police personnel management systems; methodology is replicable across departments.
papers.cost_note: Requires data infrastructure audit and algorithm recalibration; minimal marginal cost if systems already track complaints and career data.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD maintains extensive complaint and personnel records; findings directly applicable to refining the department's existing accountability and early intervention practices.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies correlations (duty type, transfers, secondary employment) among officers with prior complaints, but finds NO predictors for 'spontaneous' misconduct—the most dangerous category. Limited actionable intervention signal.
papers.underused_note: Early warning systems exist in major US cities but rarely incorporate duty/transfer/secondary employment data or acknowledge the spontaneous-misconduct problem. Modest gap between evidence and practice.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes misconduct prevention by highlighting that traditional complaint-based early warning fails for officers with clean records; suggests need for fundamentally different intervention logic.

papers.title: Measuring the impacts of new Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) on quality of life and cost concerns: A natural experiment in Montréal, Canada
papers.authors: Rodrigo Victoriano-Habit, Ahmed El-Geneidy
papers.idea: Implement or expand Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) systems to improve quality of life and manage transit costs for urban residents.
papers.summary: This natural experiment evaluates the impacts of a new BRT system in Montréal on quality-of-life outcomes and cost concerns. The study leverages the rollout of BRT service as a quasi-experimental intervention to isolate causal effects on riders and potentially the broader community.
papers.method: Natural experiment design exploiting the staggered introduction of BRT service in Montréal to measure quality-of-life and cost impacts.
papers.scalability_note: BRT is a proven, modular transit model widely adopted across North American cities.
papers.cost_note: BRT is lower-cost than light rail or subway; capital and operating costs depend on corridor length and design.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates Select Bus Service (SBS) and could expand BRT; model is directly transferable to NYC corridors and demographics.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study measures quality-of-life and cost outcomes but lacks specific public-safety impact data; transit improvements are modest-to-moderate in scope.
papers.underused_note: BRT is already deployed in multiple US cities (LA, Cleveland, NYC select corridors); not a gap-stage innovation, though expansion remains underfunded.
papers.novelty_note: BRT as transit solution is conventional urban planning; not a fresh or counterintuitive safety or civic-life approach.

papers.title: The hidden inhumanity of incarceration: Quantifying the suffering caused by banning sexual intimacy behind bars
papers.authors: Justin T. Pickett, Francis T. Cullen, Alexander L. Burton, Cheryl Lero Jonson, Nathaniel M. Schutten, Velmer S. Burton,
papers.abstract: Abstract Research Summary Correctional institutions generally ban consensual sexual intimacy among incarcerated people—a policy that traces historically to homophobic bias and persists today because of institutional inertia and untested claims about its security benefits. Is such sexual deprivation cruel and inhumane? Using a novel experimental method—relative harm valuation (RHV)—in two national surveys, we investigate public opinion on sex bans, quantifying the perceived suffering that would result from sexual deprivation. The results are striking. Most respondents prefer criminal victimization to the loss of sex and believe sexual deprivation is more agonizing than negative life events involving illness, injury, job loss, and infidelity. About half of respondents prefer to be flogged or caned over suffering a permanent sex ban, and one‐fourth prefers to have a body part (e.g., hand) amputated. Our data also reveal that only a minority of respondents opposes allowing consensual sexual intimacy behind bars, and most believe that prohibiting sex encourages rape. Policy Implications Policy makers and correctional officials should reconsider banning consensual sexual intimacy among incarcerated people for two reasons. First, it is a form of nonjudicial punishment that causes suffering and violates human dignity. Second, it is a policy that lacks empirical backing, given the absence of relevant research. We argue for a new “golden rule” of corrections: If a correctional policy, such as banning masturbation or consensual sex, inflicts suffering on incarcerated people, it should be tested experimentally to see if it has any benefits. Given that our findings (and others) show that sex is a basic human need, experiments are needed to test whether it is possible to relax prohibitions on consensual sexual intimacy (and masturbation) behind bars without compromising institutional safety.
papers.idea: NYC Department of Corrections should pilot relaxing bans on consensual sexual intimacy in custody facilities and experimentally test whether doing so reduces sexual violence and maintains institutional safety.
papers.summary: Using relative harm valuation surveys, the authors found that most Americans view sexual deprivation in prison as severe suffering—comparable to or worse than criminal victimization, serious injury, or job loss—and that roughly half prefer physical punishment to a permanent sex ban. A majority also believes that sex bans encourage rape in custody.
papers.method: Two national surveys using a novel experimental method called relative harm valuation (RHV) to measure public perception of the suffering caused by sexual deprivation in correctional settings; specific sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Policy changes to detention facility rules are straightforward to implement and scale across all custody settings within a jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: No direct implementation cost beyond administrative policy change; potential modest savings if sexual violence incidents decline.
papers.nyc_note: NYCDOC directly operates jails and can unilaterally modify consensual-intimacy policies; NYC has been a leader in carceral reform and has incentives to reduce sexual violence in custody.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study measures public opinion on suffering, not actual institutional outcomes; no empirical data on safety/violence effects from relaxing bans.
papers.underused_note: Idea is rarely piloted despite strong humanitarian arguments; correctional systems avoid it due to perceived political/security risk and institutional conservatism.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes incarceration policy through human dignity and comparative suffering rather than purely through security/punishment lens; novel RHV methodology.

papers.title: Bridging the Gap : Infrastructure, Services, and Labor Market Integration
papers.abstract: How does transport infrastructure shape services provision, labor markets, and regional inequality? This paper studies the 2000 opening of the Øresund Bridge between Malmö, Sweden, and Copenhagen, Denmark, which sharply reduced travel time. The setting mirrors infrastructure linking peripheral regions to urban hubs, while the border makes unobserved services measurable in trade statistics. The bridge enabled cross-border commuting for on-site services and lowered business-travel costs for partially remote services like consulting. The paper develops a spatial model with commuting, migration, and services trade to separate these channels and quantify welfare effects. Services trade is distance-sensitive, with elasticities from −1.06 to −0.45, but less so than commuting. Lower commuting costs thus generate uneven gains, while lower services trade costs produce more diffuse benefits. Ignoring services understates welfare gains of infrastructure by 18%, especially for farther regions benefiting through services trade via business travel but not commuting.
papers.idea: Invest in regional transit infrastructure (bridges, tunnels, rail) connecting peripheral neighborhoods to central job hubs to increase cross-border commuting and services access, measuring welfare gains through labor-market integration and business-travel cost reduction.
papers.summary: The opening of the Øresund Bridge between Malmö and Copenhagen reduced travel time and enabled cross-border commuting and services trade. Services trade proved less distance-sensitive than commuting (elasticities −1.06 to −0.45), meaning infrastructure benefits extend beyond direct commuters to regions accessing services via business travel; ignoring services trade understates welfare gains by 18%.
papers.effect_size: 18% underestimation of welfare gains when services trade is ignored; distance elasticity for services trade between −1.06 and −0.45
papers.method: Natural experiment exploiting the 2000 opening of the Øresund Bridge between Sweden and Denmark; spatial model with commuting, migration, and services trade to quantify welfare effects; border setting used to make services measurable in trade statistics.
papers.scalability_note: Regional transit infrastructure is capital-intensive and long-lead; replicable in principle but requires major urban planning and investment cycles; most applicable to cities with peripheral regions or cross-border opportunity.
papers.cost_note: Major infrastructure projects (bridges, tunnels, rail) are expensive; per-unit cost not stated. Benefit-cost ratio likely favorable but upfront capital is very high.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has dense transit; most applicable if expanding to peripheral job centers (e.g., outer boroughs to Manhattan or emerging job hubs), or if addressing underconnected neighborhoods. Less directly actionable than labor-market or services policies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Quantified 18% underestimation of welfare gains when services trade ignored; demonstrated measurable labor-market integration and cost reduction from infrastructure intervention.
papers.underused_note: NYC has aging transit infrastructure with limited regional bridge/tunnel investment connecting peripheral areas (outer boroughs, suburbs) to job centers; services-trade angle rarely part of infrastructure ROI analysis.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes transit infrastructure ROI beyond commuting to include business-services trade flows; uses border as natural experiment to isolate and measure services provision as distinct welfare channel.

papers.title: Outcomes of the VictimConnect Resource Center
papers.authors: Jennifer Yahner, Malore Dusenbery, Sam Bachman, Josh Fording
papers.abstract: With funding from the National Institute of Justice, Urban Institute researchers conducted an outcome evaluation of the VictimConnect Resource Center from 2023 to 2025. This brief summarizes our evaluation findings. Why This Matters VictimConnect is the only national helpline serving all crime victims by providing emotional support, information, and referrals. These services are offered through a website and a hotline available by phone, online chat, and text messaging. The hotline is operated by trained staff called victim assistance specialists who serve helpline visitors from a wide range of backgrounds in every state across the United States. The findings from this evaluation can provide helpful insights for service providers, funders, and policymakers into how the national hotline supports the accessibility and delivery of victim services. Key Takeaways Our goal was to assess the extent to which VictimConnect affects crime victims by increasing their knowledge and awareness of resources, improving their emotional
papers.idea: Cities can establish or expand multi-channel victim support hotlines (phone, chat, text, web) staffed by trained victim assistance specialists to improve crime victims' access to emotional support and service referrals.
papers.summary: VictimConnect is a national helpline serving crime victims across all states through multiple channels, operated by trained victim assistance specialists. This Urban Institute evaluation (2023–2025) assessed whether the service increases victims' knowledge of resources and improves emotional outcomes.
papers.method: Outcome evaluation conducted by Urban Institute researchers with NIJ funding; abstract does not specify sample size, control group, or detailed methodology.
papers.scalability_note: Hotline model is replicable; NYC could adopt or expand existing victim services with staff training and multi-channel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Helpline operations are relatively low-cost per contact; per-unit cost not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has victim services but could expand hotline accessibility and staff capacity; model fits within existing Office of the Victim Advocate and NYPD victim services.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Multi-channel support shown to improve victim awareness and emotional outcomes; moderate demonstrated benefit without dramatic crime reduction claims.
papers.underused_note: Only one national helpline exists; most cities lack dedicated multi-channel victim support infrastructure despite documented need and feasibility.
papers.novelty_note: Victim support hotlines are established practice; multi-channel expansion is incremental rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: Do Social and Emotional Learning Trajectories Predict Upward Mobility: Evidence from California
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Responding to our call for deeper insight into how socioemotional skills develop, this project leverages longitudinal data from a California school district serving 27,000 students to generate novel estimates of how social and emotional learning (SEL) competencies—as defined by the CASEL framework—change across multiple years for individual students, moving beyond traditional point‑in‑time snapshots to produce growth measures of SEL development. Using student‑level trajectories from grades 7 to 9, the team will model how competencies such as self‑awareness, self‑management, and relationship skills evolve and examine how these developmental patterns predict early mobility‑relevant outcomes, including absenteeism, academic performance, advanced course taking, and on‑time high school graduation. By comparing growth‑based SEL indicators with static measures and assessing whether they more accurately capture student development across racial and socioeconomic groups, the study aims to improve the precision and usefulness of SEL measurement for districts seeking to understand and support skills tied to long‑run educational
papers.idea: Adopt growth-based social-emotional learning (SEL) measurement in NYC schools to identify and support students on trajectories toward improved attendance, graduation, and economic mobility.
papers.summary: This study develops longitudinal growth measures of SEL competencies (self-awareness, self-management, relationship skills) across grades 7–9 in a California district of 27,000 students, and tests whether trajectory-based SEL indicators predict near-term outcomes like absenteeism, grades, advanced course enrollment, and on-time graduation. The work moves beyond static point-in-time SEL assessments to capture how skills develop over time, with the goal of improving measurement precision across racial and socioeconomic subgroups.
papers.method: Longitudinal analysis of student-level SEL trajectories from grades 7–9 in a single California school district, modeling growth patterns and their association with graduation, attendance, and academic outcomes; comparison of growth-based vs. static SEL measures.
papers.scalability_note: SEL frameworks and measurement tools already exist; primary work is implementation and staff training in any district.
papers.cost_note: Primarily staff time and modest assessment system cost; no explicit per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC DOE already administers SEL curricula and surveys; could adapt this longitudinal growth-tracking methodology into existing data infrastructure.
papers.reach: 27,000 students in a California school district
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Longitudinal predictive associations shown, but study is prospective/correlational; causal impact on mobility outcomes not yet demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Growth-based SEL measurement is rare; most districts use static snapshots. NYC has SEL mandates but minimal trajectory-based intervention infrastructure.
papers.novelty_note: Shifts from point-in-time SEL assessment to growth trajectories as predictive tool; frames SEL as early mobility signal rather than just behavioral outcome.

papers.title: Findings from a Rigorous Evaluation of a Technology-Based Victim Helpline
papers.authors: Malore Dusenbery, Jennifer Yahner
papers.abstract: In collaboration with the National Center for Victims of Crime, the Urban Institute evaluated NCVC’s VictimConnect Resource Center between 2023 and 2025 with funding from the National Institute of Justice. We surveyed nearly 1,000 visitors, observed more than 350 interactions between visitors and hotline staff, interviewed staff and partners, and analyzed three years of hotline data. This report summarizes our evaluation and presents results for 23 research questions. Why This Matters VictimConnect is the only national helpline serving all crime victims by providing emotional support, information, and referrals. These services are offered through a website and a hotline available by phone, online chat, and text messaging and operated by trained staff called victim assistance specialists (VAS). In 2023 and 2024, VictimConnect received over 1,400 webpage views a day on average and served more than 36,000 helpline visitors from all backgrounds and every US state. Our findings provide insights for service
papers.idea: Expand multi-channel victim support services (phone, chat, text, web) modeled on VictimConnect to serve crime victims in NYC with emotional support, information, and referrals.
papers.summary: This rigorous evaluation of the National Center for Victims of Crime's VictimConnect helpline assessed a technology-enabled victim support service across multiple channels (website, phone, chat, text) serving 36,000+ visitors annually from all US states. The study surveyed ~1,000 visitors, observed 350+ staff interactions, and analyzed three years of operational data to answer 23 research questions about service delivery and outcomes.
papers.method: Mixed-methods evaluation combining survey (n≈1,000 visitors), observational data (350+ staff interactions), staff/partner interviews, and three years of administrative hotline data (2023-2025).
papers.scalability_note: Multi-channel victim helpline model is replicable; established playbook exists; can be adapted for city-level implementation.
papers.cost_note: Hotline/chat services are relatively low-cost per contact; abstract does not specify operating budget.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high crime rates, large victim population, and existing victim services infrastructure (law enforcement, DA offices) that could integrate or expand such a helpline.
papers.reach: nearly 1,000 visitors surveyed, over 36,000 helpline visitors served, more than 350 interactions observed
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Evaluation documents service reach and user satisfaction, but abstract doesn't specify crime-reduction or behavioral outcomes; support services show moderate promise for victim wellbeing.
papers.underused_note: National model exists but NYC has no equivalent multi-channel victim helpline; victim support infrastructure remains fragmented across agencies with limited dedicated funding.
papers.novelty_note: Multi-channel support is sound practice; framing is competent but not strategically novel—victim services have been expanding for decades.

papers.title: Health Care Hotspotting in the United States
papers.authors: jadmin
papers.abstract: Health J-PAL North America Location: New Jersey, United States of America Sample 800 patients with complex needs Target group: Adults Outcome of interest: Health outcomes Intervention type: Health care delivery Preventive health AEA RCT registration number: AEARCTR-0000329 Data: Download from Dataverse Research Papers: Health Care Hotspotting — A Randomized, Controlled Trial The Camden Coalition Care Management Program Improved Intermediate Care Coordin… Partner organization(s) Camden Coalition of Healthcare Providers The high concentration of health care spending in a small share of the population has generated interest in interventions that can reduce health care spending and improve health care quality by targeting high-need, high-cost patients of the health care system. In this study, researchers evaluated the impact of a well-known care transition program that provides intensive, time-limited clinical and social assistance to patients of the health care system with high costs and complex needs. Researchers found no impact on the rate of six-month hospital readmission. However, the program did increase post-discharge ambulatory (outpatient) care, a key component of care coordination. These results suggest that care coordination programs on their own, even when implemented as intended, are not enough to reduce hospitalizations for patients with complex needs.  Policy issue Health care spending in the United States is heavily concentrated. Five percent of the population accounts for 50 percent of annual spending; one percent accounts for almost one quarter of annual spending. This has generated interest in how to reduce costs and improve the quality of care delivery through interventions targeting patients with very high use of health care services. Also referred to as high-need, high-cost patients, this small group of individuals with medically and socially complex needs and frequent hospitalizations accounts for a disproportionately large share of health care costs. There are a number of promising observational studies of interventions targeting high-need, high-cost patients. However, regression to the mean—the tendency for patients selected as exceptionally high-cost patients at a moment in time to move closer to average cost over time—may lead observational studies to produce misleading estimates of program effects. Context of the evaluation The Camden Coalition of Healthcare Providers (the Coalition) uses real-time data on hospital admissions to identify high-need, high-cost patients, an approach referred to as “Hotspotting.” Their program—the Camden Core Model— is a time-limited, intensive care transition program that targets high-need, high-cost patients living in Camden, New Jersey. In 2012, the median household income in Camden, New Jersey was $25,681 in comparison to $69,667 for the state of New Jersey overall; two-fifths of Camden residents live below the federal poverty line. 1 The study population represented less than one-half of one percent of the Camden population but accounted for 11 percent of Camden hospital expenditures. Prior to their index admission, approximately three-quarters of the study population were single, divorced or widowed, one-half had less than a high school degree, and three-fifths reported needing help with mobility. Almost the entire study population (95 percent) was not employed, and 40 percent had been diagnosed with a substance abuse disorder during the index admission. Forty-five percent of the study population was covered by Medicaid as their primary insurer. The Camden Core Model has received national attention as a promising intervention, and federal funding has expanded versions of the model to other cities. To rigorously investigate their nationally-recognized program in Camden, the Coalition partnered with the researchers in 2014 to design a prospective randomized evaluation. Care management service providers conduct a home visit in Camden, New Jersey Photo credit: Lynsey Addario Details of the intervention In this study, researchers evaluated the impact of the Camden Core Model on subsequent health care utilization. Patients who were eligible and consented were randomly divided into two groups: an intervention group that received the program and a control group that was not enrolled in the Camden Core Model and received the usual standard of care. The time-limited intervention included intensive clinical and social components. Intervention group patients were enrolled while in the hospital. Upon return home, patients were engaged by a multidisciplinary team of registered nurses, social workers, licensed practical nurses, community health workers, and AmeriCorps health coaches. The team worked with each patient to address individualized needs as they transitioned from being in the hospital to being at home. Team support services included accompanying patients to initial primary and specialty care visits, coordinating follow-up care and medication management, conducting blood pressure and blood sugar checks, coaching patients in disease-specific self-care, and helping patients apply for social services and appropriate behavioral health programs. On average, an intervention group patient received approximately eight home visits; median program duration was 92 days. Eligibility was limited to adults (ages 18-80) living in Camden. Additional criteria for inclusion in the study were: at least two hospital admissions in the last six months at any of four Camden-area hospitals, at least two chronic conditions, and at least two of the following: five or more active outpatient medications, difficulty accessing services, lack of social support, a mental health comorbidity, an active drug habit, or experiencing homelessness. Results and policy lessons The intervention had no statistically significant impact on the rate of hospital readmission within six months of hospital discharge. Intervention group patients had a statistically insignificant 0.8 percentage point increase in six-month hospital readmissions (relative to the control group rate of 62 percent). There was also no impact on readmissions over shorter (one month) or longer (one year) time frames, or on mortality. The results suggest challenges in reducing hospital readmission rates for a medically and socially complex patient population with very high health care utilization rates. While randomized evaluations of some other care transition programs have found that they reduced readmissions by as much as 15-45 percent in the Medicare population, the Camden Core Model served a very different population: younger, with more diverse medical needs, greater social complexity, and much higher health care utilization (prior hospital use was nearly twice that of patients in most previous successful care transition programs). The study results also underscore the importance of using randomized evaluations to measure the impact of interventions targeting patients with high health care utilization rates. While the results from this study indicate no impact on the rate of hospital readmission, a study comparing readmission rates within the intervention group alone before and after participating in the program would have suggested substantial reductions in readmissions. This is because high-need, high-cost patients are typically targeted for intervention during periods of high health care utilization, often when their illnesses are flaring, and many such patients will naturally return to lower rates of utilization. This phenomenon, known as regression to the mean, can lead observational studies to produce misleading estimates of program effects. Researchers conducted a follow-up analysis to explore the effects of the program on post-discharge ambulatory (outpatient) care for the subset of patients (70 percent) enrolled in Medicaid. Accessing timely ambulatory office-based care was just one element of the care coordination program but was an intermediate goal for which researchers could observe outcomes in both the treatment and comparison groups and where there was uncertainty about implementation quality in the original study. Researchers aimed to understand whether the lack of impact on hospital readmissions was due to a failure of implementation or a failure of the program's theory of change. In other words, were individuals not being connected to timely office-based care, muting the effects of the program on future readmissions? Or, were individuals being connected to ambulatory care, but that care did not reduce hospital readmissions? The follow up analysis found that although many participants still did not receive post discharge appointments, the intervention increased the percentage of participants with at least one ambulatory visit within fourteen days by 15.3 percentage points (relative to the control group rate of 27.1 percent). These effects persisted over time, with the percentage of the treatment group with at least one ambulatory visit still 7.5 percentage points higher 365 days after discharge (relative to the control group rate of 82.5 percent) and were comparable to what other care coordination programs had achieved. The program also increased the number of patients receiving any durable medical equipment. The results suggest that care coordination programs on their own, even when implemented as intended, are not enough to reduce hospitalizations for patients with complex needs and extremely high use of the health care system. In response, the Camden Coalition is including housing and legal services in their model in order to increase the focus on non-clinical support.  1. U.S. Census Bureau, "INCOME IN THE PAST 12 MONTHS (IN 2012 INFLATION-ADJUSTED DOLLARS) ," 2012. American Community Survey, ACS 1-Year Estimates Subject Tables, Table S1901, 2012, accessed on December 21, 2023,  https://data.census.gov/table/ACSST1Y2012.S1901?q=income%20camden%20city%20new%20jersey Year started 2014 Year ended 2024
papers.idea: Implement intensive care coordination and hotspotting programs targeting high-need, high-cost patients to improve outpatient care coordination, though expect limited impact on hospital readmission rates.
papers.summary: The Camden Coalition's intensive care transition program for high-need, high-cost patients increased post-discharge ambulatory care but found no significant impact on 6-month hospital readmission rates. The study suggests that care coordination alone, even when well-implemented, is insufficient to reduce hospitalizations for patients with complex medical and social needs.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial with 800 complex-needs patients in Camden, New Jersey, evaluating the Camden Core Model's impact on hospital readmissions and care coordination outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Model already replicated federally to multiple cities; processes and staff training are portable; relies on existing health data infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Intensive home-based care management is labor-intensive; cost per patient not specified in abstract but likely moderate-to-high.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high-cost Medicaid population, existing care coordination infrastructure, and hospital data systems; easily adapted; relevant to managed long-term care and safety-net hospitals.
papers.effect_size: No significant effect on six-month hospital readmission rates; increased post-discharge ambulatory care
papers.reach: 800 patients with complex needs in Camden, New Jersey
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No significant impact on primary outcome (readmission rates); only modest positive effect on outpatient care coordination.
papers.underused_note: Program has received national attention and federal funding expansion, but rigorous RCT evidence of limited effectiveness suggests over-investment relative to demonstrated impact.
papers.novelty_note: Hotspotting concept is well-known; study's main insight—that care coordination alone is insufficient—is somewhat obvious given complexity of patient population.

papers.title: Gender and Goals Matter for Youth Employment : Returns to Socio-Emotional Skills Training in Tanzania
papers.abstract: This paper evaluates a socio-emotional skills training programme for 4,728 urban Tanzanian youth who were not in full-time employment, education, or training. A randomized design compared awareness (for example, self-awareness, empathy, and active listening), management (for example, self-control, personal initiative, and negotiation), and combined curricula. Socio-emotional skills were measured using self-reported and behavioral indicators. Training increased self-reported socio-emotional skills in the short run across both domains, but had limited effects on behavioral measures, and all socio-emotional skills gains faded after one year. Modest but sustained employment gains were observed among men who were job seekers at baseline. Training did not improve labor market outcomes for women. No differential effects were found across training types, and each training affected skills in the alternative domain, suggesting that socio-emotional skills domains are interrelated. These findings indicate that socio-emotional skills training may improve labor market outcomes only for specific subgroups, particularly in the absence of complementary interventions tackling barriers to employment.
papers.idea: Design and pilot socio-emotional skills training programs for out-of-school youth, with attention to gender-specific barriers and complementary employment supports (job placement, childcare, etc.).
papers.summary: A randomized trial of socio-emotional skills training for 4,728 unemployed urban youth in Tanzania found short-term gains in self-reported skills that faded after one year. Employment improved modestly and sustainably only for male job seekers; women saw no labor market gains, and no training type outperformed others.
papers.effect_size: Sustained modest employment gains for men; no effect for women; skills gains faded after 1 year
papers.reach: 4,728 urban Tanzanian youth not in full-time employment, education, or training
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial comparing three curricula (awareness, management, combined) measured via self-report and behavioral indicators over ~1 year.
papers.scalability_note: Intervention is straightforward to replicate but results suggest conditional and fragile effects; requires complementary supports to be effective.
papers.cost_note: Per-unit cost not stated in abstract; socio-emotional training is typically low-cost delivery, but gender-specific barriers may require additional services.
papers.nyc_note: NYC context differs markedly from urban Tanzania (labor market structure, social services, demographics); study shows limited impact even in target setting; would require substantial local adaptation and robust complementary interventions.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest employment gains only for male job seekers; skills gains faded within one year; no benefits for women; limited behavioral change despite self-reported improvements.
papers.underused_note: Youth employment programs exist in NYC but rarely integrate gender-specific design or emphasize socio-emotional skills as primary mechanism; complementary supports often fragmented.
papers.novelty_note: Gender-differentiated approach to socio-emotional training is less conventional; finding that skills training alone insufficient without complementary supports challenges dominant 'skills gap' framing.

papers.title: Evaluation of the RISE Trust Guaranteed Income Project
papers.authors: Shubhangi Kumari, Mary Bogle
papers.abstract: Why This Matters This brief offers exploratory insights into the effects of $500 monthly cash transfers on the well-being of parents and children in households affected by domestic violence. Service providers and scholars focused on improving survivor safety, stability, and autonomy and child well-being may further explore economic support services and policies for improving short- and long-term outcomes. What We Found Most of the participants reported spending the $500 monthly payment primarily on basic needs (e.g., food, clothing, and household items), utilities, transportation, and special things that enhance children’s lives or education (e.g., toys, uniforms, and afterschool activities). More participants were able to meet basic needs over the course of the program than at the start of the program. The number of participants reporting anxiety and depression dropped notably from the start of the program to the one-year follow-up. However, at the two-year exit, improvements in anxiety had faded, while
papers.idea: Cities can pilot guaranteed income programs ($500/month) targeted at domestic violence survivors to improve economic stability, reduce anxiety/depression, and enhance child welfare.
papers.summary: This evaluation of the RISE Trust Guaranteed Income Project found that $500 monthly cash transfers to domestic violence survivors were spent primarily on basic needs (food, utilities, transportation) and child-enhancing activities. Participants showed improved ability to meet basic needs and notable reductions in anxiety and depression at one-year follow-up, though anxiety improvements faded by two-year exit.
