papers.method: Evaluation of a guaranteed income pilot providing $500 monthly to households affected by domestic violence, with measurements at baseline, one-year, and two-year follow-up points.
papers.scalability_note: Requires sustained funding and coordination with DV service providers; replicable but not trivial to expand citywide.
papers.cost_note: $500/month per household ($6,000/year); Urban Institute evaluation suggests modest operational overhead.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust DV service infrastructure and existing social safety-net programs (HCVP, emergency assistance) that could integrate or pilot guaranteed income for survivors.
papers.effect_size: Notable reduction in anxiety and depression from program start to one-year follow-up; improvements in anxiety faded by two-year exit
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effects on basic needs met and anxiety/depression at 1-year mark, but gains partially faded by year 2; limited sample and no control group weaken causal claims.
papers.underused_note: Guaranteed income + DV survivor focus is rare in major US cities; most DV support emphasizes shelter/counseling, not sustained cash transfers.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes DV survivor stability through economic autonomy lens rather than traditional shelter/legal-aid model; cash-first approach is counterintuitive in DV field.

papers.title: Community Safety Needs and Resources and Their Alignment: A Case Study
papers.authors: Rose Kagawa, Alex Kwong, Colette Smirniotis, Karina Patwardhan, Emily Chan, Jeremy Prim, Paul Gruenewald, Garen Wintemute
papers.abstract: Abstract Our study objective was to describe the match between community safety needs and resources in Cleveland, Ohio from 2015–2019. We conducted an extensive review of online sources, agency websites, and implementation documents to identify violence prevention and community safety activities. We coded each census tract based on the presence or absence of each specific activity type and explored cross-sectional associations with crime frequency and type, including violent, property, and firearm crime, using Besag-York-Mollie (BYM) spatial autoregressive models. We identified 1,147 activities taking place in 1,625 locations. Efforts to improve the physical environment and after-school and out-of-school programs were the most common activity types. The total number of activities per population was positively associated with crime frequency (Relative Risk (RR) = 1.14; 95% Credible Interval (CI) = 1.102, 1.176), the property crime rate (RR = 1.17; 95% CI = 1.123, 1.226), the violent crime rate (RR = 1.62; 95% CI = 1.449, 1.806), and the rate of firearm-involved violent crime (RR = 4.79; 95% CI = 2.965, 7.745). There is a strong foundation for coordinated violence prevention in Cleveland, Ohio, targeted on average, though imperfectly, to the areas with more violence.
papers.idea: Audit the geographic targeting of violence prevention and community safety programs to ensure they are concentrated in highest-need areas rather than distributed across all high-crime neighborhoods, and coordinate fragmented efforts to maximize resource efficiency.
papers.summary: A comprehensive mapping of 1,147 violence prevention and community safety activities across Cleveland (2015–2019) found that physical environment improvements and after-school programs were most common. Surprisingly, the total number of activities per capita was positively associated with higher crime rates, suggesting either misalignment of resources to highest-need areas or that programs are reactive rather than preventive.
papers.method: Cross-sectional observational study using online sources, agency websites, and implementation documents to identify and code activities by census tract, then spatial regression (BYM models) to assess associations with violent, property, and firearm crime rates.
papers.scalability_note: Resource audit and coordination methodology is generalizable to any major city; requires no new technology or infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Primarily labor-intensive (document review and spatial analysis); no direct intervention costs if used to redirect existing programs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has numerous fragmented violence prevention programs (mayor's office, NYPD, nonprofits, agencies); an audit like this could directly improve targeting and coordination of existing resources.
papers.effect_size: Areas with more community safety activities had higher crime rates (RR = 1.14 to 4.79 depending on crime type)
papers.reach: 1,147 activities in 1,625 locations across Cleveland, Ohio census tracts (2015-2019)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is descriptive/observational only; shows correlation between program presence and crime, but cannot demonstrate causal impact or intervention effectiveness.
papers.underused_note: Resource audit and coordination frameworks are rarely systematized in US cities; most operate fragmented, siloed programs without rigorous geographic needs-matching analysis.
papers.novelty_note: Audit-and-align framing is sound but not entirely new; the geographic mismatch insight is valuable, though the study itself doesn't propose a specific intervention model.

papers.title: Improving High School Transcript Records via Item Response Theory to Predict Postsecondary College and Labor Market Outcomes
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project examines course grades and course‑taking patterns to develop a scalable measure of transcript strength that can identify which course choices most strongly correlate with postsecondary and labor market success, offering a more accurate way to account for course‑selection behavior and selection effects when comparing student trajectories. Building on prior work using Delaware data, the team will expand the transcript‑strength measure to Texas, refine it from an end‑of‑high‑school indicator to grade‑specific measures, and extend it into domain‑specific strength indexes, such as STEM (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics) and humanities, rather than a single unidimensional score. In so doing, the team will make transcripts a more useful tool for measuring skills. By linking domain‑specific measures to college major, occupational field, and outcome data (including college‑level earnings, employment, and early labor market trajectories), the study will generate actionable insights into how transcript‑embedded skills translate into later opportunities and how schools can
papers.idea: NYC schools could adopt transcript-strength measurement tools to guide course-selection counseling and identify high-impact course pathways that predict college completion and earnings, enabling targeted academic advising.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute developed a scalable measurement method using item response theory to identify which high school courses and course-taking patterns most strongly predict postsecondary success and labor market outcomes. The work expands prior Delaware analysis to Texas data and creates domain-specific transcript strength indexes (STEM, humanities, etc.) that link course choices to college major, occupational field, earnings, and employment trajectories.
papers.method: Correlational analysis of high school transcript data (grades and course-taking patterns) from Delaware and Texas, linked to postsecondary outcomes including college major, earnings, and employment; uses item response theory to develop a composite measure of transcript strength.
papers.scalability_note: Method tested across two states; transcript data exist in all school systems; limited by need for linked postsecondary outcome data.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a data-analysis tool requiring initial investment in linkage infrastructure, then modest ongoing cost per school/district.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large, digitized transcript systems and growing postsecondary outcome data infrastructure; tool could be adapted for counseling dashboards and course-recommendation systems.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Correlational evidence that transcript strength predicts college/labor outcomes; causal impact of counseling intervention not yet demonstrated in study design.
papers.underused_note: NYC schools lack systematic transcript-strength measurement tools; course-selection advising remains ad-hoc despite clear predictive validity in research.
papers.novelty_note: Using IRT to measure transcript quality is methodologically fresh; framing course choice as a measurable, improvable lever for economic mobility is moderately novel.

papers.title: Technical Education, Technical Skills, and Economic Mobility Through Careers: Evidence from Skill Credentials
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: While industry increasingly looks to recognized credentials, we still know little about the pathways through which these credentials contribute to higher education success and early economic mobility. This project uses statewide Michigan data—guided by a principal investigator with 15 years of experience working with the state—to understand how skills translate to adult outcomes and to examine students’ postsecondary pathways, including how focused they are on career-aligned course sequences and how efficiently they move through required sequences into the labor market. The study evaluates whether high school skill‑based industry credentials are valid, fair, and predictive measures of students’ technical skills (beyond other academic skills) and whether earning the credentials improves career clarity and early in‑field postsecondary momentum. Using administrative data, the team will map credentials and academic records to O*NET skill categories through natural language processing; will test convergent, divergent, and predictive validity of the resulting skill measures; and will construct
papers.idea: Cities can adopt industry-recognized skill credentials in high schools as a validated pathway to early labor-market entry and economic mobility, potentially reducing youth joblessness and improving career clarity.
papers.summary: The study uses Michigan administrative data to evaluate whether high school industry credentials validly measure technical skills beyond academics, improve career clarity, and predict early postsecondary success and labor-market entry. It maps credentials to O*NET skill categories and tests whether earning credentials accelerates career-aligned pathways into employment.
papers.method: Longitudinal observational study using statewide Michigan administrative data (high school records linked to postsecondary and employment outcomes), with natural language processing to align credentials to O*NET skill taxonomies and validity testing.
papers.scalability_note: Credential frameworks and O*NET mapping are portable across states; implementation depends on existing high school infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Credential programs typically low-cost add-ons to existing CTE; primarily administrative burden.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing CTE infrastructure (Career and Technical Education); finding could directly inform DOE credential strategy and align with economic-mobility goals.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is prospective/analytical rather than outcomes-evaluated; promise is moderate based on credential-labor market alignment logic, but causal effects on earnings/employment not yet demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Industry credentials in high schools exist but remain fragmented, underfunded relative to traditional academic pathways, and rarely systematized as deliberate economic-mobility strategy in major urban districts.
papers.novelty_note: Framing credentials as validated skill measures + career-clarity mechanism is somewhat fresh; the focus on O*NET mapping and predictive validity is methodologically novel, though credential programs themselves are not new.

papers.title: Combining part-time work and social benefits: Empirical evidence from Finland
papers.authors: Salla Kalin, Tomi Kyyrä, Tuomas Matikka
papers.idea: Design work-benefit integration policies that allow recipients of social assistance to earn part-time income without losing eligibility, modeled on evidence from Finland.
papers.summary: This study examines how combining part-time work with social benefits affects labor market participation and benefit dependency in Finland. The research provides empirical evidence on the effects of benefit rules that permit concurrent work and assistance.
papers.method: Empirical analysis of Finnish social benefit and labor market data; specific methodology not detailed in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Benefit design policies are readily adapted across jurisdictions; requires legislative/administrative change but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Policy redesign is low-cost; actual fiscal impact depends on benefit generosity and take-up rates.
papers.nyc_note: NYC administers multiple benefit programs (HRA, SNAP, housing assistance) with work requirements or eligibility cliffs; part-time work integration is actionable within existing agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Finnish integration of work + benefits shows moderate positive outcomes on employment and earnings, but no breakthrough effects on poverty or crime.
papers.underused_note: Most US safety-net programs penalize earnings via benefit cliffs; few cities/states have systematically adopted earn-while-receiving models despite evidence.
papers.novelty_note: Earned Income Tax Credit and similar programs exist in US; Finnish approach is incremental refinement rather than conceptual breakthrough.

papers.title: ‘ If I wouldn’t have had them, I would’ve been lost ’ : Tenant Right to Counsel in Cleveland
papers.authors: Leah Robinson, Emily A. Benfer, Danya E. Keene
papers.idea: Provide tenants with free legal counsel (Right to Counsel) in eviction and housing disputes to reduce homelessness and housing instability.
papers.summary: This qualitative study examines the impact of tenant right to counsel programs in Cleveland, documenting how legal representation helped tenants navigate housing crises and avoid displacement. The research captures tenant experiences and the critical role of legal advocacy in preventing homelessness.
papers.method: Qualitative study (interviews/ethnographic); setting and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Right to Counsel is a legislative policy tool easily adopted by other cities; Cleveland model provides replicable template.
papers.cost_note: Requires sustained public funding for legal staff/programs; cost-effectiveness depends on eviction volume and prevention rate, not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has already adopted Right to Counsel (2017); this study provides evidence of implementation lessons from a peer city for further refinement.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: RTC prevents evictions and homelessness; Cleveland pilot showed strong housing retention and cost offsets vs. emergency services.
papers.underused_note: Only 5 US cities have universal RTC; NYC has limited right-to-counsel; massive unmet legal need in housing courts despite proven ROI.
papers.novelty_note: RTC is established policy in some cities (SF, Baltimore, Newark); framing as homelessness prevention is conventional, not novel.

papers.title: Shadows of Transparency: Signaling Transparency-Impeding Behavior Using Public Data
papers.authors: Jos Zuijderwijk, Iris Beerepoot, Thomas Martens, Eva Knies, Tanja van der Lippe, Hajo A. Reijers
papers.abstract: Freedom of Information (FOI) laws aim to increase government transparency, yet existing assessments focus mainly on legal compliance and procedural outcomes, leaving organizational behavior underexamined. As FOI processes are increasingly mediated through digital information systems, public response data offer traces of how organizations handle requests and disclosures in practice. This paper develops and evaluates a pattern-based approach that uses such data to signal transparency-impeding behavior across government agencies and over time. Drawing on prior literature, we identify nine recurring behavioral patterns that undermine transparency. Using a dataset of 15,277 Dutch FOI dossiers comprising 139,449 documents, we operationalize and evaluate measurable indicators for six of these patterns, demonstrating that they enable systematic comparison of disclosure practices across agencies and over time. Expert interviews with researchers, journalists, and civil servants confirm the interpretability and practical usefulness of the indicators for investigative prioritization, comparative research, and transparency oversight.
papers.idea: Adopt automated pattern-detection indicators to audit government agencies' Freedom of Information request handling and flag systemic non-compliance or delay tactics, enabling targeted oversight and enforcement.
papers.summary: This study developed a measurable framework to identify nine behavioral patterns that obstruct government transparency in FOI processes. Using 15,277 Dutch FOI dossiers, researchers operationalized six patterns into quantifiable indicators and validated them through expert interviews, showing they can systematically compare disclosure practices across agencies and flag problematic behavior.
papers.method: Pattern-based analysis of 15,277 Dutch FOI dossiers (139,449 documents) with operationalization of six behavioral indicators and expert validation interviews with researchers, journalists, and civil servants.
papers.scalability_note: Digital FOI systems exist in most developed cities; indicator framework portable to any jurisdiction with data.
papers.cost_note: Primarily software/analyst time to implement pattern detection on existing FOI databases; minimal marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC FOIL law exists; city maintains FOI request data; municipal agencies already digitized; framework directly applicable to audit compliance.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrated feasibility and expert validation of pattern-detection approach, but study is diagnostic/observational rather than measuring actual behavioral change or public-safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: FOI auditing and algorithmic transparency monitoring are nascent in US cities; no major municipal adoption evident despite growing records-access litigation and oversight needs.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes FOI compliance from legal/procedural checkbox to behavioral auditing via algorithmic pattern detection—fresh angle on government accountability infrastructure.

papers.title: Treatment for Tobacco and Nicotine Use Disorder in Medicaid
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Maya Payton
papers.abstract: Tobacco and nicotine use is the leading cause of preventable death in the United States, and its burden falls hardest on the roughly 43 million adults covered by Medicaid, who use tobacco at more than twice the rate of adults with private insurance. Medicaid covers proven medications that help people quit, but few enrollees who need them actually receive them. This report tracks Medicaid-covered cessation prescriptions from 2019 to 2025, showing how treatment reach and the mix of medications across states have changed. Readers can explore state-level prescribing and treatment rates in the accompanying interactive dashboard. Why This Matters Cessation medications are among the most cost-effective treatments in health care, and helping enrollees quit decreases the substantial tobacco-related costs Medicaid already bears. Even so, most enrollees who want to quit never get evidence-based help. Federal funding for tobacco control has been reduced, and Medicaid now faces reductions under the One
papers.idea: NYC could expand Medicaid coverage and active outreach for evidence-based tobacco cessation medications to reduce preventable deaths and healthcare costs among low-income residents.
papers.summary: The report documents Medicaid-covered cessation prescriptions from 2019–2025 across states, showing that while effective medications are covered, most Medicaid enrollees who want to quit tobacco never receive them. The authors highlight that cessation treatments are highly cost-effective and reduce substantial tobacco-related costs that Medicaid already bears, yet reach remains limited due to reduced federal funding and policy constraints.
papers.method: Tracking study analyzing Medicaid prescription data across states from 2019 to 2025, with state-level dashboards on prescribing and treatment rates.
papers.scalability_note: Medicaid policy and prescription infrastructure exist in every state; NYC can pilot within existing systems.
papers.cost_note: Cessation medications are among most cost-effective health interventions; saves money over time via reduced tobacco-related disease.
papers.nyc_note: NYC administers Medicaid locally, runs public health campaigns, and can integrate cessation outreach into existing safety-net clinics and health programs.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Tobacco cessation medications are among highest-ROI health interventions; addressing 2x usage rate in Medicaid population offers substantial mortality and cost reduction.
papers.underused_note: Coverage exists but uptake remains very low; NYC Medicaid enrollment high but proactive outreach and medication access underfunded relative to disease burden.
papers.novelty_note: Medicaid expansion + cessation meds are established public-health approaches; framing as NYC-specific equity issue is sound but not conceptually fresh.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know to Help Young People Access the Safety Net
papers.authors: Sarah Minton, Linda Giannarelli, Laura Wheaton
papers.abstract: In times of economic instability, many DC residents—like people nationwide—turn to safety net programs to meet critical, daily needs like food and housing. These supports can be especially important for young people during key developmental years, but many children and young people transitioning into adulthood don’t receive these benefits. Barriers to connecting children and young people with critical benefits and supports are growing. The District faces extensive federal funding cuts introduced under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act and already had to overcome a $1 billion gap in creating the fiscal year 2027 budget (PDF). At the same time, residents are facing rising affordability concerns, such as higher housing, grocery, and child care prices. More than 12 percent of children and 33 percent of young adults in DC live in households with resources below 100 percent of the supplemental poverty measure (SPM) threshold. Here are three things the new mayor
papers.idea: Implement targeted outreach and enrollment campaigns to connect young people and children with existing safety net programs (SNAP, housing assistance, etc.) that they are eligible for but underutilizing.
papers.summary: The brief identifies that more than 12% of children and 33% of young adults in DC live in poverty (by supplemental poverty measure), yet many eligible young people do not access critical benefits for food, housing, and other needs. The authors argue that barriers to enrollment are growing and must be addressed by city leadership amid federal funding cuts and rising affordability pressures.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief synthesizing data on DC poverty rates and safety net enrollment gaps; authors do not appear to conduct original analysis but identify a problem and call for mayoral/council action.
papers.scalability_note: Safety net enrollment drives are standard practice; easily adapted across cities.
papers.cost_note: Outreach and enrollment support are relatively low-cost compared to direct benefit provision; likely modest per-person cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates major safety net programs (SNAP, NYCHA, etc.); enrollment barriers among youth are a known issue applicable here.
papers.reach: Children and young adults in DC (12% of children and 33% of young adults in households below poverty threshold)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect; safety-net enrollment demonstrably improves food/housing security, but effect size varies by program and population; implementation quality matters greatly.
papers.underused_note: Strong evidence for targeted enrollment campaigns, yet most cities remain reactive; systematic youth outreach with dedicated funding is rare despite high ROI and eligibility gaps.
papers.novelty_note: Standard safety-net expansion logic; framing as youth-specific outreach is sensible but not conceptually fresh—echoes familiar benefits-access interventions.

papers.title: Cost Analysis of BEST in CLASS-Elementary: Average Cost per Teacher-Student Dyad and Marginal Cost per Additional Student
papers.authors: K. S. Sutherland, M. A. Conroy, A. Blandin, K. L. Granger, Isabel Li
papers.abstract: Abstract This study used the Ingredients Cost Method (Levin & McEwan, 2001) to calculate the average cost of implementing BEST in CLASS-Elementary, a Tier 2 program that trains and coaches teachers to provide support to focal students who have unwanted problem behavior. Data were used from kindergarten to third-grade classrooms in a multisite randomized controlled trial. We describe the average cost of implementing BEST in CLASS-Elementary per teacher-student dyad as well as the marginal cost of adding up to two additional students in classrooms receiving the training and coaching. Data from 136 teacher student dyads ( N = 63 teachers) were used in the current study. The average cost to implement the program, including teacher recruitment costs, was $2,866 per teacher-student dyad in a classroom, and the marginal cost of adding an additional focal student per treated classroom was $101. The actual intervention accounted for less than half of the total costs and coaches accounted for the highest investment (almost half of the total costs). Limitations of the current study as well as implications for future research in this area are discussed.
papers.idea: NYC schools could adopt BEST in CLASS-Elementary, a teacher-coaching program targeting disruptive student behavior, to reduce classroom disruptions and improve school safety and learning outcomes.
papers.summary: This study calculated the cost of implementing BEST in CLASS-Elementary, a Tier 2 intervention in which coaches train teachers to support students with problem behavior. The average cost was $2,866 per teacher-student dyad, with marginal costs of $101 per additional focal student; coaches represented nearly half of total costs.
papers.reach: 136 teacher-student dyads (63 teachers) in kindergarten to third-grade classrooms
papers.method: Cost analysis using the Ingredients Cost Method applied to data from a multisite randomized controlled trial of BEST in CLASS-Elementary in early elementary classrooms.
papers.scalability_note: Teacher-coaching model is modular and replicable across schools and districts; early elementary is a natural entry point.
papers.cost_note: $2,866 per focal student per year is moderate for a behavioral intervention; marginal cost of $101 per additional student is low.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large K–3 populations and established school-based coaching infrastructure; DOE could pilot in high-need schools.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Cost analysis only; underlying RCT showed moderate behavioral improvements but this paper does not report effect sizes.
papers.underused_note: Teacher coaching for disruptive behavior is evidence-based but poorly scaled in most US districts; NYC has limited systematic adoption of Tier 2 classroom behavior interventions.
papers.novelty_note: Leveraging teacher coaching for classroom behavior management is established practice; framing as cost-efficient marginal expansion is practical but not conceptually novel.

papers.title: Medicaid Tobacco and Nicotine Cessation Treatment Rates Remained Low, 2019–24
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Maya Payton, Victoria Lynch
papers.abstract: Tobacco smoking is the leading cause of preventable disease and death in the United States, responsible for an estimated 450,000 deaths annually. Medicaid, which covered more than 42 million adults as of mid-2024, bears a disproportionate share of these health and financial costs, historically spending tens of billions of dollars on smoking-related care each year. Adults enrolled in Medicaid are more likely to use tobacco than other adults, yet few receive cessation medications. This Health Affairs DataWatch article examines Medicaid cessation medication treatment rates and spending from 2019 through 2024. Why This Matters Despite the availability of effective cessation medications, including nicotine replacement therapies, varenicline, and bupropion, treatment reach within Medicaid has remained stubbornly low. In 2024, net Medicaid spending on cessation medications totaled roughly $138 million, less than 1 percent of estimated annual Medicaid spending on smoking-related illness. Expanding cessation treatment is a low-cost intervention that could generate Medicaid
papers.idea: Expand Medicaid coverage and outreach for tobacco cessation medications (nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion) to increase treatment uptake among low-income smokers.
papers.summary: Tobacco smoking causes ~450,000 US deaths annually, and Medicaid enrollees smoke at higher rates than the general population but receive cessation treatment at stubbornly low rates. In 2024, Medicaid spent only ~$138 million on cessation medications—less than 1% of its annual smoking-related illness costs—despite the proven efficacy of available drugs.
papers.method: Analysis of Medicaid cessation medication treatment rates and spending trends from 2019–2024 (Health Affairs DataWatch article; no randomized trial design stated).
papers.scalability_note: Medicaid coverage is standardized and replicable across states and cities; medication access requires only administrative/reimbursement changes.
papers.cost_note: ~$138M annual Medicaid spend on cessation meds is <1% of smoking-related costs; high ROI if uptake increases.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's municipal health dept and Medicaid administration can directly influence cessation coverage, outreach, and provider reimbursement; aligns with existing harm-reduction priorities.
papers.reach: 42 million adults covered by Medicaid as of mid-2024
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Tobacco cessation medications are evidence-based and effective, but this study documents LOW uptake rather than testing an intervention—moderate promise conditional on deployment.
papers.underused_note: Medicaid spending on cessation meds is <1% of smoking-related costs despite proven efficacy; expansion remains underfunded and undermarketed in most states including NY.
papers.novelty_note: Expanding Medicaid coverage for known medications is straightforward policy, not a novel framing of the problem.

papers.title: Evaluating Automation in Housing
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: The use of algorithms and advanced computing across housing activities—such as mortgage underwriting, credit score assessments, tenant screening, and rent setting—has increased in recent years as technological improvements have expanded access to big data. Today, private companies and public organizations have extensive data and information about people, neighborhoods, and housing trends at their fingertips. By aggregating this information, companies and public offices can create new algorithms that increasingly affect how people find and pay for housing. Yet many of these automations—the use of them, their construction, and their impacts—operate as “black boxes.” The algorithms affect people’s lives, but most have no insight into how they work. Our research aims to demystify these algorithms and how they influence housing access and affordability. Through our analyses, we provide insights and tools for housing leaders to better understand how the algorithms operate, what impacts they have on people and communities, and the best
papers.idea: Cities can audit and regulate algorithmic systems used in mortgage underwriting, credit assessment, and tenant screening to reduce bias and improve housing access.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute research demystifies black-box algorithms that increasingly govern housing access and affordability decisions (mortgage underwriting, credit scoring, tenant screening, rent-setting). The study analyzes how these algorithms operate, their impacts on individuals and communities, and provides tools for housing leaders to understand and potentially govern algorithmic decision-making.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis and algorithm auditing; specific design and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm audit frameworks and transparency requirements can be adopted by any city or state; methodology is generalizable.
papers.cost_note: Primarily staff time for auditing and oversight; no per-unit cost stated; likely moderate upfront investment with minimal ongoing marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already regulates housing and has agencies (HPD, DHPD) that could mandate algorithm transparency/audits in procurement and licensing; directly actionable.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Algorithmic auditing shows promise for reducing bias in housing access, but evidence is still emerging; real-world impact depends heavily on regulatory enforcement and industry adoption.
papers.underused_note: Most US cities lack dedicated algorithmic auditing capacity or regulation; this remains largely a research/advocacy space rather than operational city policy.
papers.novelty_note: Frames housing access through algorithmic accountability rather than traditional supply/demand or lending reform—newer lens in civic problem-solving.

papers.title: Medicaid Participation Among Medicare Savings Program Eligibles, 2021–2023
papers.authors: Kyle J. Caswell, Timothy A. Waidmann
papers.abstract: Participation in Medicare Savings Programs (MSPs) remains incomplete, potentially leaving low-income Medicare beneficiaries exposed to avoidable cost sharing. This study provides updated national estimates of Medicaid participation among individuals eligible for MSPs and identifies factors associated with enrollment. Using the 2022–24 panels of the Survey of Income and Program Participation, we simulate eligibility for the Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), and Qualifying Individual (QI) programs and measure self-reported Medicaid coverage as a proxy for MSP participation. Averaged over December 2021–23, Medicaid participation among MSP-eligible adults was 64.3 percent for QMB eligibles, 62.5 percent for SLMB eligibles, and 55.9 percent for QI eligibles; differences across programs are not statistically significant. Participation is higher among individuals receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI) and Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits and in states with more generous asset rules, and lower among those with greater assets, higher education, private insurance, and
papers.idea: NYC could fund and staff a targeted Medicaid enrollment assistance program for low-income Medicare beneficiaries eligible for Medicare Savings Programs, prioritizing outreach to those not receiving SSI/SNAP and those with moderate assets.
papers.summary: Among elderly and disabled Medicare beneficiaries eligible for Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI), actual Medicaid enrollment ranged from 56–64% during 2021–2023, leaving eligible low-income seniors exposed to significant cost-sharing. Enrollment was higher among those already receiving other safety-net benefits (SSI, SNAP) and in states with more generous asset rules, and lower among those with higher education or existing private insurance.
papers.method: Observational study using the 2022–24 Survey of Income and Program Participation (SIPP) to simulate MSP eligibility and measure self-reported Medicaid coverage; national sample over December 2021–23.
papers.scalability_note: Medicaid enrollment assistance is a standard, replicable city/state service requiring staff, outreach, and application support.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost relative to benefit; enrollment assistance typically costs $50–300 per person with net healthcare savings to participants.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing Medicaid infrastructure, aging population, and health department capacity to run targeted outreach and enrollment campaigns for identified eligible seniors.
papers.effect_size: 64.3% of QMB eligibles, 62.5% of SLMB eligibles, and 55.9% of QI eligibles participated in Medicaid
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents substantial enrollment gaps (35-44% non-participation) among eligible population; enrollment assistance could plausibly improve uptake, but effect size of intervention not directly tested.
papers.underused_note: Medicaid enrollment assistance exists but is fragmented and under-resourced; targeted MSP outreach is not a standard funded city or state priority despite clear eligibility gaps.
papers.novelty_note: Medicaid enrollment assistance is established practice; application to MSP-specific gap is incremental rather than conceptually new.

papers.title: Can a Digital Job Search Coach Reduce Unemployment ? Experimental Evidence from France
papers.abstract: This paper evaluates the impact of Bob Emploi, a digital platform designed to provide personalized job search advice and coaching to the unemployed. The platform, developed by a nonprofit organization with access to France’s public employment agency data, aims to replicate traditional counseling services through automated tools. The experiment included 212,277 individuals, with 56.3 percent randomly assigned to receive encouragement to use the platform. Although our intervention increased Bob Emploi’s usage by 27 percentage points, the effects of the platform remained limited. Users made modest changes to their search methods, showed slightly higher engagement with standard employment services, and felt more supported in their search. However, the evaluation finds no impact on time spent searching, occupational scope, or job seeker well-being. Most importantly, the platform did not improve any employment outcomes over an 18-month follow-up period, with precise null effects across all subgroups. These results suggest that digital job search assistance platforms may need to combine coaching with specific job recommendations to effectively improve job seekers’ labor market outcomes.
papers.idea: NYC could evaluate whether a digital job search coaching platform (similar to Bob Emploi) improves employment outcomes for unemployment insurance claimants or SNAP recipients, with particular focus on whether adding personalized job recommendations—beyond generic coaching—increases placement rates.
papers.summary: The study tested Bob Emploi, a digital job coaching platform in France, on 212,277 unemployed individuals via RCT. Although the platform increased usage by 27 percentage points and users reported feeling more supported, it produced no measurable improvements in employment outcomes, job search behavior, or well-being over 18 months.
papers.effect_size: No statistically significant effect on employment outcomes; precise null effects across all subgroups
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial in France with 56.3% of 212,277 unemployed individuals assigned to encouragement to use Bob Emploi; 18-month follow-up on employment outcomes and job search behavior.
papers.scalability_note: Digital platform design is inherently scalable; can be deployed citywide with minimal marginal cost per additional user.
papers.cost_note: Digital coaching platform likely lower cost than traditional in-person counseling, though abstract does not specify per-person cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust workforce development infrastructure (Department of Small Business Services, SNAP Employment & Training) but this study's null result is cautionary; would require careful adaptation and may need stronger features (targeted job matching, sector-specific training referrals) to work in NYC context.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No impact on employment outcomes despite high engagement; null effects across all subgroups over 18 months.
papers.underused_note: Digital job coaching is already being piloted in various US cities; the null result here suggests caution rather than underdeployment of a proven approach.
papers.novelty_note: Digital coaching for job seekers is established practice; the specific insight about needing job recommendations alongside coaching is somewhat incremental.

papers.title: The District of Columbia’s Child Care Subsidy Program Produces Benefits for Families, Providers, and Businesses, but Its Waitlist Policy Creates Uncertainty for Some Providers
papers.authors: Diane Schilder, Justin B. Doromal
papers.abstract: Diane Schilder, a senior fellow at the Urban Institute, prepared a written testimony with coauthor Justin Doromal, a senior research associate. They discuss how the proposed child care subsidy budget will impact DC parents, children, child care providers, and employers. The testimony focused on five key points: Research reveals robust child care subsidy funding produces benefits for working parents in terms of economic stability, for children in terms of school readiness, and for providers by stabilizing their businesses and educators. Subsidized child care providers serve over 7,000 children annually and rely on subsidies as well as funding from the Pay Equity Fund to provide high-quality child care to young children. The proposed subsidy budget may result in retaining a waitlist, which will delay access to affordable care for many DC families. States that have implemented child care subsidy waitlists have documented negative impacts on parental employment, children’s stable participation in
papers.idea: Eliminate or substantially reduce child care subsidy program waitlists to ensure continuous access and reduce gaps in parental employment and child care stability.
papers.summary: DC's child care subsidy program demonstrates measurable benefits for working parents' economic stability, children's school readiness, and provider business sustainability, serving over 7,000 children annually. However, the program's waitlist policy creates uncertainty for providers and delays access for families, with research from other states showing documented negative impacts on parental employment and children's participation continuity.
papers.method: Written testimony synthesizing research findings on subsidy program outcomes; appears to combine program data from DC (7,000+ children served) with comparative evidence from other states on waitlist impacts.
papers.scalability_note: Child care subsidy programs are standard municipal/state infrastructure; waitlist elimination is straightforward policy lever applicable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Requires expanded funding to clear waitlists; abstract does not specify incremental cost but notes program already produces ROI for employers and parents.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates child care subsidy program (NYCCARE); waitlist management and funding allocation are direct levers for city policy; directly parallel to DC context.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence of benefits for parental employment, child development, and provider stability; testimony cites robust research but focuses on policy risk (waitlists) rather than measuring a specific intervention's impact.
papers.underused_note: Child care subsidy waitlists remain common in many US cities despite documented harms; few jurisdictions have eliminated them despite strong economic case.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional policy argument (eliminate waitlists for access); framing as public-safety angle is somewhat novel but the core idea is standard child-welfare advocacy.

papers.title: Rising Heat in the Labor Market : The Impact of Rising Temperatures on Firms and Jobs in Europe
papers.abstract: This paper studies the impact of rising heat on labor markets across Europe by combining geocoded district-level, establishment-level, and wet-bulb temperature data. At the district level, it constructs a panel covering 1,525 districts across 32 countries from 1980 to 2023, disaggregated by 10 economic sectors to estimate dynamic labor market responses to wet-bulb temperature increases. The paper complements the analysis using representative, geocoded surveys covering more than 46,000 establishments. The granular microdata spanning many countries and decades are essential to identify the gradual impact of wet-bulb temperature changes on labor outcomes and allow assessing how firms and local labor markets respond to similar long-term trends under different institutional settings. The analysis finds that a one-standard deviation increase in yearly wet-bulb temperature (0.7 degrees Celsius) reduces employment growth by 0.3 percent two years later. From a long-term perspective, the results indicate that the increase in wet-bulb temperature since 1980 may have reduced employment in Europe by around 1.1 million jobs, equivalent to more than half of a normal year of employment growth. The effect is stronger in heat-exposed, low-wage, and labor-intensive sectors, as well as in small and young establishments, and varies systematically with regional income. The increased temperature leads to declines in total hours worked and gross value-added levels. In contrast to the negative employment impact, real average wage growth rises after temperature increases because low-skill workers and low-wage establishments bear the brunt of the employment impact within sectors. As a result, the composition of employment shifts toward higher-paid jobs. The impact of rising wet-bulb temperature is in part explained by limited adaptation. Fewer than one-third of the small establishments and about half of the large establishments in Europe invest in cooling technology, with establishments that pay lower wages and face less favorable local business environments being less likely to adapt.
papers.idea: Cities should subsidize or mandate cooling technology in small, low-wage establishments and labor-intensive sectors to preserve employment and hours worked during heat waves.
papers.summary: Rising wet-bulb temperatures reduced European employment growth by 0.3% per 0.7°C increase, with effects concentrated in heat-exposed, low-wage, and small firms. Fewer than one-third of small establishments have cooling technology, and those with lower wages and weaker business environments are least likely to adapt. The cumulative effect since 1980 may have cost Europe ~1.1 million jobs.
papers.effect_size: 0.3% reduction in employment growth per 0.7°C increase; ~1.1 million jobs lost in Europe since 1980
papers.reach: 1,525 districts across 32 European countries, 1980–2023; 46,000+ geocoded establishments
papers.method: Panel analysis of district-level employment and establishment-level microdata combined with wet-bulb temperature data across multiple countries and decades to estimate dynamic labor market responses.
papers.scalability_note: Cooling technology adoption programs can be deployed at city or sectoral level; financial incentives and enforcement are straightforward policy levers.
papers.cost_note: Cooling subsidies require upfront capital but may pay for themselves via retained employment, wages, and tax revenue; per-unit cost not provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has authority over economic development, small-business lending, and building codes; could tie NYCEDC grants or tax breaks to cooling investment in vulnerable sectors (restaurants, warehouses, retail).
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate but significant real-world impact (1.1M jobs lost in Europe; 0.3% employment growth reduction per 0.7°C increase); effect concentrated in vulnerable sectors and small firms.
papers.underused_note: Heat-adaptive labor policy is rarely integrated into city economic development or worker protection frameworks; NYC has no systematic cooling subsidies or mandates for low-wage workplaces despite rising summer temperatures and heat-exposed sectors.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes climate adaptation from environmental/health lens to labor-market and economic-security lens; evidence-based case for proactive workplace cooling as job preservation, not just worker comfort.

papers.title: Risk and Public Judgments on Police Pursuits: A Nationally Representative Conjoint Experiment
papers.authors: Scott M. Mourtgos, Ian T. Adams, Kyle McLean, Geoffrey P. Alpert
papers.abstract: Police vehicle pursuits emphasize a central tension between law enforcement imperatives and public safety. While modern policies emphasize balancing apprehension needs against risks of continuation, little is known about whether public intuitions mirror this framework. We address this gap using a nationally representative conjoint experiment ( N = 3,334) that varied eleven contextual factors across randomized pursuit scenarios. Respondents rated perceived risk, indicated whether pursuits should be terminated, and made forced-choice tradeoffs. Across all outcomes, high-risk situational factors (e.g., high speeds, adverse weather, heavy traffic, urban settings, etc.) increased perceived danger and termination support, while more serious underlying offenses, particularly violent felonies, reduced it. These patterns align with the proportional balancing logic embedded in contemporary policy and training. Findings suggest restrictive, risk-sensitive pursuit policies are broadly consistent with public expectations, though tolerance for risk in violent felony cases may limit support for outright prohibitions.
papers.idea: Calibrate police pursuit policies to align with demonstrated public risk tolerance by context (weather, traffic density, offense severity), grounding policy in public expectations rather than enforcement intuition alone.
papers.summary: A nationally representative conjoint experiment (N=3,334) found that public support for terminating police pursuits correlates with situational risk factors (speed, weather, traffic, urban setting) and inversely with offense severity. Respondents' risk-balancing judgments align with contemporary proportional-balancing frameworks in modern pursuit policy, though tolerance for risk increases markedly in violent felony cases.
papers.method: Nationally representative conjoint experiment in which respondents rated perceived risk and termination preferences across 11 randomized contextual factors in simulated pursuit scenarios.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are survey-based and policy-agnostic; any city can adapt risk-sensitive pursuit policies. Implementation requires command-level policy change, not infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: No implementation cost beyond policy retraining; survey itself was research expense, not operational.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD already has pursuit policies; this study validates proportional risk-balancing logic and can inform refinement, though violent-felony tolerance may complicate restrictive policies in high-crime contexts.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive conjoint study maps public preferences; no causal intervention tested or implemented, so no direct impact evidence on outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Public input on pursuit policy exists in some jurisdictions but is rarely systematized or empirically grounded; most policies still rely on law-enforcement judgment and liability concerns rather than calibrated public deliberation.
papers.novelty_note: Framing policy calibration around public risk tolerance is somewhat fresh; most debates focus on officer safety or suspect harm, not explicit public-expectation alignment.

papers.title: A Renewed Effort to Control and Eliminate Congenital Syphilis in the United States
papers.authors: Chrysovalantis Stafylis, Jeffrey D. Klausner
papers.idea: Implement or strengthen congenital syphilis screening and treatment programs in prenatal care to reduce maternal-to-child transmission.
papers.summary: No abstract available; title suggests a policy or programmatic review of efforts to control and eliminate congenital syphilis in the U.S.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract provided.
papers.scalability_note: Congenital syphilis prevention is a standard, replicable public health intervention applicable across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Prenatal screening and antibiotic treatment are inexpensive relative to neonatal infection costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established prenatal care infrastructure; syphilis screening is routine but could be strengthened if gaps are identified.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Prenatal syphilis treatment prevents 98%+ of congenital transmission; well-established public health intervention with robust evidence base.
papers.underused_note: Despite proven efficacy, congenital syphilis rates have surged in recent years; implementation gaps persist in under-resourced prenatal care systems, particularly affecting low-income communities.
papers.novelty_note: Standard public health practice; not a novel concept, but framing as a city-level public-safety/health priority may be underappreciated.

papers.title: Telehealth and Hybrid Service Use for Major Depression and Substance Use Disorders Among U.S. Adults, 2022–2023
papers.authors: Wenhua Lu, Yifan Liu, Anderson Sungmin Yoon
papers.idea: Expand telehealth and hybrid (in-person + remote) mental health and addiction treatment services through NYC Department of Health and community providers to increase access to care for depression and substance use disorders.
papers.summary: This study examined patterns of telehealth and hybrid service use for major depression and substance use disorders among U.S. adults in 2022–2023. The research likely documents prevalence of telehealth adoption, demographic differences in access, and potentially treatment engagement or outcomes by service modality, though specifics cannot be determined from the title and abstract alone.
papers.method: Descriptive epidemiological study analyzing telehealth and hybrid service use patterns for depression and substance use disorders among U.S. adults during 2022–2023; exact data source and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Telehealth infrastructure is technology-enabled and replicable across health systems and geographic regions with minimal capital constraints.
papers.cost_note: Telehealth typically reduces per-visit costs relative to in-person care and reduces patient travel burden; specific cost data not provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has advanced health IT infrastructure and large populations with depression and substance use disorders; aligns with existing HHC and community health center expansion efforts.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Telehealth expands access and utilization; moderate evidence for comparable clinical outcomes to in-person, but not a crime-reduction intervention per se.
papers.underused_note: Telehealth expanded during COVID but remains fragmented; NYC has pockets of adoption but not systematic, well-funded citywide integration for SUD/mental health.
papers.novelty_note: Telehealth expansion is now familiar post-pandemic; framing as a public-safety lever is somewhat fresher but not entirely novel.

papers.title: Partisan Versus Nonpartisan School Board Elections
papers.authors: Andrew J. Hill, Daniel B. Jones, Breyon J. Williams
papers.abstract: School boards are central to local education policy and governance in the United States. Given their localized nature, the methods of electing school boards vary widely. Most boards are elected on a nonpartisan basis—where candidate partisan affiliation does not appear on the ballot—but in some states, there has been a shift toward partisan elections. Our paper focuses on North Carolina, where a sizable fraction of districts has recently shifted to partisan elections. Using a difference-in-differences approach, we find that partisan elections affect the partisan, racial, and gender composition of boards. We then explore impacts beyond politics, testing for changes in school and student outcomes.
papers.idea: NYC could study or pilot shifting school board elections from nonpartisan to partisan format (or vice versa) to test effects on board composition, diversity, and student outcomes.
papers.summary: This difference-in-differences study examined North Carolina school districts that shifted to partisan board elections, finding changes in the partisan, racial, and gender composition of elected boards. The authors tested whether these electoral-format changes also affected school and student outcomes beyond demographic shifts.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences design comparing North Carolina school districts that shifted to partisan elections versus those that remained nonpartisan, analyzing board composition and school/student outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Electoral rules are easy to change and apply uniformly across districts; NYC already has school board elections and could test format shifts.
papers.cost_note: Changing ballot format is minimal-cost; main cost is research/analysis to measure effects.
papers.nyc_note: NYC school board elections are currently nonpartisan; city could study whether partisan format would improve board diversity or student outcomes, but abstract does not report the actual effects found, limiting actionability.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study shows compositional changes to boards but limited evidence of direct student outcome improvements.
papers.underused_note: Electoral format is actively debated in education policy circles; not a neglected idea, though implementation remains contested.
papers.novelty_note: Institutional design question is well-known in political science; framing as NYC pilot is practical but not conceptually fresh.

papers.title: Management of Health Care Facilities and Patient Attendance during Major Disruptions : Evidence from Kenya
papers.abstract: This paper measures and analyzes management practices in the Kenyan health care sector, drawing on a nationally representative survey and linked administrative data. The paper adapts the World Management Survey to measure management quality in primary health care facilities and hospitals, surveying 429 primary health care facilities and 73 hospitals. Primary health care facilities are the primary point of contact for most patients, providing treatment for common infectious diseases and chronic conditions, as well as services related to maternal and child health. Management quality is low on average, and the distribution is highly compressed. The analysis uses administrative data to test the association between the management quality and performance of primary health care facilities, measured by outpatient attendance, during a period of disruption that included the COVID-19 pandemic and a public health workers' strike. Overall attendance fell during this period. Private facilities experienced a smaller decline than public facilities, consistent with substitution during the strike. Within the private sector, better-managed facilities showed greater resilience, driven primarily by operations management. These results underscore the role of management quality in strengthening facility-level resilience and the complementarity of public and private sectors in absorbing healthcare shocks.
papers.idea: Audit and improve operations management practices in public health facilities to increase resilience during service disruptions and maintain patient attendance.
papers.summary: The study surveyed 429 primary health care facilities and 73 hospitals in Kenya to measure management quality and its relationship to patient attendance during COVID-19 and a health workers' strike. Overall attendance fell during disruption, but private facilities and better-managed facilities showed greater resilience, with operations management being a key driver. The findings suggest that management quality—particularly operations practices—strengthens facility-level resilience to shocks.
papers.reach: 429 primary health care facilities and 73 hospitals in Kenya
papers.method: Observational study using a nationally representative survey adapted from the World Management Survey, linked with administrative data on patient attendance during a disruption period including COVID-19 and a public sector strike.
papers.scalability_note: Management practice assessment and operational improvements are generalizable across health systems and can be systematized; Kenya data suggests applicability to urban health infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Management audits and operational improvements are relatively low-cost; primarily require staff training and process redesign rather than capital investment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has centralized public health infrastructure (DOHMH) where operations audits could be conducted; however, Kenya's primary health facility structure differs somewhat from NYC's clinic network, limiting direct transferability.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Observational study showing correlation between management quality and resilience during disruptions; causal inference limited but plausible mechanism demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Operations management interventions in public health systems are rarely systematized or invested in; most focus on funding/staffing rather than process improvement.
papers.novelty_note: Applying management science metrics to public health is somewhat fresh for the sector, but operations management itself is conventional in other industries.

papers.title: Family Matters : Social Networks and Women’s Economic Empowerment
papers.abstract: Social networks are a critical driver of women’s economic empowerment, particularly in contexts where women face mobility and normative constraints. Using baseline data from an impact evaluation in Nigeria, this study examines the association between social capital and labor market outcomes among vulnerable women, including those affected by HIV, survivors of gender-based violence, and caregivers of children living with HIV or exposed to sexual violence. Logistic and OLS models along with the community fixed effects are used to estimate the associations between social network characteristics and four labor market outcomes. The study finds that women with larger networks—specifically, having a family member in any occupation—are associated with 0.776 higher log-odds of paid work, two additional weekly work hours, and US$ 2 higher weekly earnings. Occupational diversity and prestige within family networks are also positively associated with improved labor outcomes, while weak ties (with friends and acquaintances) show limited effects. These findings, which remain consistent across Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) and post-LASSO analyses, highlight the importance of strong, family-based occupational networks and support targeted investments in women’s social capital to promote economic empowerment.
papers.idea: NYC could establish or expand occupational mentorship and network-building programs targeting economically vulnerable women (especially survivors of violence, caregivers, and those affected by HIV) that connect them to employed family members and professionals in diverse occupations.
papers.summary: The study found that vulnerable women in Nigeria with larger family networks—particularly those with family members in paid work—had significantly higher odds of employment, more weekly work hours, and higher weekly earnings. Occupational diversity and prestige within family networks mattered, while weak ties had limited effects.
papers.effect_size: 0.776 higher log-odds of paid work, +2 weekly work hours, +$2 weekly earnings for women with family members in any occupation
papers.method: Cross-sectional analysis using baseline data from an impact evaluation in Nigeria; logistic and OLS regression models with community fixed effects examining associations between social network characteristics and four labor market outcomes among vulnerable women.
papers.scalability_note: Network-building and mentorship programs are replicable and can be deployed through existing NYC workforce development, nonprofits, and social service agencies; though context-specific adaptation needed.
papers.cost_note: Program costs depend on model (peer-matching vs. formal mentorship), but likely moderate relative to potential earnings gains.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive workforce development infrastructure (NYC Department of Consumer Affairs, nonprofit job-training orgs), social services for survivors and caregivers, and HIV support networks; adapting them to explicitly build occupational networks is feasible.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate positive associations (0.776 log-odds for paid work, $2/week earnings gain) in observational data; meaningful but not transformative, and causality unclear.
papers.underused_note: Social-capital and occupational-mentorship programs for vulnerable women remain underfunded and fragmented in NYC despite evidence of economic benefit.
papers.novelty_note: Family-network activation as economic pathway is not entirely new, but framing it as a primary lever for vulnerable women (especially DV survivors, caregivers) is somewhat fresh.

papers.title: Change in Income Packaging Before and After Prison
papers.authors: Anke Ramakers, Robert Apel, Paul Nieuwbeerta, Anja Dirkzwager
papers.abstract: Objective Apply the concept of income packaging to formerly incarcerated men by examining how an individual's income portfolio changes from pre- to post-incarceration, in terms of total income, source-specific income, and income shares. Method A zero-inflated Poisson model is used to examine changes in income likelihood (extensive margin) and amount (intensive margin), both for total income and five different income sources (formal work, public assistance, informal work, illegal activities, and other sources). To examine changes in all income shares simultaneously, a fractional estimator represents a multinomial logistic regression of proportions (the proportion of total income from each of five sources). Results Individuals experience substantial income loss upon release from prison. Formal employment and public assistance are highly stable sources of income. There is no reduction in the number of workers, but workers do experience decay in earnings. Public assistance serves as an important safety net upon release, as more individuals rely on it after imprisonment. Conclusion The findings reveal how returning individuals make ends meet from smaller budgets and highlight the potential utility of concepts as income packaging and bureaucratic capital in refining theories and understanding the financial wellbeing of released individuals.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Expand public assistance safety net and employment support programs for returning citizens to stabilize post-release income and reduce recidivism risk.
papers.summary: Using regression models on income data from formerly incarcerated men, the study found substantial income loss upon release from prison, with formal employment and public assistance serving as the most stable income sources. Public assistance uptake increased post-release, suggesting it functions as an important safety net for returning citizens managing reduced budgets.
papers.method: Zero-inflated Poisson and fractional multinomial logistic regression models analyzing changes in income sources and amounts before and after incarceration; study design, sample size, and data source not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Policy levers (public assistance, employment programs) are standard and scalable, but finding is about income sources post-release, not a tested intervention.
papers.cost_note: Depends on scope of assistance expansion; no cost data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust reentry programs and public assistance infrastructure; expanding employment support or assistance eligibility for returning citizens is directly feasible.
papers.effect_size: Substantial income loss upon release from prison; increased reliance on public assistance
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents substantial income loss and identifies public assistance as stabilizing factor, but does not directly measure recidivism reduction from expanded programs.
papers.underused_note: Public assistance for returning citizens remains fragmented, underfunded, and stigmatized; few cities systematically optimize 'income packaging' post-release despite clear need.
papers.novelty_note: Framing reentry through 'income packaging' and 'bureaucratic capital' concepts is fresher than standard employment-only approaches, though safety-net expansion itself is conventional.

papers.title: Beliefs and Actions under Government Policy Uncertainty: Evidence from Student Loan Forgiveness
papers.authors: Dmitri K. Koustas, Michael Weber, Constantine Yannelis
papers.abstract: How does uncertainty about future government policy affect households’ beliefs and subsequent borrowing, spending and debt payment behavior? We study these questions through the lens of student loan forgiveness in the United States, which following electoral promises, was announced in 2022 but never implemented due to judicial rulings. We conduct a customized information provision experiment embedded in a survey eliciting real-time beliefs about future debt forgiveness and repayment, which we link to credit bureau data, employment verification data, and nondurables consumption. Eligible borrowers who are more optimistic about forgiveness reduce payments on student loans by \$40 per month and increase non-durable spending by $100 per month. We also find some evidence optimistic borrowers may postpone durable spending waiting for uncertainty to resolve. Borrowers optimistic about future payment pauses make fewer payments on their student loans, reduce payment by $40 per month and are 7.5 percentage points more likely to be delinquent after payments resume. A quantification exercise finds welfare losses from incorrect beliefs can exceed 43 percent of the initial loan balance. Our results provide micro-evidence on the role of policy uncertainty in household decision-making, and have implications for government announcements and commitment policy.
papers.idea: Cities could clarify and commit to transparent timelines for government benefits/policies to reduce household uncertainty and improve financial decision-making.
papers.summary: The study examined how uncertainty about student loan forgiveness affected household beliefs and behavior using an information experiment linked to credit bureau data. Borrowers optimistic about forgiveness reduced loan payments by $40/month and increased spending by $100/month, while those expecting payment pauses were 7.5 percentage points more likely to become delinquent; welfare losses from incorrect beliefs exceeded 43% of initial loan balance.
papers.method: Customized information provision experiment embedded in survey eliciting beliefs about debt forgiveness, linked to credit bureau data, employment verification, and consumption data; setting was U.S. student loan market during 2022 forgiveness announcement.
papers.scalability_note: Federal student loans are not a municipal lever; cities cannot implement loan forgiveness or repayment policy.
papers.cost_note: Study itself required survey + data linkage; policy implementation (if applicable federally) depends on mechanism.
papers.nyc_note: Student loan policy is federal. NYC has no direct authority over loan forgiveness or repayment terms.
papers.effect_size: $40 per month reduction in student loan payments and $100 per month increase in non-durable spending for optimistic borrowers; 7.5 percentage points higher delinquency likelihood
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effects on household financial behavior ($40/month loan payment reduction, $100/month spending increase, 7.5pp delinquency rise); welfare losses substantial but study is correlational on real outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Policy clarity/transparency is recognized as important, but the specific application to NYC benefit programs (SNAP, housing vouchers, tax credits) remains underdeveloped; not a major gap.
papers.novelty_note: Framing policy uncertainty as a direct lever for household financial stability is somewhat fresh; most city interventions focus on access/eligibility rather than belief/clarity management.

papers.title: Satellite‐Derived Environmental Metrics and the Micro-Spatial Ecology of Crime: Extending Routine Activity and Social Disorganization Theory with Big, Complex Data
papers.authors: Matthew DeMichele, Joshua D. Freilich
papers.idea: Use satellite-derived environmental metrics to identify micro-spatial crime patterns and inform targeted place-based crime prevention.
papers.summary: This paper extends routine activity and social disorganization theory by incorporating satellite-derived environmental data to analyze crime at fine spatial scales. The study appears to develop a methodological framework for linking environmental metrics (likely vegetation, built environment, land use) to micro-level crime variation.
papers.method: Theoretical and methodological paper extending criminological theory with satellite imagery data; no empirical results stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Satellite data widely available; methodology transferable across cities.
papers.cost_note: Satellite imagery is increasingly free/low-cost; analysis requires technical capacity.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has satellite coverage and agencies capable of spatial analysis; could inform place-based prevention.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Satellite metrics show moderate predictive promise for micro-spatial crime patterns, but actual crime reduction effects from deployment are not demonstrated in this conceptual/methods paper.
papers.underused_note: Satellite environmental data for crime prevention is theoretically sound but rarely operationalized in major US police departments; most still rely on traditional hotspot mapping.
papers.novelty_note: Applying satellite imagery to routine activity theory is fresher than conventional crime mapping, but the underlying theories (routine activity, social disorganization) are well-established.

papers.title: Reducing Child Neglect
papers.authors: Marla McDaniel
papers.abstract: Child neglect accounts for most child welfare system involvement in the United States, yet it remains understudied and inadequately prevented. Neglect is closely tied to material hardship, suggesting contributing conditions are often economic rather than willful disregard. This policy brief reports national statistics on neglect, summarizes causal research on prevention, and explores how economic supports and legislative choices shape neglect rates. It shows why current evidence is insufficient and how the field can adopt a more deliberate, measurable agenda to reduce neglect safely. Why This Matters National child welfare data consistently show that neglect drives most child welfare system involvement. Understanding how income supports, neglect definitions, and reporting rules shape neglect rates can help policymakers and administrators direct attention toward preventable conditions and reduce unnecessary child welfare involvement while ensuring child safety. Key Takeaways Neglect represents the majority of substantiated child maltreatment cases year after year. Neglect accounts for more
papers.idea: Expand economic support programs (income assistance, housing subsidies, food benefits) as a primary lever to reduce child neglect and prevent unnecessary child welfare system involvement.
papers.summary: Child neglect drives most child welfare system involvement in the US, yet it is understudied and under-prevented. The brief argues neglect is more often tied to material hardship and economic conditions than willful disregard, and that income supports, benefit definitions, and reporting policies significantly shape neglect rates. Understanding these linkages can help policymakers reduce both neglect and unnecessary system involvement while protecting child safety.
papers.method: Policy brief synthesizing national child welfare statistics and causal research on neglect prevention; exact empirical methods and data sources not detailed in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Income support and social benefit programs scale easily across cities and states; proven infrastructure exists.
papers.cost_note: Expanding existing benefit programs (SNAP, housing vouchers, TANF) is cost-effective per child protected and may reduce costly child welfare involvement.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already administers robust human services; coordination between ACS, DSS, and housing/income support agencies to target neglect prevention is directly actionable.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Economic supports show moderate promise for reducing neglect; causal evidence exists but remains mixed and limited in rigor compared to other interventions.
papers.underused_note: Despite neglect driving majority of child welfare involvement, economic prevention remains significantly underfunded relative to investigation/removal infrastructure in most US cities including NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Reframing neglect as primarily economic rather than moral/parental failure is somewhat fresh for child welfare, though poverty-prevention logic is established elsewhere.

papers.title: How Effective Are Emission Trading Systems in Reducing Emissions ? Empirical Evidence from the EU, New Zealand, and the Republic of Korea
papers.abstract: Emission trading schemes are among the primary pricing instruments to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Currently, more than 35 emission trading schemes are in operation around the world at multinational, national, and subnational levels. How effective these systems are in reducing emissions is a key empirical question. This study examines the effectiveness of three large-scale emission trading schemes implemented by the European Union, New Zealand, and the Republic of Korea in reducing carbon dioxide emissions. The study employs a generalized synthetic control method on data from 2005–20. The findings show that the EU emission trading scheme led to a cumulative reduction of 21.3 percent in carbon dioxide emissions from the electricity sector and 15.6 lower total carbon dioxide emissions, relative to a scenario with no emission trading scheme. In New Zealand, the emission trading scheme had no statistically significant effect on total national carbon dioxide emissions—largely because nearly half of New Zealand’s emissions come from agriculture, a sector not covered by the country’s emission trading scheme. The effectiveness of Korea’s emission trading scheme varied substantially across phases. Although initially it did not halt the rise in emissions, it became a progressively strong driver of decarbonization over time.
papers.idea: NYC could design and implement a cap-and-trade emission trading scheme for large buildings and industrial facilities, modeled on the EU's proven approach, to achieve measurable reductions in carbon emissions.
