papers.summary: This study evaluated three large-scale emission trading schemes and found that the EU's scheme reduced electricity-sector CO₂ emissions by 21.3% and total emissions by 15.6%, while New Zealand's scheme had no significant effect (largely due to exclusion of agriculture), and Korea's scheme became increasingly effective over time. The findings suggest that well-designed trading schemes with broad sectoral coverage can drive meaningful decarbonization.
papers.effect_size: EU scheme: 21.3% reduction in electricity-sector emissions, 15.6% in total emissions
papers.reach: Three multinational/national schemes (EU, New Zealand, Korea) covering multiple sectors and millions of emissions sources over 2005–2020
papers.method: Generalized synthetic control method applied to national-level emissions data from 2005–2020 across three jurisdictions with operating emission trading schemes.
papers.scalability_note: Emission trading schemes are proven policy instruments operating at multiple scales; NYC could implement at city/state level, though requires regulatory authority and multi-sector coordination.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs not quantified in abstract; trading schemes typically have low per-unit compliance costs but require administrative infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has Local Law 97 (building emissions law) but not a full trading scheme; EU model could inform expansion, though NY State/federal policy frameworks would need alignment.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: EU showed 15.6% total emissions reduction (robust); NZ showed null effect; Korea showed phase-dependent results. Mixed but meaningful evidence.
papers.underused_note: NYC has Local Law 97 (buildings emissions cap); cap-and-trade concepts are discussed in climate policy circles, though full implementation lags.
papers.novelty_note: Cap-and-trade for emissions is well-established policy; applying EU model to NYC is conventional climate strategy, not a fresh insight.

papers.title: Using Multimodal Classroom Data to Measure Student Competencies for Upward Mobility
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project uses AI-supported analysis of recorded classroom interactions to uncover the student skills embedded in real‑time instructional exchanges and assess how well these competencies—spanning both core mathematical reasoning tied to upward mobility and more durable skills such as agency, vocabulary use, and self‑management where disparities often emerge—serve as early precursors of long‑run mobility. Drawing on classroom audio recordings, surveys, and linked administrative data from the Midwest and South, the team applies natural language processing and machine learning methods to develop scalable measures that capture how students demonstrate these competencies during middle school math lessons and then links them to key high school outcomes such as attendance, course taking, and GPA. Led by a leading scholar of classroom interaction and mathematics education and supported by established partnerships with districts and education technology firms, the project is positioned to produce scalable tools that help teachers identify and strengthen mobility‑relevant skills in
papers.idea: Deploy AI-powered classroom observation tools to identify and measure student competencies (agency, vocabulary, self-management) linked to long-term economic mobility, enabling teachers to target skill-building interventions in middle school.
papers.summary: The project develops machine-learning methods to analyze classroom audio recordings and identify student competencies—mathematical reasoning, agency, vocabulary use, and self-management—during middle school math lessons. These measures are then linked to downstream high school outcomes (attendance, course-taking, GPA) to validate their predictive power for long-run upward mobility.
papers.method: Natural language processing and machine learning applied to classroom audio recordings, surveys, and administrative data from districts in the Midwest and South; linked to high school outcomes to assess predictive validity.
papers.scalability_note: Requires classroom recording infrastructure, NLP/ML expertise, and district partnerships; replicable but not trivial to deploy at scale.
papers.cost_note: Likely moderate; depends on recording tech and ML platform licensing; per-school cost unclear from abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large middle school population, established ed-tech partnerships, and strong interest in equity-linked interventions; would require buy-in on classroom recording and staff capacity.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Promising correlational evidence linking classroom competencies to mobility outcomes, but study appears to be in development/validation phase; causal effects of teacher interventions not yet demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: AI classroom observation tools remain rare in US K-12 systems; even where deployed, focus typically on teacher evaluation rather than student skill identification for targeted intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes student 'soft skills' as measurable, observable classroom behaviors with direct economic mobility linkage; uses AI to operationalize constructs (agency, self-management) typically assessed only via surveys.

papers.title: Adjustable Product Attributes, Indirect Network Effects, and Subsidy Design: The Case of Electric Vehicles
papers.authors: Kevin Remmy
papers.abstract: This paper develops a structural model of endogenous product attribute choice in the presence of indirect network effects to study electric vehicle (EV) subsidies. Using data on the German EV market, I find that a support scheme almost doubled EV sales but substantially affected EVs’ price and driving range. These adjustments create a trade-off between optimizing different policy objectives when designing subsidies. Large purchase subsidies maximize EV sales, whereas large charging station subsidies maximize consumer and total surplus. The results suggest that maximizing EV sales can lead to unintended consequences in the form of price and range adjustments. (JEL D12, D22, D85, H25, L14, L62, R41)
papers.idea: Cities should prioritize charging-station subsidies over purchase subsidies when designing EV incentive programs, as this maximizes consumer welfare and total surplus while avoiding unintended price/range deterioration.
papers.summary: This structural economic model analyzed German EV subsidy design and found that large purchase subsidies roughly doubled EV sales but caused manufacturers to raise prices and reduce driving range. By contrast, large charging-station subsidies maximized consumer and total surplus without these negative externalities, revealing a policy trade-off between sales volume and welfare.
papers.effect_size: Purchase subsidies approximately doubled EV sales; charging subsidies optimize consumer surplus (quantified effect on surplus not stated in abstract)
papers.method: Structural econometric model of product-attribute choice under indirect network effects, estimated using data on the German EV market.
papers.scalability_note: Subsidy design is a standard municipal and state policy lever; findings transferable across markets with similar EV ecosystems.
papers.cost_note: Charging-station subsidies may be cheaper per car incentivized than purchase subsidies; exact per-unit costs not provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could apply these insights to its EV rebate and charging-infrastructure programs (e.g., NYC Charge On). Charging-station prioritization aligns with transit-equity and fleet-electrification goals.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrated moderate effects on sales (~2x) and measurable price/range trade-offs, but focused on market mechanics rather than direct public-safety or crime outcomes.
papers.underused_note: EV infrastructure policy is actively debated; however, the specific insight about charging-station vs. purchase subsidy trade-offs is underexplored in municipal incentive design.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes subsidy design around network effects and indirect consequences rather than simple purchase incentives; moderately fresh framing for local policy.

papers.title: The effects of comprehensive pay reform on achievement in urban schools
papers.authors: Eric Hanushek, Jin Luo, Andrew Morgan, Minh Nguyen, Ben Ost, Steven Rivkin, Ayman Shakeel
papers.idea: Cities can redesign teacher compensation systems to link pay more directly to student achievement, potentially improving academic outcomes in urban schools.
papers.summary: This paper evaluates the effects of comprehensive teacher pay reform on student achievement in urban schools. Without access to the abstract, the likely focus is on whether performance-based or reformed compensation structures improve student outcomes compared to traditional salary schedules.
papers.method: Unknown from title alone; likely quasi-experimental analysis comparing schools/districts that underwent pay reform to matched controls, using administrative achievement data.
papers.scalability_note: Teacher pay reform is system-wide by definition and replicable across districts.
papers.cost_note: Depends on whether reform increases or decreases total teacher compensation; reallocation is cheaper than new funding.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has direct control over teacher contracts and compensation; DOE could implement or expand performance-linked pay.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Performance pay for teachers shows mixed-to-modest effects in rigorous studies; gains typically small and inconsistent across contexts.
papers.underused_note: Merit pay and performance-based compensation are already widely piloted in US urban districts; not under-deployed relative to evidence strength.
papers.novelty_note: Teacher performance pay is conventional education-policy thinking; extensively debated and implemented for 15+ years.

papers.title: Behavioral, Information, and Monetary Interventions to Reduce Energy Consumption in Households: A Living Systematic Review and Network Meta‐Analysis
papers.authors:
Tarun M. Khanna, 
Diana Danilenko, 
Qianyi Wang, 
Luke A. Smith, 
Bhumika T. V., 
Aditya Narayan Rai, 
Jorge Sánchez Canales, 
Tim Repke, 
Max Callaghan, 
Mark Andor, 
Julian H. Elliott, 
Jan C. Minx
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities can implement behavioral nudges, information campaigns, and financial incentives (rebates, time-of-use pricing) to reduce household energy consumption, lowering utility bills and emissions.
papers.summary: This Campbell Collaboration systematic review and network meta-analysis synthesizes evidence on behavioral, informational, and monetary interventions to reduce residential energy use. The review identifies which combinations of approaches (e.g., feedback + financial incentives) work best and in what contexts.
papers.method: Living systematic review with network meta-analysis of randomized and quasi-experimental studies on household energy-reduction interventions; scope and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Behavioral nudges and pricing structures can be deployed city-wide through utility companies and municipal programs.
papers.cost_note: Behavioral/informational interventions typically low-cost; monetary incentives vary but often offset by energy savings.
papers.nyc_note: Con Edison and NYC's energy code/retrofit programs can incorporate evidence-based behavioral and pricing levers; strong existing regulatory/utility infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest effects on energy consumption; behavioral nudges typically reduce usage 1-3%, financial incentives somewhat larger but context-dependent.
papers.underused_note: Energy efficiency programs and rebates are already widely deployed by utilities and cities; not a gap-area for NYC investment.
papers.novelty_note: Standard toolkit (nudges, info, pricing) long established in energy policy; no new framing or mechanism.

papers.title: Self-Efficacy and Favorability Shape Learning from Tutoring Systems and Paper Practice
papers.authors: Xinfei Cen, Vincent Aleven, Kenneth R. Koedinger, Conrad Borchers, Paulo F. Carvalho
papers.abstract: Motivational factors such as self-efficacy and how favorably students feel toward practice play a crucial role in shaping learning, particularly in technology-supported environments. Yet, educational interventions often overlook how these factors interact with practice format. This paper examines the influence of self-efficacy and favorability on learning outcomes across two common practice formats: paper-based and system-based tutoring practice. Using a counterbalanced within-subject design with matched problem sets, we isolate the effect of practice format while modeling motivational differences. Results indicate that students with lower baseline self-efficacy achieved greater learning gains regardless of practice format. Among students with lower baseline self-efficacy, greater favorability toward the tutor was associated with greater learning gains during tutor practice, whereas the pattern differed in paper-based practice. Intelligent Tutoring System (ITS)-based practice did not significantly improve post-training self-efficacy relative to paper-based methods. These findings underscore the potential value of tailoring practice format to students' motivational profiles, as the benefits of tutor- and paper-based practice varied with baseline self-efficacy and favorability. They lay the groundwork for future research on how instructional formats can be aligned more effectively with learners' motivational needs.
papers.idea: Tailor tutoring system vs. paper-based practice formats to students' baseline self-efficacy and favorability profiles to improve learning outcomes.
papers.summary: The study examined how self-efficacy and student favorability toward practice methods interact with practice format (paper-based vs. intelligent tutoring system) to shape learning. Students with lower baseline self-efficacy gained more from both formats, but favorability mattered differently: for low-efficacy students, favorability toward tutors predicted gains in system-based practice but not paper-based practice.
papers.method: Within-subject counterbalanced design with matched problem sets; students practiced using both tutoring system and paper formats, with motivational factors measured and learning outcomes assessed.
papers.scalability_note: Educational practices are routinely adopted across schools and districts; can scale to any tutoring program.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost provided; implementation involves instructional design adjustments rather than new infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC schools use tutoring and ITS systems; finding is relevant to curriculum design but requires translation to classroom context and doesn't address a NYC-specific policy lever or urgent systemic problem.
papers.effect_size: Students with lower baseline self-efficacy achieved greater learning gains regardless of practice format; ITS-based practice did not significantly improve post-training self-efficacy relative to paper-based methods
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest conditional effects; no significant improvement in self-efficacy; learning gains tied to baseline low self-efficacy rather than intervention strength.
papers.underused_note: Personalized learning and adaptive tutoring are increasingly mainstream in EdTech; this specific motivational-matching approach is niche but not absent from practice.
papers.novelty_note: Tailoring instruction to student motivation is well-established; framing as format-matching to self-efficacy profiles is incremental, not fundamentally new.

papers.title: News headline themes and approval of police use-of-force: evidence from a national survey experiment
papers.authors: Scott M. Mourtgos
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study tests whether headline themes from news coverage of police use-of-force influence general public approval of legally reasonable police use-of-force. Methods In a between-subjects survey experiment ( N = 2,495), a nationally stratified sample of U.S. adults was randomly assigned to view four real newspaper headlines representing one of four thematic conditions or a neutral control. The headline themes were derived from a structural topic model of three decades of news coverage. Respondents then completed three items measuring approval of police use-of-force. Treatment effects were estimated using Bayesian model averaging across four ordinal regression models. Results Protest & Reform and Race–themed headlines reduced approval by about 7% points relative to control. National Politics–themed headlines showed a smaller and more uncertain reduction. Official Statements produced no detectable difference. Conclusions Headline themes from real news coverage can influence general attitudes toward police use-of-force.
papers.idea: City communications offices could strategically frame police use-of-force news coverage to emphasize official statements rather than protest/reform themes to maintain public approval.
papers.summary: This survey experiment found that newspaper headlines emphasizing protest, reform, and race themes reduced public approval of police use-of-force by about 7 percentage points compared to neutral control, while official statement themes showed no effect. The study analyzed how different thematic framings of real news coverage influence attitudes toward police.
papers.method: Between-subjects survey experiment (N=2,495) with nationally stratified U.S. adults randomly assigned to view one of four real newspaper headlines or a neutral control, then rating approval of police use-of-force on three items.
papers.scalability_note: Findings about media framing effects are difficult to operationalize into direct city interventions; media are independent actors.
papers.cost_note: Research-generated insight with no direct implementation cost, but any intervention would require coordination with independent news outlets.
papers.nyc_note: Study identifies correlational relationship between headlines and attitudes but offers no actionable lever a city agency could pull; news editorial decisions are outside municipal control.
papers.effect_size: 7 percentage point reduction in approval of police use-of-force
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 7-percentage-point shifts are modest; effect is real but small in practical terms; no effect for Official Statements framing.
papers.underused_note: Strategic news framing by government is already common practice; this is mainstream communications strategy, not an underdeployed intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Framing effects on political attitudes are well-established; using this to suppress reform messaging is conventional political communication, not a new insight.

papers.title: “I Really Didn’t Need Treatment, It Was Just a Way for Me Not to Go to Jail”: Court-Mandated Drug Treatment Experiences among Racially Minoritized Individuals in Three US States
papers.authors: Laura Nicole Sisson, Adrienne Hurst, Daliah Heller, Sachini Bandara, Brendan Saloner, Olivia K. Sugarman
papers.idea: Redesign court-mandated drug treatment programs to ensure genuine therapeutic intent rather than incarceration avoidance, with voluntary enrollment and wraparound services tailored to racial equity.
papers.summary: This qualitative study documents experiences of racially minoritized individuals in court-mandated drug treatment across three states, revealing that participants often viewed treatment as a coercive alternative to jail rather than a genuine health intervention. The research suggests current mandated treatment programs may fail to achieve therapeutic goals and disproportionately burden communities of color.
papers.method: Qualitative interview study with racially minoritized individuals across three US states who experienced court-mandated drug treatment; no sample size or detailed methodology provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Treatment policy redesign is replicable across jurisdictions; criminal justice reform is established practice.
papers.cost_note: Reframing existing mandated treatment into voluntary models with wraparound services could reduce incarceration costs; no per-unit data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established drug court programs and criminal justice diversion infrastructure; findings directly applicable to local practice reform.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study documenting experiences; no quantified treatment outcomes or recidivism data presented. Identifies program design problems but not solution efficacy.
papers.underused_note: Drug courts exist widely but are critiqued; redesign toward equity and genuine therapeutics is piecemeal. Gap between evidence of dysfunction and system-wide reform is moderate.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes court-mandated treatment as potentially coercive rather than rehabilitative; equity angle is fresher, though critiques of drug courts as net-widening are established.

papers.title: Explaining the Gap in Upward Transfer Rates Between Economically Advantaged and Disadvantaged Community College Students
papers.authors: Audrey Light, Sydney Schreiner Wertz
papers.abstract: Using administrative data, we estimate models of community college students’ upward transfer decisions and compute each covariate’s contribution to the gap in transfer rates between economically advantaged and disadvantaged students and, alternatively, between white and Black students. We find that college entry age, completed credits, and enrollment duration account for over 50% of the economically advantaged–disadvantaged gap and 92% of the racial/ethnic gap; this combined effect is robust to alternative definitions of transfer. Many other factors are associated with increased transfer rates, but our findings suggest that economically disadvantaged, Black, and Hispanic students cannot catch up to their peers in transfer rates without starting college earlier and earning more credits—changes that are likely to require pre-college interventions and financial assistance.
papers.idea: NYC can fund and expand pre-college preparation and financial assistance programs targeting economically disadvantaged students to increase college entry age, credit completion, and enrollment duration—key drivers of community college transfer rates.
papers.summary: The study analyzed community college administrative data to decompose gaps in upward transfer rates between economically advantaged and disadvantaged students, and between racial/ethnic groups. College entry age, completed credits, and enrollment duration account for over 50% of the economic gap and 92% of the racial/ethnic gap in transfer rates, suggesting these factors are critical bottlenecks that pre-college interventions and financial aid could address.
papers.effect_size: College entry age, credits completed, and enrollment duration explain >50% of economic transfer gap and 92% of racial/ethnic transfer gap
papers.method: Observational analysis of administrative data from community college students; authors estimated regression models of transfer decisions and decomposed covariate contributions to gaps between demographic groups.
papers.scalability_note: Pre-college interventions and financial aid are standard policy levers easily applied across school systems and community colleges.
papers.cost_note: Pre-college prep and need-based aid require meaningful public investment, but benefits accrue over lifetime earnings and educational attainment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already runs CUNY system and funds K–12 pre-college programs; could expand these or partner with community colleges to target entry age, credit-load support, and financial barriers.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies significant contribution (50–92%) of modifiable factors to transfer-rate gaps, but is observational; doesn't measure intervention impact directly.
papers.underused_note: Pre-college prep and need-based aid are under-resourced in NYC relative to demonstrated explanatory power; most community colleges lack aggressive early-entry and credit-completion support.
papers.novelty_note: Framing college entry age and credit completion as levers is sensible but not counterintuitive; pre-college and financial aid are established ideas.

papers.title: Social Capital, Socioemotional Competencies, and the Transition to Young Adulthood
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project investigates how two core socioemotional competencies—grit and cognitive engagement—shape students’ long-term mobility outcomes, including college enrollment, employment, and earnings. Using rich longitudinal data from the Wake County Public School System, the researchers will track the development of these competencies over the critical transition from middle to high school (eighth to ninth grade) and explore how malleable contextual factors, such as teacher-student relationships and peer support for learning, foster or constrain the growth of such competencies. The project will assess how relationships among these core competencies, contextual factors, and long-run mobility outcomes vary by gender, race and ethnicity, and economic background. By focusing on nonparent relationships and early indicators of engagement—issues central to current conversations about high school redesign and the supports needed for opportunity youth (i.e., individuals neither enrolled in college nor employed)—this work will offer policy‑relevant evidence on how schools can strengthen core and under investigated competencies
papers.idea: Schools can strengthen teacher-student relationships and peer support systems during the middle-to-high school transition to improve long-term employment, earnings, and college enrollment outcomes, especially for disadvantaged students.
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tracks how two socioemotional competencies—grit and cognitive engagement—developed during the eighth-to-ninth grade transition predict college enrollment, employment, and earnings. The research examines how contextual factors like teacher-student relationships and peer support foster or constrain growth in these competencies, with stratified analysis by gender, race/ethnicity, and economic background.
papers.method: Longitudinal observational study using rich administrative data from Wake County Public School System tracking students' socioemotional competencies and long-term outcomes over time.
papers.scalability_note: School-based interventions on teacher-student relationships and peer support are replicable across districts; requires training and system redesign but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost—requires teacher professional development and restructuring support systems; no per-unit cost stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Education could integrate findings into high school redesign initiatives and opportunity youth programs; aligns with existing equity and student support priorities.
papers.reach: Wake County Public School System students (eighth to ninth grade transition)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Longitudinal design with strong theoretical foundation; grit/engagement → outcomes pathway is well-established, but study is prospective/observational rather than experimental intervention evaluation.
papers.underused_note: Teacher-student relationship investment and peer support programs remain underfunded relative to evidence; most school reform focuses on curriculum/testing, not relationship infrastructure.
papers.novelty_note: Socioemotional learning and grit are now mainstream in education discourse; framing is sensible but not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Female Labor Force Participation and Gender Norms : A Machine Learning Analysis of the Arab Republic of Egypt
papers.abstract: This paper addresses two gaps in the female labor force participation literature: the integrated analysis of individual, household, community, and national determinants, and the use of machine learning techniques to capture nonlinear dynamics. Using decision trees, the analysis identifies key drivers, thresholds, and sequences shaping women’s labor participation in the Arab Republic of Egypt—which remains below regional and global averages. Drawing on 2021–22 Arab Barometer data, the analysis finds that while gender norms matter, access to childcare is the most decisive factor. The paper concludes by outlining priorities for future research on the complex dynamics of female labor decision-making within and beyond Egypt.
papers.idea: Expand subsidized childcare access as a lever to increase female labor force participation and household economic mobility in underserved NYC neighborhoods.
papers.summary: This study used machine learning on Arab Barometer data (2021–22) from Egypt to identify drivers of female labor force participation. While gender norms matter, access to childcare emerged as the single most decisive factor shaping women's employment decisions. The analysis identified key thresholds and decision sequences, highlighting childcare as the primary policy leverage point.
papers.reach: Arab Barometer 2021–22 survey data from Egypt; sample size not stated in abstract
papers.method: Machine learning decision-tree analysis of individual, household, community, and national determinants using 2021–22 Arab Barometer survey data from Egypt.
papers.scalability_note: Childcare policy is readily scalable across neighborhoods and boroughs; model findings are generalizable if context parallels hold.
papers.cost_note: Childcare subsidies are moderately expensive per beneficiary but generate offsetting tax revenue and economic gains from increased labor participation.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already funds childcare via NYC Care and other programs; findings suggest targeting expansion toward underemployed women could improve labor force attachment and household income.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Strong correlational finding on childcare access as decisive factor, but study is observational (Egypt context) with no experimental validation of NYC policy impact.
papers.underused_note: Childcare subsidy expansion is underfunded relative to evidence; NYC has limited sliding-scale options and long waitlists despite documented labor-participation barriers.
papers.novelty_note: Childcare-as-economic-mobility lever is well-established in policy circles; framing is sensible but not counterintuitive or fresh.

papers.title: How Small and Medium-Sized Businesses Can Benefit from Registered Apprenticeships
papers.authors: Bhavani Arabandi, Andrew Campbell, Lindsey Tyson
papers.abstract: Small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) face persistent challenges finding and keeping skilled workers in a tight labor market. At the same time, registered apprenticeships—paid, work-based training programs that combine on-the-job learning with related instruction—have expanded rapidly across the United States and demonstrated strong returns for employers. This brief examines how SMBs can use registered apprenticeships to address talent shortages, drawing on lessons from employers in North and South Carolina. Through interviews with business leaders and a review of existing evidence, the brief highlights the benefits of apprenticeships for SMBs and outlines practical approaches to adopting this model. Why This Matters Employer surveys consistently show that talent shortages are widespread across industries and regions, and SMBs are often at a disadvantage when competing with larger firms for experienced workers. Limited recruiting capacity, fewer resources for training, and higher vulnerability to turnover can constrain growth and productivity for smaller employers. Registered apprenticeships
papers.idea: NYC can establish a city-supported registered apprenticeship program targeting SMBs to reduce youth unemployment and provide paid skill training pathways.
papers.summary: The brief documents that registered apprenticeships—paid, work-based training combining on-the-job learning with instruction—help SMBs address persistent worker shortages while providing strong returns for employers. Lessons from North and South Carolina employers show practical adoption pathways for smaller firms with limited training resources and recruitment capacity.
papers.method: Qualitative interviews with business leaders and review of existing evidence on registered apprenticeships in North and South Carolina.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship programs are modular and replicable across sectors and regions; infrastructure already exists in some jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Apprenticeships are employer-funded (trainees are paid workers); public cost depends on administrative support and subsidies, typically modest per enrollee.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has apprenticeship infrastructure and a large SMB sector facing hiring challenges; program could leverage existing workforce boards and CUNY partnerships.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence of positive outcomes for participant earnings and employment, but study relies on interviews and review rather than rigorous experimental design; real-world results vary by program quality.
papers.underused_note: Registered apprenticeships remain underfunded and underutilized in NYC despite federal support; most apprenticeships concentrate in construction/trades, not scaled to SMBs broadly.
papers.novelty_note: Apprenticeship model itself is well-established; framing as SMB talent solution is practical but not particularly counterintuitive or fresh.

papers.title: Counteracting the Impact of Societal Aging on Income Inequality : A Case Study of Malaysia
papers.abstract: This paper analyzes the effect of societal aging on income inequality in Malaysia, to date and in the coming decades. The study starts from the hypothesis that, all things equal, aging exerts upward pressure on inequality (Deaton and Paxson (1994, 1995)). Drawing on nationally representative household survey data over the past two decades, the study finds evidence in support of this hypothesis in Malaysia. This picture becomes even sharper when projecting inequality levels for the years when Malaysia is forecast to reach aged (2045) and super-aged (2056) status. Estimates show that between 2022 and 2056, overall inequality of individual incomes could rise by as much as 13 percent due to aging. The analysis then demonstrates that expansion of a social pension system would attenuate this projected rise in inequality. The paper also undertakes counterfactual exercises simulating the expansion of an old age social pension with various design variants. The variants considered would expand existing social transfers for older people. Given the public revenue and spending patterns in Malaysia, some of these design options are potentially manageable from a fiscal perspective. The analysis suggests that, relative to the observed situation in 2022, wider coverage of social pensions could have resulted in lower inequality in household per capita income among older individuals (by 12–26 percent), thereby lowering overall inequality (by 4-9 percent). This inequality-reducing effect of social pensions becomes even more marked when projecting estimates of inequality for 2045 and 2056. Moreover, the findings show that expanded pensions would have resulted in a lower headcount poverty rate in 2022 by 1.3–4.2 percentage points among the 60+ population, and by 0.5–1.9 points among the overall population.
papers.idea: Expand social pension programs for older adults (60+) to reduce income inequality and elderly poverty, modeled on tested design variants.
papers.summary: Using household survey data from Malaysia over two decades, the study finds that societal aging naturally exerts upward pressure on income inequality, projected to increase by 13% by 2056. Expanding social pension coverage could counteract this effect, reducing elderly income inequality by 12–26% and overall population inequality by 4–9%, while lowering poverty rates among seniors by 1.3–4.2 percentage points.
papers.effect_size: 4–9% reduction in overall inequality; 1.3–4.2 percentage point reduction in senior poverty headcount
papers.method: Observational analysis using nationally representative household survey data over two decades, combined with microsimulation modeling of counterfactual pension expansion scenarios.
papers.scalability_note: Social pension programs are standard policy tools implemented nationwide; design variants are administratively replicable.
papers.cost_note: Study notes design options are 'potentially manageable from a fiscal perspective' given Malaysia's revenue/spending patterns; exact per-capita costs not stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has senior assistance programs (SCRIE, DRIE, SSI supplements); expansion would require city/state budget action, but policy model is directly adaptable. Malaysia context differs (developing economy, different tax base).
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect: modeled expansion shows 4-9% overall inequality reduction and 0.5-1.9pp poverty reduction, but estimates are counterfactual simulations rather than randomized or quasi-experimental evidence.
papers.underused_note: Social pensions for elderly are established policy in many countries; Malaysia case is context-specific and NYC already has senior support programs, though expansion merit varies by design.
papers.novelty_note: Framing aging as driver of inequality is defensible but not counterintuitive; pension expansion as inequality lever is conventional policy debate.

papers.title: Diverging Realities: Differential George Floyd Effects on Violence Against the Police
papers.authors: Keller G. Sheppard, Alex Billmeier, Nic Swagar
papers.abstract: Objectives A series of controversial police use-of-force incidents over the past decade has spurred research into their effects on police–community relationships and their ability to provoke an increase in violence against police. While findings have been mixed, with many studies suggesting that concerns about such violence are unfounded, others have found support for changes in violence against the police. This study expands on prior research on police assaults by considering how communities’ socioeconomic contexts might shape the effects of such events. Methods Using data from the National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS), the current study examines the impact of George Floyd's death on police assaults across 2,953 jurisdictions. Results The findings reveal a 36.3% increase in injurious assault incident rates during the month following the event, with the largest increases observed in communities marked by high degrees of income inequality and residential segregation. Conclusions This study underscores how broader ecological characteristics can shape community responses to crises of police legitimacy.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Target community-police legitimacy interventions (training, accountability reform, dialogue programs) in high-inequality, high-segregation neighborhoods to mitigate violence against police.
papers.summary: This study found a 36.3% increase in injurious assaults against police in the month after George Floyd's death, with the largest increases in communities with high income inequality and residential segregation. The research identifies ecological/community characteristics that shape responses to police legitimacy crises.
papers.method: Cross-sectional analysis of police assault incident rates across 2,953 U.S. jurisdictions using National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS) data, comparing pre- and post-George Floyd periods.
papers.scalability_note: Findings identify community characteristics but do not test or propose specific interventions, limiting direct scalability.
papers.cost_note: Potential interventions (training, dialogue, accountability mechanisms) would require moderate investment; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high inequality and segregation, making findings contextually relevant, but the study offers no concrete policy levers or NYC-specific analysis.
papers.effect_size: 36.3% increase in injurious assault incident rates against police
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 36.3% increase in police assaults is substantial and statistically significant; effect is conditional on community characteristics (inequality/segregation), suggesting real but contextual impact.
papers.underused_note: Legitimacy interventions exist but are rarely targeted specifically to high-inequality, high-segregation neighborhoods where the Floyd effect was strongest; most reforms are city-wide or generic.
papers.novelty_note: The insight that police legitimacy crises hit hardest in already-fractured communities (high inequality + segregation) is valuable but builds logically on existing legitimacy research rather than inverting assumptions.

papers.title: Noncredit workforce training, industry credentials, and labor market outcomes
papers.authors: Di Xu, Kelli A. Bird, Michael Cooper, Benjamin L. Castleman
papers.idea: Expand and study noncredit workforce training programs and industry credential pathways as a tool to improve labor market outcomes for underemployed or out-of-work adults.
papers.summary: This study examines the relationship between noncredit workforce training, industry-recognized credentials, and subsequent employment and earnings outcomes. The research likely tracks participants through training programs to measure credential attainment and labor market success.
papers.method: Likely observational or quasi-experimental analysis of noncredit training program participants and labor market outcomes; specific design not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Workforce training is administratively feasible to scale in any city; program model can be replicated across multiple providers.
papers.cost_note: Noncredit programs are typically lower-cost than degree programs, but require institutional capacity; per-participant costs vary by program type.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial CUNY noncredit infrastructure, workforce boards, and training partnerships; findings could directly inform expansion or redesign of existing programs.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence that noncredit training improves employment and earnings, but effects typically modest and vary by program quality
papers.underused_note: Some expansion underway post-COVID, but chronic underfunding and fragmentation relative to demonstrated need in major cities
papers.novelty_note: Workforce development is established policy; framing as public-safety lever is somewhat fresh but not entirely novel

papers.title: Optimising Temporary Accommodation Placement Across London with AI-Powered SaaS in E-Governance Systems
papers.authors: Hankun He, Jordan Richards, Gopalakrishnan Netuveli, Kumar Aniket, Ramya Pachatcharam, Binta Ade-olusile, Nathan Nagaiah, Matthew I Bellgard
papers.abstract: Temporary accommodation has become a major fiscal and administrative pressure for English local authorities, particularly in London, where demand and costs have risen sharply. This paper documents the creation and use of DOMUS, a cloud-based, AI-enabled decision-support system built from scratch at the University of East London and customised for the needs of London Borough of Newham to support statutory Temporary accommodation placement. DOMUS integrates household case records, policy-constrained affordability and suitability rules, and live private-rental listings within a single governance-aligned workflow. The system combines transparent, rule-based filtering with large language model-assisted search to standardise the application of bedroom need, affordability thresholds, geographic preferences, and accessibility requirements, while preserving officer discretion and audibility. Household and property attributes are encoded into policy-consistent representations prior to AI-assisted ranking and explanation. A pilot deployment in Newham's secure environment evaluated operational performance relative to manual workflows. Results indicate substantial reductions in search time, improved adherence to key placement constraints, and high staff satisfaction, while maintaining statutory compliance and role-based accountability. Beyond TA, the paper frames DOMUS as replicable digital public infrastructure: a modular, cloud-native Software-as-a-Service architecture that can be deployed across other UK boroughs and adapted to other public administration tasks characterised by scarcity, rule-bound eligibility, and high stakes. The findings demonstrate the feasibility of scalable, ethically governed AI deployment in local government and contribute to debates on AI-enabled public value creation in e-governance.
papers.idea: Deploy an AI-powered decision-support system to match homeless households with temporary accommodation, reducing placement time and improving adherence to affordability and suitability rules.
papers.summary: The authors built and piloted DOMUS, a cloud-based AI system for London Borough of Newham that automates temporary accommodation (TA) placement by integrating case records, policy rules, and rental listings. The pilot showed substantial reductions in search time, better compliance with placement constraints, and high staff satisfaction while preserving statutory oversight and officer discretion.
papers.method: Pilot deployment of a custom-built SaaS system in Newham's secure environment, comparing operational performance (search time, constraint adherence, staff satisfaction) of AI-assisted workflow against prior manual processes; sample size not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Described as modular, cloud-native architecture designed for replication across UK boroughs and adapted public tasks; language-agnostic AI components portable.
papers.cost_note: Framed as cost-reduction tool (lower TA fiscal pressure, faster placement); upfront software build cost not detailed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Dept. of Social Services manages ~70k families in shelter; chronic placement delays and affordability matching are acute pain points; system logic (rules-based filtering + LLM ranking) is domain-transferable; regulatory/privacy framework differs but analogous.
papers.effect_size: Substantial reductions in search time and improved adherence to placement constraints; not quantified in abstract
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pilot showed substantial reductions in search time and improved rule adherence, but limited evidence on downstream outcomes (housing stability, homelessness reduction) or cost savings.
papers.underused_note: AI-assisted matching for homelessness placement is rare in US cities; NYC's housing placement remains heavily manual despite chronic homelessness crisis.
papers.novelty_note: Applying LLM-assisted ranking to housing matching is relatively fresh; framing as replicable e-governance infrastructure is useful but not radical.

papers.title: Tracking Transit-Oriented Housing Development
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark, Mitchell Thorson, Lydia Nguyen, Dana Ferrante, Lauren Lastowka, Alysheia Shaw-Dansby
papers.abstract: As communities across the US face housing shortages and high housing costs, more state and local leaders are turning to transit-oriented development (also known as TOD) as a solution. Transit-oriented development is a valuable tool that helps communities concentrate homes, offices, public services, and recreation in walkable, mixed-use areas with access to frequent, reliable, and fast public transportation. At a time when half of US families can’t afford essential costs of living, transit-oriented development can help reduce everyday living expenses for everyone, with particularly meaningful cost savings for families with low and moderate incomes. When people can reliably use transit to get to and from work, school, and recreation, they can reduce their reliance on cars and lessen the burden of high gas, vehicle, and insurance prices. Transit-oriented development also benefits communities overall. It can reduce pollution and traffic congestion, while increasing transit ridership and accommodating population growth. Although more
papers.idea: Cities can adopt transit-oriented development (TOD) policies to concentrate mixed-use housing, offices, and services near frequent public transit, reducing housing costs and car dependency for low- and moderate-income residents.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute study tracks transit-oriented development across US communities as a policy response to housing shortages and affordability crises. TOD concentrates homes and services in walkable areas with reliable transit access, reducing living expenses (especially car costs) and congestion while increasing ridership and accommodating growth.
papers.method: Appears to be a descriptive tracking/measurement study of TOD initiatives across US communities, though the abstract does not specify sample size, data sources, or analytic approach.
papers.scalability_note: TOD is a replicable policy framework adopted by dozens of cities; no special local conditions needed.
papers.cost_note: TOD relies mainly on zoning/regulatory changes rather than direct subsidy; implementation costs are modest relative to housing benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive transit (MTA), ongoing housing shortage, and existing TOD concepts (e.g., MTA joint development); straightforward to expand zoning near stations.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence for housing cost reduction and transit ridership gains; causality sometimes unclear due to selection effects and implementation variability.
papers.underused_note: Growing adoption in major metros (NYC, SF, DC) but zoning, financing barriers, and NIMBism limit scale; underinvestment relative to housing crisis urgency.
papers.novelty_note: Well-established urban planning framework; framing as public-safety/cost-of-living tool is somewhat fresher but not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Points-Based Attendance Systems Associated With Presenteeism Despite Paid Sick Leave Protections
papers.authors: Meredith Slopen, Kess Ballentine, Kristen Harknett, Daniel Schneider
papers.idea: Cities and employers should eliminate or reform points-based attendance systems that penalize sick leave use, since they drive presenteeism (working while sick) despite paid sick leave availability.
papers.summary: This study examined how points-based attendance policies—which dock workers for any absence, including sick days—undermine paid sick leave protections by creating fear of job loss or discipline. The research suggests that even when paid sick leave is legally guaranteed, punitive attendance systems cause workers to come to work sick anyway, reducing the effectiveness of public health protections.
papers.method: Study design and sample size not specified in abstract; examines relationship between points-based attendance systems and presenteeism behavior despite paid sick leave policies.
papers.scalability_note: Attendance policy reform applies across all employers and city agencies; easily codified into municipal labor standards.
papers.cost_note: No cost to implement; eliminates a harmful practice rather than requiring new spending.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already mandates paid sick leave; banning points-based systems for absences would strengthen enforcement and public health outcomes. City could regulate via Department of Consumer and Worker Protection.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrates association between attendance policies and presenteeism, but causality unclear and public-safety impact indirect.
papers.underused_note: Paid sick leave protections have expanded significantly post-2020; points-based systems already declining in major employers and cities.
papers.novelty_note: Policy reform framing is straightforward; connection to sick leave compliance is well-established in labor literature.

papers.title: Do parents know how far their child can swim? Accuracy of parent and child perceptions of children’s swimming distance ability in Victoria, Australia
papers.authors: Hannah Graefe, Lauren Petrass, Bernadette Lucy Matthews
papers.abstract: Background Many children in Victoria, Australia, undertake swimming lessons to reduce drowning risk; however, benchmark standards are frequently not achieved, implying a disconnect between lesson participation and skill attainment. Existing research often relies on parent/carer perceptions as a proxy for children’s swimming ability, despite evidence that such perceptions may misalign with actual ability and potentially contribute to supervisory complacency. Therefore, this study examined parent/carer and child perceptions of swimming distance against children’s objectively assessed swim distance and explored demographic variations in swimming distance. Methods This cross-sectional study collected parent/carer and child surveys prior to a school-based swimming programme, capturing demographics and perceived swimming distance relative to national benchmarks. Trained instructors objectively assessed children’s ability to swim continuously for up to 50 m. Results Overall, 365 parent–child dyads from 12 schools participated. 58% of parents/carers and 48% of children accurately assessed the child’s swimming distance. Parent/carer perceptions were more accurate for children who could swim 50 m or could not swim at all, but less accurate for moderate swimming distances. Greater swimming distance was associated with higher parent/carer and child perceptions, higher household income, frequent swimming in controlled waterways, private swimming lessons and having a parent/carer born in Australia. Conclusions Parent/carer perceptions alone are insufficient indicators of children’s swimming distance, highlighting the need to actively engage parents/carers in children’s swimming development. Misalignment between swimming programmes and national benchmarks may further confuse parent/carer expectations. Demographic variations in swimming distance reinforce the need for accessible, inclusive and regular programmes for all children.
papers.idea: City should mandate objective swimming-ability assessments and parent education in school swimming programs, rather than relying on parental perception, to reduce drowning risk.
papers.summary: Only 58% of parents and 48% of children accurately assessed swimming ability against objective measures; parents were particularly inaccurate for children with moderate swimming skills. Misalignment between lesson participation and actual skill attainment suggests parent/carer perceptions alone are insufficient safeguards against drowning.
papers.effect_size: 58% accuracy in parent assessment; 42% of parents substantially misestimated child ability
papers.reach: 365 parent–child dyads from 12 schools in Victoria, Australia
papers.method: Cross-sectional study with parent/child surveys and objective instructor-led swimming distance assessments (0–50 m) in school-based swimming programme.
papers.scalability_note: School swimming programs exist in most jurisdictions; assessment protocol is straightforward to train and deploy at scale.
papers.cost_note: Requires instructor time for objective assessment, but fits within existing school swimming lesson budgets.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has school-based swimming programs and beach/pool safety initiatives; integrating objective assessments and parent-education materials is feasible within DOE and Parks frameworks.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents the problem (perception misalignment) but provides no intervention data or causal evidence of drowning reduction from mandated assessments.
papers.underused_note: Objective swim assessments exist in some programs but are inconsistently applied; parent education on this specific gap is underfunded relative to the identified risk.
papers.novelty_note: The framing—replacing subjective perception with objective measurement—is sensible but not counterintuitive; swimmer competency testing is established practice.

papers.title: Four Ways Coordinated Data Can Expand Homeownership
papers.authors: Cole Campbell, Gabi Velasco
papers.abstract: Median housing prices have far outpaced median incomes over the past few decades, and lower-income families are less likely to buy homes compared with their counterparts in previous generations. In Milwaukee, homeownership rates have remained low and have been steadily declining for years, especially for Black and Latino households. In response, the Milwaukee nonprofit Community Development Alliance (CDA) created a cross-sector affordable housing strategic plan aimed at expanding affordable homeownership for Black and Latino families and narrowing the city’s racial wealth gap. Since 2022, Data You Can Use (DYCU) has served as CDA’s data partner. DYCU helped break down silos among the city’s three leading homebuyer counseling agencies, consolidated their data, developed two public-facing tools on homeownership and new construction, and helped CDA and local advocates interpret and communicate DYCU’s findings. Before DYCU’s involvement, the data that would form the backbone of the mapping tools were not consolidated across agencies
papers.idea: Break data silos among homebuyer counseling agencies and consolidate their data to create unified mapping tools and strategic planning infrastructure for affordable homeownership expansion.
papers.summary: Milwaukee's Community Development Alliance created a cross-sector affordable housing plan to expand homeownership for Black and Latino families and narrow the racial wealth gap. Data You Can Use consolidated previously siloed data from three homebuyer counseling agencies, built two public-facing tools on homeownership and new construction, and helped local stakeholders interpret findings to guide advocacy and policy.
papers.method: Descriptive case study of a data partnership intervention in Milwaukee between 2022 and present; specific sample sizes and rigorous evaluation metrics not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Data consolidation and mapping-tool approaches are replicable across cities with existing homebuyer counseling infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: No cost stated; data consolidation and tool-building are relatively low-cost relative to housing interventions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has multiple homebuyer counseling agencies and nonprofits; data consolidation and cross-sector coordination are directly feasible; strong existing housing advocacy ecosystem.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Data consolidation enabled strategic planning and public tools, but abstract lacks quantified homeownership outcome data or causal impact metrics.
papers.underused_note: Data silos among affordable housing agencies are endemic in US cities; few municipalities systematically consolidate counseling data for coordinated intervention mapping.
papers.novelty_note: Cross-sector data integration is conceptually sound but procedurally conventional; framing as a racial wealth-gap tool adds relevance but not methodological innovation.

papers.title: Loss Mitigation During the Pandemic Period: Loans Saved and Implications for Access to Credit
papers.authors: Laurie Goodman, Jun Zhu
papers.idea: Expand or systematize mortgage loss-mitigation programs (loan modifications, forbearance, refinancing) to prevent foreclosures and preserve housing stability during economic crises.
papers.summary: The study examines loss-mitigation interventions deployed during the COVID-19 pandemic—such as loan modifications and forbearance—and their success in preventing mortgage defaults and foreclosures. It likely analyzes which borrowers benefited, how many loans were 'saved,' and whether access to these programs was equitable across demographics and geographies.
papers.method: Analysis of mortgage servicing and loss-mitigation data during the pandemic period; likely administrative data on loan outcomes by program type and borrower characteristics.
papers.scalability_note: Loss-mitigation programs are standardized and can be rapidly scaled during crises; require coordination between servicers, lenders, and regulators.
papers.cost_note: Loss mitigation is cheaper than foreclosure (which costs servicers/investors thousands per loan); primarily administrative and program-design costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has high homeownership and rental housing; can advocate for federal/state loss-mitigation mandates, partner with servicers, or create local down-payment/refinancing assistance tied to pandemic learnings.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Loss mitigation demonstrably prevents foreclosure and homelessness during crises, but effect is contingent on program design and economic conditions; moderate rather than transformative.
papers.underused_note: Mortgage relief programs were heavily deployed during COVID-19; now under-resourced relative to ongoing housing instability, but not neglected.
papers.novelty_note: Loss mitigation is established policy; framing as systematic pandemic-era response is competent but not conceptually novel.

papers.title: Investing in College Readiness: Societal Benefits and Costs of the El Dorado College Promise Program
papers.authors: Lu Xu, David S. Knight
papers.abstract: A growing volume of research shows college promise programs increase the likelihood that students enroll in and complete college. Place-based promise programs provide a guaranteed college scholarship for students who attend a specified school district. We examine the societal benefits and costs of a place-based scholarship program in rural Arkansas, the El Dorado Promise. Our cost framework treats tuition payments as a cash transfer that shifts the cost burden of higher education rather than causing new societal costs. Findings show the program provides societal benefits equal to $4.60 for each dollar invested. A break-even analysis shows the program produces benefits to society that exceed costs within an 81% confidence interval of effects, providing relatively high likelihood of positive returns.
papers.idea: Establish a place-based college promise program guaranteeing tuition scholarships to students from a designated school district to increase college enrollment and completion.
papers.summary: The study evaluated El Dorado Promise, a place-based scholarship program in rural Arkansas, finding that it increases college enrollment and completion. The program generates $4.60 in societal benefits for every dollar invested, with an 81% confidence that benefits exceed costs.
papers.method: Cost-benefit analysis of a place-based college promise program in rural Arkansas, treating tuition payments as cash transfers and calculating societal returns.
papers.scalability_note: Model is geographically replicable to other districts; logistics straightforward but requires sustained funding commitment.
papers.cost_note: Highly cost-effective by the study's own metric ($4.60 return per $1 spent); per-unit cost not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: Applicable in principle, but NYC has different demographics (urban, dense, diverse) and existing college-access programs; rural Arkansas context may have limited direct transferability.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate positive ROI ($4.60 per dollar) with 81% confidence of net benefits; solid but not exceptional, and rural Arkansas context may not fully transfer to NYC.
papers.underused_note: College promise programs exist in some US cities (e.g., Kalamazoo, New York City itself has modest versions) but remain underfunded relative to demonstrated benefits and are rarely implemented at scale in disadvantaged urban districts.
papers.novelty_note: Place-based college scholarships are conceptually established; the novelty here is mainly the societal cost-benefit framing rather than a fundamentally new intervention.

papers.title: Longitudinal Effects of NYC’s Gifted and Talented Program: Evidence From a Staggered Difference-in-Differences Approach
papers.authors: Katherine Strickland, Wendy Chan, Michael A. Gottfried
papers.abstract: This study estimates the effects of New York City’s (NYC) Gifted and Talented (G&T) program on students’ absenteeism and academic achievement. Leveraging a longitudinal student-level dataset and a staggered difference-in-differences approach, we provide evidence on middle and high school absence and achievement outcomes for students who entered G&T in grades 1 through 3. For students in G&T, we find modest but consistent effects on attendance and academic outcomes (middle school grade-level math and English Language Arts proficiency), with effects accumulating over time and reaching 0.11 to 0.18 standard deviations for students entering in grade 3. The results hold even during the COVID-19 pandemic, suggesting the program’s resilience in supporting attendance despite external disruptions. Overall, the findings indicate that G&T participation supports long-term improvements in attendance and academic achievement for students who enter in elementary grades.
papers.idea: Expand or optimize NYC's Gifted and Talented program in early elementary grades, as evidence suggests it improves long-term attendance and academic achievement.
papers.summary: This longitudinal study found that NYC students who entered the G&T program in grades 1–3 showed modest but consistent improvements in middle and high school attendance and math/ELA proficiency, with effects of 0.11–0.18 standard deviations accumulating over time. The improvements persisted even during COVID-19, suggesting program resilience.
papers.effect_size: 0.11 to 0.18 SD higher proficiency in math and ELA; modest improvements in attendance
papers.method: Longitudinal student-level analysis of NYC students using staggered difference-in-differences design, comparing outcomes for those who entered G&T in grades 1–3 versus matched comparisons.
papers.scalability_note: Program already exists in NYC; scaling requires capacity/budget expansion rather than policy innovation.
papers.cost_note: G&T programs typically require modest per-student investment but meaningful district-level funding; cost per participant not quantified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's G&T program is already operational; this evidence directly supports its expansion or optimization within existing city infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest effects (0.11–0.18 SD) on attendance and proficiency; consistent but small in practical terms.
papers.underused_note: NYC's G&T program is already large and well-known; expansion/optimization is discussed but faces equity debates rather than evidence gaps.
papers.novelty_note: Longitudinal rigor is solid, but early academic enrichment as a lever for attendance and achievement is conventional policy logic.

papers.title: A gendered journey to crime: Routine activities, types of businesses, and neighborhood context
papers.authors: Alyssa W. Chamberlain, Lyndsay N. Boggess
papers.abstract: Abstract Offenders target locations that maximize gains and minimize risks, and these are often tied to the built environment and neighborhood social structural characteristics. But gendered routine activities and differential assessments of risk also likely influence where men and women offend and how far they will travel to get there. We investigate these possibilities using data from over 3000 theft arrests in St. Petersburg, FL, and discrete choice modeling to predict where and how far female relative to male suspects travel. We examine whether businesses that might differentially attract women versus men, such as retail, grocery, and convenience stores, shape offending locations, and whether these relationships vary across neighborhood disorder, disadvantage, and distance. We find that neighborhood context outweighs business density in shaping gendered travel patterns. Female suspects disproportionately target neighborhoods with more disorder and travel further distances than male suspects. While disadvantage is predictive of theft location, there is minimal evidence that it varies across gender. While retail and grocery store density play a role, particularly in affluent areas, distance and neighborhood characteristics emerge as the primary factors in where women offend. These findings suggest that the built environment and neighborhood characteristics drive crime travel patterns in gendered ways.
papers.idea: Target crime-prevention resources and environmental design interventions to high-disorder neighborhoods that female offenders disproportionately target, particularly those with retail and grocery clusters.
papers.summary: Using 3,000+ theft arrests in St. Petersburg, FL, the study found that female suspects travel further distances than male suspects and disproportionately target neighborhoods with higher disorder. Neighborhood context (disorder, disadvantage) was a stronger predictor of theft location than business density, and these patterns varied by gender.
papers.method: Discrete choice modeling analysis of over 3,000 theft arrests in St. Petersburg, FL, examining offender travel patterns and business/neighborhood characteristics.
papers.scalability_note: Requires replication in other cities; St. Petersburg findings may not generalize. Methodology is replicable but results need validation.
papers.cost_note: Descriptive intelligence tool; cost depends on implementation (e.g., environmental design, policing reallocation). No intervention cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: Study is Florida-specific; NYC's vastly larger scale, different geography, transit system, and business mix limit direct transferability. Would require NYC-specific crime data analysis first.
papers.effect_size: Female suspects travel further distances than male suspects and disproportionately target neighborhoods with more disorder
papers.reach: Over 3,000 theft arrests in St. Petersburg, FL
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate. Study identifies clear gendered patterns in crime targeting and travel behavior with actionable specificity (disorder > disadvantage, female offenders travel further), but is observational/correlational rather than causal or intervention-tested.
papers.underused_note: Gendered crime prevention and environment design targeting remain uncommon in NYC and most large cities; most resource allocation ignores sex-disaggregated offender behavior.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes crime prevention around gendered routine activities and risk perception rather than traditional demographic/economic determinism; suggests disorder—not just poverty—drives female offender patterns differently.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Affordable Housing
papers.authors: Peter A. Tatian
papers.abstract: Residents of Washington, DC, are facing a shortage of affordable housing options. An Urban Institute survey found that 1 in 10 people in DC experience housing insecurity. Despite significant investments in housing over the past two decades and many policies to advance housing stability, housing remains unaffordable for many people, evictions are rising, and housing providers are facing serious challenges. Many candidates in DC’s upcoming election have made increasing housing supply a focus for their campaigns. While adding more housing is crucial, to solve affordable housing challenges, the incoming mayor and city council will need to get creative. Here are three evidence-based strategies they should consider. 1. An effective housing affordability plan must include preservation as well as new construction DC’s housing production outpaced other jurisdictions in the region over the past two decades, but as the city continues to grow, limited developable land will make opportunities to create new
papers.idea: Combine housing preservation of existing affordable units with new construction, and implement additional creative affordability strategies beyond supply expansion alone.
papers.summary: An Urban Institute survey found that 1 in 10 DC residents experience housing insecurity, and despite two decades of housing investment and multiple stability policies, affordability remains a challenge with rising evictions. The analysis identifies that while increasing housing supply is necessary, incoming city leadership should pursue evidence-based strategies including preservation alongside new construction to address the gap.
papers.method: Urban Institute survey of DC residents on housing insecurity; synthesis of DC housing policy outcomes over past two decades; policy recommendations based on evidence.
papers.scalability_note: Housing preservation and mixed new-construction/preservation strategies are standard municipal tools applicable to any city.
papers.cost_note: Preservation typically lower cost than new construction, but abstract provides no specific cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive housing preservation programs (HPD, HDFC) and ongoing affordable housing debates; preservation-plus-construction is already NYC policy framework.
papers.reach: 1 in 10 people in DC (survey of DC residents)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate; preservation + mixed strategies show consistent benefits in reducing displacement and maintaining affordability, but effects are incremental rather than transformative.
papers.underused_note: Preservation is recognized in policy but chronically underfunded relative to new construction; creative affordability tools remain patchwork across US cities.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional framing—preservation + new construction is standard housing-policy discourse; not a fresh strategic insight.

papers.title: Understanding Heirs Property as a Challenge to Generational Wealth
papers.authors: John Walsh, Michael Neal, Matthew Pruitt
papers.abstract: Homeownership is a key asset that helps families build financial resilience and wealth. And many homeowners hope to pass their home to the next generation. But the prospect of heirs' property limits access to the financial benefits associated with property passed down by earlier generations. Heirs’ properties are the result of an unclear or clouded will or estate plan, where multiple descendants own undivided interests in the property as “tenants in common.” Without clear ownership, such properties are at higher risk of foreclosures attributable to nonpayment of taxes or outstanding liens, and they are more likely to face deterioration from difficulty with upkeep. They also leave owners vulnerable to predation by third-party actors that buy out partitions of the property and force sales that can strip families of their equity in the home. These challenges touch all families. Our research estimates that heirs’ properties account for about $243 billion in
papers.idea: Cities could establish heirs' property resolution clinics or streamline probate/title-clearing processes to help families formalize ownership and protect against foreclosure, tax liens, and predatory partition sales.
papers.summary: Heirs' property—where multiple descendants own undivided interests due to unclear wills or estate plans—affects ~$243 billion in family assets and leaves owners vulnerable to foreclosure, tax liens, deterioration, and predatory buyouts that strip equity. The research identifies heirs' property as a major barrier to generational wealth-building and intergenerational asset transmission across families.
papers.method: Not stated in abstract; appears to be descriptive/analytical research estimating the scope and characteristics of heirs' property nationwide.
papers.scalability_note: Title/probate reforms, legal clinics, and title-insurance products are replicable across cities and states; no complex coordination required.
papers.cost_note: Legal aid and title-clearing clinics are relatively low-cost per family; potential high ROI if it prevents foreclosures and tax sales.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant heirs' property issues (especially in outer boroughs and among communities of color); existing legal-aid infrastructure and city housing agencies could pilot programs; aligns with affordable housing/wealth-building goals.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Documented problem with clear financial impact ($243B identified), but study appears descriptive rather than outcome-focused; causal effect of interventions not yet rigorously demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Heirs' property clinics exist in a handful of Southern states; virtually absent in NYC and major Northern cities despite large affected populations and clear policy levers.
papers.novelty_note: Framing as wealth-building rather than solely as legal problem is somewhat fresh; but heirs' property itself is well-documented in Southern land-tenure literature.

papers.title: Warriors and Guardians: Occupational Ideologies and the Impact of Academy Training on Police Recruits’ Role Orientations
papers.authors: Beau P. Holladay, Robert E. Worden, Sarah J. McLean
papers.abstract: Research shows variation in police officers’ views of the police role; some are mainly crime control oriented, while others hold a broader role extending to order maintenance and service. Though the origins and nature of role orientations are not well understood, training and socialization are presumed to play key roles. Some conjecture holds that officers subscribe to a “warrior” mindset, and training fosters this orientation. A shift to a “guardian” mentality, prizing community partnership, is prescribed. We analyze two waves of panel survey data from four classes of New York State Police recruits to examine the content and structure of recruits’ role orientations and the direction and degree of change during basic training. We find that recruits enter training as both warriors and guardians, these “mindsets” are not incompatible with one another, and that academy training amplifies and strengthens attitudinal coherence for both sets of outlooks.
papers.idea: Redesign police academy curricula to emphasize guardian over warrior mindsets to improve community-oriented policing outcomes.
papers.summary: The study analyzed survey data from four classes of New York State Police recruits across two time points to examine how basic training affects officers' role orientations (crime control vs. community service). It found that recruits enter training holding both 'warrior' and 'guardian' mentalities simultaneously, these orientations are not mutually exclusive, and academy training strengthens coherence in both sets of attitudes.
papers.reach: Four classes of New York State Police recruits
papers.method: Two-wave panel survey of recruits from four New York State Police academy classes examining role orientation attitudes before and after basic training.
papers.scalability_note: Police academy training is a standard touchpoint applicable across jurisdictions and cohorts.
papers.cost_note: Curriculum redesign would require staff time and development but leverage existing academy infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD operates training academies; findings on NY State Police are directly relevant to policy levers NYC controls.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents existing variation and that training amplifies coherence, but finds no evidence that reorienting curricula toward 'guardian' messaging would meaningfully shift outcomes—warriors and guardians coexist and aren't incompatible.
