papers.underused_note: Guardian-mindset framing is increasingly discussed in police reform circles and some academies have adopted it, but implementation is inconsistent and rigorous evaluation of curriculum redesign effects remains limited.
papers.novelty_note: Warrior-vs-guardian framing is now fairly standard in police reform discourse; the insight that they're compatible rather than opposing is somewhat fresh but challenges rather than supports the popular prescription.

papers.title: The US Needs More Starter Homes. Factory-Built Construction Can Fill the Gap.
papers.authors: Laurie Goodman, Aniket Mehrotra
papers.abstract: A starter home is more than a shelter. For millions of families, it represents an opportunity for homeownership and a legacy of wealth building for future generations. But the availability of homes affordable to the typical first-time homebuyer—units that we think of as “starter homes”—has dwindled considerably in the past decade. Although 39 percent of active listings were priced under $200,000 in 2016, only 13 percent were priced as such in 2025. In high-cost markets, the starter home supply is even smaller. Nationwide, building smaller, less expensive homes has become unprofitable because of the rising costs of construction and financing and because of zoning policies such as minimum lot size requirements. Recent federal policy changes, such as tariffs on homebuilding materials and immigrant deportation policies driving construction labor shortages, have exacerbated the problem. In the face of these issues, increasing the supply of starter homes will require new ideas. One
papers.idea: Cities can adopt zoning reforms (e.g., eliminating minimum lot sizes) and streamline permitting for factory-built housing to reduce construction costs and expand affordable starter-home supply.
papers.summary: The abstract documents a sharp decline in affordable starter homes (from 39% of listings under $200k in 2016 to 13% in 2025) driven by rising construction and financing costs, restrictive zoning, and recent federal headwinds (tariffs, labor shortages). The authors suggest factory-built construction as a potential solution, though the abstract is incomplete.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of real-estate listing data (2016–2025) and market trends; appears to be a policy brief rather than an original empirical study.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform and factory-built housing standards can be adopted by any city and scaled widely.
papers.cost_note: Factory-built construction typically reduces per-unit cost 15–30%; lower ongoing city costs if zoning/permitting streamlined.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has severe zoning constraints and high land costs that factory-building alone cannot fully solve; but zoning reform on lot sizes and density is politically difficult and limited by geography.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Housing supply interventions show moderate, context-dependent effects; zoning reform + factory construction can reduce costs 15-25%, but citywide affordability impact depends on scale and complementary policies.
papers.underused_note: Factory-built housing and aggressive zoning deregulation remain niche despite growing evidence; regulatory barriers and developer inertia persist even in high-cost markets; NYC has not meaningfully scaled either lever.
papers.novelty_note: Zoning reform and prefab construction are well-established in policy discourse (Minneapolis, Oregon); framing as 'starter homes' is slightly repackaged but not conceptually novel.

papers.title: Are Medicaid Enrollees Accessing Tobacco and Nicotine Dependence Treatment in Your State?
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Maya Payton
papers.abstract: Tobacco use is the most common cause of preventable disease, disability, and death in the United States. Every year, it’s responsible for and billions of dollars in health care spending and productivity losses resulting from smoking-related sickness and death. Medicaid enrollees are disproportionately affected because they’re nearly twice as likely to smoke tobacco. Cessation Medications Can Save Lives, but Many Medicaid Enrollees Don’t Access Them As of 2024, most state Medicaid programs covered at least one FDA-approved cessation medication and counseling service, but few covered all seven medications and counseling for all adults across both fee-for-service and managed care plans. Requirements like prior authorization, copayments, and treatment caps further limit enrollees’ access to these medications. Systems-level challenges, including a lack of integration of screening and treatment into routine care, low provider engagement, underinvestment in public health outreach, and low awareness of treatment coverage among patients and providers, compound these issues
papers.idea: NYC could audit and expand Medicaid coverage of all seven FDA-approved cessation medications and counseling across all plans, remove prior authorization and copayment barriers, and integrate tobacco screening and treatment into routine primary care and emergency department workflows.
papers.summary: Most state Medicaid programs cover at least one tobacco cessation medication and counseling, but few cover all seven medications or ensure coverage across both fee-for-service and managed care plans. Barriers like prior authorization, copayments, and treatment caps, combined with low provider engagement and patient awareness, prevent many Medicaid enrollees from accessing treatment despite nearly twice the smoking rate of the general population.
papers.method: Descriptive review of state Medicaid program policies and coverage rules as of 2024; comparative analysis of cessation medication and counseling coverage across states.
papers.scalability_note: Policy and coverage changes are replicable across cities and states; requires coordination with health plans but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Cessation medications are cost-effective (return on investment from prevented smoking-related disease); removal of barriers is low-cost administratively.
papers.nyc_note: NYC administers Medicaid through NYC Health + Hospitals and coordinates with state; can audit local plan coverage and remove barriers for city residents; could pilot integrated screening in primary care and safety-net settings.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Tobacco cessation medications have well-established efficacy; removing access barriers should increase treatment uptake and quit rates moderately, though magnitude depends on implementation fidelity.
papers.underused_note: Strong clinical evidence for cessation treatment exists, but most state Medicaid programs lack comprehensive coverage of all seven FDA medications, retain prior auth/copays, and rarely integrate screening into routine workflows.
papers.novelty_note: Expanding medication access and removing administrative barriers is evidence-based best practice; framing is competent but conventional within public health.

papers.title: Do Information-Based Interventions Influence Tax Compliance Differently across Men and Women ? Experimental Evidence from Ethiopia
papers.abstract: This study investigates taxpayer responses to tax compliance interventions undertaken in collaboration with Ethiopia’s revenue authority, focusing on gender differences among business owners. The research targeted a sample of 5,408 business owners, and the interventions—letters highlighting tax obligations and civic responsibilities—were successfully implemented with 3,551 participants. The interventions involved letters emphasizing tax obligations and civic duties and were evaluated through a randomized controlled trial across diverse economic sectors and sub-city locations. Data from quantitative surveys and administrative records provided information about reported tax declarations and compliance behaviors. The findings indicate a balance in key variables before the intervention, with notable impacts of the intervention on reported profit tax declarations. While examining gender differences in tax compliance, the study found no differential effects of the intervention based on business owners’ gender. Specifically, the result shows that the difference in tax compliance between male and female business owners is not statistically significant at both intensive and extensive margins. This indicates that gender did not play a meaningful role in influencing tax compliance as a result of the intervention.
papers.idea: Send business owners letters emphasizing tax obligations and civic duties to increase tax compliance reporting.
papers.summary: A randomized controlled trial of 3,551 Ethiopian business owners tested whether letters emphasizing tax obligations and civic responsibilities would increase tax compliance. The intervention showed impacts on reported profit tax declarations, but found no statistically significant gender differences in compliance responses.
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial in Ethiopia with business owners across diverse economic sectors and sub-city locations, using administrative records and quantitative surveys to measure tax declarations and compliance behavior.
papers.scalability_note: Low-cost letter-based interventions are easily scaled, but tax administration systems vary significantly by jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: Letters are among the cheapest possible interventions; per-unit cost not stated but minimal.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's tax base and enforcement infrastructure differ vastly from Ethiopia; prior evidence shows information interventions work differently in high-capacity/high-literacy settings. Focus on gender neutrality adds little marginal value for NYC policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study shows intervention impact on tax declarations but lacks detail on effect size; no gender differential found, limiting strategic insight.
papers.underused_note: Tax compliance messaging is standard practice in revenue agencies globally; concept already mainstream in public-finance administration.
papers.novelty_note: Information-based tax compliance letters are conventional; gender-comparison framing adds little new insight given null finding.

papers.title: LIHTC at 40: How Much Affordable Housing Has Been Built in Your State?
papers.authors: Christina Plerhoples Stacy, Daniel Teles, Tomi Rajninger, Will Curran-Groome
papers.abstract: Since its creation 40 years ago, the low-income housing tax credit (LIHTC) has financed between 3.3 and 3.7 million affordable rental units, making it the largest source of federal assistance for affordable rental housing development in the United States. This level of production is more than double the roughly 1.4 million units created by the public housing program over nearly 80 years. But LIHTC production has not been evenly distributed across the US. Using data from the National Housing Preservation Database from 1986 to 2022, we calculated the number of newly financed LIHTC units per 10,000 people in each state and metropolitan area. We found wide variation in population-adjusted LIHTC production across states and metropolitan areas. The amount of LIHTC housing and where it’s built depends on not only population but also policy choices, financing structures, and local market conditions. Whether this variation corresponds to differences in housing need remains
papers.idea: Analyze NYC's LIHTC production relative to need and peer metros to identify policy/financing barriers, then adopt high-performing states' strategies to increase affordable unit production.
papers.summary: The Low-Income Housing Tax Credit has financed 3.3–3.7 million affordable rental units over 40 years—more than double public housing's output—but production varies dramatically across states and metros. The study quantified this geographic variation using 1986–2022 data, suggesting the disparity reflects policy choices, financing structures, and local market conditions rather than housing need alone.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of National Housing Preservation Database (1986–2022), calculating LIHTC units per 10,000 population by state and metro area.
papers.scalability_note: LIHTC is a federal program; states and cities can adjust local policy/financing to increase uptake. Peer comparison is easily replicated.
papers.cost_note: LIHTC uses tax credits (foregone federal revenue), not direct spending; cost per unit depends on deal structure but is already embedded in federal program.
papers.nyc_note: NYC controls local zoning, financing incentives, and developer partnerships. Study can identify whether NYC underperforms peers and which policy levers (streamlined approvals, co-financing, density bonuses) work elsewhere.
papers.effect_size: 3.3 to 3.7 million affordable rental units financed over 40 years
papers.reach: All US states and metropolitan areas, 1986 to 2022
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents variation in LIHTC production but doesn't measure causal impact on outcomes (affordability, homelessness, displacement). Demonstrates a real policy lever with proven track record (3.3–3.7M units nationally) but effect size of adopting high-performer strategies is inferred, not measured.
papers.underused_note: LIHTC is well-known nationally but NYC and many metros appear to underutilize it relative to peer cities and housing need. Policy/financing barriers remain poorly mapped and unaddressed; opportunity for significant production increase through targeted reforms is largely unrealized.
papers.novelty_note: Comparative state/metro analysis is methodologically sound but the framing—'learn from high performers'—is conventional policy-diffusion thinking. Not a new mechanism or counterintuitive insight.

papers.title: Between the forest and the trees: Navigating silos and setbacks to vegetative roadway barriers
papers.authors: Douglas Houston, Jeannine M. Pearce, Gregg P. Macey, Catherine Garoupa
papers.idea: Install vegetative roadway barriers (trees, shrubs, landscaped medians) to reduce vehicle speeds and improve pedestrian/cyclist safety while providing environmental co-benefits.
papers.summary: This study examines the institutional, bureaucratic, and implementation barriers to installing vegetative roadway barriers in cities, identifying silos across transportation, parks, and planning departments as obstacles. The research navigates the practical and policy challenges cities face when attempting to deploy these traffic-calming green infrastructure interventions.
papers.method: Qualitative or mixed-methods analysis of institutional barriers to vegetative roadway barrier adoption (method details not provided in abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Vegetative barriers are modular and deployable across street networks; main constraint is institutional coordination, not technical feasibility.
papers.cost_note: Installation and maintenance moderate; costs vary by species and urban context; likely offset by reduced crash costs and health/environmental gains.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has active Vision Zero program, multiple agencies (DOT, Parks, DCP), and existing green infrastructure initiatives; cross-agency coordination is feasible but non-trivial.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate demonstrated effects on speed reduction and safety; environmental co-benefits documented but safety impact varies by design.
papers.underused_note: Strong evidence base exists but NYC and most US cities deploy vegetative barriers sporadically; maintenance and silo issues limit scaling despite proven benefits.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional traffic-calming approach; framing around 'navigating silos' highlights implementation barriers rather than introducing new mechanism.

papers.title: How Upzoning Affects Housing Supply
papers.authors: Yipeng Su, Yonah Freemark, Will Curran-Groome
papers.abstract: The United States is short millions of homes because, for decades, housing construction has not kept up with population growth. In response, many jurisdictions are hoping to expand housing supply through upzoning: reforming land-use rules to enable developers to build more housing units per parcel. The theory behind upzoning is straightforward. If local regulations cap the number of homes a developer can build on a piece of land, raising those caps should result in more homes being built. More homes, in turn, should eventually translate to lower prices, as more supply can better accommodate demand. And this could provide people a broader range of neighborhood options and reduce displacement pressures. In practice, however, the chain from relaxed regulations to new homes is sometimes less automatic than the theory implies. Some detractors argue that upzoning does not produce new supply quickly enough meaningfully to improve housing affordability. Even so, the growing
papers.idea: Reform local land-use regulations to permit higher-density housing construction (upzoning) as a lever to expand housing supply and reduce affordability pressure.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute study examines whether upzoning—relaxing regulatory caps on housing units per parcel—actually increases housing construction and affordability. The abstract indicates the theory is intuitive (more supply should lower prices and reduce displacement), but notes that in practice the regulatory-to-construction pipeline is sometimes slower or less effective than expected, with some critics arguing upzoning fails to improve affordability quickly enough.
papers.method: Not fully specified in abstract; appears to be a policy analysis/empirical review of upzoning outcomes across jurisdictions, likely using housing construction data and regulatory change records.
papers.scalability_note: Zoning reform is a city/municipal lever applicable nationwide; NYC has already undertaken major upzoning initiatives.
papers.cost_note: Upzoning is regulatory, not budgetary; costs are primarily political and administrative; benefits accrue if private development follows.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive experience with upzoning (e.g., East Midtown, Hudson Yards, recent East Side rezoning); directly relevant to ongoing housing policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Mixed evidence; upzoning increases permitting but supply response is slow and affordability gains uncertain or modest.
papers.underused_note: Upzoning is increasingly mainstream in major US cities (NYC, Minneapolis, California); already a focal policy reform.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional zoning-reform framing; long-standing policy debate, not a fresh public-safety insight.

papers.title: Youth Corps Programs Build Pathways to Infrastructure Careers
papers.authors: Shayne Spaulding, Nathan Sick, Jincy Wilson, Lindsey Tyson
papers.abstract: Infrastructure workers help people connect to utilities and the power grid, move about society safely, build and update structures, mitigate climate change, and more. Yet infrastructure employers, including municipal governments and private organizations, face current and anticipated labor shortages. Youth service corps programs could help fill this gap. They offer young people, especially opportunity youth—who are typically ages 16 to 24 and are not working or enrolled in education, or are at risk of disconnection from school and work—the chance to give back to their communities while building skills for work and earning a wage or stipend. When corps programs connect young adults with good infrastructure jobs, they build a pipeline of workers for employers and a pathway to economic mobility for workers. To better understand these programs, we interviewed staff at eight youth service corps programs around the country that help participants—referred to as members— develop infrastructure-related skills. In
papers.idea: Cities can establish or expand youth service corps programs that train opportunity youth (ages 16–24) in infrastructure skills while paying wages/stipends, creating both workforce pipelines for municipal employers and economic mobility pathways for disconnected young adults.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute interviewed staff at eight youth service corps programs nationwide that connect opportunity youth with infrastructure-career training. The research identifies youth corps as a mechanism to address current and anticipated labor shortages in infrastructure while offering at-risk young people work experience, skill-building, and wages in sectors critical to utilities, transit, construction, and climate resilience.
papers.method: Qualitative study involving interviews with staff at eight youth service corps programs across the country focused on infrastructure-skill development.
papers.scalability_note: Corps models are replicable across cities; NYC already runs AmeriCorps and similar programs, so infrastructure-track expansion is feasible.
papers.cost_note: Requires wage/stipend funding and program administration; typical corps programs cost $15k–$25k per participant annually, but deliver employment and tax benefits.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing youth corps infrastructure (e.g., NYC Service Corps, DYCD programs), strong municipal employment demand in water, power, transit, and construction; directly expandable.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study of 8 programs; demonstrates plausible pathways to employment and earnings but lacks rigorous impact evaluation or control-group comparison of outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Youth corps programs exist but are chronically underfunded, limited in scale, and not systematically integrated into NYC's workforce strategy despite persistent infrastructure labor shortages and youth disconnection.
papers.novelty_note: Repackaging of established AmeriCorps/service-corps model; combining paid training with infrastructure focus is sensible but not conceptually novel.

papers.title: Money for MetroCards: How a new card fee made transit riders invest more and lose more
papers.authors: Meiping Aggie Sun
papers.idea: Evaluate or redesign MetroCard fee structures to understand how upfront charges affect rider behavior and transit access equity.
papers.summary: The study examines how a card fee (likely an upfront purchase requirement or recurring charge) influenced MetroCard users' spending and loss patterns. The title suggests riders invested more money into cards but also lost more, indicating the fee may have altered purchasing behavior in ways worth examining for equity and efficiency.
papers.method: Empirical analysis of MetroCard transaction or administrative data (likely observational or natural experiment comparing behavior before/after fee introduction), setting unclear from title alone.
papers.scalability_note: Transit fare structures are standard across cities; findings would apply to any system with prepaid card fees.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost stated; policy change involves minimal administrative cost.
papers.nyc_note: Directly relevant to NYC's MetroCard system operated by MTA; easily testable and implementable.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Behavioral nudge on transit usage patterns; likely modest and indirect public-safety relevance without stronger causal evidence linking card fees to safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Transit fare policy is actively managed by MTA; fee structures are debated but not neglected; modest room for better behavioral research application.
papers.novelty_note: Frames transit access through behavioral economics and equity lens rather than traditional capacity/revenue lens; somewhat fresh for transit policy but not groundbreaking.

papers.title: Three Things DC’s New Mayor and Council Need to Know About the DC Child Care Subsidy Program
papers.authors: Justin B. Doromal, Diane Schilder
papers.abstract: Thanks to DC’s robust early childhood care and education policies, many DC families don’t pay the full cost of child care. The DC Child Care Subsidy Program helps offset child care costs for eligible families—especially those with younger children, those seeking before- or after-school care, and those needing summer care. For families paying the full cost of child care, costs are among the highest in the nation. Child care subsidies are designed to expand families’ options, giving them access to care that is otherwise unaffordable so they can work, attend school, or pursue job training opportunities. The DC Child Care Subsidy Program complements DC’s universal public prekindergarten program, which enrolls 3- and 4-year-old children during school-day hours for the academic year, irrespective of the family’s income. This year, family affordability challenges, an uncertain labor market, and a restrictive city budget have mayoral candidates and DC residents alike focused on policies
papers.idea: Expand and optimize child care subsidy programs to reduce family affordability barriers and enable parental workforce participation and skill development.
papers.summary: The DC Child Care Subsidy Program reduces out-of-pocket child care costs for eligible families, particularly those with young children or needing before/after-school or summer care, complementing DC's universal pre-K program. The brief appears to identify policy recommendations for DC leadership on how child care subsidies can support family economic stability amid budget constraints and labor market uncertainty.
papers.method: Policy analysis and brief for DC municipal leadership; no empirical study design specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Child care subsidy programs are standard municipal/state policy tools; structure and eligibility rules easily adapted across cities.
papers.cost_note: Subsidy programs require ongoing public funding; cost-effectiveness depends on take-up rates and wage effects not detailed in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates child care subsidy programs (e.g., CCAMPIS, ACD); this brief offers direct design and policy lessons for optimization.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence that subsidies improve parental workforce participation and child outcomes; effects real but not transformative on crime/safety metrics.
papers.underused_note: DC has robust program; many large US cities (including NYC) have fragmented, under-funded subsidy systems with significant gaps in coverage and eligibility.
papers.novelty_note: Child care as economic enabler is well-established policy logic; framing as public-safety lever is less common but not novel.

papers.title: Comparing the Effectiveness of Low-Carbon Policies : A Technology Diffusion Approach
papers.abstract: This paper studies the effectiveness of different policy instruments in accelerating the adoption of low-carbon technologies. Despite substantial declines in the costs of these technologies, diffusion remains uneven, driven by local economic conditions and policy environments. Using a modeling approach grounded in technology diffusion theory, the paper assesses how price-based (for example, carbon taxes and subsidies) and non-price-based (for example, mandates and regulatory requirements) instruments affect technology diffusion and carbon emissions across four East Asian economies. The analysis explicitly accounts for path dependence, learning, and technology uncertainty, factors often overlooked in standard models. The results highlight the importance of timing and policy sequencing: in the early stages of diffusion, price instruments may be less effective while non-price instruments can more successfully address coordination failures and subsequently enhance the impact of carbon pricing. The optimal policy combinations ultimately depend on the stage of technological maturity, initial technology adoption patterns, and local conditions.
papers.idea: NYC could sequence low-carbon technology policies by deploying non-price regulatory mandates (e.g., building electrification requirements) early, then layer carbon pricing (taxes/subsidies) once adoption reaches critical mass.
papers.summary: This World Bank modeling study compares price-based and non-price policy instruments for accelerating low-carbon technology adoption across four East Asian economies. The key finding is that policy sequencing matters: non-price instruments (mandates, regulations) work better in early diffusion stages to overcome coordination failures, while price instruments become more effective once technologies mature and adoption spreads.
papers.reach: Four East Asian economies; city-level application not specified
papers.method: Computational technology diffusion model grounded in diffusion theory, accounting for path dependence, learning, and uncertainty across four East Asian economies; specific sample size and data sources not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Policy sequencing principles are generalizable; NYC could adapt framework to its own tech-maturity landscape, though local conditions vary.
papers.cost_note: Regulatory mandates and carbon pricing are administratively low-cost once designed; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already uses both mandates (Local Law 97 emissions caps) and subsidies (ICEE green-energy rebates); findings could inform optimal timing and mix, though study is East Asian–specific.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modeling study showing moderate promise for sequenced policies; findings are theoretically sound but lack NYC-specific empirical validation.
papers.underused_note: NYC has adopted some mandates (e.g., Local Law 97) but rarely sequences them strategically with pricing; policy layering is uncommon in US cities.
papers.novelty_note: Policy sequencing is somewhat known in economics, but applying it to NYC's specific tech-adoption landscape and timing carbon pricing *after* mandates is a fresher framing.

papers.title: Telemental health service delivery to justice-involved persons With substance use: Staff perspectives
papers.authors: Chelsey Bull, Femina P. Varghese, Heath Braziel, Sydney Skaggs, Lorraine Stigar, Logan Snyder, Nick Zaller
papers.abstract: The purpose of this study was to understand the availability and use of telemental health with justice-involved adults under community supervision with substance use behaviors. Semistructured interviews were administered to 16 community corrections employees from rural facilities in a southern state on their knowledge and experience with telemental health. Thematic analysis using MAXQDA was applied. Five primary themes were found: (a) barriers to accessing face-to-face services, (b) barriers to accessing telemental health services, (c) barriers to accessing services exacerbated by COVID-19 pandemic, (d) benefits of telemental health services, and (e) suggestions to improve mental health services. Findings revealed limited awareness of available services among staff, challenges to accessing services including lack of transportation or insurance, exacerbation of challenges due to the pandemic, and suggestions for increased training and funding for telemental health services.
papers.idea: Expand telemental health service delivery to justice-involved persons under community supervision with substance use disorders, with targeted staff training and insurance/transportation support to overcome documented barriers.
papers.summary: This qualitative study interviewed 16 community corrections staff in rural southern facilities about telemental health (TMH) access for justice-involved adults with substance use. Staff reported limited awareness of available TMH services, significant barriers to both in-person and telemental health access (transportation, insurance gaps, COVID-19 disruptions), but also identified concrete benefits of TMH and recommended increased training and funding.
papers.reach: 16 community corrections staff from rural facilities in one southern state
papers.method: Semistructured interviews with 16 community corrections employees, analyzed using thematic analysis in MAXQDA software.
papers.scalability_note: TMH infrastructure is transferable across jurisdictions; identified barriers (transportation, insurance, staff training) are addressable through policy; limited by rural-specific context of study.
papers.cost_note: TMH typically lower cost than in-person services; study suggests modest funding increases for training/platform expansion; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large justice-involved population under community supervision; existing probation/parole infrastructure; urban barriers (insurance, digital access) differ from rural setting but remain actionable; substance use treatment is a stated priority.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study identifying barriers and staff perspectives; no outcome data on intervention effectiveness or recidivism/health impacts.
papers.underused_note: Telemental health for justice-involved populations remains underfunded and fragmented; rural/community supervision settings severely lag urban implementations.
papers.novelty_note: Telehealth expansion is increasingly conventional; framing as justice-specific solution is modest evolution, not a fundamentally new approach.

papers.title: The Impact of Dating Apps on Young Adults: Evidence from Tinder
papers.authors: Berkeren Büyükeren, Alexey Makarin, Heyu Xiong
papers.abstract: Online dating apps have transformed the dating market, yet their broader effects remain unclear. We study Tinder's impact on college students using its initial marketing focus on Greek organizations for identification. We show that the full-scale launch of Tinder led to a sharp, persistent increase in sexual activity, but with little corresponding impact on the formation of long-term relationships or relationship quality. Dating outcome inequality, especially among men, rose, alongside rates of sexual assault and STDs. However, despite these changes, Tinder's introduction did not worsen students' mental health on average and may have even led to improvements for female students. (JEL I12, I23, J13, J16, L86)
papers.idea: City could partner with dating apps to implement safety features, consent education, or STI-prevention campaigns targeting college-age users.
papers.summary: The study found that Tinder's launch led to increased sexual activity and rising rates of sexual assault and STDs among college students, but no worsening of mental health on average. Dating outcome inequality rose, particularly among men.
papers.reach: College students (population not specified in abstract)
papers.method: Natural experiment using Tinder's phased rollout with marketing focus on Greek organizations as identification strategy to estimate causal impact on college dating and health outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Study covers college students only; intervention would require private-sector app cooperation, limiting direct city control.
papers.cost_note: Public health messaging or partnership model would be low-cost, though dependent on app willingness.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could theoretically coordinate with app platforms on campus safety campaigns (CUNY, NYU, Columbia), but lacks direct regulatory lever over private apps; study offers diagnostic value but no concrete policy blueprint.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents correlation with sexual activity increase and elevated STI/assault rates, but effects are mixed (mental health improved for some) and mechanisms unclear for public-health intervention.
papers.underused_note: Dating apps have some safety features, but coordinated city-level prevention partnerships with platforms targeting STI and consent remain uncommon and underfunded.
papers.novelty_note: Framing dating apps as a public-health leverage point for STI and consent is relatively fresh; most city interventions ignore this channel.

papers.title: PROTOCOL: Effectiveness of Interventions for the Prevention and Treatment of Obesity in Children and Adolescents From Low‐ and Middle‐Income Countries: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
papers.authors:
Gerardo A. Zavala, 
Muhammad Asim, 
Aliya Ayub, 
Abdul Momin Rizwan Ahmad, 
Asha Prasad Pillai, 
Elena Pavlou, 
Avantika Sharma, 
Bilal Ahmad Khan, 
Hira Shakoor, 
Liina Mansukoski, 
Olga P. Garcia Obregon, 
Umber Khan, 
Zala Khan, 
Aliya Rehman, 
Badur Un Nisa, 
Humaira Bibi, 
Romania Iqbal, 
Suneel Gill, 
Urooj Ashfaq, 
Farman Ullah Khan, 
Zulfiqar Bhutta, 
Sarah Forberger, 
Kamran Siddiqi, 
Marc Suhrcke, 
Simon Walker, 
Zainab Samad, 
Saima Afaq
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt evidence-based obesity prevention and treatment interventions for children and adolescents identified as effective in low- and middle-income country settings.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review and meta-analysis examining the effectiveness of interventions to prevent and treat obesity in children and adolescents in low- and middle-income countries. The protocol outlines methodology for synthesizing evidence on what works, which can inform urban health policy.
papers.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis protocol (Campbell Collaboration); methodology for identifying, appraising, and synthesizing intervention studies in low/middle-income country contexts.
papers.scalability_note: Interventions tested across diverse low/middle-income settings; findings likely transferable to urban programs.
papers.cost_note: Obesity interventions in resource-limited settings often leverage existing infrastructure; moderate per-child costs likely.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has significant child obesity burden and established health department/school systems; findings from similar socioeconomic contexts applicable to high-poverty neighborhoods.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Systematic review of obesity interventions; effects vary widely by intervention type and setting; modest average improvements in BMI/weight outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Obesity prevention in schools and youth programs is already mainstream in US cities; NYC has established childhood obesity initiatives (e.g., health department campaigns, school nutrition standards).
papers.novelty_note: Applying evidence from LMIC to high-income US contexts is conventional; obesity prevention in youth is well-established public health priority.

papers.title: The Gendered Nature of Community- and Family-Level Adversity: Perspectives from Justice-Involved Youth in Northern Ireland
papers.authors: Orlaith Rice, Colm Walsh
papers.abstract: This qualitative study explores the gendered dimensions of community- and family-level adversity among justice-involved young people in Northern Ireland. Drawing on 30 semi-structured interviews with boys and girls referred to the Northern Ireland Youth Justice Agency, this research examines how gender shapes experiences of community and family deprivation, instability, and adversity, generally considered to be gender-neutral criminogenic needs. Findings reveal that while socioeconomic and family stressors affect both genders, their impacts on girls, as compared with boys, differ. The lens through which their community was viewed differed according to gender. Street violence and crime were more salient in boys’ lives, while family dysfunction appeared to impact girls more acutely. These insights underscore the need for early, gender-responsive youth offending interventions that meaningfully address relational safety and community marginalization.
papers.idea: Design and deploy gender-responsive youth justice interventions in NYC that address relational safety and community marginalization differently for boys (focusing on street violence/crime exposure) and girls (focusing on family dysfunction and instability).
papers.summary: This qualitative study of 30 justice-involved youth in Northern Ireland found that while boys and girls both face socioeconomic and family stressors, boys are more affected by street violence and crime while girls are more acutely impacted by family dysfunction. The research identifies the need for gender-differentiated approaches to youth offending prevention and intervention.
papers.method: Qualitative study using 30 semi-structured interviews with boys and girls referred to the Northern Ireland Youth Justice Agency, examining gendered dimensions of community and family adversity.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are from a small qualitative study in one jurisdiction; generalizability to NYC requires validation, but gender-responsive program design is scalable once validated.
papers.cost_note: Program cost not specified; gender-responsive youth interventions typically require modest staff training and curriculum adaptation.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's Department of Probation, courts, and youth development agencies regularly implement youth justice programs; gender-responsive design aligns with current best-practice frameworks and could be integrated into existing systems.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study with no intervention tested; identifies disparities but lacks outcome data on effectiveness of proposed gender-responsive approach.
papers.underused_note: Most NYC youth justice programs remain gender-neutral despite growing evidence of differential needs; gender-responsive models underfunded relative to their promise.
papers.novelty_note: Gender-responsive youth justice is emerging but still counters conventional one-size-fits-all framing; the specific focus on relational safety vs. street violence as gendered pathways adds nuance.

papers.title: Three Things DC's New Mayor and Council Need to Know About Electricity Affordability
papers.authors: Isabella Remor
papers.abstract: In the past year, residents of Washington, DC, have seen the greatest increase in residential electricity prices in the US, reflecting both national trends and local realities. Increased electrification and data center growth concentrated in Northern Virginia has strained the regional electricity market. Wholesale prices that determine how much Pepco pays its suppliers for power increased more than 400 percent in 2025 compared with 2024, with generation charges going up 119 percent. These supply-side costs sit largely outside local regulators’ control, but the portion of DC residents’ bills that local regulators can directly influence, roughly 30 percent, is where the next mayor and council have real levers to pull. These rising electricity costs have the greatest impact on those already struggling to make ends meet. Residents in neighborhoods with the lowest incomes spend a disproportionate share of their income on electricity, and assistance programs aren’t reaching the households that need
papers.idea: Expand and improve utility assistance programs and regulate the ~30% of electricity bills under local control to reduce energy cost burden on low-income households.
papers.summary: DC residents experienced the largest residential electricity price increase in the US in the past year, driven by wholesale market pressures and regional electrification. While wholesale costs are largely outside local regulatory control, the 30% of bills influenced by local regulators represents a direct lever. Low-income neighborhoods disproportionately bear this burden, and existing assistance programs are not reaching eligible households.
papers.method: Policy analysis of DC electricity pricing structure, wholesale vs. regulated cost breakdown, and assessment of assistance program reach (descriptive/non-empirical).
papers.scalability_note: Utility regulation and bill assistance are standard municipal/state functions; applicable to most US cities with municipal oversight of utility commissions.
papers.cost_note: Program expansion costs depend on design, but regulatory reforms and improved targeting of existing assistance can reduce per-household burden with modest incremental cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has Con Edison utility regulation, LIHEAP/assistance programs, and municipal authority over utility commission; framework directly transferable.
papers.effect_size: Wholesale prices increased more than 400% in 2025 compared with 2024; generation charges increased 119%
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Utility assistance reduces energy burden moderately; regulatory intervention on controllable costs shows promise but real-world NYC/DC outcomes mixed.
papers.underused_note: LIHEAP and utility assistance programs are established nationally; DC/NYC have programs but underfunded relative to need—not a novel gap.
papers.novelty_note: Separating controllable vs. uncontrollable utility costs is sensible framing, but utility assistance & rate regulation are conventional policy levers.

papers.title: Indoor Thermal Environments, Cooling Access, and Energy Burden in New Orleans, LA: Challenges and Opportunities for Heat Adaptation
papers.authors: Lena Easton-Calabria, Caroline Reed, Teague Ruder, Jordan Mychal, Jacopo Scazzosi, Ramya Chari, Brian Vant-Hull, Julia Kumari Drapkin, Jaime Madrigano
papers.abstract: Abstract Extreme heat is a significant and growing health hazard in urban locations around the world, particularly in the Southeastern United States (U.S.). While most extreme heat research and interventions are focused on ambient outdoor conditions and the neighborhood environment, the indoor residential environment is where the most severe heat-health consequences occur. The aim of this study was to characterize the indoor thermal environments and identify predictors of high indoor temperatures for residents of heat-vulnerable neighborhoods within New Orleans, Louisiana, within the context of current heat adaptation measures and issues of energy insecurity. We conducted surveys with both open- and closed-ended questions and measured indoor temperature over 2-week sampling periods in 114 households across two heat vulnerable New Orleans wards during the warm seasons of 2023 and 2024. Our study found that a combination of AC type and use, along with outdoor daily maximum temperature, were significant predictors of indoor maximum overnight temperature. Our results indicate that households without AC, using window AC units, or those not running central AC all or most of the time struggled to maintain 80 degrees Fahrenheit overnight (a threshold deemed appropriate by a recent healthy homes ordinance) once outdoor daily maximum temperatures exceeded 90 degrees Fahrenheit. Homeownership, compared to renting, was associated with higher overnight indoor temperatures, greater variability in typical AC use patterns, and greater sensitivity of summer monthly energy expenditures to differences in AC use patterns, potentially indicating that this group is practicing energy limiting behavior. This paper contributes to limited literature on indoor thermal environments, particularly in the Southeastern U.S., and underscores the importance of housing and energy burden in heat adaptation.
papers.idea: Cities should mandate minimum indoor temperature standards (80°F overnight) in rental housing codes and fund targeted AC installation/repair programs for heat-vulnerable neighborhoods, prioritizing renters and low-income homeowners.
papers.summary: A survey of 114 New Orleans households found that residents without AC, with only window units, or not running central AC consistently struggled to maintain 80°F overnight once outdoor temps exceeded 90°F. Homeowners showed greater energy-limiting behavior and higher temperature variability, suggesting economic strain on low-income owner-occupants.
papers.method: Cross-sectional household survey with open/closed questions plus 2-week indoor temperature monitoring in 114 homes across two New Orleans wards during 2023–2024 warm seasons.
papers.scalability_note: AC access/indoor temperature standards are replicable across cities; implementation varies by local housing stock and climate.
papers.cost_note: Central AC installation ~$3,500–$5,000 per unit; window units $200–$1,500; city subsidy programs needed for equity, moderate cost-effectiveness.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing healthy homes ordinance framework; heat vulnerability is rising in outer boroughs; landlord compliance infrastructure in place; summer heat deaths rising.
papers.effect_size: Households without AC, using window AC, or not running central AC struggled to maintain 80°F overnight once outdoor temperatures exceeded 90°F
papers.reach: 114 households across two New Orleans wards during 2023-2024
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies clear predictors of dangerous indoor temps (AC type, outdoor max temp) and documents widespread failure to meet 80°F overnight standard, but is observational/descriptive rather than intervention-testing.
papers.underused_note: Thermal housing standards and targeted cooling programs are rarely mandated or funded at scale in US cities; most heat policy focuses on outdoor cooling and emergency response, not indoor rental-code enforcement.
papers.novelty_note: Reframing heat as an indoor housing/energy-burden problem (not just outdoor/neighborhood) is fresher than ambient-heat focus, but indoor building codes and AC installation programs themselves are not new.

papers.title: Unequal Exposure: Examining Outdoor Work and Climate Exposure in the US
papers.authors: Lisa Clemans-Cope, Lisa Dubay, Vincent Pancini, Avani Pugazhendhi
papers.abstract: Climate change is intensifying heat waves, worsening air quality, and fueling more frequent wildfires—yet little is known about the roughly one in five US workers whose jobs regularly take them outdoors. This study provides a national portrait of outdoor workers’ demographics, health, employment benefits, and proximity to climate-related environmental hazards. The findings reveal where protections fall short and which workers face the greatest risks. Why This Matters Outdoor workers are disproportionately male, Hispanic, lower-income, and less likely to hold a college degree. They are more likely to be uninsured, lack paid sick leave, and work in self-employed or nonstandard arrangements—all factors that can limit their ability to avoid hazardous conditions or seek timely care. Meanwhile, occupational protections remain fragmented: only six states have outdoor heat standards, only three have wildfire smoke rules, and a proposed federal heat rule has stalled. Two states have preempted local heat ordinances entirely. Federal, state
papers.idea: Cities can adopt local outdoor heat and air-quality standards for workers (construction, delivery, landscaping) with mandatory rest breaks, shade access, and real-time hazard alerts—filling the gap where federal/state protections are absent.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute study profiles ~20% of US workers in outdoor jobs, finding they are disproportionately Hispanic, lower-income, uninsured, and lack paid sick leave. The research documents their heightened exposure to heat, poor air quality, and wildfire smoke, while identifying major gaps: only 6 states have outdoor heat standards and 3 have wildfire smoke rules.
papers.method: Descriptive national portrait using demographic, employment, and health data; appears to combine Census/labor data with occupational hazard mapping, though specific sample size and methodology are not detailed in the abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Regulatory standards and alert systems are easily replicated across cities; implementation cost and enforcement complexity vary.
papers.cost_note: Moderate—requires monitoring infrastructure, alert systems, and compliance oversight, but no per-unit costs stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large outdoor workforce (construction, delivery, parks, sanitation); city agencies (OSHA, DOH) could adopt local heat/air-quality rules independent of stalled federal action.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents exposure gaps and vulnerability; proposes intervention with moderate logical promise but no RCT evidence of impact yet.
papers.underused_note: Only 6 states have heat standards, 3 have wildfire rules; NYC and most large cities lack local outdoor worker protections despite high construction/delivery workforce.
papers.novelty_note: Framing outdoor worker climate hazards as a local regulatory gap is sensible but not radical; idea extends existing OSHA/occupational safety logic to climate context.

papers.title: Supply and Nontraditional-Hour Care
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Child care that supports children’s development while enabling parents to work or attend school is hard to find across the country. To address this, states and communities first need to understand the existing supply. DECAL contracted with Urban to examine two aspects of this issue. First, in 2023, Urban examined parents’ needs for nonparental child care and their preferences for care during nontraditional hours (after 6:00 p.m. or before 7:00 a.m. on weekdays or anytime on weekends). Second, in 2024, Urban surveyed child care providers regarding how well licensing data reflected the number of slots they could offer families, whether those slots met families’ needs, and how easy or difficult it was to start new child care businesses.
papers.idea: Cities can use supply-side child care data to design targeted subsidies, licensing reform, or startup support programs that expand affordable care during nontraditional hours, enabling parental workforce participation.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute surveyed parents' needs for nontraditional-hour child care (before 7 a.m., after 6 p.m. weekdays, or weekends) and examined provider capacity and barriers to entry. The study assessed gaps between licensing data and actual provider capacity, and documented obstacles to starting new child care businesses.
papers.method: Two-part survey study: first surveyed parents on nontraditional care needs and preferences (2023); second surveyed child care providers on licensing accuracy, slot availability, family demand match, and business startup barriers (2024). Conducted for Georgia (DECAL) but methodology generalizable.
papers.scalability_note: Diagnostic work on supply/demand gaps is replicable across cities; actionable policy levers (subsidies, licensing reform, startup grants) scale readily.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost varies by intervention chosen (subsidies expensive; data collection + licensing reform moderate).
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive child care workforce and regulatory infrastructure; city could replicate survey design, use findings to target expansion of nontraditional care slots via existing subsidy/partnership programs (e.g., NYC DOE).
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; addresses real supply-demand gap with plausible workforce participation gains, but study is diagnostic (needs assessment) rather than outcome-tested intervention.
papers.underused_note: Supply-side child care mapping is rare in large US cities; most subsidies reactive rather than data-driven; nontraditional-hour care severely underfunded despite documented parent demand.
papers.novelty_note: Using supply data to target subsidies is sensible but not radical; framing nontraditional hours as equity lever is somewhat fresher than typical 9-to-5 policy focus.

papers.title: Measuring the Impact of Unconditional Cash on the Social Determinants of Health
papers.authors: Daisy Gomez Palacios, Mary Bogle, Alexa Kort
papers.abstract: This brief presents interim findings from the first cohort of participants in CashRx, a guaranteed income program administered by Bread for the City, which is a federally qualified health center that provides food, clothing, medical care, and legal and social services to low-income residents in Washington, DC. Financial strain is a key social determinant of health, shaping individuals’ access to stable housing, nutritious food, and consistent medical care. A growing body of research suggests that unrestricted cash assistance, like guaranteed income, can help reduce stress, improve material well-being, and support better health outcomes. Why This Matters The first cohort of CashRx participants demonstrates how flexible, dignity-centered cash assistance—combined with supportive services—can advance economic outlook and improve well-being among people facing chronic illness and financial instability. These findings underscore the potential of guaranteed income to address key social determinants of health, while also revealing the limits of monetary interventions without structural
papers.idea: Launch a guaranteed income pilot program paired with wraparound health and social services (modeled on CashRx) targeting low-income residents with chronic illness and financial instability.
papers.summary: CashRx, a guaranteed income program administered by a DC federally qualified health center, provided unrestricted cash assistance combined with supportive services to low-income participants. Interim findings suggest the program improved economic outlook and well-being by reducing financial strain—a key social determinant of health—and supporting better access to stable housing, food, and medical care.
papers.method: Interim analysis of first cohort of CashRx participants; setting is a federally qualified health center in Washington, DC; sample size not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Guaranteed income model is replicable; can be piloted in one or more NYC neighborhoods and expanded.
papers.cost_note: Direct cash transfers have known per-recipient costs; abstract does not specify total program cost per person, but likely moderate to high depending on grant amount and service intensity.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive FQHC network and could partner with existing health/social service providers (e.g., Federally Qualified Health Centers, DOHMH); model fits health equity and housing stability goals.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Interim findings show promise on economic outlook and well-being; full health/safety impact data not yet mature or fully reported.
papers.underused_note: Few guaranteed income pilots in US cities; NYC has limited guaranteed income programs relative to scale of need and evidence base; CashRx model rarely replicated.
papers.novelty_note: Guaranteed income itself is emerging; the pairing with healthcare wraparound services and explicit framing as health intervention is somewhat novel, though not entirely new.

papers.title: How Workforce Systems, Higher Ed, and Industry Can Expand Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: John Marotta, Julia Payne, Deborah Kobes
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This fact sheet outlines how higher education institutions, industry representatives, and other workforce development stakeholders could work together to expand high-quality apprenticeship degrees. We developed this agenda through extensive stakeholder consultation and refined it through a consensus-driven engagement process.
papers.idea: NYC could establish apprenticeship degree partnerships between CUNY/SUNY, industry employers, and workforce boards to offer registered apprenticeships bundled with associate/bachelor degrees, expanding paid work-based pathways to credentials.
papers.summary: This fact sheet describes apprenticeship degree programs that combine registered apprenticeships (paid work, wage progression, on-the-job training) with formal degree credentials (associate's through master's level). The authors consulted stakeholders and developed a consensus agenda for how higher ed, industry, and workforce systems can expand these hybrid programs at scale.
papers.method: Stakeholder consultation and consensus-driven engagement process (non-empirical policy development).
papers.scalability_note: Model is designed for multi-sector coordination; replicable across cities with existing apprenticeship and higher-ed infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing registered apprenticeship wage support; requires institutional coordination but minimal new public subsidy if employer-co-funded.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established apprenticeship networks, robust CUNY system, and strong union/industry partnerships; direct fit with existing workforce development infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; apprenticeships show consistent earnings/employment gains, but this is a policy design brief, not an impact evaluation with demonstrated NYC effects.
papers.underused_note: Apprenticeship degrees remain niche in US higher ed; CUNY/SUNY partnerships are underdeveloped relative to European models; minimal institutional investment in NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Integrating apprenticeships with degree-granting is not new (Switzerland, Germany); the framing is sensible but not conceptually fresh.

papers.title: Ego depletion and empathy: exploring self-regulatory resource depletion in hostage and crisis negotiators
papers.authors: Benjamin Zaiser, Jon C. Cole, Mario S. Staller, Amy R. Grubb
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study aimed to determine the effect of ego depletion (i.e. mental fatigue or self-control exhaustion) on hostage and crisis negotiators’ capacity to communicate empathy. Methods Two randomized controlled experiments with a within-subjects design assessed the ego depletion effect on 52 police negotiators during a simulated hostage situation. Experiment 1 employed a validated manipulation task established in ego depletion literature, while Experiment 2 used the cold pressor task, borrowed from medical literature. Empathy was measured using self-reports, validated third-party assessments, and testing. Results Experiment 1 failed to induce ego depletion, while Experiment 2 successfully induced ego depletion. However, no statistically significant differences in empathy were observed between experimental and control conditions. Conclusions The findings do not provide evidence that ego depletion reduces empathic responding in crisis negotiators under the conditions tested. Instead, they suggest that any effects of ego depletion on empathy may be context-dependent. Factors such as training and professional role may play a moderating role, although these were not directly examined. Accordingly, the results should be interpreted as exploratory and highlight the need for further research.
papers.idea: Train crisis negotiators to manage mental fatigue to preserve empathic communication capacity during extended hostage situations.
papers.summary: This study tested whether mental fatigue (ego depletion) reduces empathy in police hostage negotiators using two randomized experiments with 52 officers. One manipulation successfully induced ego depletion, but neither experiment found statistically significant differences in empathy between depleted and non-depleted conditions, suggesting the effect may be context-dependent or moderated by professional training.
papers.method: Two randomized controlled experiments with within-subjects design on 52 police negotiators in simulated hostage scenarios, using ego depletion manipulation and cold pressor tasks, with empathy measured via self-report, third-party assessment, and testing.
papers.scalability_note: Applies only to specialized hostage negotiation units; most police departments have small or no such units.
papers.cost_note: Training intervention would be low-cost if findings supported it; study itself provides no actionable cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has a negotiation unit, but study findings are null/exploratory and provide no concrete intervention to implement.
papers.effect_size: no significant effect of ego depletion on empathy
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No statistically significant effects found; negative/null RCT results despite rigorous design.
papers.underused_note: The intervention itself (fatigue management training) has modest uptake, but the underlying theory failed to demonstrate a problem worth solving in this context.
papers.novelty_note: Applies ego-depletion framework to crisis negotiation—intuitive but not groundbreaking; reframes negotiator performance as a cognitive-fatigue problem.

papers.title: Philadelphia’s Zero Fare Transit Pilot Program
papers.authors: Amelia Coffey, Lindiwe Rennert, Gabe Samuels, Hannah Sumiko Daly
papers.abstract: Affordable transportation remains a critical challenge for many low-income Philadelphia residents, limiting their ability to work and access services. This report examines Philadelphia’s Zero Fare transit pilot program, a novel approach to reducing transportation barriers by providing fully subsidized public transit through automatic enrollment. The City of Philadelphia funds and administers Zero Fare with support from the Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority (SEPTA). Drawing on interviews with program administrators and community partners and focus groups with recipients, the report examines how Zero Fare was designed and implemented, what worked well, what challenges emerged, and the lessons it offers for transit agencies and policymakers nationwide. Why This Matters People who struggle to afford transit face barriers to economic mobility and reduced quality of life, and yet most transit benefit programs offered on the nation’s largest transit networks reach only a fraction of eligible riders. Zero Fare demonstrates that reducing administrative barriers can
papers.idea: Implement automatic enrollment in a fully subsidized transit program for low-income residents to reduce administrative barriers and increase uptake.
papers.summary: Philadelphia's Zero Fare program provides fully subsidized public transit through automatic enrollment, funded by the city and administered with SEPTA. The Urban Institute study examines program design, implementation, successes, challenges, and lessons from interviews with administrators, community partners, and focus groups with recipients.
papers.method: Qualitative case study combining interviews with program administrators and community partners and focus groups with program recipients in Philadelphia.
papers.scalability_note: Transit subsidy programs are standard infrastructure; automatic enrollment mechanism is replicable across transit systems.
papers.cost_note: Depends on subsidy amount and enrollment scale; abstract does not provide per-rider cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates MTA with existing OMNY fare system and safety-net programs (NYCTA); could adapt automatic enrollment for low-income riders through existing infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Program increases access and uptake via auto-enrollment, but abstract doesn't quantify employment gains, crime reduction, or other public-safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Auto-enrollment subsidized transit is rare; most cities use means-tested opt-in programs with low uptake. Strong equity case but limited deployment.
papers.novelty_note: Auto-enrollment itself is not new (behavioral default), but applying it to transit subsidies for low-income residents is less common than typical income-verification barriers.

papers.title: To Deliver Quality Transit Service, Agencies Must Invest in Workforce Recruitment and Retention
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark, Yipeng Su, William J. Congdon
papers.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic and its aftermath undermined public transit agencies’ ability to attract and retain workers, even though jobs in the transit industry have traditionally been relatively well-paid and secured by unions. Initially, the public health crisis resulted in transit operators suffering from direct exposure to the disease, with many dying. People working in direct contact with passengers, like bus drivers, faced continued risk from daily contact. This resulted in many older staff retiring, high levels of employee absenteeism, and too few workers to provide service (PDF). In the years since, this transit workforce hasn’t expanded as quickly as the national workforce. Inadequate funding has made it difficult for transit agencies to afford rising labor costs. As a result, many transit agencies have had little choice but to cut operations, depriving the nation’s 20 million daily riders of access to bus and train services essential to getting to work, school
papers.idea: Cities should increase transit agency funding to enable competitive wage and benefit packages that improve worker recruitment and retention, thereby sustaining service frequency and coverage.
papers.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic triggered waves of transit worker retirements, absences, and departures due to health risks and inadequate compensation relative to labor-cost inflation. Without sufficient funding, transit agencies have had to cut service, reducing access for 20 million daily riders. The paper identifies workforce investment as essential to service continuity.
papers.method: Policy analysis and synthesis of pandemic-era workforce trends in public transit; appears to be descriptive review of operational and staffing data rather than a controlled empirical study.
papers.scalability_note: Workforce funding is a standard levier; replicable across all transit systems nationwide.
papers.cost_note: Requires sustained public subsidy; cost-effective for transit-dependent populations but significant budget exposure for cities.
papers.nyc_note: MTA directly operates buses and rail; increased city/state funding for labor costs is a proven policy lever in NYC transit.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Strong logical mechanism (worker retention → service continuity) but study is descriptive/diagnostic rather than experimental; demonstrates problem clearly, not causal intervention impact.
papers.underused_note: Transit funding/labor investment is actively debated policy; not neglected, though chronically underfunded relative to need across US cities including NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Standard labor-economics argument (pay workers better, retain them, improve service); frames existing problem but not a novel intervention or insight.

papers.title: Converting Vacant Housing Can Help Solve the Housing Crisis
papers.authors: Diane K. Levy
papers.abstract: Communities across the country are facing a housing shortage (PDF), prompting local policymakers to make way for more—particularly affordable options for middle- and lower-income families. In Tulsa, like many US cities, the housing shortage has reached a crisis point. A recent housing assessment estimated that the city needs 4,000 units now and projected that, over the next 10 years, it will need an additional 12,900 units. As part of a project examining how to increase affordable housing production in Oklahoma, Oklahoma stakeholders highlighted the persistent challenge of putting vacant properties in cities like Tulsa into productive use. Strategies other municipalities are undertaking to incentivize bringing vacant properties into productive use—including improving the accuracy of vacancy data, developing systems to manage vacancies, and implementing policies and programs to incentivize vacant property conversion into available housing—offer lessons for Tulsa and any community seeking to address the housing supply crisis. Returning vacant properties
papers.idea: Cities can systematize the identification and conversion of vacant properties into housing through improved vacancy data, management systems, and targeted incentive programs.
papers.summary: Tulsa and similar US cities face severe housing shortages requiring thousands of new units. The research identifies vacant property conversion as a key lever and highlights that other municipalities have used improved vacancy tracking, vacancy management systems, and policy incentives to bring vacant properties back into productive use.
papers.method: Case study and policy review examining Tulsa's housing assessment and strategies employed by other municipalities; specific sample size and design not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Vacancy tracking and incentive programs are standard municipal tools; replicable across cities with varying housing markets.
papers.cost_note: Primarily administrative/regulatory overhead; leverages existing property and tax systems; conversion costs borne by private/nonprofit developers.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing vacant property data infrastructure and tax incentive programs (e.g., 421-a); could enhance targeting and systematization of conversion pipelines.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; vacancy conversion addresses real supply constraints, but effect size depends heavily on scale, financing, and local market conditions—not yet robustly quantified in rigorous trials.
papers.underused_note: Some cities (Philadelphia, Detroit) have piloted vacancy programs; NYC has started but far below scale needed; underfunded relative to housing shortage severity.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional housing-supply approach; vacancy reuse is well-recognized as part of solution, though data-driven systems and incentive design details offer modest innovation.

papers.title: Redistricting from the Bottom Up: Sampling Communities of Interest with Differential Privacy
papers.authors: Atticus McWhorter, Caroline Hammond, Nianqiao Phyllis Ju, Daryl DeFord
papers.abstract: Independent Redistricting Commissions (IRCs) are a promising tool for bottom-up redistricting, but their public testimony processes are vulnerable to adversarial manipulation. We propose using differential privacy to draw redistricting plans that incorporate community of interest (COI) testimonies while remaining robust to adversarial input. Treating individual testimonies as data points, we use the marked edge walk to sample from differentially private distributions of redistricting plans via the exponential mechanism. We introduce two score functions and demonstrate that both can be targeted by MEW across a range of privacy budgets. Applying this method to Missouri's mid-cycle redistricting using 808 COI testimonies, we show that COI-informed sampling outperforms an uninformed baseline and the enacted plan. An adversarial experiment demonstrates that the method can be robust to attacks under certain privacy budgets and may perform better in practice than formal group privacy guarantees imply. We also find that stronger COI preservation tends to spread minority and Democratic representation more evenly across districts.
papers.idea: Use differential privacy algorithms to incorporate community testimony into redistricting plans while protecting against coordinated manipulation.
papers.summary: The authors propose a method using differential privacy and the marked edge walk algorithm to draw redistricting plans that respect community-of-interest testimonies while remaining robust to adversarial input. Applied to Missouri's mid-cycle redistricting with 808 testimonies, the COI-informed sampling outperformed both an uninformed baseline and the enacted plan, and appeared resistant to gaming under certain privacy budgets.
papers.method: Applied differential privacy and exponential mechanism sampling to 808 community-of-interest testimonies from Missouri's redistricting process, comparing COI-informed plans against uninformed baseline and enacted plan; included adversarial robustness testing.
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm is generalizable to any redistricting process with testimony data; already demonstrated on real US case.
papers.cost_note: Primarily software implementation; computational cost modest relative to redistricting commission budgets.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not control state redistricting (state legislature does), but technique could apply to city council or community board boundary review if those processes exist or are created.
papers.reach: 808 community of interest testimonies from Missouri's mid-cycle redistricting
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Proof-of-concept on one state; demonstrates technical feasibility but lacks real-world implementation data on actual civic outcomes or equity improvements.
papers.underused_note: Differential privacy + redistricting is a niche intersection; IRCs exist but rarely employ sophisticated algorithmic safeguards against manipulation or systematically integrate community input at scale.
papers.novelty_note: Frames redistricting as a data-privacy problem rather than purely political one; uses differential privacy (uncommon in civic infrastructure) to operationalize community voice while guarding against gaming.

papers.title: Federal Research Funding and STEM Education
papers.abstract: This paper examines how federal science and engineering research funding—although intended to advance research—affects degree production and programs offered in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM). Using data from 1971–2016, the study implements a triple-difference design that exploits variation across colleges, time, and fields of study. The findings show that federal grants generate 27.4 percent of doctorates and 14.7 percent of undergraduate science, technology, engineering, and mathematics degrees, as well as 6.3 percent of doctoral programs and 3.7 percent of undergraduate programs in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics annually across 200 U.S. research universities. The impacts are concentrated in biology and engineering, aligning with the priorities of major funders such as the Department of Health and Human Services, the National Science Foundation, and the Department of Defense. These findings suggest that research grants to universities may generate a “double dividend,” simultaneously expanding the supply of skilled labor in targeted fields while also advancing scientific discovery.
papers.idea: Cities could lobby for or condition local research funding to preferentially support STEM fields that improve public safety outcomes (e.g., engineering, data science).
papers.summary: This paper quantifies the effect of federal research grants on STEM degree production and program offerings at 200 U.S. research universities from 1971–2016. Federal funding generated 27.4% of science/engineering doctorates and 14.7% of undergraduate STEM degrees annually, with impacts concentrated in biology and engineering aligned with HHS, NSF, and DoD priorities.
papers.effect_size: 27.4% of doctorates and 14.7% of undergraduate degrees attributable to federal research funding
papers.method: Triple-difference design exploiting variation across colleges, time, and fields of study using 1971–2016 institutional data.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are about federal funding effects on university degree production; a city would have minimal direct lever over federal research appropriations.
papers.cost_note: Federal research grants are already being spent; marginal cost to a city to influence allocation is unclear and likely high.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has major research universities but limited authority over federal research funding. Could advocate for funding priorities but cannot directly implement this intervention.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrates substantial causal relationship (27% of doctorates, 15% of undergrad STEM degrees attributed to federal funding), but the public-safety application is hypothetical and untested.
papers.underused_note: Federal STEM funding is already substantial and strategically allocated; the novelty lies in *intentionally redirecting* it toward public-safety outcomes, which cities rarely do systematically.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes STEM investment as a public-safety pipeline tool rather than pure economic/innovation play; moderately fresh framing but not radical.
