papers.title: Geographic Networks of Gun Violence: Exploring Hot Spot Connectivity Through Ballistic Evidence and Social Network Analysis
papers.authors: Eric L. Piza, Cassie McMillan, Daniel Trovato
papers.abstract: Objectives While crime-and-place research consistently demonstrates that a minority of places account for most of the crime in a given jurisdiction, much less is known about the extent to which these hot spots are interconnected. Informed by the social network analysis literature, we explore whether gun violence networks occur in spatial forms, with hot spots connected by distinct firearms used in the commission of crime. Methods We construct a network where ballistic evidence from crime guns ties together street segments in Kansas City, Missouri. Data from the National Integrated Ballistic Information Network is used to construct connections between street segments that contain ballistic evidence associated with the same crime gun. Results Findings indicate that street segments classified as gun violence hot spots were more likely to be connected to one another than would be expected by chance (i.e., hot spot homophily). This suggests hot spots do not operate as self-contained entities but are components of a larger geographic network of gun violence. Conclusion The current study suggests that crime gun linkages can enhance our understanding of how gun violence hot spots not only share certain structural features but also are connected in ways suggestive of spatial and social interdependence.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Use ballistic network analysis to identify interconnected gun violence hot spots and coordinate cross-neighborhood enforcement strategies.
papers.summary: The study found that gun violence hot spots in Kansas City are not isolated but form geographic networks connected by crime guns that appear at multiple locations. Hot spots showed homophily—they were more likely to be linked to other hot spots than random chance would predict—suggesting spatial and social interdependence.
papers.method: Network analysis using ballistic evidence from the National Integrated Ballistic Information Network (NIBIN) to connect street segments in Kansas City where the same crime guns were recovered.
papers.scalability_note: Requires NIBIN participation and ballistic matching infrastructure; feasible but not trivial to replicate across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing NIBIN database; analysis cost moderate; no new field interventions proposed.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD has ballistic database and hot-spot mapping capacity; method could transfer but requires ballistic linking workflow and criminal justice coordination.
papers.effect_size: Gun violence hot spots more likely to be connected to one another than expected by chance
papers.reach: Street segments in Kansas City, Missouri
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrates meaningful network connectivity between hot spots, but study is observational/descriptive rather than interventional—no direct evidence that acting on this knowledge reduces violence.
papers.underused_note: Ballistic network mapping is technically sophisticated and rarely deployed systematically for cross-neighborhood enforcement coordination in major US cities; most rely on traditional hot-spot policing without network lens.
papers.novelty_note: Frames gun violence as a networked spatial phenomenon rather than isolated incidents; linking ballistic evidence to social-network logic is a relatively fresh analytical approach for police strategy.

papers.title: Mapping the Relationship between Neighborhood-Level Factors and Nutrition Environments of Restaurants Offering Children’s Menus
papers.authors: Megan Knapp, Maria M. Munoz, Yin Wang, Charles Stoecker, Lisa Hofmann, Melissa Fuster
papers.abstract: Abstract This study examined the nutritional environments of restaurants offering children’s menus and their association with neighborhood-level factors in a southern United States city with disproportionately high childhood obesity rates. On-site assessments were conducted to generate nutrition environment scores for restaurants offering children’s menus in New Orleans, Louisiana ( n = 191). Restaurants were geocoded and linked to neighborhood characteristics, including racial/ethnic composition and socioeconomic opportunity measured by the Social and Economic Child Opportunity Index (COI-SE). Multivariate linear regressions assessed associations between nutrition environment scores and neighborhood characteristics, adjusting for restaurant-specific attributes. Restaurants located in neighborhoods with COI-SEs above the median had significantly higher nutrition environment scores (+ 2.41 points; 95% CI 0.47, 4.35; p = 0.015). This relationship appears to be driven by the associations with the healthy options and facilitators to healthy eating sub-scores. Neighborhood racial/ethnic composition was not associated with nutrition environment scores. Restaurant characteristics were associated with nutrition environment quality: sit-down restaurants had lower scores than fast-food restaurants (− 2.35 points; 95% CI −4.45, −0.24; p = 0.029), while chain restaurants scored higher than independent establishments (+ 2.13 points; 95% CI 0.44, 3.82; p = 0.014). These findings suggest that neighborhood socioeconomic opportunity and restaurant characteristics shape children’s exposure to healthier restaurant food environments. Health strategies that prioritize place-based policies and support healthier children’s menu offerings, particularly in lower-opportunity neighborhoods, may help address inequities in food environments and reduce disparities in child health.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt place-based policies requiring or incentivizing restaurants—especially independent sit-down restaurants in lower-opportunity neighborhoods—to offer healthier children's menu options, modeled on existing food-service regulations.
papers.summary: The study found that restaurants in neighborhoods with higher socioeconomic opportunity offered significantly better nutrition environments for children's menus, while neighborhood racial/ethnic composition was not a driver. Chain restaurants and fast-food outlets had healthier children's menus than independent sit-down restaurants, suggesting that restaurant type and business model shape menu quality more than customer demographics.
papers.method: Cross-sectional on-site nutrition assessments of 191 restaurants offering children's menus in New Orleans, geocoded and linked to neighborhood socioeconomic opportunity and demographic data; multivariate linear regression adjusted for restaurant characteristics.
papers.scalability_note: Menu standards and restaurant incentive programs are modular; applicable across any city with restaurant licensing authority.
papers.cost_note: Minimal direct cost if leveraging existing health department inspection infrastructure; incentives (tax breaks, marketing) depend on design.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already regulates restaurant nutrition labeling and has active health department oversight; could expand via menu-review requirements or zoning/licensing incentives targeting lower-opportunity areas.
papers.effect_size: 2.41 points higher nutrition environment scores in neighborhoods with above-median socioeconomic opportunity
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Cross-sectional associations found, but no intervention tested; modest effect size (~2.4-point difference on nutrition score); no causal evidence of policy impact on child health outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Menu labeling and some restaurant nutrition standards exist in NYC/CA, but targeted place-based incentives for independent sit-down restaurants in low-opportunity neighborhoods remain limited and underfunded.
papers.novelty_note: Food-environment mapping is established; linking children's menus specifically to neighborhood opportunity is somewhat novel but frames problem through conventional food-policy lens rather than behavioral or systemic innovation.

papers.title: How Are Earned Wage Access Products Regulated in Your State?
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Use of earned wage access (EWA) products has increased significantly in recent years. In 2022, an estimated 10 million workers used these products to access more than $31.9 billion in earned wages before their scheduled payday. At a time when many Americans are struggling to make ends meet, EWA products can help users bridge temporary cash shortfalls or navigate short-term emergency expenses by allowing them to access wages they have already earned but not yet been paid. But most products charge users a fee for faster access to their wages. And some don’t clearly disclose their fee and cost structures. Despite their growing popularity, EWA products have not been defined consistently under federal regulations. This has led to a patchwork of state laws that differ regarding how EWA products are classified, how transparent their pricing and fees must be, and which user safeguards they must implement. This tool provides a
papers.idea: NYC could adopt transparent disclosure and consumer-protection standards for earned wage access (EWA) products, or regulate them as a city to protect low-wage workers from predatory fees and unclear pricing.
papers.summary: Earned wage access products allow workers to access already-earned wages before payday, with 10 million users accessing $31.9 billion in 2022. Most products charge fees, often with unclear disclosure, and regulatory frameworks vary widely by state, creating consumer protection gaps.
papers.method: Regulatory landscape mapping and comparison tool across states; descriptive/inventory study of state-level EWA laws and classification schemes.
papers.scalability_note: Regulatory frameworks can be adopted city- or state-wide; applies to any jurisdiction with wage-earning workers.
papers.cost_note: Low implementation cost—primarily requires drafting disclosure/fee standards and light monitoring; no direct subsidy needed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could push for state-level regulation or adopt local transparency/anti-predation standards for city contractors; federal/state authority may limit city-only action, but advocacy and worker-protection measures are feasible.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Consumer protection & disclosure standards show moderate promise in reducing predatory financial products; evidence base exists from payday lending & similar regulations, but EWA-specific impact data limited.
papers.underused_note: Most states lack clear EWA regulation; NYC has not enacted specific disclosure/protection standards despite rapid product adoption affecting millions of low-wage workers.
papers.novelty_note: Applying consumer-protection frameworks to EWA mirrors existing payday/fintech regulation approaches; framing is sound but not conceptually fresh.

papers.title: Do pensions enhance effort and selective retention?: Evidence from public schools
papers.authors: Michael Bates, Andrew C. Johnston
papers.idea: Reform public-school pension structures to improve teacher effort, retention, and school performance.
papers.summary: This study examines whether pension design affects teacher effort and retention in public schools. The analysis likely tests whether changes to pension incentives influence how long teachers stay in the profession and how hard they work.
papers.method: The study examined public-school data to assess the relationship between pension structures and measures of teacher effort and retention; specifics of design and sample not available in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Pension policy is set at state and district level; findings could apply to any large urban school system.
papers.cost_note: Pension restructuring has no direct per-pupil cost beyond administrative implementation.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Teachers' Pension Fund is state-governed; city has limited unilateral control but could advocate for evidence-based reforms.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pension reforms show moderate effects on teacher retention and effort, but causal links to student outcomes are indirect and context-dependent.
papers.underused_note: Pension reform is actively debated in policy circles; implementation varies widely but is not neglected as a lever.
papers.novelty_note: Pension structure as a workforce-management tool is well-established; framing is conventional HR economics.

papers.title: Glucagon‐like peptide‐1 receptor agonist use and violent crime among US adults
papers.authors: Daniel C. Semenza, Christopher Thomas
papers.abstract: Abstract Glucagon‐like peptide‐1 receptor agonists (GLP‐1 RAs), a class of medications widely prescribed for diabetes and obesity, have exhibited emerging effects on substance use, reward processing, and impulse control. This study examines whether current GLP‐1 RA use moderates established behavioral pathways to violent crime from impulsivity and alcohol use. Data come from a 2025 nationally representative US survey ( n = 7521; 821 lifetime GLP‐1 RA users). Negative binomial regression models with overlap weighting compared current ( n = 597) and former ( n = 224) users on self‐reported violent criminal behavior in the past year. Although impulsivity and alcohol use were strongly associated with violent criminality, these associations were significantly weaker among current GLP‐1 RA users (GLP‐1 RA × impulsivity incidence rate ratio (IRR) = 0.38, p = 0.002; GLP‐1 RA × alcohol use IRR = 0.48, p = 0.023). Robustness checks consistently supported the GLP‐1 RA × impulsivity interaction, whereas less consistent support was found for the alcohol interaction. Results suggest that GLP‐1 RAs may attenuate widely documented behavioral risk mechanisms like impulsivity linked to aggression, pointing to novel biosocial hypotheses regarding pharmacological influences on violent criminality. Future longitudinal and experimental research should confirm these findings and test causal mechanisms.
papers.idea: Expand access to GLP-1 receptor agonist medications (e.g., semaglutide, tirzepatide) through city health programs and criminal justice diversion initiatives, as a complementary intervention to reduce violent crime among high-risk individuals with impulsivity or substance-use issues.
papers.summary: This cross-sectional study of 7,521 US adults found that current GLP-1 RA users showed significantly weaker associations between impulsivity and violent crime (IRR = 0.38) and between alcohol use and violent crime (IRR = 0.48) compared to non-users. The authors suggest GLP-1 RAs may attenuate behavioral risk pathways to violence by affecting reward processing and impulse control.
papers.method: Cross-sectional negative binomial regression analysis using 2025 nationally representative US survey data; 597 current GLP-1 RA users and 224 former users compared on self-reported violent criminal behavior in past year, with overlap weighting for covariate balance.
papers.scalability_note: GLP-1 RAs are expensive (~$1,000–$1,500/month), limiting universal deployment; requires integration into existing health/criminal-justice systems; observational evidence does not yet support population-level rollout.
papers.cost_note: GLP-1 RAs cost $1,000–$1,500/month; NYC would need insurance or subsidy; cost-per-crime-prevented not estimated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could pilot GLP-1 access via Medicaid/health dept. or criminal-justice reentry programs, but evidence is observational and causal mechanisms unproven; medication cost and medical contraindications limit reach.
papers.effect_size: 62% reduction in the impulsivity-violent crime association (IRR = 0.38) and 52% reduction in the alcohol use-violent crime association (IRR = 0.48) among current GLP-1 RA users
papers.reach: 7,521 US adults (597 current GLP-1 RA users)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect on impulsivity pathway (IRR 0.38); weaker/less consistent on alcohol pathway. Cross-sectional data limits causal inference; robustness checks support impulsivity finding but not alcohol.
papers.underused_note: GLP-1s are prescribed for metabolic conditions, not positioned as crime-reduction tools; criminal justice/public health integration for this use case is nearly absent in US cities.
papers.novelty_note: Genuinely counterintuitive: repurposing a metabolic medication as a behavioral/impulse-control intervention for violence prevention is a fresh biosocial angle not in mainstream criminal justice or harm-reduction discourse.

papers.title: Whose hotel does the AI recommend? An algorithm audit of reputation signals in LLM-assisted hotel selection
papers.authors: Mirza Samad Ahmed Baig, Syeda Anshrah Gillani, Asher Ali
papers.abstract: Travelers increasingly ask large language model (LLM) assistants which hotel to book, making these systems gatekeepers of property visibility -- yet what moves their recommendations is undocumented. We conduct a pre-specified algorithm audit using a randomized choice-based conjoint: across personas, prompt templates, and twelve open-weight and proprietary models, assistants choose among five hotels whose guest rating, review volume and recency, management response, chain affiliation, price, eco-certification, and list position are independently randomized. We estimate the average marginal component effect of each signal on the probability of recommendation. Guest rating and price dominate (a top rating raises selection by 31.6 percentage points; a high price lowers it by 30.0), reproducing human valence-and-price primacy but over-weighting eco-certification and ignoring management response. List position -- a content-free artifact -- shifts recommendations causally, worth about \$12 per night. Stated reasons track revealed weights imperfectly. The findings ground generative engine optimization and the accountability of AI infomediaries in causal evidence.
papers.idea: Cities could require transparency disclosures or audits of AI hotel-recommendation systems to prevent algorithmic bias that favors chains or penalizes independent properties.
papers.summary: The study audited large language models' hotel recommendations and found that guest ratings and price dominate recommendations, but also that list position (a content-free artifact) causally shifts selections worth ~$12/night, and that AI systems over-weight eco-certification while ignoring management response. The researchers used a randomized conjoint experiment across multiple models and personas.
papers.method: Pre-specified algorithm audit using randomized choice-based conjoint experiment varying guest rating, review volume/recency, management response, chain affiliation, price, eco-certification, and list position across twelve open-weight and proprietary LLM models.
papers.scalability_note: Requires specialized auditing expertise and ongoing monitoring of new models; not a scalable city service or program.
papers.cost_note: Audit itself is low-cost research, but enforcement/compliance monitoring would require city resources.
papers.nyc_note: Hotels are a tourist/commercial sector; no direct city-service or resident-welfare lever. NYC could theoretically audit or regulate AI intermediaries, but lacks clear authority and the problem affects travelers (not residents primarily) and hotel operators.
papers.effect_size: Top guest rating increases selection probability by 31.6 percentage points; high price decreases it by 30.0 percentage points; list position shifts recommendations worth ~$12 per night
papers.reach: 12 open-weight and proprietary LLM models tested across multiple personas and prompt templates
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Audit documents algorithmic bias mechanisms but lacks demonstration of real-world public-safety or civic outcomes; methodological rigor is high but applied to a domain tangential to core safety priorities.
papers.underused_note: AI transparency and algorithmic auditing are emerging policy priorities; hotel-recommendation bias is niche and low public-safety relevance compared to deployment barriers in criminal justice or housing contexts.
papers.novelty_note: Applying algorithmic-audit methodology to LLM infomediaries is relatively fresh; framing as a civic transparency issue is sound but the hotel domain itself is conventional.

papers.title: From Democracies to Autocracies: How AI Systems Enable Authoritarianism by Design
papers.authors: Jeba Sania, Marta Ziosi, Fazl Barez
papers.abstract: AI-enabled authoritarianism is not confined to autocracies. In this paper, we provide greater transparency by investigating and mapping the lifecycles of six AI systems deployed in different political regimes, ranging from the US to China. By drawing on an extensive range of sources (academic publications, investigative research reports, third-party evaluations, media interviews, government procurement notices), we conduct a systematic, qualitative comparison across systems to identify the critical technical and operational features that enable authoritarianism within their respective political contexts. We find that enabling features include the centralization and co-optation of administrative data for law enforcement and political punishment, regulatory gaps that fail to deter misuse, weak user compliance that nullifies human oversight mechanisms, and the encoding of protected group traits that identify members of vulnerable populations. We find that these features are present across systems deployed in autocratic and democratic regimes, albeit in varying configurations. We also find that both centralized and fragmented AI systems can contribute to authoritarianism by exploiting governance gaps: centralized systems directed by executive authorities, particularly within security and military institutions, are often not subjected to formal oversight mechanisms, while fragmented systems diffuse accountability between stakeholders, paving the way for entrenchment. These findings reveal that AI-enabled authoritarianism is distributed, resulting from design and operational choices made by developers, administrators, and users alike. We conclude with recommendations for developers and policymakers to mitigate these risks.
papers.idea: Cities could audit and reform AI systems used in law enforcement and administrative data to prevent authoritarianism through design and governance controls.
papers.summary: This paper maps six AI systems across autocratic and democratic regimes to identify technical and operational features that enable authoritarian misuse, including centralization of administrative data for law enforcement, regulatory gaps, weak oversight, and encoding of protected group traits. The authors find that both centralized and fragmented AI systems can enable authoritarianism through different governance failure modes, and that such risks exist across political systems, not just autocracies.
papers.method: Qualitative systematic comparison of six AI systems across different political contexts using academic publications, investigative reports, third-party evaluations, media interviews, and government procurement notices; no empirical evaluation of a specific intervention.
papers.scalability_note: Policy frameworks and audit procedures can scale, but require political will and context-specific adaptation.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs vary widely depending on scope of AI system audit and governance reform; no unit costs provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could apply governance recommendations to NYPD surveillance systems and city data platforms, but paper provides only high-level mitigation strategies, not concrete implementation blueprints.
papers.reach: 6 AI systems across different political regimes (US to China)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Systematic qualitative mapping identifies real vulnerabilities in AI governance; demonstrates mechanism but lacks quantified impact on actual outcomes or harm reduction.
papers.underused_note: NYC and most US cities lack systematic AI audits for law enforcement/admin systems; no routine governance controls or design-phase oversight built into procurement.
papers.novelty_note: Frames AI governance as authoritarianism-prevention rather than bias/accuracy; cross-regime comparison reveals design patterns applicable to democracies often overlooked.

papers.title: Advancing and Defending Homeownership Gains
papers.authors: Lydia Lo, Michael Neal, Ilina Mitra, Matthew Pruitt, Violet Sulka/Hewes, Jung Hyun Choi
papers.abstract: Why This Matters The conditions needed to facilitate homebuying have remained under immense pressure over the past year. Despite experiencing significant pressures, local collaboratives under the Wealth Opportunities Realized Through Homeownership (WORTH) Initiative have continued to support households’ ability to achieve or preserve homeownership. Collaboratives have demonstrated a strong commitment to their mission of reaching all high-barrier communities by seeking new methods for facilitating impact, including employing technology. And they have shown a willingness to test new ideas and engage with each other, resulting in more homebuying and preservation activity under WORTH. Key Takeaways This report shows the following findings: As challenges to new home purchases have persisted, collaboratives sharpened their focus on home preservation activities. Foreclosure prevention is the leading home preservation tool WORTH collaboratives used. Resolving heirs’ property was an emerging strategy more collaboratives started using. Rising construction costs have limited innovative housing supply programs. New home construction
papers.idea: Cities can establish or strengthen local collaborative networks (like WORTH) that combine foreclosure prevention, heirs' property resolution, and homeownership counseling to help high-barrier households achieve or preserve homeownership.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute evaluated the WORTH Initiative, a network of local collaboratives supporting homeownership in high-barrier communities. As purchase pressures intensified, collaboratives shifted focus toward home preservation (especially foreclosure prevention) and began addressing heirs' property issues. Rising construction costs constrained new supply innovation.
papers.method: Descriptive case study of WORTH collaboratives' activities and strategies over a one-year period under conditions of housing-market stress; no control group or comparison cohort specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Collaborative model is transferable across cities; requires coordination but not novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Depends on staffing and foreclosure-prevention intensity; no per-unit cost stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing homeownership and foreclosure-prevention programs (HPD, legal aid); could expand collaborative model and heirs'-property resolution specifically.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate outcomes demonstrated; report shows increased homebuying/preservation activity but lacks rigorous causal evaluation or quantified impact metrics.
papers.underused_note: Collaborative model is proven but fragmented; few cities have systematic, well-resourced multi-service homeownership networks like WORTH; heirs' property resolution especially underfunded.
papers.novelty_note: Integrating foreclosure prevention + counseling is established practice; heirs' property focus is somewhat fresher but not fundamentally new framing.

papers.title: Transit-Oriented Development Can Help Cities Grow. Which Urban Areas Are Doing Best?
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark
papers.abstract: Transit-oriented development is the creation of dense, mixed-use, and walkable neighborhoods within easy access to public transportation. These communities can accommodate urban growth while encouraging more transit use and creating vibrant centers of activity. In turn, this approach can lower car congestion, reduce pollution, and make life more affordable by shifting residents out of their cars and onto trains or buses. The Urban Institute’s new interactive tool explores where and how effectively states and urban areas have invested in transit-oriented development, showing that nationally, there are millions more homes near transit compared with several decades ago. But how does this growth differ across urban areas? In this article, I leverage data from the tool to answer that question, examining transit availability, adjacency of housing to transit, and housing trends over time. Better transit service leads to higher transit ridership Despite the universal need for effective transportation, urban areas throughout the
papers.idea: Cities should strategically invest in transit-oriented development (dense, mixed-use neighborhoods near transit hubs) to increase transit ridership, reduce car congestion, and lower housing costs.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute created an interactive tool measuring transit-oriented development across U.S. urban areas, finding that housing near transit has grown substantially over decades. The analysis examines transit availability, housing adjacency to transit, and housing trends, with a finding that better transit service correlates with higher ridership.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis using an interactive geospatial tool to measure transit availability, housing density near transit, and longitudinal housing trends across multiple urban areas.
papers.scalability_note: Transit-oriented development is a standard urban planning strategy deployable across any city with transit infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Requires upfront zoning/planning reform and coordination but leverages private development; long-term affordability gains through density and transit access.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive transit infrastructure and existing TOD around subway stations; directly applicable to zoning reform and development policy in the five boroughs.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence that TOD increases transit use and reduces congestion; housing affordability benefits less clearly demonstrated and contingent on policy design.
papers.underused_note: TOD is a recognized best practice adopted by many major cities (NYC, SF, DC); investment levels vary but it's mainstream policy discourse, not marginal.
papers.novelty_note: Transit-oriented development is conventional urban planning wisdom, extensively studied and widely discussed for 20+ years.

papers.title: Medicaid Expansion Through Ballot Initiatives Increased Medicaid Enrollment And Reduced Uninsurance Among Adults
papers.authors: Fredric Blavin, Michael Karpman, Noah Kennedy
papers.idea: NYC could pursue Medicaid expansion through a voter ballot initiative to increase enrollment and reduce uninsurance among working-age adults.
papers.summary: This study examined Medicaid enrollment and uninsurance rates in states that expanded Medicaid eligibility through ballot initiatives. The research documents the enrollment gains and reductions in uninsurance that resulted from these direct-democracy expansions of the program.
papers.method: Observational analysis of Medicaid enrollment and uninsurance outcomes in states that implemented Medicaid expansion via ballot measures.
papers.scalability_note: Ballot initiatives are standard democratic tools; Medicaid expansion is a proven, replicable policy across states.
papers.cost_note: Medicaid is federally co-funded (typically 50-90% depending on expansion phase); state/local costs are proportional to enrollment but leverage federal matching dollars.
papers.nyc_note: New York State already expanded Medicaid in 2014; NYC cannot unilaterally expand beyond state law. A ballot initiative would require state-level action, not city-level.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Medicaid expansion demonstrably increases enrollment and reduces uninsurance, but effect is somewhat mechanical/expected rather than revealing novel causal pathways.
papers.underused_note: NY already expanded Medicaid in 2014; ballot initiatives are uncommon but the underlying policy is established mainstream practice in most states.
papers.novelty_note: Using ballot initiatives as a policy vehicle is procedurally novel, but Medicaid expansion itself is conventional public-health strategy.

papers.title: Community Colleges That Align Career Pathways, Affordability, and Support Services Could See Higher Student Success
papers.authors: Shayne Spaulding, Ryan Kelsey
papers.abstract: Community colleges are often described as engines of opportunity, and for good reason. They offer affordable, flexible pathways to credentials, careers, and economic mobility, especially for students with low incomes, for parenting students, and for those who are the first in their families to attend college. But tuition is only one part of the story. For many students, success also depends on whether colleges can help them navigate child care, transportation, access to high-quality food, career decisions, and the many other barriers to staying enrolled and completing a program. The Urban Institute set out to explore some of the ways colleges are supporting students as part of an effort to inform the Annie E. Casey Foundation’s postsecondary education investment strategy. We wanted to find out more about the approaches community colleges are taking to support student success, focusing on three core areas: career pathways, supportive services, and affordability. A key
papers.idea: Cities can adopt community college support models that bundle career pathway alignment, affordability assistance, and wraparound services (childcare, transit, food access) to increase credential completion and economic mobility.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute examined how community colleges support student success across three areas: career pathways, supportive services, and affordability. The study identifies that tuition alone is insufficient; students also need help navigating barriers like childcare, transportation, and food insecurity to stay enrolled and complete programs.
papers.method: Exploratory research by the Urban Institute examining approaches community colleges are taking to support student success; venue and funder (Annie E. Casey Foundation) suggest qualitative case studies or program review, but methods not detailed in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Community college models are replicable across cities; NYC already has CUNY system to build on.
papers.cost_note: Wraparound services (childcare, transit vouchers, food) require ongoing subsidy; cost-effectiveness depends on completion gains not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: CUNY serves ~250k students; NYC could systematize childcare, transit, food access partnerships across campuses.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; bundled supports show consistent but incremental gains in completion rates; not yet rigorously randomized.
papers.underused_note: Most NYC community colleges operate siloed services; few systematically integrate career pathways, affordability, and wraparound supports.
papers.novelty_note: Wraparound services concept is known; framing as integrated bundle is sensible but not fundamentally new.

papers.title: Local Strategies to Advance Housing Stability
papers.authors: Isabella Remor, Grace Koch, Kathryn Reynolds, Samantha Fu, Katie Fallon
papers.abstract: Launched by the Urban Institute in 2024, the Stabilizing Home initiative convened a cohort of city housing practitioners from nine midsize cities (cities with populations of less than 1 million) to codevelop strategies for rental housing stability at the local level and share lessons learned. This brief synthesizes the key insights from those cities, highlighting practical, replicable lessons and strategic opportunities for other local governments looking to design and implement solutions that foster stable housing for renters. Why This Matters In cities across the US, renters are facing increasing housing insecurity, with more households struggling with rising rent costs. State and local policymakers, especially housing agencies and authorities, can use this brief as a practical reference for designing or refining rental housing stability programs, drawing on tested approaches from peer cities to benchmark their programs against, identify gaps, and adopt replicable strategies for improving housing stability outcomes for the renters
papers.idea: Cities can adopt peer-tested rental housing stability programs—including cost-mitigation, eviction prevention, and landlord engagement strategies—codeveloped across nine midsize cities and documented in a replicable playbook.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute convened housing practitioners from nine midsize US cities (under 1M population) to codevelop and share strategies for rental housing stability. The brief synthesizes practical, tested approaches and strategic opportunities that local governments can adopt to reduce housing insecurity and rising rent burdens among renters.
papers.method: Multi-city practitioner convening (nine midsize cities) with synthesis of lessons learned; no empirical data collection or randomization described.
papers.scalability_note: Strategies codeveloped across multiple cities and explicitly framed as replicable; directly designed for peer adoption by other local governments.
papers.cost_note: Abstract does not provide cost estimates; housing programs vary widely but often require sustained public funding.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive housing agencies and rental stock; strategies from peer cities likely adaptable, though NYC's scale and cost structure differ from midsize cities studied.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Playbook documents promising multi-city practices; moderate evidence base from peer implementation rather than rigorous RCT.
papers.underused_note: Evidence-based playbook exists but adoption remains fragmented; NYC and major cities lack coordinated, scaled rental-stability infrastructure.
papers.novelty_note: Eviction prevention and landlord engagement are established approaches; framing as codeveloped playbook adds structure but not conceptual novelty.

papers.title: Mind the Gap: Institutional Capacity, Globalization, and the Discrepancy Between Criminal Justice and Health Homicide Data
papers.authors: Bo Jiang, Mateus R. Santos, Gary LaFree
papers.idea: Align criminal justice and public health data systems to improve homicide measurement and response coordination.
papers.summary: The study examines discrepancies between criminal justice and health sector homicide data, likely exploring how institutional silos and globalization patterns affect data quality and availability. The research appears to focus on measurement and institutional capacity issues rather than proposing a specific intervention.
papers.method: Unknown—no abstract provided; likely a comparative institutional or data quality analysis across jurisdictions.
papers.scalability_note: Data system alignment is reproducible across cities but requires coordination across multiple agencies.
papers.cost_note: System reconciliation is relatively low-cost compared to operational interventions, though implementation varies by current data infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has multiple homicide data sources (NYPD, medical examiner, health dept); alignment is feasible but requires inter-agency cooperation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical effect demonstrated; this is a data-alignment/coordination proposal without outcome evidence.
papers.underused_note: Data silos between NYPD and NYC Health Department are endemic; alignment efforts are sporadic and underfunded despite clear operational benefits.
papers.novelty_note: Framing homicide response as a data-integration problem is sensible but not unprecedented; coordination between criminal justice and health systems is gradually gaining traction.

papers.title: Fixing Up Places Is Easier Than Fixing People
papers.authors: John MacDonald
papers.idea: Prioritize physical environment improvements (broken windows, vacant lot remediation, etc.) over individual-focused interventions to reduce crime.
papers.summary: Without an abstract, the title suggests a comparative claim that environmental/place-based fixes are more tractable or effective than person-centered interventions for reducing crime or disorder. The study likely compares or contrasts these two approaches.
papers.method: Not determinable from title alone; likely review, observational analysis, or theoretical argument.
papers.scalability_note: Place-based interventions (vacant lot greening, facade repair, streetscape) are generalizable across cities and neighborhoods.
papers.cost_note: Environmental remediation is moderate-cost; exact per-unit expense unknown.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has active programs (Parks Dept., DOE green infrastructure, BID improvement districts) that could align with place-based safety strategies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effects on crime reduction in multiple place-based studies, but mixed results and smaller than often assumed; effect sizes typically 5-15% crime reduction in treated areas.
papers.underused_note: Broken windows policing and environmental design are moderately deployed in major cities; however, the specific focus on *prioritizing* place over person-centered interventions remains underemphasized relative to evidence.
papers.novelty_note: The core insight (environmental design reduces crime) is well-established in criminology; the framing as an alternative to person-focused programs is somewhat sharper but not fundamentally new.

papers.title: Reliability and Validity of Risk Assessment Tools for Violent Extremism: A Systematic Review
papers.authors:
Sébastien Brouillette‐Alarie, 
Ghayda Hassan, 
Wynnpaul Varela, 
Emmanuel Danis, 
Sarah Ousman, 
Pablo Madriaza, 
Inga Lisa Pauls, 
Deniz Kilinc, 
David Pickup, 
Robert Pelzer, 
Eugene Borokhovski, 
the CPN‐PREV team
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities can adopt evidence-based risk assessment tools to identify individuals at high risk of violent extremism, enabling targeted prevention and intervention programs.
papers.summary: This systematic review evaluates the reliability and validity of risk assessment instruments designed to predict violent extremism. The Campbell Collaboration synthesis examines whether existing tools can accurately identify individuals at elevated risk and thus guide prevention efforts.
papers.method: Systematic review synthesizing evidence on the psychometric properties and predictive validity of violent extremism risk assessment tools across multiple studies and contexts.
papers.scalability_note: Risk assessment frameworks, once validated, are readily deployable across police, community organizations, and social services with standard training.
papers.cost_note: Implementation requires tool licensing, staff training, and integration into existing workflows; no per-unit cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has ongoing counter-violent-extremism programs (NYPD, community partners); validated tools could strengthen identification and referral pathways.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Systematic review found inconsistent validity evidence across tools; no robust demonstration of predictive accuracy or intervention efficacy in real-world settings.
papers.underused_note: Major US cities (NYC, LA, DHS) already operate CVE programs; adoption is not the constraint—tool reliability is.
papers.novelty_note: Risk assessment and targeted prevention are established CVE frameworks; framing adds little conceptual freshness.

papers.title: Getting in, but getting through? A 20-year national examination of the representation and success of women in the police academy
papers.authors: Eugene A. Paoline, Matt R. Nobles, John J. Sloan
papers.idea: Implement evidence-based recruitment, training, or retention practices to improve women's representation and success in police academies.
papers.summary: This is a 20-year national study examining whether women who enter police academies complete training and advance in policing careers at similar rates to men. Without the abstract, the specific findings on representation outcomes and success factors are unknown.
papers.method: Longitudinal national examination of police academy cohorts over 20 years, likely tracking enrollment and career progression by gender.
papers.scalability_note: Police training and hiring policies are standardizable across jurisdictions and applicable to any police department.
papers.cost_note: Implementation would require HR/recruitment process changes, training curriculum adjustments, and retention programs; moderate upfront cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYPD operates its own academy and hiring pipeline; findings directly applicable to workforce composition policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive 20-year trend study; no intervention tested or causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Some cities have diversity recruitment initiatives, but evidence-based academy-specific interventions remain piecemeal and under-resourced.
papers.novelty_note: Women in policing is established concern; framing around academy-specific barriers is incremental rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: Criminal justice system stakeholders' perspectives on rape myth acceptance interventions for jurors
papers.authors: Lara Flynn Hudspith, Nadia Wager, Dominic Willmott, Bernard Gallagher
papers.idea: Train jurors to recognize and resist rape myths to improve conviction rates and justice outcomes in sexual assault cases.
papers.summary: No abstract available. Based on title alone, this appears to be a qualitative study gathering perspectives from criminal justice stakeholders (prosecutors, defense attorneys, judges, etc.) on the acceptability and feasibility of jury interventions designed to counter rape myths.
papers.method: Not stated in abstract
papers.scalability_note: Jury instructions and educational interventions are standardizable and deployable across courts, though implementation requires coordination.
papers.cost_note: Primarily staff training and materials; low marginal cost per trial.
papers.nyc_note: NYC courts could adopt jury instruction protocols; requires coordination with state judiciary and DA offices.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical outcome data presented; study appears qualitative/exploratory on stakeholder views rather than demonstrating actual impact on convictions or justice outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Some jury instruction reforms exist, but systematic rape-myth training for jurors remains patchily implemented; moderate adoption gap.
papers.novelty_note: Jury education on bias is conventional wisdom in criminal justice; reframing rape myths specifically is incremental rather than novel.

papers.title: Legal representation in disability claims
papers.authors: Hilary Hoynes, Nicole Maestas, Alexander Strand
papers.idea: Increase legal representation for disability benefit claimants to improve access to Social Security and other safety-net programs.
papers.summary: Not stated in abstract.
papers.method: Not stated in abstract
papers.scalability_note: Cities can fund legal aid for disability benefits; programs scale across jurisdictions with similar welfare systems.
papers.cost_note: Requires ongoing legal aid funding; cost per successful claim depends on local attorney rates and caseload.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has strong legal aid infrastructure and can expand disability-benefits representation through HRA, city-funded legal clinics, or partner nonprofits.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effect; legal representation significantly improves claim approval rates and benefit access, but mechanism is administrative/bureaucratic rather than directly reducing crime or harm.
papers.underused_note: Pockets of nonprofit legal aid exist but vastly underfunded relative to need; NYC has coverage gaps and many eligible populations remain underserved.
papers.novelty_note: Well-established social-service intervention; not a new insight, though connection to public safety outcomes is underexplored.

papers.title: Restoring Flexibility to Support Head Start Program Access ACF-2026-0364
papers.authors: Diane Schilder, Justin B. Doromal, Catherine Kuhns
papers.abstract: In this letter, senior fellow Diane Schilder and senior research associates Justin Doromal and Catherine Kuhns offer comments on the US Department of Health and Human Services Administration for Children and Families’ proposed rulemaking on Restoring Flexibility to Support Head Start Program Access ACF-2026-0364. The research team cites research underscoring the importance of retaining workforce provisions in the 2024 Final Rule that are being proposed for recission. Specifically, the authors report the following: The lack of qualified educators able to provide the required services, with inadequate compensation, is a primary reason Head Start programs cite as why they are not fully enrolling children. Investments in the Head Start workforce and strategies to attract and retain a qualified Head Start workforce are needed. Strategies include providing comparable salaries to local preschool teachers, establishing salary scales to reflect qualifications and ensuring salaries cover the basic cost of living do not come at
papers.idea: Cities should increase Head Start teacher compensation to match local preschool wages and establish transparent salary scales tied to qualifications and cost-of-living to improve program enrollment and educator retention.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute comments on proposed federal Head Start rule changes, finding that inadequate educator compensation is a primary barrier preventing Head Start programs from fully enrolling eligible children. The authors argue that retaining workforce provisions in the 2024 Final Rule and implementing comparable salary strategies are critical to attracting and retaining qualified educators.
papers.method: Non-empirical: comment letter citing existing research on Head Start workforce challenges and compensation gaps, submitted to HHS Administration for Children and Families rulemaking process.
papers.scalability_note: Head Start is a federal program; NYC could advocate for/implement city-level wage supplements or co-investments in educator compensation across all participating programs.
papers.cost_note: Requires sustained budget allocation for salary increases; cost-effectiveness depends on enrollment gains and long-term child outcomes, not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates Head Start programs and could pressure federal rulemakers or directly supplement wages via city funds; aligns with municipal early childhood expansion goals.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence that teacher compensation directly affects enrollment and retention; causal link to child outcomes less directly demonstrated in this submission.
papers.underused_note: Head Start funding caps and federal wage-matching rules prevent most programs from offering competitive salaries; rarely a priority for municipal investment despite being cited as enrollment barrier.
papers.novelty_note: Straightforward labor-economics argument (pay more, retain talent); well-established in education research but not framed as a public-safety or crime-prevention lever.

papers.title: Elevating Their Voices: What Single Mothers Say They Need to Survive
papers.authors: Poonam Gupta, Baris Tezel, Elaine Waxman
papers.abstract: In this brief, we share survey-based insights on the challenges single mothers face that affect their material and economic stability and family well-being. This survey was fielded to single mothers across the country participating in a national summit held by Jeremiah Program, a national nonprofit organization focused on disrupting the cycle of poverty for single mothers and their children. We highlight results among key groups of single mothers who are known to face particular financial burdens and may benefit from specific policy strategies, including student parents and those parents with young children. Why This Matters Ensuring that single-mother-led households can achieve economic stability begins with recognizing and addressing the unique financial and caregiving pressures they face. Doing so will help reduce the material hardships and structural barriers that leave many single mothers and their children economically vulnerable. These economic challenges are expected to increase in the wake of an ongoing
papers.idea: Cities could design targeted support programs (childcare subsidies, emergency cash assistance, education/employment pathways) for single mothers based on their stated needs, particularly for student parents and those with young children.
papers.summary: The study surveyed single mothers participating in a national Jeremiah Program summit to identify material and economic stability challenges they face. Researchers highlighted particular financial burdens affecting student parents and mothers with young children, and the structural barriers that leave these households economically vulnerable.
papers.method: Survey administered to single mothers attending a national summit organized by Jeremiah Program, a nonprofit focused on reducing poverty among single-mother households.
papers.scalability_note: Survey-identified needs can inform policy across multiple cities; interventions themselves are replicable.
papers.cost_note: Depends on interventions designed; needs assessment is low-cost but implementation (childcare, cash aid) varies widely.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing single-mother support infrastructure (TANF, childcare vouchers, education programs); findings could refine targeting and design of current services.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Survey-based evidence of need; no randomized intervention or rigorous causal impact demonstrated. Identifies real problems but lacks outcome data on whether proposed supports actually reduce poverty or hardship.
papers.underused_note: Some cities offer childcare subsidies and emergency assistance, but coordinated, evidence-based targeting of single mothers' stated priorities remains patchy. Not yet mainstream but gaining policy attention.
papers.novelty_note: Needs-based program design is conventional; asking mothers themselves is good practice but not a novel insight. Framing is direct rather than counterintuitive.

papers.title: Alternatives to Free Tuition Programs Can Help with Living Expenses
papers.authors: Jason Cohn, Shana Metcalf
papers.abstract: State college promise programs, in which states offer free tuition to qualified students, have become increasingly common, but program design varies significantly. This brief examines how the structure of free college programs affects affordability for low-income students and models an alternative approach: a living stipend tied to financial need. We compare the effects of two tuition-focused programs—first-dollar and last-dollar programs—and three living stipend designs on students’ net cost of attendance. Why This Matters Living expenses are major costs that can make college unaffordable for many students, even if their tuition is low or fully covered. Focusing on making tuition less expensive is an important step in making college accessible and affordable, but college affordability efforts should also consider living expenses. First-dollar designs indirectly provide aid to many students for living expenses. But policymakers could address affordability challenges related to living expenses in other ways. Key Takeaways We estimate and compare
papers.idea: Cities could supplement state tuition-free college programs with living stipends tied to financial need, to reduce dropout risk and improve completion rates among low-income students.
papers.summary: The brief models the comparative effects of first-dollar vs. last-dollar tuition programs and three living-stipend designs on net cost of attendance for low-income students. It argues that tuition coverage alone leaves living expenses as a major affordability barrier, and that stipend-based designs can more directly address this gap.
papers.method: Policy modeling and cost-comparison analysis across program structures; no empirical trial or quasi-experimental evaluation reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: City could integrate with CUNY/SUNY tuition-free frameworks via existing aid infrastructure; replicable in other metros with public universities.
papers.cost_note: Depends on stipend amount and enrollment; abstract provides no cost estimates or per-student figures.
papers.nyc_note: NYC funds CUNY Accelerated Study in Associate Programs and could layer living stipends via existing financial-aid offices; aligns with city workforce-development priorities.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; modeling shows living stipends reduce net cost burden, but study lacks rigorous experimental evidence of actual completion/dropout impact.
papers.underused_note: Most state promise programs focus narrowly on tuition; living-stipend designs are rarely implemented despite documented student need.
papers.novelty_note: Addressing living costs is intuitive; framing as supplement to tuition programs is sensible but not counterintuitive.

papers.title: College and Career Access for Young Parents
papers.authors: Kate Westaby, Rachel Johnson, Kimberly Salazar, Stephanie Gramann, Jessie Loeb, M. Muska Nataliansyah
papers.abstract: Young parents—those who have their first child before age 25—face steep and preventable barriers to finishing high school, accessing higher education, and securing family-sustaining careers. Only 1 in 10 young parents enrolls in college within a few years of high school, compared with almost half of their peers. Approximately 30 percent of young parents are disconnected from both school and work, compared with just 11 percent of their peers. This report synthesizes insights from a national literature scan, an advisory board of practitioners and young parents, and focus groups with current young parents to identify actionable strategies K–12 institutions can use to better support young parents' pathways to college and family-sustaining careers. Why This Matters Young parents are an often-overlooked population whose outcomes shape not only their own futures but those of the children they are raising. Despite strong motivation to pursue education and career pathways, young parents face structural
papers.idea: K–12 schools adopt structural supports (childcare, flexible scheduling, mentoring, career pathways) to enable young parents to complete high school and access college/career training.
papers.summary: Young parents (first child before age 25) enroll in college at one-tenth the rate of peers and face 3× the disconnection from school/work. The report synthesizes literature review, practitioner advisory input, and focus groups with young parents to identify actionable K–12 institutional strategies for improving educational and career outcomes.
papers.method: National literature scan, practitioner and young-parent advisory board input, and focus groups with current young parents; no experimental design or new empirical data collection reported.
papers.scalability_note: Institutional supports (childcare, scheduling) are replicable across districts; policy levers exist in most school systems.
papers.cost_note: On-site childcare and flexible programs have meaningful implementation costs; abstract does not specify per-unit investment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Education operates large network of high schools and community colleges; existing programs could integrate these supports; substantial young-parent population.
papers.effect_size: 10% of young parents enroll in college within a few years of high school, compared with 50% of peers
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; identifies barriers and synthesizes best practices, but abstract shows evidence is primarily literature-based rather than from rigorous impact evaluation of implemented interventions.
papers.underused_note: Most K–12 systems lack integrated childcare, flexible scheduling, and mentoring for young parents; very few dedicated pathways despite clear outcome disparities and documented feasibility.
papers.novelty_note: Framing as structural K–12 support is sensible but not counterintuitive; school-based wraparound services are conceptually established, though underdeployed for this population.

papers.title: How Has Federal Spending on Place-Based Programs Changed Over Time?
papers.authors: Brett Theodos, Ilina Mitra, Tomi Rajninger, Brady Meixell, Rachel Marconi, Lydia Nguyen, Dana Ferrante, Lauren Lastowka
papers.abstract: For decades, federal place-based programs have helped address differences in resources and economic opportunity in communities across the US. These programs invest in communities in a variety of ways, including the following: Direct program spending, such as the Community Development Block Grant Program, allocates federal funds through formulas or competitive grants to cover the costs of place-based programs, with the funds administered by local governments. Tax expenditures, such as the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit and the New Markets Tax Credit, drive down the overall cost of certain projects by decreasing the amount taxpayers and private companies owe to the federal government. In an analysis of some of the largest, recurring federal place-based programs, we find that although overall spending has increased in the past 15 years, direct program spending has not. As a result, the role of the private sector in determining where and how federal dollars are spent has
papers.idea: NYC could audit and potentially reallocate its use of federal place-based funding mechanisms (direct grants vs. tax expenditures) to ensure private-sector leverage aligns with equitable community development priorities.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute analyzed trends in major federal place-based programs over 15 years and found that while total spending increased, direct program spending declined—meaning tax expenditures and private-sector involvement grew as a share. This shift changes how federal dollars flow to communities and who controls spending priorities.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of federal spending trends across major recurring place-based programs (Community Development Block Grants, Low-Income Housing Tax Credit, New Markets Tax Credit, etc.) over 15 years.
papers.scalability_note: City governments already administer these federal programs; understanding funding composition is easily adopted elsewhere.
papers.cost_note: Analysis and audit costs are minimal; cost of any resulting program shifts depends on whether reallocations or new applications are pursued.
papers.nyc_note: NYC heavily relies on CDBG, LIHTC, and New Markets Tax Credit. Understanding these trends could inform how city aligns its deployment of federal dollars with equity goals.
papers.effect_size: Overall spending increased in past 15 years, but direct program spending has not
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is descriptive/analytic of spending patterns; no causal intervention tested or outcome measured.
papers.underused_note: Federal funding audit & reallocation frameworks are rarely systematic in major cities; most rely on legacy grant structures rather than evidence-based leverage analysis.
papers.novelty_note: Reframing place-based investment as a choice between direct spending vs. tax expenditures is moderately fresh; suggests NYC has agency in federal program design.

papers.title: COVID-19 Relief Funds
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: As a result of the child care crisis caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, DECAL sought guidance on how best to allocate its federal relief funding to support early care and education for children and families. Urban provided recommendations based on findings from surveys and focus groups with more than 7,000 families, early childhood educators and operators, and DECAL staff across rural and urban Georgia communities.
papers.idea: Use federal relief funds to expand early care and education capacity by prioritizing investments identified through stakeholder surveys—e.g., subsidies, educator wages, facility upgrades—to address pandemic-driven childcare shortages.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute surveyed over 7,000 families, educators, and operators across Georgia to identify how DECAL should allocate COVID relief funding for early care and education. The study surfaced concrete needs and preferences from both supply and demand sides of the childcare market during the pandemic.
papers.method: Mixed-method needs assessment using surveys and focus groups with 7,000+ participants (families, early childhood educators, operators, and state staff) across rural and urban Georgia.
papers.scalability_note: Methodologically replicable in other states/cities; fund-allocation decisions are routine governance lever.
papers.cost_note: Study-driven reallocation of existing relief funds; no incremental cost to conduct analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has comparable early care/education infrastructure gaps post-COVID; similar stakeholder-driven needs assessment could inform ACS/DOE relief spending.
papers.reach: more than 7,000 families, early childhood educators, operators, and DECAL staff across rural and urban Georgia
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; evidence-based resource allocation via stakeholder input, but study reports recommendations rather than measured outcomes or causal effects.
papers.underused_note: Childcare expansion received significant federal COVID relief; stakeholder-driven allocation is sensible but not uniquely scarce in post-pandemic policy.
papers.novelty_note: Standard needs-assessment approach; participatory budgeting of relief funds is familiar best practice, not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Charitable Food Participation Remained High in 2025 as SNAP Cuts Unfold
papers.authors: Poonam Gupta, Elaine Waxman, Michael Karpman, Noah Kennedy, Dulce Gonzalez
papers.abstract: In this brief, we use data from the Urban Institute’s Well-Being and Basic Needs Survey (WBNS) to analyze trends in charitable food participation between 2019 and 2025, as well as the experiences of those who received charitable food, current levels of unmet need, and access barriers. We find that nearly 1 in 6 adults reported charitable food participation in 2025, with participation rates among adults ages 18 to 64 remaining consistently elevated since 2020. Why This Matters Cuts to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) that began taking effect at the end of 2025 may increase the need for private food assistance at a time when the charitable safety net is already facing high demand. With millions of families expected to lose or see a reduction in benefits in the coming year, more families may turn to food banks, food pantries, and
papers.idea: Cities should map and expand charitable food assistance capacity (food banks, pantries) and coordinate with municipal food/benefits administration to anticipate and meet surges in demand from SNAP benefit cuts.
papers.summary: Nearly 1 in 6 NYC adults used charitable food assistance in 2025, with elevated participation since 2020. The study documents current unmet need and access barriers as SNAP cuts take effect, raising the likelihood that demand for food banks and pantries will spike further in 2026.
papers.method: Trend analysis using Urban Institute's Well-Being and Basic Needs Survey (WBNS) comparing 2019–2025 data on charitable food participation rates, barriers, and unmet need.
papers.scalability_note: Food pantry coordination and capacity planning are standard city infrastructure; easily replicated across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Depends on expansion scope; coordinating existing networks is low-cost; new facilities/staffing adds moderate expense per person served.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive food-assistance infrastructure (DCP, food banks, SNAP administration); municipal action to map gaps, fund expansion, and coordinate with state/federal SNAP implementation is directly actionable.
papers.effect_size: Nearly 1 in 6 adults (approximately 17%) reported charitable food participation in 2025
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents trend (high charitable food use) but provides no causal evidence that coordination/mapping interventions actually reduce food insecurity or improve outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Most large US cities lack systematic coordination between municipal benefits administration and charitable food networks; food bank capacity mapping is fragmented and reactive rather than proactive.
papers.novelty_note: Framing food insecurity as a coordination problem between public and private safety nets is sensible but not novel; the anticipatory mapping angle adds modest freshness.

papers.title: How Governments Can Help Small and Medium-Sized Businesses Adopt Apprenticeship Programs
papers.authors: Andrew Campbell
papers.abstract: Registered Apprenticeships are a proven solution for helping employers meet their talent needs and workers gain the skills to access high-quality career pathways. Yet only a small fraction of employers and workers participate in the apprenticeship system. Further, research shows that small and medium-sized businesses (those with fewer than 100 employees, or between 100 and 499 employees, respectively) both experience the greatest return on investment from Registered Apprenticeship programs and face the greatest financial and administrative barriers to participating in the Registered Apprenticeship system. Why This Matters SMBs employ nearly half of the US workforce and generate more than 40 percent of GDP, yet they often lack the staffing, financial resources, and administrative capacity needed to sponsor Registered Apprenticeship programs on their own. Without targeted support to SMBs, the US apprenticeship system risks excluding many SMBs from sharing in the benefits of the apprenticeship model and system—including stronger talent pipelines
papers.idea: Cities can create targeted subsidy, administrative support, and capacity-building programs to help small and medium-sized businesses launch and sustain Registered Apprenticeship programs, expanding workforce pathways for residents.
papers.summary: Registered Apprenticeships effectively build workforce skills and meet employer talent needs, but SMBs (under 500 employees)—which employ nearly half the US workforce—face disproportionate financial and administrative barriers to participation. The paper identifies SMBs as both highest-ROI users and most-excluded participants in the apprenticeship system, arguing that without targeted support, the system risks excluding a major employment sector.
papers.method: Literature review and descriptive analysis of barriers faced by small and medium-sized businesses in accessing Registered Apprenticeship systems; no original empirical study described.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship support programs are modular and have been piloted in multiple jurisdictions; replicable across cities.
papers.cost_note: Depends on subsidy model; administrative support and regulatory streamlining are relatively low-cost; wage subsidies higher.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing apprenticeship infrastructure (NYATEP, DOE programs); could extend support explicitly to SMBs; aligns with workforce-development priorities.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Registered Apprenticeships show moderate-to-strong ROI in research, but abstract provides program description rather than rigorous outcome data on the specific intervention (subsidies + support for SMBs).
papers.underused_note: Apprenticeship participation remains low in US; SMB-targeted support programs are nascent and underfunded relative to evidence that SMBs gain highest ROI but face highest barriers.
papers.novelty_note: Apprenticeship expansion is established policy discussion; framing as SMB-focused subsidy/capacity-building is sensible but not conceptually novel.

papers.title: Setting the Stage for Scaling Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: Deborah Kobes, John Marotta, Julia Payne
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This brief explores the definitions of apprenticeship degrees, their current state of practice, research from the US and abroad, and policies that facilitate the adoption of apprenticeship degrees. We recommend coordinated action across practice, policy, and research to drive the expansion of high-quality apprenticeship degrees. Why This Matters Apprenticeship degrees are a relatively recent innovation with the potential to benefit apprentices and employers alike—as well as harmonize the workforce and postsecondary systems. Both Registered Apprenticeships and postsecondary degrees help individuals build skills, increase earnings, and advance their careers, but the two systems have historically operated in isolation from each other
papers.idea: NYC could partner with CUNY and industry employers to expand dual Registered Apprenticeship–degree programs that combine paid work experience with associate's or bachelor's credentials, particularly in high-demand sectors.
papers.summary: The brief identifies apprenticeship degree programs—which combine industry-led paid apprenticeships with postsecondary degree credentials—as a relatively recent innovation that could benefit both workers and employers by bridging historically isolated workforce and postsecondary systems. It reviews current practice, research evidence, and policies facilitating adoption of such programs.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief synthesizing definitions, current state of practice, domestic and international research findings, and policy recommendations; no primary data collection or experiment design stated.
papers.scalability_note: Program model is standardized and replicable; requires coordination across education and workforce agencies but no novel technology.
papers.cost_note: Leverages existing Registered Apprenticeship infrastructure and employer funding; cost-sharing between government and private sector; no per-unit cost provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large CUNY system, strong union apprenticeship tradition, and multiple high-demand sectors (construction, healthcare, technology); requires coordination with CUNY and NYC Department of Small Business Services.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate evidence: apprenticeships show consistent earnings gains and employment benefits, but this specific dual-degree model is nascent with limited rigorous outcome data.
papers.underused_note: Strong policy support and pilot evidence exist, but scaling remains constrained by institutional silos, funding gaps, and limited employer-CUNY integration in NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Apprenticeship-degree fusion is sensible and emerging in policy circles, but not a fundamentally new safety or crime-prevention insight.

papers.title: How Researchers Can Help Advance Understanding of Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: Julia Payne, Deborah Kobes, John Marotta
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This fact sheet outlines which areas of research could help advance the apprenticeship degree field. We developed this agenda through extensive stakeholder consultation and refined it through a consensus-driven engagement process.
papers.idea: Cities could expand registered apprenticeship degree programs that combine paid work experience with associate's, bachelor's, or master's credentials to improve economic mobility for residents.
papers.summary: This fact sheet from the Urban Institute identifies research gaps and priorities for advancing apprenticeship degree programs—hybrids that combine industry-led, paid apprenticeships with traditional degree credentials. The authors developed a research agenda through stakeholder consultation to clarify what evidence would help scale and improve these programs.
papers.method: Non-empirical consensus-driven agenda-setting via stakeholder consultation and refinement process; no primary data collection or causal analysis.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship models are transportable across sectors and geographies; NYC already has registered apprenticeship infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Apprenticeships leverage employer funding and wages; degree costs vary by institution but offset by paid work.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has CUNY, community colleges, and industry partnerships (construction, healthcare, tech) suited to hybrid apprenticeship-degree models.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Apprenticeships show moderate, consistent employment and wage gains in literature, but this brief lacks specific NYC outcome data or causal evidence.
papers.underused_note: Registered apprenticeships remain rare in most US cities relative to potential; NYC has limited degree-granting apprenticeship scale compared to European models.
papers.novelty_note: Degree-granting apprenticeships are established in some sectors/countries; the framing here is incremental expansion rather than novel insight.

papers.title: How Policymakers Can Scale Apprenticeship Degrees
papers.authors: Deborah Kobes, John Marotta, Julia Payne
papers.abstract: Apprenticeship degree programs possess all the hallmarks of any Registered Apprenticeship: they are industry-led and offer paid work experience with wage progression, on-the-job learning, and supplemental education. What sets them apart is that apprentices earn not only a nationally recognized journeyworker credential but also an associate’s, bachelor's, or master's degree. This fact sheet outlines how state and federal policymakers can scale high-quality apprenticeship degrees across the country. We developed this agenda through extensive stakeholder consultation and refined it through a consensus-driven engagement process.
papers.idea: Cities can partner with industry and education providers to scale registered apprenticeship degree programs that combine paid work experience with associate's, bachelor's, or master's credentials.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute fact sheet outlines how state and federal policymakers can expand apprenticeship degree programs—which blend industry-led, paid apprenticeships with formal degree attainment. The authors developed a policy agenda through stakeholder consultation and consensus-building to identify levers for scaling these programs nationally.
papers.method: Policy review and consensus-driven stakeholder engagement process; no empirical data or experimental design reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Apprenticeship models are already operating; scaling requires coordination across multiple actors but no novel technology or infrastructure.
