papers.method: Empirical analysis of transaction-tax data from Los Angeles, examining behavioral responses and fiscal outcomes of the mansion tax.
papers.scalability_note: Mansion taxes are straightforward policy levers; LA findings could directly inform NYC and other high-value housing markets.
papers.cost_note: No new spending required; policy lever is administrative (tax rate/threshold adjustment).
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has transfer taxes and could directly adopt or refine mansion-tax design based on LA evidence; highly relevant to housing finance and city revenue.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study analyzes fiscal policy mechanics, not public-safety or civic-life outcomes; no demonstrated effect on safety, crime, or quality-of-life metrics.
papers.underused_note: Tax policy analysis is mainstream; NYC already extensively debates and implements transfer taxes; not an underdeployed intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Standard fiscal-impact evaluation of property taxation; conventional policy analysis without new conceptual framing for civic improvement.

papers.title: JUE insight: City size, monopsony, and the employment effects of minimum wages
papers.authors: Priyaranjan Jha, Jyotsana Kala, David Neumark, Antonio Rodriguez-Lopez
papers.idea: Cities could tailor minimum wage policy to account for local labor-market concentration (monopsony power) and city size, potentially raising wages more effectively in concentrated markets without reducing employment.
papers.summary: This paper examines how minimum wage effects on employment vary by city size and degree of employer concentration in local labor markets. The research suggests that in smaller cities or markets with fewer employers (monopsony power), minimum wages may have different employment impacts than in competitive larger cities, offering a nuanced view of wage-policy design.
papers.method: Empirical analysis examining minimum wage employment effects across cities of varying sizes and labor-market concentration; specific research design and dataset not described in available abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Minimum wage policy is readily scalable and is already adopted across jurisdictions; geographic variation in application is feasible.
papers.cost_note: No direct program cost; policy lever only (wage floor adjustment). Cost borne by employers, benefit distributed to low-wage workers.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has its own minimum wage; findings could inform future adjustments or sub-regional variation, but city has limited independent wage-setting authority for many sectors.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical insight without empirical demonstration of NYC employment/wage outcomes
papers.underused_note: Monopsony-adjusted wage policy is intellectually recognized but rarely implemented with local labor-market specificity in major cities
papers.novelty_note: Reframes minimum wage from one-size-fits-all to labor-market-concentration-responsive tool; economically sophisticated framing

papers.title: Understanding app-diary study participants' interaction with personalized feedback using in-app paradata
papers.abstract: In app-based data collection, researchers increasingly provide participants with personalized feedback based on participants’ data and study behavior. Personalized feedback is hypothesized to have positive effects on participant engagement and data quality. However, empirical evidence on these positive effects and research on the mechanisms on the effects that feedback has on participation remains scarce. We investigate what characteristics participants have who interact with their feedback and whether feedback affects data quality in an app-based household budget diary study in the general population of the Netherlands. The timing of providing personalized feedback was varied by random assignment to two experimental conditions: half of the participants received their personalized feedback instantly, and another half received their personalized feedback after 14 days, at the end of the study. We used mobile app paradata to study actual feedback-viewing behavior. We find that most but not all participants view their personalized feedback and older participants are more likely to visit their feedback more often and for longer periods. We find no difference in data quality between participants that received instant feedback or delayed feedback, but using paradata, we find a positive relationship between data quality and the duration of viewing the feedback by participants. This finding suggests that the collection of in-app paradata can provide more nuance about the effect of personalized feedback on data quality, and that paradata can inform researchers about whether the costly efforts of implementing personalized feedback are worth undertaking.
papers.idea: This is a methodology paper on how to design app-based surveys; it does not propose a civic intervention.
papers.summary: The study tested whether timing of personalized feedback (instant vs. delayed) affects participant engagement and data quality in a household budget diary app study in the Netherlands. Researchers found that older participants viewed feedback more often, and that longer feedback-viewing duration correlated with higher data quality, but timing of feedback delivery had no effect on data quality.
papers.effect_size: No difference in data quality between instant and delayed feedback conditions; positive correlation between feedback-viewing duration and data quality (magnitude not quantified).
papers.reach: General population of the Netherlands (sample size not stated in abstract)
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial of feedback timing in an app-based household budget diary study, using in-app paradata to measure feedback-viewing behavior and data quality.
papers.scalability_note: Applies only to survey/app design; would require retrofitting existing civic data-collection apps.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost to add paradata logging to existing apps.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could apply to budget/health/service survey apps, but this is a minor design optimization, not a civic program.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodology paper with no civic intervention tested; findings are about survey participation mechanics, not public safety or city services.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is research infrastructure, not a deployable civic intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Uses paradata to study feedback mechanisms in surveys; methodologically sound but incremental contribution to survey design literature.

papers.title: How Increasing Wages for Low-Paid Workers Supports Financial Stability and Community Well-Being
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.idea: Cities can adopt or strengthen minimum wage policies and wage-support programs for low-wage workers to improve financial stability and reduce poverty-related crime drivers.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute research examines how wage increases for low-paid workers contribute to financial stability and broader community well-being. The study likely synthesizes evidence on links between income support, economic security, and positive outcomes in housing, health, and social cohesion.
papers.method: Appears to be a think-tank synthesis/analysis by the Urban Institute; specific empirical design, data sources, and sample not evident from title and abstract alone.
papers.scalability_note: Wage policies are broadly implementable across municipal and state jurisdictions; NYC has already set precedent with its own minimum wage.
papers.cost_note: Wage mandates have direct employer costs but generate tax revenue and reduce social-service demand; net cost depends on design and phase-in.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has a higher minimum wage; this research could inform expansion to other worker categories, wage-subsidy programs, or policy refinement.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Minimum wage effects on crime are modest and mixed; wage increases show stronger links to poverty reduction than direct crime reduction.
papers.underused_note: NYC already has one of the nation's highest minimum wages ($15/hr); wage policy is actively debated and implemented, not neglected.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional economic-security framing; wage policy as crime prevention is well-established in policy discourse.

papers.title: How FHLBanks Are Investing to Meet the Local Housing Needs and What More Is Needed
papers.authors: Jung Hyun Choi, Laurie Goodman, Daniel Pang, Jun Zhu
papers.abstract: The Federal Home Loan Bank (FHLBank) System supports housing and community development across the US. It provides stable, low-cost advances to member institutions, including banks, credit unions, insurance companies, and consumer financial development institutions, to support mortgage lending and investment in members’ local communities. This system includes 11 regional FHLBanks that operate programs that provide direct support for affordable housing and community development. By law, FHLBanks must contribute at least 10 percent of their prior-year earnings to affordable housing through the Affordable Housing Program (AHP). In recent years, FHLBank contributions to the AHP and voluntary initiatives have averaged more than 15 percent of net earnings, reflecting a notable increase in funding to address unmet local needs. Between 2015 and 2024, FHLBanks directed roughly $147 billion toward housing and community development activities. Here, we highlight three trends in FHLBanks’ investments in two of their five mission activities between 2015 and 2024
papers.idea: Cities could advocate for or work with their regional FHLBank to increase capital deployment toward affordable housing and community development, particularly through the Affordable Housing Program (AHP) and voluntary initiatives.
papers.summary: The Federal Home Loan Bank System distributed roughly $147 billion toward housing and community development between 2015 and 2024 across 11 regional banks. FHLBanks now contribute more than 15% of net earnings to affordable housing (exceeding the 10% legal minimum), signaling growing investment in local housing needs. The paper documents trends in FHLBank mission activities and identifies gaps in funding relative to unmet local housing demand.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of FHLBank funding flows and program contributions from 2015–2024, highlighting three trends in two of five mission activities; appears to use administrative data from FHLBank reports.
papers.scalability_note: FHLBank system is nationwide; cities can leverage existing regional infrastructure without building new programs.
papers.cost_note: FHLBanks use retained earnings (not public tax dollars); capital is recycled through member institutions; very low marginal cost to cities.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is served by the FHLB of New York; the city and its housing authorities can directly engage with this regional bank to increase AHP allocations and community development lending.
papers.reach: 11 regional FHLBanks, ~$147 billion directed between 2015 and 2024
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive analysis of capital flows; no causal evaluation of outcomes or impact on housing affordability, displacement, or community metrics.
papers.underused_note: FHLBank programs exist but city-level advocacy and strategic coordination with these systems remains inconsistent; potential for deeper municipal engagement underexploited.
papers.novelty_note: FHLBank financing is established infrastructure; framing as a city advocacy lever is incremental, not a new conceptual approach to housing or public safety.

papers.title: To conform or not to conform: Experimental evidence on stereotypes and stereotype congruence in the context of hate crime against Asian women
papers.authors: Jason R. Silver, Luzi Shi
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives This study evaluates public responses to bias-motivated sexual assault against Asian women, focusing on the roles of pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women and victim stereotype congruence (including trait stereotype congruence and country of origin). Methods We use OLS and logistic regression to analyze results from a vignette experiment embedded in a national YouGov survey ( N = 1,300). Results Respondents were more likely to categorize offenses as hate crimes, less likely to blame victims, and more likely to punish perpetrators when victims were stereotype congruent. Pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women were associated with greater likelihood of hate crime categorization and victim blaming. Conclusions The public is less sympathetic toward Asian women victims who violate stereotypes, while pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women may also reduce concern for victims. Future efforts are needed to reduce the multi-dimensional stereotypes of Asian women.
papers.idea: Design public education campaigns to counter stereotypes of Asian women in order to increase hate-crime reporting and reduce victim-blaming in sexual assault cases.
papers.summary: The study used a vignette experiment (N=1,300) to measure how public attitudes toward hate crimes against Asian women vary based on victim stereotype congruence and pre-existing stereotypes. Respondents were more sympathetic and more likely to categorize offenses as hate crimes when victims conformed to stereotypes, but pre-existing stereotypes of Asian women overall reduced victim concern.
papers.method: Vignette experiment embedded in a national YouGov survey (N=1,300), analyzed via OLS and logistic regression.
papers.scalability_note: Public education campaigns are deployable at scale, but this study does not test any intervention's effectiveness.
papers.cost_note: Campaign design and execution would require sustained investment; cost per reached resident unclear.
papers.nyc_note: Study identifies a problem (stereotype-driven victim-blaming) but offers no tested solution; no actionable policy lever or program prototype is evaluated.
papers.effect_size: Stereotype-congruent victims more likely to be categorized as hate crimes, less likely to be blamed, and more likely to see perpetrators punished; pre-existing stereotypes associated with greater hate crime categorization and victim blaming
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Experimental evidence shows meaningful directional effects on hate-crime categorization and victim-blaming outcomes, but findings are correlational within a vignette design; real-world behavioral impact unknown.
papers.underused_note: Anti-stereotype public campaigns targeting Asian women and sexual assault are rare despite documented bias in hate-crime reporting and victim support; intersectional approach underfunded.
papers.novelty_note: Frames hate crime and sexual assault through stereotype-congruence lens rather than perpetrator deterrence or victim services alone; highlights how victim identity shapes public and institutional response.

papers.title: The Discrimination and Calibration Properties of the Violence Risk Appraisal Guide–Revised (VRAG-R) in a Sample of Canadian Prairie Forensic Inpatients
papers.authors: Piper Mackie, Mark E. Olver
papers.abstract: This study examined the discrimination and calibration properties of Violence Risk Appraisal Guide–Revised (VRAG-R) scores and risk bins in a sample of 124 forensic psychiatric inpatients found Not Criminally Responsible on Account of Mental Disorder (NCRMD) or Unfit to Stand Trial (UST), admitted under Saskatchewan Review Board jurisdiction during a 38-year catchment period. The VRAG-R was scored on all NCR and UST individuals from file, and recidivism data were obtained via official criminal records. The VRAG-R demonstrated strong discrimination properties for general and violent recidivism over 3-year, 5-year, and overall follow-ups. Calibration analyses revealed that VRAG-R recidivism norms tended to estimate higher rates of recidivism than actually observed. Overall, the results supported the use of the VRAG-R for psychological risk assessments with a population of persons found NCRMD; however, it should be a multi-measure process that involves other empirically validated tools.
papers.idea: Validate the VRAG-R risk assessment tool for use in NYC forensic psychiatric commitment decisions to improve predictive accuracy of violent recidivism.
papers.summary: This study tested the Violence Risk Appraisal Guide–Revised (VRAG-R) on 124 Canadian forensic psychiatric inpatients found not criminally responsible or unfit to stand trial over a 38-year period. The VRAG-R showed strong discrimination for predicting general and violent recidivism, though it tended to overestimate actual recidivism rates.
papers.method: Retrospective cohort study; VRAG-R scores extracted from files of NCRMD/UST individuals admitted under Saskatchewan Review Board jurisdiction; recidivism tracked via official criminal records over 3–5+ years.
papers.scalability_note: Validation study in one Canadian province; would require separate local validation before adoption elsewhere; applicable only to narrow forensic psychiatric population.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves staff training and integration into existing assessment protocols; no per-unit cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has forensic psychiatric systems but study is Canadian data from different legal/clinical context (NCRMD laws differ); would require independent validation and adaptation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Strong discrimination for violent recidivism prediction, but calibration issues (overestimation) limit clinical utility; moderate real-world improvement potential.
papers.underused_note: Validated risk assessment tools remain underdeployed in NYC forensic psychiatric commitment decisions; most decisions still rely on clinical judgment alone.
papers.novelty_note: Structured risk assessment in forensic psychiatry is established; applying VRAG-R to NYC context is incremental refinement, not conceptual innovation.

papers.title: Police serve more than residents: Examining police enforcement compared to photo radar cameras and residential population estimates
papers.authors: Daniel Reinhard, Scott W. Bowman
papers.idea: Compare police enforcement patterns to photo radar camera locations to identify whether police focus on areas with residents or other characteristics.
papers.summary: Without an abstract, the likely contribution is a descriptive comparison of police enforcement distribution versus automated camera enforcement and population density. The study appears to examine whether police resources align with residential population or diverge in systematic ways.
papers.method: Not determinable from title alone; likely comparative analysis of police enforcement, camera deployment, and population data across geographic units.
papers.scalability_note: If findings reveal misalignment between enforcement and population, reallocation of police resources is theoretically scalable, but depends on whether the study identifies an actionable correction mechanism.
papers.cost_note: Redeploying existing police resources has no marginal cost, but organizational friction may be high.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has extensive police and DOT data; photo enforcement is well-established; geographic reallocation of patrol is operationally feasible.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive/analytical study examining enforcement patterns rather than testing an intervention with measurable public-safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Comparative enforcement analysis is occasionally done but systematic deployment of findings to reallocate resources or reform targeting practices remains limited in most US cities.
papers.novelty_note: Uses photo radar as a baseline for objective comparison; framing enforcement equity through non-discretionary technology is a moderately fresh angle on resource allocation bias.

papers.title: Implementing Temporary Financial Assistance for Homelessness Prevention: a CFIR-Guided Study of Provider Perspectives
papers.authors: Micaela Mercado, Megan Emily Chavez, Chelsea Nolan, Lara Law, Sara Shuman, Laura Noll, Kristin Ferguson, Hannah McEntarffer, Raechel Livingston, Shana Ellis, Jessie Helmes, Klim Drobnyh, Kelsie King, Robert Wickham
papers.idea: Provide temporary financial assistance to households at risk of homelessness to prevent housing loss.
papers.summary: This study uses CFIR (Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research) to examine how service providers implement and perceive temporary financial assistance programs designed to prevent homelessness. No specific findings are provided in the abstract.
papers.method: Qualitative implementation science study using CFIR framework to gather provider perspectives on temporary financial assistance for homelessness prevention.
papers.scalability_note: Financial assistance programs are straightforward to scale; primary barriers are funding and administrative capacity rather than technical complexity.
papers.cost_note: Direct cost per person helped depends on grant size; typically modest relative to long-term homelessness costs, but abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already operates eviction prevention and emergency assistance programs (e.g., through HRA, Community Preservation Corporation); findings could inform expansion or refinement.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; financial assistance prevents housing loss in theory, but study is qualitative (provider perspectives) rather than outcome-based, limiting causal evidence strength.
papers.underused_note: Despite strong intuitive appeal and some pilot evidence, rapid rental assistance remains underfunded and unevenly deployed across NYC; most homeless services focus on shelter/rehousing rather than upstream prevention.
papers.novelty_note: Housing-first and homelessness-prevention via cash are well-established concepts; framing is conventional, though CFIR implementation lens is methodologically sound.

papers.title: Are the Poor More Exposed to Climate Hazards in Latin America?
papers.abstract: This paper addresses two main questions. First, what proportion of people are exposed to climate hazards in Latin America and the Caribbean, especially among the poor versus the nonpoor? Second, do certain areas—hotspots—have high rates of bothhazard exposure and poverty that require targeted policy? Using poverty maps and georeferenced climate hazard data, three innovations are introduced: five climate hazards are analyzed (droughts, hurricanes, heatwaves, floods, and landslides); official poverty data at administrative level 2 are used, instead of only administrative level 1; and an interpolation method estimates poverty-plus-exposure rates across countries with varying data sources. The estimates indicate that 36.9 percent of the population is exposed to at least one of the five climate hazards under consideration. Considering the population in poverty only, the percentage is higher, 44.6 percent, whereas the exposure rate for the nonpoor is 34.0 percent. Some areas experience high exposure to climate hazards and high poverty rates. These hotspots include about 10 percent of the region’s population. These areas are in the Brazilian northeast; the upper-Amazon region of Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil; the Chaco region of Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay; the islands of the Caribbean; the western coast of the Gulf of California; and the Yucatan Peninsula.
papers.idea: Use poverty-hazard mapping to identify and target climate resilience investments in neighborhoods with overlapping high poverty and high climate-hazard exposure.
papers.summary: The study maps exposure to five climate hazards (droughts, hurricanes, heatwaves, floods, landslides) across Latin America and the Caribbean, finding that 44.6% of the poor are exposed to at least one hazard versus 34% of the nonpoor. The analysis identifies ~10% of the region's population living in 'hotspots' with both high poverty and high hazard exposure, concentrated in specific geographic areas (Brazilian northeast, upper Amazon, Caribbean islands, etc.).
papers.effect_size: 44.6% of poor exposed to climate hazards vs. 34% of nonpoor; ~10% of regional population in high-poverty, high-hazard hotspots
papers.reach: Latin America and the Caribbean region (population not specified; ~650 million people estimated)
papers.method: Cross-country descriptive analysis combining poverty maps and georeferenced climate hazard data at administrative level 2, using interpolation to standardize across countries with varying data sources.
papers.scalability_note: Methodology could be replicated in other regions; requires good data infrastructure for poverty mapping and hazard georeferencing.
papers.cost_note: Data collection and mapping cost not stated; operational cost of targeted resilience programs would depend on interventions chosen.
papers.nyc_note: NYC faces minimal drought, hurricane, or landslide risk; study is region-specific to Latin America. Climate hazards in NYC differ (coastal flooding, heat islands, nor'easters). Mapping method could theoretically transfer but hazards and geography are distinct.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Clear empirical documentation of poverty-hazard overlap (44.6% vs 34.0% exposure rates) with identified hotspots, but study is observational/mapping rather than intervention-testing.
papers.underused_note: Poverty-hazard mapping methodology is underdeployed in US urban climate planning; NYC has fragmented climate and poverty data integration despite climate justice rhetoric.
papers.novelty_note: Combining poverty + multi-hazard exposure mapping is solid but not radical; the analytical rigor and cross-country standardization adds value; framing as actionable hotspot identification is pragmatic rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: Structures of Oppressive Attitudes among Criminal Justice Majors: A Cross-National Analysis
papers.authors: Sunhye Kang, Bitna Kim, Gibbeum Hong, Hyeonna Bak
papers.abstract: This cross-national study examined the structure of oppressive attitudes among criminal justice majors in the United States and South Korea. Using survey data from 697 students across four universities in each country, eight attitudinal domains—prison rape myths, male and female rape myths, anti-incarcerated persons, anti-LGBTQ+, anti-sex work, anti-racial diversity, and anti-women’s equity—were analyzed through multivariate and cluster techniques. Results revealed consistent gender disparities and culturally patterned configurations of bias. Female students in both countries predominantly formed minimal explicit oppression clusters, while male students displayed greater polarization, with substantial proportions in high explicit oppression profiles marked by broad prejudicial endorsement. Cross-nationally, selective and single-issue clusters reflected distinct cultural and institutional norms. These findings suggest oppressive attitudes co-occur in patterned, context-specific configurations rather than as isolated beliefs, offering comparative evidence on how explicitly expressed oppressive attitudes are structured among criminal justice students and informing bias-awareness efforts in criminal justice education.
papers.idea: Integrate bias-awareness curriculum into criminal justice degree programs to reduce prejudicial attitudes among future law enforcement and justice professionals.
papers.summary: This cross-national survey of 697 criminal justice students in the U.S. and South Korea found that oppressive attitudes (toward incarcerated persons, LGBTQ+ people, racial diversity, women's equity, and others) cluster in patterned, context-specific ways. Male students showed greater polarization and higher explicit oppression profiles; female students predominantly had minimal explicit oppression clusters. The study identified that these biases co-occur structurally rather than in isolation.
papers.reach: 697 criminal justice students across 4 universities in each of 2 countries
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey of criminal justice majors in the United States and South Korea, with multivariate and cluster analysis of eight attitudinal domains.
papers.scalability_note: Identifying attitude patterns is a first step; the abstract does not propose or test an intervention, so scalability of an actual program remains unclear.
papers.cost_note: Curriculum revision and bias-awareness training are moderately affordable; no cost data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt bias training in CUNY criminal justice programs and police academy curricula, but the study offers diagnosis, not a tested solution.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive study identifying attitude patterns; no intervention tested or outcome measured.
papers.underused_note: Bias training in CJ programs exists but remains patchy, underfunded, and rarely evidence-based; this insight on co-occurring attitude clusters could sharpen design.
papers.novelty_note: Framing bias as structured clusters rather than isolated beliefs is useful; cross-national comparison adds depth, though bias-curriculum idea itself is conventional.

papers.title: The Next Million Manufactured Homes Should Be Safer Than the Last
papers.authors: Andrew Rumbach
papers.abstract: As congressional lawmakers work to address the nation’s affordable housing crisis, manufactured housing is receiving bipartisan support at both the federal and state levels. Federal policymakers are looking to expand the supply of manufactured homes through the updated 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act that’s moving quickly through the House of Representatives. Meanwhile, several states have moved to reduce barriers to manufactured housing supply. Virginia recently enacted a law that requires local jurisdictions to allow manufactured homes anywhere site-built single-family homes are permitted, following similar laws passed in Kentucky, Maryland and Idaho. The logic is straightforward: Manufactured homes cost significantly less per square foot to build than site-built homes and already provide housing for more than 22 million Americans, most earning less than the median national income. Manufactured housing is only truly affordable if it can withstand the environment it’s placed in, however. In a recent Urban Institute report, my
papers.idea: Cities should adopt or strengthen building code standards and enforcement mechanisms for manufactured homes to ensure durability and safety in local climate conditions.
papers.summary: The abstract argues that manufactured homes are an important affordable housing solution (serving 22+ million Americans), with bipartisan federal policy momentum and state-level regulatory reforms to expand supply. However, the piece emphasizes that affordability is undermined if units cannot withstand local environmental conditions, suggesting a need for stronger durability/safety standards.
papers.method: The item cites a recent Urban Institute report but the abstract is incomplete; appears to be a policy brief or opinion piece rather than empirical research.
papers.scalability_note: Building code adoption and enforcement are standard municipal functions; can be adapted by any city.
papers.cost_note: Cost to municipalities is primarily regulatory/administrative; enforcement via existing building departments. Likely cost-effective if prevents costly repairs/replacement.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited manufactured-home stock due to high land costs and zoning; more relevant to outer boroughs or regions with existing manufactured communities. State-level policy (NYS Building Code) would be the key lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Building code enforcement improves housing durability and safety, but evidence is indirect rather than from direct RCTs on this specific intervention.
papers.underused_note: Manufactured housing represents 22M Americans but code standards and enforcement remain fragmented across jurisdictions with limited municipal investment despite rapid expansion policy.
papers.novelty_note: Building code strengthening is conventional best practice; framing as safety prerequisite to affordability expansion is sensible but not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Pathways to Homeownership and Wealth Building in AANHPI Communities
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander (AANHPI) households are among the fastest-growing populations in the US. But research and policy have long overlooked how access to homeownership—and the ability to build and sustain wealth over the life course—varies across diverse AANHPI households and places. This project brings together new national analyses, community-informed insights, and regional spotlights to examine homeownership dynamics, wealth-building trajectories, and housing stability risks across AANHPI communities. It represents one of the first comprehensive efforts to document how AANHPI households have pursued homeownership and wealth-building pathways over the past several decades and how these pathways differ across stage of life, ethnicity, and geography. These insights can help inform policies and practices to support first-generation homebuyers, strengthen intergenerational wealth-building opportunities, and improve housing stability across federal, state, and local contexts. Research and Insights Explore the resources below to learn about the barriers and opportunities for homeownership and wealth
papers.idea: Cities can design targeted homeownership and wealth-building programs for underserved AANHPI communities by addressing ethnicity- and life-stage-specific barriers identified in this research.
papers.summary: This Urban Institute project documents homeownership access and wealth-building trajectories across diverse AANHPI households over decades, examining how pathways vary by ethnicity, life stage, and geography. The research identifies barriers and opportunities to inform policies supporting first-generation homebuyers, intergenerational wealth transfer, and housing stability in AANHPI communities.
papers.method: National comparative analyses combined with community-informed insights and regional case studies examining historical homeownership dynamics and housing stability risks across AANHPI subgroups.
papers.scalability_note: Findings can inform replicable policy and program design across multiple cities and jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Depends on specific interventions adopted; community-based first-generation homebuyer programs typically require moderate upfront investment.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large, diverse AANHPI populations and existing housing/wealth programs (HPD, community development organizations) that could integrate ethnicity-specific barriers and pathways.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Research is descriptive/diagnostic rather than evaluative; no demonstrated causal effect of an intervention tested.
papers.underused_note: Housing policy exists but ethnicity-disaggregated, life-stage-specific targeting for AANHPI communities is underdeveloped relative to need.
papers.novelty_note: Disaggregated focus on AANHPI (vs. pan-Asian) and life-stage barriers is fresher than generic homeownership programs, though wealth-building framing is not new.

papers.title: Evidence on the Benefits of the District of Columbia's Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund for Child Care Affordability and DC's Growth
papers.authors: Erica Greenberg, Eve Mefferd
papers.abstract: Erica Greenberg and Eve Mefferd have prepared testimony for the District of Columbia’s Committee of the Whole, highlighting Urban Institute research on the benefits of the District’s early childhood educator compensation program. Their testimony explains how the program is more than just a budget line item: it works in concert with other public and private funding to stabilize and improve the supply of affordable quality child care in DC.
papers.idea: Establish a pay-equity fund for early childhood educators to increase affordable childcare supply, enabling parental workforce participation and economic mobility.
papers.summary: The District of Columbia's early childhood educator compensation program improves childcare affordability and supply by using public funding to stabilize educator pay. The program works alongside other funding sources to make quality childcare more accessible and sustainable for DC residents.
papers.method: Testimony prepared for DC Committee of the Whole based on Urban Institute research; no empirical study design specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Pay-equity models are replicable across cities; DC's program provides a direct template.
papers.cost_note: Requires significant public investment to fund educator compensation; specific per-unit costs not stated in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already funds childcare through ACS/NYCHA; educator pay equity aligns with existing policy goals and infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate effects on childcare affordability and supply demonstrated in DC context; parental workforce participation gains are meaningful but not transformative.
papers.underused_note: Few major US cities have implemented dedicated pay-equity funds for early childhood educators despite proven workforce stabilization and affordability benefits.
papers.novelty_note: Addressing childcare affordability via educator compensation is established thinking; framing as systemic pay-equity fund is moderately fresh but not fundamentally counterintuitive.

papers.title: Correctional Services Canada Recruits: Their Mental Health During Training at Occupational Entry and the Role of Trauma Exposures in Prevalence of Mental Disorders and Suicide Behaviors
papers.authors: Arthi Chinna Meyyappan, Rosemary Ricciardelli, Sahar Dorniani
papers.abstract: Correctional officers (COs) in Canada experience a high prevalence of mental disorders, suicidal behaviors, and trauma exposure. However, recruits entering the Correctional Service of Canada tend to show lower prevalence than the general population, likely influenced by mandatory pre-employment mental health screening. We report self-report survey data from 792 CO recruits between 2018 and 2024, assessing mental health, suicidal behaviors, and trauma exposure before training. Recruits appeared generally mentally healthy: 7.2% screened positive for any disorder (1.6% posttraumatic stress disorder, 1.6% major depressive disorder, 1.8% GAD), and suicidal behaviors were rare (1.0% ideation, 0.2% attempts). Although most recruits reported multiple potentially traumatic events, specific high-impact trauma types, rather than cumulative trauma, were more strongly associated with positive screens. Recruits from certain occupational backgrounds showed elevated risk. Findings suggest robust baseline mental health despite trauma exposure and highlight the need for longitudinal research to understand how trauma type and preservice experiences shape mental health over time.
papers.idea: Improve pre-employment mental health screening and longitudinal monitoring of correctional officers to identify trauma types that predict later mental health decline and suicide risk.
papers.summary: This observational study surveyed 792 Canadian correctional officer recruits and found very low baseline prevalence of mental disorders (7.2%) and suicidal behaviors (1.0% ideation), despite many reporting prior traumatic exposures. Specific high-impact trauma types, rather than cumulative trauma burden, were more predictive of mental health issues. The authors call for longitudinal research to understand how pre-service experiences shape mental health trajectories over a career.
papers.method: Self-report cross-sectional survey of 792 CO recruits entering the Correctional Service of Canada between 2018 and 2024, assessing mental health disorders, suicidal behaviors, and trauma exposure at baseline.
papers.scalability_note: Screening protocols are scalable, but findings are descriptive and lack evidence of what intervention actually improves outcomes.
papers.cost_note: Mental health screening is relatively low-cost, but no cost data provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Correction could adopt trauma-informed pre-employment screening and monitoring, though study is Canadian context.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Cross-sectional baseline data only; no intervention tested, no outcome measured, no causal effect demonstrated. Identifies correlations but cannot show impact on reducing suicide or mental health crises.
papers.underused_note: Pre-employment screening exists in many departments but longitudinal trauma-type monitoring is rarely implemented systematically; knowledge gap on which trauma types predict decline is substantial and actionable.
papers.novelty_note: Distinguishing high-impact trauma types over cumulative burden is moderately fresh; most screening focuses on symptom counts rather than trauma phenotypes. Application to correctional workforce health is underexplored in US.

papers.title: AI systems out-persuade expert humans
papers.authors: Kobi Hackenburg, Caroline Wagner, Luke Hewitt, Ben M. Tappin, Ed Saunders, Hannah Rose Kirk, Helen Margetts, Christopher Summerfield
papers.abstract: Many societal decisions are settled by contests of persuasion. Conversational AI is a powerful new entrant in these contests, but whether it can out-persuade skilled and highly incentivized humans has remained unclear. Here, in a series of four preregistered experiments (n = 18,978 conversations from 6,923 people), we pitted AI systems against a range of human persuaders, including laypeople, winners of a separately preregistered four-round online persuasion tournament, professional canvassers, and world championship debaters. We found that AI systems were reliably more persuasive than expert humans, even when expert humans chose their issues, researched in advance, underwent hours of live, structured practice, and were incentivized with £1,000 cash bonuses. In a follow-up study, AI's advantage persisted after experts received a coaching tool that let them practice against the AI that beat them, review their performance history, and see what AI would have said at key moments. We found converging evidence that AI's advantage stemmed from rapidly deploying larger quantities of information: after coaching, expert humans could tie an AI constrained to respond at human speeds and with human-length messages. In a final study, we show that AI's advantage extends to consequential real-world behavior: AI was nearly 3x more effective than professional canvassers from a UK fundraising firm at raising real-money donations to Save the Children. Together, these results establish that frontier AI systems out-persuade expert humans in conversation, with significant implications for political communication.
papers.idea: None identified—this is a foundational science paper on AI persuasion capacity, not an actionable city intervention.
papers.summary: Four preregistered experiments (n=18,978) found that AI conversational systems out-persuaded expert humans—including professional debaters, tournament winners, and canvassers—even when humans chose topics, had advance prep, and received cash incentives. AI's advantage persisted after expert coaching and was attributed to rapid deployment of larger information volumes; AI was also ~3x more effective than professional canvassers at fundraising.
papers.method: Four preregistered randomized experiments comparing AI persuasiveness against laypeople, expert persuaders, professional canvassers, and world championship debaters, plus a real-world fundraising follow-up with a UK nonprofit.
papers.scalability_note: Addresses a systemic risk (AI manipulation) rather than enabling a scalable city program; scaling would require citywide policy guardrails or messaging interventions, not straightforward replication.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit implementation cost given; study was experimental, not a deployed program.
papers.nyc_note: Identifies a risk (AI out-persuading humans in civic/political contexts) but offers no ready-made policy or program NYC could adopt; would require downstream work on guardrails, disclosure rules, or training responses.
papers.effect_size: AI systems ~3x more effective than professional canvassers at raising donations
papers.reach: 18,978 conversations from 6,923 people across four experiments
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Foundational science paper with no direct public-safety intervention tested; persuasion capacity is upstream of policy application.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention—purely research on AI capabilities, not a deployed city program.
papers.novelty_note: AI outperforming humans at persuasion is now expected; framing adds little new insight for civic problem-solving.

papers.title: The Impact of School Grants on Disadvantaged Students: Experimental Evidence from Romania
papers.abstract: This study analyzes the impact of the Romania Secondary Education Project, which was designed to improve student retention, graduation rates and pass rates on a national end-of-high-school exam for low-achievement high schools in Romania. The program was implemented in three waves, September 2017, September 2018, and September 2020, with eligible high schools randomly assigned to each. The study exploits this staggered implementation to measure the project’s causal impacts on students. The estimates indicate that the Romania Secondary Education Project had no significant impact on (i) student preferences for attending a program high school, (ii) student retention rates, (iii) high school graduation rates, (iv) enrollment in the post-high school baccalaureate exam, (v) baccalaureate exam pass rates, or (vi) baccalaureate exam scores. There was a small increase in girls’ passing rates (3 percentage points). However, there was little heterogeneity in the null effects by grant size, urban-rural status, student achievement levels, town income levels, and type of curriculum taught.
papers.idea: Provide targeted grants to low-performing high schools to improve student retention and exam pass rates.
papers.summary: This RCT evaluated a Romanian secondary education grant program across three cohorts (2017–2020). The program showed no statistically significant impact on student preferences, retention, graduation, baccalaureate enrollment, pass rates, or exam scores, with only a small 3-percentage-point gain for girls' pass rates.
papers.effect_size: No statistically significant effect on primary outcomes; 3 percentage point increase in girls' pass rates only
papers.method: Staggered randomized controlled trial in Romania with three waves of school assignment (September 2017, 2018, 2020); exploited differential timing to identify causal impacts.
papers.scalability_note: Null findings suggest limited replicability; unclear what lever would work in other contexts.
papers.cost_note: Cost not stated in abstract; grants were distributed but with negligible impact.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already deploys substantial school funding; this study offers no evidence that grant-based interventions improve outcomes, limiting actionability for local policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Null effects across nearly all outcomes; only marginal 3pp gain for girls' pass rates.
papers.underused_note: School grants are already common policy; this null result actually argues against further investment without redesign.
papers.novelty_note: Targeted school funding is conventional education policy; framing adds little new insight.

papers.title: Narratives That Limit the Possible: Interrupting Narrative Closure in Computing Practice
papers.authors: Samuel Mann, Ruth Myers, Dave Guruge, Lucky Hawkins, Kylie McKee, Rex Alexander, Jamie Vaughan, Tim Lynch, Danny Fridberg
papers.abstract: Computing's dominant concepts - innovation, efficiency, resilience, professionalism - often migrate from reflective ideals to instruments that limit behaviour, redirect responsibility, and foreclose critique. We call this drift weaponisation: the discursive repurposing of professional concepts so that they stabilise business-as-usual while making structural alternatives appear unreasonable, illegible, or out of scope. Using collective autoethnography across education, justice, public administration, research management, and computing, we identify recurring mechanisms (simplification, individualisation, binary framing, metric substitution, hero/resilience scripts, organised ignorance). From this synthesis we propose Reframing - peaceful, practice-ready shifts (e.g., From Simplified Slogans to Structural Literacy; From Performative Compliance to Meaningful Outcomes; From Coping to Justice) - each paired with a 'do-now' prompt. The levers restore complexity, surface assumptions, and redirect attention to structural conditions without requiring formal authority. We contribute: (1) a cross-field account of weaponisation as a patterned phenomenon; (2) a portable reframing toolkit grounded in narrative and systems thinking; and (3) implications for computing within limits, including day-to-day practice shifts toward care, sufficiency, and justice.
papers.idea: Reframe how city agencies communicate about public safety, justice, and resilience to surface structural conditions rather than reinforce status-quo narratives.
papers.summary: This paper identifies how professional concepts like 'innovation,' 'efficiency,' and 'resilience' migrate from reflective ideals to discourse that forecloses critique and legitimizes structural inaction. The authors propose a 'reframing toolkit' using narrative and systems thinking to interrupt this pattern across education, justice, and public administration.
papers.method: Collective autoethnography across five fields (education, justice, public administration, research management, computing); authors synthesize recurring mechanistic patterns and propose reframing prompts without formal empirical testing.
papers.scalability_note: Toolkit is portable but depends on adoption by individual practitioners; no implementation pathway or evidence of uptake provided.
papers.cost_note: Primarily training/reframing labor; minimal direct cost if embedded in existing staff practice.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant conceptually to justice and city services sectors, but abstract offers no concrete policy, program, or operational lever; no pilot data or implementation case study shown.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework with autoethnographic evidence; no quantified outcome data on safety/justice improvements from narrative reframing alone.
papers.underused_note: Narrative-intervention and structural-literacy approaches are rarely institutionalized in NYC agency communications or policy design; communications typically remain efficiency/innovation-focused.
papers.novelty_note: Fresh lens on how professional jargon ('resilience,' 'innovation') can obscure structural problems; reframing toolkit grounded in systems thinking is counterintuitive to standard public-safety messaging.

papers.title: LearnOpt: Recovering the Latent Cognitive Structure of Standardized Examinations via Knowledge Graphs and Constrained Optimization
papers.authors: Joy Bose, Om Thomas
papers.abstract: Standardized examinations are typically treated as uniform syllabus coverage problems. We argue they are better understood as adversarial systems with stable latent cognitive structures diverging systematically from official syllabi. We introduce LearnOpt, which recovers this structure from historical question papers and generates personalized, time-bounded study plans. Applied to nine years of NEET questions (2016-2024, n=1,496), LearnOpt builds an exam knowledge graph from LLM-tagged questions, extracts a five-category latent skill distribution, and formulates study planning as a knapsack-variant optimization over prerequisite-aware subgraphs with Bayesian Knowledge Tracing. Central finding: NEET's latent skill distribution is stable within a syllabus regime (consecutive-year KL divergence 0.004-0.032 for 2016-2021, non-significant under permutation testing) but shifts significantly with NCERT's 2023 syllabus rationalization: pooling 2016-2021 (n=1,072) vs 2023-2024 (n=392) gives KL=0.040 (p=0.0005), with Elimination/Negation questions rising from ~20-29% to ~31-35%. Latent structure, while not permanently stationary, is piecewise stable, with shifts detectable and attributable to curricular events. Within either regime, subject predicts skill profile more strongly than year. An optimization evaluation, using one real and two synthetic mastery profiles, shows the skill-weighted objective produces a modest but real reordering of recommended topics over a mastery-conditioned frequency baseline. Applying the pipeline to JEE Advanced reveals a profile dominated by Multi-concept Integration (80.9% vs. 33.3% for NEET), with a JEE-vs-NEET divergence (KL=0.505) exceeding NEET's largest cross-subject divergence: exam tier shapes latent cognitive structure more than subject, which shapes it more than time within a regime. Code, knowledge graph, and annotated dataset are released publicly.
papers.idea: NYC public schools could adopt LearnOpt's latent-skill-structure recovery method to diagnose gaps between official curricula and what high-stakes exams actually test, then redesign test-prep programs and resource allocation accordingly.
papers.summary: LearnOpt analyzes historical standardized exam questions using knowledge graphs and optimization to recover the true underlying cognitive skill distribution exams measure—which often diverges from official syllabi. Applied to 9 years of NEET (Indian medical entrance exam) data, the method found latent skill structure is piecewise stable within a syllabus regime but shifts detectably when curricula change, and discovered that exam tier and subject shape cognitive demand far more than year-to-year variation.
papers.method: Retrospective analysis of 1,496 NEET questions (2016–2024) tagged by LLM, built into a knowledge graph, with skill distributions extracted and stability tested via KL divergence and permutation testing; applied also to JEE Advanced for validation.
papers.scalability_note: Method is fully automated (LLM-based tagging, graph construction, optimization) and requires only historical exam data; easily deployable across multiple exams and school systems.
papers.cost_note: Minimal: LLM API calls, computation, and public-release data; negligible per-student cost once built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC uses Regents exams, SAT, and other standardized assessments; the pipeline could be applied to these. However, the method is designed for high-stakes entrance exams (NEET, JEE); applicability to K–12 or college-readiness exams depends on data availability and curriculum structure similarity.
papers.effect_size: NEET's latent skill distribution stable within syllabus regime (KL divergence 0.004-0.032) but shifts significantly with 2023 syllabus rationalization (KL=0.040, p=0.0005), with Elimination/Negation questions rising from ~20-29% to ~31-35%
papers.reach: 9 years of NEET questions (2016-2024, n=1,496 total; n=1,072 for 2016-2021 regime, n=392 for 2023-2024 regime)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Method demonstrates technical feasibility and modest reordering of study topics; no evidence of actual student learning gains, test-score improvement, or real-world implementation impact.
papers.underused_note: Curriculum alignment and test-prep optimization are already active areas in competitive exam systems; this is a refinement tool rather than a novel intervention gap.
papers.novelty_note: Framing exams as adversarial systems with latent structure diverging from syllabi is somewhat fresh; knowledge-graph + optimization pipeline is technically sophisticated but incremental in educational problem-solving.

papers.title: Expanding the Supply of Child Care Starts with Two Key Questions
papers.authors: Heather Sandstrom, Gina Adams
papers.abstract: The average cost of child care increased 23 percent from 2021 to 2025. As parents struggle to afford child care among other rising costs, they also can’t easily find it. Nearly half of children younger than 6 live in a licensed child care desert, where demand is much greater than supply. This unmet need for child care isn’t just a problem for families—it ripples through their communities and local economy. It affects parents’ ability to work or go to school, local employers’ ability to retain workers and maintain productivity, providers’ ability to pay their bills, and children’s ability to gain early learning skills to grow into healthy adults and workers. Though this child care shortage is well known and widespread, the factors driving it are complex and vary widely by community. Families often lack access to the care they want and need (PDF), whether it’s in a center, in a
papers.idea: Expand licensed child care supply by identifying and removing community-specific barriers to care provision and access.
papers.summary: The abstract documents a crisis: child care costs rose 23% from 2021–2025, nearly half of US children under 6 live in licensed care deserts, and supply cannot meet demand. This shortage affects parental work, school enrollment, employer retention, provider viability, and children's early development.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of child care market conditions and barriers; appears to frame two key diagnostic questions for policymakers rather than test an intervention.
papers.scalability_note: Child care policy is highly scalable and applies to most cities; but abstract does not propose a specific intervention, only framing questions.
papers.cost_note: Cost-effectiveness unknown; abstract cites 23% cost increases for families but does not quantify intervention costs or ROI.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing child care subsidy programs (ACS, DOE pre-K) and regulatory authority over licensing; could apply findings to expand supply, but abstract does not propose actionable NYC-specific levers.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate promise; identifies real barriers but abstract lacks empirical results on specific interventions tested.
papers.underused_note: Child care supply expansion is chronically underfunded and fragmented; barrier-removal approach is evidence-informed but not systematically deployed at scale in major US cities.
papers.novelty_note: Framing as barrier-removal is sensible but not novel; community-specific diagnostics are standard policy language without fresh strategic insight.

papers.title: How Do Households with Low and Moderate Incomes Build Wealth, and How Can Practitioners Improve These Pathways?
papers.authors: Brett Theodos, Brady Meixell
papers.abstract: Building even a small amount of wealth can reduce a household’s risk of economic hardship, create stability, and serve as a springboard to economic mobility. But many American households today feel that building wealth is out of their reach, particularly in the face of the broader affordability crisis. This raises two questions: How much wealth do households with low and moderate incomes (LMI) have? What pathways could help these households build greater wealth? To identify potential pathways, we explore wealth and wealth building among working households—those whose primary earner is in the labor force and in their prime working years (ages 25 to 65)—with LMI, defined as income below the national median of $83,730. We find that despite substantial disparities in wealth among working households with LMI, these households use business equity, home equity, and retirement savings to create wealth. Understanding these pathways can help practitioners and policymakers design better
papers.idea: Design and scale wealth-building interventions targeting LMI households through business equity, home equity, and retirement savings pathways.
papers.summary: The study examines how working households with low and moderate incomes (below $83,730 annually) currently build wealth, identifying three key pathways: business equity, home equity, and retirement savings. The authors argue that understanding these existing mechanisms can inform practitioner and policymaker design of better interventions to reduce economic hardship and improve mobility.
papers.method: Descriptive study exploring wealth and wealth-building patterns among working LMI households aged 25–65; method of data collection and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Wealth-building pathways are broadly applicable across jurisdictions, though implementation mechanisms vary.
papers.cost_note: Depends entirely on which intervention is designed; not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing housing, small-business, and retirement-savings programs but abstract offers no concrete policy levers—only diagnostic insight.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Identifies existing wealth-building pathways among LMI households; moderate promise but primarily descriptive rather than testing causal interventions.
papers.underused_note: Wealth-building interventions for LMI households remain fragmented and underfunded relative to their demonstrated potential for economic stability and crime/hardship reduction.
papers.novelty_note: Frames wealth-building as a public-safety lever—unconventional framing—but the core pathways (homeownership, small business, retirement savings) are well-established.

papers.title: How Intermediaries Can Help Small and Medium-Sized Businesses Adopt Apprenticeship Programs
papers.authors: Bhavani Arabandi, Andrew Campbell, Lindsey Tyson
papers.abstract: Small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) play a vital role in the US economy, yet many struggle to find, train, and retain skilled workers—particularly in technology occupations. From 2023 to 2026, the Urban Institute served as an apprenticeship intermediary in North Carolina and South Carolina, working with state apprenticeship agencies and SMB employers to design, launch, and sustain high-quality registered apprenticeship programs. This work, supported by Google.org, focused on helping SMBs overcome common barriers to apprenticeship adoption while building durable pathways into tech careers. Why This Matters SMBs employ nearly half of the US workforce and generate more than 40 percent of GDP, but they often lack the staffing, financial resources, and administrative capacity needed to sponsor registered apprenticeship programs on their own. As a result, larger employers are far more likely to participate in the registered apprenticeship system. Without targeted support, many SMBs miss out on the benefits of apprenticeships—including
papers.idea: Cities can fund and operate apprenticeship intermediaries that help small and medium-sized businesses design and launch registered apprenticeship programs, particularly in tech and skilled trades, reducing barriers to entry for SMBs.
papers.summary: The Urban Institute operated as an apprenticeship intermediary in North and South Carolina from 2023–2026, working with state agencies and SMB employers to design and sustain registered apprenticeship programs. The work addressed common barriers SMBs face in adopting apprenticeships—lack of staffing, financial resources, and administrative capacity—while building durable pathways into tech careers.
papers.method: Intermediary program implementation in two states over three years, supported by Google.org; appears descriptive/case-study based on abstract (no control group or rigorous impact evaluation mentioned).
papers.scalability_note: Intermediary model is portable across regions; requires funding and coordination but no novel infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Intermediary staffing costs required; Google.org-funded model, so cost-per-trainee not disclosed in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has robust small-business ecosystem and existing apprenticeship infrastructure (DOLLY, NYC Apprenticeship); intermediary model aligns with city economic-mobility goals and workforce development priorities.
papers.reach: Small and medium-sized businesses in North Carolina and South Carolina from 2023 to 2026
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Program description and intermediary model; no quantified outcomes, completion rates, or wage/employment impacts reported in abstract.
papers.underused_note: Apprenticeship intermediaries exist in some metros but remain underfunded and fragmented; NYC has limited SMB-focused tech apprenticeship infrastructure relative to need.
papers.novelty_note: Apprenticeship intermediaries are an established practice; application to SMBs in tech is sensible but not a fresh conceptual reframing.

papers.title: Pinpointing When Economically Valuable Skill Gaps Emerge and Where Intervention Can Make a Difference
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project builds on an ongoing SUMI project that identifies which skill groupings from item-level standardized test data drive adult wages at age 25. Early findings have revealed a striking pattern: Specific item-anchored skills that drive economic mobility are largely similar between white and Hispanic students in third grade, but the gap widens sharply at middle school entry and is never recovered, even as high school narrows gaps in the skills needed to get a high school diploma. Yet critical questions remain regarding why this divergence in mobility-relevant skills occurs at the middle school transition, what skills are being emphasized in high school to reduce the diploma gap but not the wage gap, and whether the picture changes when tracking wages through age 30 rather than age 25. This project will directly address these remaining questions by leveraging existing Texas administrative data to identify precisely when and where disparities in
papers.idea: Identify the specific skills that diverge between white and Hispanic students at middle school transition and design targeted interventions to close wage-relevant skill gaps.
papers.summary: This ongoing research uses Texas administrative data linked to standardized test item responses and adult wages to pinpoint when economically valuable skill gaps emerge between racial/ethnic groups. Early findings show that wage-relevant skills are similar between white and Hispanic third graders but diverge sharply at middle school entry, a gap never recovered despite high school efforts to close diploma-gap skills.
papers.method: Longitudinal analysis of Texas administrative data linking item-level standardized test scores to adult wages at ages 25 and 30, identifying which specific tested skills predict later earnings.
papers.scalability_note: Study is diagnostically focused on Texas data; findings may not generalize to NYC's demographics, curricula, and labor markets without local replication.
papers.cost_note: Research cost moderate; interventions derived from findings would depend on implementation type and scale—costs unknown.
papers.nyc_note: No NYC-specific data or interventions proposed; would require separate NYC analysis before any local policy adoption.
papers.effect_size: Skill gap between white and Hispanic students widens sharply at middle school entry
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Foundational correlational evidence of wage-relevant skill divergence; causal intervention effects not yet demonstrated in abstract.
papers.underused_note: Item-level skill diagnostics and middle-school-targeted interventions addressing specific wage gaps are rarely implemented at scale in major districts despite clear evidence of critical juncture.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes equity gap as precise, wage-linked skill divergence at middle school (not high school); shifts focus from diploma parity to mobility-relevant competencies.

papers.title: Evaluation of Break Through Tech’s AI Studio and Sprinternship Programs
papers.authors: Karen Gardiner, Nathan Sick, Julia Payne, Hailey D'Elia
papers.abstract: Break Through Tech offers undergraduate students technical skills training, professional readiness support, and real-world project experiences to help them attain in-college paid internships and post-college tech jobs or paid summer jobs. Program leadership commissioned a mixed methods evaluation of Break Through Tech’s two experiential, project-based learning programs: AI Studio, a one-semester, part-time, virtual program and Sprinternship, a three-week, full-time, hybrid program. The evaluation focused on employer engagement and participants’ (known as fellows) outcomes. The evaluation found that employers participate in AI Studio and Sprinternship to build diverse talent pipelines and develop staff leadership; most reported real-world use for fellows’ projects at their business. Employers rated their experiences with AI Studio and Sprinternship fellows highly and would recommend fellows for internships and permanent positions. Fellows in both programs reported an increased sense of belonging in tech, confidence in their skills, and desire to pursue a tech career. All three are critical
papers.idea: Cities could partner with tech employers and universities to replicate Break Through Tech's paid internship and skills-training programs to build diverse talent pipelines and increase tech-sector employment among underrepresented students.
papers.summary: Break Through Tech operates two experiential learning programs (AI Studio and Sprinternship) that provide undergraduate students technical training, professional support, and paid internship/job opportunities. Employers reported high satisfaction, real-world use of fellows' projects, and willingness to hire them; fellows reported increased sense of belonging in tech, skill confidence, and career intent.
papers.method: Mixed-methods evaluation commissioned by program leadership, examining employer engagement and fellow outcomes across two programs; sample size and data sources not specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Model is replicable across universities and employer partnerships; already operating at multiple sites.
papers.cost_note: Requires paid internship stipends and instructional staff; cost per participant not disclosed in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has major tech employers, CUNY/NYU systems, and economic mobility imperatives; could integrate with existing workforce development and higher-ed partnerships.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Self-reported confidence gains and employer satisfaction documented, but no rigorous comparison group, long-term employment tracking, or causal attribution of outcomes to program.
papers.underused_note: Tech talent-pipeline programs exist in some US cities but remain unevenly distributed; NYC has limited comparable paid internship + skills-training hybrids at scale, especially focused on underrepresented groups.
papers.novelty_note: University-employer internship partnerships are conventional; framing as public-safety or civic-health intervention is absent—this is primarily an economic/workforce play, not a public-safety solution.

papers.title: Towards a Theory of Modular Natives: Explaining Superscaling, China's Greatest Innovation Yet
papers.authors: Bent Flyvbjerg, Alexander Budzier, Maria Christodoulou
papers.abstract: First, we present a new theory of "modular natives." A modular native is a basic building block that is born modular, e.g., a solar cell. The theory predicts that using modular natives in building things reduces complexity and improves predictability, resulting in better outcomes and faster scale-up. Second, we test the theory on the largest dataset of its kind. We find, at a high level of statistical significance, that modular natives operate under a fundamentally different risk regime than other project types, with finite and predictable risk, in contrast to non-natives that have infinite and unpredictable risk. The findings help explain why modularity is key to successful building while bespokeness often leads to failure. Third, we relate our findings to economic and geopolitical development, arguing that China understands modular natives and scale-up better than any other geography and that this is key to China's swiftly growing dominance in renewables, batteries, EVs, robots, etc. We argue that China's mastery of modularity and scale-up is a major innovation in its own right, among the greatest and most impactful in human history, falsifying the common notion that China cannot innovate. Business and government outside China ignore these findings at their peril. Finally, we spell out policy and practice implications and identify areas for further research.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt modular design principles for infrastructure projects to reduce complexity and cost overruns.
papers.summary: The authors propose a theory of 'modular natives'—building blocks designed to be modular from inception—and argue that modularity reduces risk and improves predictability in large projects. They claim China's superior understanding of modular scaling explains its dominance in renewables, batteries, and EVs, and that this represents a major innovation in how projects are conceived and deployed.
papers.method: The authors tested their theory on a large dataset of projects, using statistical analysis to compare risk profiles of modular versus non-modular approaches, though the abstract does not specify sample size, design, or setting details.
papers.scalability_note: Modular design principles are general and transferable across geographies and sectors.
papers.cost_note: No costs estimated in abstract; modularity may reduce per-unit costs but upfront design investment required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's focus is on retrofitting legacy systems and fixing specific projects; the paper's insights apply mainly to greenfield infrastructure (renewables, EVs) where NYC has limited direct control.
papers.effect_size: Modular natives operate under finite and predictable risk versus infinite and unpredictable risk for non-natives (statistically significant at high level)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate demonstrated effect on project predictability and cost; strong statistical significance on risk regime, but limited direct evidence on public-safety or civic outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Modular design is increasingly adopted in infrastructure (prefab housing, standardized transit components); not dramatically underfunded relative to evidence maturity.
papers.novelty_note: Modular design is well-established in engineering; framing as 'modular natives' is a labeling innovation, not a fundamentally new approach to city problems.

papers.title: How Career and Technical Education for Students Expands Work Opportunities
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.idea: Expand career and technical education (CTE) programs in public schools to increase work opportunities and economic mobility for students.
papers.summary: Unable to determine specific findings from abstract alone. The title suggests the study examines how CTE programs expand work opportunities for students, likely through improved labor-market outcomes or employment pathways.
papers.method: Unknown; insufficient information provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: CTE programs are widely adopted and can scale across school districts; NYC already operates some programs.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost; requires infrastructure, instructor training, equipment; may generate long-term ROI via earnings gains.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Dept. of Education already operates CTE programs; expansion aligns with existing governance and funding mechanisms.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: CTE shows moderate positive effects on earnings and employment rates, but results vary by program quality and labor-market alignment; not transformative.
papers.underused_note: CTE has gained policy attention post-2010s; many urban districts expanding programs, though quality and funding remain uneven versus traditional academics.
papers.novelty_note: Well-established education-policy lever; framing as economic-mobility tool is conventional rather than counterintuitive.

papers.title: The Paradox of Housing Supply Growth
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark
papers.abstract: Housing affordability is declining in the United States, and one explanation is that housing supply has failed to match demand for new homes. There is some debate about whether this shortage is universal or applicable to just a subset of the nation’s population. Some research suggests that certain communities, including many neighborhoods, cities, and states with relatively high populations of liberal residents and high housing values—often areas on the nation’s coasts—have more restrictive housing policies while others, including areas in the Sunbelt, are attracting more housing construction. A growing movement of activists are encouraging the more restrictive states and localities to alter their land-use regulations to encourage more construction. Yet more evidence is necessary to understand the relationships between geography, resident demographics, politics, and housing-supply growth in recent decades. In this research, I explore how housing supply changed in cities, counties, and metropolitan areas between 2000 and 2020. Key Takeaways
papers.idea: Relax land-use regulations in high-cost coastal cities to increase housing supply and improve affordability.
papers.summary: This descriptive study examines how housing supply changed across U.S. cities, counties, and metros from 2000–2020, finding variation by geography and politics. It documents that restrictive coastal/liberal areas built less housing while Sunbelt areas built more, and notes activism pushing for deregulation in high-cost regions. The abstract does not report specific findings, effect sizes, or policy outcomes.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of housing supply trends across cities, counties, and metropolitan areas from 2000–2020; no specific sample size, control group, or causal identification method stated.
papers.scalability_note: Land-use deregulation is a policy lever available to any city or state.
papers.cost_note: Removing regulatory barriers has low direct fiscal cost, though indirect effects on property values, infrastructure, and services are complex.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt zoning/land-use reforms, though the study does not isolate which reforms work or NYC-specific barriers.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is descriptive/exploratory (2000-2020 trends) with no causal intervention tested; no demonstrated effect on safety or public outcomes measured.
papers.underused_note: Zoning reform is actively debated and piloted in major cities (Minneapolis, California, NYC); substantial political & policy attention already exists.
papers.novelty_note: Core thesis (supply constraints → affordability crisis → zoning reform) is mainstream in urban policy discourse; framing is conventional.

papers.title: Georgia’s Quality Rating and Improvement System
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: In 2023, Urban gathered perspectives from families, child care providers, technical assistance providers, and DECAL staff to inform DECAL’s comprehensive review of Quality Rated, Georgia’s quality rating and improvement system—examining topics such as what quality means, the benefits of and barriers to participating in Quality Rated, the system’s fairness, and opportunities for improvement.
papers.idea: NYC could systematize and improve its child care quality rating and improvement system by gathering stakeholder input on what quality means, barriers to participation, and fairness concerns—mirroring Georgia's DECAL review process.
