papers.summary: Urban Institute conducted a qualitative review of Georgia's Quality Rated system in 2023, gathering feedback from families, providers, technical assistance staff, and state officials on system design, perceived benefits, barriers, fairness, and improvement opportunities. The study appears to have identified stakeholder priorities and pain points in how quality is measured and communicated in the child care market.
papers.method: Qualitative interviews and focus groups with multiple stakeholder types (families, providers, TA providers, state staff) in Georgia; sample size and full methodology not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Quality rating systems exist across multiple states and can be adapted; methodology is transferable to other jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Qualitative research and system review are moderate-cost; operational improvements depend on findings.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has child care quality oversight and subsidy programs (NYCDOE, ACS); a similar stakeholder review could improve transparency and participation in existing systems.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is qualitative stakeholder input, not a rigorous outcome evaluation; no demonstrated causal effect on child safety, development, or family outcomes.
papers.underused_note: NYC already operates a quality rating system (Star Ratings); the gap is in systematic stakeholder review processes, not the absence of rating systems themselves.
papers.novelty_note: Stakeholder engagement in program review is standard practice; framing is conventional public-administration methodology rather than a novel intervention.

papers.title: How Local Leaders in Boone County, Missouri, Are Advancing Upward Mobility for Residents
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Dignity and belonging. Economic success. Power and autonomy. These are the things all people need to achieve upward mobility. But how do you help people access them? Boone County, Missouri’s, Upward Mobility initiative has an answer. Its work over the past five years has shown that though helping residents exit poverty and access long-term upward mobility is multifaceted and complex, it’s possible when local leaders are committed to partnership, have a deep understanding of the community’s needs and strengths, and prioritize helping residents feel dignity and belonging. Below, hear from four leaders of Boone County’s Upward Mobility initiative about how they engage in upward mobility work and what motivates them to keep working toward a future where all residents have opportunities to thrive. Convening the Community to Advance Upward Mobility Like in many parts of the country, rents in Boone County have climbed in recent years. From 2019 to 2024
papers.idea: Convene local leaders across sectors to design and implement community-centered upward mobility initiatives grounded in resident dignity, belonging, and multi-stakeholder partnership.
papers.summary: Boone County, Missouri's five-year Upward Mobility initiative demonstrates that helping residents exit poverty requires sustained commitment to partnership, deep community understanding, and prioritizing dignity and belonging. The program addresses concrete local challenges like rising rents by convening diverse stakeholders to co-design solutions.
papers.method: Descriptive case study featuring qualitative interviews with four initiative leaders in Boone County, Missouri; no experimental or control group comparison.
papers.scalability_note: Community convening and partnership models are replicable, though specific implementation will vary by local context and leadership capacity.
papers.cost_note: Convening and coordination are relatively low-cost; costs depend on interventions adopted (housing, workforce, etc.), which abstract does not detail.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has strong multi-stakeholder governance structures and agencies; dignity-centered convening model could integrate into existing economic mobility initiatives.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive case study with no quantified outcomes, control groups, or rigorous impact evaluation. Leadership commitment and partnership noted but effects on resident mobility undemonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Multi-sector convening and dignity-centered frameworks are increasingly mainstream in community development; not a gap-filling intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Stakeholder partnership and dignity framing are established best-practice language in nonprofit/civic work; conventional approach reframed as fresh but not substantively novel.

papers.title: Correction to: The Changing Nature of Homicide in the Twin Cities: An Interrupted Time Series Analysis of the Police Murder of George Floyd
papers.authors: Ryan P. Larson, James A. Densley, Jillian K. Peterson, Jaycee Manchi, N. Jeanie Santaularia, Christopher E. Robertson, Christopher Uggen
papers.idea: No actionable intervention is identifiable from the title and absent abstract.
papers.summary: This appears to be a correction/erratum to a time-series analysis examining homicide trends in Minneapolis before and after the police killing of George Floyd in May 2020. Without the abstract, the specific findings and policy implications are unknown.
papers.method: Interrupted time series analysis of homicide data in the Twin Cities; likely using pre- and post-Floyd data.
papers.scalability_note: Time-series designs are setting-specific; generalization depends on whether findings reflect local conditions or broader patterns.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; appears to be a research study rather than an intervention with direct costs.
papers.nyc_note: Findings specific to Minneapolis may not transfer directly; NYC has different policing structure, demographics, and homicide drivers.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No intervention tested; observational analysis only.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention—cannot be deployed or underused.
papers.novelty_note: Descriptive study of homicide trends, not a novel approach.

papers.title: One-Fifth of the World’s Population Is at High Risk of Climate-Related Hazards
papers.abstract: Climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events. When these events occur, they threaten lives and livelihoods. This paper estimates the global population at high risk of climate-related hazards by combining household-level vulnerability data with local exposure to four types of events: agricultural droughts in rural areas, floods, heatwaves, and cyclones. Under current climate conditions, 4.5 billion people are expected to experience these hazards at intensities exceeding hazard-specific thresholds chosen to capture the likely occurrence of systemic impacts within a lifetime. One-third of this population is considered highly vulnerable, based on seven dimensions that influence ability to cope and recover: income, education, access to finance, social protection, drinking water, electricity, and access to services and markets. Overall, one in five people globally are considered at high risk, meaning they are both likely to experience at least one of these hazards and face severely limited capacity to recover from their impacts. Although the share of the global population at high risk has nearly halved since 2010 due to decreased vulnerability, the number of people exposed has increased, and progress has been uneven across regions. This study introduces a new global population headcount indicator based on household survey data and high-resolution spatial data to monitor climate risks across countries and over time.
papers.idea: Cities could use household vulnerability indices (income, education, finance access, water, electricity, services) to prioritize climate adaptation and disaster preparedness in high-risk neighborhoods.
papers.summary: The study combines household vulnerability data with exposure to four climate hazards (drought, floods, heatwaves, cyclones) to estimate that 1 in 5 people globally (roughly 1.5 billion) face high climate risk. One-third of the 4.5 billion exposed to these hazards lack capacity to recover due to low income, education, and service access. Although vulnerability has declined since 2010, the absolute number exposed has grown unevenly across regions.
papers.effect_size: 1 in 5 globally at high risk; vulnerability decreased but exposure increased since 2010
papers.reach: 4.5 billion people exposed to hazards; 1.5 billion at high risk globally
papers.method: Cross-sectional analysis combining household survey data with high-resolution spatial exposure data for four climate hazard types, assessed against hazard-specific thresholds.
papers.scalability_note: Methodology could be adapted to any city with household surveys and climate hazard mapping; creates replicable monitoring framework.
papers.cost_note: Requires data infrastructure (surveys, spatial mapping) and analysis; moderate upfront cost, low ongoing cost if integrated into city services.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited agricultural drought and cyclone risk; floods and heatwaves are relevant. Framework is global/national; abstract offers no concrete NYC policy or program to implement.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrates significant correlation between vulnerability indices and climate hazard exposure, but is observational/descriptive rather than testing an intervention with causal impact.
papers.underused_note: NYC has basic flood/heat maps but rarely integrates multi-dimensional household vulnerability indices into adaptation planning; most climate resilience spending is infrastructure-focused, not targeted to vulnerable populations.
papers.novelty_note: Combining vulnerability dimensions (income, education, finance, services) with hazard exposure is methodologically sound but incremental; the idea of equity-based targeting is established in development work, less so in US city climate policy.

papers.title: Breaking point: Exploring side bets, support, and pension investment as predictors of turnover intention in urban policing
papers.authors: Kenneth M. Quick, Kevin T. Wolff
papers.idea: Reduce police turnover by addressing factors such as side bets (career investments), workplace support, and pension structures.
papers.summary: No abstract available; title suggests the study examines correlates of turnover intention among urban police officers, specifically exploring side bets, organizational support, and pension investment as predictors.
papers.method: Not determinable from title alone; likely correlational survey or administrative data analysis of police officers.
papers.scalability_note: Findings about retention drivers could inform HR policy, but implementation depends on pension/compensation authority and data on actual effect sizes.
papers.cost_note: Likely involves HR/benefits process changes; cost depends on specific recommendations (pension reform, support programs).
papers.nyc_note: NYPD is a major city agency; HR and compensation policy levers exist. However, pension structure is heavily constrained by state law.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is correlational/predictive, not causal; no intervention tested or outcome measured.
papers.underused_note: Police retention via improved workplace support and benefits is already a known management concern; modest gap between research and practice.
papers.novelty_note: Framing turnover predictors (support, pension, career investment) is standard HR/organizational research; not a novel public-safety intervention.

papers.title: The Nonprofit Workforce Faces Compounding Challenges. What Does That Mean for Communities?
papers.authors: Hannah Martin
papers.abstract: The nonprofit workforce powers essential services in every community, from animal shelters and substance use treatment programs to employment assistance and support for at-risk young people. But the staff who make these programs and services possible are under strain. Nonprofits accounted for 12.8 million jobs in the US in 2022 (the most recent year of available data). That represents a significant portion (9.9 percent) of the country’s private workforce. Yet staff vacancies, employee burnout, and rising demand threaten to destabilize the nonprofit workforce—and communities’ access to vital programs and services. Funders and policymakers are well positioned to address these challenges. To help them do so, this article explores national and state-level data on the nonprofit workforce, explains the connection between nonprofits and communities, and offers potential solutions that leaders of the nonprofit sector support. What workforce challenges are nonprofits facing? Nonprofits are navigating compounding challenges that make it hard to
papers.idea: Cities could work with funders and policymakers to address nonprofit workforce retention, vacancies, and burnout through sector-wide coordination.
papers.summary: The nonprofit sector employs 12.8 million people (9.9% of US private workforce) and delivers essential community services, but faces staff vacancies, burnout, and rising demand that threaten program stability. The article explores national and state data on nonprofit workforce challenges and offers potential solutions supported by sector leaders.
papers.reach: 12.8 million nonprofit employees nationally; state-level data referenced
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of national and state-level nonprofit workforce data with discussion of challenges and potential solutions from sector leaders.
papers.scalability_note: Workforce and funding interventions are general and could apply across cities and states.
papers.cost_note: Likely requires significant new investment in compensation, benefits, or training; no cost analysis in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a large nonprofit sector delivering services in human services, education, health, and safety, but abstract offers no specific NYC examples or tailored guidance.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Establishes clear causal link between nonprofit workforce stability and service delivery; moderate strength but not a direct intervention study with quantified outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Sector-wide workforce coordination is rarely deployed as a coordinated strategy in major cities; most funding remains program-siloed rather than cross-sector.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes nonprofit instability as a systems problem requiring coordinated funder/policy action rather than individual org responsibility; somewhat fresh but not entirely counterintuitive.

papers.title: Examining Measurements of Fear of Crime Across Surveys: Content, Cognitive Processes, and Psychometric Properties
papers.abstract: Purpose: To enhance the understanding of different survey questions and the opportunities they provide to measure fear of crime as a latent trait, examining both crime-type and consequence items. Methods: Items from five surveys used in the Stockholm Region were coded into content areas. Cognitive interviews (n = 19) were analysed for response processes. Rasch analyses were conducted on the Swedish Crime Survey (n = 4,264) and the City of Stockholm Crime Survey (n = 7,333). Results: The surveys covered overlapping topics but used distinct phrasings. Response comprehension was generally adequate, yet some terms were interpreted differently. Respondents found it difficult to isolate fear of crime from other motives in the consequence items. There was no clear advantage to either frequency- or consequence-framed items, whereas intensity stems imposed a lower response burden. Psychometrically, unidimensional solutions were hard to achieve for either crime type or consequence item sets. The evidence suggests heterogeneity across population groups. Conclusions: Current practice involves both strengths and weaknesses. This study suggests paths toward enhanced measurement of the fear of crime. Measuring fear of crime as a single, invariant latent trait is far from straightforward. The heterogeneity of responses across population groups indicates that different groups evaluate their fear of crime types and consequences differently, challenging the assumption of unidimensionality. However, the cognitive interviews suggest ways to clarify item wording and reduce respondent burden.
papers.idea: Standardize fear-of-crime survey instruments across city agencies to improve measurement comparability and policy targeting.
papers.summary: This methods paper compares five Swedish crime-fear surveys to understand how different question formats (crime-type vs. consequence items, frequency vs. intensity frames) measure the latent construct of fear of crime. Cognitive interviews and psychometric analysis revealed that responses are heterogeneous across population groups, unidimensional measurement is difficult, and some survey items impose unnecessary response burden.
papers.reach: Two Swedish surveys (n=4,264 and n=7,333); 19 cognitive interviews
papers.method: Content coding of five surveys, cognitive interviews (n=19) to examine response processes, and Rasch psychometric analysis of two large Swedish crime surveys (total ~11,597 respondents).
papers.scalability_note: Measurement guidance is context-specific to Stockholm; applicability to NYC requires localized validation.
papers.cost_note: Survey redesign and cognitive testing are relatively low-cost; main investment is staff time.
papers.nyc_note: NYC collects fear-of-crime data via NYPD and BJS surveys; findings could improve instrument design, but Swedish context limits direct transfer.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies measurement problems and suggests improvements, but demonstrates no actual intervention effect on fear or safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Survey standardization is moderately adopted in some jurisdictions; NYC agencies use disparate fear-of-crime measures, but the specific psychometric evidence here is not yet driving policy changes.
papers.novelty_note: Measurement standardization is conventional practice in research; the cognitive-process angle adds some nuance but remains methodological rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: Letter in Response to the Department of Education’s Request for Comment on the Proposed Rule ED-2026-OPE-0100, Accountability in Higher Education and Access through Demand-driven Workforce Pell
papers.authors: David Pitts
papers.abstract: In a public comment to the US Department of Education, David Pitts, vice president of Urban's Justice and Safety Division and interim vice president of the Work, Education, and Labor Division, responds to a proposed rule that would establish a single earnings-based accountability framework covering nearly every Title IV program. The comment focuses on how the framework would affect Prison Education Programs (PEPs) and the broader population of students with histories of incarceration. It identifies three concerns: IRS earnings data systematically undercapture the earnings of formerly incarcerated workers, the proposed exclusion for PEP students does not reach those who finish credentials at a main campus after release from prison, and community colleges, HBCUs, and certificate programs serving justice-impacted students face compounded vulnerability under the rule. The comment offers targeted recommendations the Department can implement through preamble language and existing discretionary authority.
papers.idea: Cities could advocate for federal accountability rules that better capture earnings of formerly incarcerated workers and protect prison education programs from penalization under earnings-based metrics.
papers.summary: This is a public comment letter responding to a proposed federal Department of Education rule on accountability for Title IV programs. It identifies three concerns specific to Prison Education Programs and justice-impacted students: IRS earnings data undercaptures formerly incarcerated workers' income, the proposed exclusion fails to cover those completing credentials post-release, and community colleges and HBCUs serving this population face compounded vulnerability.
papers.method: Public comment letter analyzing a proposed federal regulation and offering recommendations; non-empirical advocacy document.
papers.scalability_note: Primarily a federal policy advocacy document; city-level leverage is indirect and limited to lobbying or coordinating with state/federal actors.
papers.cost_note: If implemented, involves regulatory clarification only; no direct program costs implied.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally change federal education accountability rules; relevance is only through advocacy, lobbying, or alignment with state-level action on prison education.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: This is advocacy/policy commentary rather than an empirical study with measured outcomes; no direct evidence of impact on safety or reentry outcomes presented.
papers.underused_note: Prison education & reentry support remain chronically underfunded; federal accountability rules rarely account for justice-involved populations' unique earnings patterns and barriers.
papers.novelty_note: Framing earnings metrics as a hidden penalty on reentry programs is somewhat fresh; applying federal accountability scrutiny to protect rather than defund prison education is less conventional.

papers.title: Using Price Incentives to Bound Welfare from Pay-as-You-Go Solar Electricity
papers.abstract: Non-price barriers suppress demand at the adoption margin, complicating efforts to quantify the welfare effects of rural electrification. This paper studies demand for pay-as-you-go (PAYGo) solar electricity using a randomized experiment with 800 existing PAYGo customers in Kenya and Rwanda post-adoption. The experiment randomly assigns incentives that lower the effective price of usage for consumers who meet monthly purchase thresholds. Although average demand is unchanged, consumers with the highest pre-experimental demand increase their purchases by 6–7% in response to the incentive. These responses are used to estimate a lower bound on consumer surplus from PAYGo solar. The study finds large gains for high-demand consumers, but benefits deteriorate substantially for low-demand consumers. Combining these estimates with evidence from the literature on the environmental externalities of solar home systems, the marginal value of public funds of PAYGo solar subsidies is at most 1.7 in Kenya and 2 in Rwanda.
papers.idea: Use price incentives to increase household solar adoption and usage in low-income areas.
papers.summary: A randomized experiment with 800 PAYGo solar customers in Kenya and Rwanda tested price incentives tied to monthly purchase thresholds. High-demand consumers increased usage by 6–7%, but average demand was unchanged and low-demand consumers saw no benefit. The study estimates that PAYGo solar subsidies have marginal value of public funds of at most 1.7–2.0, indicating limited welfare gains relative to cost.
papers.effect_size: 6–7% increase in purchases among highest-demand consumers; no average effect
papers.reach: 800 existing PAYGo customers in Kenya and Rwanda
papers.method: Randomized experiment assigning price incentives to 800 post-adoption PAYGo solar customers in Kenya and Rwanda, with demand responses used to estimate consumer surplus and cost-effectiveness.
papers.scalability_note: Study is context-specific to rural off-grid Africa; pay-as-you-go solar is not a primary urban electrification need in NYC or dense US cities.
papers.cost_note: Subsidy cost-effectiveness is low to modest (1.7–2.0 MVPF); study does not isolate per-unit costs of the incentive itself.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has universal grid access; study addresses rural off-grid electrification in developing countries. No direct policy application to NYC's public-safety, housing, transit, or core service challenges.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: 6-7% usage increase only among high-demand subset; no average treatment effect; rural electrification study with limited public-safety relevance.
papers.underused_note: Not a public-safety intervention; energy access is distinct policy domain from NYC crime/safety priorities.
papers.novelty_note: Price-incentive framing for adoption is conventional; application to solar in Kenya/Rwanda lacks NYC contextual novelty.

papers.title: Ambient Information: Measuring Student Capacities from AI-Interaction Chat Data
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project develops novel, highly scalable measures of cognitive, social, and emotional skills by analyzing behavioral data from students’ interactions with AI tools. The study is led by a distinguished scholar with deep research-practice partnerships with school systems nationwide and conducted in collaboration with leading AI companies. The project will draw on naturally occurring student–AI interaction data from several widely used AI platforms and traditional data from school district partnerships, including surveys, assessments, and administrative records. These data partnerships enable the project to develop new measures of higher-order thinking and self-management skills (such as engagement, persistence, and self-regulation) that can be captured frequently from passively generated AI interaction data and validated against traditional measures of student skills. The project lays the groundwork for building a scalable and comparable system for assessing and strengthening the durable skills students need for long‑run success. Data Sources Data: Administrative data from school districts and
papers.idea: Use AI interaction data to develop scalable measures of student cognitive and social-emotional skills for assessment and intervention.
papers.summary: The project develops novel measurement approaches by analyzing behavioral data from students' interactions with AI tools to capture higher-order thinking and self-management skills (engagement, persistence, self-regulation). These AI-derived measures are validated against traditional assessments and administrative data from school districts. The goal is to create a scalable, comparable system for assessing durable skills linked to long-term student success.
papers.method: Observational analysis of naturally occurring student–AI interaction data from widely used AI platforms, combined with school district administrative data, surveys, and assessments; partnership-based approach with school systems and AI companies.
papers.scalability_note: AI interaction data is passively generated and widely available; platform-agnostic methodology could apply across many districts.
papers.cost_note: Relies on naturally occurring data; minimal direct intervention cost once measurement infrastructure built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has large school system with potential AI tool adoption, but abstract provides no concrete actionable policy or program a city could adopt—only measurement development.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is developmental/foundational (measure creation), not yet evaluating an intervention with demonstrated student outcomes.
papers.underused_note: AI-assisted assessment is emerging but sparse in K–12; scalable social-emotional measurement tools remain underdeveloped in practice despite policy interest.
papers.novelty_note: Using passive AI interaction data as a continuous, scalable proxy for cognitive and emotional skills is a genuinely fresh approach to real-time student assessment.

papers.title: A theory evaluation of U.S. firearm laws and their likely effectiveness in reducing gun-related crime
papers.authors: Daniel P. Mears, Jessica C. Arthur
papers.idea: Evaluate which existing U.S. firearm laws are most effective at reducing gun crime and adapt the most promising ones to NYC policy.
papers.summary: This appears to be a theory evaluation paper that reviews U.S. firearm laws and their likely effectiveness in reducing gun-related crime. Without an abstract, the study's specific findings and scope are unclear, but the work focuses on assessing theoretical connections between law types and crime reduction rather than testing a new intervention.
papers.method: Theory evaluation of existing firearm laws (specific methods, data sources, and scope unknown without abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Firearm law policy is applicable across jurisdictions, though NYC has limited local regulatory authority on guns (state/federal preemption).
papers.cost_note: Policy evaluation is low-cost; implementation cost depends on which laws are recommended.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has severe state and federal constraints on firearm regulation; most actionable recommendations would require state/federal change.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theory evaluation only; no empirical outcome data on actual crime reduction reported.
papers.underused_note: Firearm policy is heavily debated and researched nationally; NYC already has stringent gun laws. Gap is modest.
papers.novelty_note: Comparative analysis of existing laws is standard policy approach; not a new intervention or mechanism.

papers.title: Examining the role of the social bond in the formation of violent crime intentions among incarcerated men
papers.authors: Jeff A. Bouffard, Nicole Niebuhr
papers.idea: Strengthen social bonds among incarcerated men as a violence-prevention intervention within correctional facilities.
papers.summary: No abstract available; title suggests the study examines correlational or descriptive relationships between social bond strength and violent crime intentions among a sample of incarcerated men.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract provided.
papers.scalability_note: Study concerns incarcerated populations and theory-building; unclear if findings translate to actionable, city-level interventions beyond correctional program design.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; would depend heavily on implementation model.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could integrate social-bond interventions into DOC programming, but no concrete program or evidence of effectiveness is evident from the title alone.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical foundation is sound (social bond theory well-established), but this appears to be an examination/correlational study rather than a rigorous intervention trial with measured behavioral outcomes.
papers.underused_note: In-prison violence reduction through social bonding is underfunded relative to its potential; most correctional systems prioritize security/punishment over rehabilitation infrastructure that builds prosocial ties.
papers.novelty_note: Social bond theory is classical criminology (Hirschi); applying it to incarcerated populations is straightforward rather than counterintuitive.

papers.title: Survey-to-Survey Poverty Monitoring under Economic Shocks : The Role of Proxies and Flexible Nonlinear Learners
papers.abstract: In many low-income countries, consumption surveys are too infrequent to track poverty during economic shocks. Survey-to-survey imputation can fill this gap, but its reliability depends on whether prediction models estimated in one period remain valid in another. This paper develops a formal identification framework for the transportability of survey-to-survey estimators and shows that failures during crises arise primarily from missing shock-responsive information rather than from insufficient model flexibility. When such information is omitted, both linear models and flexible learners yield biased poverty estimates. When fast-changing proxy variables that track unobserved welfare changes are included, out-of-sample validity can be restored under well-defined conditions. Poverty estimation, however, requires a stronger condition—stability of the full conditional distribution of welfare—beyond that needed for mean welfare estimation. Monte Carlo simulations and evidence from Afghanistan, Uganda, and Rwanda support these predictions. The findings imply that improving questionnaire content is more important than increasing model complexity for timely poverty measurement in the face of shocks.
papers.idea: Improve poverty-monitoring survey questionnaires to include shock-responsive proxy variables so that city agencies can track welfare changes during economic crises.
papers.summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework for when survey-to-survey imputation can reliably estimate poverty during economic shocks. It shows that model complexity matters less than including fast-changing proxy variables that track unobserved welfare changes; when such proxies are omitted, both linear and flexible models produce biased poverty estimates.
papers.method: Theoretical identification framework with Monte Carlo simulations and empirical validation using household consumption survey data from three low-income countries.
papers.scalability_note: Requires redesign of survey instruments and coordination across data collection cycles; moderately difficult to implement at scale.
papers.cost_note: Main cost is questionnaire redesign and retraining enumerators; no per-unit cost stated. Likely modest relative to survey operations.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has frequent, high-quality poverty and economic data; the gap this addresses (infrequent surveys in low-income countries) does not apply. Relevance would be only if the city wanted to test new proxy variables for real-time welfare monitoring during crises.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate: demonstrates improved poverty-tracking validity during crises when shock-responsive proxies are included; simulation and real-world evidence support the approach, but effect is methodological rather than direct intervention.
papers.underused_note: NYC and large US cities rarely employ dynamic poverty-monitoring systems with shock-responsive survey design; crisis-era welfare tracking remains episodic and delayed rather than systematic.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes poverty measurement as a questionnaire-design problem (not just a modeling problem); insight that proxy variable selection matters more than algorithmic complexity is counterintuitive for data-driven audiences.

papers.title: Information Frictions and Employee Sorting between Start-ups
papers.authors: Kevin A. Bryan, Mitchell Hoffman, Amir Sariri
papers.abstract: Would workers apply to better firms if they were more informed about firm quality? Collaborating with 26 science-based start-ups, we create a custom job board and invite business school alumni to apply. The job board randomizes across applicants to show coarse expert ratings of all start-ups' science and/or business model quality. Making ratings visible strongly reallocates applications toward higher-rated firms. This reallocation holds, restricting to high-quality workers. Treatments operate in part by shifting worker beliefs about firms' right-tail outcomes. Despite these benefits, workers make posttreatment bets indicating highly overoptimistic beliefs about start-up success, suggesting a problem of broader informational deficits. (JEL D22, D83, J22, J23, J24, M13, M51)
papers.idea: Create transparent, expert-rated job boards for early-stage companies to help workers make better employment choices.
papers.summary: The study randomized job-board display of expert quality ratings for 26 science-based start-ups among business school alumni applicants. Making ratings visible reallocated applications toward higher-rated firms and shifted worker beliefs about firms' outcomes, but workers still held overoptimistic beliefs about start-up success overall.
papers.reach: 26 start-ups, business school alumni applicants (exact N not stated)
papers.method: Randomized controlled trial conducted with researchers collaborating with start-ups to create a custom job board that randomized rating visibility across applicants.
papers.scalability_note: Requires ongoing expert rating infrastructure and partnership with private employers; not a standalone city program.
papers.cost_note: Custom job board and expert rating system would require moderate upfront investment; per-applicant cost not stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could theoretically develop a rated job board, but the study focuses on start-up hiring (private sector), not a traditional city service or policy lever. Limited direct municipal application.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study shows information can shift job application choices, but no evidence on actual employment outcomes, job quality, earnings, or public-safety relevance.
papers.underused_note: Job-board transparency is already common practice; not a gap between evidence and deployment in NYC civic context.
papers.novelty_note: Straightforward application of information economics to hiring; no counterintuitive insight or novel safety/quality mechanism.

papers.title: Quantifying the Value of the Federal Home Loan Bank System
papers.authors: Bryson Berry, Jung Hyun Choi, Laurie Goodman, Daniel Pang, Kathryn Reynolds, Katie Visalli, John Walsh, Jun Zhu
papers.abstract: This brief summarizes a three-part study that quantifies the economic value of the Federal Home Loan Bank (FHLBank) System to the US financial and housing finance systems. The three studies examine (1) how the system supports member liquidity and financial stability, (2) expands mortgage and community lending, and (3) funds affordable housing and community development programs. Across multiple methodologies and scenarios, the research estimates that the FHLBank System generates between $94.2 billion and $133.2 billion in annual economic value through crisis prevention, increased lending activity, and mission-oriented investments. Why This Matters The FHLBank System plays a central but often underexamined role in the US housing finance system. This research provides a comprehensive assessment of the FHLBank System’s economic value and broader contributions to financial stability, housing finance, and community investment. The findings are relevant for policymakers, regulators, housing finance practitioners, and community lenders evaluating the FHLBank System’s future role, mission
papers.idea: City could lobby federal regulators to expand FHLBank lending programs in its jurisdiction to increase affordable housing funding.
papers.summary: This brief summarizes research quantifying the annual economic value of the Federal Home Loan Bank System, estimating $94.2–$133.2 billion in value through crisis prevention, expanded lending, and affordable housing/community development funding. The study examines how FHLBanks support member bank liquidity, mortgage lending, and mission-oriented investments.
papers.method: Three-part quantitative study using multiple methodologies and scenarios to model FHLBank System's economic contributions to financial stability, lending activity, and community investment; no specific data source, sample size, or empirical design specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: FHLBank programs are federal infrastructure; cities cannot independently create or reform them, only participate/advocate.
papers.cost_note: FHLBank system operates at federal level; city participation in existing programs has marginal cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally adopt or reform federal FHLBank system; could only work through member banks or federal advocacy, neither direct city lever.
papers.effect_size: $94.2 billion to $133.2 billion in annual economic value generated
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Quantifies substantial systemic value ($94-133B annually) but does not directly measure causal impact of expanding programs on NYC housing outcomes
papers.underused_note: FHLBank lending expansion is a viable but underutilized municipal advocacy strategy; most cities do not actively lobby regulators for program expansion
papers.novelty_note: Federal housing finance mechanisms are well-known; framing city lobbying as a tool is practical but not conceptually novel

papers.title: Teaching Teachers to Use Computer Assisted Learning Effectively
papers.authors: Philip Oreopoulos, Chloe R. Gibbs, Michael Jensen, Joseph P. Price
papers.idea: Train teachers in effective use of computer-assisted learning (CAL) tools to improve student outcomes.
papers.summary: This study examines whether professional development for teachers on how to effectively integrate computer-assisted learning into their instruction can improve student achievement. The paper tests whether teacher training on CAL implementation drives measurable gains in student learning.
papers.method: Likely experimental or quasi-experimental design testing teacher training interventions on CAL use, but specific methodology unclear from title alone.
papers.scalability_note: Teacher professional development programs are readily scalable across school districts; CAL tools already widely adopted in many systems.
papers.cost_note: Teacher PD requires staff time and materials but is relatively low-cost compared to hardware or curriculum overhauls.
papers.nyc_note: NYC schools use various CAL platforms; training programs could be implemented via DOE professional development infrastructure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: CAL effectiveness is highly variable and context-dependent; meta-analyses show modest average gains, often nullified without strong teacher training.
papers.underused_note: Teacher professional development in ed-tech is mainstream in most US districts; widespread adoption and investment already occurring.
papers.novelty_note: Standard ed-tech training approach; conventional wisdom in education policy for past 15+ years.

papers.title: Evaluating How LLMs Handle Education and Workforce Pathway Data
papers.authors: Erika Tyagi, Kristin Blagg, Emily Gutierrez
papers.abstract: AI tools are rapidly becoming one of the primary ways people find and interpret information—including questions about education and workforce pathway data. These tools have the potential to make public data more accessible, but how well do they handle questions grounded in it? Can they find the right sources, use the data correctly, and communicate information in ways that serve their users? To find out, we conducted a structured evaluation in which subject-matter experts assessed how three leading large language models (LLMs) responded to 100 curated questions about education and workforce pathway data. Why This Matters Most public data systems were built to serve human users navigating websites and documentation, not to be discovered and used by AI systems. When that gap goes unaddressed, models often return answers that look authoritative but aren't—and by attributing incorrect information to specific tools and sources, they risk eroding trust in the data infrastructure
papers.idea: Audit and improve how AI systems surface education and workforce data to ensure accuracy and maintain public trust in data infrastructure.
papers.summary: The authors evaluated three leading LLMs on 100 curated questions about education and workforce pathways, finding that AI tools often return confident-sounding but inaccurate answers and misattribute information to sources. The study highlights a gap between how public data systems were designed (for human web navigation) and how LLMs discover and use them.
papers.method: Structured evaluation where subject-matter experts assessed LLM responses to 100 curated questions about education and workforce pathway data; no sample size given for models or institutions tested.
papers.scalability_note: Diagnostic tool useful for government but requires ongoing audits per LLM; not a scalable intervention per se.
papers.cost_note: Primarily staff expertise (subject-matter experts); marginal deployment cost once audit framework built.
papers.nyc_note: NYC publishes education and workforce data (CUNY, workforce boards); audit results could inform vendor selection or API design, but impact on resident outcomes is indirect.
papers.reach: 3 large language models evaluated on 100 questions
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is diagnostic/evaluative rather than interventional; documents LLM failures but doesn't demonstrate a deployed solution's impact on actual outcomes.
papers.underused_note: AI auditing is emerging but inconsistently applied; data infrastructure improvements are recognized as important but underfunded relative to need.
papers.novelty_note: Framing data-infrastructure trust through LLM audit lens is relatively fresh; applies established evaluation methods to a new problem domain.

papers.title: Occupational Mobility and Green Transition : A Stylized Estimation of Skill Investment Needs in South Asia
papers.abstract: This paper estimates the share of green employment in seven South Asian countries using an O*NET-based definition and finds that, on average, 24 percent of jobs in the region are currently green. The paper then assesses the potential for non-green workers to transition into green occupations. Among all non-green workers, 57 percent could transition to green jobs with limited reskilling, 16 percent with moderate upskilling, and 27 percent with full skills reconversion. These results suggest three policy priorities—promoting on-the-job training, strengthening firm-based and public–private training systems, and adopting a dual strategy that invests in reskilling and upskilling today’s workforce while simultaneously advancing forward-looking education reforms that equip future cohorts for new and emerging green occupations.
papers.idea: City workforce development agencies could design and fund reskilling/upskilling programs targeting non-green workers for transition into green jobs, with tiered intensity (on-the-job training, moderate upskilling, full conversion) matched to worker capacity.
papers.summary: The study estimates that 24% of jobs in seven South Asian countries are currently green, and assesses the feasibility of transitioning non-green workers into green occupations. It finds that 57% of non-green workers could transition with limited reskilling, 16% with moderate upskilling, and 27% requiring full skills reconversion. The paper recommends promoting on-the-job training, strengthening firm-based and public–private training systems, and combining workforce upskilling with forward-looking education reform.
papers.effect_size: 57% of non-green workers could transition to green jobs with limited reskilling
papers.reach: Seven South Asian countries (population and labor force size not stated)
papers.method: Descriptive estimation of green employment shares and skill-gap analysis using O*NET occupational database applied to labor force data from seven South Asian countries.
papers.scalability_note: Training programs are standard public policy tools, easily adapted to city/regional labor markets; requires institutional capacity and funding but proven models exist.
papers.cost_note: Training programs range widely in cost; abstract provides no cost data. Moderate per-participant cost typical for public workforce development.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing workforce development infrastructure (NYCDOE, DYCD, CUNY) and green-jobs initiatives; could fold skill-transition pathways into CEWD and union apprenticeship programs. South Asian context requires local labor-market adaptation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modeling study with no empirical demonstration of actual workforce transition outcomes or earnings/employment impacts.
papers.underused_note: Green job training and workforce development are active policy areas in most major US cities; this framing is not novel or underdeployed relative to existing workforce programs.
papers.novelty_note: Standard workforce development logic applied to green transition; tiered training intensity is conventional practice.

papers.title: At-risk Youth in Honduras: A Psychometric Evaluation of the Instrumento de Medicion de Comportamientos (IMC)
papers.authors: Hyunjung Cheon, Charles M. Katz, Yi Zheng
papers.abstract: Prior research highlights the importance of developmental domains—community, family, school, peer, and individual—in shaping youth problem behaviors such as delinquency, drug use, and violence. However, few instruments measuring risk and protective factors have been psychometrically evaluated in developing countries, particularly in Central America. This study examines the psychometric properties of the Instrumento de Medicion de Comportamientos (IMC), a prevention-oriented survey assessing risk and protective factors, using a sample of Honduran youth. We evaluated the IMC’s internal consistency, construct validity, concurrent validity, and measurement invariance. Findings indicate the IMC demonstrates acceptable reliability and validity in measuring key risk and protective constructs in Honduras. These findings support the IMC as a tool for identifying at-risk youth, informing prevention and early intervention efforts, and extending the psychometric evaluation of youth risk and protective factor instruments to Latin America, highlighting the importance of locally developed instruments for evidence-based crime prevention and youth violence interventions.
papers.idea: Adopt the IMC survey to screen NYC youth for risk and protective factors to guide prevention and early intervention resource allocation.
papers.summary: This study validated the psychometric properties (reliability, construct validity, concurrent validity, measurement invariance) of the Instrumento de Medicion de Comportamientos (IMC), a survey measuring risk and protective factors across five developmental domains in a Honduran youth sample. The IMC demonstrated acceptable reliability and validity for identifying at-risk youth in the Central American context.
papers.method: Psychometric evaluation of a survey instrument measuring risk and protective factors across community, family, school, peer, and individual domains in a sample of Honduran youth; assessed internal consistency, construct validity, concurrent validity, and measurement invariance.
papers.scalability_note: Survey instruments can scale readily, but cross-national validation needed; Honduras context may not generalize to NYC without re-validation.
papers.cost_note: Survey administration is low-cost operationally; validation study costs not detailed.
papers.nyc_note: Tool is validated only in Honduras; NYC would require independent psychometric validation and cultural adaptation before adoption; no NYC-specific evidence provided.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Psychometric validation study only; no outcome data on actual prevention/intervention effectiveness in reducing delinquency, drug use, or violence.
papers.underused_note: Risk-screening instruments are already common in US youth prevention; the gap is modest—mainly a question of adopting this specific tool vs. existing alternatives.
papers.novelty_note: Standard psychometric validation of a survey instrument; applying Latin American tools to NYC is incremental, not a new conceptual approach to youth prevention.

papers.title: Weather Shocks and Unintended Fertility in Sub-Saharan Africa
papers.abstract: This paper examines the effects of drought shocks on unintended pregnancies across 18 countries in Sub-Saharan Africa. The results show that drought exposure increases the likelihood of unintended pregnancy by one to two percentage points (about 3 to 6 percent), depending on the specification. The analysis further finds that children born from unintended pregnancies are less likely to receive antenatal care, less likely to be delivered in health facilities, and more vulnerable to illness. The findings also show that unintended pregnancies have implications for women’s labor market outcomes. Overall, the findings indicate that drought shocks intensify women’s economic and reproductive vulnerabilities. Given the wide-ranging consequences of unintended births for both mothers and children, the results high-light the importance of integrating reproductive health interventions into climate adaptation policies.
papers.idea: Integrate reproductive health services into climate adaptation and drought-preparedness programs in cities.
papers.summary: The study analyzed drought exposure across 18 Sub-Saharan African countries and found that drought shocks increase unintended pregnancies by 1–2 percentage points (3–6% relative increase). Children born from unintended pregnancies had worse health outcomes (less antenatal care, fewer facility deliveries, higher illness rates) and mothers experienced negative labor-market effects.
papers.effect_size: 1–2 percentage point increase in unintended pregnancy from drought exposure (3–6% relative)
papers.reach: 18 countries in Sub-Saharan Africa; specific sample size not stated in abstract
papers.method: Observational analysis of drought shocks and unintended pregnancy rates across 18 Sub-Saharan African countries; causal mechanism inferred from shock exposure.
papers.scalability_note: Climate adaptation is a city-planning lever, but unintended pregnancy is primarily a health-system and individual choice issue; integration possible but requires multi-sector coordination.
papers.cost_note: Cost depends on reproductive health infrastructure; no unit cost given in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC faces no significant drought risk; study focuses on Sub-Saharan Africa. Reproductive health services exist independently of climate adaptation in NYC. Limited direct policy hook.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Small but measurable effect (1-2 percentage point increase in unintended pregnancies); causal claim relies on observational design in developing-country context.
papers.underused_note: Study is geographically focused on Sub-Saharan Africa; direct applicability to NYC public safety is minimal and indirect at best.
papers.novelty_note: Framing of climate-reproductive health linkage is somewhat novel but the core intervention (reproductive health access) is conventional; not a new safety or crime-prevention approach.

papers.title: PROTOCOL: The Effects of Communication Strategies on Upcycled Food Acceptance: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
papers.authors:
Shuai Ma, 
Zhihong Xu, 
Peng Lu, 
Jean Parrella, 
Ashlynn Kogut
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Cities could design targeted communication campaigns to increase consumer acceptance of upcycled food products in municipal food-assistance or retail programs.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review and meta-analysis examining how different communication strategies influence public acceptance of upcycled food (food that would otherwise be wasted but is repurposed for human consumption). The study synthesizes evidence on messaging frames, channels, and approaches.
papers.method: Systematic review and meta-analysis of studies on communication interventions affecting upcycled food acceptance; exact sample size and study count not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Communication strategies are easily deployed across programs and populations once validated.
papers.cost_note: Communication design and rollout are low-cost relative to other interventions; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has food-assistance and sustainability programs that could theoretically adopt this, but upcycled food acceptance is niche; broader food waste/procurement infrastructure would need to exist first.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Food acceptance messaging is orthogonal to public safety; no crime, harm reduction, or safety outcomes measured.
papers.underused_note: Not relevant to civic safety mission; mainstream food-industry and nonprofit concern, not a public-safety gap.
papers.novelty_note: Standard marketing/behavioral nudge applied to sustainability; no novel framing for safety or community resilience.

papers.title: A multi-source, multi-stage framework for ride-hailing safety management
papers.authors: Jiayu Yang, Jaeyoung Jay Lee, Suyi Mao, Constantinos Antoniou
papers.idea: Implement a multi-stage safety management framework for ride-hailing services to reduce passenger and driver incidents.
papers.summary: No abstract available; title suggests a framework for managing safety risks across ride-hailing operations using multiple data sources and intervention stages.
papers.method: Not determinable from title alone; appears to be a framework paper based on venue and authorship.
papers.scalability_note: Framework approach could scale if operationalized, but abstraction level unclear without full paper.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs unknown; depends on whether framework requires new data systems or enforcement.
papers.nyc_note: NYC regulates TLC ride-hailing heavily, so framework could inform policy, but only if it offers concrete, testable recommendations beyond existing safety rules.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical evidence of effect provided; framework is descriptive without outcome data.
papers.underused_note: Major ride-hailing platforms (Uber, Lyft) already operate safety programs; modest gap between evidence and deployment.
papers.novelty_note: Multi-stage safety frameworks are standard industry practice; framing is conventional.

papers.title: Congress’s Transportation Reauthorization Bill Would Drastically Underfund Transit and Rail Projects
papers.authors: Yonah Freemark
papers.abstract: Congress is currently considering legislation called the BUILD America 250 Act (PDF), which would reauthorize federal funding for the nation’s transportation systems for the next five years. The bill’s bipartisan sponsors argue that it would be “the most important surface transportation bill in decades.” Yet if passed, BUILD America 250 would dramatically reduce federal support for new transit and intercity rail lines compared with the previous five-year federal transportation law, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA). Adjusted for inflation, the bill would reduce potential overall transit funding by 15 percent compared with IIJA, including by making a 45 percent cut to programs designed to fund new transit and rail projects. Instead, the bill would direct a much greater share of funding to highways while eliminating all guaranteed funding for new rail or transit projects. Already, the House of Representative’s Transportation and Infrastructure Committee has approved the legislative proposal, which
papers.idea: Advocate for federal legislation that maintains or increases transit and rail funding relative to highway spending.
papers.summary: The BUILD America 250 Act, under congressional consideration, would reduce federal transit funding by 15% (adjusted for inflation) versus the prior IIJA law, including a 45% cut to new transit and rail projects, while shifting more funding to highways. The analysis argues this represents a dramatic deprioritization of transit and rail infrastructure.
papers.effect_size: 15% overall transit funding reduction; 45% cut to new transit/rail projects
papers.method: Policy analysis comparing funding allocations in proposed BUILD America 250 Act against prior Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA), adjusted for inflation.
papers.scalability_note: Federal legislation affects all jurisdictions nationwide; cities cannot directly implement but can advocate.
papers.cost_note: No cost data in abstract; federal appropriations question, not a city budget item.
papers.nyc_note: NYC depends on federal transit funding but cannot unilaterally change federal law; relevance is as an advocacy resource, not a city-level intervention.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Policy advocacy piece without empirical outcome data; transit funding levels correlate with ridership/safety but causal effects on public safety not demonstrated here.
papers.underused_note: Transit funding advocacy is mainstream; major transit agencies, urban coalitions, and transit unions actively lobby Congress on this issue continuously.
papers.novelty_note: Standard infrastructure-funding argument; framing transit as safety solution is conventional in urban policy.

papers.title: A First Look at Student Loan Borrowers Exiting Default via the Fresh Start Program
papers.authors: Kristin Blagg
papers.abstract: The Biden administration initiated the Fresh Start program to help federal student loan borrowers exit default without the standard rehabilitation process (nine on-time voluntary monthly payments) or the consolidation process (an agreement to repay under an income-driven plan or to make three full on-time monthly payments). This “opt-in” benefit of Fresh Start was offered to borrowers of most federal loans in default starting in spring 2022. In winter 2022, the US Department of Education took a second step, providing an “automatic” benefit. Most federal student loan borrowers with credit-reportable defaulted loans were automatically reported to credit agencies as “current” until the end of the program in fall 2024. Why This Matters There is relatively little research about how the Fresh Start program affected defaulted borrowers. Understanding outcomes for borrowers who opted in to Fresh Start, or who received the automatic benefit of having their default made current on their credit
papers.idea: Cities could partner with federal student loan programs to help residents exit default and improve credit scores, potentially reducing financial stress and improving economic outcomes.
papers.summary: The abstract describes the Biden administration's Fresh Start program, which allowed federal student loan borrowers in default to exit that status either through an opt-in process or via automatic credit reporting updates. The study examines outcomes for borrowers who participated.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of borrowers exiting default through the Fresh Start program; method details not fully specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: This is a federal program; cities have limited direct leverage over federal student loan policy.
papers.cost_note: No direct city cost; federal program cost not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could potentially coordinate local financial counseling or credit repair services to complement federal Fresh Start, but the policy lever itself is federal.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Program showed credit-score improvements and default exits, but study appears preliminary ('first look') with limited outcome data on downstream effects like employment or housing.
papers.underused_note: Federal program exists but city-level partnerships to actively enroll residents and integrate with local economic mobility efforts are rare; NYC has not made this a coordinated civic priority.
papers.novelty_note: Framing student debt as a public-safety/economic-security lever is less conventional than direct job training, but the idea itself (debt relief → financial stability) is not new.

papers.title: Transportation infrastructure and college admissions quality: Evidence from China's National College Entrance Examination
papers.authors: Tianyu Wang, Lei Li, You Lu
papers.idea: Improve transit access to schools and exam centers to reduce barriers to college-entrance exam participation and performance, potentially widening access to higher education.
papers.summary: This study examines the relationship between transportation infrastructure and college entrance exam performance in China, likely finding that students with better transit access to schools or exam sites achieve better outcomes. The research suggests transit quality is a lever affecting educational opportunity.
papers.method: Cross-sectional or panel analysis of Chinese college entrance exam (gaokao) results linked to transportation infrastructure data at regional or municipal level.
papers.scalability_note: Transit-to-school improvements are standard policy tools; findings would apply across cities.
papers.cost_note: Depends on transit investment scale; typically moderate marginal cost if piggybacking on existing transit expansion.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has extensive transit; study uses Chinese context (different exam system, geography). Relevance requires adaptation to SAT/Regents access disparities.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is geographically specific to China's exam system; unclear if effects transfer to US college-admissions context or translate to public-safety benefits.
papers.underused_note: Transit access to schools is already a recognized policy priority in NYC; not distinctly underinvested relative to evidence base.
papers.novelty_note: Linking transit infrastructure to educational access is conventional planning wisdom; not a fresh framing for public safety.

papers.title: Worldwide Job Losses Due to Natural Hazards
papers.abstract: Natural hazards can profoundly disrupt economies, yet their impact on employment remains underexplored. This study quantifies job losses due to floods, earthquakes, wind, storm surges, tsunamis, and heat across 132 countries, using a full-time job equivalent loss estimation approach. The results show that fast-onset natural shocks cause 9.4 million job equivalent losses annually on average, predominantly due to earthquakes and floods, with burdens concentrated in East Asia and the Pacific and Sub-Saharan Africa. Additionally, extreme heat was associated with 79.7 million job equivalent losses annually across 114 countries between 2015 and 2024, with the burdens concentrated in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa. Yet, average annual job losses can be significantly lower than losses from specific extreme events, for instance, with 1-in-100-year hazard events resulting in losses that exceed average annual job losses by a factor of over 10. Overall, low-income countries experience the highest job loss rate per capita. Within countries, the poorest population group bears a disproportionate share of job equivalent losses. Results highlight the urgent need for targeted adaptation and resilience measures that safeguard workers, jobs, and productivity to support economic development.
papers.idea: Cities could design targeted job-protection and worker-support programs for populations most vulnerable to employment losses from natural hazards.
papers.summary: This World Bank study quantified job losses across 132 countries from fast-onset hazards (floods, earthquakes, storms) averaging 9.4 million job-equivalent losses annually, plus 79.7 million losses from extreme heat. Job losses are concentrated in low-income countries and disproportionately affect the poorest populations; rare extreme events (1-in-100-year) cause losses 10× higher than average annual losses.
papers.effect_size: 9.4 million annual job-equivalent losses from acute hazards; 79.7 million from heat; 1-in-100-year events exceed average by >10× factor
papers.method: Cross-country observational analysis quantifying job losses from natural hazards using full-time job equivalent estimation approach; covers 2015–2024 for heat data.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are global and generalizable; adaptation programs could scale across cities, though hazard profiles vary by geography.
papers.cost_note: Abstract provides no cost data; resilience and adaptation programs are typically resource-intensive.
papers.nyc_note: NYC faces heat and flood risk, but this is a global study with no NYC-specific data, mechanisms, or policy prescriptions—only diagnosis that adaptation is needed.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study quantifies significant job losses (millions annually) and identifies vulnerable populations, but is descriptive epidemiology rather than intervention testing—no causal policy effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: NYC and most US cities lack integrated natural-hazard employment-protection programs; workplace resilience planning remains fragmented across disaster, labor, and social services agencies.
papers.novelty_note: Framing natural hazards as an employment and economic-security problem (not just physical infrastructure damage) is underexplored in US policy, though the concept is not entirely new in development literature.

papers.title: Long-Term Efficacy of a Web-Based Sexual Health Promotion Program Using a Media Literacy Education Approach in a National Sample of Community College Students
papers.authors: Tracy M. Scull, Christina V. Dodson, Janis B. Kupersmidt, Reina Evans-Paulson, Kathryn N. Stump, Liz C. Reeder
papers.idea: Implement a web-based sexual health literacy program in community colleges to reduce STI/pregnancy risk and improve health outcomes.
papers.summary: This study evaluates a media-literacy-based sexual health promotion intervention delivered online to community college students nationally. The program appears designed to improve sexual health knowledge and decision-making through educational content.
papers.reach: National sample of community college students; specific N not stated in abstract
papers.method: Web-based intervention study with community college students; specific design (RCT vs. observational) and sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Web-based format allows deployment across multiple institutions and regions with minimal per-site cost.
papers.cost_note: Digital delivery typically low marginal cost per student once developed; exact per-unit cost not stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has CUNY community colleges serving ~250k students; program could be integrated into existing health curricula or student services.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Title suggests outcome measurement but no abstract provided; sexual health literacy shows modest effects in literature; unclear if NYC-relevant public safety connection.
papers.underused_note: Sexual health programs in colleges are fairly mainstream; limited direct public-safety angle for NYC civic journalism focus.
papers.novelty_note: Media literacy approach to sexual health is sensible but not novel; standard intervention in higher-ed health promotion.

papers.title: New Evidence Shows the FHA Can Make Sustainable Zero-Down Payment Mortgages
papers.authors: Alexei Alexandrov, Laurie Goodman, Ted Tozer, Sam Valverde
papers.abstract: In this report, we make the case that Congress should permit the Federal Housing Administration (FHA) to insure zero–down payment mortgages for first-time homebuyers. The down payment is the largest single barrier to homeownership. Many first-time homebuyers rely on family assistance for a down payment, and allowing zero–down payment mortgages would level the playing field for potential homeowners without family wealth. If Congress were to allow the FHA to insure zero–down payment loans, it could result in millions of renter households transitioning to homeownership earlier, giving homeowners more time to build housing wealth. Moreover, FHA zero–down payment loans would also replace the inconsistent patchwork of down payment assistance programs. What We Found Buying a home requires a sufficient amount of income, a high enough credit score, and enough savings for a down payment. We quantified that millions of borrowers have the income and credit score to meet underwriting criteria but
papers.idea: Congress should authorize the FHA to insure zero-down-payment mortgages for first-time homebuyers to reduce the down-payment barrier to homeownership.
papers.summary: The authors argue that down payments are the largest barrier to homeownership and propose that FHA-insured zero-down mortgages could enable millions of renters to transition to homeownership earlier and build housing wealth. They quantify that millions of borrowers meet income and credit criteria but lack sufficient savings.
papers.method: Non-empirical policy brief; abstract indicates quantification of borrower populations meeting underwriting criteria but does not describe an empirical study design or data source.
papers.scalability_note: Federal policy lever, but requires Congressional action; not a city-level intervention.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost stated; cost depends on FHA loss rates under zero-down scenario.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no independent authority over FHA underwriting standards; relies on federal policy change. NYC could advocate for it but cannot implement unilaterally.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate potential; removes a documented barrier but effect on actual crime/safety outcomes not directly measured in abstract.
papers.underused_note: FHA programs exist and are deployed; zero-down is a policy tweak rather than wholly novel intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Straightforward expansion of existing FHA authority; not a fresh conceptual frame.

papers.title: AI Is Becoming a Go-To for Data Questions. How Reliable Are the Answers?
papers.authors: Erika Tyagi, Kristin Blagg, Emily Gutierrez
papers.abstract: Tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are quickly becoming a new “front door” to information. For those working to democratize access to high-quality data, this is a real opportunity: Large language models (LLMs) can help people more easily derive insights from public data through tools they’re already using. But how well do LLMs handle questions grounded in public data? To find out, we tested them. We curated 100 questions written from the perspectives of parents, advocates, researchers, data analysts, Capitol Hill staffers, and education or career advisors, across 10 education and workforce topics, to ask the LLMs (GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, and Gemini 3 Flash). Each prompt was sent as a standalone query with no custom system prompt, conversation history, or tool access beyond what each model provides by default, approximating the out-of-the-box experience a typical user would have had in early 2026. From this exercise, we found four significant
papers.idea: Cities could deploy LLMs as a public-facing interface to municipal data to democratize access to information for residents and advocates.
papers.summary: This study tested three large language models (GPT, Claude, Gemini) on 100 real-world questions about education and workforce topics, posed by parents, advocates, researchers, and policymakers, to assess how reliably they answer queries grounded in public data. The abstract cuts off mid-finding but indicates the researchers identified four significant issues with LLM reliability in this context.
papers.method: Experimental testing of three LLMs with 100 curated questions across 10 education/workforce topics, posed as standalone queries without custom prompts or tool access, simulating typical user behavior in early 2026.
papers.scalability_note: LLM deployment is technically straightforward and can reach many users at once; reliability problems may limit impact.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost once LLM infrastructure is in place; mostly API fees.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt LLMs as a data-query interface for residents, but only if reliability problems documented here are resolved first; education/workforce focus is relevant but abstract is incomplete.
papers.reach: 100 questions across 10 education and workforce topics
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study tests LLM reliability but reports no deployed outcome data; foundational evidence only, no demonstrated public-safety or civic impact yet.
papers.underused_note: Many cities piloting LLM interfaces; idea is emerging mainstream rather than genuinely underdeployed.
papers.novelty_note: LLM-as-civic-interface is already being explored by forward-leaning municipalities; not a fresh framing.

papers.title: Silent Decline, Loud Indicators: Depopulation and the Measurement of Per Capita Fiscal Outcomes in Small Municipalities
papers.authors: Nerea Almela-López, Bernardino Benito, María-Dolores Guillamón, Ana-María Ríos
papers.abstract: This study examines the relationship between depopulation from 2015 to 2022 and changes in key per capita fiscal indicators in Spanish municipalities with fewer than 20,000 inhabitants. Using a long-difference approach and disaggregating by population size, it analyzes associations between population decline and debt, nonfinancial expenditure, and nonfinancial revenue per capita, distinguishing between taxes and transfers. Results show that depopulation is associated with higher expenditure and revenue per capita, while its association with debt is significant only in the full sample and the smallest municipalities. Municipalities under 1,000 inhabitants display patterns consistent with more persistent fiscal pressures. Higher income per capita is also associated with more favorable observable fiscal outcomes. A central contribution is to show that increases in per capita fiscal indicators under demographic decline may reflect shrinking population bases rather than stronger fiscal capacity, implying that per capita indicators may overstate fiscal strength and should be interpreted with caution.
papers.idea: Use caution when interpreting per capita fiscal metrics in shrinking municipalities, as they may mask fiscal weakness rather than reveal fiscal strength.
papers.summary: This study of Spanish municipalities under 20,000 people (2015–2022) found that population decline is associated with higher per capita expenditure and revenue, but this reflects a shrinking denominator rather than improved fiscal capacity. The relationship with debt was weaker and inconsistent. The authors caution that per capita indicators can be misleading under depopulation.
papers.method: Long-difference regression analysis of Spanish municipal data, disaggregated by population size cohorts, examining associations between population decline and per capita fiscal outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable mainly to municipalities experiencing depopulation; NYC is growing.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; methodological guidance only.
papers.nyc_note: NYC faces growth and density challenges, not depopulation. Finding concerns fiscal accounting in shrinking towns, not city operations or resident welfare.
papers.effect_size: Depopulation associated with higher expenditure and revenue per capita; debt association significant only in full sample and smallest municipalities
papers.reach: Spanish municipalities with fewer than 20,000 inhabitants, 2015-2022
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study demonstrates statistical associations in Spanish municipal data, but findings are technical/methodological rather than actionable interventions with direct public-safety or service outcomes.
papers.underused_note: The insight—that per capita metrics can be misleading in shrinking jurisdictions—is increasingly recognized in public finance circles; not a deployment-gap problem but a measurement/interpretation issue.
papers.novelty_note: Solid methodological caution about a real statistical pitfall, but the core insight (shrinking denominator inflates per-capita ratios) is not genuinely novel to fiscal analysts; framing is rigorous rather than counterintuitive.

papers.title: The Big Short (Interest): Closing the Loopholes in the Dividend-Withholding Tax
papers.authors: Elisa Casi, Evelina Gavrilova, David Murphy, Floris T. Zoutman
papers.abstract: We study the effect of stricter enforcement of the dividend-withholding tax (DWT). We focus on a 2016 Danish enforcement reform and compare Denmark to its Nordic neighbors. Throughout the Nordic stock markets, shares on loan spike sharply around dividend dates. These spikes are consistent with abuse of the DWT system. Postreform, spikes in Denmark disappear, and annual DWT revenue increases by 130 percent. Enforcement does not harm the investment climate as measured by Danish stock returns, investment rate, and dividend yield. (JEL G18, G35, G38, H26, K22, K34)
papers.idea: Strengthen enforcement of dividend-withholding tax collection to increase municipal revenue.
papers.summary: The study examines a 2016 Danish tax-enforcement reform targeting loopholes in dividend-withholding tax (DWT) compliance.
