After stricter enforcement, spikes in share lending around dividend dates disappeared and annual DWT revenue increased 130%, with no measurable harm to stock returns, investment rates, or dividend yields.
papers.method: Difference-in-differences comparison of Denmark (post-reform 2016) against Nordic neighbors (control), analyzing share-lending patterns around dividend dates and revenue outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Tax enforcement mechanisms are country/state-level; replicable but requires coordination with federal IRS and state tax authorities.
papers.cost_note: Enforcement-only intervention; likely low upfront cost; generates revenue net of administrative costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no independent dividend-withholding tax authority; this is federal/state tax policy beyond municipal control.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Tax enforcement study with no public-safety relevance; outside civic safety scope.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable to public safety; is a tax policy matter for revenue agencies.
papers.novelty_note: Standard tax-enforcement intervention; no novel public-safety framing.

papers.title: The Interorganizational Effects of Reform Prosecution Declination Policies
papers.authors: Nicholas Goldrosen
papers.idea: Redesign prosecutor declination policies to improve coordination across criminal justice agencies.
papers.summary: Unable to assess. No abstract provided, so the study's specific findings, setting, and policy implications are unknown.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract available to determine design, data source, or sample.
papers.scalability_note: Title suggests it may be a case study or theoretical analysis of organizational effects; unclear if generalizable.
papers.cost_note: Policy coordination likely low-cost, but unknown without details.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a large prosecutor system (DA offices); declination policy is relevant, but applicability depends on study's actual scope and recommendations.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical results reported; title and idea are framing/design concepts without demonstrated outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Some progressive DAs adopt declination policies, but systematic interagency coordination mechanisms remain inconsistently implemented across jurisdictions.
papers.novelty_note: Framing declination as an interorganizational coordination tool is somewhat fresh; most debate focuses on individual prosecutorial discretion rather than systemic feedback loops.

papers.title: Effectiveness of Interventions to Reduce Carbon‐Emissions Within Secondary Healthcare: Systematic Review and Evidence and Gap Map
papers.authors:
Liz Shaw, 
Noreen Orr, 
Hassanat M. Lawal, 
Simon Briscoe, 
Xiaoyu Yan, 
Jo Thompson Coon, 
G. J. Melendez‐Torres, 
Clara Martin‐Pintado, 
Ruth Garside
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Hospitals and secondary healthcare facilities can reduce carbon emissions through operational and clinical interventions.
papers.summary: This is a systematic review and evidence gap map synthesizing research on interventions to reduce carbon emissions within secondary (hospital-based) healthcare settings. It maps existing evidence and identifies gaps in what works to lower healthcare sector carbon footprint.
papers.method: Systematic review and evidence gap map synthesizing published interventions targeting carbon reduction in secondary healthcare; Campbell Collaboration peer-reviewed methodology.
papers.scalability_note: Healthcare systems are common across cities; carbon-reduction practices in hospitals are readily transferable.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost disclosed; operational efficiencies often pay for themselves.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's hospital system (HHC, major private systems) could adopt reviewed interventions, but this is not a direct policy lever for city government and does not address immediate resident welfare.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Climate/emissions outcomes unrelated to public safety; no crime, safety, or civic resilience data presented.
papers.underused_note: Environmental sustainability in healthcare is a growing institutional priority, not a public-safety intervention gap.
papers.novelty_note: Standard sustainability framing; no novel connection to safety or social outcomes.

papers.title: From Trade War to Green Transition: Optimal Electric Vehicle Tariffs with Revenue-Funded Subsidies
papers.authors: Panle Jia Barwick, Jack Collison, Pinelopi K. Goldberg, Shanjun Li, Yucheng Wang
papers.abstract: We study the optimal design of trade and industrial policy when governments pursue environmental objectives alongside traditional welfare goals. Motivated by the global transition to electric vehicles (EVs) and growing concerns about competitiveness, energy security, and climate change, we develop a framework in which policymakers choose tariffs and domestic production subsidies to maximize welfare, defined as the sum of consumer surplus, domestic profits, environmental benefits, and tariff revenue. We combine a theoretical model of differentiated-product oligopoly with a structural demand model estimated using vehicle-level data from 13 countries that together account for the vast majority of global EV sales. Our central finding is that the optimal policy combines a moderate tariff on imported EVs with a subsidy to domestic EV production financed through tariff revenue. This policy substantially outperforms both outright protectionism and laissez-faire. Relative to current policies, it preserves consumer access to affordable EVs, accelerates fleet electrification, supports domestic producers, and remains budget-neutral. For the United States, the optimal policy more than doubles EV market share, generates over $45 billion in annual welfare gains, and avoids approximately 95 million tons of lifetime CO2 emissions. A key mechanism underlying these results is the pass-through of tariffs and subsidies to prices, which depends critically on demand curvature, product substitution, and market structure. More broadly, our results suggest that effective industrial policy requires careful attention to market structure and country-specific conditions, balancing consumer, producer, fiscal, and environmental objectives rather than adhering to ideological prescriptions.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt tariff-subsidy policies on imported versus domestically-produced electric vehicles to accelerate EV adoption and reduce emissions.
papers.summary: The study develops a theoretical and empirical framework to design optimal tariffs and subsidies for EV markets across 13 countries. The authors find that a moderate tariff on imported EVs paired with domestic EV production subsidies (revenue-neutral) outperforms both protectionism and laissez-faire, potentially doubling US EV market share and generating $45B in annual welfare gains.
papers.method: Theoretical oligopoly model combined with structural demand estimation using vehicle-level sales data from 13 countries representing most global EV sales.
papers.scalability_note: Tariff and trade policy are federal/national-level instruments, not municipal or city-level levers.
papers.cost_note: Study claims budget-neutral design, but tariff design and subsidy administration carry implementation costs; no per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no authority over tariffs or international trade policy; these are federal/national instruments. EV incentives exist but at state/federal level.
papers.effect_size: More than doubles EV market share and generates over $45 billion in annual welfare gains for the United States
papers.reach: 13 countries accounting for vast majority of global EV sales
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical model with structural estimation shows substantial effects (doubled EV share, $45B welfare gains, 95M tons CO2 avoided), but these are simulated policy counterfactuals, not real-world experimental results.
papers.underused_note: EV incentives exist widely (federal tax credits, state rebates) but tariff-subsidy bundles specifically designed via this optimization framework are not standard municipal policy; moderate deployment gap.
papers.novelty_note: Framing tariff-subsidy coordination as welfare-maximizing trade policy is somewhat fresh, but EV subsidies and protectionist tariffs are each conventional; the bundled optimization is incrementally novel.

papers.title: Environmental Threat and the Nation: Earthquake Risk, Distributive Priority, and Expressive Attachment
papers.authors: Hector Galindo-Silva
papers.abstract: This paper studies how long-run earthquake risk shapes national identity, separating a distributive margin (national membership as a rule for allocating scarce resources) from an expressive margin (pride, willingness to fight, and affective attachment). Linking World Values Survey respondents (1981-2022; 63 countries, 494 subnational regions) to subnational seismic-risk geography, I find that people living closer to high-risk zones express stronger national in-group orientation: more pride, more willingness to fight, and more priority for nationals when jobs are scarce. Family attachment and out-group hostility do not rise, while religiosity increases in parallel. The expressive margin is conditional: the pride response is pronounced where state-religion alignment and a cohesive religious field lend the symbolic infrastructure to cast disaster as a shared national ordeal, and indistinguishable from zero where they do not. A complementary design exploiting earthquakes between adjacent survey waves finds no average short-run response, yet the response it does detect concentrates among older, place-attached residents who cannot leave -- consistent with attitudes tracking a chronic, inescapable risk rather than single events. Together, the results point to a demand-side origin of national attachment: where a covariate shock would overwhelm local and family insurance, people turn to larger communities of protection and meaning -- the nation and religion -- a logic I formalize in a simple social-interaction model.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: The study examines how living in earthquake-risk zones correlates with stronger national identity and in-group preference, particularly pride and prioritization of nationals for jobs. The effect appears mediated by state-religion alignment and is driven by chronic risk exposure rather than acute earthquake events.
papers.method: Observational analysis linking World Values Survey respondents (1981–2022; 63 countries, 494 subnational regions) to seismic-risk geography, with a supplementary quasi-experimental design exploiting earthquakes between survey waves.
papers.scalability_note: Fundamentally a study of how a fixed environmental hazard shapes national attachment; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Study is theoretical/observational; no intervention cost applies.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is not in a seismic risk zone at the scale studied; findings describe attachment to nation/religion, not a city policy lever.
papers.effect_size: Stronger national in-group orientation (more pride, willingness to fight, priority for nationals in job allocation) in high-risk earthquake zones; effect conditional on state-religion alignment
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is descriptive social science with no public-safety intervention or causal policy lever tested.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention; no deployment pathway exists.
papers.novelty_note: Interesting link between environmental risk and national identity, but not a novel safety or crime-reduction mechanism.

papers.title: The Topology of Cyberspace and Cybercrime Journeys: A Framework for Analyzing Online Offender Mobility
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework for analyzing online offender mobility. Drawing on multidisciplinary insights, we extend two established criminological models for studying offline offending—the geometry of crime and crime journeys—by adapting their core concepts to cyberspace and developing a topology in which the cyber place is defined and becomes the unit of analysis. Just as offenders travel before, during, and after committing crime offline, we argue they also undertake trips before, during, and after committing cybercrime, and that like offline journeys these cybercrime journeys comprise identifiable and measurable components. We further distinguish between human journeys (what offenders perceive) and data journeys (what happens to the information they transmit). We explicitly demonstrate how concepts from the geometry of crime and crime journeys translate from offline to online crime and mobility. The proposed approach enables the systematic formulation of research questions and the measurement of behavioral patterns, facilitating the generation and accumulation of knowledge on cybercrime offending. The paper illustrates the framework’s theoretical relevance within environmental criminology and its practical application for cybercrime analysis through concrete examples, including an empirical human journey into web hacking as a proof-of-concept.
papers.idea: Adapt environmental criminology frameworks to map cybercriminal pathways and identify intervention points in online infrastructure.
papers.summary: This paper presents a theoretical framework extending offline criminology models (geometry of crime, crime journeys) to cyberspace, proposing a topology of cyber places as units of analysis. It distinguishes between human journeys (offender perception) and data journeys (information flow) in cybercrime, with a proof-of-concept case study in web hacking.
papers.method: Theoretical framework development with illustrative examples and one empirical case study of web hacking journeys; no explicit sample size or evaluation data reported.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is conceptual/methodological; applicability depends on downstream empirical validation and translation into cyber-defense practices that cities could operationalize.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost not addressed; framework development itself is low-cost, but operational deployment would require integration into cybercrime units.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited direct control over cybercrime enforcement (FBI, state AG, private sector lead); framework could inform NYPD cyber units or city IT security, but is not an actionable city policy lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Proof-of-concept framework with one illustrative example; no rigorous outcome evaluation or demonstrated intervention success.
papers.underused_note: Cybercrime analysis exists but environmental criminology lens is emerging; unclear deployment in NYC infrastructure or policing practice.
papers.novelty_note: Fresh adaptation of offline environmental criminology to cyber domain; human vs. data journey distinction is genuinely creative framing.

papers.title: When AI Says "I have been in similar situations": Synthetic Lived Experience in Peer-Like Caregiver Support
papers.authors: Drishti Goel, Violeta J. Rodriguez, Daniel S. Brown, Ravi Karkar, Dong Whi Yoo, Koustuv Saha
papers.abstract: Caregivers often turn to online communities for informational and emotional support. In these spaces, peer supporters frequently draw on personal narratives to respond to emotionally complex caregiving situations. As LLMs are increasingly designed as peer-like sources of support, they introduce a critical tension: AI can provide immediate, private, and nonjudgmental support, but it cannot authentically possess the lived experiences that make human peer support meaningful. Yet, when prompted to sound peer-like, LLMs may generate language that implies lived experience. This creates a synthetic lived experience paradox: the same experiential language that may make AI support feel warm, relatable, and peer-like can also falsely position the system as someone with lived experience. We examine this paradox in the context of family caregivers of people living with Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD). Drawing on caregiver support exchanges from online communities and prompted peer-like responses from three LLMs -- LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, and MedGemma -- we analyze how human peers use personal narratives and how AI incorporates similar narrative forms. Psycholinguistic analysis shows that peer responses used significantly more first-person and past-focused language than peer-like AI responses. Qualitatively, we identify seven types of personal narratives in human peer support and show that AI often captures their emotional work, but can fabricate experiential grounding. These findings reveal a narrative authenticity gap: peer-like AI can generate synthetic lived experience without the real experience that makes peer support meaningful. We argue that caregiver-support AI systems need mechanisms to distinguish supportive peer-like framing from fabricated lived experience, ensuring that models can offer warmth and validation without falsely positioning themselves as experiential peers.
papers.idea: Develop design guidelines for AI peer-support systems to distinguish authentic peer support from fabricated lived experience, enabling cities to responsibly deploy AI caregiving assistants.
papers.summary: The study analyzed how human peer supporters and three LLMs (LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, MedGemma) respond to Alzheimer's caregivers in online communities. It found that peer-like AI can generate emotionally supportive language that mimics lived experience without authentic grounding, creating a "narrative authenticity gap." The researchers call for mechanisms to ensure AI caregiving systems offer warmth and validation without falsely positioning themselves as experiential peers.
papers.method: Psycholinguistic analysis of caregiver exchanges from online communities and prompted LLM responses; qualitative coding of seven narrative types in human peer support versus AI responses; no stated sample size.
papers.scalability_note: Design principles could inform multiple AI systems, but requires case-by-case implementation and user-testing.
papers.cost_note: Primarily a design/training issue for existing LLM platforms; minimal marginal cost once guidelines developed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could incorporate these principles into caregiving AI deployed via public health programs or DOHMH digital health initiatives, but the study offers no specific policy mechanism or local proof of concept.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Observational/analytical study identifying a design risk rather than demonstrating an intervention with measured outcomes; no randomized trial or deployed system evaluation.
papers.underused_note: AI peer-support design ethics rarely operationalized in NYC health/social systems; no established guidelines for distinguishing authentic vs. synthetic lived experience in deployed caregiving AI.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes AI caregiving as an authenticity problem rather than capability problem; 'synthetic lived experience paradox' is a conceptually fresh lens on LLM peer-support design.

papers.title: Understanding Scam Trends and Rail Paths from Reddit Self-Disclosure Narratives
papers.authors: Yangjun Zhang, Mirko Bottarelli, Mark Hooper, Carsten Maple
papers.abstract: Online scam behavior is inherently multi-stage, and the lifecycle includes temporally ordered rails and events rather than isolated signals. Existing works analyze characteristics of scam types and rails, but they do not track scam trends across years. Moreover, the work on the relations between rails is hampered due to the lack of open-source datasets with annotations and coverage of different scam types. To address these gaps, we build a dataset to analyze the yearly trend of scam characteristics and rail paths using Reddit self-disclosure narratives from 2023 to 2025. We collect 21,304 posts from scam-related subreddits with at least one rail among identity, communication, platform, and payment for trend analysis by heuristic annotation. Then, we label 1,800 posts containing explicit or recoverable scam chains by an LLM-assisted method for scam path analysis. The method is evaluated with human annotation. Lastly, we run a topic model on the comments of the posts to analyze the community support behavior. The results reveal that scam processes are predominantly multi-rail. Across years, different scam types and rail components dominate. Different scam types vary systematically in path complexity. Reddit support behaviors have become more detailed over time. This work supports synthetic scam chain data simulation and AI-related scam risk assessment, though findings may not generalise to other platforms.
papers.idea: Use Reddit scam narratives and LLM-assisted analysis to identify emerging fraud patterns and alert consumer-protection agencies and residents to new scam tactics.
papers.summary: The study analyzed 21,304 Reddit posts from 2023–2025 to map scam trajectories across identity, communication, platform, and payment stages. Results show that scams predominantly involve multiple interconnected stages (rails), with different scam types and dominant components varying year-to-year, and community support discussions becoming more detailed over time.
papers.method: Analysis of Reddit self-disclosure narratives using heuristic annotation for trend analysis (21,304 posts) and LLM-assisted labeling of scam chains (1,800 posts), with human validation and topic modeling of community comments.
papers.scalability_note: Method relies on Reddit data; generalizability beyond Reddit unclear; requires ongoing data collection and LLM infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Moderate cost: LLM annotation at scale, data infrastructure, and human validation needed; no per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: Paper offers pattern-detection infrastructure but no concrete NYC consumer-protection policy, enforcement, or resident intervention. Would require downstream translation into alerts, education, or fraud task-force guidance.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive dataset and trend analysis; no demonstrated causal effect on fraud reduction, prevention, or consumer outcomes.
papers.underused_note: LLM-assisted fraud-pattern detection is emerging but inconsistently integrated into consumer-protection workflows; NYC/state agencies have limited real-time scam-alert infrastructure.
papers.novelty_note: Using Reddit narratives + LLM to map scam lifecycles and rails is methodologically fresh; framing fraud as multi-stage pathways (not isolated incidents) is moderately novel for consumer protection.

papers.title: Exploring the mobilities of Kenyan older people living in informal settlements using a capability approach
papers.abstract: Despite significant discussions on transport inequalities in African cities, older people's mobility needs remain under-researched. To gain a deeper understanding of such issues, this study focuses on older people living in Kenyan informal settlements and uses the capability approach to investigate their options for mobility, activities and locations, barriers, coping strategies and unfulfilled mobility needs. The study employs a qualitative approach comprising four focus groups with 24 participants and a follow-on community engagement workshop with people aged above 50 years old living in the informal settlement of Kibera in Nairobi. The results indicate that older people face significant challenges in their everyday mobility. Living with poor health conditions, level of poverty, lack and poor quality of walking infrastructure, personal safety and ageist behaviour are the main factors shaping and affecting their mobility. The capability approach offers a framework to understand older people’s mobility practices and implications for wellbeing. It enables an analysis of how mobility is shaped by their needs, constraints and decision-making processes, and how this translates into participation in life. It also offers an exploration of how mobility enables and supports a wider capability set, outlining its multidimensional social impacts.
papers.idea: Improve walking infrastructure and safety measures in low-income neighborhoods to enable older residents to move independently.
papers.summary: A qualitative study of 24 older residents in Kibera, Nairobi found that poor health, poverty, inadequate walking infrastructure, personal safety concerns, and ageism significantly constrain mobility. The capability approach framework identified how these barriers limit older people's participation in daily activities and community life.
papers.reach: 24 participants in focus groups; 1 informal settlement (Kibera, Nairobi)
papers.method: Qualitative study with four focus groups (24 participants aged 50+) and a community engagement workshop in an informal settlement in Nairobi, Kenya.
papers.scalability_note: Single settlement case study with no tested intervention or replicable program model; limited generalizability even within Kenya.
papers.cost_note: Infrastructure improvements (sidewalks, lighting, safety features) have moderate per-capita costs depending on scope and local conditions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has extensive sidewalk and transit infrastructure; findings from informal Nairobi settlement have limited direct applicability, though older-adult mobility barriers may overlap.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study identifying barriers; no intervention tested or outcome measured.
papers.underused_note: Pedestrian/accessibility infrastructure is standard planning practice in US cities, though often underfunded for low-income areas; the age-equity framing is less common.
papers.novelty_note: Capability approach applied to older-adult mobility is thoughtful; the focus on informal settlements is less relevant to NYC, but elderly pedestrian safety via infrastructure is somewhat fresh.

papers.title: Improving Capstone Team Outcomes through Dynamic Skill Matching and Preference Alignment
papers.authors: Brandon Pardi, Garret Castro, Michael Pisman, Avash Adhikari, Santosh Chandrasekhar
papers.abstract: Team-based projects are a cornerstone of engineering and computing courses, but unstructured team formation often leads to poor project outcomes due to misaligned student interests and inadequate skill coverage. This paper introduces a novel, three-stage methodology for creating effective student teams by integrating student preferences with project skill requirements. In the first stage, students complete a survey to report their project interests and self-assessed skills. Next, a Large Language Model (LLM) analyzes project descriptions to extract the necessary skills for each project's success. Finally, a dynamic assignment algorithm matches students to projects, simultaneously maximizing skill coverage and preference alignment. The algorithm iteratively prioritizes projects with unfulfilled skill needs to optimize team balance. Preliminary evaluations show our approach produces teams with higher skill coverage and better preference satisfaction compared to random or manual assignment approaches. Our approach also overcomes limitations of widely-used tools like CATME Team-Maker, which do not explicitly account for project skill fulfillment. Our findings point toward an effective and customizable strategy for improving student motivation and learning outcomes in project-based courses.
papers.idea: Use LLM-based skill-matching algorithms to assign students to capstone team projects in engineering and computing courses.
papers.summary: The paper presents a three-stage methodology that combines student surveys, LLM-extracted project skill requirements, and a dynamic matching algorithm to form student teams. Preliminary evaluations show the approach produces higher skill coverage and preference satisfaction than random or manual assignment, and outperforms the CATME Team-Maker tool.
papers.method: Descriptive/methodological paper introducing an algorithm; preliminary evaluation comparing assignment outcomes across methods (sample size and empirical details not stated in abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Algorithm is automatable and could be adopted across universities; requires minimal infrastructure beyond LLM access.
papers.cost_note: Minimal marginal cost once LLM infrastructure exists; primarily development and deployment overhead.
papers.nyc_note: Applicable only to university capstone programs within CUNY/NYU; no direct lever for city agencies or public safety, housing, transit, or other civic-policy domains.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Modest improvements in academic team composition; no evidence of public-safety or civic impact.
papers.underused_note: Educational tool optimization; not a public-safety or city-life intervention.
papers.novelty_note: LLM application to team-matching is incremental; the problem itself (team formation) is conventional.

papers.title: Co-Creating Buildable and Open Social Robot Study Companions with University Students
papers.authors: Farnaz Baksh, Matevž B. Zorec, Feiazie Baksh, Karl Kruusamäe
papers.abstract: Open-source social robots offer accessibility, repairability, and student empowerment, yet the build itself often presents a barrier. Existing platforms either ship pre-assembled, foreclosing hands-on learning, or expose students to unfamiliar fasteners, opaque wiring, and inaccessible service points that erode engagement. Whether targeted mechanical redesign can lower this barrier whilst maintaining structural integrity remains untested. Here we show that Design for Assembly (DfA) and Design for Disassembly (DfD) interventions reshape how a build feels before they shorten how long it takes. Working with university students in Guyana and Estonia, we applied the Double Diamond framework to co-create the Robot Study Companion (RSC) v4.1: mapping pain points, then redesigning its chassis around twist-lock fasteners, snap-fit joints, and tool-free service latches. Across two studies with developers and first-time builders, system usability climbed from Poor to Excellent (SUS 59.4 to 89.4), perceived workload trended downward (NASA-TLX 4.29 to 4.00), and mean assembly time trended downward (21.4 to 13.7 minutes, with juniors' learning effect), whilst orientation cues and navigation continuity for first-time builders emerged as the next documentation frontier. Perceived workload, not completion time, appears to govern whether students take up open hardware.
papers.idea: Redesign open-source social robots using Design for Assembly principles to improve student engagement in hands-on STEM learning.
papers.summary: The study applied design-for-assembly and design-for-disassembly interventions to an open-source social robot, redesigning fasteners and service points. Across two university cohorts in Guyana and Estonia, system usability improved from Poor to Excellent (SUS 59.4 to 89.4), perceived workload declined, and assembly time decreased from 21.4 to 13.7 minutes.
papers.effect_size: SUS score improved from 59.4 to 89.4 (30-point gain); assembly time decreased from 21.4 to 13.7 minutes (~36% reduction)
papers.reach: Two university cohorts (Guyana and Estonia); exact N not stated in abstract
papers.method: Co-design study using Double Diamond framework with university students in two settings, measuring usability (SUS), workload (NASA-TLX), and assembly time across developer and first-time builder groups.
papers.scalability_note: Open-hardware robot design is niche; limited applicability beyond academic STEM labs and maker communities.
papers.cost_note: Open-source platform; redesign involves engineering labor but no stated per-unit cost.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant only to after-school STEM programs, community maker spaces, or school robotics clubs; no direct city-policy lever or public-safety/civic-service connection.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate improvements in usability (SUS 59→89) and workload reduction in controlled academic setting; limited real-world public-safety applicability.
papers.underused_note: This is an educational robotics/STEM engagement study with no demonstrated connection to NYC public safety priorities; adoption depends on school district STEM policy, not civic safety innovation.
papers.novelty_note: Design for Assembly/Disassembly is established UX practice; application to open-source robots is incremental, not a new problem-solving framework.

papers.title: Demographic Patterns in Cybersecurity Culture: Insights from a Global Organisation Supporting Safety-Critical and Critical Infrastructure Sectors
papers.authors: Tita Alissa Bach, Amandine Kaiser
papers.abstract: This study investigates demographic differences in cybersecurity culture in a large global organisation supporting safety critical and critical infrastructure sectors to target CSC improvement. A global survey was administered to all internal and external employees of a total of 21148 employees, with 6502 responses. The questionnaire evaluates nine CSC dimensions such as Password Management, Governance, Email Use. Anonymous survey responses were analysed using Kruskal-Wallis tests and Dunns post hoc comparisons to identify differences across demographic variables including employment, recruitment paths, managerial role, gender, age, tenure, and work base. CSC was broadly consistent across the organisation, with statistically significant but small to moderate demographic effects. CSC variations were observed across employment, age, recruitment paths, and line managerial role. In general, fulltime, internal, permanent, older employees, Merge and Acquisition recruits, and line managers consistently scored higher across multiple CSC dimensions. Parttime, younger, external employees, and those with 6 to 20 years of tenure in general scored lower. These patterns highlight higher-scoring groups that may act as CSC carriers and lower-scoring groups that may benefit from tailored improvement measures, enabling organisational learning. Our study offers a practical, scalable way to assess CSC, generating meaningful insights despite industrial constraints. It enables organisations to benchmark maturity, identify gaps, and prioritise targeted improvements using workforce diversity as a guide.
papers.idea: Target cybersecurity culture training within city agencies to demographic groups (younger, part-time, external staff) identified as lower-scoring on security practices.
papers.summary: The study surveyed 6,502 employees across a global critical-infrastructure organization and found statistically significant but small-to-moderate demographic variations in cybersecurity culture. Full-time, older, internal, permanent employees and line managers scored higher on nine dimensions (password management, governance, email use); part-time, younger, and external staff scored lower.
papers.method: Cross-sectional anonymous survey of 21,148 employees (6,502 responses) with Kruskal-Wallis and Dunns post hoc tests comparing nine cybersecurity-culture dimensions across seven demographic variables in one large global organization.
papers.scalability_note: Survey methodology is portable; training interventions are standard. But effects are small and context-specific to one organization.
papers.cost_note: Mainly staff time for targeted training; no per-unit cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has critical infrastructure and city agencies; could adopt survey + targeted training. But abstract offers no evidence that demographic targeting improves outcomes or reduces actual breaches—only that groups differ in survey responses.
papers.effect_size: Small to moderate demographic effects on cybersecurity culture
papers.reach: 6,502 employees (from 21,148 total) in a global organisation
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Statistically significant but explicitly small-to-moderate effects; cybersecurity culture gaps unlikely to directly reduce physical public safety incidents.
papers.underused_note: Cybersecurity training is standard practice in most large organizations; not a gap-between-evidence-and-practice problem for NYC public safety.
papers.novelty_note: Demographic targeting of training is conventional HR practice; applying it to cybersecurity is routine, not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Hanging, strangulation and suffocation deaths of undetermined intent: hidden suicides on the rise in recent years in Argentina?
papers.authors: Carlos Marcelo Leveau
papers.abstract: Background In Argentina, a significant proportion of violent deaths are classified as being of undetermined intent (DUI). Hanging/strangulation/suffocation (HASU) accounted for 77% of suicides, while undetermined deaths (DUI) by HASU rose sharply during 2015–2023. This study aims to (1) identify spatial and spatiotemporal clusters of both mortality causes, (2) determine whether these clusters coincide geographically and (3) describe the overlap in their spatiotemporal distributions. Methods This is a retrospective quantitative study based on secondary data analysis. Spatial autocorrelation and space-time scan statistics techniques were used to detect spatial and spatiotemporal clusters of HASU-DUI and HASU-suicide in Argentina during 2015–2023. Results Low-suicide-risk clusters were primarily observed in Buenos Aires, Córdoba and southern Santa Fe provinces. During 2020–2023, the Greater Buenos Aires cluster expanded westward across the province. Concurrently, HASU-DUI exhibited spatial diffusion within Buenos Aires Province, with this spread occurring predominantly in regions characterised by low HASU-suicide rates. The spatiotemporal analysis identified distinct geographic clustering of HASU deaths (both suicides and DUI) across Argentina (2015–2023). We observed inverse spatial patterns—low-risk suicide clusters and high-risk DUI clusters expanded—with overlapping areas showing declining suicides and rising DUIs post-2020. Conclusions Our spatiotemporal analysis offers tentative indications of possible recent reclassification patterns between DUI and suicides in Argentina. These findings offer tentative indications that combining undetermined deaths by HASU (Y20) with official suicide data may provide a more complete mortality picture in Argentina, though this interpretation requires further investigation.
papers.idea: Implement reclassification protocols and standardized autopsy/coroner procedures to improve accuracy of suicide vs. undetermined-intent death coding, enabling better epidemiologic tracking and targeted prevention.
papers.summary: This study used spatial clustering analysis on Argentine mortality data (2015–2023) to identify geographic patterns in hanging/strangulation/suffocation deaths classified as either suicide or undetermined intent. It found inverse spatial patterns: low-suicide-risk clusters coincided with high undetermined-intent clusters, particularly after 2020, suggesting possible misclassification or reclassification drift rather than true epidemiologic shifts.
papers.method: Retrospective secondary data analysis using spatial autocorrelation and space-time scan statistics on national death registry data to detect and compare spatial/spatiotemporal clusters of two cause-of-death classifications.
papers.scalability_note: The findings are primarily diagnostic (reclassification patterns in one country); any intervention would require separate design and validation.
papers.cost_note: Implementing improved coroner/medical examiner protocols and training would require upfront investment; medium cost relative to other health/safety interventions.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has relatively standardized medical examiner procedures; the study's main value is flagging a data-quality issue in Argentina's system rather than proposing a tested NYC intervention.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies correlational patterns and reclassification hypotheses, but does not demonstrate that improved coding protocols would reduce deaths or improve outcomes; findings are suggestive rather than causal.
papers.underused_note: Standardized autopsy/coroner procedures exist in many jurisdictions but classification protocols vary widely; Argentina-specific findings have limited direct NYC applicability, though death-coding standardization is moderately underemphasized in US public-health policy.
papers.novelty_note: Reframing undetermined-intent deaths as a hidden epidemiologic signal and using spatiotemporal analysis to detect reclassification drift is analytically fresh, though standardization itself is conventional wisdom.

papers.title: Understanding Fear of Hate Crime: The Roles of Vulnerability, Identity, and Social Cohesion
papers.authors: Carlos M. Gonzales
papers.abstract: Understanding fear of crime requires attention to the interplay of identity, social context, and institutional trust. This study applies the vulnerability perspective to examine fear of hate crime—specifically crimes motivated by race, ethnicity, nationality, gender, and sexuality—as a distinct dimension of fear of crime during a period of heightened public awareness of identity-based violence. Using nationally collected data from the COVID-19 era, the study assesses how social and physical vulnerabilities and perceived neighborhood social cohesion shape fear of hate crime in comparison with fear of overall crime. Extending prior work that relies on identity-based proxies, this research incorporates measures of perceived social cohesion and direct experiences of hate-motivated victimization. Findings highlight both distinct and overlapping predictors of general and hate crime-specific fear, offering insight into how vulnerability and community context shape fear through partially divergent processes.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; study is descriptive analysis of fear correlates, not a tested program or policy lever.
papers.summary: This observational study examines how vulnerability, identity, and perceived neighborhood social cohesion shape fear of hate crime versus general crime during the COVID-19 era using national survey data. The research identifies distinct and overlapping predictors of general versus hate-crime-specific fear, emphasizing the roles of perceived social cohesion and direct victimization experiences.
papers.method: Cross-sectional observational analysis using nationally collected COVID-era survey data to assess associations between vulnerability measures, perceived social cohesion, victimization, and fear of hate crime.
papers.scalability_note: Study identifies predictors but proposes no intervention, program, or policy mechanism for cities to implement or scale.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; study is measurement/analysis, not a deployed intervention with cost data.
papers.nyc_note: Findings may inform understanding of NYC residents' fear of hate crime but do not identify a concrete program, policy, or practice NYC can adopt.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive study with no intervention tested; identifies correlates of fear but no causal mechanism or actionable outcome demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is pure descriptive analysis, not a deployable intervention or policy tool.
papers.novelty_note: Applies existing vulnerability framework to hate crime fear; adds social cohesion measure but incremental rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: Assessing The Real-World Economic Value of Weather Forecasts under Compounding Extremes : A Decision-Specific Framework
papers.abstract: Assessing the real-world economic value of weather forecasts remains challenging, particularly in the context of high-impact extreme events. Although meteorological skill has improved substantially in recent years—driven by steady advances in physics-based models and impressive breakthroughs in artificial intelligence-based forecasting—operational evaluations still focus primarily on standard skill metrics, with limited consideration of how improvements in meteorological skill translate into economic value. This study proposes a flexible framework to assess the economic value of weather forecasts, with penalty functions that explicitly account for compounding losses as well as declining user trust in cases of repeated false alarms. In addition, the framework allows for varying cost—loss ratios to represent heterogeneous prevention costs and vulnerability structures. The framework is applied to cities exposed to weather-related natural hazards, comparing the relative economic value of leading physics-based and data-driven forecasting systems from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts. The value of forecasts is highly sensitive to assumptions about compounding losses, penalty structures, and prevention costs, which often substantially alter conclusions drawn from meteorological skill alone. For instance, in some cities in Southern Europe, the higher sensitivity of the physics-based Integrated Forecast System high-resolution model (IFS HRES) makes it better suited when protection costs are small relative to potential losses, while the higher specificity of the data-driven Artificial Intelligence Forecasting System (AIFS) makes it better when protection costs are higher. These findings underscore the importance of evaluating economic value under realistic risk scenarios to ensure that improvements in predictive accuracy translate into meaningful societal and economic benefits.
papers.idea: Cities should adopt data-driven weather forecasting systems (AIFS) when prevention costs are high relative to potential losses, and physics-based systems (IFS HRES) when costs are low.
papers.summary: The study proposes a framework for assessing the economic value of weather forecasts by accounting for compounding losses and user trust erosion from false alarms. Applied to Southern European cities, it finds that the choice between physics-based and AI-driven forecasting systems should depend on the cost-loss ratio: physics-based models are more valuable when protection costs are low relative to disaster losses, while AI models are better when protection costs are high.
papers.reach: Multiple cities in Southern Europe (exact number not stated)
papers.method: Comparative framework analysis of two operational forecasting systems (IFS HRES and AIFS) applied to weather-exposed cities, using penalty functions to model compounding losses and false-alarm costs.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is generalizable across cities and forecasting systems; adoption requires only policy decision about which forecast product to prioritize.
papers.cost_note: No implementation costs detailed; likely low if cities already subscribe to forecast data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC faces hurricane and flooding risks; framework applicable to emergency preparedness. However, NYC already has sophisticated emergency systems; marginal value of switching forecast products unclear.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Framework is theoretically sound but demonstrates relative tool comparison, not a proven intervention reducing actual harm or costs in practice.
papers.underused_note: Weather forecasting systems are already widely deployed in major cities; the specific decision-rule innovation (cost-loss matching) has limited documented real-world adoption but isn't a gap in basic capability.
papers.novelty_note: Cost-loss ratio matching for model selection is a pragmatic reframing, but weather forecasting optimization itself is established; applies existing decision theory to an operational choice.

papers.title: Madeline Brown on Trump account enrollment systems
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Since the program began last summer, 6 million children have been enrolled in Trump accounts. But enrollment isn’t automatic, meaning millions of eligible children may miss out on this wealth-building opportunity. “If a program like this is really going to be as transformative as it has the potential to be, we have to focus on who already is not taking advantage of [early life wealth-building] programs,” said Madeline Brown, senior policy associate at the Urban Institute, in an interview with MarketWatch. “What we set up is an enrollment strategy system where they are still getting lost.” For inquiries, please email media@urban.org.
papers.idea: Redesign enrollment systems for child savings/wealth-building accounts to reduce friction and automatically enroll eligible children, increasing uptake among disadvantaged populations.
papers.summary: Since launching last summer, a Trump-account program enrolled 6 million children, but enrollment is not automatic, leaving millions of eligible children unenrolled. Urban Institute researcher Madeline Brown identifies enrollment systems as a barrier, highlighting that enrollment-strategy design is causing eligible families to be lost from the program.
papers.method: Interview-based commentary by a policy researcher at the Urban Institute; no original empirical study described.
papers.scalability_note: Enrollment-system design is easily replicable across jurisdictions; automatic enrollment is a proven, scalable mechanism.
papers.cost_note: Enrollment redesign is a low-cost intervention; leverages existing administrative infrastructure.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could pilot automatic enrollment in savings programs for eligible children; applies to any city-administered or city-aligned child wealth program.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No experimental evidence presented; relies on enrollment mechanics logic rather than demonstrated impact on outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Auto-enrollment is already standard practice in retirement savings (401k) and has gained traction in some state college savings programs; modest room for expansion but not neglected.
papers.novelty_note: Auto-enrollment is well-established behavioral economics principle; application to child savings accounts is straightforward, not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Breaching the Public Trust and Sentencing Canadian Police Officers: Addressing Rotten Apples, but not Rotten Barrels or Orchards
papers.authors: Danielle McNabb, Kate Puddister
papers.abstract: When a police officer commits a criminal offense, they not only violate the very laws they are legally entrusted to uphold, but in doing so, they also violate the public’s trust. In Canada, the importance of public trust is recognized through the criminal offense of “breach of trust by a public officer,” and as an aggravating factor in sentencing. As there is a significant dearth of research on police criminality and on the sentencing of police officers in Canada, we present an empirically grounded examination of 93 sentencing decisions between 1990 and 2024, involving over 100 police officers convicted for breaches of trust. The study sheds light on the nature of police-perpetrated breaches of trust, and on the ways in which courts sentence police-offenders in Canada. Our study underscores that although sentencing is an important element of police accountability, it does little to address the systemic aspects of police criminality.
papers.idea: Not applicable—study is descriptive analysis of sentencing patterns, not an intervention or policy lever.
papers.summary: This is a descriptive empirical study of 93 Canadian sentencing decisions (1990–2024) involving over 100 police officers convicted of breach of trust. The authors document the nature and sentencing outcomes of these cases and conclude that sentencing alone, while important for accountability, does not address systemic or organizational drivers of police misconduct.
papers.reach: 93 sentencing decisions, over 100 Canadian police officers, 1990–2024
papers.method: Empirical analysis of Canadian court sentencing decisions over a 34-year period, examining case characteristics and sentencing outcomes in breach-of-trust cases involving police.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are context-specific to Canadian criminal law and sentencing frameworks; direct transfer to U.S. jurisdictions requires substantial adaptation.
papers.cost_note: No new program or intervention proposed; cost of research only.
papers.nyc_note: Study focuses on Canadian sentencing law and case outcomes; insights on systemic accountability are general but the study itself identifies no NYC-implementable practice or policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive analysis only; no intervention tested or causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Sentencing and judicial accountability are established (if imperfect) mechanisms; study documents existing practice rather than proposing novel deployment.
papers.novelty_note: Frames police accountability as individual sentencing vs. systemic reform—a useful analytical distinction, though the 'rotten barrel' metaphor is not entirely new.

papers.title: 2026 DC Election Briefing Book: What Mayoral and Council Candidates Need to Know
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: For the first time in 12 years, DC residents will elect a new mayor. They’ll vote in at least three new members of the DC Council. And, for the first time ever, residents will use ranked-choice voting in the June primaries. This momentous election cycle comes amid a period of economic uncertainty for DC: tens of thousands of workers have lost jobs in the past year, and the District continues to face challenges balancing its budget. Residents with lower incomes are struggling to afford their rent, child care, utility bills, health insurance, and other essentials. Created by the Urban-Greater DC initiative, this briefing book offers candidates, voters, and journalists critical background information and the latest evidence on some of the most important issues facing DC, including early education, housing, the labor market, and public safety. In the resources below, we share what policymakers need to know to confront inequities and
papers.idea: Not applicable—this is a briefing book for candidates, not a concrete intervention or policy.
papers.summary: This is a candidate briefing document for DC's 2026 mayoral and council elections, created by the Urban Institute to synthesize evidence on key issues (early education, housing, labor market, public safety, etc.) facing the district during economic uncertainty. It does not propose or test a specific intervention.
papers.method: Literature synthesis and evidence compilation; no original empirical study.
papers.scalability_note: Briefing books are jurisdiction-specific and informational; not a scalable program or policy intervention.
papers.cost_note: Production cost is modest; however, this is not a service delivery or capital program.
papers.nyc_note: Some DC evidence may transfer to NYC contexts (housing, safety, labor), but the document targets DC politics and is not NYC-specific.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Not an intervention study; briefing document with no direct effect measurement.
papers.underused_note: Candidate briefing books are standard election-cycle practice; not a policy gap.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional informational product for electoral cycle; no new methodology or insight.

papers.title: Descriptive epidemiological profile of road traffic injuries in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations in South Australia
papers.authors: Lavender Otieno, Patrick Sharpe, Simranjeet Singh Dahia, Brett Shannon, Holger Moeller, Marnie Campbell, Bobby Porykali, Sadia Hossain, Nicole Kelly, Daniel Y Ellis, Jodi Gray, Hossein Afzali, Rebecca Q Ivers, Courtney Ryder
papers.abstract: Background Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities experience a disproportionate burden of road traffic injuries yet little work has been done to develop a comprehensive epidemiological profile. Methods We analysed data from the South Australian Trauma Registry for road traffic injuries in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults (≥18 years) admitted to hospital between July 2018 and June 2023. Descriptive statistics summarised demographics and hospital presentation characteristics, and an ordinal logistic regression examined the association between length of stay and injury severity scores (ISSs). Indigenous research methodologies guided study design and interpretation. Results A total of 127 patients were identified, all sustaining blunt trauma. Most patients identified as male (63%) and 30% were aged 25–34 years. Road traffic injuries frequently occurred on roads with speeds ≥60 km/hour (60%) and over 60% occurred in the afternoon/evening. Approximately 52% of cases reported ISS >12, and 32% of patients arrived at the hospital more than 12 hours after the time of injury. Median hospital stay was 9 days (IQR: 3.1 to 17.0) and greater ISS scores were associated with longer hospital stays (OR=1.2, 95% CI 1.08 to 1.26, p<0.001). Conclusion Our findings suggest a need to focus on addressing the overrepresentation of men and individuals of working age while also targeting key risk factors like speed, with improved support and resourcing for rural and remote communities. Central to these efforts should be community-led co-design initiatives to ensure relevant and sustainable prevention strategies.
papers.idea: Target speed-reduction and evening-hour safety interventions on high-speed rural roads in underserved communities.
papers.summary: This descriptive epidemiological study profiled 127 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults hospitalized for road traffic injuries in South Australia between 2018–2023. Most injuries occurred on high-speed roads (≥60 km/h) in afternoon/evening hours; 52% had severe injuries (ISS >12) and 32% arrived at hospital >12 hours post-injury.
papers.effect_size: Not quantified in abstract; OR=1.2 (95% CI 1.08–1.26) for length of stay per unit ISS increase
papers.reach: 127 patients in South Australia over 5 years
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of trauma registry data for road traffic injuries among Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults (≥18 years) admitted to hospital; ordinal logistic regression examined association between ISS and length of stay.
papers.scalability_note: Geographically limited to one state; findings are population-specific and lack tested interventions.
papers.cost_note: Speed and timing interventions (enforcement, signage, lighting) are moderate-cost; no intervention costs provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is urban with very different road speeds, demographics, and geography than rural South Australia; findings may not transfer; Aboriginal/Torres Strait Islander context is not applicable.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive epidemiology only; no intervention tested or evaluated for effectiveness.
papers.underused_note: Speed enforcement and evening safety campaigns exist in many jurisdictions but are rarely tailored to specific underserved/Indigenous rural communities or co-designed with them.
papers.novelty_note: Community-led co-design lens is fresher; the speed/timing findings are confirmatory but applied specifically to Indigenous disparities adds some novelty.

papers.title: Land Concentration and Long-Run Development in the Frontier United States
papers.authors: Cory Smith
papers.abstract: I study the long-run economic effects of land concentration on the American frontier. Using quasi-random variation in initial land allocations from a checkerboard formula, I analyze a large database of property assessments and find that historical concentration reduced modern land values by 4.5 percent and fixed capital by 23 percent. Modern effect sizes are 23–64 percent of their historical equivalents, indicating significant rates of both persistence and convergence over the last 150 years. Using archival data on tenant contracts, I argue that the low-powered incentives of share agreements discouraged investment by large-scale owners with long-term effects. (JEL D82, G31, N41, N51, Q12, Q15, Q24)
papers.idea: Historical land concentration patterns inform modern land policy design, but study offers no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: Smith analyzes how initial land allocations on the American frontier (via checkerboard formula) affected long-run economic outcomes. He finds that historical land concentration reduced modern land values by 4.5% and fixed capital by 23%, with effects persisting but also partially converging over 150 years. The mechanism appears to be low-powered incentive structures in sharecropping contracts that discouraged investment.
papers.effect_size: 4.5% reduction in modern land values; 23% reduction in fixed capital
papers.method: Quasi-experimental analysis using quasi-random variation in historical land allocations (checkerboard formula), property assessment database, and archival tenant contract data from American frontier.
papers.scalability_note: Study is historical analysis of 19th-century frontier conditions with no proposed policy mechanism for modern cities.
papers.cost_note: No intervention or cost model proposed; study is retrospective analysis.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no frontier land or sharecropping context; findings about historical tenure structures lack direct policy lever for modern urban housing or land-use problems.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Historical patterns show measurable persistence (4.5% land value reduction), but study is retrospective analysis without forward-looking intervention or causal mechanism testable for modern application.
papers.underused_note: Study offers no actionable policy lever; historical land concentration is not a deployable intervention in modern NYC.
papers.novelty_note: Applies standard economic persistence analysis to frontier land history; insight that historical inequities have long-run effects is conventional.

papers.title: The Female Sensitivity Hypothesis: Evidence From Experimental Economics
papers.authors: Felipe Araujo, Neeraja Gupta, Lise Vesterlund
papers.abstract: We assess the empirical evidence for the hypothesis that women more than men respond to changes in treatment. First, we examine whether the results of over two hundred experimental economics papers support the female sensitivity hypothesis. Second, using data from two studies (DellaVigna and Pope, 2022; Exley et al., 2025), we conduct over two hundred pairwise tests of the hypothesis. Both analyses show that gender is not predictive of responsiveness to treatment. We further examine how the hypothesis has been disseminated in the literature and find that it is referenced predominantly by papers that support it.
papers.idea: None identified—this is a methodological critique of the female sensitivity hypothesis in experimental economics, not a policy intervention.
papers.summary: The paper questions whether women respond more strongly than men to experimental treatments. A meta-analysis of 200+ experimental economics papers and re-analysis of two existing studies found no gender difference in treatment responsiveness, and notes the hypothesis has been cited selectively in the literature.
papers.method: Systematic review and re-analysis of experimental economics studies; meta-analytic assessment of 200+ pairwise hypothesis tests across two datasets (DellaVigna & Pope 2022; Exley et al. 2025).
papers.scalability_note: Pure research contribution; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; cost not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable city policy or program identified. Theoretical/methodological paper on research bias in economics literature.
papers.effect_size: no significant effect of gender on responsiveness to treatment
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological critique finding NO support for hypothesis; not a policy intervention with measurable outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Not a deployable intervention—purely academic methodological analysis of experimental design literature.
papers.novelty_note: Standard academic critique of bias in hypothesis reporting; debunking framing is conventional.

papers.title: Children Are Not the Enemy: Child-Fit Security as an Alternative to Bans and Surveillance
papers.authors: Kopo M. Ramokapane, Rui Huan, Zaina Dkaidek, Awais Rashid
papers.abstract: Digital technologies are now central to children's learning, play, communication, identity formation, and social participation. Yet dominant approaches to children's online safety often rely on containment mechanisms, including bans, age gates, parental controls, monitoring, and screen-time restrictions. These approaches can be useful in specific contexts, but they often frame child protection primarily as a problem of restricting access to systems designed for adults. In this paper, we argue that this framing is inadequate for children's digital lives and insufficient as a security paradigm. We propose Child-fit security, a design paradigm in which technologies likely to be used by children treat a child as legitimate users, not attackers to be excluded, vulnerabilities to be patched, or risks to be managed. In this paradigm, children's wellbeing, development, privacy, safety, agency, and rights become core security requirements. This shifts the focus of protection from apps, accounts, and data to the child-system relationship, which means protecting both the child and their participation. We conceptualise child-fit security, contrast it with containment-oriented approaches, define its core principles, and discuss its implications for security design. We conclude by presenting a research agenda for making child-fit security operational.
papers.idea: Redesign digital platforms and applications used by children to center child wellbeing, privacy, and agency as core security requirements rather than relying on bans, surveillance, and parental controls.
papers.summary: This paper proposes 'Child-fit security,' a design paradigm that treats children as legitimate users of technology rather than threats to be excluded or managed. The authors argue that dominant approaches relying on containment mechanisms (bans, age gates, monitoring) are inadequate and insufficient, and instead advocate for shifting protection focus from data/apps to the child-system relationship.
papers.method: Conceptual paper presenting a design paradigm framework; contrasts child-fit security with containment-oriented approaches and defines core principles with implications for security design.
papers.scalability_note: Design principles are generalizable across platforms and jurisdictions; adoption depends on platform/vendor willingness rather than local implementation constraints.
papers.cost_note: Implementation costs vary widely by platform; requires design/engineering investment but no direct per-user spending implied.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could influence city-contracted platforms or public digital services, but most child-facing technology is privately owned; limited direct city policy lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Conceptual framework paper with no empirical outcome data; proposes design principles rather than demonstrating causal effects on child safety or wellbeing outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Child-centered design security is rarely implemented at scale; most platforms still rely on age-gating, parental controls, and surveillance rather than fundamental redesign around child agency and privacy.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes child safety from a containment/restriction problem to a participatory design problem; challenges the assumption that children are threats rather than legitimate users with rights.

papers.title: How Much Can We Trust LLM Search Agents? Measuring Endorsement Vulnerability to Web Content Manipulation
papers.authors: Yimeng Chen, Zhe Ren, Firas Laakom, Yu Li, Dandan Guo, Jürgen Schmidhuber
papers.abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based search agents synthesize open-web content into actionable recommendations on behalf of users, creating a risk that attacker-published pages are transformed into endorsed claims. We introduce SearchGEO, a controlled evaluation framework for measuring endorsement corruption in LLM-based web-search agents, combining a web-evidence manipulation pipeline, a five-mode attack taxonomy, and multiple output-level metrics. We evaluate 13 LLM backends on 308 cases each. Results show that vulnerability patterns vary across backends: overall attack success rate (ASR) ranges from 0.0% on Claude-Sonnet-4.6 to 31.4% on Gemini-3-Flash, the strongest attack mode differs by model family, and the same deployment scaffold could amplify or decrease ASR on different backends. An auxiliary agent-skill probe, where endorsement becomes an install command, exposes a sharp split among otherwise robust backends: Claude over-rejects while GPT over-trusts. These findings argue for treating recommendation reliability under adversarial search content as a first-class dimension of backend safety evaluation.
papers.idea: City agencies could audit LLM-based search tools they deploy for public-information services to measure vulnerability to adversarial web-content manipulation.
papers.summary: This paper introduces SearchGEO, a framework for testing whether large language model search agents can be manipulated into endorsing false claims planted on attacker-controlled web pages. Testing 13 LLM backends across 308 cases, the authors found attack success rates ranging from 0% (Claude-Sonnet) to 31.4% (Gemini-3-Flash), with variation in which attack modes work best on different models.
papers.method: Controlled evaluation framework combining web-evidence manipulation pipeline, five-mode attack taxonomy, and output-level metrics applied to 13 LLM backends on 308 test cases each.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only if a city adopts LLM search agents; methodologically useful but not a scalable intervention itself.
papers.cost_note: Testing framework itself is low-cost; deployment of mitigations would vary.
papers.nyc_note: NYC agencies do not yet widely rely on LLM search agents for public services. Relevant only if/when they do. No immediate policy or program lever.
papers.effect_size: Attack success rate ranges from 0.0% to 31.4% across LLM backends
papers.reach: 13 LLM backends evaluated on 308 cases each
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Identifies a vulnerability but doesn't demonstrate an intervention or real-world harm reduction; primarily a diagnostic/measurement study.
papers.underused_note: LLM audit practices exist in tech but are rarely systematized by municipal agencies deploying AI for public services; moderate gap between capability and deployment.
papers.novelty_note: Applies adversarial-testing rigor to a specific public-sector AI risk, but the core idea of auditing LLM robustness is emerging in tech/policy circles.

papers.title: Transportation Infrastructure and Total Factor Productivity : Development Heterogeneity and Resilience under Adverse Shocks
papers.abstract: Weak total factor productivity (TFP) growth has become a central concern in explaining sluggish growth performance, particularly in emerging-market and low-income economies. At the same time, constrained fiscal and investment conditions have increased the importance of using scarce public resources effectively. These conditions make it important to investigate, from a cross-country perspective, what is associated with productivity performance and what shapes productivity losses during adverse shocks. The paper examines this issue along two margins: a structural margin focused on transportation infrastructure, a public-investment-intensive form of capital that may enhance productive efficiency, and a resilience margin through which governance and infrastructure may shape the productivity costs of adverse shocks. Using annual cross-country panel data for more than 100 countries and a dynamic panel system GMM framework, the analysis finds that transportation infrastructure is positively associated with TFP, with larger estimated payoffs in lower-income countries. Severe crises are associated with larger productivity losses in poorer economies, but stronger rule of law and political stability are linked to smaller losses in the lowest-income quartile. Road infrastructure is also associated with smaller productivity losses during moderate downturns. The findings suggest that the productivity relevance of transportation infrastructure is greater where development constraints are more binding, while governance and infrastructure shape productivity costs during adverse episodes.
papers.idea: Invest in road infrastructure to improve total factor productivity and economic resilience, particularly in lower-income areas.
papers.summary: This cross-country macroeconomic study finds that transportation infrastructure is positively associated with total factor productivity (TFP), with larger payoffs in lower-income countries. It also finds that stronger rule of law, political stability, and road infrastructure are associated with smaller productivity losses during economic crises.
papers.reach: More than 100 countries, annual panel data
papers.method: Dynamic panel system GMM (generalized method of moments) analysis of cross-country annual data for 100+ countries examining associations between transportation infrastructure and TFP, controlling for governance factors.
papers.scalability_note: High-level macroeconomic findings; requires substantial capital investment over long timeframes; generalization to city level uncertain.
papers.cost_note: Transportation infrastructure is capital-intensive; study provides no unit costs or cost-effectiveness estimates.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has mature transit/road infrastructure; study is global macro-level analysis without city-specific evidence or actionable levers for NYC policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Moderate cross-country association between road infrastructure and TFP growth; larger effects in lower-income countries, but macro-level study without NYC-specific validation.
papers.underused_note: Infrastructure investment is mainstream policy priority; NYC already invests heavily in roads/transit. Not underdeployed relative to evidence strength.
papers.novelty_note: Framing infrastructure via TFP productivity lens is standard development economics; connection to public safety is indirect and not central to study.

papers.title: A context-driven meta-analysis of job satisfaction in policing: evidence from South Korea
papers.authors: Jae Seong Jang
papers.idea: Improve police officer job satisfaction through targeted interventions identified in cross-national research.
papers.summary: Unable to assess—no abstract provided. Likely a meta-analysis synthesizing job-satisfaction drivers among police officers in South Korea, potentially identifying organizational or management levers.
papers.method: Meta-analysis of job satisfaction research in South Korean policing context; no details on included studies, sample, or outcomes available.
papers.scalability_note: Job-satisfaction interventions are typically organizational and replicable, but context-specific to Korean policing.
papers.cost_note: Likely low-cost HR/management changes, but absent concrete findings, cost unclear.
papers.nyc_note: South Korean police system differs materially from NYPD in structure, labor law, and culture; transferability unproven without actionable findings.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Meta-analysis of job satisfaction correlates, not interventions with measured crime/safety outcomes; indirect link to public safety.
papers.underused_note: Police wellness and retention programs are increasingly common in major US departments; not a neglected area.
papers.novelty_note: Job satisfaction in policing is well-established research; framing as 'context-driven' adds minimal new insight.

papers.title: Tax Evasion and the Contribution-Benefit Link : The Case of Parental Benefits in Hungary and Latvia
papers.abstract: This paper studies the interaction of the contribution-benefit link with tax evasion in the context of parental benefits in Hungary and Latvia. Across the two countries, institutional settings, and time periods, earnings and employment patterns suggest substantial pre-pregnancy underreporting, followed by partial formalization during pregnancy to increase benefits. Using policy reforms, the paper shows that the reporting response tracks changing incentives. The results indicate that even with third-party reporting, income underreporting can be common. Linking benefits to contributions when earnings are underreported may reduce evasion temporarily but can impose net fiscal costs depending on program design.
papers.idea: Design parental-benefit programs with contribution-benefit linkages that reduce incentives for pre-pregnancy income underreporting.
papers.summary: The study examines how linking parental benefits to prior contributions affects tax evasion in Hungary and Latvia. It finds that workers systematically underreport earnings before pregnancy to qualify for larger benefits, then partially formalize income during pregnancy; while stricter contribution-benefit links can reduce evasion temporarily, they may impose net fiscal costs depending on program structure.
papers.reach: Two countries (Hungary, Latvia); multiple time periods
papers.method: Observational analysis of earnings and employment patterns across two countries using policy reforms as quasi-experimental variation to track reporting responses.
papers.scalability_note: Parental benefit programs are country-level welfare instruments; adaptation requires redesigning existing national social-insurance architecture rather than piloting a discrete intervention.
papers.cost_note: Fiscal cost depends on program design and evasion baseline; study suggests some designs impose net costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no parental benefit program of its own (US federal/state level); findings are specific to European contribution-based systems; limited direct levers for city policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Shows behavioral response to incentives but highlights that contribution-link design may impose net fiscal costs; effect is demonstrative rather than clearly beneficial.
papers.underused_note: Parental-benefit policy is primarily a social-insurance design question, not a public-safety intervention; limited relevance to NYC civic-safety mandate.
papers.novelty_note: Applies standard tax-incentive logic to benefit design; not a novel framing for public safety or urban life improvement.

papers.title: Using Survey Data to Understand the Health Needs of Difficult to Reach Populations : Evidence from a Community Survey Regarding the Individual and Contextual Correlates of Sex Life Happiness Among European Men Who Have Sex With Men
papers.abstract: Being happy with one’s sex life is an important facet of sexual health. Several studies have found associations between sex life happiness and a range of individual (or proximate) and contextual (usually country-level) factors amongst men who have sex with men (MSM). Using a novel dataset, the 2017 European Men-Who-Have-Sex-With-Men Internet Survey (EMIS-2017), this paper simultaneously explores the association of both individual and contextual variables, sex life happiness, and health. Understanding and quantifying this link is important for policy makers concerned with improving health outcomes in minority, and often marginalized, populations. Results: Recency of sex and/or being in a steady sexual relationship had the largest positive associations with higher self-reported sex life happiness. Being single had the largest negative association. Among individual-level factors, not having experienced homophobia and being out to a majority of one’s social network were most strongly associated with sex life happiness.
