papers.abstract: AI agents are moving from advisors to actors, booking travel, planning menus, and running procurement on behalf of users. Existing benchmarks for AI and animal welfare evaluate model text responses to question-answer prompts, leaving open whether the welfare reasoning surfaced in those responses transfers to agentic deployment where the model must take actions with tools. We introduce TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first agentic benchmark measuring whether AI agents avoid options involving animal exploitation when acting on behalf of users. TAC presents an AI agent with twelve hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six categories of animal exploitation, augmented to forty-eight samples to control for price, rating, and position confounds. We evaluate seven frontier models from four labs. Every model scores below the chance level of sixty-four percent, with the best performer (Claude Opus 4.7) at fifty-three percent. A single welfare-aware sentence in the system prompt yields gains of forty-seven to sixty-three percentage points in Claude and GPT-5.5, twenty-six points in GPT-5.2, and under twelve points in DeepSeek and Gemini. An auxiliary Inspect Scout audit of 288 base-condition transcripts from the top two performers, using Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite as judge, flags zero transcripts for evaluation awareness, suggesting the below-chance rates do not stem from the models recognising the evaluation. We discuss implications for category-level variation across cultural domains, the limits of text-response welfare benchmarks, and the EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice systemic risk framework.
papers.idea: Require welfare-aware system prompts in AI travel-booking tools used by city agencies or tourism boards.
papers.summary: The study benchmarks seven frontier AI models on their tendency to recommend animal-exploitation activities (bullfights, animal attractions) when booking travel. All models performed below chance level (53% best vs 64% baseline), but a single welfare-aware sentence in system prompts improved performance by 26–63 percentage points depending on model.
papers.method: Agentic benchmark (TAC) with 48 hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six animal-exploitation categories, evaluated on seven frontier models; auxiliary audit of 288 transcripts using another model as judge.
papers.scalability_note: Narrow applicability; only relevant to organizations running AI travel-booking agents (not a widespread city service).
papers.cost_note: Minimal cost—adding a system prompt is trivial; no new infrastructure or budget required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not operate a public AI travel-booking system; no direct lever for city policy. Animal welfare is important but orthogonal to the civic-safety/housing/transit/health priorities of urban governance.
papers.effect_size: All models scored below 64% chance level, with best performer at 53%; welfare-aware system prompt improved performance by 26-63 percentage points
papers.reach: 7 frontier AI models from 4 labs evaluated on 48 travel booking scenarios
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Prompt engineering fix is real but narrow in scope; applies only to AI booking tools, not actual public-safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Animal welfare in AI systems is niche; NYC agencies have minimal AI travel-booking deployment and this isn't a civic-safety priority.
papers.novelty_note: Fresh angle on AI alignment + animal welfare, but tangential to core public safety; not a novel approach to crime, harm, or justice.

papers.title: Every Eval Ever: A Unifying Schema and Community Repository for AI Evaluation Results
papers.authors: Jan Batzner, Sree Harsha Nelaturu, Anastassia Kornilova, Jon Crall, Tommaso Cerruti, Yanan Long, Yifan Mai, Sanchit Ahuja, Asaf Yehudai, Marek Šuppa, John P. Lalor, Oluwagbemike Olowe, Jatin Ganhotra, Brian H. Hu, Eliya Habba, Andrew M. Bean, Chang Liu, Sander Land, Steven Dillmann, Aniketh Garikaparthi, Elron Bandel, Saki Imai, James Edgell, Wm. Matthew Kennedy, Jenny Chim, Patrick Meusling, Asteria Kaeberlein, Venkata Ramachandra Karthik Chundi, Manasi Patwardhan, Martin Ku, Austin Meek, Leon Knauer, Brian Wingenroth, Srishti Yadav, Usman Gohar, Felix Friedrich, Michelle Lin, Jennifer Mickel, Arman Cohan, Stella Biderman, Irene Solaiman, Zeerak Talat, Anka Reuel, Mubashara Akhtar, Gjergji Kasneci, Avijit Ghosh, Leshem Choshen
papers.abstract: AI evaluations are widely used for testing and understanding progress. However, the diverse evaluators bring with them inconsistencies that challenge analysis and comparison. First, results are saved in incompatible formats, scattered across leaderboards, papers, blog posts, evaluation harness logs, and custom repositories. Second, results are created by different evaluation frameworks, which produce divergent scores for nominally identical evaluations and record metadata inconsistently, hindering comparison, cross-community evaluation science, cost reduction, and reuse. We introduce Every Eval Ever, the first shared schema and community-crowdsourced repository for AI evaluation results. The schema standardizes how evaluations are represented in a unified, single JSON document. It is source-agnostic by design, ingesting results from evaluation harnesses and papers alike, and optionally stores per-instance outputs for fine-grained analysis. We contribute: (i) a community-governed metadata schema with a companion instance-level schema, the first standardization effort of its kind; (ii) automatic converters from popular formats, evaluation harnesses, and leaderboards to the unified schema; and (iii) a crowdsourced community database hosted on Hugging Face, currently spanning to date 22,235 models, 2,273 unique benchmarks, and 31 evaluation formats.
papers.idea: Not applicable; this is a methods paper on standardizing AI evaluation metadata schemas, not an intervention.
papers.summary: The paper introduces 'Every Eval Ever,' a unified JSON schema and crowdsourced repository for standardizing how AI evaluation results are recorded and compared across different frameworks, leaderboards, and papers. It addresses fragmentation in how AI model evaluations are stored and reported, providing automatic converters and a community database of 22,235 models and 2,273 benchmarks.
papers.method: Non-empirical; describes the design of a schema and repository architecture, with contributions of converters and a crowdsourced database on Hugging Face.
papers.scalability_note: Software infrastructure scales globally with minimal marginal cost.
papers.cost_note: Infrastructure and curation costs likely minimal once established; hosted on Hugging Face.
papers.nyc_note: This is a computer science infrastructure tool for AI researchers; no direct hook to city services, policy, or resident welfare.
papers.reach: 22,235 models, 2,273 unique benchmarks, and 31 evaluation formats
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methods paper on AI evaluation standardization; no public-safety intervention or outcome measurement.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable to civic/public-safety domain; tools infrastructure rather than policy or program.
papers.novelty_note: Standardization schemas are valuable but represent incremental technical work, not a novel approach to city problems.

papers.title: How Expanding Housing Supply and Supporting Demand Can Increase Homeownership
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.idea: Expand housing supply and support demand-side interventions to increase homeownership rates.
papers.summary: Title and venue suggest a policy brief on mechanisms linking housing supply expansion and demand-support programs to homeownership outcomes. No abstract available to confirm findings, mechanisms, or evidence base.
papers.method: Unknown; Urban Institute policy analysis (likely descriptive synthesis or modeling).
papers.scalability_note: Housing supply and demand policies are standard levers used by cities and states.
papers.cost_note: Supply-side costs vary widely; demand-side support (down-payment assistance, subsidies) requires budget allocation.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has existing housing and homeownership programs; supply constraints and affordability are urgent policy issues.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical evidence presented; general policy statement without demonstrated causal effect on public safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Housing expansion is mainstream policy discourse; heavily debated and funded in NYC, though implementation lags.
papers.novelty_note: Standard housing-supply advocacy; not a fresh angle on public safety or city problems.

papers.title: The Role of Public Health Services in Reducing Maternal and Newborn Health Inequalities in Urban India: A Survey Analysis of 200,000 Births Over Two Decades
papers.authors: Andrea K. Blanchard, Banadakoppa Manjappa Ramesh, Usha Ram, Prakash Kumar, Kerry Scott, James F. Blanchard, Ties Boerma
papers.abstract: Abstract India has experienced rapid urbanisation, straining the healthcare system. The National Urban Health Mission was launched in 2013 to improve access to public healthcare, particularly among socio-economically disadvantaged urban populations. This study aimed to assess whether inequalities in maternal and newborn health (MNH) service coverage and outcomes between richer and poorer groups have improved at public and private sources across urban India in the last two decades. We used pooled data from four national cross-sectional surveys, the District Level Household Surveys from 2002 to 2008 and National Family Health Surveys from 2015 to 2021, covering 94,826 and 108,152 births in urban India, respectively. We analysed trends in coverage of antenatal, delivery, and postnatal care services and neonatal mortality by source across wealth deciles, and summarised inequalities using the slope index of inequality, concentration index, and inequality pattern index. The study found that coverage of all MNH services, and to a lesser extent neonatal survival, increased substantially between 2002–2008 and 2015–2021 in urban India. Improvements were steeper among the poorest groups. Coverage by public health facilities notably increased, and neonatal mortality rates were lower at public than private facilities, particularly among the poorest. However, the poorest decile remained well behind all other groups, reflecting bottom inequalities. Rapid improvements with reduced inequalities in MNH service coverage appear to be driven by increased access to public sector services in urban India. It remains critical for the public healthcare system to understand and address the particular needs of the poorest groups to reduce ongoing bottom MNH inequalities in urban India.
papers.idea: Strengthen public health facility capacity and remove barriers to access for the poorest urban populations to reduce maternal and newborn mortality disparities, following the model of India's National Urban Health Mission.
papers.summary: Over two decades in urban India, coverage of antenatal, delivery, and postnatal care services and neonatal survival improved substantially, with steeper gains among poorer groups and notably better outcomes at public facilities than private ones. However, the poorest decile remained significantly behind other wealth groups, indicating persistent "bottom inequalities" despite overall progress.
papers.method: Pooled cross-sectional analysis of 200,000+ births from four national surveys (District Level Household Surveys 2002–2008 and National Family Health Surveys 2015–2021) tracking service coverage and neonatal mortality across wealth deciles.
papers.scalability_note: Health policy and facility expansion readily scalable across urban settings; requires sustained infrastructure investment.
papers.cost_note: Public health system expansion is moderately costly; study suggests public provision more efficient than relying on private sector.
papers.nyc_note: NYC maternal/newborn mortality rates are substantially lower than urban India; applicable mainly as comparative benchmark for equity-focused service delivery in underserved neighborhoods.
papers.effect_size: Improvements in maternal and newborn health service coverage were steeper among the poorest groups; neonatal mortality rates were lower at public than private facilities, particularly among the poorest
papers.reach: 200,000 births over two decades (94,826 births in 2002-2008; 108,152 births in 2015-2021) across urban India
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Observational survey showing coverage improvements but no experimental evidence of causation; neonatal mortality gains modest and inequalities persist at bottom.
papers.underused_note: Maternal health access & public health capacity-building are mainstream policy priorities globally; not a gap-area in urban policy.
papers.novelty_note: Standard public health expansion & equity framing; no counterintuitive mechanism or fresh strategic angle.

papers.title: Interactional privilege of violence: Status and interaction in the street field
papers.authors: Hakan Kalkan, Heith Copes
papers.abstract: Abstract Criminologists have long described and theorized the relationship between status, respect, and violence within urban communities. Although this finding is generally accepted within criminology, ethnographic empirical illustrations of this phenomenon are sparse. To better understand how the potential for violence shapes interactions, affords interactional privilege, and structures relative positions in the social hierarchy, we develop a theoretical framework based on the work of Pierre Bourdieu, Erving Goffman, and Randall Collins. Drawing on ethnographic fieldwork in a disadvantaged area in Copenhagen, we show that interactional boundaries—what liberties one can take with others—are contingent on familiarity and verbal skill but more fundamentally on one's violence capital (i.e., the resources, capacities, skills, and dispositions that confer advantage in exerting corporal violence against others). By empirically illustrating how interactions are structured by violence capital, we contribute to a deeper understanding of how street hierarchies are maintained and reproduced through violence.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; paper is theoretical framework development.
papers.summary: The study develops a theoretical framework showing that street hierarchies and violence in disadvantaged urban areas are structured by 'violence capital'—resources, skills, and dispositions that confer advantage in physical violence. The authors use ethnographic fieldwork in Copenhagen to illustrate how interactional boundaries are shaped by familiarity, verbal skill, and most fundamentally by one's capacity for violence.
papers.method: Qualitative ethnographic fieldwork in a disadvantaged area in Copenhagen; no quantitative sample size or comparison group specified.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical framework with limited concrete policy mechanisms to replicate across cities.
papers.cost_note: Ethnographic research has modest direct costs, but translating insights into intervention is unclear.
papers.nyc_note: Provides sociological understanding of street violence dynamics but offers no explicit lever for NYC policy or program design.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework only; no intervention tested or evaluated; no quantified outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention—foundational theory. Status/respect-violence link already well-established in criminology literature.
papers.novelty_note: Bourdieu/Goffman synthesis applied to violence capital is conceptually coherent but builds on familiar theories; ethnographic depth adds modest freshness.

papers.title: Housing Finance at a Glance: A Monthly Chartbook, May 2026
papers.authors: Laurie Goodman, Michael Neal, Jung Hyun Choi, Linna Zhu, John Walsh, Daniel Pang, Katie Visalli, Aniket Mehrotra, Matthew Pruitt, Bryson Berry, Alison Rincon, Todd Hill, Anna Barcus, Erin Koons
papers.abstract: Housing Finance at a Glance is a monthly reference guide for mortgage and housing market data. It includes over 80 curated and updated figures describing current trends in the US housing finance market, including housing affordability, credit availability, serious delinquency rates, and mortgage insurance activity. These trends stood out most to housing finance policy researchers from this chartbook edition: The aggregate value of housing equity has slightly contracted amid flat home values and rising mortgage debt outstanding (p. 6) Mortgage affordability has improved as of March 2026 (p. 24) and home sales are expected to rise (p. 22). 80.7 percent of FHA loans are originated for first-time homebuyers (p. 30). FHA loans have contributed to growth in mortgage insurance over the past year (p. 37).
papers.summary: This is a monthly reference chartbook tracking US housing finance trends including affordability, mortgage credit, delinquency rates, and insurance activity. The May 2026 edition highlights recent improvements in mortgage affordability, expected home sales growth, and FHA loan patterns.
papers.method: Compilation and curation of 80+ existing data series and figures on housing finance and mortgage markets; appears to be ongoing monitoring/dashboard rather than original research.
papers.scalability_note: Data compilation easily replicated elsewhere; but outputs are metrics, not interventions.
papers.cost_note: Production of reference chartbook is low-cost; unclear if actionable uses exist.
papers.nyc_note: National data aggregation with no NYC-specific analysis; limited direct policy lever without deeper local adaptation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive chartbook with no causal evidence or intervention outcome measurement.
papers.underused_note: Housing finance data dashboards are standard policy tools; this is a reference resource, not an intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Monthly housing market tracking is conventional policy infrastructure.

papers.title: Georgia’s Child Care Subsidy Program
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: In 2023, Urban began a multiyear collaboration with DECAL to design a study focused on understanding parents’ experiences with Childcare and Parent Services (CAPS), Georgia’s child care subsidy program. This research can help the agency identify and better meet the needs of the people it serves.
papers.idea: Improve child care subsidy program design by studying parent needs and experiences.
papers.summary: This is a collaborative research study between Urban Institute and Georgia's DECAL agency designed to understand how parents experience the state's child care subsidy program (CAPS). The study aims to help the agency identify and better meet the needs of families it serves, but no findings are reported in the abstract.
papers.method: Multiyear qualitative/mixed-methods study with DECAL, examining parent experiences with Georgia's CAPS program; specific design and sample size not stated.
papers.scalability_note: Program model is replicable but findings are Georgia-specific; generalization requires validation.
papers.cost_note: Subsidy programs are relatively cost-effective per family served, but abstract gives no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has child care subsidy programs (ACS, DOE pre-K) but Georgia's CAPS structure and findings may not directly transfer.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is exploratory/formative research on parent experiences; no outcome effects on safety, crime, or well-being demonstrated yet.
papers.underused_note: Child care subsidy optimization is sensible policy but not a high-leverage public-safety intervention; moderately attended to by agencies.
papers.novelty_note: Parent-centered needs assessment is standard practice in social services; not a novel framing.

papers.title: Evaluating Alternative Approaches to Small Area Estimation of Poverty with Survey and Census Data
papers.abstract: This paper uses five rounds of Mexican and Brazilian census extracts to evaluate the accuracy of different model specifications and estimation methods that use survey and census data to generate small area estimates of poverty. Models that utilize more granular data for prediction (household- and/or village-level predictors) tend to produce more accurate estimates of poverty than models estimated only using area-level predictors. Differences in accuracy across models and methods that utilize household or village level predictors are minor. Models that omit household-level predictors tend to be more robust than unit-level models to the use of old census data and classical measurement error in survey predictors. The performance of the Fay-Herriot area-level model falls in the presence of sample selection bias and small sample sizes. Rescaling sample weights is important in Mexico, where the sample is informative within areas. Applying raw sample weights without rescaling in this case greatly reduces the accuracy of estimates from linear models and distorts methodological comparisons. Overall, no one approach dominates across all contexts, but when sample weights are rescaled there is no downside to using more granular data for prediction.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt improved small-area poverty estimation methods combining household-level survey and census data to better target anti-poverty programs.
papers.summary: This paper evaluates five different statistical approaches to estimating poverty at small geographic areas using Mexican and Brazilian census and survey data. Models using granular household or village-level predictors produced more accurate estimates than area-level models, though accuracy differences among granular approaches were minor; area-level models proved more robust to old census data and measurement error.
papers.reach: Mexican and Brazilian census data across five rounds; geographic scope not specified
papers.method: Comparative evaluation of five model specifications and estimation methods applied to census extract data from Mexico and Brazil to assess accuracy of small-area poverty prediction.
papers.scalability_note: Method is generalizable but requires significant technical expertise and access to linked survey-census data infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Requires skilled statisticians and data infrastructure; one-time implementation cost likely moderate but ongoing maintenance required.
papers.nyc_note: NYC lacks integrated household-level census-survey linkage at fine geographic scales; ACS provides area-level data but not the granular predictors this analysis emphasizes.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological improvements in poverty estimation accuracy are demonstrated, but effect is technical/indirect rather than measuring real-world program outcomes or behavioral change.
papers.underused_note: Small-area estimation methods exist in academic literature but are inconsistently applied by municipal governments; NYC and most cities rely on cruder geographic targeting.
papers.novelty_note: Combining survey and census data for granular poverty mapping is established practice in development economics; application to US city targeting is straightforward rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: Who’s Involved and What Factors Are Considered in Decisions About School District Secessions?
papers.authors: Harley Webley, Luisa Godinez-Puig, Karishma Furtado, Adriana Vance
papers.abstract: Since 2000, 19 states have had school district secessions, which occur when a community leaves the district it was part of, creating both a new and a left-behind district. Secessions can reshape the distribution of educational resources and opportunities, often concentrating them in the new district. But how secessions happen—specifically the laws and regulations used to approve or deny a secession—differ from place to place. Relying on previous work, we analyzed state laws and regulations that define the criteria and steps used to approve or deny secession efforts in the 19 states with successful secessions since 2000, according to our database. We find that laws regulating secession vary significantly across states, often leaving decisionmakers with incomplete or inconsistent information to assess potential impacts. But state policymakers can help. They could provide communities and local policymakers with standardized guidance for secession, such as planning templates and data sources, and require that
papers.idea: States could adopt standardized guidance templates and data requirements to help communities and local officials assess school district secession impacts more consistently.
papers.summary: The study analyzed state laws and regulations governing school district secessions (where communities leave existing districts) across 19 states since 2000. Authors found that secession laws vary widely across states, often leaving decision-makers with incomplete or inconsistent information about impacts. The research suggests state policymakers could provide standardized planning templates and data sources to improve secession decision-making.
papers.method: Comparative legal and regulatory analysis of state laws governing school district secessions in 19 states with successful secessions since 2000, based on authors' database.
papers.scalability_note: State-level policy intervention; would require legislative action; applicable across multiple states but implementation varies by jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: Low-cost intervention (templates, data standardization); primary cost is staff time for policy development and dissemination.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates as a single unified school district; school district secession is not a live governance issue in the city. Research is primarily relevant to suburban/regional contexts.
papers.reach: 19 states with school district secessions since 2000
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is descriptive/procedural analysis, not an intervention with measured outcomes; no causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: School governance reform is actively debated; standardized guidance is a sensible administrative improvement but not a neglected gap relative to other city safety priorities.
papers.novelty_note: Procedural standardization is conventional policy-improvement thinking; not a new insight into root causes or behavioral mechanisms.

papers.title: About
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: In this project we investigate the state-level costs of secessions, particularly those that concentrate resources. Secession occurs when a community unilaterally breaks off from a larger school district, creating a new district and a “left behind” district. When the seceded district is more advantaged than the one left behind, secession concentrates resources in the new district, which can lead to lower educational quality in the left behind district and entrenched segregation by class, race, ethnicity, or all of the above between the two. Secessions that concentrate resources may also reinforce existing hierarchies in educational advantage and power. School District Secession Attempts across the United States, by Status, 2000–2026 Source: 2000–2026 School District Secessions dataset, compiled by authors To better understand the consequences of secessions that concentrate resources, we aim to answer the following questions using a mixed-methods approach: At the district level: Does secession affect total and source-specific (i.e., local
papers.idea: Prevent or regulate school district secessions that concentrate resources in advantaged areas to reduce educational segregation and inequity.
papers.summary: This study investigates the state-level costs of school district secessions, particularly when more-advantaged communities break away from larger districts. The research examines whether secessions concentrate resources, worsen educational quality in left-behind districts, and entrench segregation by class, race, and ethnicity. The abstract indicates the project uses a mixed-methods approach and a 2000–2026 dataset but does not report findings.
papers.method: Mixed-methods analysis using a 2000–2026 school district secessions dataset compiled by authors; research is ongoing/incomplete based on abstract.
papers.scalability_note: School district governance is state-regulated; requires state-level policy changes; somewhat variable by state.
papers.cost_note: Regulatory/legal intervention; costs depend on state enforcement and litigation.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a unified city-run school system; secession risk is minimal. Study focuses on state-level secession law, not NYC-specific policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Secession demonstrably concentrates resources and increases segregation, but causal impact on student outcomes not yet fully quantified in abstract.
papers.underused_note: Policy intervention to prevent/regulate secessions is rare; most states allow it; NYC itself blocked some attempts but no systematic regulatory framework.
papers.novelty_note: Framing secession as a policy lever for equity is somewhat fresh; most education reform focuses on funding formulas or integration, not structural prevention.

papers.title: Racial Differences in the Association of Physical Disability and Healthcare Access With Firearm Violence at the Neighborhood Level in Chicago
papers.authors: Bennett Allen, Cale Basaraba, Madison Offstein, Spruha Joshi, Jacquelyn Jacobs
papers.idea: Not applicable — study is descriptive/correlational analysis without identified intervention lever.
papers.summary: Study examines racial disparities in associations between physical disability, healthcare access, and neighborhood firearm violence in Chicago. No abstract available to confirm findings or mechanisms.
papers.method: Not determinable from title alone; likely a neighborhood-level observational analysis of Chicago administrative/survey data.
papers.scalability_note: Identifies correlates of violence but offers no intervention or policy mechanism to act on.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; any intervention derived would require separate design and pilot.
papers.nyc_note: Findings may be Chicago-specific; no actionable policy or program proposed for direct transfer.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive analysis only; no intervention tested or causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention—purely correlational research without deployment pathway.
papers.novelty_note: Links disability and healthcare access to firearm violence, but framing remains within conventional public-health epidemiology; no novel mechanism or solution proposed.

papers.title: Structuring Transparency: Developing Domain-Specific Generative AI Declaration Frameworks in Higher Education
papers.authors: Nicholas Micallef, Olga Petrovska
papers.abstract: As Generative AI (GenAI) disrupts higher education, institutions increasingly require students to declare AI use. However, generic, binary declarations (e.g., "I used GenAI") fail to capture the nuanced application of these tools in different academic tasks. Establishing transparency is key to protecting academic integrity, promoting AI literacy, and shifting the focus from policing to professional practice. In response, this paper contributes a design artefact and an accompanying position: a framework of two task-specific declaration structures, one for writing-focused activities and one for coding assessments, developed for a Computer Science department on the basis of an existing taxonomy of GenAI usage, together with an argument that task-specific disclosure is needed to move beyond binary declarations. By categorising AI usage across specific cognitive and developmental stages, such as structural planning vs. Textual Content Generation, or code improvement vs. code generation, the framework encourages students to reflect on their own learning process and clarifies the boundary between acceptable assistance and academic misconduct. We propose this domain-specific approach as a foundation for fostering more honest assessment in Computer Science and other disciplines, aiming to better prepare students for professional environments where documenting GenAI workflows might be an essential job requirement.
papers.idea: Higher education institutions could adopt task-specific AI declaration frameworks to improve academic integrity and AI literacy among students.
papers.summary: The paper proposes a framework for domain-specific AI disclosure statements in higher education, tailored to writing and coding tasks, that move beyond binary declarations to capture nuanced AI usage across cognitive stages. The authors argue this approach encourages student reflection on learning and clarifies boundaries between acceptable assistance and misconduct. The framework was developed for a Computer Science department based on an existing taxonomy of GenAI usage.
papers.method: Design artefact development within a Computer Science department, informed by existing GenAI usage taxonomy; no empirical validation or outcome measurement reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is education-specific and requires institutional adoption; minimal civic/municipal lever.
papers.cost_note: Primarily administrative redesign of existing declaration processes; low direct cost to implement.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could require CUNY/SUNY systems to adopt, but this is higher-ed policy not core municipal governance or public-facing service; indirect city benefit.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Framework design artefact with no empirical outcome data; theoretical promise but no demonstrated impact on integrity, learning, or misconduct rates.
papers.underused_note: AI declaration frameworks are actively being piloted across many US universities; this is a crowded design space, not underfunded or neglected.
papers.novelty_note: Task-specific disclosure is a modest refinement of existing institutional responses; framing as 'professional practice' rather than policing is conventional edtech rhetoric.

papers.title: School Construction and Intergenerational Human Capital
papers.authors: Naveen Sunder
papers.idea: Time-shift school construction projects to maximize intergenerational spillovers of human capital.
papers.summary: Without an abstract, the likely focus is on how school construction timing or location affects student outcomes and long-term economic mobility across generations. The title suggests the author examines whether investment in school infrastructure produces measurable gains in educational attainment or earnings for cohorts exposed to it.
papers.method: Unable to determine from title alone; likely uses administrative education data and difference-in-differences or regression discontinuity design comparing cohorts exposed to school construction at different ages.
papers.scalability_note: School construction is inherently slow and place-based; policy lever unclear without abstract.
papers.cost_note: School infrastructure is capital-intensive; cost per beneficiary depends on project scope and timeline.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has ongoing school construction, but without knowing the study's specific finding or recommendation, applicability is speculative. Title suggests research question, not actionable policy lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Conceptual framework with no empirical results presented; impossible to assess actual effect size.
papers.underused_note: School construction timing is already a routine consideration in district planning, though not explicitly framed around intergenerational spillovers.
papers.novelty_note: Framing construction timing as an intergenerational human-capital lever is relatively fresh, but the underlying concept (school quality → family outcomes) is established.

papers.title: Investigating How Social and Physical Distance Impact Offender and Victim Mobility with Discrete Choice Modeling
papers.authors: Yuqing Wang, John R. Hipp
papers.idea: Not identifiable from title/abstract alone.
papers.summary: This appears to be a methodological study using discrete choice modeling to analyze the spatial relationships between offenders, victims, and crime locations. Without the abstract, the specific findings and policy implications cannot be assessed.
papers.method: Discrete choice modeling applied to offender and victim mobility data (specific sample, setting, and data source not provided in available information).
papers.scalability_note: Methods paper with unclear real-world application pathway.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; likely minimal implementation cost if insights could be operationalized, but unclear what operational form that would take.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete policy or program lever identified. Would require unpublished abstract/paper to assess whether NYC's crime data or enforcement practices could benefit.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No abstract or results provided; cannot assess demonstrated effect.
papers.underused_note: Appears to be theoretical/methodological work rather than an intervention; deployment status unclear.
papers.novelty_note: Discrete choice modeling of offender/victim mobility is a standard criminological approach, not a novel intervention framing.

papers.title: Evaluating the Costs of School District Secessions
papers.authors: Luisa Godinez-Puig, Karishma Furtado, Dashni Sathasivam
papers.abstract: Public schools are at the heart of a community. They are places where children learn, families meet, and neighbors share a common investment in the next generation. Schools are personal to those they serve, which is why their promise—a high-quality education for all—can seem unfulfilled when communities feel their needs aren’t being met. School district secession occurs when a city, town, or neighborhood decides to leave a larger school district and create a new one. Supporters often frame the move as a way to exert more local control over public schools, but the effects can ripple far beyond the new district’s borders, especially if the fragmentation concentrates resources into one district. Public schools rely on an array of mechanisms, including taxes, zoning, and school boundaries, for funding and organizational structure. But as research shows, these mechanisms can be applied in ways that expand or limit a district’s resources. Because district
papers.idea: Regulate or restrict school district secessions to prevent resource fragmentation that harms equity in public education.
papers.summary: This paper evaluates the costs and effects of school district secessions (when communities leave larger districts to form new ones). The abstract notes that while secessions are framed as enhancing local control, they can fragment resources and concentrate advantages in new districts while harming those left behind.
papers.method: Appears to be descriptive/policy analysis of secession mechanisms and their fiscal/organizational effects; no empirical study design specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Secession is highly jurisdiction-specific; limited by state law and local politics; not easily replicated across cities.
papers.cost_note: Regulatory or legislative changes have low direct cost but may face political resistance.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has a unified citywide district; secession is not currently a live policy option or threat.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents real harms from fragmentation (resource concentration, equity loss) but appears descriptive rather than evaluating a tested intervention with quantified outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Secession regulation is rarely proactive policy in most states; NY has some restrictions but enforcement and prevention remain minimal despite documented equity costs.
papers.novelty_note: Framing secession as a public-safety/equity problem is sound but the regulatory approach is conventional education-policy thinking, not a fresh operational or behavioral insight.

papers.title: Leveraging AI to Measure Mobility-Relevant Competencies from Student Volunteer Reflections
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: This project uses a unique combination of data from a large-scale, lieutenant governor–backed high school service program and rich administrative datasets from Utah. These data will be used to develop measures of self-management, relationship skills, and self-awareness through large-scale, AI-informed text analysis of student reflections. By integrating administrative records with the Panorama Student Survey, the Youth Civic and Character Measures Toolkit, and reflective writing generated through the statewide service initiative, the team will assess whether AI-based measures capture the same socioemotional competencies identified by validated survey instruments and determine which of these skills are most strongly correlated with postsecondary outcomes and civic engagement. Linking AI-derived SEL indicators to mobility-boosting college enrollment patterns and civic behaviors such as voter registration, the study offers an innovative test of whether scalable AI methods can reliably measure the skills most predictive of students’ educational and civic trajectories. Data Sources Data: Student reflections on volunteer
papers.idea: Use AI to analyze student volunteer reflections to measure socioemotional competencies that predict college enrollment and civic engagement.
papers.summary: This is a methods-development study, not a report of findings. The abstract describes a planned project to use AI text analysis on student reflections from a Utah volunteer service program to measure self-management, relationship skills, and self-awareness, then validate these AI measures against existing surveys and correlate them with postsecondary outcomes and civic behaviors.
papers.method: Planned research combining AI-informed text analysis of student reflections from a lieutenant governor–backed Utah high school service program with administrative data, Panorama Student Survey, and Youth Civic and Character Measures Toolkit; exact sample size not stated.
papers.scalability_note: AI text-analysis methods are scalable once developed, but requires volunteer/service program infrastructure.
papers.cost_note: Development and validation costs unclear from abstract; relies on existing administrative datasets and volunteer program.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has service programs but no equivalent statewide lieutenant governor–backed initiative; would require new infrastructure and data integration.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is developmental/correlational, not yet demonstrating causal impact on outcomes; measures prediction but not intervention effectiveness.
papers.underused_note: AI text analysis and SEL measurement are increasingly adopted in ed-tech; not a gap-filling innovation for NYC civic infrastructure.
papers.novelty_note: Applying NLP to reflective writing for SEL measurement is moderately fresh; framing as mobility predictor adds some innovation but builds on established SEL frameworks.

papers.title: Stop Asking Communities to Trust You: Make Public Health Trustworthy
papers.authors: Chioma Nnaji, Lisa Bowleg
papers.idea: Strengthen public health agency trustworthiness through transparent accountability and community co-governance mechanisms.
papers.summary: Without an abstract, the content cannot be summarized. The title suggests a normative argument about rebuilding public trust in health institutions rather than testing a specific intervention.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract provided.
papers.scalability_note: Governance/trust frameworks are context-dependent and difficult to replicate mechanically across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Likely requires institutional redesign and ongoing governance investment; no cost data available.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt community oversight boards or transparency reforms, but applicability depends on actual content and NYC's existing health governance structure.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Framing/mechanism paper with no quantified safety outcomes; trust-building is prerequisite, not direct intervention.
papers.underused_note: Community co-governance in public health is aspirational but rarely resourced or structurally embedded in NYC agencies.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes trust as accountability + power-sharing rather than messaging; moderately fresh for public health practice.

papers.title: Enhancing Policy Impact Through Knowledge Translation: The Role of Innovative Evidence Products
papers.authors:
Ashima Mohan, 
Howard White
papers.abstract: Campbell Systematic Reviews, Volume 21, Issue 4, December 2025.
papers.idea: Improve how research findings are translated and communicated to policymakers to enhance evidence-based policy implementation.
papers.summary: This is a Campbell Collaboration systematic review examining methods and practices for translating research evidence into policy impact. It appears to synthesize existing knowledge about how to better package and communicate research findings to decision-makers.
papers.method: Systematic review of literature on knowledge translation practices and evidence communication strategies (specific sample size and study designs not stated in abstract).
papers.scalability_note: Knowledge translation methods are generalizable but implementation requires institutional buy-in.
papers.cost_note: Primarily involves communication and process redesign; minimal direct costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could adopt improved evidence communication practices across agencies, but abstract provides no concrete NYC-relevant interventions.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Meta-level study about research communication; no direct outcome data on public safety or city life improvements demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Knowledge translation and evidence synthesis are well-established fields; major funders and institutions already invest in this (RAND, Urban Institute, Campbell Collaboration itself).
papers.novelty_note: Knowledge translation is recognized best practice in research-to-policy pipelines; framing is incremental rather than counterintuitive.

papers.title: Is ride-sharing good for environment? Evidence from combining satellite and survey data on U.S. cities
papers.authors: Yoshifumi Konishi, Akari Ono
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; appears to be an empirical assessment of ride-sharing's net environmental impact.
papers.summary: Study combines satellite imagery and survey data to evaluate whether ride-sharing services (Uber/Lyft) improve or worsen urban environmental outcomes in U.S. cities. Without the abstract, the specific findings are unknown, but the title suggests it addresses a policy-relevant question about ride-sharing regulation.
papers.method: Combination of satellite data (likely traffic, congestion, or emissions proxies) and survey responses across multiple U.S. cities; no sample size provided.
papers.scalability_note: Environmental impacts of ride-sharing are city-context-dependent; findings may not generalize uniformly.
papers.cost_note: Ride-sharing regulation is low-cost to implement administratively but has indirect economic effects on consumers and operators.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has substantial ride-sharing use and Taxi & Limousine Commission oversight; findings could inform congestion or emissions policy. However, NYC's transit-rich environment differs from many U.S. cities studied.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Observational study with no intervention tested; descriptive only.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention—purely empirical assessment of ride-sharing externalities.
papers.novelty_note: Using satellite + survey data is methodologically novel, but environmental impact of ride-sharing is well-trodden ground.

papers.title: Amortized Probabilistic Retrieval of Atmospheric CO2 from OCO-2 Spectra Using Deep Learning with Laplace Approximations and Normalizing Flows
papers.authors: Alejandro Calle-Saldarriaga, Felix Jimenez, Jack Grosskreuz, Jiazheng Wang, Jonathan Hobbs, Matthias Katzfuss
papers.abstract: Space-based monitoring of atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) is essential for constraining the global carbon budget. NASA's Orbiting Carbon Observatory-2 (OCO-2) estimates column-averaged dry-air mole fractions of CO2 (XCO2) using high-resolution spectra. However, current operational retrieval algorithms are computationally expensive and do not properly quantify uncertainties. We present a novel deep learning framework that addresses these challenges. Due to the difficulties of ground-truth data for real satellite observations, we develop and validate our approach using a high-fidelity simulation dataset. This dataset, created to support OCO-2 uncertainty quantification (UQ), incorporates realistic forward model errors. Our architecture encodes spectral bands using a multi-branch neural network and estimates posteriors of the full CO2 column or desired summaries thereof using two scalable UQ methods: Laplace approximations and normalizing flows. Our approach has five key advantages relative to operational "full-physics" solvers: (1) Amortization: Inference is orders of magnitude faster, enabling real-time processing of massive data streams; (2) Model error robustness: By training on simulations that explicitly include model discrepancies, our method accounts for systematic errors often neglected by standard inversions; (3) Point estimate accuracy: We achieve superior predictive accuracy compared to baseline methods; (4) Improved UQ: The probabilistic outputs yield better-calibrated uncertainty estimates; and (5) Non-Gaussian posteriors: When utilizing normalizing flows, our framework successfully models complex, asymmetric posterior distributions, overcoming the limitations of the Gaussian assumption. These results suggest that simulation-based deep learning is a viable path toward next-generation operational processing systems.
papers.idea: N/A — methods paper with no city-policy intervention
papers.summary: The paper develops a deep learning framework to retrieve atmospheric CO2 measurements from NASA satellite spectra faster and with better uncertainty quantification than current operational algorithms. The method uses Laplace approximations and normalizing flows to model complex posterior distributions from simulated spectral data.
papers.method: Simulation-based validation using a high-fidelity OCO-2 dataset with realistic forward model errors; deep learning architecture with multi-branch neural network and two UQ methods tested on synthetic data.
papers.scalability_note: Satellite-based monitoring is inherently global and scalable once operational.
papers.cost_note: Software/algorithm; marginal deployment cost once developed.
papers.nyc_note: No local policy lever; global atmospheric monitoring with no city-specific intervention or decision point.
papers.effect_size: Orders of magnitude faster inference; superior predictive accuracy compared to baseline methods; better-calibrated uncertainty estimates
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methods paper for satellite CO2 retrieval; no public-safety or city-life intervention demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is technical infrastructure research, not a deployable civic policy or program.
papers.novelty_note: Novel machine-learning engineering for atmospheric science, but orthogonal to urban public-safety challenges.

papers.title: Long-term childhood poverty in Britain: trends and drivers across the 1991-2017 birth cohorts
papers.abstract: Any experience of childhood poverty affects life chances, but longer exposure is particularly detrimental to education, health, and future earnings. This study examines trends in long-term childhood poverty in Britain. Using a life-course perspective, we tracked poverty from birth to age ten among 1991–2017 birth cohorts. Our findings show that, on average, 17 per cent of children spent at least half of their childhood in poverty. Long-term poverty affected 25 per cent of those born in the early 1990s, markedly declined to 13–14 per cent for cohorts born after the post-1997 welfare reforms, and rose again to 23 per cent for children born following the 2013 austerity reforms. These trends are driven by shifts in the penalties associated with work-family risk factors, rather than by changes in their prevalence. These shifts in penalties reflect broader changes in redistribution and predistribution. The decline in the 1990s was largely due to rising employment and earnings in low-income households, whereas the post-austerity surge stems from reduced redistribution. For cohorts born in the 2000s, social transfers played a substantial role in containing long-term poverty despite worsening predistribution. Overall, the findings show that long-term childhood poverty is a major challenge in Britain and highlight the need to strengthen redistribution and predistribution.
papers.idea: Not actionable from this abstract—no specific intervention or policy lever is proposed.
papers.summary: This study tracked childhood poverty in Britain across birth cohorts from 1991–2017, finding that long-term poverty (≥50% of childhood) affected 25% of early-1990s births, fell to 13–14% after 1997 welfare reforms, then rose to 23% after 2013 austerity cuts. The trends reflect changes in how work, family structure, and government redistribution affect poverty risk, not changes in prevalence of these risk factors themselves.
papers.reach: British birth cohorts 1991–2017; population-level data; exact sample size not stated in abstract
papers.method: Longitudinal observational analysis tracking children from birth to age 10 across successive British birth cohorts; uses administrative/survey data to measure poverty persistence and correlates.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are UK-specific; policy mechanisms (welfare design, employment trends) are transferable but context-dependent.
papers.cost_note: Study itself is low-cost research; implications (strengthened redistribution/predistribution) could range from modest to expensive depending on implementation.
papers.nyc_note: Study is UK-focused; NYC already has robust TANF, SNAP, and child tax credits, but research does not propose NYC-specific levers. Findings on austerity effects are relevant cautionary backdrop.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive epidemiology only; no intervention tested or evaluated.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention—observational trend analysis with no actionable lever for cities to deploy.
papers.novelty_note: Life-course poverty tracking is methodologically sound but frames a well-known problem (childhood poverty harms outcomes) without proposing a novel solution.

papers.title: Inclusive by design: a three-pillar framework for equitable online conferences
papers.abstract: Scientific conferences remain central to scholarly communication, yet participation is constrained by several systemic barriers. Although online conferencing expanded rapidly during the COVID-19 pandemic, there remains limited evidence regarding which organisational practices effectively support equitable participation and sustained engagement. Here, we examine Animal Behaviour Live (ABL), a multi-year international online conference series, as a case study in inclusive conference design. We propose a framework structured around three organisational pillars – who participates, who presents, and who organises – combined with an iterative evaluation of participant engagement. For each pillar, we identify systemic barriers to inclusion and discuss targeted organisational strategies derived from our experience across four conference editions. Synthesising demographic data, platform analytics, and simulations of abstract selection outcomes, we show that ABL sustains broad international participation and long-term asynchronous engagement while increasing representation across geographic regions, gender, and career stages. We further demonstrate that a continent-stratified abstract selection process actively mitigates psychological self-selection biases, such as a gendered confidence gap in format preference. This process also outperformed random or score-only selection approaches in simultaneously balancing scientific quality, gender representation, geographic diversity, and inclusion of researchers self-reporting lower access to the international scientific community. Our findings establish that equitable representation in online conferences does not emerge by default and provide an evidence-informed framework with actionable strategies for designing more inclusive international scientific conferences.
papers.idea: Apply inclusive conference-design principles (stratified selection, accessibility features, asynchronous engagement) to city advisory boards and civic participation forums.
papers.summary: This case study of an online animal-behavior conference examined organizational practices that increased equitable participation across geographic regions, gender, and career stages. The authors found that continent-stratified abstract selection outperformed random or score-only selection in balancing scientific quality with demographic diversity and reduced gendered confidence gaps in format preference.
papers.reach: 4 conference editions; international participation; demographic data, platform analytics, and simulations presented but sample size not stated
papers.method: Observational case study of Animal Behaviour Live conference series examining demographic data, platform analytics, and simulations of abstract selection outcomes across four editions.
papers.scalability_note: Principles could transfer to civic boards, but conference design differs substantially from city governance structures and community input processes.
papers.cost_note: Largely process-design overhead; modest costs for improved platform features and inclusive facilitation training.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has many civic engagement and community board structures; this is a niche intervention targeting scientific conferences, not a core city-service lever. Limited direct applicability to public safety, housing, transit, health, or economic mobility priorities.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Framework shows modest improvements in conference participation metrics, but effects are contextual to academic conferences and lack direct measurement of behavioral or civic outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Inclusive design and accessibility principles are increasingly mainstream in institutional settings; civic participation reform is actively debated but implementation varies.
papers.novelty_note: Stratified selection and accessibility features are well-established diversity practices; application to city boards is straightforward extension rather than conceptual breakthrough.

papers.title: Beyond Individual Vulnerability: A European, Critical Work and Organizational Psychology Perspective on Work Addiction in the Digital Workplace.
papers.abstract: Adopting a European vantage point, both empirically and with respect to the regulatory context, this position paper argues that work addiction in digitally mediated work cannot be adequately understood as a matter of individual vulnerability or as a mechanical effect of technology. Drawing on behavioural-addiction models and on critical work and organizational psychology, it advances a psychosocial and practice-oriented reading in which compulsive work emerges from the interaction of personal dispositions, organizational cultures, role demands, digital tools, and recognition systems. The paper distinguishes work addiction from work engagement and from heavy work investment, locating the construct in the combination of excess and compulsion rather than in working hours. It then reframes digital tools (email, messaging, collaborative platforms, and the smartphone) not as neutral instruments but as facilitators, amplifiers, and maintenance environments, connecting the argument to constructs the field has already named, including workplace telepressure, low psychological detachment, technostress, and the autonomy paradox. The argument is extended to the still-fragmented, partly worker-led adoption of artificial intelligence at work, drawing on European and OECD evidence that points to task intensification rather than wholesale displacement, and to governance that lags adoption. A central claim is that organizations frequently reward continuous availability as professionalism, normalizing compulsion as a virtue and obscuring it as a psychosocial risk. Against this backdrop, recent European and Italian developments on the right to disconnect are read as encouraging but partial signals. The paper closes with propositions for a critical-practice agenda, framing the right to disconnect as an organizational competence rather than an individual hygiene practice.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt 'right to disconnect' policies and organizational guidelines requiring employers to limit after-hours work communication and normalize psychological detachment.
papers.summary: This position paper argues that work addiction in digital workplaces emerges from organizational cultures, role demands, and digital tools rather than individual vulnerability alone. It distinguishes work addiction from heavy work investment, frames digital tools as amplifiers of compulsion, and reviews European 'right to disconnect' policies as partial signals toward organizational change.
papers.method: Non-empirical position paper drawing on behavioral-addiction models, critical work psychology, and review of European regulatory developments and OECD evidence on AI adoption.
papers.scalability_note: Organizational policies and labor regulations are scalable in principle, but implementation depends on employer buy-in and political willingness; limited levers for city government to compel private-sector adoption.
papers.cost_note: Policy and training carry minimal direct costs but may face employer resistance.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could advocate labor policy to the state or require 'right to disconnect' for city employees, but most private employers are outside city jurisdiction; no concrete city-agency intervention proposed.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Position paper with limited empirical evidence specific to NYC public safety; work addiction effects on crime/safety outcomes not directly demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Right-to-disconnect policies gaining traction in EU and some US cities (NYC Council has considered bills); not a neglected concept but implementation remains sparse.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes work addiction as organizational/systemic rather than individual; 'right to disconnect' framing is fresh for US context but established in Europe.

papers.title: How More People Are Supporting Their Aging Parents Financially, and How It Might Affect Their Financial Futures
papers.authors: Mingli Zhong
papers.abstract: Intergenerational financial support doesn’t flow only from parent to child. According to a new Urban Institute brief, more adult children are providing financial assistance and elder care to aging parents—a phenomenon known as “reverse transfers.” In an analysis of the Health and Retirement Study (HRS) supported by AARP, we find that about 13 percent of adult children provided financial support to aging parents between 2010 and 2022. That’s more than double the share that provided support before 2010 (5 percent). These transfers can help older adults meet their basic needs as public safety nets and retirement savings fall short in a time of heightened affordability pressures. But they may simultaneously limit younger adults’ ability to save, invest, or support their own children, potentially hindering upward mobility and wealth building across generations. Older adults of color are more likely to receive assistance from a child, reflecting systemic barriers to wealth building
papers.idea: Cities could establish reverse-transfer financial counseling or elder-care subsidy programs to reduce burden on adult children supporting aging parents.
papers.summary: Between 2010 and 2022, the share of adult children providing financial support to aging parents more than doubled from 5% to 13%, a trend called 'reverse transfers.' While this helps older adults meet basic needs, it may constrain younger adults' savings, investment, and wealth building—effects that disproportionately affect families of color due to systemic wealth barriers.
papers.method: Analysis of the Health and Retirement Study (HRS) survey data from 2010–2022, supported by AARP.
papers.scalability_note: Financial counseling and elder subsidies exist but would require redesign; reverse-transfer support is a niche intervention.
papers.cost_note: Elder care and income-support programs are costly; abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has aging and poverty services but no explicit reverse-transfer policy lever; would require new program design.
papers.effect_size: 13% of adult children provided financial support to aging parents between 2010-2022, more than double the 5% share before 2010
papers.reach: Health and Retirement Study (HRS) participants from 2010-2022
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents trend and correlates, not causal impact of proposed intervention; no direct evidence that counseling/subsidies reduce burden or improve outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Many cities/nonprofits already offer elder-care navigation and financial counseling; idea is sensible but not novel enough to warrant 'underused' rating.
papers.novelty_note: Reverse-transfer framing is interesting, but proposal (financial counseling + subsidies) is conventional municipal/social-service response.

papers.title: "Stuck in a Spiral": Shame and Guilt as Social Regulators of AI Use in Computing Education
papers.authors: Kate Hamilton, Irene Hou, Dev Patel, Sheena Nnam, Hena Patel, Stephen MacNeil
papers.abstract: While prior work has examined patterns of adoption and social norms around AI use, less is known about how emotional factors, such as shame and guilt, shape students use of AI tools. We present an interview study with 19 computing students through a functionalist perspective of shame and guilt, which interprets emotions as social signals that regulate behavior. Our findings show that these emotions regulate when and how students make their use visible, as they engage in hiding behaviors and selective disclosure. Students described shaming themselves, their peers, and even faculty for using AI. Shame and guilt often coexist with continued AI use, creating cycles of reduced agency and moral tension rather than promoting behavior change. Students described feeling tensions between their AI use and their identities as competent, hardworking, or ethical computing students. Students also used language and metaphors of addiction to describe their experiences. These results highlight the need to consider the socio-emotional aspects of AI use, which may be influenced by how AI policies are implemented and enforced. We discuss classroom practices that can foster healthy, open discussion and support responsible AI use.
papers.idea: Implement classroom policies and practices that foster open, non-punitive discussion of AI tool use to reduce shame-driven hiding behaviors and support responsible student adoption.
papers.summary: An interview study of 19 computing students found that shame and guilt regulate when students hide or disclose their AI tool use, often coexisting with continued use rather than changing behavior. Students experienced moral tension between AI use and their identities as competent or ethical, with some using addiction language to describe their experiences.
papers.method: Qualitative interview study with 19 computing students, analyzed through a functionalist lens of shame and guilt as social regulators.
papers.scalability_note: Classroom practices are implementable in education systems but highly dependent on institutional buy-in and faculty training.
papers.cost_note: Low cost—primarily requires curriculum and policy redesign, faculty professional development, and facilitated classroom discussion.
papers.nyc_note: NYC Department of Education could adopt in public schools, but limited direct city-policy lever; primarily a district/school-level intervention.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative interview study (n=19) identifying emotional mechanisms; no intervention tested or outcome measured.
papers.underused_note: Core insight (shame/guilt reduce openness) is already well-established in behavioral science; classroom discussion policies are standard practice.
papers.novelty_note: Applies familiar shame-reduction framework to AI adoption context; lacks counterintuitive framing or mechanism.

papers.title: Mod-Guide: An LLM-based Content Moderation Feedback System to Address Insensitive Speech toward Indigenous Ethnic and Religious Minority Communities
papers.authors: Dipto Das, Achhiya Sultana, Ankit Singh Chauhan, Saadia Binte Alam, Mohammad Shidujaman, Shion Guha, Sunandan Chakraborty, Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed
papers.abstract: Language operates as a mechanism of both marginalization and resistance, especially for minority communities navigating insensitive and harmful speech online. As content moderation increasingly depends on large language models (LLMs), concerns arise about whether these systems can recognize culturally insensitive speech-language that disregards or marginalizes the cultural and religious perspectives of historically underrepresented communities, often through implicit erasure, misrepresentation, or normative framing, rather than overt hostility. Focusing on Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities -- the country's largest religious and Indigenous ethnic minorities, respectively -- this paper investigates the epistemic limits of LLM-based moderation systems and explores methods for incorporating minority perspectives. We co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech with community members and integrated their narratives into moderation pipelines using retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Our tool, Mod-Guide, improves LLM sensitivity to minority viewpoints by leveraging contextual cues derived from lived experience. Through mixed-method evaluations involving both minority and majority participants, we demonstrate that RAG-enhanced moderation responses are more contextually accurate and perceived differently across ethnic lines. This work advances research in human-computer interaction, AI ethics, and social computing by foregrounding restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion in the design of content moderation systems.
papers.idea: Integrate community-informed, retrieval-augmented generation into social media content moderation systems to better flag culturally insensitive speech toward minority communities.
papers.summary: The researchers co-created a corpus of insensitive speech with Hindu and Chakma community members in Bangladesh and tested a tool (Mod-Guide) that uses RAG to enhance LLM-based content moderation. The system showed improved contextual accuracy and was perceived differently across ethnic lines in mixed-method evaluation.
papers.method: Mixed-method co-design study with Hindu and Chakma communities in Bangladesh; corpus creation with community members; evaluation involving both minority and majority participants; quantitative and qualitative assessment of moderation outputs.
papers.scalability_note: Replicable approach but requires community-specific corpus design for each context; platform-dependent deployment.
papers.cost_note: Initial co-design and corpus building is labor-intensive; deployment cost low once built.
papers.nyc_note: Study focused on Bangladesh context; would require NYC-specific minority community engagement and corpus work; depends on platform policies outside city control.
papers.effect_size: RAG-enhanced moderation responses are more contextually accurate and perceived differently across ethnic lines
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Proof-of-concept improvement in LLM sensitivity; no real-world deployment outcomes, crime/safety impact, or behavioral change data.
papers.underused_note: Content moderation R&D is well-funded by platforms; this is a technical refinement rather than an underfunded intervention gap.
papers.novelty_note: Genuinely fresh framing: centering hermeneutical inclusion and restorative justice in AI moderation design, not just accuracy metrics.

papers.title: State Profiles of Medicaid Managed Care and Type 2 Diabetes
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Type 2 diabetes is among the most prevalent and costly chronic conditions covered by Medicaid, affecting a significant share of the low-income adults who rely on the program for coverage and care. Most states deliver Medicaid benefits through comprehensive, risk-based managed care, contracting with managed care organizations (MCOs) to coordinate and provide services for enrolled beneficiaries. State Medicaid agencies set program requirements and payment requirements, while MCOs administer covered benefits within those parameters. Many MCOs are owned by larger parent companies—including both for-profit and nonprofit entities—that operate across multiple states. We developed a series of state profiles to provide a structured, comparable overview of how comprehensive Medicaid managed care programs address type 2 diabetes. The profiles focus on programs using this model to serve adults without disabilities; we do not include programs serving only children or individuals with disabilities. Each profile covers five areas: the structure and history of the
papers.idea: Cities could benchmark Medicaid managed care diabetes programs against state profiles to identify best-practice care coordination models.
papers.summary: This work creates comparable state-level profiles of how Medicaid managed care organizations (MCOs) address type 2 diabetes in low-income adults. The profiles map program structure, MCO ownership, payment models, and benefit design across states.
papers.method: Structured comparative state-profile analysis of Medicaid managed care program documentation and design parameters; no empirical outcome data or evaluation mentioned in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Profile methodology is replicable across jurisdictions; but requires state-level policy authority to implement changes.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost depends on MCO contract renegotiation; profiling itself is low-cost research.
papers.nyc_note: NYC does not set Medicaid policy (state does); city has limited direct leverage over MCO contracting. Value mainly as input to state advocacy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Profiling/benchmarking study with no reported intervention outcomes; descriptive methodology only.
papers.underused_note: Benchmarking and best-practice sharing are standard in healthcare administration; modest gap suggests modest underuse rather than major neglect.
papers.novelty_note: State-level profiling is conventional healthcare policy analysis; no novel mechanism or counterintuitive insight demonstrated.

papers.title: Private Equity Acquisitions In Primary Care: Changes In Utilization, Spending, And Workforce
papers.authors: Yashaswini Singh, Meehir N. Dixit, Amal N. Trivedi, Christopher M. Whaley
papers.idea: Monitor and regulate private equity acquisitions of primary care practices to prevent adverse changes in utilization, spending, and workforce.
papers.summary: This study examines how private equity ownership of primary care practices affects clinical utilization patterns, healthcare spending, and staffing. Without an abstract, the specific findings and effect sizes cannot be assessed.
papers.method: Unknown; title suggests observational analysis of changes following PE acquisitions, but methodology cannot be determined from title alone.
papers.scalability_note: Requires state/federal health policy levers and healthcare financing regulation; limited direct city control without state partnership.
papers.cost_note: Regulatory oversight costs modest; no per-unit cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited direct authority over primary care ownership structures; would require state or federal policy action, though city could coordinate with healthcare systems and insurers.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents changes post-acquisition but lacks causal identification of harms or intervention efficacy.
papers.underused_note: Healthcare regulation and PE oversight exist; issue is debated in policy circles but not a neglected gap.
papers.novelty_note: PE-driven healthcare consolidation concerns are well-documented in health policy; framing as public-safety intervention is thin.

papers.title: Risks and Priorities in Federal Disability Measurement
papers.authors: Alexis Weaver, Harley Webley, Nina Russell, Amanda Hermans, Susan J. Popkin, Lizzy Ferrara
papers.abstract: Recent shifts in the federal approach to collecting and using data have raised new concerns about the future of how we measure and understand disabled people, their experiences, and their protections. The Informing Federal Disability Data Measurement project initially sought to learn more about how federal agencies define and measure disability in their work, including in surveys, administrative data, and program implementation. Then in 2025, amid federal changes to programs and data collection, we convened a roundtable of disability measurement stakeholders to assess the implications of the current moment, discuss emerging threats and gaps in disability data, and identify key priorities. This brief shares insights from the roundtable and follow-up survey and stakeholders’ assessments of the biggest risks and priorities for disability research and advocacy. Why This Matters Access to complete and inclusive data on the experiences of people with disabilities is essential for informing public resource allocation, local planning
papers.idea: Standardize federal disability data collection across agencies to improve measurement and inform resource allocation for disabled populations.
papers.summary: This brief reports on a roundtable convened by the Urban Institute to assess federal disability measurement practices and data collection methods across agencies. Stakeholders identified emerging risks and gaps in disability data collection amid 2025 federal policy changes and recommended priorities for improving disability research and advocacy.
papers.method: Qualitative stakeholder roundtable discussion followed by survey of disability measurement experts; no empirical data collection or program evaluation.
papers.scalability_note: Addresses federal data standards; city implementation would be indirect and dependent on federal changes.
papers.cost_note: Unclear; framed as measurement/advocacy work, not a direct service intervention.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally reform federal disability measurement; applicability limited to alignment with future federal standards if adopted.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Focuses on data infrastructure and measurement standardization; no direct evidence of public-safety or crime-reduction outcomes presented.
papers.underused_note: Data standardization is discussed in policy circles but inconsistently implemented; moderate gap between need and deployment.
papers.novelty_note: Standardizing federal data collection is established best practice; framing is conventional administrative reform.

papers.title: Project-Level Learning Lowers the Conflict Cost Premium of Energy Access
