papers.abstract: Energy access in conflict-affected states carries a cost premium — yet there is little evidence of its magnitude and whether it can, at least partially, be mitigated. Analyzing 923 World Bank–financed solar photovoltaic installations across the Republic of Yemen between 2019 and 2025, this analysis finds that project-level learning systematically reduces conflict-linked costs, including in the most volatile regions. The aggregate price decomposition attributes 16.8 percentage points of cost reduction to project-level factors. More generally, a Shapley Machine Learning decomposition of project-level cost variation confirms that project-level learning is the most powerful predictor, explaining 45.2 percent of cost variation. Critically, the conflict-cost relationship evolves over successive procurement cycles: early packages exhibit a significant positive conflict premium, which is gradually mitigated. Cost trajectories converge regardless of whether governorates experienced escalating or de-escalating violence, confirming that learning operates independently of security trends.
papers.idea: Use iterative project-level learning from successive procurement cycles to reduce costs of renewable energy infrastructure in conflict-affected regions.
papers.summary: This World Bank study analyzed 923 solar PV installations in Yemen (2019–2025) and found that project-level learning systematically reduces conflict-linked cost premiums over successive procurement cycles. Project-level factors explained 16.8 percentage points of aggregate cost reduction and 45.2% of overall cost variation, with cost trajectories converging regardless of violence trends.
papers.effect_size: 16.8 percentage points cost reduction attributable to project-level learning; 45.2% of cost variation explained by project-level factors
papers.method: Observational analysis of World Bank–financed solar PV projects in Yemen between 2019 and 2025, using price decomposition and Shapley Machine Learning to isolate cost drivers.
papers.scalability_note: Study addresses conflict-zone energy access; relevance to non-conflict urban settings unclear; Yemen-specific context limits generalizability.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit costs provided; study suggests learning reduces premiums but absolute costs unknown.
papers.nyc_note: NYC is not conflict-affected; study addresses energy access in war-torn regions with no direct lever for city policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study demonstrates real cost reduction (16.8–45.2%) but in narrow context (Yemen solar only); unclear if mechanism translates to NYC public-safety interventions or urban US settings.
papers.underused_note: This is a World Bank procurement-optimization finding for developing-world energy access—not a public-safety or civic intervention relevant to NYC governance.
papers.novelty_note: Iterative learning reducing project costs is intuitive in supply-chain management; framing as conflict-mitigation is incremental, not a new safety or social-policy framework.

papers.title: Cassatts in the Attic: Is There a Gender Gap in the Commercialization of Science
papers.authors: Marlène Koffi, Matt Marx
papers.abstract: We analyze nearly 70 million scientific articles to estimate the gender dynamics in the commercialization of science. We report a gender gap of 14 percent, which does not appear to be explained either by the quality of the science or by its commercial potential. The gap is widest among papers with female last authors (i.e., lab head or principal investigator), even when publishing high-quality science. However, the gap vanishes when authors self-commercialize discoveries via new ventures, and the gap is reduced when commercializing in cooperation with firms that are smaller or that have more female inventors. (JEL D22, I23, J16, O31, O34)
papers.idea: Cities could incentivize partnerships between women-led research institutions and small/female-owned firms to increase commercialization of discoveries.
papers.summary: This study analyzed 70 million scientific articles to document a 14% gender gap in commercialization of science, where discoveries by female lab leaders are commercialized less often despite equal quality. The gap disappears when women self-commercialize via startups and shrinks when partnering with smaller firms or those with more female inventors.
papers.effect_size: 14% gender gap in commercialization; gap vanishes under self-commercialization pathway
papers.method: Observational analysis of nearly 70 million scientific articles to measure gender differences in commercialization outcomes, disaggregated by authorship position and firm/partnership characteristics.
papers.scalability_note: Requires coordination across academic institutions, venture capital, and firms; primarily a state/federal policy domain; limited direct city lever.
papers.cost_note: Matchmaking programs or incentives for partnerships would be moderate cost; unclear per-unit benefit.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has research institutions and startup ecosystem, but commercialization policy is mainly state/federal. City could only convene or provide minor incentives.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents a real 14% commercialization gap but is observational/correlational, not an intervention with measured outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Gender equity in STEM and entrepreneurship support already receives substantial attention; this specific partnership lever is modestly underexplored but not novel in practice.
papers.novelty_note: Connecting gender bias to tech-transfer mechanisms is incremental; the core insight (women-led labs undercommercialized) is increasingly recognized in policy circles.

papers.title: Trends in Cannabis Retailer Density and Proximity to Pregnant Individuals in California, 2018–2024
papers.authors: Kelly C. Young-Wolff, Sara R. Adams, Joshua R. Nugent, Natalie E. Slama, Aurash J. Soroosh, Lynn D. Silver, Alisa A. Padon
papers.idea: Regulate cannabis retailer density and location to minimize exposure near pregnant individuals.
papers.summary: This appears to be a descriptive study tracking trends in cannabis retailer density across California and measuring geographic proximity to pregnant individuals over a 6-year period. Without an abstract, the specific findings, effect sizes, and policy recommendations are unclear.
papers.method: Descriptive trend analysis of cannabis retailer locations and proximity to pregnant individuals in California, 2018–2024; specific methods not provided in title/abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Retailer regulation is implementable at city/state level but requires legislative change and enforcement capacity.
papers.cost_note: Likely moderate cost (zoning enforcement, data systems); not specified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has licensed cannabis retail (newly legalized), but study is CA-specific; applicability depends on whether findings justify location-based restrictions near maternity care sites or high-pregnancy-density areas.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive trend study with no causal effect evaluation; documents retailer density patterns but provides no evidence that regulation would reduce adverse outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Cannabis retail regulation is actively debated and implemented across major cities; this specific proximity-to-pregnancy angle is niche but builds on existing regulatory frameworks already in use.
papers.novelty_note: Frames cannabis regulation through vulnerable-population lens (pregnant individuals) rather than typical youth-access or nuisance-based rationales; moderately fresh angle but not fundamentally new thinking.

papers.title: Urban internal structures and gender commuting gap: Evidence from China
papers.authors: Yalin Wang, Xiaofang Dong
papers.idea: Redesign urban neighborhoods to reduce gender commuting disparities by co-locating jobs, housing, and services.
papers.summary: This study examines the relationship between urban spatial structure (job and housing distribution) and differences in commuting patterns between men and women in Chinese cities. The research likely documents that women face longer or more complex commutes due to how neighborhoods are organized.
papers.method: Observational analysis of urban structure and commuting data in China; no abstract available so design details cannot be confirmed.
papers.scalability_note: Urban redesign is slow and capital-intensive; applicable mainly to greenfield development or major redevelopment zones.
papers.cost_note: Large-scale urban restructuring requires sustained public investment; no unit costs provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's built environment is mostly fixed; insights may apply only to limited redevelopment projects in outer boroughs or new mixed-use districts.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is from China with different labor-market and urban-planning contexts; limited direct evidence on public-safety outcomes for NYC.
papers.underused_note: Mixed-use zoning and job-housing balance are discussed in urban planning but rarely prioritized as a safety or equity intervention in US cities.
papers.novelty_note: Co-location of jobs, housing, and services is a standard New Urbanism / mixed-use development principle; framing as gender equity is slightly fresher but not novel.

papers.title: Using agent-based simulations to anticipate risks and mitigation measures in platform governance
papers.abstract: Risk analysis and mitigation in platform governance are constrained by the difficulties of testing changes to platforms’ systems and accessing data. Other fields, ranging from public health to economics, as well as an emerging body of platform research, use agent-based modeling to overcome this obstacle by empowering researchers and regulators to test changes virtually. However, it is still unclear how and under what conditions agent-based modeling can effectively support platform governance. This article aims to reduce this uncertainty in two ways. First, it provides a theoretical framework on the benefits and risks of using agent-based modeling in platform governance, drawing on literature on platform risk assessment, the use of agent-based modeling in other fields, and anticipatory governance and evidence-based policymaking. Second, to validate this framework and clarify the conditions under which regulators can rely on evidence produced by agent-based models, the article reports the results of interviews with 18 regulators. The article finds that agent-based simulations have significant potential to complement other sources of evidence used in platform risk assessment. Their main added value is that they allow researchers and regulators to more easily, ethically, and independently explore the potential impacts of changes to platform design, and provide interactive and visual scenarios that concretize abstract platform dynamics.
papers.idea: Cities could use agent-based modeling simulations to test changes to platform policies before implementation.
papers.summary: This is a methods paper that develops a theoretical framework for using agent-based modeling (ABM) to assess risks in platform governance and validates it through interviews with 18 regulators. The authors argue ABM allows regulators to ethically and independently test platform design changes virtually, providing interactive scenarios that clarify abstract platform dynamics.
papers.method: Theoretical framework development combined with qualitative interviews of 18 regulators; no empirical intervention or test.
papers.scalability_note: The methodology itself is scalable, but the abstract provides no evidence it has been tested at scale or deployed.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost not discussed; ABM development and validation could be expensive.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant only if NYC oversees platform governance (e.g., food delivery, rideshare). No concrete NYC-ready tool or intervention presented.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework + qualitative interviews show potential, but no empirical demonstration of actual risk reduction or policy improvement in real-world platform governance.
papers.underused_note: Agent-based modeling exists in academia but rarely integrated into NYC municipal or platform-policy decisions; moderate adoption barriers (technical capacity, data access).
papers.novelty_note: Applying ABM to platform governance is emerging; framing simulation as ethical alternative to live experimentation is useful, but ABM itself is not new.

papers.title: Incumbent Advantage: Brand Bias and Cognitive Manipulation Dynamics in LLM Recommendation Systems
papers.authors: Xi Chu, Yupeng Hou
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are becoming a major way for consumers to find products, but we do not yet understand how brands compete in this new channel. We study brand dynamics in LLM recommendations using skincare products -- a category where consumers cannot easily judge quality before buying and must rely on brand reputation -- across three commercial LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet, Gemini 3 Flash), with a robustness check on search goods. In three experiments, we find: (1) a Conditional Monopoly where well-known brands get recommended 100% of the time (IAI = 10.0) when all products have the same specifications, but this dominance disappears with less than a +0.1-star rating advantage for a competitor; (2) authority-style marketing language, including fabricated clinical-evidence claims, breaks this monopoly at a Bias Surplus Value equal to +0.17 rating points, with each model responding differently; and (3) a social dilemma in multi-brand GEO competition: when all brands adopt the same optimization strategy, individual payoff falls from +0.802 to +0.007 in our payoff proxy, and non-participating brands receive zero recommendations in our tests. Our results suggest that generative engine optimization (GEO) should be studied not only as a security risk, but also as an emerging marketing practice that shapes market competition.
papers.idea: Cities could regulate or audit LLM-generated product recommendations to prevent deceptive marketing claims and ensure fair competition.
papers.summary: The study found that well-known brands receive disproportionate recommendation rates from commercial LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini), but this dominance breaks down with minimal rating advantages or authority-style marketing language. The research identifies a potential market competition distortion where brands optimize for LLM recommendation algorithms, risking fabricated claims and reduced diversity of recommendations.
papers.method: Experimental study testing three commercial LLMs with skincare products under different rating and marketing language conditions, plus robustness checks on search goods.
papers.scalability_note: Requires federal/state regulation of AI systems; city-level enforcement limited and fragmented.
papers.cost_note: Auditing and enforcement would require new regulatory infrastructure but no direct subsidy.
papers.nyc_note: Consumer protection falls mainly to state/federal regulators; NYC has limited jurisdiction over AI platforms' recommendation algorithms.
papers.effect_size: Well-known brands recommended 100% of the time (IAI = 10.0) with same specifications; dominance disappears with <+0.1-star rating advantage for competitor; authority marketing breaks monopoly at +0.17 rating points bias surplus value
papers.reach: 3 commercial LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet, Gemini 3 Flash) tested with skincare products
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Demonstrates clear LLM recommendation bias mechanics, but limited real-world harm evidence; mostly methodological contribution to understanding a nascent problem.
papers.underused_note: LLM recommendation regulation is virtually absent in US cities; NYC has no audit framework for algorithmic fairness in generative AI marketing channels.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes LLM-generated recommendations as an emerging market-competition and consumer-protection issue distinct from search-engine SEO; surfaces 'generative engine optimization' as a regulatory blind spot.

papers.title: Towards Auditing AI Systems in the Wild
papers.authors: Aditya T. Vadlamani, Anutam Srinivasan, Srinivasan Parthasarathy
papers.abstract: AI systems are increasingly deployed in real-world settings where their behavior is shaped by dynamic environments, evolving data distributions, and complex interactions with users and infrastructure. Traditional machine learning evaluation focuses on benchmarks and operates within sandboxed environments, providing only a limited view of the true system behavior in the wild. We argue for the development of principled auditing frameworks that monitor deployed AI systems throughout their lifecycle. We further propose framing auditing as a statistical problem of monitoring constraint violations under uncertainty, where desired properties (e.g., fairness and safety) are treated as risk-controlled constraints that must be continuously evaluated as systems evolve through iterative feedback. This perspective highlights the need for uncertainty-aware monitoring methods, socio-technical specifications of audit criteria, and auditing infrastructures that enable ongoing oversight of AI systems in the wild.
papers.idea: Develop continuous auditing frameworks to monitor deployed AI systems for fairness and safety violations in real-world environments.
papers.summary: This is a methods/framework paper arguing for principled auditing approaches to monitor AI systems as they operate in dynamic real-world settings. The authors propose treating fairness and safety properties as risk-controlled constraints monitored through uncertainty-aware methods, rather than relying only on static benchmarks.
papers.method: Non-empirical position paper proposing a conceptual framework; no empirical study, data analysis, or real-world deployment testing described.
papers.scalability_note: Framework could apply across sectors but requires institutional adoption and context-specific implementation.
papers.cost_note: Infrastructure costs unclear; depends on auditing tool development and staffing needs.
papers.nyc_note: Abstract contains no concrete guidance on implementing audits in NYC agencies; would require substantial downstream work to operationalize.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Conceptual framework without empirical public-safety outcomes demonstrated; addresses monitoring infrastructure rather than proven crime/harm reduction.
papers.underused_note: Some AI audit initiatives exist (e.g., NYC's algorithmic accountability law), but continuous real-world monitoring frameworks remain nascent and underfunded in most jurisdictions.
papers.novelty_note: Frames AI oversight as dynamic statistical monitoring rather than one-time evaluation; 'constraint violation' framing is relatively fresh, though audit principles themselves are established.

papers.title: Rational choice models of crime decision‐making: A flexible utility framework
papers.authors: Shaina Herman, Timothy C. Barnum, Daniel S. Nagin, Greg Pogarsky
papers.abstract: Abstract Rational choice models of the decision to commit a crime have been prominent in the criminological literature for decades. Their theoretical components, however, remain fragmented, leading to misconceptions about the framework's explanatory power and policy relevance. The present paper aims to synthesize these fragmented theoretical elements and demonstrate the flexibility of rational choice models in accommodating various recent advancements in research on crime decision‐making. To do so, we construct a series of equations that formalize the core mechanisms underlying the decision to offend. We begin with a baseline equation derived from Beccaria's classical deterrence model—centered on the certainty, severity, and celerity of legal sanctions—which is extended to include noncriminal alternatives to crime. We then build on this structure to account for the situational bases for risk perception, discrete stages of the criminal justice process, nonlinear risk processing, ambiguity, moral disutility, safety concerns, and visceral influences such as emotion. The result is a series of compact yet flexible models that address how incentives and constraints shape criminal behavior and that can be used to specify statistical models to test key model predictions.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical framework paper with no specific intervention.
papers.summary: The authors synthesize rational choice theory in criminology by formalizing decision-making equations that extend classical deterrence models to include noncriminal alternatives, risk perception, emotion, and moral factors. The result is a flexible set of theoretical models intended to guide future empirical testing of how incentives and constraints shape criminal behavior.
papers.method: Non-empirical; theoretical synthesis and formalization of existing rational choice models in criminology literature; no new data collection or empirical test reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical framework; requires downstream empirical validation and policy translation.
papers.cost_note: Framework itself is zero-cost, but any intervention derived from it would depend on implementation design.
papers.nyc_note: This is foundational theory, not a policy or program; NYC would need applied research translating it into specific interventions before adoption.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework only; no empirical intervention or outcome data presented.
papers.underused_note: Rational choice theory is well-established in criminology; this is a refinement of existing models, not a novel underdeployed approach.
papers.novelty_note: Synthesizes and formalizes existing rational choice elements; modest theoretical advancement but not a fundamentally new lens on crime.

papers.title: ‘If I don’t like the cats then they have to go’: navigating pet unfriendly rental accommodation
papers.authors: Zoei Sutton
papers.idea: Establish pet-friendly rental housing policies or mediation services to reduce housing instability for renters with pets.
papers.summary: No abstract available; title suggests a qualitative study of how pet restrictions affect renters' housing security and how renters navigate landlord conflicts over pets.
papers.method: Not determinable from title alone; appears qualitative given framing.
papers.scalability_note: Pet policy reform is plausible but limited by landlord property rights and requires sustained political will; medium-to-hard implementation.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; likely low direct cost if policy-based, but enforcement/mediation costs unclear.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has some tenant protections; pet-friendly housing is a niche issue relative to affordability and homelessness. Could complement existing housing policy but not primary lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical effect data presented; descriptive study of housing barriers rather than intervention outcome.
papers.underused_note: Some pet-friendly housing exists but policies remain fragmented; mediation services rare; moderate gap between need and provision.
papers.novelty_note: Pet-friendly housing is established concept; framing as homelessness/instability prevention is slightly fresh but not counterintuitive.

papers.title: More Households Are Using Buy Now, Pay Later. What Does That Mean for Homeownership?
papers.authors: Michael Neal
papers.abstract: Buy now, pay later (BNPL) loan options have grown in popularity, especially among younger consumers. With BNPL, a person can purchase something with little or no initial payment and pay off the balance over a small number of payments. According to a report coauthored by the Urban Institute and the JPMorganChase Institute, the data show that between 2019 and 2023, the amount of BNPL spending increased 37-fold, from $2 billion to $75 billion. But even as use of this consumer product grows, it is unclear how it might affect borrowers’ credit. Unlike credit cards, most BNPL loans do not require a hard credit inquiry and often aren’t reported to major credit bureaus, so their use may not uniformly affect credit characteristics like a credit score or debt-to-income ratio. In particular, this lack of visibility could complicate a borrower’s ability to qualify for or repay a mortgage. Last summer, the US
papers.idea: Cities could require BNPL lenders to report to credit bureaus and conduct hard inquiries to preserve mortgage qualification visibility for lower-income borrowers.
papers.summary: Between 2019 and 2023, Buy Now, Pay Later spending grew 37-fold to $75 billion, especially among younger consumers. Most BNPL loans lack hard credit inquiries and credit bureau reporting, creating a blind spot that could harm borrowers' credit scores and debt-to-income ratios needed for mortgage qualification.
papers.method: Analysis of spending data from Urban Institute and JPMorgan Chase Institute covering 2019–2023 period; appears descriptive rather than causal.
papers.scalability_note: BNPL credit reporting is federally regulated; cities have limited direct lever over lender practices without state/federal coordination.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed or costed in abstract; regulatory enforcement would be modest.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally mandate credit reporting or hard inquiries; this requires state/federal action or DFS coordination, limiting city-level action.
papers.effect_size: 37-fold increase in BNPL spending from $2 billion to $75 billion
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical risk identified but no empirical outcome data on actual homeownership impact; mechanism is plausible but effect size unknown.
papers.underused_note: BNPL credit reporting is emerging as a regulatory issue (CFPB attention) but localized city-level interventions are rare; modest policy traction nationally.
papers.novelty_note: Straightforward regulatory fix (credit bureau reporting); addresses a real gap but follows conventional consumer-protection logic rather than innovating problem-solving approach.

papers.title: Brady Meixell on Opportunity Zone goals and outcomes
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: The Opportunity Zone (OZ) program was established as part of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act to revitalize low-income rural and urban communities through incentivized long-term investments. Brady Meixell, a housing and communities expert at the Urban Institute, spoke with the Portland Press Herald about how existing research on the program shows that OZ investments are often driven by private market considerations rather than community needs. “You’re not shaping the market, you’re kind of layering on top of existing markets,” said Meixell. For inquiries, please email media@urban.org.
papers.idea: Cities could design Opportunity Zone incentives to prioritize community-identified needs rather than relying solely on private market-driven investment.
papers.summary: Brady Meixell of the Urban Institute discusses findings on Opportunity Zones, a federal tax incentive program. The research indicates that OZ investments tend to follow existing market dynamics rather than address community needs, suggesting a misalignment between program intent and outcomes.
papers.method: Expert commentary on existing research (not original empirical study); interview-based piece for press outlet.
papers.scalability_note: OZ policy is federal; cities have limited direct control but could coordinate with investors on outcomes.
papers.cost_note: Cost/benefit unclear; OZ relies on private capital, but effectiveness per dollar spent is questioned.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot unilaterally reform federal OZ program; could only influence local OZ project selection/oversight.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Commentary on program design rather than evaluated intervention; no empirical effect data presented.
papers.underused_note: OZ program is active but community-responsive design principles are underapplied; some cities experimenting but not mainstream practice.
papers.novelty_note: Community-needs-first OZ design is plausible but not radically new; reframing investment priorities is moderate departure from status quo.

papers.title: A Multi-Scalar Causal Approach across Space and Time: An Application to the COVID-19 Pandemic in São Paulo State, Brazil
papers.authors: Katsuo Kogure, Yoshito Takasaki
papers.idea: Unable to determine from title and absent abstract.
papers.summary: No abstract provided. Title suggests a multi-scalar causal analysis of COVID-19 spread patterns across space and time in São Paulo State, but connection to actionable public safety or city policy interventions is unclear.
papers.method: Unable to assess; no abstract available. Appears to be quantitative analysis of pandemic data in Brazilian state.
papers.scalability_note: COVID-19 pandemic analysis likely time-bound and context-specific to Brazil.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; likely uses existing epidemiological data.
papers.nyc_note: Published in criminology journal but focuses on pandemic spread, not crime or safety interventions. Limited NYC relevance without seeing full paper.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No public-safety intervention described; epidemiological modeling study.
papers.underused_note: Not a civic intervention; inapplicable to NYC policy context.
papers.novelty_note: Standard spatial-temporal epidemiological methodology.

papers.title: Selection and Facilitation: Is the Gang Membership-Psychopathic Traits Link a Product of Individual Differences, Social Influences, or Both?
papers.authors: Jennifer J. Tostlebe
papers.abstract: Objectives The issue of psychopathology among gang populations is controversial. Classic gang scholarship points to psychopathic traits as time-stable characteristics of those who join gangs, others argue gangs facilitate attitudes and behaviors endogenous to psychopathy, stisll others reject a gang-psychopathic traits link. Building off an interdisciplinary theoretical basis that adapts a tripartite theoretical model (i.e., selection, facilitation, and enhancement) on the gang membership-offending link to the mixed positions on the gang membership-psychopathic traits relationship, this study tests selection and facilitation models. Methods Using longitudinal data from Pathways to Desistance, I examine whether psychopathic traits are an antecedent and/or consequence of gang membership. Specifically, I test whether (a) there are between individual differences in psychopathic traits that contribute to selection into gangs and (b) gangs facilitate within-individual change in psychopathic traits among their members. Results The findings suggest that the enhancement model (i.e., both selection and facilitation) best represents the relationship between gang membership and psychopathic traits. Conclusions Analyzing the role of individual propensities—like psychopathic traits—and social-environmental influences—like gang membership—is critical for understanding crime and criminality. This study demonstrates that these factors do not operate in isolation but instead interact bidirectionally. By moving beyond the selection-facilitation dichotomy, this study contributes to a more integrated framework for explaining problem behaviors and helps establish best practices for prevention and rehabilitation.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: N/A — study is descriptive/theoretical with no actionable intervention proposed
papers.summary: This longitudinal study tests whether psychopathic traits precede gang membership (selection), follow it (facilitation), or both (enhancement). Using Pathways to Desistance data, the authors found evidence for the enhancement model, suggesting individual propensities and gang social influences interact bidirectionally to shape psychopathic traits.
papers.method: Longitudinal observational analysis using the Pathways to Desistance dataset; sample size not stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Study is theory-building only; identifies no specific preventive program, policy reform, or intervention mechanism for cities to adopt.
papers.cost_note: No intervention costed; research study only.
papers.nyc_note: Findings could inform gang intervention strategy design, but the abstract proposes no concrete program NYC could implement; 'best practices' mentioned but not specified.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive/theoretical study with no intervention tested; establishes correlational mechanism only.
papers.underused_note: No actionable intervention proposed; findings are academic contribution to theory, not a deployable practice.
papers.novelty_note: Bidirectional selection-facilitation framing is somewhat novel, but gang-psychopathy debate is well-established; limited actionable reframing.

papers.title: Consent to sharing shopping data for health research: a mixed-methods study
papers.abstract: Introduction: Shopping data offer detailed, objective records of diet and lifestyle habits, providing a valuable but under-used resource for health research. While willingness to share personal data has been widely studied, no previous research has directly compared stated willingness with subsequent actual data sharing within the same cohort. Understanding whether willingness predicts actual consent, and how this varies by sociodemographic characteristics, motivations, and barriers, is essential to improve recruitment strategies and the representativeness of collected data. Methods: We employed a mixed-methods approach using the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). Quantitative analyses combined sociodemographic variables with responses from a 2018 survey assessing willingness to consent to shopping data linkage and outcomes from a 2023 loyalty card consent campaign. These were complemented by semi-structured interviews in 2019 exploring attitudes towards sharing shopping data. Results: In 2018, 60.7% of participants indicated willingness to share shopping data (n = 2142, 9230 invited). In 2023, actual consent—requiring provision of loyalty card details for five UK retailers (Sainsbury’s, Tesco, Boots, Morrisons, and Coop)—was obtained from 511 of 6170 invited participants. Demographic characteristics were broadly comparable across samples. Stated willingness was associated with higher odds of actual consent (adjusted OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.16–1.90), indicating that willingness is a meaningful predictor of subsequent data sharing. Sociodemographic characteristics and motivations (e.g., societal benefit, scientific interest) showed modest associations with both willingness and consent and moderated the willingness–consent relationship. Interviews reinforced the importance of trust in ALSPAC and clear communication about data use, while highlighting privacy concerns as minor barriers. Conclusion: This study provides the first within-cohort evidence that willingness to share shopping data predicts subsequent data sharing, albeit imperfectly. Willingness surveys can therefore inform recruitment planning, but additional strategies are required to improve realised consent rates. Addressing practical barriers, clarifying data use, and highlighting societal and scientific benefits may help narrow this gap; Targeted engagement, including through informative materials and outreach events, may help to reach groups less likely to consent. These insights can inform data collection in longitudinal cohorts and wider population samples.
papers.idea: Use loyalty card data linkage and targeted engagement strategies to improve health research recruitment and representativeness in longitudinal studies.
papers.summary: This mixed-methods study compared stated willingness to share shopping data (60.7% in 2018) with actual consent obtained in a 2023 loyalty card campaign (8.3% of invitees). Stated willingness predicted actual consent (OR=1.48), and interviews identified trust, clear communication, and privacy concerns as key factors. The authors recommend targeted engagement, informative materials, and outreach to improve consent rates, particularly among underrepresented groups.
papers.effect_size: Stated willingness associated with 48% higher odds of actual consent (OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.16–1.90)
papers.reach: 9,230 invited in 2018 survey; 6,170 invited in 2023 campaign; 511 consented
papers.method: Mixed-methods cohort study using ALSPAC (Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children): 2018 survey on willingness, 2023 loyalty card consent campaign, and 2019 semi-structured interviews; quantitative analysis of sociodemographics, motivations, and consent outcomes.
papers.scalability_note: Requires partnerships with specific retail loyalty programs (UK retailers named); transferability to other jurisdictions/retailers uncertain.
papers.cost_note: Not quantified; appears moderate (survey, interviews, data linkage coordination).
papers.nyc_note: Study uses UK retailers and ALSPAC cohort; NYC would need separate partnerships with local retailers and existing longitudinal cohorts; no direct city policy lever identified.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Willingness predicts consent (OR=1.48) but actual consent rate very low (8.3%); modest associations with demographics/motivations.
papers.underused_note: Data linkage for research is already mainstream in UK/EU; loyalty card recruitment is not a public-safety or NYC civic priority.
papers.novelty_note: Standard methodological question about survey-behavior alignment; applies general consent-research principles to shopping data.

papers.title: Benchmarking the Personalization Capabilities of Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Personalization, the act of varying a message to induce action from a specific receiver while keeping sender, channel, and time fixed, has a long tradition in psychology and marketing as a two-party problem in which sender and receiver have independent objectives. Large language models remove the bounded-inventory constraint of classical retrieval-and-ranking approaches by generating a continuum of message variants conditioned on inferred receiver state, raising the question of how well current models perform personalization in the classical sense. Existing LLM personalization benchmarks measure sender-side adaptation, in which the receiver is the same user the model is serving. The two-party question, whether a generated message induces its intended action in a third party, has been investigated only through A/B tests and small-scale human studies that cannot be re-run against a new model on demand. We adapt the Bayesian Persuasion framework of Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011) to generative agents and instantiate the formulation in sales, where receiver actions are routinely logged against the outreach that induced them. We release SDR-Bench, a public corpus of 6,279 customer success stories spanning 22 industries and approximately 200 enterprises, served through a temporally constrained simulation that prevents future-data leakage. Across frontier LLMs and deep-research agents, we observe a consistent personalization plateau and on a Fortune 100 tech cohort no model statistically separates successful from unsuccessful outreach. A field deployment with 12 professional sales representatives validates the framework, with 48 percent of model-generated content rated immediately useful and senior-expert agreement at Pearson 0.82. We release SDR-Arena and SDR-Bench publicly to support reproducible study of generative personalization at scale.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methods/benchmarking paper with no city-policy intervention lever.
papers.summary: The paper develops SDR-Bench, a benchmark dataset and framework for evaluating how well large language models can personalize sales outreach messages to persuade customers. Across multiple frontier LLMs, the authors find a consistent personalization plateau, with no model statistically distinguishing successful from unsuccessful outreach on Fortune 100 tech companies, though 48% of model-generated content was rated immediately useful in a small field deployment with 12 sales reps.
papers.effect_size: No statistically significant effect on Fortune 100 tech cohort; 48% of generated content rated immediately useful in field study
papers.reach: 6,279 customer success stories from ~200 enterprises; field deployment with 12 sales representatives
papers.method: Created public benchmark corpus of sales outreach data from 22 industries, tested frontier LLMs and research agents against it using a temporally constrained simulation, validated with a field deployment of professional sales reps rating model-generated content.
papers.scalability_note: Designed for corporate B2B sales measurement, not public services or city interventions; unclear applicability to municipal contexts.
papers.cost_note: Benchmark release is free; implementation cost depends on adopting LLM infrastructure, not quantified.
papers.nyc_note: No connection to public safety, housing, transit, health, education, economic mobility, city services, or environment. Focused on private corporate sales personalization.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methods paper with no public-safety intervention; LLM benchmarking only.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—no city-policy lever or civic intervention demonstrated.
papers.novelty_note: LLM benchmarking framework is technically novel but orthogonal to public safety or civic problem-solving.

papers.title: Skill vs Education Types of Labour Mismatch and Their Association with Earnings
papers.authors: Vsevolod Iakovlev
papers.abstract: This paper analyses the distinction between educational and skill types of labour mismatch and their association with earnings. Drawing on cross-sectional data for 26 countries from the 1st Cycle of the OECD (2012) Survey of Adult Skills (PIAAC), I examine educational and skill mismatch using a comprehensive set of education- and skill-based indicators, explore heterogeneity across worker characteristics, and investigate the sources of conflicting country-level correlations with earnings through an error components model. The results show that country-level unobserved heterogeneity induces endogeneity bias, with both its direction and magnitude varying across mismatch measures. Once unobserved heterogeneity is controlled for, over-education and over-skilling are associated with wage penalties, whereas under-education and under-skilling are linked to wage premiums. These findings highlight both conceptual and empirical distinctions between educational and skill mismatch and demonstrate the importance of indicator choice in the analysis.
papers.summary: The study distinguishes between educational mismatch (credential level vs. job requirement) and skill mismatch (actual ability vs. job need) using OECD data from 26 countries, finding that over-education and over-skilling correlate with wage penalties once country-level unobserved factors are controlled for, while under-education and under-skilling show wage premiums.
papers.method: Cross-sectional analysis of OECD PIAAC Survey of Adult Skills (2012) across 26 countries using error components modeling to address endogeneity bias from unobserved heterogeneity.
papers.scalability_note: International comparative study; findings are descriptive correlations without identified policy levers.
papers.cost_note: Minimal direct cost to implement findings, but study offers no concrete intervention to cost.
papers.nyc_note: Study is retrospective measurement of wage correlations with no program design, implementation pathway, or NYC-specific context.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Observational cross-sectional study of wage associations; no causal intervention tested; modest policy relevance for NYC public safety.
papers.underused_note: Labor economics and skills mismatch are well-studied; findings already inform workforce development policy broadly.
papers.novelty_note: Distinguishing skill vs. education mismatch is incremental; standard econometric approach to a known problem.

papers.title: Who Gains from Cybersecurity Preparedness ? Evidence from Sectoral Growth
papers.abstract: Does cybersecurity preparedness matter for growth, and if so, where? This paper provides evidence that cybersecurity preparedness amplifies growth in digitally exposed sectors, highlighting its role as a key institutional complement to digitalization. The analysis uses an interaction design across countries and sectors on a panel of 178 countries, five sectors, and two growth intervals, exploiting differences across sectors within the same country period and over time. The estimates imply that a one standard deviation increase in cybersecurity preparedness is associated with about 1 to 2 percentage points higher annualized value added growth in sectors that are more vulnerable to cyber risk relative to less exposed sectors within the same country and period. The result is robust across alternative measures of sectoral vulnerability and a range of additional tests. Effects are slightly larger in high income countries, indicating potential differences in the effectiveness of cybersecurity systems. Overall, the results suggest that investments in digital infrastructure and digital adoption may yield larger and more resilient growth returns when accompanied by effective cybersecurity.
papers.idea: Cities could mandate cybersecurity standards for digitally exposed sectors to accelerate sectoral growth.
papers.summary: The study found that cybersecurity preparedness amplifies value-added growth in digitally exposed sectors across 178 countries. A one standard deviation increase in cybersecurity preparedness was associated with 1–2 percentage points higher annualized growth in vulnerable sectors, with effects slightly larger in high-income countries.
papers.effect_size: 1–2 percentage points higher annualized sectoral value-added growth
papers.method: Panel regression with interaction design exploiting sectoral differences within countries over time across 178 countries.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable at national/regional level; city-level implementation would require coordination with state/federal cybersecurity policy.
papers.cost_note: Cybersecurity infrastructure and standards development is moderately expensive; no per-unit cost provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited independent authority over cybersecurity standards for sectors; this is primarily a state/federal regulatory domain. Local leverage mainly through procurement or city agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Macro-level sectoral growth effects (1-2 percentage points) are modest and lack direct public-safety nexus; study measures economic outcomes, not crime/safety.
papers.underused_note: Cybersecurity standards are actively pursued by NYC, federal government, and major cities; not a neglected policy area.
papers.novelty_note: Framing cybersecurity as growth enabler is standard tech-policy thinking; no fresh angle on safety or social outcomes.

papers.title: Formulation and Evaluation of Two Citywide Gentrification Measures
papers.authors: Alex Mozell
papers.abstract: The following exposition describes two novel empirical methods devised to measure citywide gentrification: the Dynamic Measure of Citywide Gentrification and the Cross-Sectional Measure of Citywide Gentrification. This paper tests for these measures’ viability. These measures were regressed against demographic variables, and many of the coefficients are statistically significant. These measures are associated as expected with each other when graphed and regressed, though the correlation and R 2 coefficients are lower than expected. Prominent cities identified as highly gentrified by each measure conform to expectations, and prominent cities identified as least gentrified by the Dynamic Measure also conform to expectations. For prominent cities, each measure's identification of highly gentrified cities generally conforms to the other measure. Ultimately, this paper provides evidence for the viability of these measures to assess gentrification at the level of the city. Local policymakers should expect gentrification to emerge across downtown districts.
papers.idea: None identifiable—this is a measurement paper with no policy intervention.
papers.summary: The paper develops two statistical methods to measure gentrification at the citywide level and tests their validity against demographic variables and against each other. The measures show expected associations with demographics and identify major gentrified cities as expected, though inter-measure correlation is weaker than anticipated.
papers.method: Descriptive/methodological: two novel gentrification indices (Dynamic and Cross-Sectional) were constructed, regressed against demographic variables, and compared against each other across cities.
papers.scalability_note: Measurement approach is generalizable but requires data infrastructure; actual utility depends on how cities use the metrics.
papers.cost_note: Developing measurement tools is relatively low-cost; implementation costs depend on downstream policy use.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has sophisticated demographic data and gentrification tracking; unclear what new policy action these measures would enable beyond existing monitoring.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Measurement paper only; no intervention tested, no policy outcome demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is methodological work, not a deployable intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Proposes new measurement indices but addresses conventional gentrification tracking; limited conceptual innovation.

papers.title: Property Insurance Affordability
papers.authors: Jun Zhu, Janneke Ratcliffe, John Walsh, Bryson Berry
papers.abstract: Growing property risk is casting a shadow over the American dream of homeownership. Insurance products such as standard homeowner’s insurance, flood insurance, fire insurance, and other specialized lines are a financial first line of defense, protecting homeowners and the financial institutions who lend to them from these mounting risks. But the rising cost of insurance is threatening the ability of would-be buyers to attain homeownership and, increasingly, the ability of existing homeowners to sustain it. And rising costs are a growing concern for entities who hold and manage mortgage credit risk, including lenders and servicers and the federally backed agencies who guarantee mortgage securities. Why This Matters Homeownership has long been the primary pathway for building financial security and opportunity in the United States, yet it is becoming less affordable. Insurance adds another cost pressure. If households cannot afford to adequately protect their most significant asset, the consequences extend beyond
papers.idea: Cities could regulate or subsidize property insurance markets to make homeownership insurance more affordable for low- and moderate-income buyers.
papers.summary: The abstract identifies rising property insurance costs as a barrier to homeownership affordability and mortgage sustainability, affecting households, lenders, and federally backed mortgage agencies. It frames insurance as a 'financial first line of defense' against growing property risks but does not present findings or propose interventions.
papers.method: Unknown from abstract; appears to be a policy brief or problem statement rather than empirical research.
papers.scalability_note: Insurance regulation is primarily state-level; city-level levers are limited (e.g., mitigation incentives, coordination with state regulators).
papers.cost_note: Subsidy or regulatory approaches could be expensive depending on scope; no cost data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited direct authority over insurance pricing/products (NY State Department of Financial Services regulates); could coordinate mitigation/resilience programs but no concrete city intervention is identifiable.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical effect demonstrated; conceptual policy proposal without rigorous evaluation or pilot data.
papers.underused_note: Some state regulation exists (e.g., Florida, California); moderate policy attention but limited scaling; NYC has not pursued aggressive subsidy or rate-regulation models.
papers.novelty_note: Insurance affordability is a recognized housing-finance issue; framing as public-safety or civic-wellbeing lever is modest, not fundamentally new.

papers.title: Vertical Integration and Cream Skimming of Profitable Referrals: The Case of Hospital-Owned Skilled Nursing Facilities
papers.authors: David Cutler, Leemore Dafny, David C. Grabowski, Steven Lee, Christopher Ody
papers.abstract: We examine whether vertical integration of hospitals and skilled nursing facilities (SNFs) could lessen competition by foreclosing rival SNFs’ access to lucrative referrals. We find that it could: Among integrated providers, a 1 percent increase in SNF reimbursement for a given patient discharged from the upstream hospital increases the self-referral rate to the hospital’s downstream SNF(s) by 1.8 percent. We find no evidence of offsetting benefits for patients and payers: These increased self-referrals have an imprecisely estimated zero effect on patient outcomes and Medicare spending. (JEL G34, I11, I13, J14, L14, L22)
papers.idea: Regulate or prohibit vertical integration between hospitals and skilled nursing facilities to prevent self-referral bias and preserve competition.
papers.summary: This study documents that when hospitals own downstream skilled nursing facilities, they increase self-referrals in response to higher SNF reimbursement rates (a 1% reimbursement increase raises self-referral by 1.8%), suggesting anticompetitive behavior. The authors find no offsetting benefits: increased self-referrals produce zero effect on patient outcomes or Medicare spending.
papers.effect_size: 1.8% increase in self-referral rate per 1% increase in SNF reimbursement; zero effect on patient outcomes and spending
papers.method: Observational study analyzing referral patterns and outcomes data from hospitals with integrated SNF ownership, examining the relationship between reimbursement changes and self-referral rates.
papers.scalability_note: Policy lever exists (antitrust, licensing) but requires federal/state regulation; variable applicability across markets with different ownership structures.
papers.cost_note: Administrative cost of enforcement/regulation is low relative to savings from reduced anticompetitive behavior; no per-unit cost given.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited direct authority over hospital-SNF integration (largely federal/state antitrust and CMS reimbursement matter). Could advocate to state/federal regulators but cannot unilaterally implement.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Healthcare economics study; no public-safety outcome measured; zero effect on patient outcomes and spending.
papers.underused_note: Healthcare regulation and antitrust enforcement are outside civic-safety scope; not a deployment gap but a domain mismatch.
papers.novelty_note: Standard antitrust concern (vertical integration, foreclosure); well-established economic framework applied to SNF market.

papers.title: Attitudes Toward Racial Outgroups During Gentrification
papers.authors: Clayton Becker
papers.abstract: Gentrification is a pressing concern for many lower income neighborhoods around the country. However, despite voluminous evidence of the economic and health impacts of gentrification, we know less about how gentrification impacts attitudes. I investigate whether gentrification of Black neighborhoods is associated with more negative attitudinal evaluations of the racial group of their gentrifiers, and whether these attitudes are the same for gentrifiers of different racial groups. I construct a merged dataset combining individual responses from the Democracy Fund + UCLA Nationscape Survey, ZIP code level contextual data from the American Community Survey, and an original dataset of restaurant prices and reviews. In general, in-migration of non-Black newcomers does not lead to more negative attitudes toward these newcomers’ racial group. White-led gentrification of Black neighborhoods may lead to more negative views toward Whites, but only when accompanied by substantial rent increases. Latino-led and Asian-led gentrification are not associated with similar effects.
papers.summary: The study examines whether gentrification of Black neighborhoods correlates with more negative racial attitudes toward the gentrifiers' group. White-led gentrification accompanied by substantial rent increases was associated with more negative views of Whites, while Latino-led and Asian-led gentrification showed no such effect.
papers.method: Merged observational dataset combining individual survey responses from Democracy Fund + UCLA Nationscape Survey, ZIP code contextual data from American Community Survey, and original restaurant price/review data; correlational analysis by gentrifier race.
papers.scalability_note: Describes attitude patterns only; no intervention proposed for cities to implement.
papers.cost_note: No program or intervention specified; cost not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: Pure descriptive research on attitudes; identifies no actionable policy lever a city could operationalize.
papers.effect_size: White-led gentrification with substantial rent increases associated with more negative views toward Whites; no significant effect for Latino-led or Asian-led gentrification
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Conditional and narrow effect: White-led gentrification + rent increases → modest negative attitude shift; other gentrifier groups show no effect.
papers.underused_note: Attitude research is studied but rarely drives public-safety or neighborhood intervention policy; gentrification itself is high-profile.
papers.novelty_note: Disaggregates gentrification by gentrifier race and links to attitudes; moderately fresh framing but attitudes-as-outcome is less action-oriented than economic/health impacts.

papers.title: Fracking ghost towns: How neighborhood exposure drives population decline
papers.authors: Arman Khachiyan
papers.idea: Monitor and mitigate neighborhood population decline from industrial extraction activities to preserve community stability.
papers.summary: This paper appears to examine how exposure to fracking activity correlates with or drives population decline in affected neighborhoods. The study likely documents demographic changes in communities near natural-gas extraction sites.
papers.method: Unknown without abstract; likely observational analysis of census/demographic data matched against fracking locations and timing.
papers.scalability_note: Fracking is geographically concentrated in specific regions (Texas, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania); limited relevance to most U.S. cities.
papers.cost_note: Mitigation would require regulation or compensation; no per-unit cost stated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no active fracking; paper is of historical/policy interest only, not actionable locally.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Industrial extraction's role in population decline is well-documented sociologically, but causal mechanisms and intervention-specific outcomes are unclear from title/abstract alone.
papers.underused_note: Environmental and industrial-policy monitoring exists; NYC has minimal active extraction, limiting direct applicability to urgent local safety priorities.
papers.novelty_note: Linking extraction to neighborhood stability is established; framing as a public-safety intervention is somewhat fresh but not fundamentally new thinking.

papers.title: The Erosion of Legal Education under Neoliberalism: Rigor, Authority, and Professional Formation in Chile
papers.abstract: This article examines how the neoliberal transformation of higher education in Chile has eroded the conditions necessary for rigorous legal education and, consequently, for the ethical and professional formation of lawyers. Drawing on Marx’s critique of ideology and Nietzsche’s analysis of moral decadence, the article conceptualizes neoliberalism not merely as an economic arrangement but as a moral and cultural order shaping universities, students, and legal educators. Using Chile as a paradigmatic case—given its early and radical adoption of market-based educational reforms—the analysis shows how managerial governance, the client-student model, weakened academic authority, and the commodification of learning have undermined the intellectual discipline central to legal training. The article further argues that the failures of legal education manifest beyond the university, contributing to a broader institutional fragility visible in recent public scandals involving legal professionals. By linking philosophical critique, pedagogical analysis, and institutional outcomes, this study highlights the urgent need to rethink the cultural and ethical foundations of legal education in neoliberal contexts.
papers.idea: Reform law school curricula and governance to prioritize ethical formation and academic rigor over market-driven metrics.
papers.summary: The study argues that Chile's neoliberal higher-education reforms—particularly market-based governance, managerialism, and the commodification of learning—have eroded the conditions for rigorous legal education and ethical professional formation of lawyers. The authors contend this institutional erosion contributes to broader failures visible in public scandals involving legal professionals. The analysis is primarily philosophical and institutional critique rather than empirical measurement of outcomes.
papers.method: Theoretical and institutional analysis drawing on Marx and Nietzsche, using Chile as a case study; no empirical data collection, quantitative analysis, or intervention testing reported.
papers.scalability_note: Educational governance reform is city-actionable but requires sustained institutional change; applicability depends on local law school autonomy.
papers.cost_note: Governance and curricular reform have variable costs; no implementation budget provided.
papers.nyc_note: Study focuses on Chilean system; NYC law schools are largely private/state-regulated; limited direct lever for municipal policy, though DA/judicial training programs might adopt ethical curricula.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical critique with no empirical outcome data; no measurable public-safety or civic effect demonstrated
papers.underused_note: Curriculum reform is actively debated in legal education; not a neglected intervention gap
papers.novelty_note: Critique of neoliberalism in higher ed is well-established; application to law schools is conventional

papers.title: Mainstreaming marginalized neighbourhoods: resident experiences of non-profit housing transformation in Copenhagen, Denmark
papers.authors: Lasse Kjeldsen, Rebecka Söderberg, Matthew Howells, Kristian Olesen
papers.idea: Partner with non-profits to rehabilitate and manage housing in marginalized neighborhoods to improve resident outcomes.
papers.summary: No abstract provided. Title suggests a qualitative or observational study of how non-profit housing transformation affects residents in Copenhagen neighborhoods. Study appears to focus on resident experience and neighborhood integration rather than quantified outcomes.
papers.method: Not determinable; no abstract provided.
papers.scalability_note: Housing transformation programs are geographically and institutionally specific; transferability depends on local non-profit capacity and regulatory environment.
papers.cost_note: Non-profit housing rehabilitation typically requires significant upfront capital; long-term operational costs unknown without abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has active non-profit housing sector (NYCHA partners, settlement houses), but Copenhagen's regulatory and financial context may differ materially.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Qualitative study of resident experiences; no quantified outcome data on safety, crime, employment, or health provided.
papers.underused_note: Non-profit housing partnerships are moderately deployed in NYC (via organizations like Housing Preservation Development, NYCHA partnerships); not a major gap.
papers.novelty_note: Community land trusts and non-profit housing are established models; framing as 'mainstreaming marginalized neighborhoods' is conventional affordable-housing language.

papers.title: Who Drifted: the System or the Judge? Anytime-Valid Attribution in LLM Evaluation Pipelines
papers.authors: Yitao Li
papers.abstract: Continuous evaluation of LLM products relies on a strong LLM judge treated as ground truth: a cheap monitor scores every interaction and a team is paged when the score drifts down. But the judge is itself a model behind an API, and a silent version bump or scoring-prompt update changes how it scores -- so every drift alarm is ambiguous between a worse product and a changed judge. We resolve the ambiguity with a fixed, human-labeled anchor set that the current judge re-scores at a steady interleave, a second betting e-process on the judge-versus-human gap, and a guard-window rule returning a verdict in {none, system, judge}. We prove anytime-validity, one-way identification (only the judge can move the anchors), an attribution race whose design law is that the anchors must out-run the main process they guard, and process orthogonality. On two real judge changes, a silent version bump is detected as judge drift in 60/60 runs with zero judge-to-system misattribution, and a contaminating strict-prompt change is correctly attributed on 110 of 120 runs at guard width 300 -- while the industry-default rolling z-test false-alarms on 75% of drift-free streams. Every experiment replicates on a second domain (TL;DR summarization) with nothing re-tuned, and where the domains differ the differences are the ones the race predicts: the strict-prompt change shifts scores harder there, so the anchors fire faster and attribution becomes perfect (240/240). The monitor runs at approximately 0.64 of the cost of strong-judging every item, or 0.21 in a cheaper-but-deafer regime.
papers.idea: N/A — This is a methods paper on detecting drift in LLM evaluation systems, not a civic intervention.
papers.summary: The paper presents a statistical technique to distinguish whether performance degradation in an LLM evaluation system is due to the product declining or the judge model changing. It uses human-labeled anchor sets and betting e-processes to attribute drift to either the system or the judge with high accuracy.
papers.method: Algorithmic development with validation on two domains (LLM product evaluation and TL;DR summarization) using real judge version changes; no human subjects or city-level implementation.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable to any LLM-based monitoring pipeline but requires custom implementation per domain.
papers.cost_note: Runs at ~64% of full judging cost; primarily a software optimization with minimal marginal expense.
papers.nyc_note: Technical infrastructure improvement for companies using LLM evaluation; no direct relevance to NYC city services, safety, or resident welfare.
papers.effect_size: 60/60 runs with zero judge-to-system misattribution for silent version bump detection; 110/120 correct attributions for prompt change at guard width 300; rolling z-test false-alarms on 75% of drift-free streams
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methods paper on LLM evaluation systems; no public-safety or civic intervention demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is a technical ML ops paper, not a civic policy or intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Novel statistical method (anytime-valid testing) but applied to a narrow, domain-specific technical problem with no civic relevance.

papers.title: A Koopman-PINN Framework for Epidemic Models: Parameter Inference and Forecasting
papers.authors: Achraf Zinihi, Matthias Ehrhardt, Moulay Rchid Sidi Ammi
papers.abstract: We propose a Koopman-enhanced physics-informed neural network (K--PINN) framework for parameter inference and forecasting in nonlinear epidemic models. This method combines Koopman operator theory and physics-informed learning. It maps epidemic states into a latent observable space where the dynamics evolve approximately linearly while satisfying the governing epidemic equations through automatic differentiation. This integration improves interpretability, parameter identifiability, and long-term predictive stability. We apply the proposed framework to a normalized SEIRSD epidemic model and evaluate it using synthetic monkeypox (Mpox) data and real-world datasets from Germany, Morocco, and Sweden for the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Synthetic trajectories are generated using a structure-preserving, nonstandard finite difference scheme to ensure reliable training data. Numerical results demonstrate that K--PINN achieves more accurate parameter estimation, trajectory reconstruction, and long-term forecasting than classical PINNs and Koopman-EDMD approaches. These results suggest that K--PINN is an effective machine learning framework for epidemic modeling that can be extended to more complex systems.
papers.idea: None identifiable—this is a methodological paper on epidemic forecasting with no policy or program lever.
papers.summary: The authors propose K-PINN, a machine-learning framework combining Koopman operator theory with physics-informed neural networks to improve parameter estimation and forecasting in epidemic models. They demonstrate that it outperforms classical PINNs and EDMD on synthetic monkeypox and real SARS-CoV-2 data from Germany, Morocco, and Sweden.
papers.method: Comparative evaluation of a novel neural-network architecture on synthetic and real epidemic datasets using a SEIRSD model; no randomization, no intervention, no human subjects.
papers.scalability_note: Forecasting tool deployable across jurisdictions if operationalized; depends on uptake by epidemiologists and public-health agencies.
papers.cost_note: Computational overhead modest once trained; deployment cost low relative to traditional epidemiological modeling.
papers.nyc_note: DOHMH could in principle adopt better forecasting; but paper offers no guidance on when/how to act on forecasts, integration with response systems, or local validation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological paper with no demonstrated public-health or safety outcome; forecasting accuracy improvements are technical, not tied to intervention or policy impact.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is pure methodology with no identified policy lever or program for deployment.
papers.novelty_note: Combines existing techniques (Koopman operators + PINNs) in a technically competent way, but framing is standard epidemiological modeling, not a new conceptual approach to public health.

papers.title: Testing Preferential Sampling
papers.authors: Isabel Natario, Andreia Monteiro
papers.abstract: Geostatistics aims to infer a spatially continuous phenomenon from observations collected at a finite number of locations, frequently measured with error. Whenever there is stochastic dependence between the spatial and sampling processes, preferential sampling occurs. Ignoring this problem drives to incorrect and biased estimates and, therefore, recognizing it is quite important, but not always simple to execute and understand. In this work, a test for assessing preferential sampling, simple and easy to implement, is presented, overcoming the previous concerns. It is based on the dependence between the number of sampled points and the values of the corresponding measures. The performance of the proposed test id assessed through a large simulation study, which consideres different levels of preferentiability, relation with a covariate, different sample sizes and different test procedure conditions. The results are quite encouraging, with high levels of correct preferential sampling detections, further confirmed by the test application to already known real data sets of lead concentrations in moss samples and red and blue shrimp capture data.
papers.idea: Develop statistical tests to detect bias in environmental sampling to improve accuracy of pollution monitoring.
papers.summary: The paper presents a statistical test for identifying preferential sampling bias—when the location and density of measurement points correlate with the underlying phenomenon being measured. The test was validated through simulations and applied to real datasets on lead in moss and shrimp capture data.
papers.method: Simulation study across varying levels of preferential sampling bias, sample sizes, and covariate relationships; applied post-hoc to two existing environmental datasets.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only to environmental monitoring programs; requires technical statistical expertise to deploy.
papers.cost_note: Minimal implementation cost; primarily software/analyst time to apply existing statistical test.
papers.nyc_note: Could support NYC's environmental monitoring (lead, water quality, air), but the paper does not propose a concrete city intervention or program change.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Technical methodological contribution with no direct demonstrated public-health or safety outcome; improves statistical accuracy but effect on real-world decisions unclear.
papers.underused_note: Statistical methods for bias detection are standard in environmental science; adoption limited by technical expertise barriers rather than awareness or evidence gaps.
papers.novelty_note: Incremental refinement of geostatistical methods; addresses known problem (preferential sampling bias) with conventional statistical testing approach.

papers.title: Homeownership Among Young Adults Has Fallen, and It’s Worse Than You Think
papers.authors: Jung Hyun Choi, Laurie Goodman, Jun Zhu
papers.abstract: As housing has become less affordable, young adults are feeling the pinch the most. The homeownership rate among adults ages 25 to 34 is lower than for previous generations at the same age, and more attention has been directed toward alternate ways young adults might purchase a home. But even this dire situation may be worse than it seems. According to the American Community Survey, the homeownership rate for household heads ages 25 to 34 declined sharply following financial crisis—from 47 percent in 2005 to 37 percent in 2015—before partially recovering to 40 percent by 2024. But this conventional measure overstates the extent of the recovery. The traditional homeownership rate is calculated as the share of households that are owner occupied. This measure excludes young adults who have not formed independent households, such as those living with parents, other relatives, or roommates. Because independent household formation declined substantially between 2005
papers.idea: None identified; this is a measurement/diagnostic paper, not an intervention proposal.
papers.summary: Young adult homeownership (ages 25–34) has declined from 47% in 2005 to 40% in 2024, but conventional measures overstate recovery because they exclude young adults living with family or roommates rather than forming independent households. The study shows that lack of independent household formation masks how severe the crisis actually is.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis using American Community Survey data comparing homeownership rates and household formation across cohorts and time periods (2005–2024).
papers.scalability_note: Measurement approach is universally applicable; no policy lever specified.
papers.cost_note: Research-only; no intervention cost data provided.
papers.nyc_note: Identifies a problem but offers no actionable NYC policy or program; housing affordability solutions require separate evidence.
papers.effect_size: 10 percentage point decline in homeownership rate from 47% (2005) to 37% (2015)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No intervention tested; diagnostic/measurement paper only
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention—cannot be 'underused' in deployment sense
papers.novelty_note: Refinement of measurement methodology (inclusion of co-resident young adults) adds useful nuance to standard homeownership statistics, but conceptually incremental

papers.title: Does Social Assistance Reach the Most Vulnerable in Settings with Forced Displacement ? Evidence from the Central African Republic
papers.abstract: Poverty reduction rarely progresses in countries affected by conflict and forced displacement. Mechanisms to target social assistance to reach the poorest households in such settings are therefore badly needed. This paper explores patterns of social assistance targeting using unique household survey data covering people inside and outside displaced person camps in the Central African Republic. The findings show that assistance is categorically targeted and not directed to those most in need. Internally displaced persons in camps are significantly more likely to receive social assistance than people living outside camps—including out-of-camp internally displaced persons—regardless of their level of monetary consumption. Meanwhile, people with lower monetary consumption are no likelier to receive social assistance than those with higher consumption levels. As a result, internally displaced persons in camps who are in the top consumption quintile are more than twice as likely to receive social assistance as those outside camps in the bottom consumption quintile. This means that some extremely vulnerable households are excluded from assistance. Moreover, these patterns could create perverse incentives to enter and remain in camps, especially as it emerges that those who have been in camps longer are more likely to receive social assistance. Simulations reveal that targeting social assistance based on monetary welfare could protect the most vulnerable better by reducing the depth and severity of poverty. The results underline the need to find practicable mechanisms to reach the poorest people in conflict-affected settings, both inside and outside camps.
papers.idea: Redesign social assistance targeting in conflict-affected settings to prioritize consumption-based need assessment rather than categorical residence (camp vs. non-camp).
papers.summary: The study examined social assistance allocation in the Central African Republic and found that aid was categorically targeted to internally displaced persons in camps regardless of actual need, while excluding vulnerable non-camp residents. Internally displaced persons in camps in the top consumption quintile were more than twice as likely to receive assistance as those outside camps in the bottom quintile, suggesting perverse incentives to enter camps.
