papers.title: In Tablets We Trust ? Validation of the Use of Tablet-Based Tools for Land Area Measurement in Household Surveys
papers.abstract: Accurate and scalable methods for land area measurement are essential for estimating key agricultural indicators, such as crop yields, as well as understanding agricultural relationships. A significant body of literature has demonstrated systematic bias in farmer estimates of land area, the most inexpensive option, and at the same time provided evidence supporting the use of handheld GPS units for objective measurement. Despite the successful adoption of handheld GPS measurement in many survey operations, new tablet-based tools offer potential for smoother, more cost-effective measurement of land area. Using data collected in a methodological experiment in Uganda, this study evaluates the accuracy and operational trade-offs of tablet-based area measurement tools, both with and without the support of GPS-boosting devices, relative to handheld Garmin GPS units. The findings demonstrate that both tablet-based approaches are highly correlated with handheld GPS measurements, with relatively small but statistically significant differences in means. On average, the boosted and unboosted tablet measures exhibit absolute percentage errors of approximately 11 and 12 percent, respectively, with underestimation more pervasive than overestimation. Importantly, the findings highlight that even small differences in land area measurement can lead to substantial variations in yield and production estimates, underscoring the critical role of the measurement method for agricultural productivity analysis and policy. From an implementation perspective, the tablet-based approach offers clear advantages, with a significantly reduced burden of data cleaning and processing compared to handheld GPS data and enumerators facing few technical challenges.
papers.idea: Adopt tablet-based land measurement tools in agricultural surveys to reduce measurement error and operational cost.
papers.summary: This study tested tablet-based tools (with and without GPS boosting) against handheld GPS units for measuring land area in Uganda. Both tablet approaches showed high correlation with GPS units, with absolute errors around 11–12%, though small measurement differences can meaningfully affect yield estimates.
papers.effect_size: 11–12% absolute error on tablet-based measurement vs. handheld GPS; statistically significant but small differences in means
papers.method: Methodological experiment in Uganda comparing tablet-based area measurement tools (boosted and unboosted) to handheld Garmin GPS units on accuracy and operational efficiency.
papers.scalability_note: Study was in Uganda's agricultural context; limited applicability to urban U.S. cities with minimal agricultural surveys.
papers.cost_note: Tablets reduce data cleaning burden and enumerator technical challenges vs. GPS, suggesting cost savings, but no per-unit cost stated.

papers.underused_note: This is a technical agricultural surveying method with no direct public-safety application; adoption depends on development agencies and agricultural ministries, not civic safety.
papers.novelty_note: Straightforward technology substitution (tablet vs. handheld device); no new conceptual framing of a problem.

papers.title: Device Effects and Response Quality in Online Surveys: Evidence from Czech GGS Paradata
papers.abstract: This study examines device effects on response behaviour and data quality in the Czech Generations and Gender Survey, Wave II, using keystroke-level paradata from a fully mobile-optimised CAWI instrument. We compare respondents completing the survey on PCs/laptops and smartphones across indicators capturing cognitive load, satisficing, and respondent effort, including response latency, completion time, answer changes, page revisits, item nonresponse, midpoint use, “don’t know” responses, and straightlining. Descriptive analyses and multivariate models controlling for age, gender, and education show that most device differences are small or negligible. Smartphone use is not associated with longer completion times or systematically lower engagement once demographic composition is accounted for. The only robust and substantively meaningful device effect is a higher incidence of straightlining among smartphone respondents, alongside a very small increase in item nonresponse. Education consistently attenuates device-related differences. Overall, the findings indicate broad comparability across devices in a mobile-optimised survey, while highlighting specific behavioural domains where smartphones continue to shape response processes.
papers.idea: None—this is a methods/measurement study with no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The study analyzed how respondents' choice of device (PC/laptop vs. smartphone) affects survey response quality in the Czech Generations and Gender Survey. Most device differences were small; smartphones showed slightly higher straightlining and item nonresponse, but no meaningful differences in completion time or engagement once demographics were controlled.
papers.effect_size: Small device effects; main finding: higher straightlining on smartphones (magnitude not quantified); education attenuates differences
papers.reach: Czech Generations and Gender Survey Wave II (sample size not stated in abstract)
papers.method: Descriptive and multivariate analysis of keystroke-level paradata from a mobile-optimized online survey, controlling for age, gender, and education.

papers.cost_note: Study itself is low-cost academic research; findings inform survey design but create no direct service cost.

papers.title: How the Basel Re-Proposal Will Impact the Mortgage Market
papers.authors: Jim Parrott, Laurie Goodman, Mark M. Zandi
papers.abstract: On March 19, 2026, the US banking regulators released a proposal to revise the Basel framework that will govern bank capital requirements. In this paper, the authors discuss how the revised framework would affect the mortgage market. They conclude that it would have a moderate impact as drafted, primarily by incentivizing banks to originate and hold more lower-risk mortgages and encouraging nonbanks in the mortgage market to pursue bank charters or partnerships with banks to take advantage of the changes and remain competitive. The revised framework’s impact would be more substantial if policymakers were to clarify the definition of “regulatory residential real estate exposure,” expand the definition of “securitization,” and give credit for the transfer of mortgage credit risk, attracting greater bank participation in the market and providing a larger mortgage rate reduction for most borrowers.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a federal banking regulation analysis with no city-level policy lever.
papers.summary: The paper analyzes how proposed revisions to the Basel banking framework would affect the mortgage market by incentivizing banks to originate lower-risk mortgages and encouraging nonbank lenders to seek bank partnerships. The authors project moderate impacts on mortgage availability and rates, with potential for larger effects if regulators adopt clarifying policy amendments.

papers.title: How Insights from Private-Sector Data Can Strengthen Economic Policymaking
papers.authors: Madeline Brown, Rekha Balu, Annie Heinrichs
papers.abstract: The US economy is changing faster than public data systems can keep up. That’s because these systems were built for a very different economy: one with slower-moving production cycles and clearer categories of economic activity. In contrast, today’s economy is more hyperlocal and more uneven across people and places. At the same time, staffing reductions have led to delays in data releases and low response rates on national surveys like the decennial census. These challenges have made it more difficult for federal statistical infrastructure—typically a backbone for economic data—to provide timely indicators. In particular, it’s a serious challenge for state and local policymakers, who for decades have relied on federal datasets for essential insights into the job market, affordability, and financial security in their communities. Data collected by private companies could help fill this gap. Banks, supermarkets, online job boards, and other companies are already collecting consumer data that can
papers.idea: Cities could incorporate private-sector data (from banks, retailers, job boards) into local economic monitoring systems to supplement slow federal datasets.
papers.summary: The paper argues that federal statistical systems are too slow and outdated to track today's hyperlocal, uneven economy. Private companies collect real-time consumer and labor-market data that could fill gaps in federal datasets for state and local policymakers. The authors propose using private data to improve timeliness of indicators on jobs, affordability, and financial security.
papers.method: Position paper/essay; no empirical study conducted. The abstract describes the problem and proposes private-data as a solution but does not present research findings or test the proposal.

papers.cost_note: Depends heavily on private-sector pricing and data-access negotiation; unclear from abstract.

papers.title: A Qualitative Exploration of Black and White Women’s Perceptions of Procedural Justice
papers.authors: Kaelyn Sanders
papers.abstract: Procedural justice theory has a lengthy history of application in criminological research. Over time, the theory has been applied to police stops, arrests, court procedures, and other aspects of the criminal legal system. However, much of the extant research examines procedural justice generally among minoritized groups and fails to utilize intersectional approaches focusing on race and gender. The current study addresses this limitation by using qualitative data from 336 system-involved women in Michigan to explore White and Black women’s perceptions of procedural justice. The findings revealed that Black women have somewhat more nuanced and unsatisfactory perceptions of procedural justice, but White women’s perceptions were not overwhelmingly positive either. In addition, women in both groups felt that judges were more procedurally just than the police. Both theoretical and practical implications are presented.
papers.idea: Not applicable—no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This qualitative study explores how Black and White women system-involved in Michigan perceive procedural justice in policing and courts. Black women reported more negative and nuanced perceptions than White women, and both groups perceived judges as more procedurally just than police, but no specific policy or program lever is proposed or tested.
papers.method: Qualitative study using interview or focus-group data from 336 system-involved women in Michigan comparing perceptions of procedural justice by race and gender.

papers.title: Reforming Payment for Clinician-Administered Drugs — Most-Favored-Nation Pricing in Medicare Part B
papers.authors: Thomas Hwang, Lisa Clemans-Cope, Aaron Kesselheim
papers.abstract: The federal government is proposing a new way to lower the amount that Medicare pays for drugs administered in doctors’ offices and clinics. The Global Benchmark for Efficient Drug Pricing (GLOBE) model would tie Medicare payments for certain high-cost drugs (single-source drugs and sole-source biological products in select therapeutic classes) to prices charged in other wealthy countries. This paper examines how the proposal works, what it could accomplish, and where it falls short. Why This Matters Americans pay far more for prescription drugs than patients in other countries. Medicare Part B, which reimburses providers for drugs administered by clinicians, including cancer therapies, spent $46 billion on drugs in 2024, up from $22 billion a decade earlier. Patients bear a share of those costs directly. Reforms that bring prices closer to international levels could save money for Medicare, taxpayers, and patients alike. Policymakers, patient advocates, and health care providers all have
papers.idea: Cities could advocate for or pilot most-favored-nation drug pricing models for municipal employee health plans and safety-net hospitals.

It analyzes how the proposal works, its potential savings for Medicare and patients, and its limitations. The study is a policy analysis of a federal Medicare reform, not a city-level intervention.

papers.title: The good, the bad, the ugly: A qualitative exploration of life‐course posttraumatic growth for individuals formerly sentenced to juvenile life without parole
papers.authors: Abigail R. Henson, J. Z. Bennett, Megan Forney, Grace Martinez
papers.abstract: Abstract This study explores the profound and often overlooked developmental journeys of individuals sentenced to life without parole as minors. Through in‐depth life‐history interviews with 30 formerly incarcerated people, we examine how they forged personal growth amid the cumulative trauma of long‐term confinement. Our analysis reveals developmental paths aligned with the posttraumatic growth framework, which recognizes how strength and transformation can emerge from profound adversity. Participants shared accounts of how incarceration shaped their identities, values, and sense of purpose, even in the absence of institutional support for meaningful change. Findings challenge readers to reflect on the paradox between the human capacity for change over time and long‐term incarceration, particularly as it relates to individuals who entered confinement during a critical period of ongoing psychosocial development.
papers.idea: N/A — study documents lived experience but does not propose a specific intervention, program, or policy lever.
papers.summary: Through interviews with 30 formerly incarcerated individuals sentenced to juvenile life without parole, the study documents how some experienced personal growth and transformation despite long-term confinement, even absent institutional support. Findings highlight a paradox between human capacity for change and the practice of indefinite juvenile incarceration.
papers.method: Qualitative life-history interviews with 30 formerly incarcerated individuals; posttraumatic growth framework applied to analysis.
papers.scalability_note: Qualitative exploration of individual narratives; no program or intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; research-only.

papers.underused_note: Study itself is descriptive research, not a deployable intervention; findings could inform policy but no gap between evidence and implementation of a specific practice.

papers.title: Ethnic Diversity, Historical Economic Exchange, and Development: Evidence from Andean Peru
papers.authors: Miriam Artiles
papers.abstract: Is ethnic diversity good or bad for economic development? Most studies find corrosive effects. This paper shows that historical exposure to economic exchange can mitigate these effects in the long run. I collect data from a natural experiment of Peru's colonial history: the forced resettlement of native populations in the sixteenth century. Where the resettlement concentrated ethnically diverse populations with a history of internal crop exchange, contemporary populations perform better systematically. Additional evidence suggests that prior experience with mutually beneficial crop exchange shaped more open attitudes toward out-group members. Economic complementarities helped sustain long-run, market-oriented cooperation and local trade. (JEL F54, J15, N16, N36, N56, O15, Q12)
papers.idea: N/A — study is historical/theoretical with no actionable city policy lever.
papers.summary: This paper uses a natural experiment from 16th-century Peru (forced resettlement) to show that ethnic diversity's negative effects on development can be mitigated by prior experience with mutually beneficial economic exchange. The mechanism appears to be that history of trade built more cooperative, open attitudes toward out-group members, sustaining longer-term local commerce.
papers.method: Natural experiment exploiting colonial-era resettlement patterns in Peru, comparing contemporary outcomes in regions with vs. without prior crop-exchange history; relies on historical administrative data and contemporary census/economic measures.

papers.title: Following Socio-Environmental Conflict Narratives About Energy Transition in Chile: A Spatio-Temporal Analysis Using Dynamic Topic Modeling
papers.abstract: Understanding the construction of socio-environmental narratives at a national scale is a complex challenge, particularly when research remains fragmented across disconnected case studies. In Chile, the energy transition has generated territorial disputes as extractive industries and renewable energy projects expand, yet large-scale systematic analyses of how these conflicts are represented in public discourse remain scarce. This paper addresses this gap by applying a spatio-temporal topic modelling framework to a corpus of 1,996 validated news articles covering conflicts related to the energy transition in Chile from 2011 to 2025. Using RollingLDA, a dynamic adaptation of latent Dirichlet allocation that prevents information leakage from future documents, we identify twelve topics that provide insights into the public narratives surrounding socio-environmental conflicts. Our analysis reveals how specific conflicts, such as the HidroAysén dam project, the Dominga mining controversy, and pollution in sacrifice zones such as Quintero-Puchuncaví, have evolved over time, with some narratives declining while others, including green hydrogen development and lithium extraction, have emerged as central concerns. We complement this temporal analysis with a spatial dimension by mapping the prevalence of topics across Chilean regions through an interactive dashboard. By combining established methods, our work offers a reproducible framework that can be adapted to topic modelling results incorporating spatial and temporal dimensions, enabling the tracking of how socio-environmental narratives emerge, evolve, and fade over time. Please also refer to the GitHub repository at https://github.com/JonasRieger/t2s2026.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methods paper with no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: This paper develops a spatio-temporal topic modeling framework (RollingLDA) to analyze 1,996 Chilean news articles about energy-transition conflicts from 2011–2025. It identifies 12 topics tracking how narratives around dam projects, mining, lithium extraction, and pollution have emerged, evolved, and faded over time, with results visualized in an interactive dashboard.
papers.method: Spatio-temporal topic modeling (RollingLDA, a dynamic adaptation of LDA) applied to a corpus of validated news articles; results mapped across regions via dashboard.
papers.scalability_note: The framework is methodological and transferable, but requires substantial technical expertise and corpus building; no evidence it improves actual outcomes.
papers.cost_note: Computational costs are modest once corpus is built; primary cost is researcher time.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has little energy-transition conflict at the scale Chile does (no large hydroelectric or lithium disputes). The method could track NYC environmental narratives, but offers no policy lever or actionable guidance for city agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methods paper with no intervention or outcome evaluation; demonstrates analytical technique only.
papers.underused_note: This is not a deployable public-safety intervention—it is a methodological framework for narrative analysis.
papers.novelty_note: Spatio-temporal topic modeling is established; application to energy conflicts in Chile is contextually specific but not conceptually novel.

papers.title: Household coping mechanisms under grid failure: Evidence from a high electrification context in Lebanon
papers.authors: Majd Olleik, Haytham M. Dbouk, Anne Neumann, Elsa Bou Gebrael, Sebastian Zwickl-Bernhard
papers.abstract: Despite near-universal electrification in many countries, electricity supply shortages continue to shape household energy use. This paper examines how households adapt to chronic grid failure in high-electrification, high-dependence contexts, using Lebanon as a case study. Drawing on original survey data from 1,000 households, we analyze both supply-side coping mechanisms such as diesel generators and solar photovoltaic (PV)-battery systems, and demand-side adaptations, including load shifting and demand suppression. The results reveal a landscape of household responses, where socioeconomic status plays a central role in determining access to backup solutions and the extent of met demand. While diesel generators remain widespread, a transition toward PV-battery systems is observed, especially among financially capable households. However, decentralized self-generation is associated with inefficiencies, including substantial levels of curtailed solar generation. On the demand side, households exhibit reductions in electricity use, leading to distinct consumption profiles depending on the type of backup system employed. These findings highlight the importance of distinguishing between met and unmet demand when assessing energy needs under unreliable supply. The paper contributes to the literature by providing a quantitative characterization of the interaction between self-generation and demand adaptation in a supply-constrained high-electrification context. It also offers empirical demand profiles that incorporate suppressed consumption, addressing a key gap in electricity system planning. From a policy perspective, the results underscore the need to account for unmet demand, address inequities in access to coping technologies, and reduce inefficiencies in decentralized systems.
papers.idea: Cities could mandate or incentivize distributed solar-battery systems over diesel generators to reduce air pollution and improve grid resilience during outages.
papers.summary: The study surveyed 1,000 Lebanese households to understand how they cope with chronic electricity shortages. It found that wealthier households shift toward solar-PV-battery systems while poorer households rely on diesel generators; both strategies reduce electricity demand but create inefficiencies and equity gaps.
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey of 1,000 households in Lebanon analyzing self-reported coping mechanisms and energy consumption patterns.
papers.scalability_note: Policy lessons are generalizable to supply-constrained contexts, but requires chronic grid failure scenario—not present in NYC.
papers.cost_note: Incentivizing solar-battery systems is capital-intensive; diesel generators are cheaper upfront but externalize health/environmental costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has reliable grid supply and does not face chronic electricity shortages; findings address grid-failure coping, not standard urban challenges.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Observational study of household choices under crisis; no causal intervention tested or evaluated for public-safety impact.
papers.underused_note: Solar-battery incentives are already mainstream policy in NYC and nationally; not a novel deployment gap.
papers.novelty_note: Framing grid resilience through distributed solar is conventional; the Lebanese context is geographically distant from NYC policy relevance.

papers.title: Distributional Forecasting of EU Asylum Applications with Dynamic Multivariate Count Models
papers.authors: Gregor Zens, Jakub Bijak
papers.abstract: We propose a Bayesian framework for joint distributional forecasting of monthly asylum applications across the EU-27. The model decomposes latent application intensities into country-specific random walks and common factors, with idiosyncratic and shared shocks allowed to exhibit heavy tails or stochastic volatility. Using Eurostat data from 2008 to 2026, we evaluate predictive distributions in a rolling out-of-sample exercise, scoring overall distributional accuracy and upper-tail risk. Three findings emerge. First, the preferred specification varies across countries, scoring rules, and horizons, underscoring the need to align models with policy-specific loss functions. Second, joint EU-27 models improve on country-by-country benchmarks, with the largest gains in the upper tail, where preparedness costs are most relevant. Third, random-walk log-intensities provide a useful short-run description of national asylum-application dynamics, especially when combined with flexible innovation dynamics. We conclude by discussing implications for national and EU-level agencies involved in asylum forecasting and preparedness planning.
papers.idea: None—this is a forecasting methodology paper with no actionable intervention lever.
papers.summary: The authors develop a Bayesian statistical model to forecast EU-27 asylum applications month-by-month, decomposing application intensities into country-specific trends and shared factors. The model improves on single-country benchmarks, especially for upper-tail risk forecasting (sudden spikes), and shows that random-walk specifications work well for short-term prediction.
papers.method: Bayesian multivariate count model estimated on Eurostat monthly data (2008–2026), evaluated via rolling out-of-sample distributional forecasts and scoring rules.
papers.scalability_note: A forecasting tool could be deployed across agencies, but implementation depends on uptake by national/EU policy bodies.
papers.cost_note: Statistical modeling is cheap relative to operations; cost is in adoption and integration into planning workflows, not material per-unit cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no direct asylum-application forecasting role; EU asylum policy is national/supranational. Tangential relevance only to immigrant services planning if NYC wished to forecast intake.
papers.effect_size: Joint EU-27 models improve on country-by-country benchmarks, with largest gains in upper-tail forecasting
papers.reach: EU-27 countries, monthly data from 2008 to 2026
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pure forecasting methodology with no intervention tested or evaluated; no causal effect on public safety or city outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is academic methodology, not an implementable intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Sophisticated Bayesian forecasting is technically novel but methodologically conventional; does not reframe a policy problem.

papers.title: Who Governs Cyberspace in the age of AI? A Global Stocktake of Cybersecurity Governance
papers.abstract: Cyberspace is not ungoverned—it is systematically misgoverned. International instruments, national strategies, and voluntary frameworks have proliferated while security outcomes deteriorated. This inversion—more governance, less resilience—is the "Proliferation Paradox." The paper introduces "Governance Debt": the institutional deficit that accumulates when AI-driven technological change outpaces governance adaptation. This debt compounds across three dimensions: Capacity Debt (threat velocity exceeds institutional response cycles), Authority Debt (no enforcement jurisdiction over borderless AI infrastructure), and Legitimacy Debt (governance authority migrates to unaccountable private actors). This paper examines cybersecurity governance in 29 countries. It identifies three institutional models: Integrated Command, Nodal Hybrid, and Fragmented Sectoral. Key finding: institutional architecture predicts operational resilience more consistently than strategy quality or financial investment. The analysis covers 66 governance instruments and reveals four critical weaknesses in how AI challenges current systems: Scale (velocity), Attribution (identity), Dependence (vulnerability to foreign platforms), and Agency (autonomous systems without accountability). The paper calls cloud providers, AI developers, and standards bodies "private leviathans": they exercise sovereign-scale power over cyberspace without democratic mandate or meaningful accountability. Using the OECD Strategic Foresight Toolkit, the paper stress-tests six governance models across four 2030 scenarios. Two extremes fail: total centralization (a Global Cyber Agency) and total market-led governance. The "Adaptive Federated Stack"—a five-layer architecture—distributes authority across existing institutions without creating a new apex body. This model performs across all scenarios and resolves all three Governance Debt dimensions. The central argument: between 2026 and 2030, governance success requires interoperability, not unity.
papers.idea: Cities could adopt the 'Adaptive Federated Stack' governance model to coordinate cybersecurity resilience across municipal agencies and private platform dependences.
papers.summary: The paper identifies a 'Proliferation Paradox' in global cybersecurity governance: more instruments exist but resilience has deteriorated. It proposes an 'Adaptive Federated Stack'—a five-layer distributed authority architecture—as superior to centralized or market-led alternatives for managing AI-driven cyber threats across borders.
papers.method: Comparative institutional analysis across 29 countries using OECD Strategic Foresight Toolkit stress-testing against four 2030 scenarios; no empirical outcome measurement.
papers.scalability_note: Model is macro-level governance architecture; unclear how to operationalize or test at city scale without evidence of prior implementation.
papers.cost_note: Likely requires significant new coordination infrastructure and institutional redesign; no cost estimates provided.
papers.nyc_note: Cybersecurity governance is primarily federal/national; NYC has limited direct authority over cloud providers and AI developers ('private leviathans'). Model may inform NYPD/city IT coordination but lacks concrete municipal lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical model stress-tested in scenarios, not empirically evaluated in real-world municipal deployment.
papers.underused_note: Cybersecurity governance is actively pursued by cities; this is an architectural framework, not an underdeployed intervention with proven public-safety impact.
papers.novelty_note: Federated governance models are established; framing around 'Governance Debt' and private leviathans is conceptually fresh but not operationally novel.

papers.title: The Tone of Awareness: Topic, Sentiment, and Toxicity Maps During Mental Health Month on TikTok
papers.authors: Henrique Ferraz de Arruda, Andreia Sofia Teixeira, Pranay Gundala Reddy, Anindya Mondal, Kleber Andrade Oliveira, Filipi Nascimento Silva
papers.abstract: Despite raising concerns about the mental health effects associated with the usage of TikTok, little is known about how related content is framed by creators and received by audiences. We collect the content of 28,341 TikTok videos and 80,130 comments from Mental Health Awareness Month (May) in 2023 and 2024 via the TikTok Research API, and study how the tone of awareness varies across topics and years. We characterize "tone" as the emotional and interpersonal framing of mental health discourse, operationalized through sentiment and toxicity measures. We extract topics from video text using BERTopic and log-odds keywords, then quantify topic-conditioned sentiment (XLM-T) and toxicity (Detoxify) separately for video transcriptions and comments. Sentiment captures the affective valence of content, while toxicity reflects the presence of harmful or abusive language. We find a stable set of recurring themes across years, spanning clinical conditions, emotional disclosure, self-care, and campaign-oriented content, with engagement highly skewed toward a small subset of topics. All sentiment and toxicity analyses are computed separately for video content and comments, allowing us to distinguish between content production and audience reception. Sentiment in videos is often negative for emotionally charged topics, while comments tend to shift toward more mixed or positive polarity, especially for suicide prevention. Toxicity is low in median overall, but exhibits longer-tailed outliers in comments than in videos that are more pronounced in comments and concentrated in specific topics (e.g., "Duet", "Suicide Prevention", and "Psychisch"). Overall, our results provide a topic-level decomposition of mental health discourse on TikTok during awareness-month campaigns.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; this is a descriptive measurement study of social media discourse.
papers.summary: The study analyzed 28,341 TikTok videos and 80,130 comments from Mental Health Awareness Month (May 2023–2024) to characterize how mental health content is framed by creators and received by audiences. Researchers found recurring themes (clinical conditions, emotional disclosure, self-care, campaigns), with negative sentiment in videos about emotionally charged topics but mixed/positive sentiment in comments; toxicity was generally low but with outlier spikes in specific topics like suicide prevention.
papers.method: Cross-sectional content analysis of TikTok videos and comments collected via TikTok Research API, with automated topic extraction (BERTopic), sentiment analysis (XLM-T), and toxicity detection (Detoxify) applied to transcriptions and comments separately.
papers.scalability_note: Passive measurement of existing discourse; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: Zero implementation cost; observation-only study with no program or service delivery.
papers.nyc_note: While NYC could theoretically monitor mental health discourse on social platforms, the study offers no actionable policy lever, intervention design, or evidence base for a city program. It is descriptive only.
papers.reach: 28,341 TikTok videos and 80,130 comments from May 2023 and 2024
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Purely descriptive analysis with no intervention tested or causal effects demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention; social media monitoring studies are routine in tech and public health research.
papers.novelty_note: Applies standard NLP tools (BERTopic, sentiment analysis, toxicity detection) to Mental Health Awareness Month content—incremental methodological application rather than new insight.

papers.title: Learning at Scale : Infrastructure, Aid Effectiveness, and World Bank Performance
papers.abstract: This paper reexamines aid effectiveness using ex-post economic rates of return from 2,500 World Bank–financed infrastructure projects over six decades. Three facts emerge. First, average value creation is high: the mean economic rate of return is 24 percent (median 18.5 percent). Second, returns rise over time: after relative stability through the mid‑1980s, economic rates of return increased from the late 1980s onward, consistent with improved selection, design, and delivery. Third, economic rates of return are systematically higher in countries with weak institutions. In such countries, high-quality World Bank performance is associated with about a 12‑point increase in the average economic rate of return. A plausible mechanism is talent deployment. Higher‑quality task team leaders are disproportionately assigned to the most challenging contexts and linked to faster time‑to‑effectiveness and higher economic rates of return, despite longer, more intensive implementation. Overall, alongside country fundamentals, World Bank performance and talent deployment materially raise returns to scarce capital—especially where governance is weak.
papers.idea: Deploy high-performing project managers to challenging infrastructure projects to improve implementation speed and returns, especially in weak-governance contexts.
papers.summary: This paper analyzes 2,500 World Bank infrastructure projects over 60 years and finds that economic returns average 24% (median 18.5%), have risen since the late 1980s, and are systematically higher in weak-governance countries. High-quality World Bank task team leaders assigned to difficult contexts correlate with ~12-point increases in economic rate of return despite longer implementation timelines.
papers.effect_size: 12 percentage point increase in economic rate of return in weak-governance countries
papers.reach: 2,500 World Bank–financed infrastructure projects over six decades, global scope
papers.method: Ex-post analysis of economic rates of return from a portfolio of 2,500 World Bank infrastructure projects spanning six decades; observational assessment of task-team leader quality and assignment patterns.
papers.scalability_note: Study is about World Bank global development lending; finding on talent deployment is contextual to weak-governance countries and may not transfer to mature-economy city governance.
papers.cost_note: No per-unit cost stated; study focuses on aid effectiveness metrics, not implementation costs.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has strong institutions and mature governance; main finding (high-quality performers in weak-governance contexts) does not apply. No concrete policy lever for a city government.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study concerns international infrastructure aid effectiveness, not public safety or NYC-relevant interventions; tangential relevance to city governance.
papers.underused_note: This is about World Bank project management globally, not a NYC public-safety practice that could be deployed or underfunded locally.
papers.novelty_note: Talent deployment to hard contexts is intuitive management practice; framing as aid-effectiveness study rather than actionable city policy.

papers.title: Publications
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Introduction to the Special Issue on The Future of Nonprofit Regulation in the U.S.: Context, Commentary, and Contemplations, Nonprofit Policy Forum January 2026 Cindy M. Lott and Elizabeth T. Boris Regulatory Breadth Index: A New Measurement of State-Level Charity Regulation (Presentation) November 2019 Cindy M. Lott, Mary Shelly and Nathan Dietz Testing Nonprofit State Culture: Its Impact on the Health of the Nonprofit Sector (Subscription required) November 2018 Sarah L. Pettijohn and Elizabeth T. Boris Bifurcation of State Regulation of Charities: Divided Regulatory Authority Over Charities and Its Impact on Charitable Solicitation Laws April 2018 Cindy M. Lott, Mary L. Shelly, Nathan Dietz, and Marcus Gaddy State Regulation of the Charitable Sector: Enforcement, Outreach, Structure, and Staffing February 2018 Shirley Adelstein and Elizabeth T. Boris State Nonprofit Culture: Assessing the Impact of State Regulation on the Government-Nonprofit Relationship (Presentation) November 2017 Sarah L. Pettijohn and Elizabeth T. Boris Exploring the
papers.idea: States and cities could align nonprofit regulatory frameworks to improve sector accountability and service delivery.
papers.summary: This appears to be a collection listing of Urban Institute publications on state-level charity and nonprofit regulation, including work on regulatory breadth indices, state regulatory culture, and the fragmentation of charitable solicitation oversight. The items cover how regulation affects the nonprofit sector's health and the government-nonprofit relationship.
papers.method: Mixed methods: includes policy analysis, measurement frameworks (regulatory breadth index), surveys on state nonprofit culture, and presentation of descriptive findings on enforcement and staffing patterns across states.
papers.scalability_note: Regulatory frameworks must be adapted to state/local context; findings are transferable but implementation requires statutory change.
papers.cost_note: Regulatory harmonization could reduce compliance costs for nonprofits but requires administrative investment in redesign.
papers.nyc_note: NYC operates within NY State regulatory regime; limited direct city-level control over charity regulation unless state delegates authority.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Regulatory framework alignment is theoretically sound but lacks rigorous causal evidence of impact on safety outcomes or service delivery.
papers.underused_note: Nonprofit regulation is an active policy area; most states and cities already have regulatory structures, though fragmentation persists.
papers.novelty_note: Alignment and standardization of regulatory frameworks is conventional governance thinking, not a counterintuitive intervention.

papers.title: Horizontal Equity of Taxation : Citizen Beliefs and Policy Preferences
papers.abstract: Horizontal inequity occurs when employees and self-employed workers with the same income end up with different effective tax burdens, due to the difficulty of enforcing taxes on the self-employed. Based on detailed micro-tax simulation models integrated with household surveys in 25 developing countries, this paper shows that tax systems incur large horizontal inequities in practice, and reforms that improve vertical equity worsen horizontal equity by a comparable amount. In-person and online surveys across multiple countries reveal widespread concern about horizontal equity. Randomized information treatments heighten this concern but do not shift tax preferences toward addressing horizontal inequity.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a behavioral study of tax attitudes, not an actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The paper documents widespread horizontal inequity in tax systems across 25 developing countries and shows that survey respondents express concern about this inequity. Randomized information treatments increased concern but did not shift policy preferences toward addressing the problem.
papers.method: Micro-tax simulation models integrated with household surveys and randomized information treatments across multiple countries.
papers.scalability_note: Focuses on attitude measurement and survey experiments, not a tested policy lever; no intervention with clear implementation pathway.
papers.cost_note: Survey and simulation costs unspecified; not designed as an implementation-ready tool.
papers.nyc_note: Study addresses developing-country tax enforcement; NYC tax code and self-employment compliance landscape differ substantially, and paper offers no actionable reform design—only evidence that information alone does not shift preferences.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No actionable intervention tested; descriptive study of tax attitudes with null treatment effects on policy preferences.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention at all—purely observational research on citizen beliefs about taxation.
papers.novelty_note: Horizontal equity framing is standard tax-policy vocabulary; findings (information doesn't shift preferences) align with known limits of awareness campaigns.

papers.title: Nonprofit Leaders Reported Growing Uncertainty from 2024 to Early 2025
papers.authors: Hannah Martin, Grace Koch, Katie Fallon, Laura Tomasko, Mirae Kim, Lewis Faulk, Elizabeth T. Boris
papers.abstract: This report examines changes in nonprofit leaders’ outlooks for their organizations’ ability to successfully pursue their missions from 2024 to spring 2025. In it we ask, What can be learned from how these leaders’ outlooks changed during a period of uncertainty? And how can that inform our understanding of the current moment and how nonprofits can prepare for the years to come? Key Findings Forty-eight percent of nonprofit leaders reported worsened outlooks from 2024 to spring 2025, while only 19 percent reported improved outlooks. Leaders of larger organizations and arts, culture, and humanities organizations; education organizations; and public, societal benefit organizations were more likely to report worsened outlooks. Leaders whose organizations experienced government funding disruptions or reported financial challenges were more likely to report worsened outlooks. How We Did It This is a mixed-methods analysis using qualitative and quantitative data from the Nonprofit Trends and Impacts Study, which provides nationally
papers.idea: Monitor and stabilize nonprofit funding streams to preserve organizational capacity for public-benefit missions during fiscal uncertainty.
papers.summary: This report documents a decline in nonprofit leaders' confidence from 2024 to spring 2025, with 48% reporting worsened outlooks vs. 19% improved. Organizations dependent on government funding or facing financial challenges were disproportionately affected, particularly in arts, education, and public benefit sectors.
papers.method: Mixed-methods analysis (qualitative and quantitative) from the Nonprofit Trends and Impacts Study, nationally representative survey of nonprofit leaders.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are diagnostic; any intervention would depend on identifying specific funding mechanisms or policy levers to stabilize nonprofit revenues.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; stabilizing funding would require budget or restructured contracts, not costless.
papers.nyc_note: NYC relies heavily on nonprofits for human services, but this abstract offers no concrete intervention; would need deeper findings on government funding disruptions and actionable city-level responses.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive survey of sentiment; no causal intervention or demonstrated impact on public safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Nonprofit funding stabilization is recognized policy concern; not a neglected gap but under-resourced in practice.
papers.novelty_note: Monitoring nonprofit health is conventional wisdom; framing as public-safety infrastructure is slightly fresher but not fundamentally new.

papers.title: Education Governance and Race: An Analysis of School Board Discourse Using Large Language Models
papers.authors: Kylie L. Anglin, Alvin Christian, Brian Jacob, John Singleton
papers.abstract: Media accounts suggest that American school board meetings have transformed from bureaucratic affairs into arenas of conflict, particularly around race. Yet systematic evidence remains limited. Analyzing over 40,000 meeting minutes from 2018 to 2022, we examine the prevalence and predictors of race-related discourse. Using large language models and natural language processing, we find that race-related content appears in 24% of meetings, predominately voiced by officials. Opposition related to racial issues occurs in only 1% of meetings, coming from both officials and the public. Race-related discussions—both affirmative and oppositional—are most common in suburban, high-income, highly educated districts in the Northeast and West, where local discourse tracks national race-related discourse. Oppositional content also peaks in politically competitive areas.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a descriptive analysis of school board discourse patterns, not a testable intervention.
papers.summary: Using NLP and LLMs on 40,000+ school board meeting minutes (2018–2022), the authors found that race-related discourse appeared in 24% of meetings, mostly from officials; oppositional content appeared in only 1% of meetings. Geographic and political patterns emerged: race-related discussions were most common in suburban, high-income, highly educated districts in the Northeast and West, and in politically competitive areas.
papers.effect_size: 24% of meetings contained race-related discourse; 1% contained oppositional race-related content
papers.reach: 40,000+ school board meeting minutes from 2018–2022; specific number of districts not stated in abstract
papers.method: Descriptive text analysis of school board meeting minutes using large language models and natural language processing to detect and classify race-related discourse; no experimental manipulation or counterfactual comparison.
papers.scalability_note: Text analysis methods are replicable across settings, but findings are descriptive patterns without prescriptive levers.
papers.cost_note: NLP infrastructure has upfront costs; monitoring ongoing discourse is relatively low-cost once implemented.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could theoretically apply the same text-analysis method to its own school board minutes, but the study itself offers no actionable policy recommendation for a city to implement.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive analysis only; no intervention tested, no causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention—purely observational mapping of discourse patterns.
papers.novelty_note: LLM application to school board text is methodologically fresh, but findings (racial discourse is contentious, varies by region/politics) align with existing qualitative reporting.

papers.title: Uganda's Pioneer Refugee Model : Advances in Inclusion, Self-Reliance, and Pathways Forward
papers.abstract: Uganda hosts 1.96 million refugees and asylum seekers under one of Africa’s pioneering refugee policy frameworks, granting freedom of movement, the right to work, and access to social services. Yet, despite these provisions, most refugees remain in designated settlements rather than relocating to areas with greater economic opportunity. This paper examines the factors that shape settlement patterns and constrain mobility within Uganda’s inclusive, self-reliance-oriented model. It uses a mixed-methods approach that combines literature review, administrative data analysis, and semi-structured interviews. The findings show that incentives to remain in settlements and structural constraints to mobility increase aid dependence and limit refugees’ ability to pursue economic opportunities elsewhere. The paper concludes with policy recommendations to strengthen mobility-based self-reliance, reduce aid dependency, and further consolidate Uganda’s role as a regional pioneer in refugee policy.
papers.idea: Reduce structural barriers and settlement incentives to increase refugee/migrant geographic mobility and economic self-reliance.
papers.summary: Uganda's refugee policy grants freedom of movement and work rights, yet most refugees remain in designated settlements rather than relocating to areas with better economic opportunity. Mixed-methods analysis found that settlement incentives and structural constraints increase aid dependence and limit economic mobility.
papers.reach: 1.96 million refugees and asylum seekers in Uganda
papers.method: Mixed-methods study combining literature review, administrative data analysis, and semi-structured interviews in Uganda.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are context-specific to Uganda's refugee policy framework; limited applicability to NYC's distinct immigration and settlement contexts.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; likely low implementation cost if removing settlement incentives, but depends on policy redesign.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no refugee settlement system analogous to Uganda's. Study addresses rural/designated-settlement mobility in a low-income country; minimal direct policy lever for a major US city.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study identifies constraints and barriers rather than testing an intervention; no experimental outcome data on mobility or economic impact provided.
papers.underused_note: Uganda's framework is already recognized as a regional model; mobility incentives are conceptually sound but implementation gaps are the actual barrier, not lack of awareness.
papers.novelty_note: Reframing aid dependency through mobility constraints is logical but not counterintuitive; economic self-reliance via geographic freedom is conventional development thinking.

papers.title: Defendant race and neuropsychological response: an exploratory study
papers.authors: Lauren C. Porter, Will Kalkhoff, Joshua Pollock, Brian D. Johnson
papers.abstract: Abstract Objectives Scholars often highlight negative stereotyping and implicit bias as key mechanisms driving racial disparities in punishment, however these psychological processes present challenges for empirical observation and measurement. In our study, we investigate the relationship between race and neuropsychological response to a sample of booking photos of White and Black male defendants. Methods Thirty participants were shown 500 photos of defendants from Minneapolis, MN in a laboratory setting. While viewing the photos, we collected and analyzed electroencephalograms (EEGs), which capture neurological activity. We examined four markers of brain activity known as event related potentials (ERPs), which have been associated in prior work with the processing of facial stimuli, racial bias and/or expectation violation. Results Our findings are mixed, but we find that images of White male defendants were linked with larger amplitudes on one ERP that has been associated with expectation violation and one ERP that has been associated with more “effortful” and “deep” processing of facial stimuli. Conclusion Our study provides a preliminary and exploratory step towards incorporating these kinds of neurological data in the study of racial bias in sentencing and other criminal legal system outcomes.
papers.idea: Use EEG neuroimaging during judicial review to detect and counteract implicit racial bias in sentencing decisions.
papers.summary: This exploratory study measured brain activity (via EEG event-related potentials) in 30 laboratory participants viewing 500 booking photos of White and Black male defendants from Minneapolis. Findings were mixed but suggested White defendants' images triggered larger amplitudes in neural markers associated with expectation violation and deeper facial processing, suggesting potential differential cognitive processing by race.
papers.method: Laboratory experiment with 30 participants viewing defendant booking photos while EEG recorded brain activity; analysis of four event-related potential (ERP) markers.
papers.scalability_note: EEG is labor-intensive, requires equipment/expertise per participant, and unclear how lab findings translate to actual courtroom use.
papers.cost_note: EEG equipment, technicians, and analysis are expensive per participant; impractical for mass deployment in courts.
papers.nyc_note: No clear path to implementation in NYC courts; raises ethical/legal concerns about requiring judges/jurors to undergo neuroimaging; findings too preliminary to inform policy.
papers.effect_size: White male defendant photos linked with larger amplitudes on two ERPs (expectation violation and effortful processing)
papers.reach: 30 participants viewing 500 defendant photos from Minneapolis, MN
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Exploratory lab study with mixed results; no demonstrated effect on actual sentencing outcomes or bias reduction.
papers.underused_note: Not deployed because evidence is too preliminary and speculative; EEG markers lack validated causal link to judicial bias or sentencing changes.
papers.novelty_note: Creative use of neuroscience to make implicit bias measurable, but application to real courtrooms remains highly speculative.

papers.title: The Case for a Universal Child Tax Credit
papers.authors: Katherine Michelmore
papers.source: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.venue: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management
papers.idea: Cities could implement or advocate for a universal child tax credit to reduce child poverty and improve family economic stability.
papers.summary: Unable to assess—no abstract provided. The title suggests this is likely a policy-analysis piece arguing for a universal child tax credit as an anti-poverty intervention, but without content details, the empirical findings (if any) are unknown.
papers.method: Unknown; title only.
papers.scalability_note: Tax credits are federally or state-administered; city implementation is limited unless it creates a local supplement.
papers.cost_note: Depends heavily on credit size and eligibility; federal universal credit would be costly but spreading across large population.

papers.underused_note: Federal CTCR already exists; city-level implementation rare but not evidence-starved; mainstream policy debate.
papers.novelty_note: Standard redistributive tax policy; well-established economic intervention with no fresh framing for public safety.

papers.title: Relationships Matter: Rebuilding Policymaker Trust in Public Health Leadership
papers.authors: Shelley A. Hearne, Keshia M. Pollack Porter
papers.idea: Build trust between public health officials and elected policymakers through improved relationship-building and communication.
papers.summary: No abstract available. The title suggests a focus on how relationship-building and trust can improve public health leadership effectiveness, likely through improving policymaker receptivity to public health recommendations.
papers.method: Unknown; no abstract provided
papers.scalability_note: Relationship and trust-building interventions are contextual and require local adaptation; modest transferability across jurisdictions.
papers.cost_note: Likely low direct cost (training, convenings, communication protocols); no budget data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has established health bureaucracies, but trust interventions require local buy-in and ongoing effort; unclear if study addresses city-scale applications.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical evidence of public-safety outcomes presented; purely prescriptive.
papers.underused_note: Trust-building is standard practice; not a novel gap in NYC governance.
papers.novelty_note: Conventional wisdom about stakeholder communication and relationship management.

papers.title: Blindly Discriminating: The GI Bill and Racial Inequality
papers.authors: Lukas Althoff, Christiane Szerman
papers.abstract: The World War II GI Bill was the largest education subsidy in US history and a cornerstone of the postwar US transition to a knowledge economy. Although formally race-blind, the program's decentralized administration left implementation to local officials and segregated institutions, with sharply different consequences for Black and white veterans. This paper quantifies the GI Bill's impact on Black and white Americans' economic outcomes across two generations, using a regression discontinuity around WWII service eligibility cutoffs and a new data linkage from veterans in the 1940 and 1950 censuses to their sons' neighborhood outcomes between 1990 and 2025. The GI Bill widened racial inequality, doubling white veterans' college completion while steering Black veterans into often-fraudulent vocational programs with no earnings returns. The disparities persisted across generations, increasing the white-Black gap in sons' adult-neighborhood outcomes, including a 5-percentage-point (47 percent) widening of the racial college gap. Unequal returns to the same eligibility account for the intergenerational gap, with no contribution from prewar differences in socioeconomic status or geography. In sum, access to the GI Bill was not nearly the same economic opportunity for Black Americans as it was for white Americans, highlighting that race-blind policy does not guarantee racial equality.
papers.idea: None—this is a historical analysis with no actionable policy lever identified.
papers.summary: This paper quantifies how the formally race-blind GI Bill produced sharply unequal outcomes for Black and white WWII veterans due to decentralized, segregated implementation, and how those disparities persisted intergenerationally in sons' outcomes through 1990–2025. The program doubled white veterans' college completion while steering Black veterans into fraudulent vocational programs with no earnings returns, widening the racial college attainment and neighborhood-outcome gaps across generations.
papers.method: Regression discontinuity around WWII service eligibility cutoffs, linked to 1940 and 1950 census data on veterans and matched to their sons' neighborhood outcomes 1990–2025.
papers.scalability_note: Historical policy analysis; GI Bill no longer exists in this form and cannot be redesigned by cities.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; no cost analysis applicable.
papers.nyc_note: Historical analysis of a defunct federal program; no municipal policy lever or implementation pathway.
papers.effect_size: 5-percentage-point (47 percent) widening of the racial college gap across generations; GI Bill doubled white veterans' college completion while steering Black veterans into vocational programs with no earnings returns
papers.reach: Veterans from 1940 and 1950 censuses and their sons' outcomes through 2025
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Historical analysis only; no current intervention or policy lever tested.
papers.underused_note: Not actionable—descriptive history, not a deployable public-safety or civic-improvement practice.
papers.novelty_note: Well-documented historical critique; institutional racism in implementation is established scholarship.

papers.title: Playing it Safe? The Effect of Abortion Bans on Sexual Behavior
papers.authors: James M. Flynn, Caitlin K. Myers, David R. Munro
papers.abstract: We estimate the effect of total abortion bans enacted after Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health on sexual behavior. Using synthetic and standard difference-in-differences designs, we analyze gonorrhea rates and over-the-counter contraceptive purchases as indicators of sexual activity and contraceptive use. We find that total abortion bans reduced gonorrhea rates by 21% among the population aged 15–44 and increased condom purchases by 5.4%. County-level analyses suggest the response reflects awareness of state policy rather than changes in travel distance to providers. These findings are consistent with a framework in which abortion restrictions increase the perceived cost of unprotected sex, inducing more cautious behavior.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; study documents a behavioral response to state-level abortion policy, not a city-implementable program.
papers.summary: The study uses difference-in-differences analysis to estimate how total abortion bans (post-Dobbs) affected sexual behavior in U.S. states. It finds that bans reduced gonorrhea rates by 21% among ages 15–44 and increased condom purchases by 5.4%, suggesting people responded to perceived increased costs of unprotected sex by adopting safer practices.
papers.effect_size: 21% reduction in gonorrhea rates; 5.4% increase in condom purchases
papers.method: Synthetic and standard difference-in-differences designs analyzing gonorrhea rates and OTC contraceptive purchases as proxies for sexual activity and contraceptive use, with county-level sub-analyses to isolate policy awareness from travel distance.
papers.scalability_note: Study documents response to state-level abortion bans; no city intervention or program lever identified.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: Study outcome (state abortion bans) is outside NYC municipal authority; no city policy tool is identified or tested.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study documents behavioral response to policy, not an implementable intervention with city-level public safety outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable; this is observational research on state policy effects, not a deployable program or practice.
papers.novelty_note: Standard difference-in-differences analysis of policy spillovers; framing is conventional economic behavioral response to regulatory change.

papers.title: Profit-aware graph framework for cross-platform ride-sharing: Analyzing allocation mechanisms and efficiency gains
papers.authors: Xin Dong, Jose Ventura, Vikash V. Gayah
