papers.title: Strategies for preventing and reversing polarized online discourse
papers.authors: Leon Klingborg, Kenneth Mavor, Alexander J. Stewart
papers.abstract: Political polarization poses a variety of challenges for modern democracies. Entrenched disagreements on policy can prevent constructive discourse and compromise, and high levels of affective polarization threaten to undermine social cohesion and support for institutions. Finding ways to promote constructive discourse while maintaining free expression has proved a challenge for social media platforms, media outlets and policy makers alike. Here we develop a computational model -- based in psychology -- of online discourse and opinion dynamics under complex individual identities, which we use to assess the capacity of realistic interventions to prevent or reverse polarization. We show that changes to the range of acceptable opinions in a society -- i.e. the Overton window -- have a limited impact on polarization, and that attempts to ``optimize'' the Overton window can even trigger the onset of polarization. In contrast, interventions that shift attention towards under-discussed topics, or increase the costs of violating existing norms, are often effective at preventing polarization, but are less successful at reversing it. Most strikingly, increasing the salience of influential individuals, who model non-polarized discourse, can be highly effective at both preventing and reversing polarization. However we also find that once polarization has set in, even the most successful interventions result in latent extremism when identities are complex. Our work suggests that restricting speech by shrinking the range of acceptable discourse is an ineffective way to tackle polarization, whereas enforcement of existing norms, attention nudges and the presence of elites who model good behavior can be highly effective.
papers.idea: Use computational models to identify and amplify non-polarized influencers and shift media attention to under-discussed topics as a way to prevent online political polarization.
papers.summary: A computational model based on psychological theory of opinion dynamics shows that restricting speech (narrowing the Overton window) is ineffective at reducing polarization, while enforcement of existing norms, attention shifts to neglected topics, and amplification of non-polarized influential figures are more effective. However, once polarization sets in, even successful interventions leave latent extremism when identities are complex.
papers.method: Computational/agent-based model grounded in psychology; no empirical data, RCT, or real-world implementation described.
papers.scalability_note: Model-based; unclear how to operationalize 'salience of influential individuals' or 'shift attention' at city scale without knowing which platforms/outlets or mechanisms.
papers.cost_note: Costs would depend entirely on implementation approach (media campaigns, platform partnerships, etc.); abstract provides no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: Abstract offers no concrete policy levers a city government could directly adopt; mostly addresses social media platforms and media outlets, not municipal agencies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Computational model only; no real-world intervention tested or evaluated with actual behavioral outcomes.
papers.underused_note: This is a theoretical research contribution, not a deployable public-safety intervention. Social media moderation and influencer amplification are already actively pursued by platforms.
papers.novelty_note: Modeling online polarization is established; findings (norm-enforcement, elite modeling) align with existing social psychology theory.

papers.title: What the North Can Learn from the South Sex Work, Self-Organisation, and Extraterritorial Power in the Global South. A Theoretical Synthesis.
papers.abstract: This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work in the Global South and argues for an inverted learning gradient. Where an earlier companion paper showed that in the Global North the future of sex work is decided in labour law and in digital and financial infrastructure that the state would have to provide, this paper shows that in the Global South the protective infrastructure exists where sex workers have built it themselves - as peer organisation, cooperative banking, and community health networks, often before and without the state. Drawing on peer-reviewed research and movement scholarship - including Mgbako's fieldwork on African sex worker activism, the literature on the Sonagachi project and the Durbar collective in India, research on the Latin American network RedTraSex, and Agustin's analysis of the 'rescue industry' - the paper makes three claims. First, the degree of self-organisation, not the legal framework, is the strongest predictor of protective capacity in the South. Second, the same extraterritorial US power that regulates the North through platform law operates in the South through funding and visa policy, exemplified by the PEPFAR anti-prostitution pledge, the 2012 visa denials that forced an alternative AIDS conference to Kolkata, and the 2025 dismantling of PEPFAR itself - which strikes hardest at precisely those structures that had made themselves dependent on it. Third, the learning gradient between North and South runs in both directions: the South can learn institutional permanence from the North, the North can learn self-built infrastructure and peer efficiency from the South. South Africa's ongoing decriminalisation process serves as the Southern counterpart to the Belgian experiment. Related work https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/uc57b_v1
papers.idea: Not applicable—this is a theoretical synthesis without a concrete, actionable intervention.
papers.summary: This paper synthesizes literature on sex worker self-organization in the Global South, arguing that peer-built protective infrastructure (cooperatives, community health networks) rather than legal frameworks predicts protective capacity, and that US extraterritorial power (PEPFAR, visa policy) shapes outcomes. The paper calls for bidirectional learning between North and South on institutional design.
papers.method: Theoretical synthesis drawing on peer-reviewed research and movement scholarship from Africa, India, and Latin America; no primary data collection or empirical evaluation reported.
papers.scalability_note: Provides conceptual framework and international case examples but no testable intervention model or implementation pathway for a U.S. city context.
papers.cost_note: Unknown; focuses on peer self-organization rather than formal program costs.
papers.nyc_note: Centered on Global South contexts and international policy levers (PEPFAR, visa law); no NYC-specific data, programs, or direct applicability pathway identified.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical synthesis without empirical intervention testing or demonstrated outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention; no deployment metrics applicable.
papers.novelty_note: Inverted learning gradient and peer-infrastructure framing challenge typical North-to-South development assumptions.

papers.title: How to Detect and Measure the AI Dangers to Democracy
papers.authors: Giulia Sandri, Claudio Novelli
papers.abstract: Research on artificial intelligence and democracy has grown quickly over the last decade. A shared conclusion in this literature is that AI does not create new democratic problems so much as it makes old ones worse. We now see this across information ecosystems, in elections, and in public administration. However, despite growing evidence, we lack a clear way to prioritize risks in this area, compare them across domains, and identify where democratic control is most likely to break down. So, our problem is: How can we systematize the problems that AI systems pose to democratic processes? This paper argues that principal agent theory may fit the task. In many phases of democratic systems, principals delegate key functions to AI systems and their providers without really being able to monitor how these systems operate or the outputs they produce. Treating AI as a delegation problem helps identify accountability gaps and other governance failures. Most importantly, as we shall illustrate, it provides metrics for empirical assessments of AI impact on democracy. As a second analytical element, we draw on the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and its seven characteristics of trustworthy AI, which supply substantive criteria for evaluating delegated tasks. Operationalized across the three domains through measurable indicators and domain specific trustworthiness criteria, we propose an analytical framework that centers on institutional assessability as the central condition for democratic control over AI. However, we stress that how severe a harm is, and how much risk is acceptable, are evaluative judgments that current methodologies neither acknowledge nor operationalize. This becomes acute when such evaluative judgments are (silently) delegated to private vendors. We identify this as a strong limitation left for future work.
papers.idea: Establish institutional audit frameworks to monitor AI system delegation and accountability in city government using principal-agent theory and NIST trustworthiness criteria.
papers.summary: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for systematizing risks that AI systems pose to democratic processes by applying principal-agent theory and NIST AI Risk Management Framework criteria. It argues that treating AI deployment as a delegation problem can identify accountability gaps, but acknowledges that evaluative judgments about acceptable risk levels remain unmeasured and often silently delegated to private vendors.
papers.method: Non-empirical conceptual framework drawing on principal-agent theory and NIST standards; no new data collection or empirical testing described.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is abstractly generalizable but requires substantial institutional customization per domain and jurisdiction.
papers.cost_note: Audit infrastructure requires initial design and ongoing staffing but no per-unit costs specified.
papers.nyc_note: Relevant to NYC governance (algorithm audits in criminal justice, welfare, education) but paper provides no concrete implementation guidance, metrics, or case studies for a city to operationalize.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework with no empirical outcome data on public safety or crime reduction.
papers.underused_note: AI governance audits are emerging in major cities; not niche, but implementation varies widely.
papers.novelty_note: Principal-agent framing of AI accountability is thoughtful; applying NIST criteria to city government is moderately fresh but not radical.

papers.title: Decision Theory for the Archetype Discovery Problem
papers.authors: José Luis Montiel Olea, Amilcar Velez, Zhuoheng Xu, Haomin Yu, Shunqi Zhang
papers.abstract: In the archetype discovery problem a researcher wants to summarize N heterogeneous policy effects of interest that vary over a discrete set of covariates. The goal is to partition the set of covariates into K<N groups -- the archetype sets -- and to provide a summary of the policy effects for each group. We use decision theory to show that, under a weighted mean-squared-error criterion, a procedure analogous to the Sorted Group Average Treatment Effects (GATES) solves the archetype discovery problem. The key difference is that, in the optimal procedure, archetype sets are obtained by weighted K-means clustering of the N heterogeneous policy effects, instead of relying on K equally-spaced quantiles. We show that the procedure that minimizes average risk for a given prior can be obtained by clustering the different values of the posterior mean estimate of the policy effects of interest. Similarly, an approximately minimax procedure in large samples can be obtained by clustering a consistent estimator of the policy effects. In both of these cases, an exact solution to the weighted K-means clustering problem can be found using a simple and well-known dynamic programming algorithm.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a statistical methods paper with no concrete policy application described.
papers.summary: The paper develops a decision-theoretic framework for discovering policy-effect archetypes by partitioning heterogeneous treatment effects into K groups using weighted K-means clustering rather than quantile-based approaches. The method aims to optimally summarize complex, varying policy impacts across different subpopulations.
papers.method: Theoretical decision theory paper proposing an algorithm (weighted K-means via dynamic programming) for clustering heterogeneous policy effects; no empirical application, data, or case study provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Depends entirely on downstream application; method itself is a computational tool, not a policy intervention.
papers.cost_note: Computational cost is minimal, but value depends on whether a city actually uses it to redesign a program.
papers.nyc_note: Could theoretically help NYC analyze heterogeneous treatment effects from existing program evaluations, but abstract provides no concrete example or policy hook.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methods paper with no empirical policy evaluation or demonstrated real-world impact.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable; this is a theoretical statistical framework, not a deployable intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Applies existing decision theory and clustering methods to program evaluation; incremental methodological contribution rather than conceptually novel.

papers.title: Heterogeneous behavioral mechanisms in epidemiological models
papers.authors: Jessica Pavani, Rob Deardon, Alexandra M. Schmidt
papers.abstract: Traditional epidemic models frequently assume behavioral homogeneity. The susceptible-infected-recovered model provides a robust foundation for characterizing disease transmission, but it does so without accounting for how people actually respond to risk. In contrast, behavioral change models incorporate mechanisms that capture how individuals adjust their actions during an outbreak, recognizing that rising infection risk typically motivates protective behaviors. Yet both approaches share a key limitation: they overlook the inherent heterogeneity of a population. In reality, communities are a complex mixture of risk tolerances and behavioral tendencies. Ignoring this inherent heterogeneity can obscure important differences in how individuals perceive and respond to disease threats. This paper introduces a novel Bayesian mixture model designed to address this limitation by partitioning the population into two distinct behavioral patterns: risk-neutral individuals, who maintain baseline contact rates, and risk-averse individuals, who modulate their behavior in response to epidemic severity. By integrating these disparate dynamics into a unified transmission framework, the proposed model explicitly accounts for varying population behaviors often overlooked by aggregate approaches. Through simulation studies and empirical data applications, we demonstrate that this approach significantly outperforms traditional models in parameter recovery, epidemic trajectory estimation, and forecasting precision. The findings suggest that failing to account for behavioral diversity leads to biased peak estimates and artificially stretched epidemic curves. Consequently, this research provides a more nuanced computational toolkit for predicting outbreak trajectories in socially fragmented environments, ensuring that public health intervention strategies are informed by a foundation of behavioral realism.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a methodological paper on epidemic modeling without an actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The paper proposes a Bayesian mixture model that partitions populations into risk-neutral and risk-averse behavioral groups to improve epidemic forecasting. The authors show their model outperforms traditional SIR models in parameter recovery and trajectory estimation through simulations and empirical applications.
papers.method: Bayesian mixture modeling with simulation studies and empirical data applications; no sample size stated, no field intervention or real-world policy test.
papers.scalability_note: Methodological framework useful for modelers; requires integration into public health forecasting infrastructure but does not itself constitute a deployable intervention.
papers.cost_note: Moderate—adds computational complexity to existing epidemic models; no operational deployment costs estimated.
papers.nyc_note: NYC's health department could adopt this forecasting method, but the paper offers no guidance on *which behavioral interventions* to deploy based on heterogeneity; useful only as a prediction tool, not a concrete program.
papers.effect_size: Significantly improved parameter recovery, epidemic trajectory estimation, and forecasting precision compared to traditional models; traditional models produce biased peak estimates and artificially stretched epidemic curves
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological paper with no tested public-safety intervention; demonstrates modeling superiority only in simulation.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention—pure epidemiological methodology without deployment relevance to NYC safety.
papers.novelty_note: Behavioral heterogeneity in epidemic models is recognized in literature; framing is incremental, not counterintuitive.

papers.title: Position: Align AI to Our Aspirations, Not Our Flaws
papers.authors: Nikita Kazeev, Bui Nhat Huyen Phan
papers.abstract: We argue that aligning AI to aggregated human preferences is the wrong target. With current technology, one can train AIs to share the values of a Silicon Valley techno-optimist, a degrowth environmentalist, a national-conservative culture warrior, a single-party state cadre, or a devout religious traditionalist. We should not. Human values produce societies that thrive or fail on the merits of those values - from failed states and extreme inequality to declining happiness, political polarization, and government dysfunction in the world's wealthiest democracies. The pluralistic-alignment program correctly diagnoses that there is no single "humanity" to align with, but is dangerous if taken as the main directive. We argue that AI should be trained to a non-negotiable floor of objective alignment goals - competence, bounded by the constraints of factual accuracy, honesty, and lawfulness and that pluralism belongs at the surface (language, register, conventions, missing-context defaults) and across the wide band of legitimate value tradeoffs that respect the floor, but not at the level of values that violate it. We highlight the empirical reality of unfiltered pluralistic values, propose four commitments as a constructive alternative, and engage six credible objections: commercial pressure and practical feasibility, democratic legitimacy, regulatory compliance, over-reliance on institutionalist explanations, the charge that the floor itself is culturally laden, and the limits of Coherent Extrapolated Volition.
papers.idea: Establish non-negotiable AI alignment floors (factual accuracy, honesty, lawfulness) before deploying AI systems in city services.
papers.summary: This is a position paper arguing that AI systems should be aligned to objective standards (factual accuracy, honesty, lawfulness) rather than aggregated human preferences or single value systems. The authors critique both purely pluralistic alignment and value-laden alternatives, proposing instead a tiered approach with a mandatory ethical/epistemic floor and pluralism only at surface and legitimate-tradeoff levels.
papers.method: Philosophical argument with engagement of prior literature and objections; no empirical study, data analysis, or intervention testing.
papers.scalability_note: Principles are universally applicable but implementation requires institutional buy-in and developer compliance.
papers.cost_note: Depends entirely on deployment model; no cost analysis provided in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: City has limited direct leverage over AI vendor practices; would require procurement standards or regulation that aligns with vendor incentives.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Position paper with no empirical evidence of implementation outcomes or effectiveness in reducing public safety harms.
papers.underused_note: AI governance and ethics frameworks are emerging across major cities; this is a normative proposal, not a demonstrated intervention gap.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes AI alignment debate by distinguishing objective floors from value pluralism—moderately fresh framing but within existing governance-ethics discourse.

papers.title: How Universities and Hospital Systems Affect Local Economies
papers.authors: Jonathan Schwabish, Christine Baker-Smith
papers.abstract: Over the past 75 years, the US economy has shifted away from manufacturing and toward sectors like education and health care. In 1950, manufacturing accounted for about 30 percent of US jobs—a share that’s fallen to just 8 percent in 2026. Over the same period, education and health care jobs rose from about 5 percent to nearly 18 percent. These sectors are key to the economic health and development of communities around the country.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified in abstract.
papers.summary: The abstract documents a long-term sectoral shift in the US economy away from manufacturing (30% in 1950 to 8% in 2026) and toward education and health care (5% to 18% over the same period). It frames these sectors as key to community economic health but does not specify what universities or hospital systems should do differently, nor what levers cities have to influence outcomes.
papers.method: Descriptive historical trend analysis of employment shares across sectors, 1950–2026.
papers.scalability_note: Trend analysis, not an intervention; cities cannot easily reverse sectoral shifts.
papers.cost_note: No cost data provided; depends entirely on what policy response is proposed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has major university and hospital systems; abstract offers no guidance on what to do with that fact.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No intervention tested; descriptive economic trend analysis only.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention—no deployment gap to measure.

papers.title: Comment Letter in Response to the Department of Education’s Request for Comment on the Proposed Rule ED-2026-OPE-0100, Accountability in Higher Education and Access through Demand-driven Workforce Pell
papers.authors: Jason Cohn, Kristin Blagg
papers.abstract: Writing in response to a request for comment on the proposed rule ED-2026-OPE-0100, Accountability in Higher Education and Access through Demand-driven Workforce Pell: Student Tuition and Transparency System (STATS) and Earnings Accountability, Urban Institute researchers evaluate the third threshold for graduate level earnings. Their analysis suggests that a third earnings threshold (i.e., earnings within the same state for the same field of study) is missing—because of small n sizes in the American Community Survey—for just 4 percent of graduate program completers. Moreover, among programs that have all three earnings measures, this third metric is the lowest of the three just 19 percent of the time. We conclude that omitting the calculation when fewer than 30 observations are present would have minimal effects on outcomes for the “do no harm” test.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a comment letter on federal higher-education accountability metrics, not an actionable municipal intervention.
papers.summary: Urban Institute researchers submitted a comment letter to the U.S. Department of Education evaluating a proposed federal rule on graduate-program earnings accountability. They find that a third earnings threshold (intrastate, same-field comparison) is missing for only 4% of programs due to small sample sizes, and is rarely the lowest of three metrics (19% of the time), suggesting minimal harm from excluding programs below a threshold of 30 observations.
papers.method: Analysis of proposed federal earnings-accountability thresholds using American Community Survey data; no empirical study conducted by the authors.
papers.scalability_note: This is a federal comment letter; directly non-scalable as a city policy.
papers.cost_note: No cost to implement; this is advice on federal rule-writing.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has no control over federal higher-education accountability rules; the item offers no lever for municipal action.
papers.effect_size: Omitting calculations with fewer than 30 observations would have minimal effects on outcomes for the 'do no harm' test
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No municipal public-safety intervention tested; federal policy commentary only.

papers.title: Before the Exodus? Young Scientists and the Future of US Science
papers.authors: Pierre Azoulay, Raffaella Sadun, Daniela Scur
papers.abstract: Shortly after major policy changes to US science funding began in early 2025, we surveyed 916 young biomedical scientists – PhD students and postdoctoral researchers – about their career intentions and expectations. The results document a dramatic shift in sentiment. Barely half of respondents now say they are likely to remain in academia, down 22 percentage points from how they felt six months earlier. The fraction likely to stay in the United States fell by 21 percentage points. Even satisfaction with having pursued a PhD in science declined by 16 percentage points. These are not the complaints of established scientists defending their budgets, but rather the stated intentions of the next generation – the scientists who would, in ordinary times, become the principal investigators of the future.
papers.idea: No actionable city intervention identified; abstract documents a sentiment shift among scientists without proposing a policy lever.
papers.summary: A survey of 916 young biomedical scientists (PhD students and postdocs) in early 2025 found sharp declines in their likelihood of staying in academia (down 22 points), remaining in the US (down 21 points), and satisfaction with pursuing a PhD (down 16 points), reportedly following US science funding policy changes.
papers.method: Cross-sectional survey of 916 young biomedical scientists conducted shortly after early-2025 policy changes, compared to self-reported attitudes from six months prior.
papers.scalability_note: Federal science funding policy is not a city lever; any city-level response would be indirect and limited.
papers.cost_note: Unknown from abstract; city interventions to retain scientists could range widely in cost.
papers.nyc_note: NYC could theoretically fund local biomedical research or offer incentives, but the core problem (federal funding policy) lies outside city control.
papers.effect_size: 22 percentage point decrease in likelihood of staying in academia
papers.reach: 916 young biomedical scientists (PhD students and postdoctoral researchers)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Documents sentiment shift, not an intervention with measurable public-safety or city-life outcome.
papers.underused_note: No actionable intervention proposed; descriptive survey only, not a deployable policy.
papers.novelty_note: Standard survey of career sentiment; no novel framing or mechanism for city improvement.

papers.title: Dynamic Risk Adjustment in Markets with Persistent Risk and Manipulable Signals: Market Design for Health Insurance
papers.authors: Alex Chan
papers.abstract: Risk adjustment is a payment mechanism, not only a prediction problem. I extend optimal risk adjustment to dynamic insurance markets in which plans capture future residuals from persistent risk. Under an efficiency criterion, payments should reflect expected profits and losses over the enrollee relationship on margins plans control, not only one-year spending predictions. A finite-cell model separates selection, health production, and manipulable measurement. The framework implies that annual recalculation can tax prevention, high-R² prediction can reduce welfare, and lagged claims anchors are useful only with gaming safeguards.
papers.idea: Redesign health insurance risk-adjustment payment formulas to account for multi-year plan incentives rather than one-year spending predictions.
papers.summary: This paper extends optimal risk adjustment theory to dynamic insurance markets, arguing that payment mechanisms should reflect expected lifetime profits/losses under plan control rather than single-year spending predictions. The model suggests annual recalculation can discourage prevention, high-prediction accuracy may reduce welfare, and historical claims data requires gaming safeguards.
papers.method: Theoretical framework (finite-cell model) separating selection, health production, and manipulable measurement; no empirical application or data analysis presented.
papers.scalability_note: Requires federal/state insurance policy changes; limited to health insurance markets with regulatory authority.
papers.cost_note: Implementation cost unclear; primarily administrative redesign of existing payment mechanisms.
papers.nyc_note: Health insurance risk adjustment is a federal/state regulatory function; NYC has no direct lever over Medicare, Medicaid, or commercial insurers' risk models.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework with no empirical results on actual health or safety outcomes; policy redesign implications unclear.
papers.underused_note: Health insurance design is heavily regulated and actively debated by CMS, HHS, and insurers; not an overlooked gap.
papers.novelty_note: Interesting theoretical contribution to insurance economics, but framed in standard econometric terms; not a novel safety or service-delivery insight.

papers.method: Not stated in abstract; likely qualitative or institutional analysis of solar adoption barriers in Pune.

papers.title: Public perceptions of climate policy fairness and effectiveness across climate policy types and domains in 15 countries
papers.abstract: Individuals are more supportive of climate policies when they think the measures are fair and effective. This evidence, however, is primarily based on data from Western samples, leaving it open how public effectiveness and fairness perceptions vary across geographical contexts and how they are shaped by regional indicators. Here, we aim to address these questions, investigating climate policy effectiveness and fairness perceptions across four policy types and two policy domains in 15 countries, covering all continents (N=6’052). Effectiveness and fairness ratings varied between policy types and domains, and this pattern was relatively stable across nations. However, individuals in the Global South were overall more optimistic about the effectiveness of climate policies and evaluated them as generally fairer compared to the Global North. Linking psychological and contextual data further shows that effectiveness and fairness perceptions were higher in countries that are more vulnerable to climate change impacts and lower in countries with higher per capita emissions. Moreover, the impact of psychological variables such as climate change concern and political orientation on both outcomes varied as a function of national context. Taken together, our findings point to important geographical asymmetries in the extent to which the public perceives climate mitigation policies as effective and fair, shaped by how much countries contribute to and are affected by climate change.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a descriptive survey of public attitudes toward climate policy, not an actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The study surveyed 6,052 respondents across 15 countries to measure public perceptions of fairness and effectiveness of climate policies. It found that people in the Global South viewed climate policies as more effective and fair than those in the Global North, and that perceptions correlate with countries' climate vulnerability and per-capita emissions.
papers.method: Cross-national survey measuring public perceptions of climate policy effectiveness and fairness across four policy types and two domains, linked with psychological and contextual country-level indicators.
papers.scalability_note: Survey findings describe attitudes but offer no concrete policy lever or program design that a city could adopt or test.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable — no intervention is proposed.
papers.nyc_note: This is a measurement/attitude study with no actionable policy recommendation tailored to city government implementation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive survey only; no intervention tested, no causal effects demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention—purely attitudinal research with no deployment pathway.
papers.novelty_note: Cross-national attitude survey; modest insight that vulnerability and emissions exposure shape fairness/effectiveness perceptions, but not a novel policy mechanism.

papers.title: Modeling Nonlinear Ability Trajectories and Learner Heterogeneity in Online Learning: A Bayesian Nonparametric Dynamic IRT Framework
papers.authors: Zhihua Ma, Alice Xu, Icy Zhang, Guanyu Hu
papers.abstract: Online learning has amplified the need to understand how student engagement patterns influence learning outcomes, particularly given the flexibility of technology-mediated environments. To address this, we propose a Bayesian nonparametric dynamic item response theory (IRT) framework that tracks within-individual ability trajectories across instructional units. The proposed model integrates B-spline basis expansions to capture nonlinear effects of engagement behaviors on ability drift, alongside a Mixture-of-Finite-Mixtures (MFM) prior to automatically determine the number of latent learner clusters. This framework overcomes three limitations in the existing literature: (1) rigid linearity assumptions in engagement-ability relationships, (2) dependence on pre-specified cluster counts, and (3) the inability to track longitudinal ability dynamics. We apply the model to longitudinal data from 198 undergraduates completing a 9-chapter introductory statistics course on CourseKata. The model automatically identified four distinct learner profiles: struggling-declining (11\%), low-stable (23\%), mainstream-stable (55\%), and high-improving (12\%). Results indicate that ability trajectories remained remarkably stable across chapters, and engagement quantity metrics did not significantly predict ability drift. These findings suggest that in introductory online statistics education, academic ability primarily reflects a stable pre-existing characteristic rather than a dynamically malleable course outcome. Ultimately, this framework offers a flexible tool for learner profiling to inform adaptive instructional design.
papers.idea: None—this is a methodological paper on statistical modeling of online learning, not a tested intervention.
papers.summary: The authors develop a Bayesian statistical framework to track how student ability changes across chapters in an online statistics course. Applied to 198 undergraduates, the model identified four learner profiles and found that ability trajectories were stable and engagement quantity did not predict ability gains.
papers.method: Observational longitudinal study of 198 undergraduates in a 9-chapter online introductory statistics course (CourseKata), using a newly developed Bayesian nonparametric dynamic IRT model with B-spline expansions and mixture-of-finite-mixtures clustering.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is a statistical tool for analysis; requires domain expertise and computational infrastructure to apply elsewhere.
papers.cost_note: Cost of implementing the statistical model itself is low, but value depends entirely on whether actionable insights emerge—this study did not identify interventions.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete policy, program, or practice lever. City agencies would need to first decide *why* to profile online learners and *how* to use profiles to change instruction—neither addressed here.
papers.effect_size: No significant effect of engagement quantity metrics on ability drift
papers.reach: 198 undergraduates in a 9-chapter online statistics course
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological paper with no tested intervention; findings suggest engagement does NOT predict ability drift.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—no public-safety or civic intervention proposed or evaluated.
papers.novelty_note: Statistical method is sophisticated but orthogonal to civic or safety challenges; framing as learner profiling is conventional.

papers.title: Leveraging Physiological Signals to Predict Exam Outcomes with Machine Learning
papers.authors: Lala Yamazaki, Ramchandra Rimal
papers.abstract: This study investigates the application of machine learning models to predict exam outcomes using physiological data collected during examination sessions. Physiological stress indicators, including electrodermal activity, heart rate, and skin temperature, were analyzed to uncover their association with academic performance. A variety of machine learning approaches were employed, ranging from standard models like logistic regression, random forest, and support vector machines to more advanced architectures, including transformers, long short-term memory (LSTM), and gated recurrent unit (GRU) models. This diversity aimed to capture the complex interactions within the data effectively. A key focus was assessing the adaptability of transformers in processing numerical data and evaluating their performance in this novel context. Standard performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, were used to compare model efficacy. The experimental results demonstrate that while deep learning models generally excel at capturing complex relationships in physiological data, simpler models like random forests can sometimes achieve superior performance while offering computational efficiency and interpretability. Furthermore, transformers demonstrated notable versatility, showcasing performances comparable to those of the LSTM and GRU models. This research underscores the importance of experimenting with a broad class of models that align with the objectives of the problem at hand, balancing precision, efficiency, and interpretability. By elucidating the relationships between physiological signals and academic performance, this study contributes to understanding stressors affecting students' mental health. It further promotes leveraging physiological data to enhance student well-being and academic outcomes.
papers.idea: Deploy wearable physiological sensors during school exams to predict student stress and intervene to improve academic outcomes.
papers.summary: The study applied machine learning models (logistic regression, random forests, SVMs, transformers, LSTMs, GRUs) to physiological data (electrodermal activity, heart rate, skin temperature) collected during exams to predict exam performance. Results showed that simpler models like random forests sometimes outperformed deep learning approaches while offering better interpretability and efficiency.
papers.method: Machine learning comparison study analyzing physiological signals collected during examination sessions; no sample size, setting, or experimental design details provided in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Requires real-time wearable sensors on every test-taker; logistical and privacy barriers in mass school settings.
papers.cost_note: Wearable sensors and data infrastructure would be moderately expensive per student per exam; no cost data in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC schools could theoretically adopt this, but practical hurdles (sensor procurement, consent, data privacy, integration into testing protocols) are substantial; purely predictive rather than interventional.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Study is methodological (ML model comparison) with no actual intervention deployed or evaluated; no causal evidence of improved outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Not a public-safety or civic intervention; belongs in educational research, not policing/crime prevention.
papers.novelty_note: Wearable stress monitoring is emerging in education; framing as exam-outcome prediction is incremental, not fundamentally new thinking.

papers.title: Digital Twin-Based Simulation for Predictive Decision-Making in Waterway Logistics
papers.authors: Matthijs Jansen op de Haar, Daniel Frutos Rodriguez
papers.abstract: This paper investigates the potential of a Digital Twin (DT) for freight routing across inland waterway networks under uncertain water-level conditions. Existing approaches insufficiently account for increasing climate-induced volatility in water levels, which often result in higher operational costs and emissions due to the need for more expensive transport alternatives, such as road transport. These existing methods often rely on reactive countermeasures to remain resilient. To address these limitations, six interviews with experts in domains related to inland shipping were conducted to identify three common contingency scenarios and appropriate operational responses. These scenarios were subsequently incorporated into a time-sliced simulation environment in which predictive decision-making, enabled by a DT environment, was compared against reactive approaches. The results demonstrate that predictive modeling substantially reduces operational costs and modal shifts at prediction accuracies between 70% and 100%, despite extreme conditions. In addition, the predictive model achieves an average 28.3% reduction in fuel-related costs by reducing the total distance ships travel. The simulation outcomes were evaluated together with domain experts to assess the practical relevance and applicability of the proposed DT-enabled approach.
papers.idea: Use digital twin simulations to optimize freight routing on inland waterways under climate-driven water-level uncertainty, reducing modal shifts to road transport.
papers.summary: The study compared predictive decision-making (via digital twin simulation) against reactive approaches for freight routing on inland waterways facing climate-induced water-level volatility. Predictive modeling reduced operational costs and fuel use by ~28% at prediction accuracies of 70–100%, and decreased costly shifts to road transport.
papers.method: Qualitative expert interviews (n=6) to identify contingency scenarios, followed by time-sliced simulation comparison of predictive versus reactive strategies, with expert validation of results.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only to cities with substantial inland waterway freight networks; most U.S. cities lack this.
papers.cost_note: Modeling and simulation infrastructure required; ongoing operational savings possible but not quantified in abstract.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited inland waterway freight logistics; the intervention targets a niche transport mode with minimal city-policy lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Simulation-only study with no real-world implementation or outcome data; results are modeled projections in a controlled environment.
papers.underused_note: Not a public-safety intervention; infrastructure logistics optimization is outside civic-safety scope.
papers.novelty_note: Digital twin applications to logistics are emerging in industry; framing is technical rather than novel to urban safety.

papers.title: Three Key Facts on Intergenerational Wealth Transfer
papers.authors: Mingli Zhong
papers.abstract: Economic success in the US is often credited to individual effort. But wealth accumulation also depends on structural and historical factors. A projected $124 trillion will change hands between now and 2048, the largest intergenerational wealth transfer in history. This brief analyzes who inherits what, how adults without children transfer wealth, and when money flows upward, from younger to older generations. Why This Matters The data reveal two parallel realities. First, downward transfers (wealth passed from older to younger generations) are larger and more frequent among white families, reinforcing their intergenerational advantage. Second, upward transfers (financial support from adult children to aging parents) are more common among Black and Hispanic families, reflecting compensatory support within constrained economic contexts. For many families of color, wealth transfer may deliver little to nothing, or even delay younger generations’ own wealth-building efforts. Policymakers need clear data to design estate rules, retirement programs, and wealth-building
papers.idea: None identified; paper is descriptive analysis only.
papers.summary: The brief documents racial disparities in intergenerational wealth transfer: white families experience larger downward transfers reinforcing advantage, while Black and Hispanic families more commonly provide upward financial support to aging parents, constraining younger generations' wealth building.
papers.method: Descriptive analysis of wealth transfer patterns; no methodology specified in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Policy recommendations mentioned but not detailed; abstract does not propose specific interventions.
papers.cost_note: Cost unknown; abstract offers no program proposal with budget implications.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete city-level policy lever articulated. Estate and retirement rules are primarily federal/state; brief notes policymakers need data but stops short of actionable recommendations.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive analysis only; no intervention tested or causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention—observational data on wealth patterns, not a deployable policy or program.
papers.novelty_note: Well-established observation that wealth inheritance perpetuates racial inequality; framing is clear but not new to policy discourse.

papers.title: Firm’s Preferences for Emissions Reducing Measures and Willingness to Pay for a Carbon Tax in Viet Nam
papers.abstract: Viet Nam is committed to reducing its greenhouse gas emissions by 15.8% by 2030 and meeting its net-zero emission target by 2050. The industrial sector, including the power sector, is the primary emitter and its active participation is necessary to achieve the targets. This study uses a stated-preference survey Vietnamese firms to understand their preferences in reducing greenhouse gas emissions. The study finds that Vietnamese firms prefer to reduce their greenhouse gas emissions through improving energy efficiency, substituting fossil fuels with non-fossil fuels and changing production processes. The ranking of preferences differs across the size, type, ownership and geographical location of firms. Their willingness to reduce emissions is driven by anticipated future regulations, social image, and global trends. They consider the lack of finance to be the main barrier to investing in climate change mitigation measures. The study also finds that if a carbon tax were imposed at 100,000 local currency (around US$5) per ton of carbon dioxide, over 60% of firms would be willing to invest less than 5% of their annual revenue in greenhouse gas mitigation; only less than 10% of the firms are willing to allocate more than 10% their revenue for greenhouse gas mitigation. The findings also show that larger firms and state-owned firms have a higher willingness to pay for emission mitigation measures. Given the small sample size and static preference approach, the findings should be interpreted as indicative rather than conclusive.
papers.idea: Use stated-preference survey findings on firm emissions-reduction preferences to design targeted incentive structures (tax, subsidies, or regulations) for industrial decarbonization.
papers.summary: The study surveyed Vietnamese firms on their preferences for greenhouse-gas mitigation measures and willingness to pay under a carbon tax. Firms preferred energy efficiency and fuel substitution; larger and state-owned firms showed higher willingness to pay; at a hypothetical $5/ton CO₂ tax, over 60% would invest less than 5% of revenue in mitigation.
papers.method: Stated-preference survey of Vietnamese firms on emissions-reduction preferences and carbon-tax willingness to pay; authors note small sample size and static approach limit generalizability.
papers.scalability_note: Preferences are context- and time-specific to Vietnam; generalization to NYC or other jurisdictions requires new primary research.
papers.cost_note: Survey-based; low direct cost to conduct, but actual mitigation costs depend on firm investment behavior—not specified.
papers.nyc_note: Study is Vietnam-specific; NYC's industrial base, regulatory environment, and firm characteristics differ substantially; local primary research would be needed to inform policy.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Stated-preference survey with no actual behavior observed; findings are indicative only per authors; small sample acknowledged.
papers.underused_note: Carbon pricing and firm-preference-based policy design are well-established tools in climate/environmental economics; not a public-safety or NYC civic priority.
papers.novelty_note: Standard stated-preference methodology applied to industrial emissions; incremental application of existing survey techniques.

papers.title: Price Transparency, Medicare, Medicaid, And More
papers.authors: Donald E. Metz, The Editorial Staff
papers.idea: Implement price transparency requirements for healthcare providers to reduce costs and improve patient decision-making.
papers.summary: This appears to be an editorial or opinion piece on price transparency in Medicare and Medicaid programs. Without an abstract, the specific findings, evidence base, or policy recommendations cannot be determined.
papers.method: Not determinable from title alone; appears to be editorial commentary rather than empirical research.
papers.scalability_note: Price transparency is a systemic policy lever applicable across jurisdictions, but implementation complexity varies.
papers.cost_note: Administrative costs moderate; savings depend on behavioral response.
papers.nyc_note: NYC has limited direct authority over Medicare/Medicaid pricing; policy is primarily federal. City could advocate or pilot transparency measures in municipal health systems.

papers.underused_note: Price transparency is already a federal policy priority (CMS rules, transparency mandates); not a gap-in-deployment issue.

papers.authors: Samantha Teixeira, Zhirui Chen, Rebekah Levine Coley
papers.idea: Unknown; abstract not provided—cannot assess actionability.
papers.summary: Unable to assess. Title suggests qualitative study of resident perspectives on four dimensions of public housing (cost, conditions, consistency, context), but no abstract provided to determine findings or recommendations.
papers.method: Unknown; only title available. Likely qualitative (resident perspectives), but no methodological details provided.

papers.title: Thomas Brosy on proposed tax increases for Florida’s wealthiest
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis has proposed a plan that would eliminate property taxes for roughly 9 in 10 Floridians while shifting the tax burden to the state’s wealthiest homeowners, including those who live there part-time. Speaking with Bloomberg, Thomas Brosy, a tax policy expert at the Urban Institute, said if local governments shift more of their tax burden to the most valuable properties, it “could actually in practice be a way for taxing the rich.” For inquiries, please email media@urban.org.
papers.idea: Shift property tax burden from middle-class to wealthy homeowners to fund local services.
papers.summary: Thomas Brosy of the Urban Institute commented on Florida Governor DeSantis's proposal to eliminate property taxes for ~90% of Floridians while increasing taxes on wealthy homeowners. Brosy suggested this could function as a wealth-redistribution mechanism. No empirical findings or outcomes are presented.
papers.method: Quote from a tax policy expert in a media interview; no original research or data analysis conducted.
papers.scalability_note: Property tax structures are standard municipal tools implementable anywhere.
papers.cost_note: Cost depends on implementation; no per-unit estimates provided.
papers.nyc_note: NYC already has complex, high property taxes on luxury properties; this is a Florida-specific proposal with limited novel applicability.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical evidence of public-safety impact presented; purely tax-policy commentary.
papers.underused_note: Not a public-safety intervention; tax policy is mainstream political debate, not an underdeployed safety innovation.
papers.novelty_note: Progressive taxation is conventional policy; no novel framing for safety outcomes.

papers.title: Converging Paths : Intergenerational Educational Mobility and the Decline of Gender and Geographic Gaps in Bangladesh
papers.abstract: This study examines intergenerational educational mobility in Bangladesh across cohorts born between the 1950s and 1990s, using data from the 2022 Bangladesh Household Income and Expenditure Survey. Intergenerational regression coefficients and intergenerational correlations are estimated, yielding three main findings. First, while the intergenerational regression coefficient declines for the 1990s cohort, suggesting reduced persistence of the effect of parental education on children's outcomes, the intergenerational correlation, which accounts for inequality in educational attainment across both generations, follows an inverted U-shaped pattern, resulting in no net mobility change. This finding reverses earlier evidence of increasing persistence through the 1970s and indicates that educational expansion since the 1980s has progressively benefited children of less-educated parents. Second, unlike patterns observed elsewhere in the region, where urban residence confers mobility advantages, Bangladesh exhibits no urban premium. Overall mobility remains higher in rural areas, although substantial convergence occurs in the 1990s cohort. At the regional level, an East-West convergence is observed, driven by mobility improvements in traditionally less-mobile Eastern regions. Third, women historically exhibited higher mobility than men through the 1980s, with gender convergence emerging only in the 1990s cohort, largely due to accelerated male mobility gains among urban males. Bangladesh's educational mobility trajectory is thus characterized by convergence across gender, urban-rural, and region dimensions, a pattern distinct from both its historical experience and broader South Asian trends, although educational gains remain disconnected from labor market outcomes.
papers.idea: Not applicable — this is a descriptive study with no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This study documents intergenerational educational mobility trends in Bangladesh from the 1950s–1990s, finding that educational expansion since the 1980s has benefited children of less-educated parents and reduced urban-rural and gender gaps. No causal mechanisms, policy levers, or specific interventions are identified or tested.
papers.reach: Bangladesh Household Income and Expenditure Survey (population-level, exact N not stated)
papers.method: Observational analysis of intergenerational regression coefficients and correlations across cohorts using national survey data from Bangladesh.
papers.scalability_note: Findings are specific to Bangladesh's historical context; unclear what policy mechanism would be transferable.
papers.cost_note: Study involves secondary data analysis only; actual policy costs unknown.
papers.nyc_note: Bangladesh's rural-urban and regional dynamics bear little resemblance to NYC's environment; no actionable program or policy recommendation is presented.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive study only; no intervention tested or causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—no actionable intervention to deploy or underfund.
papers.novelty_note: Standard intergenerational mobility analysis; findings are contextual to Bangladesh rather than offering new conceptual framing for urban policy.

papers.title: Why Cities Customize National Policy During Implementation
papers.authors: Anders L. Nielsen
papers.abstract: Cities commonly serve as implementation agencies for the state, raising questions regarding compliance and adaptation. This paper asks why cities facing similar national demands sometimes choose different implementation strategies. It argues that local implementation strategies are a function of city council interests and local implementation conditions. The argument is tested through a comprehensive study of new Danish legislation on vulnerable social housing areas introduced in 2018, which required 10 Danish cities to develop radical physical redevelopment plans for 15 designated areas. Drawing on extensive public documents, internal archives (over 13,000 pages), and 11 key elite interviews, the analysis reveals how local implementation strategies were strongly shaped by how well the new policy aligned with city council interests and local implementation conditions. In doing so, the study contributes to a theoretical integration of urban politics scholarship with the customization argument developed in U.S. federal and EU implementation research.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical study of policy implementation mechanics, not an intervention.
papers.summary: This paper analyzes why Danish cities adopted different implementation strategies for the same national housing redevelopment mandate in 2018. The study found that local strategies were shaped by city council political interests and local conditions rather than uniform compliance.
papers.method: Comparative case study of 10 Danish cities implementing one national policy, using public documents, 13,000+ pages of internal archives, and 11 elite interviews.
papers.scalability_note: Primarily a theoretical insight about implementation variation; no specific program or practice to replicate.
papers.cost_note: Observational research; no intervention cost data provided.
papers.nyc_note: Study is contextual to Danish governance and housing policy; offers no concrete NYC-actionable intervention or program model.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical study of implementation mechanics, not an intervention with measurable public-safety or quality-of-life outcomes.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention or practice; foundational research on policy implementation theory rather than a deployable solution.
papers.novelty_note: Applies existing implementation-customization theory to urban context; incremental theoretical contribution rather than novel framework.

papers.title: Anger About Police-Civilian Relations and Support for Policing Reforms
papers.authors: Linda Balcarová, Justin T. Pickett, Amanda Graham, Francis T. Cullen
papers.abstract: Objectives Police-related anger remains undertheorized and understudied, even as evidence accumulates that issue-specific anger drives public preferences in other policy domains. We address this void in the literature by distinguishing police-related anger that is generalized from that which is situational (a state emotion), and by distinguishing anger felt toward officers from anger felt toward civilians. We theorize that individuals’ racial backgrounds and cumulative personal and vicarious experiences with police mistreatment affect both types of generalized anger and, in turn, support for policing reforms. Methods Our study uses data from two national surveys conducted by YouGov (Survey 1: N = 1,150; Survey 2: N = 1,100), which measured both types of generalized police-related anger (toward officers vs. civilians) and support for policing reforms. Results Anger about police-civilian relations is racialized. Whether it is felt toward officers or toward civilians depends on respondents’ race and past experiences with police. Both types of police-related anger strongly predict support for policing reforms. Conclusion Police-related anger is an important emotional foundation of public policy preferences in the context of policing. Group differences in experienced mistreatment help explain emotional divides as well as the racial gap in support for policing reform.
papers.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.venue: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
papers.idea: Not applicable—paper is descriptive research on anger and attitudes, not an intervention.
papers.summary: The study finds that police-related anger is racialized and varies by target (officers vs. civilians) depending on respondents' race and prior experiences with police mistreatment. Both types of anger predict support for policing reforms, suggesting emotional experience shapes policy preferences.
papers.method: Two national online surveys (YouGov; N=1,150 and N=1,100) measuring generalized police-related anger and support for reforms via self-report.
papers.scalability_note: No intervention or program to scale; measures associations between emotions and attitudes only.
papers.cost_note: No cost—observational study with no implementation lever.
papers.nyc_note: Provides context on public sentiment but does not propose a city policy, program, or practice NYC could adopt.
papers.effect_size: Both types of police-related anger strongly predict support for policing reforms
papers.reach: 2,250 survey respondents across two national surveys
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Descriptive study only; no intervention tested, no causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable intervention—purely observational research on attitudes and emotions.
papers.novelty_note: Distinguishing generalized vs. situational anger and anger toward officers vs. civilians adds some nuance, but the core insight (race shapes police attitudes and anger predicts reform support) is well-established.

papers.title: Occupational Hazard? An analysis of birth outcomes among physician mothers
papers.authors: Anupam B. Jena, David Slusky, Lilly Springer
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified; study appears to analyze health outcomes among a professional subgroup rather than propose a policy lever.
papers.summary: This study examines birth outcomes (likely adverse pregnancy and neonatal outcomes) among women physicians, comparing them to a control group. The research likely explores whether occupational stressors, long hours, or workplace hazards faced by physicians affect their own reproductive health.
papers.method: Unknown without abstract; likely observational analysis of birth outcome data linked to physician records, possibly using administrative health or employment data.
papers.scalability_note: Findings would apply only to physician populations; limited generalizability to broader city health policy.
papers.cost_note: Would require occupational interventions (scheduling, workplace accommodations) but study does not propose specific policy solutions.
papers.nyc_note: While NYC has many hospitals and physician populations, the study focuses on a narrow occupational cohort; no concrete municipal policy lever apparent.
