papers.title: An inductive-deductive circular methodology of the research process: Systematic thinking from prior assumptions to final synthesis
papers.abstract: In this essay, we reflect on the circularity of social science research and how we can attempt to put this circularity into a system to help structure our thinking and research practices through structured inductive-deductive reflection. We present our model for thinking of research as a holistic process from making our prior assumptions concrete and updating them systematically through constant comparison and a circular inductive-deductive analysis, towards a synthesis of the research findings.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a methodological essay, not an intervention.
papers.summary: This is a methods paper reflecting on how to structure social science research thinking through circular inductive-deductive reasoning. It proposes a model for researchers to make prior assumptions concrete and update them through constant comparison and synthesis.

papers.method: Methodological essay describing a proposed research framework; no empirical study or data analysis reported.

papers.title: Genetic Mirroring in Adoptees: Toward a Formal Construct Definition, Nomological Network, and Operationalization Framework
papers.abstract: Genetic mirroring, the developmentally recurring need to perceive reflected biological similarity in genetically related others, has gained clinical salience as an explanatory framework for the identity experiences of adoptees and donor-conceived individuals. Despite this, the construct lacks the theoretical architecture required for empirical investigation: a precise definition, differentiation from adjacent constructs, and a plausible operationalization pathway. The present paper addresses that gap through four coordinated contributions. First, a formal construct definition is proposed, encompassing three theoretically distinct dimensions: biological salience, embodied discontinuity, and mirroring responsiveness. Second, the construct is situated within a nomological network specifying its hypothesized relationships with established psychological variables, including identity exploration, narrative identity coherence, attachment security, genealogical bewilderment, and physical self-concept. Third, a theoretical case is made for incremental validity over existing frameworks: genetic mirroring is proposed to capture variance in adoptee psychological functioning that neither attachment theory nor narrative identity frameworks can fully account for. Fourth, a preliminary scale structure is proposed as proof of concept. The paper's aim is to transform a clinically emergent construct into a formally theorizable and measurable entity, enabling the empirical program necessary to evaluate its scientific and practical significance.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical construct-development paper with no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: This paper proposes a formal definition and measurement framework for 'genetic mirroring'—a psychological construct describing adoptees' and donor-conceived individuals' need to perceive biological similarity in genetic relatives. It situates the construct within a nomological network linking it to identity, attachment, and self-concept, and proposes a preliminary scale.
papers.method: Non-empirical theoretical paper proposing construct definition, nomological network, and preliminary scale structure as proof of concept.
papers.scalability_note: Theory/methods paper with no tested intervention; no city lever identified.
papers.cost_note: No intervention, so no cost basis.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable policy, program, or practice proposed; purely theoretical contribution to psychology literature.

papers.title: Getting Creeped Out? Open Science, Qualitative Methods, and the Dangers of Positivism Creep
papers.abstract: Many developments to reform the research landscape have occurred over the past decade. These changes have been made with broad goals to improve the ‘openness’ of research and often assumed to be ‘methodologically -agnostic’; that is, they ostensibly have benefits for all researchers occupying all epistemological and methodological positions. ‘Open science’ initiatives such as study pre-registration (i.e., specifying research aims and analytical plan ahead of data access), open data sharing, open-access publication, and open materials sharing are becoming increasingly mainstream across many fields within social research and the natural sciences. While there has been much criticism of these interventions, largely from the qualitative research community, we want to draw attention to a troubling trend in the promotion of open science: the leaking of standards relevant only to quantitative research to all paradigms. Or, as others refer to it, “positivism creep”. Here, we situate positivism creep (i.e., the creeping of positivist conventions to all research) within research policy, we highlight its increasing prevalence within open science reforms, and we warn against a future which could alienate many non-positivist scholars. We argue that the primary framing of open science as the pursuit of reproducibility and objectivity risks promoting positivism creep in the social sciences and humanities. In particular, we suggest that overly strict open research requirements placed by funders may reduce the range and variety of epistemological positions that can be taken by researchers, with particularly deleterious effects for qualitative researchers.
papers.idea: This is a methodological critique paper; no actionable intervention for cities.
papers.summary: This paper critiques the promotion of quantitative, positivist research standards (pre-registration, open data, reproducibility focus) across all research paradigms, arguing that these 'open science' reforms risk marginalizing qualitative and non-positivist scholars in the social sciences and humanities.
papers.method: Non-empirical theoretical and policy analysis of research reform trends; no original data collection or empirical evaluation.
papers.scalability_note: This is a meta-level critique of research governance, not a program or intervention.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no program or service to cost.
papers.nyc_note: No city policy levers or operational interventions identified; addresses academic research standards, not urban service delivery or public safety.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological critique paper with no tested intervention or empirical outcome measurement.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable public-safety intervention; purely academic meta-analysis of research practices.
papers.novelty_note: Restates existing qualitative-research critiques of positivism; not a new public-safety or city-life concept.

papers.title: Regularized Machine Learning for System Identification of Ship Free-Running Manoeuvres from CFD-Based Synthetic Data: A Comparative Study
papers.authors: R. F. Suárez, J. C. Berndt, M. Abdel-Maksoud
papers.abstract: This study investigates supervised machine learning techniques for identifying ship hydrodynamic coefficients from CFD-generated data from free-running simulations. Specifically, ordinary least squares and regularized regression methods are applied to Abkowitz-type manoeuvring models. Training and validation datasets are derived from URANS simulations of zig-zag and turning circle manoeuvres, which are validated against experimental benchmark data. The analysis evaluates the effects of coefficient set size, minimum training length required for predictive model training, and manoeuvre combinations on model performance. Results demonstrate the suitability of large-angle zig-zag manoeuvres for hydrodynamic system identification, provided that multicollinearity is addressed through appropriate coefficient selection, regression models, or input data variability. Larger coefficient sets offer greater model flexibility for variable conditions but are more prone to multicollinearity. Regularized regression techniques effectively mitigate multicollinearity and notably enhance prediction accuracy, as does incorporating more diverse manoeuvring data. Among tested models, Ridge regression provided the best compromise between computational efficiency and prediction accuracy.
papers.idea: Not applicable; this is a methods paper on ship hydrodynamic modeling with no city-policy lever.
papers.summary: The study develops and compares machine learning techniques (ordinary least squares, Ridge regression, and other regularized methods) for identifying ship hydrodynamic coefficients from CFD-generated synthetic data. Results show that Ridge regression balances computational efficiency and prediction accuracy, and that larger coefficient sets improve flexibility but risk multicollinearity.
papers.method: Comparative empirical study applying supervised machine learning to URANS (unsteady Reynolds-averaged Navier–Stokes) CFD simulations of ship manoeuvres (zig-zag and turning circle), validated against experimental benchmarks; no real-world city or urban context.
papers.scalability_note: Narrow technical domain (naval hydrodynamics); no application pathway to urban policy or city services.
papers.cost_note: Computational research with no human or infrastructure implementation component.
papers.nyc_note: No relevance to NYC agencies, infrastructure, or resident safety/welfare; purely maritime engineering.
papers.effect_size: Ridge regression improved prediction accuracy and mitigated multicollinearity better than other methods
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No public-safety or civic application; pure marine engineering methodology paper.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable to city policy; marine hydrodynamics research only.
papers.novelty_note: Standard machine-learning comparison study in naval engineering; no fresh framing for social problems.

papers.title: Tropical Viterbi Tubes for Decoding Uncertainty in Hidden Markov Models
papers.authors: Aurélien Nicosia
papers.abstract: Hidden Markov models are widely used to infer latent state sequences from sequential data, but Viterbi decoding reports only one most likely complete path. When decoded states carry scientific meaning, this single maximizer can conceal pathwise uncertainty created by multiple near-optimal trajectories. Conditional on a fitted HMM, we introduce the tropical Viterbi tube: the set of hidden trajectories whose complete-data log-score lies within a tolerance of the Viterbi optimum. State, transition, and change-status projections show which local features remain compatible with globally near-optimal complete paths, giving a pathwise uncertainty layer for HMMs in sequence analysis, ecology, finance, biomedical monitoring, and related domains. The tube is a posterior superlevel set on complete hidden-path space, with tolerance interpreted as a log posterior-odds loss relative to a Viterbi path. Calibrating the tolerance to a target posterior mass gives an HPD-threshold credible region for the complete latent path and conservative simultaneous projected bands. We prove monotonicity, step-function behavior, and deterministic stability guarantees, and compute projected tubes exactly by max-plus forward-backward recursions in O(TK^2) time for dense transitions. Posterior tube mass and HPD calibration are separate pathwise calculations approximated by FFBS. In a public bat-tracking application, robust foraging tube segments are enriched for feeding buzzes, whereas robust commuting segments are depleted: at eta = 0.005, enrichment is 2.25 with 95% bootstrap interval (1.73, 2.85) for robust foraging and 0.27 with interval (0.16, 0.44) for robust commuting.
papers.idea: N/A
papers.summary: This is a methodological paper introducing 'tropical Viterbi tubes,' a technique for quantifying uncertainty in Hidden Markov Model decoding by identifying sets of near-optimal hidden state trajectories. The authors demonstrate the method on bat-tracking data, showing it can identify robust behavioral segments (foraging vs. commuting) enriched or depleted for feeding buzzes.
papers.method: Theoretical paper with proof of computational properties (O(TK²) complexity) and validation on a public bat-tracking application; no city-scale dataset or experiment.
papers.scalability_note: Pure statistical methods paper; no intervention, program, or policy lever.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no intervention or cost structure.
papers.nyc_note: No city-policy application; ecological/computational research only.
papers.effect_size: 2.25x enrichment for feeding buzzes in robust foraging segments (95% CI: 1.73-2.85); 0.27x for robust commuting segments (95% CI: 0.16-0.44)
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological paper on HMM decoding; no public-safety intervention or outcome studied.
papers.underused_note: Not a deployable intervention—purely a statistical technique with no civic or safety application demonstrated.
papers.novelty_note: Novel statistical method, but orthogonal to public safety; bat-tracking example has no city-safety relevance.

papers.title: Chaining Tasks, Redefining Work: A Theory of AI Automation
papers.authors: Mert Demirer, John J. Horton, Nicole Immorlica, Brendan Lucier, Peyman Shahidi
papers.abstract: Production is a sequence of steps that can be executed (1) manually, (2) augmented with AI, or (3) fully automated within contiguous AI-executed steps called ''chains.'' Firms optimally bundle steps into tasks and then jobs, trading off specialization gains against coordination costs. We characterize the optimal assignment of humans and AI to steps and the firm's resulting job structure, showing that comparative advantage logic can fail with AI chaining. The model implies non-linear productivity gains from AI quality improvements and admits a CES representation at the macro level. Empirical evidence supports the model's key predictions that (1) AI-executed steps co-occur in chains, (2) dispersion of AI-exposed steps lowers AI execution at the job level, and (3) adjacency to AI-executed steps increases the likelihood that a step is AI-executed.
papers.idea: N/A — pure economic theory with no city-policy implementation pathway.
papers.summary: The paper develops a theoretical model of how firms optimally assign human workers and AI systems to production steps, bundling them into tasks and jobs. Empirical evidence shows AI-executed steps tend to cluster together and that proximity to automated steps increases likelihood of further automation.
papers.method: Theoretical modeling with empirical validation on firm-level job structure data; no specific sample size stated in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical framework for firm-level optimization; no city-scale intervention identified.
papers.cost_note: No costs or cost-effectiveness data provided; not an intervention.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable policy or program for a city agency to adopt; addresses private firm labor allocation decisions.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pure economic theory with no demonstrated public-safety or city-service outcome; no empirical intervention tested.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is macro labor economics, not a policy intervention or pilot program.
papers.novelty_note: Theoretical framework on task bundling is interesting but not a novel policy idea for NYC civic challenges.

papers.title: From Distorted Mirrors to Sovereign Reflections: Resisting the Grotesque Depiction of Our Digital Selves
papers.authors: Andreas Komninos, Gerasimos Vonitsanos, Spyros Sioutas
papers.abstract: As Ambient Intelligence weaves computing into everyday life, human existence has become inextricably linked to ubiquitous digital infrastructures, triggering a crisis of personal data sovereignty. Driven by extractivism and data colonialism, platforms exploit human experience as a terra nullius, enclosing proprietary user models within isolated silos. We argue that legal frameworks like the EU Digital Rights and GDPR fail to counter this power asymmetry because they regulate inert raw data while leaving monopolies over algorithmic inferences unchallenged. Consequently, current architectures act as funhouse mirrors, creating a grotesque, distorted depiction of our digital selves. Through critical posthumanism and four contemporary scenarios (entertainment, e-commerce, fintech, and telematics), this paper illustrates how data siloing inflicts cognitive amputation, ontological violence, and self-censorship. We argue that securing digital rights requires moving beyond defensive regulation toward a decolonial infrastructure that guarantees the functional transferability of algorithmic identities, reclaiming technology for the emancipation of the posthuman self.
papers.idea: Not applicable — no concrete intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper argues that current digital platforms distort personal data through algorithmic inference monopolies, violating data sovereignty. It critiques GDPR and legal frameworks as insufficient and calls for a 'decolonial infrastructure' guaranteeing transferability of algorithmic identities, illustrated through four scenarios (entertainment, e-commerce, fintech, telematics).
papers.method: Conceptual/theoretical analysis using critical posthumanism and four contemporary scenario illustrations; no empirical study, data collection, or causal testing conducted.
papers.scalability_note: Abstract proposes no implementable mechanism, program, or policy lever.
papers.cost_note: No cost information provided; concept stage only.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable intervention NYC could adopt; addresses global platform policy and philosophical frames, not a city-level program or practice.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical critique with no empirical intervention or outcome data.
papers.underused_note: No concrete public-safety intervention proposed; not applicable to deployment landscape.
papers.novelty_note: Reframes existing data-privacy discourse through posthumanist lens, but core concerns (algorithmic bias, data extraction) are well-established.

papers.title: On the Equivalence of Instantaneous and Mechanistic Reproduction Numbers
papers.authors: Jeremy Goldwasser, Ryan J. Tibshirani, Alyssa Bilinski
papers.abstract: The effective reproduction number ($R_t$) is widely used to track epidemic dynamics in real time. The standard estimation framework uses "instantaneous $R_t$," defined via the renewal equation, which relates new infections to past infections through a generation interval distribution. Compartmental models like SEIR yield a seemingly distinct quantity, "mechanistic $R_t$," based on the effective contact rate and duration of infectiousness. We prove these two definitions are equivalent under homogeneous mixing, the standard assumption in compartmental modeling. We also derive the generation interval distribution implied by SEIR dynamics. A practical consequence is that generation intervals, often treated as assumption-light inputs to renewal equation estimators, in fact encode specific compartmental structure.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a mathematical methods paper without actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The authors prove mathematical equivalence between two epidemiological definitions of reproduction number (instantaneous vs. mechanistic) and derive the generation interval distribution implied by SEIR compartmental models. This is a theoretical contribution clarifying relationships between epidemiological estimation frameworks.
papers.method: Mathematical proof of equivalence between renewal-equation and compartmental-model formulations of reproduction number; derivation of implied generation interval distributions.
papers.scalability_note: Pure mathematical theory; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No real-world implementation cost; academic research only.
papers.nyc_note: No policy or program lever; improves internal consistency of existing epidemiological estimation methods but does not propose new public health action.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pure mathematical methods paper; no intervention or empirical effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable; this is theoretical epidemiology, not a deployable public-safety intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Solid mathematical contribution to epidemiology, but not a novel framing of a social or policy problem.

papers.title: 'AI Alignment' Encompasses Competing Technical Priorities
papers.authors: Tushita Jha, Rory Svarc, Mateusz Bagiński
papers.abstract: The ML literature contains many distinct concepts falling under the heading of 'AI alignment'. After noting three concepts of AI alignment in the context of their corresponding research programs, we claim that realistic interventions may promote 'AI alignment' under one conception while being actively counterproductive from the perspective of others. We suggest that tensions between alignment ideals emerge due to differences in background threat-models, alongside differences in normative orientations. In light of our analysis, researchers aiming to further the goal of 'AI alignment' should do five things. First, they should not conflate distinctions of policy and distinctions of scientific scope; second, methodological disagreements should be acknowledged explicitly; third, researchers should distinguish between 'AI alignment' as a high-level ideal and specific 'alignment proxies' used in empirical research; fourth, they should use more granular concepts to identify both the source and nature of possible AI harms/benefits; fifth, they should explicitly acknowledge the diversity of 'alignment' concepts in both empirical work and in communication with non-technical audiences.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper is a conceptual analysis distinguishing multiple competing definitions of 'AI alignment' in machine learning research and argues that interventions promoting alignment under one definition may undermine it under another. The authors recommend that researchers use more granular terminology and acknowledge methodological disagreements.
papers.method: Conceptual/theoretical analysis of AI alignment literature; no empirical study, no data collection, no real-world intervention testing.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory with no implementable intervention.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: No concrete policy or program a city agency could adopt. Relevant only to ML researchers' internal terminology practices.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework with no empirical intervention or demonstrated real-world effect.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable public-safety intervention; conceptual analysis only.
papers.novelty_note: Clarifying taxonomy of existing ML concepts; useful but incremental contribution to technical discourse.

papers.title: Choric Masking in Ambient Release Systems: A Finite Certificate Calculus for Trace Indistinguishability under Bounded Audiences
papers.authors: Faruk Alpay, Taylan Alpay
papers.abstract: This paper develops a finite certificate calculus for ambient release systems, staged probabilistic environments in which a protected coordinate is not observed directly but can remain statistically readable through visible roles, timing, repeated movement, bounded attention, linked rooms, and post-release state. The security notion, choric masking, requires the trace law induced by a protected locus to lie inside or near the convex hull of admissible cover traces under the tests available to a specified audience. For finite horizons, trace laws form polytopes, audiences induce measurement operators, and masking becomes intersection in the projected measurement space. Exposure is certified by separating hyperplanes, kernel obstructions, hypothesis-testing bounds, Fano-type localization lower bounds, and support separation in downstream rooms. The calculus distinguishes trace residue from carrier localization, full-trace exposure from attention-filtered exposure, first-room masking from delayed post-release exposure, and unresolved system pressure from carrier hazard. It proves measurement-polytope equivalence for exact and approximate masks, dual separation certificates, data-processing laws for attention lenses, aperture identities for gaze-thinned observation, lower bounds for mandatory unique gestures, composition rules for linked releases, and a repeated-room debt theorem showing how unresolved pressure can broaden selection and shift cost onto cover populations without localizing the source. The result is a finite, checkable language for auditing privacy, unlinkability, side-channel leakage, and accountability in public-facing release systems.
papers.idea: N/A
papers.summary: This is a pure mathematics/theoretical computer science paper on formal methods for privacy and indistinguishability in probabilistic systems. It develops a calculus and proof framework for auditing information leakage in abstract 'ambient release systems' using concepts from convex geometry and hypothesis testing.
papers.method: Non-empirical; develops formal mathematical theory and proofs for a certificate calculus in staged probabilistic environments.
papers.scalability_note: Abstract mathematical framework with no concrete implementation or use case.
papers.cost_note: No deployment or operational cost relevant to city policy.
papers.nyc_note: Theoretical computer science paper with no concrete city-policy application, implementation pathway, or empirical validation.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework with no empirical public-safety evaluation or real-world impact data.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is pure formal mathematics, not a deployable civic intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Novel mathematical formalism, but addresses theoretical privacy in abstract release systems rather than a concrete urban safety problem.

papers.title: A Theory of “Political Will” for Reforms
papers.abstract: Do politicians pursue reforms when they know that status-quo policies are inefficient? Prior literature has answered this question as dependent upon reelection incentives to pander to uninformed voters. This paper shows that reform failure can arise from political selection, or the intrinsic characteristics of those who pursue leadership positions and careers in politics, even when voters would like governments to pursue efficient policies. First, the paper shows how incrementally changing the environment in pandering models can yield political selection of “status-quo” types rather than “reformers” as the driving force behind reform failures. In stark contrast to prevailing explanations, reelection incentives in this model push politicians toward reforms. Second, however, even when reelection incentives are introduced, “status-quo” types continue to resist reforms because of the reputation value in political markets of sticking to their preferred (inefficient) policy to signal their type. That is, politicians can choose inefficient policies even at the risk of losing elections because of political career concerns after exiting office. The paper thus formalizes the notion of “political will” as intrinsic motivation and political career concerns of different types of politicians. The relevance of the model is illustrated using available case studies and empirical evidence, with forward-looking implications for international development agencies that advocate reforms.
papers.idea: N/A – pure theory paper with no identified policy lever or intervention.
papers.summary: This paper develops a theoretical model explaining why politicians may resist efficient reforms due to political selection (the types of people drawn to politics) and career concerns, rather than just reelection incentives. It formalizes 'political will' as an intrinsic motivation problem but does not propose or test any concrete policy, program, or institutional change.
papers.method: Theoretical model with illustration from case studies and existing empirical evidence; no primary data collection or experimental design.
papers.scalability_note: No intervention proposed; cannot scale a theoretical insight without an actionable mechanism.
papers.cost_note: No implementation costs—purely academic theory.
papers.nyc_note: Abstract does not identify any policy instrument, program design, or reform mechanism that NYC or other cities could adopt. Aimed at international development agencies, not municipal government.

papers.underused_note: Not applicable; no concrete intervention identified to deploy or fund in cities.

papers.title: Kassandra Martinchek on young people’s barriers to financial independence
papers.authors: Urban Institute
papers.abstract: Achieving financial independence for the first time as a young adult has its challenges. But according to Kassandra Martinchek, a wealth and financial well-being expert at the Urban Institute, today's young people face “some challenges that may not have been present for other cohorts of young people.” In an interview with The Guardian, Martinchek describes how young adults are “navigating a whole new context” that includes less-reliable income and other cost pressures. For inquiries, please email media@urban.org.
papers.summary: An Urban Institute expert discusses challenges young adults face in achieving financial independence, including income volatility and cost pressures. The piece is framed as an interview highlighting that today's young people navigate a different economic context than prior cohorts.
papers.method: Interview/media commentary (non-empirical)
papers.scalability_note: No intervention identified; descriptive commentary only.

papers.title: The Economics of Cloud Infrastructure : Cost, Risk, and Comparative Advantage in a Commoditized Data Market
papers.abstract: The global data processing and storage industry is undergoing a structural transformation as cloud computing shifts from a differentiated, firm‑specific service toward a standardized and increasingly commoditized global market. International trade theory—specifically the Heckscher‑Ohlin factor proportions framework—is applied to analyze emerging patterns of specialization and comparative advantage in the provision of cloud services. Cloud output is conceptualized as a standardized data processing unit, produced under constant returns to scale using a globally uniform technology. Building on this definition, a conceptual multi‑factor unit cost model is developed that integrates traditional production factors—energy, capital and digital infrastructure, and skilled labor—with non‑factor costs distinctive to the digital economy, notably regulatory compliance costs and geopolitical and environmental risk premia. Comparative advantage is determined by the minimization of total structural data processing unit costs, inclusive of both factor and non‑factor components. This framework explains the observed concentration of data centers in locations characterized by low‑cost and reliable energy, robust connectivity infrastructure, deep pools of specialized human capital, and limited regulatory friction or systemic risk. The analysis suggests that cloud services constitute an emerging frontier of international factor‑endowment trade, where classical sources of comparative advantage interact closely with institutional quality, policy choices, and risk conditions to shape global patterns of specialization.
papers.idea: N/A — this is pure economic theory with no actionable city-policy lever.
papers.summary: The paper applies international trade theory (Heckscher–Ohlin framework) to explain why cloud data centers concentrate in regions with low energy costs, good infrastructure, skilled labor, and low regulatory burden. It models comparative advantage in cloud services as a function of both traditional production factors and non-factor costs (compliance, geopolitical risk).
papers.method: Conceptual multi-factor unit cost model derived from international trade theory; no empirical analysis reported in abstract.
papers.scalability_note: Framework is descriptive of global patterns, not a testable intervention.
papers.cost_note: No cost estimate; this is theoretical analysis.
papers.nyc_note: Explains where data centers *tend* to locate globally, but offers no actionable policy for NYC to improve resident outcomes (safety, housing, transit, health, etc.).
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No empirical evaluation; pure economic theory with no demonstrated real-world public-safety or city-policy outcome.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is abstract economics, not a policy intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Standard application of Heckscher–Ohlin trade theory to cloud computing; intellectually sound but not a fresh framing for urban problems.

papers.title: At the Edge of Risk : Assessing Mongolia’s “Plausible Worst-Case” Scenario
papers.abstract: This paper develops a plausible worst-case scenario for Mongolia in which three major disasters—a sudden collapse in coal exports to China, a prolonged dzud (severe winter disaster), and a severe flood in Ulaanbaatar—occur in quick succession. While each event has precedent in Mongolia, an increasingly unstable climate raises the likelihood of their overlap within the next 20 years. Conventional estimates of climate damage fail to capture the country’s exposure to compounding economic shocks, which could undermine development and disproportionately affect vulnerable groups. This analysis finds that the convergence of these three crises could result in a 20 percent negative shock to annual gross domestic product over a three-year period, severely disrupting output and employment. The findings underscore the need for policy decisions based on worst-case scenarios rather than mean projections, emphasizing proactive adaptation measures to mitigate systemic risks. The paper highlights key vulnerabilities and calls for a shift from reactive crisis response to strategic preparedness, multi-sectoral coordination, and resilience planning to safeguard Mongolia’s economic future.
papers.idea: N/A — paper is a macroeconomic scenario analysis with no specific policy lever identified.
papers.summary: The paper models a plausible worst-case scenario for Mongolia in which coal export collapse, severe winter disaster, and urban flooding occur together, potentially causing a 20% GDP shock over three years. It calls for shift from reactive crisis response to proactive adaptation and resilience planning, but does not propose or test specific interventions.
papers.effect_size: 20% negative GDP shock over 3-year period (in modeled scenario)
papers.method: Scenario modeling and analysis of compounding macroeconomic and climate shocks in Mongolia; no empirical study design stated.
papers.scalability_note: Country-level macroeconomic scenario model; not a replicable intervention or practice.
papers.cost_note: No cost data; analysis only.
papers.nyc_note: Focused on Mongolia's coal economy, dzud (pastoral climate shock), and national-level GDP. No concrete policy tool transferable to NYC governance or city services.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical scenario modeling with no empirical intervention or outcome data; no causal effect demonstrated.
papers.underused_note: Not a deployable public-safety intervention; macroeconomic analysis of a foreign country with no NYC applicability.
papers.novelty_note: Compound-risk scenario planning is established practice; application to Mongolia is specific but methodologically conventional.

papers.title: The Income Channel to Labor-Market Polarization
papers.authors: Diego A. Comin, Ana Danieli, Martí Mestieri
papers.abstract: We study labor-market polarization by developing a framework with heterogeneous consumers, non-homothetic preferences, and endogenous labor supply a la Roy (1951). Because income-elastic sectors are intensive in high- and low-skilled occupations, shocks that raise either the average or the dispersion of income across households shift relative labor demand toward these occupations, generating an income channel to polarization. We quantify this channel by comparing the effect of shocks that affect the distribution of household income in our model and in a version with homothetic preferences in which the income distribution does not affect sectoral composition. The income channel is sizable: it explains 42% and 210% of the increases in the relative wages of high- and low-skill workers observed between 1980 and 2016, and 26% and 51% of the increase in their hours-worked shares. A quarter of its effect operates through income dispersion, highlighting the importance of departing from representative-consumer frameworks in non-homothetic environments.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention proposed
papers.summary: The paper develops a theoretical framework showing that income distribution shocks can drive labor-market polarization through sectoral demand shifts. The authors find that this 'income channel' explains a substantial share of wage and employment polarization between 1980–2016, with income dispersion playing a key role.
papers.method: Theoretical model with heterogeneous consumers and non-homothetic preferences calibrated to match U.S. labor-market data (1980–2016); counterfactual simulations comparing outcomes with and without the income channel.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory paper; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No program or policy proposed; cost is N/A.

papers.title: Decomposing Shifts in the Beveridge Curve: Implications for Labor Market Dynamics and Inflation
papers.authors: Katharine G. Abraham, John C. Haltiwanger, Lea E. Rendell
papers.abstract: This paper explores the relationship between standard labor market tightness (vacancies divided by unemployment) and generalized labor market tightness (vacancies divided by a measure of effective searchers that accounts for potential hires from all sources including those out of the labor force or currently employed). We show that much of what the standard model attributes to variation in matching efficiency reflects changes in the ratio of effective searchers to unemployment rather than changes in “true” matching efficiency. Generalized tightness outperforms standard tightness in Phillips curve equations, both in models with tightness entering linearly and in better-fitting models with tightness entering nonlinearly. In models that distinguish between movements in generalized tightness due to movements along the generalized Beveridge curve versus Beveridge curve shifts (reflecting changes in generalized matching efficiency and other factors), changes in tightness due to Beveridge curve shifts explain more of the variation in inflation.
papers.idea: None identified; this is a theoretical labor economics paper with no actionable city-policy lever.
papers.summary: The paper decomposes shifts in the Beveridge curve (the relationship between job vacancies and unemployment) by distinguishing between standard labor market tightness and a generalized measure that includes potential workers outside the formal labor force. The authors find that changes in the composition of searchers explain more inflation variation than traditional matching-efficiency models suggest.
papers.method: Econometric analysis of Phillips curve and Beveridge curve relationships using labor market tightness measures; no experimental design, RCT, or quasi-experimental identification strategy described.
papers.scalability_note: Theoretical labor economics; no intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; only methodological/conceptual contribution.
papers.nyc_note: No city-level policy, program, or practice can be derived from this macroeconomic theory paper.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical labor economics; no public-safety or city-livability intervention tested or evaluated.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is academic macro theory, not a deployable city policy.
papers.novelty_note: Refined economic model of labor tightness; intellectually sound but standard econometric contribution, not a novel civic intervention.

papers.title: Data-Driven Automation
papers.authors: Maryam Farboodi, Andrew J. Koh, Anchi Xia
papers.abstract: We build a dynamic model of data-driven automation in which data (i) is heterogeneous and task-specific; (ii) accumulates endogenously as a byproduct of economic activity; and (iii) exhibits spillovers such that data generated by one task can augment the productivity of another. Along the transition path of automation, data plays a dual role in simultaneously augmenting the productivity of already-automated tasks and expanding the automation frontier. We derive tight conditions for the economy to be partially versus fully automated in the long-run. In the latter case, automation exhibits rich short-run dynamics that depend on the pattern of data spillovers but is always slow in the long-run: the share of tasks produced by labor decays asymptotically as a power law in time. We show that the economy is generically inefficient and analyze how a planner optimally tilts the direction of data accumulation. With endogenous capital accumulation, data-driven automation generates explosive growth but stagnant long-run wages.
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified
papers.summary: This is a theoretical dynamic model of how data accumulation drives automation over time, with data exhibiting task-specific spillovers. The authors derive conditions for partial vs. full automation and show that long-run automation is slow (power-law decay) but generates explosive growth alongside wage stagnation. They analyze optimal planner interventions on data accumulation direction.
papers.method: Mathematical dynamic model with theoretical analysis; no empirical data, natural experiment, or policy trial.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory with no tested intervention; no implementation pathway.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no program or initiative described.
papers.nyc_note: Theory paper offers no concrete policy tool, pilot, or program NYC could adopt. Wage stagnation insight is noted but no remedy is proposed or tested.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical model with no empirical evaluation or demonstrated real-world intervention effect.
papers.underused_note: Not an actionable public-safety intervention; abstract economic theory without implementation pathway.
papers.novelty_note: Standard macroeconomic modeling of automation and data spillovers; no novel public-safety or civic framing.

papers.title: Reconciling Micro Elasticities with the Macro Decline in Labor Supply
papers.authors: Loukas Karabarbounis
papers.abstract: Micro estimates of the Marshallian elasticity of labor supply are small and typically positive, whereas cross-country and time-series patterns of hours imply a strong negative relationship between wages and hours. I reconcile these two apparently contradictory observations using a single utility specification and taking into account heterogeneity in non-labor income. Micro estimates condition on non-labor income, while macro variation allows capital income to adjust alongside labor income, which strengthens the income effect. A model with heterogeneous households and exogenous capital income yields closed-form expressions in which the distribution of the labor share shapes the gap between the micro and the macro elasticities. A cross-sectional regression of hours on wages that conditions on the labor share recovers the macro elasticity. A dynamic model with heterogeneous households and incomplete asset markets reproduces both elasticities as outcomes when disciplined by joint moments of wages, hours, consumption, and wealth. The income effects that bridge the gap between the two elasticities imply marginal propensities to earn that lie in the range of estimates of micro studies on lottery winners.
papers.idea: N/A — pure theory paper with no actionable intervention lever.
papers.summary: This paper reconciles a longstanding puzzle in labor economics: why individual-level studies show small labor supply elasticities while aggregate cross-country and time-series data show strong negative relationships between wages and hours worked. The author uses heterogeneous household models and capital-income dynamics to explain the discrepancy.
papers.method: Theoretical modeling with closed-form solutions and dynamic simulations calibrated to aggregate moments of wages, hours, consumption, and wealth; no empirical intervention tested.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theory; no policy or program to scale.
papers.cost_note: No implementation cost; not actionable.
papers.nyc_note: No city-policy mechanism identified; macro labor-supply reconciliation does not translate to concrete NYC program or lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pure economic theory paper; no intervention tested or evaluated.
papers.underused_note: Not an intervention—no deployment or adoption possible; academic reconciliation only.

papers.title: Monopsony Power and the Transmission of Monetary Policy
papers.authors: Bence Bardóczy, Gideon Bornstein, Sergio Salgado
papers.abstract: This paper studies how labor market power affects the transmission of monetary policy. Using administrative U.S. Census data, we show that firms with high monopsony power—defined as those accounting for over 10 percent of the local wage bill—respond less to monetary policy in terms of their wage bill and employment. We then develop a New Keynesian model with heterogeneous firms and oligopsonistic competition to interpret these findings. Wage stickiness combined with firms’ labor market power is key to generating the heterogeneous responses that we document. Our model highlights two channels through which oligopsony shapes the aggregate effects of monetary policy: partial passthrough and misallocation. Calibrated to U.S. labor markets, the model implies that the decline in labor market power since the 1980s has increased the output response to monetary policy by about 10 percent and accounts for about 15 percent of the estimated flattening of the Phillips curve.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a theoretical macroeconomic paper with no identified actionable city-level intervention.
papers.summary: The paper finds that firms with high monopsony power (controlling >10% of local wage bill) respond less to monetary policy in wages and employment. The authors develop a theoretical model showing that wage stickiness combined with oligopsonistic competition shapes how monetary policy transmits through labor markets, and that declining labor market concentration since the 1980s has increased monetary policy's output effects.
papers.method: Administrative U.S. Census data analyzed with a calibrated New Keynesian model with heterogeneous firms and oligopsonistic competition.
papers.scalability_note: This is monetary policy macroeconomics; city governments have no lever over the Federal Reserve or aggregate monetary transmission.
papers.cost_note: N/A — no intervention proposed.
papers.nyc_note: NYC cannot implement monetary policy or directly alter firm-level monopsony power; the paper offers no local labor-market interventions.
papers.effect_size: 10% increase in output response to monetary policy; 15% of Phillips curve flattening explained by decline in labor market power since 1980s
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical macroeconomic analysis with no demonstrated public-safety or city-level intervention outcome.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—no actionable intervention identified for local deployment.
papers.novelty_note: Solid labor-economics framing, but standard macro theory without city-specific problem-solving angle.

papers.title: Don’t Give Up on Lab Experiments: Why the Field Still Needs the Lab
papers.authors: John A. List
papers.abstract: Recent enthusiasm for field experiments, and especially for natural field experiments (NFEs), in which subjects go about their daily activities unaware that any study is taking place, has sometimes been read as a verdict against the laboratory. I argue that such a verdict is wrong. Through the lens of a simple rational-choice model, I show that the four standard experimental designs (laboratory, artefactual field experiment (AFE), framed field experiment (FFE), and NFE) are comparative-static restrictions of one maximization problem, each identifying a parameter the others cannot. The model reveals that each design type has distinctive strengths and weaknesses across various dimensions of knowledge creation, including the enforcement of the conditions for causal identification, the faithfulness of the experimental environment to the theory being tested, the identification of economic primitives via theoretical structure, and the ethics of studying human subjects. On each of the dimensions, the four design types are complements rather than rivals. Nowhere is this complementarity more evident than between the two extremes. The lab enforces the conditions for causal identification that the NFE must inherit from the market; the NFE recovers the parameter that governs behaviour in the wild, free of the selection, scrutiny, and environmental distortions the lab cannot escape. A research programme using all four designs together demonstrates something no single design can produce. The framework further accommodates the recent rise of online and survey experiments as natural extensions. Our discipline’s recent drift away from laboratory evidence is leaving an important structural gap that natural field experiments, however well conceived, cannot fill.
papers.idea: This is a methods paper on experimental design philosophy with no concrete intervention proposed.
papers.summary: The paper argues that laboratory experiments remain valuable alongside field experiments, presenting a theoretical framework showing that four experimental designs (lab, artefactual field, framed field, natural field) are complementary rather than rival approaches to identifying different causal parameters.
papers.method: Theoretical paper using rational-choice modeling to analyze the comparative strengths and weaknesses of experimental methodologies; no empirical study or data.
papers.scalability_note: This is methodological guidance, not an intervention.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no program or policy proposed.
papers.nyc_note: Addresses how to conduct better research generally, not a specific NYC policy or program lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Methodological framework with no empirical intervention or outcome data; purely philosophical argument about research design.
papers.underused_note: This is a meta-analysis of experimental methods, not a public-safety intervention; already widely discussed in academic circles.
papers.novelty_note: Defends laboratory experiments against field-experiment enthusiasm; restates known trade-offs between internal and external validity rather than introducing new thinking.

papers.title: The Targets of Geoeconomic Coercion
papers.authors: Christopher Clayton, Matteo Maggiori, Jesse Schreger
papers.abstract: Great powers, like the United States and China, use existing trade and financial linkages of their economies to the rest of the world to exert geoeconomic power. The targeted entities include both foreign governments and foreign private firms. We characterize conditions under which a hegemonic country optimally targets private entities or their government in a given targeted foreign country. An advantage of coercing private entities is that they internalize less than their own government the equilibrium consequences of acquiescing to the hegemon's coercion. This allows the hegemon to build its power by exploiting the difference between the private cost to the targeted entity of the costly actions it demands and the social value to the hegemon of those actions. Coercing the government gives up some of this advantage, but offers the ability of having the targeted government potentially influence firms in its domestic economy that the hegemon finds valuable to coerce but for which it had limited direct coercive power. The relative strength of these two channels determines the optimal mix of coercion targeted at private entities and governments.
papers.idea: No actionable city-level intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper develops a theoretical model of how hegemonic powers (like the US and China) choose between coercing foreign private firms versus foreign governments using trade and financial leverage. The model characterizes when each coercion target is optimal based on which entity internalizes costs versus benefits differently.
papers.method: Non-empirical theoretical model; characterizes conditions for optimal coercion strategy targeting private entities versus governments.
papers.scalability_note: Theory paper with no implementation pathway; addresses great-power geopolitics, not local policy.
papers.cost_note: No implementation costs; no intervention.
papers.nyc_note: Concerns US-China geoeconomic competition at nation-state level; no municipal policy lever.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Theoretical framework with no empirical demonstration of effects; no public-safety intervention tested or measured.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—study is geopolitical theory, not a deployable city policy or intervention.

papers.title: Bayesian Magnetic Resonance Joint Image Reconstruction and Uncertainty Quantification using Sparsity Prior Models and Markov Chain Monte Carlo Sampling
papers.authors: Ahmed Karam Eldaly, Matteo Figini, Daniel C. Alexander
papers.abstract: We propose a novel framework for uncertainty quantification using compressed sensing magnetic resonance image reconstruction. The problem is formulated within a Bayesian framework as a linear inverse problem, with prior distributions assigned to the unknown model parameters. Specifically, the image to be reconstructed is assumed to be sparse in a given basis. We develop a general framework applicable to any basis and as examples, we test the sparsity of the image in its (1) spatial gradients using a total variation prior model, and in its (2) wavelet transform. A Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method, based on a split-and-augmented Gibbs sampler, is then employed to sample from the posterior distribution of the unknown parameters. The non-differentiable conditional distributions are efficiently sampled using a proximal MCMC method. The proposed algorithms are validated on both single-coil and multi-coil datasets using various k-space sub-sampling patterns and ratios. The results demonstrate the superior performance of each proposed approach in reconstructing images compared to its counterpart optimisation-based method. Moreover, our framework effectively quantifies uncertainty, showing a notable correlation between estimated uncertainty maps and error maps computed using ground truth and reconstructed images, compared with existing deep learning-based methods.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a mathematical methods paper with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: The paper develops a Bayesian framework for reconstructing magnetic resonance images from compressed sensing data and quantifying uncertainty in those reconstructions. The authors compare MCMC-based sampling approaches to optimization-based methods and demonstrate improved reconstruction quality and uncertainty estimation compared to deep learning baselines.
papers.method: Mathematical/computational methods paper validating algorithms on single-coil and multi-coil MRI datasets with various k-space sub-sampling patterns; no human subjects, clinical trial, or policy intervention.
papers.scalability_note: Technical algorithm paper; scalability refers to computational implementation, not policy deployment.
papers.cost_note: Cost irrelevant; no intervention or program with per-unit costs.
papers.nyc_note: Pure methods research in medical imaging. No lever for city policy, healthcare procurement, or public safety. Hospitals may eventually adopt better MRI reconstruction, but that is not a civic intervention.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pure mathematical methods paper; no public safety or city-policy outcomes measured.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is foundational medical imaging research, not a deployable civic intervention.

papers.title: The Right Call for Software Benchmarking: Consistent Decisions in Stateful Environments
papers.authors: Gábor Melis
papers.abstract: In the perpetual pursuit of performance, modern computing systems rely ever more on stateful mechanisms to accommodate the dynamics of workloads and physical environments, bolstering efficiency but confounding benchmarking and thereby the optimization of software. Indeed, by their nature, adaptive mechanisms introduce temporal dependencies between measurements and render naive estimators of individual program performance biased. Observing that rectifying such biases necessitates speculative assumptions about system dynamics, we call for prioritizing performance differentials over absolute measures and formalize software benchmarking as the decision problem of identifying the fastest program, for which relative knowledge suffices. To this end, we propose simple experiment designs admitting consistent estimators of contrasts, whereby program-specific biases cancel under tenable assumptions. These designs asymptotically yield the correct decision and afford a robust methodology for finite-budget benchmarking in stateful environments, bearing broad implications for the development of performance-sensitive software.
papers.idea: N/A — pure methods paper on software benchmarking with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: This paper addresses bias in performance benchmarking of software systems that use adaptive mechanisms and stateful environments. It proposes experiment designs that yield consistent estimators of performance contrasts, allowing researchers to identify the fastest program without requiring absolute performance measures.
papers.method: Theoretical formalization of benchmarking as a decision problem, proposing experiment designs that cancel program-specific biases under assumptions about system dynamics; no empirical study or city-scale data presented.
papers.scalability_note: Applicable only to software engineering practice, not city services or resident outcomes.
papers.cost_note: No cost implications for city residents; internal software development methodology.
papers.nyc_note: No mechanism for NYC agencies to adopt; addresses computer science benchmarking standards, not policy levers.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Pure computer science methods paper; no public-safety or civic outcome measured.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is theoretical software engineering with zero city-policy relevance.
papers.novelty_note: Standard computer science contribution; no novel framing of any civic or safety problem.

papers.title: Too Few or Too Many? Sample Size Estimation for Differential Abundance Studies
papers.authors: Michael Agronah, Benjamin M. Bolker
papers.abstract: Determining an appropriate sample size for a study is a crucial step in planning scientific research. Appropriate sample size planning avoids both inadequate and inflated sample sizes. Inflated sample sizes wastes resources, time and effort of human subjects, and lives of experimental animals. Inadequate sample sizes, a much more common problem, wastes even more resources through the inability to detect biologically meaningful differences and encourages questionable research practices like $p$-hacking. Microbiome studies are particularly challenged by small sample sizes, particularly in studies of human subjects or expensive animal models. In practice, the statistical power of taxa within a differential abundance study is influenced by the effect size (typically quantified as fold change), mean abundance of individual taxa, and the number of samples. We present a novel approach for sample size calculation for differential abundance studies as a function of effect size, mean abundance and statistical power of taxa. Our method is implemented in the power.nb R package, available at https://michaelagronah.com/power.nb/articles/stub.html. We applied our model for sample size calculation using estimates of mean abundance and fold change of taxa obtained from thirty real-world microbiome datasets. Our results showed that differential abundance microbiome studies require larger sample sizes than are currently prevalent in the literature to achieve adequate statistical power. Our framework will help researchers make informed decisions about appropriate sample sizes.
papers.idea: N/A — this is a statistical methods paper with no actionable intervention.
papers.summary: The paper presents a novel statistical framework (power.nb R package) for calculating appropriate sample sizes in microbiome differential abundance studies. The authors applied their method to 30 real-world microbiome datasets and found that such studies typically require larger sample sizes than currently used in the literature to achieve adequate statistical power.
papers.method: Theoretical statistical model development for power calculation as a function of effect size, mean abundance, and statistical power, validated against empirical estimates from 30 existing microbiome datasets.
papers.scalability_note: Statistical methods paper; not an intervention or program.
papers.cost_note: No intervention; cost is irrelevant.
papers.nyc_note: No actionable city policy, program, or practice proposed. Relevant only to researchers designing microbiome studies.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Statistical methods paper with no intervention or empirical public-safety outcome evaluated.

papers.title: A Bayesian hierarchical model for meta-analysis
papers.authors: Jing Dai, Sijie Xu, Shufei Ge
papers.abstract: Meta-analysis is a key statistical tool for synthesizing clinical trial data to evaluate treatment effects, yet traditional methods like fixed and random-effects models often fail to handle heterogeneity, study-level covariates, or hierarchical structures effectively. To overcome these limitations, we developed a Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis framework for robust parameter estimation on small samples and utilized analytical integration for efficient inference. Simulation studies indicated robust estimation of the proposed model. We applied it to the safety profiles of Oxcarbazepine (OXC) and Carbamazepine (CBZ) in epilepsy treatment. The results indicated that OXC was significantly associated with a lower risk of side effects than CBZ. The code and relevant data used in this study are openly available on GitHub at: https://github.com/xsjk/HierarchicalMetaAnalysis.
papers.idea: None—this is a statistical methods paper with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: The authors developed a Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis framework to synthesize clinical trial data and applied it to compare side-effect safety profiles of two epilepsy medications (Oxcarbazepine vs. Carbamazepine). They found OXC associated with lower side-effect risk.
papers.method: Bayesian hierarchical modeling on clinical trial meta-data; simulation studies plus application to epilepsy drug safety literature.

papers.cost_note: Not applicable—no city program to fund.
papers.nyc_note: Statistical method for academic researchers, not a lever for NYC policy or service delivery.

papers.title: Affective Political Communication and Voters' Choice of Candidates during Elections
papers.abstract: Political campaigns in modern day democracies have increasingly relied on emotional appeals, symbolic messaging and identity- based narratives to mobilise voters and shape public opinion. This study examined affective political communication and voters' choices of candidates during elections. Affective political communication involves the strategic use of emotions such as fear, hope, anger and enthusiasm to influence political attitudes and behaviours. In many political settings, political actors deploy various communication channels including campaign rallies, traditional media and social media platforms to disseminate emotionally framed messages aimed at persuading voters. The researcher adopted the library research method for the study, while the theoretical framework was hinged on Affective Intelligence Theory. Findings from the study revealed that affective political communication plays a significant role in voters' choice of candidates during elections. Also, emotional appeals embedded in campaign messages influences how voters interpret the personality, credibility and leadership qualities of candidates. Further findings revealed that media exposure particularly through social media platforms and political campaign events amplifies affective messages and contribute to the formation of positive or negative voter attitudes toward candidates. From the findings, the researcher recommended among other things, that political actors and campaign organisations adopt responsible and issue- based communication strategies that emphasise credible information rather than manipulative emotional appeals.
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This is a literature review on how emotional appeals in political campaigns influence voter candidate preferences. The study found that affective messaging, media exposure, and campaign events shape voter attitudes, and recommends that campaigns use issue-based rather than emotion-manipulative communication.
papers.method: Library research method (literature review); theoretical framework based on Affective Intelligence Theory; no original empirical study or data collection reported.
papers.scalability_note: Review/recommendation paper with no testable intervention or measurable implementation lever for city policy.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; cost not applicable.
papers.nyc_note: Paper is about electoral campaign messaging strategy, not a city service, program, or policy NYC can adopt to improve resident safety, housing, transit, health, education, or economic mobility.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: Library research method with no empirical intervention or measurement; descriptive rather than causal.
papers.underused_note: Not a public-safety or civic-service intervention; political communication strategy is outside NYC governance scope.
papers.novelty_note: Well-established understanding that emotional appeals influence voting behavior; no new mechanism or insight.

papers.title: Estimating Sloppy Directions via KDE: The Case of Kirman's Ants
papers.authors: Karl Naumann-Woleske
papers.abstract: Models whose predictions depend on only a handful of well-constrained parameter combinations, termed sloppy models, are ubiquitous in nonlinear stochastic systems. The information-geometric approach to sloppiness advocates using the symmetrized Kullback--Leibler divergence and its associated Hessian, the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM), as the natural loss function. However, prior applications have relied on analytically known or parametrically fitted distributions. In practice, for general agent-based or stochastic models the distribution must be estimated from simulation data. I demonstrate, using Kirman's ant recruitment model as a worked example, that a standard kernel density estimate (KDE) converges to the analytical FIM eigenvectors and eigenvalues with simulation budgets accessible in practice. I derive the analytical Hessian in closed form, show numerical convergence of the KDE-based estimate as a function of simulation data, and demonstrate how the stiff direction enables efficient phase exploration across the model's unimodal and bimodal regimes.
papers.idea: None; this is a mathematical methods paper with no city-policy application.
papers.summary: This paper develops a kernel density estimation (KDE) technique to estimate Fisher Information Matrix eigenvectors in sloppy models, demonstrating convergence on Kirman's ant recruitment model. The method enables efficient exploration of parameter space across different behavioral regimes in stochastic agent-based models.
papers.method: Theoretical derivation of analytical Hessian and numerical convergence testing of KDE-based estimates using simulation data from Kirman's ant recruitment model.
papers.scalability_note: Pure mathematical methods paper; no intervention or policy lever.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no implementable program.
papers.nyc_note: No connection to NYC governance, services, or policy domains.

papers.title: How Advocacy, Politics, and Implementation Challenges Shaped the Adoption of Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapy as First-Line Therapy for Malaria
papers.authors: Cordelia S. Kenney
papers.abstract: By the 1970s, the world’s premier malaria treatment at the time, chloroquine, was failing in Southeast Asia, with resistance to it documented in Africa shortly thereafter. By 2000, chloroquine had become largely ineffective in malaria-endemic countries, while malaria-spreading parasites were also becoming increasingly resistant to available alternatives, such as sulfadoxine/pyrimethamine (SP). Amid dwindling treatment options, clinical research on derivatives of artemisinin, an extract of A. annua, demonstrated the safety and efficacy of artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT). This promising new class of antimalarial drugs provided an effective alternative to the failing options on the market and has since become the standard of care in most settings. Although researchers established the efficacy of artemisinin in treating malaria in 1972 and the safety and efficacy of ACTs through clinical trials in the 1980s and 1990s, ACT did not become the first-line antimalarial treatment in most national drug policies until after 2004. In January

It analyzes advocacy, political, and implementation barriers to that adoption.

papers.title: Fiscal Procyclicality in Commodity Exporting Countries: How Much Does It Pour and Why?
papers.authors: Francisco Arroyo Marioli, Carlos A. Vegh
papers.abstract: A large literature has documented that fiscal policy is procyclical in emerging markets/developing countries and acyclical/countercyclical in advanced economies. This paper analyzes fiscal procyclicality in commodity-exporting countries. The paper makes two novel contributions. First, based on the “when it rains, it pours” phenomenon (that is, contractionary fiscal policy amplifies the business cycle), the paper shows that, on average, government spending magnifies the business cycle by 21 percent of the initial drop in output following a fall in commodity prices. Second, the paper estimates the welfare costs of fiscal procyclicality at 36 percent of the commodity business cycle. (JEL E32, E62, F14, H50, O19, Q02)
papers.idea: No actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper analyzes fiscal procyclicality (the tendency of government spending to amplify business cycles) in commodity-exporting countries. It shows that contractionary fiscal policy magnifies output drops by 21% following commodity price declines and estimates welfare costs of fiscal procyclicality at 36% of the commodity business cycle.
papers.effect_size: Government spending magnifies business cycle by 21% of initial output drop; welfare costs of 36% of commodity business cycle
papers.method: Macroeconomic analysis of fiscal policy responses to commodity price shocks in developing and emerging market economies; no specific study design or sample size provided in abstract.

papers.title: How Equality Created Poverty in Preindustrial Japan, 1600–1870
papers.authors: Yuzuru Kumon
papers.abstract: Despite well-developed economic institutions, premodern Japan, 1600–1868, had among the lowest real wages according to available estimates, around half those in preindustrial England. However, many Japanese peasants owned land, unlike their mostly landless English counterparts, due to institutional differences in land inheritance. Using a Malthusian model, I show that this greater landownership equality paradoxically led to Japan's lower wages and GDP per capita. Evidence from Japanese village censuses supports the mechanism. If, as many historians believe, high wages in Western Europe spurred industrialization, Japan's failure to industrialize first could have been shaped by its unusual preindustrial equality. (JEL D63, E23, J31, N15, N35, N45, N55)
papers.idea: N/A — no actionable intervention identified.
papers.summary: This paper uses economic modeling and historical census data to explain why preindustrial Japan (1600–1868) had lower real wages and GDP per capita than Western Europe despite more equal land ownership among peasants. The study argues that greater landownership equality paradoxically suppressed wages by increasing population density relative to land, following Malthusian dynamics, and that this may have delayed Japan's industrialization.
papers.effect_size: Japan's real wages ~50% lower than preindustrial England; mechanism is theoretical/modeled, not a quantified intervention effect.
papers.reach: Japanese village censuses (scope not specified in abstract); historical period 1600–1868.
papers.method: Malthusian economic model calibrated to preindustrial Japanese and English data, supported by village-level census evidence from historical archives.

papers.title: How Do Financial and Longevity Literacy Affect Annuity Demand?
papers.authors: Jeffrey R. Brown
papers.abstract: Deciding whether to use an annuity to draw down accumulated retirement wealth is among the most complex financial decisions individuals face. It requires reasoning about longevity risk, stochastic income streams, and largely irreversible tradeoffs, often at older ages and with limited opportunities to learn from experience. This paper reviews theoretical, empirical, and experimental research to examine how financial literacy and longevity literacy shape annuitization decisions. While some extensions of the standard lifecycle model can rationalize low annuity demand, their explanatory power depends on individuals’ ability to optimize over a set of complex risks and tradeoffs. Evidence shows that, although financial and longevity literacy are only weakly and inconsistently related to annuity take-up, they are strongly associated with retirement planning, the coherence of annuity valuations, and sensitivity to framing and choice architecture. These patterns suggest that literacy should be understood not as a direct determinant of annuity demand, but as a constraint on the extent to which observed annuitization behavior can be interpreted as revealing true preferences.
papers.idea: N/A—this is a theoretical review without an actionable city-policy intervention.
papers.summary: This paper reviews existing research on how financial and longevity literacy affect retirement annuitization decisions. It finds that literacy is weakly linked to annuity take-up but strongly associated with retirement planning coherence and susceptibility to framing, suggesting literacy constrains rather than directly determines annuity demand.
papers.method: Literature review and synthesis of theoretical, empirical, and experimental research on annuitization behavior and financial/longevity literacy.
papers.scalability_note: Pure theoretical review; no tested intervention to scale.
papers.cost_note: No intervention proposed; no cost data.
papers.nyc_note: Retirement annuity decisions are private financial choices; no direct municipal policy lever or city program implied.
papers.effect_magnitude_note: No actionable intervention tested; theoretical review of retirement finance with no public-safety or city-life outcome.
papers.underused_note: Not applicable—this is academic finance theory, not a civic intervention.
papers.novelty_note: Standard literature review on well-established economic decision-making problem.

papers.title: Dollar Erosion: Understanding the Loss of Reserve Currency Status
papers.authors: Zhengyang Jiang, Arvind Krishnamurthy, Hanno Lustig, Robert J. Richmond
papers.abstract: We quantify the impact of the loss of reserve currency status in goods and asset markets. In goods markets, the loss of seigniorage (1% of GDP per annum) makes American households spend less, mostly on U.S. goods, leaving an excess supply of U.S. goods to be cleared by a real depreciation of the dollar. The larger the home bias and the lower the elasticity of substitution between home and foreign goods, the larger the required depreciation. Standard parameters imply an 8.8% real depreciation of the dollar. In asset markets, about 50% of GDP in dollar bonds must be reabsorbed by home investors, raising the U.S. real interest rate. Standard parameters imply a 90 basis points rise, leading to an aggregate wealth loss of roughly one year of U.S. GDP.
papers.idea: N/A — this is macroeconomic theory with no city-level policy lever.
papers.summary: This paper models the macroeconomic consequences of the U.S. losing its reserve currency status, predicting an ~8.8% real dollar depreciation and a ~90 basis point rise in interest rates, resulting in aggregate wealth loss equivalent to one year of GDP.
papers.method: Theoretical economic modeling of goods and asset markets; no empirical data, experimental design, or real-world intervention tested.
papers.scalability_note: A city cannot adopt or implement monetary/reserve currency policy; this is federal domain only.
papers.cost_note: Not applicable; no intervention or program proposed.
papers.nyc_note: Reserve currency status is a federal/global macroeconomic question entirely outside NYC's policy scope.
papers.effect_size: 8.8% real depreciation of the dollar and 90 basis points rise in U.S. real interest rate

themes.label: Alcohol and addiction
themes.blurb: Cutting the harm that flows from alcohol and drugs, from outlet density and pricing to medication for opioid use disorder.

themes.label: Reentry and reemployment
themes.blurb: Smoothing the path home from jail and prison, including health coverage, benefits and a real shot at a job.

themes.label: Lead and environmental exposure
themes.blurb: Removing the environmental insults, lead chief among them, that quietly shape violence, health and learning.

themes.label: Domestic and intimate-partner violence
themes.blurb: Better ways to interrupt and respond to a category of harm that has stayed stubbornly sticky in New York City.

themes.label: Schools and the school-to-prison pipeline
themes.blurb: Keeping young people in class and out of the justice system by rethinking discipline, attendance and support.

themes.label: Gun and community violence intervention
themes.blurb: Concentrating help and deterrence on the small networks of people and places that drive most shootings.

themes.label: Behavioral and cognitive programs
themes.blurb: Low-cost programs, from cognitive behavioral therapy to summer jobs and mentoring, that shift behavior at the margin.

themes.label: Place and the built environment
themes.blurb: Treating physical space, lighting, vacant lots, greening and design, as a lever on safety.

themes.label: Policing, courts and enforcement
themes.blurb: How police, prosecutors and courts deploy their tools, from diversion and sentencing to body cameras.

themes.label: Housing and homelessness
themes.blurb: Stable housing and homelessness response as upstream public-safety and public-health infrastructure.

themes.label: Health and overdose response
themes.blurb: Public-health responses, including naloxone, treatment and crisis care, that save lives and reduce harm.

themes.label: Youth and family supports
themes.blurb: Early and family-centered investments that pay off in safety and mobility years later.

themes.label: Jobs and economic security
themes.blurb: Income, work and economic stability as foundations for safer, healthier neighborhoods.

themes.label: Other ideas
themes.blurb: Promising ideas that do not yet fit one of the bundles above.
